documents.CP-001: Canada Accessibility Policy Afterpay Last Approved: Dec 8, 2025 Effective Date: Dec 8, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Canada Accessibility Policy ("Policy") outlines Afterpay Canada Limited ("Afterpay Canada") approach to providing an inclusive and accessible environment for all Staff Members. This Policy outlines Afterpay Canada's commitment to ensuring accessibility in accordance with federal and provincial legislation in Canada. The Head of Compliance, Afterpay Canada is responsible for overseeing this Policy. Afterpay Canada conducts business in compliance with all applicable laws and regulations Please refer to the Section: Related Documentation or References of this Policy). The purpose of this Policy is to further Block's accessibility practices process by ensuring that Afterpay Canada provides an inclusive and accessible environment for all Staff Members. This Policy outlines Afterpay Canada's commitment to identifying, removing, and preventing barriers to accessibility in accordance with federal and provincial legislation in Canada. Scope: This Policy is established by Afterpay Canada and is applicable to all Afterpay Canada business lines, and jurisdictions within Canada. It outlines the standards and practices to ensure accessibility for all Staff Members. Specific Exclusions: This Policy does not apply to products or services distributed by third parties, external bank partners, or any subsidiaries operating outside of Canada. Definitions: Terminology Definition Staff Member(s) References to "Staff Member(s)" throughout this document shall refer to all full-time employees and fixed-term and contingent workers including interns, temporary workers, consultants, vendors, third-party providers, and contractors. 2. Policy Requirements 2.1 Commitment to Accessibility Afterpay Canada will perform the following on a continual basis: 1. Identifying and Removing Barriers: Identifying barriers to accessibility and implementing measures to remove these barriers; 2. Preventing Barriers: Proactively preventing the creation of new barriers to accessibility; 3. Inclusive Environment: Ensuring Afterpay Canada's services, communications, and customer interactions are inclusive and accessible to all. 2.2 Federal Legislation Accessible Canada Act, SC 2019, c 10 The Accessible Canada Act (“ACAˮ) is a federal law that aims to make Canada barrier-free by 2040. It requires organizations under federal jurisdiction to identify, remove, and prevent barriers in various areas, including information and communication technologies, the built environment, and the delivery of programs and services. 2.3 Provincial and Territorial Regulations Ontario; Accessibility for Ontarians with Disabilities Act, 2005 (“AODAˮ) This Act aims to improve accessibility across Ontario. Afterpay Canada will ensure compliance with the following: ● Accessible Feedback: Feedback processes must be accessible to persons with disabilities. Afterpay Canada will provide accessible formats and communication support upon request. Manitoba; The Accessibility for Manitobans Act, SM 2013, c 40 (“AMAˮ) This Act aims to eliminate barriers and improve accessibility in Manitoba. Afterpay Canada's compliance includes: ● Accessible Feedback: Feedback processes will be accessible, with public notifications about these options provided on Afterpay Canada's website and feedback forms. ● Accessible Communication Formats and Support: Afterpay Canada will provide accessible formats and communication supports promptly and without extra cost, after consulting with the requester. ● Customer Service Standard: Implementing policies will ensure the dignity, independence, and integration of persons with disabilities in Afterpay Canada's service delivery, providing equal opportunities for access. Nova Scotia; Accessibility Act, 2017, SNS 2017, c 2 This Act aims to make Nova Scotia accessible by 2030. Afterpay Canada will comply by providing: ● Accessible Feedback: Ensuring feedback processes are accessible, with public notifications about these options provided on Afterpay Canada's website and feedback forms. ● Accessible Communication Formats and Support: Providing or arranging for accessible formats and communication supports promptly and at no extra cost, after consulting with the individual. ● Customer Service Standard: Developing policies that respect the dignity, independence, and integration of persons with disabilities, ensuring equal access to services. Quebec; Act to Secure Handicapped Persons in the Exercise of their Rights with a View to Achieving Social, School, and Workplace Integration, CQLR c E20.1 This Act promotes the integration of persons with disabilities. Afterpay Canada will ensure compliance by providing: ● Accessible Feedback: Making feedback processes accessible and notifying the public about these options on Afterpay Canada's website and feedback forms. ● Accessible Communication Formats and Support: Providing accessible formats and communication supports promptly and without extra cost, after consulting with the requester. ● Customer Service Standard: Developing and implementing policies that respect the dignity, independence, and integration of persons with disabilities, ensuring equal access to services. British Columbia; Accessible British Columbia Act, SBC 2021, c 19 This Act aims to improve accessibility in British Columbia. Afterpay Canada will comply by: ● Accessible Feedback: Ensuring feedback processes are accessible, with public notifications about these options provided on Afterpay Canada's website and feedback forms. ● Accessible Communication Formats and Support: Providing or arranging for accessible formats and communication supports promptly and at no extra cost, after consulting with the individual. ● Customer Service Standard: Developing and implementing policies that respect the dignity, independence, and integration of persons with disabilities, ensuring equal access to services. 2.4 Web Accessibility Afterpay Canada is required to ensure its public websites and web content conforms with the Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (“WCAGˮ). This includes: ● Conformance Requirements: Ensuring websites and web content meet WCAG 2.0 Level AA standards, except for criteria requiring live captions and pre-recorded audio descriptions. ● Exemptions: This requirement does not apply to intranet websites, controlled extensions, content not directly controlled by Afterpay Canada, or situations where compliance is impracticable. 2.5 Implementation and Training Where applicable, Afterpay Canada Compliance will: 1. Training: Provide targeted accessibility training to relevant Staff Members on accessibility standards that are required and applicable to the respective business areas. 2. Feedback Mechanism: Maintain an accessible feedback process and regularly review feedback to identify areas for improvement. 3. Regular Audits: Support regular audits of Afterpay Canada's services, websites, and communication methods to ensure ongoing compliance with accessibility standards. 4. Public Notification: Support internal teams required to notify the public about the availability of accessible formats and supports through Afterpay Canada's website and customer service channels. 2.6 Conclusion Afterpay Canada is committed to creating an inclusive and accessible environment in compliance with applicable Canadian federal and provincial regulations. This Policy will be regularly reviewed and updated to ensure ongoing compliance and improvement in accessibility standards. For more information or to request accessible formats, please visit the "Accessibility" section on Afterpay Canada's website or contact the customer service team. 2.7 Document Management Document Owner This Policy is owned by the Regulatory Program Manager, Afterpay Canada or their designee. 3. Compliance & Enforcement 3.1 Roles and Responsibilities Block, Inc. Roles: ● Head of Compliance: ● Head of Compliance, Afterpay Canada, oversees implementation, ensures compliance, and reports to Block, Incʼs. executive leadership and Board. ● Compliance Program Manager: ● The Compliance Program Manager provides guidance and oversight on the applicability and implementation of this Policy to the Company's business and operational activities, ensuring that all relevant requirements are appropriately addressed and integrated into day-to-day operations. 3.2 Monitoring and Reporting On an ongoing basis, the Policy Owner and/or their designee monitors activities for compliance and objective achievement. As outlined in the Compliance Management System Policy, Compliance will provide regular updates, as applicable, to the Block, Inc. Board and executive management. Reporting may cover specific information related to: ● Policies and standards ● Procedures ● Audit/exam findings ● Issues management ● Training ● Complaints On an ongoing basis, the Policy Owner and/or their designee will monitor Policy related activities to evaluate adherence to this Policy and determine whether outputs adhere to established objectives. As outlined in the Afterpay Canada Marketing Policy, Head of Compliance, Afterpay Canada will provide regular updates to the Block, Inc. Board ("Board"). This reporting may include specific information related to policies, programs, procedures, audit/exam findings, issues management, training, and complaints. 3.3 Record Retention All Documents are retained according to the longer of: ● Block's Data Classification & Handling Policy ● Applicable regulatory requirements ● Afterpay and Square Record Retention Policy 3.4 Exceptions The Policy Owner or designee will have authority to approve exceptions and/or exemptions to this Policy provided that the proposed exception or exemption does not violate applicable laws and regulations. Policy exception requests must be documented in writing, include a reason for a request, and provide a description of the controls that Afterpay Canada will imple

Canada Complaint Policy | Afterpay Canada Ltd. 5 Confidential and Not for Distribution 1. Introduction This Complaint Policy (the “Policy”) outlines Afterpay Canada Ltd.’s (“Afterpay CA”, “the Company”) approach to managing complaints. The purpose of this Policy is to further Block’s Global Complaint Policy (“Block’s policy”) and Block’s Global Complaint Program by providing for applicable Canadian complaint requirements to ensure Afterpay CA’s compliance.The Head of Compliance, Afterpay CA is responsible for management of this Policy. 2. Purpose In addition to aligning with Block’s Policy, this Policy ensures that Afterpay CA’s complaint handling processes comply with all local laws and regulations in Canada, where the Afterpay product is offered. This Policy should be read together with the requirements set out in Afterpay’s Complaints Handling Standard Operating Procedure (“SOP”). The objective of the Policy is to ensure that Afterpay CA establishes and adheres to a consistent process for handling complaints. This involves effectively investigating, addressing, tracking, managing, and responding to Customer complaints, as well as overseeing and monitoring complaint trends to identify training needs. Additionally, the Policy sets country-specific expectations that exceed those of the Block Policy and require periodic reporting to Afterpay CA’s bank partner(s). This Policy applies to all complaints related to products offered under the Afterpay brand in Canada but does not cover routine customer inquiries about limits, features, or their Afterpay account, nor does it apply to other products offered by Block Inc. 3. Scope This Policy is established by Afterpay CA and is applicable across all Afterpay CA business lines.. Afterpay CA follows all definitions, timeframes, and expectations as outlined in Block’s Policy and Program. If Canada or a banking partner has stricter requirements than the Block Policy and Canada Complaint Policy | Afterpay Canada Ltd. 6 Confidential and Not for Distribution Program, Afterpay CA will follow the stricter standard to ensure compliance with Canadian regulations and banking partner expectations. 4. Policy Requirements 5.1 Managing Complaints (Regional Procedures) Afterpay’s Complaints Handling Procedure will ensure: ● Complaints raised in the first instance will be handled by a representative from a customer service team; and ● A customer service representative may escalate complaints to an escalations team where relevant. 5.2 Managing Complaints (Guiding Principles) When dealing with complaints, Afterpay CA must: ● Identify itself, record complaint details clearly, and determine what the Customer wants; ● Confirm the details received from the Customer; ● Empathize with the customer and deal with all Customers in a sensitive and courteous manner, taking extra care where Afterpay CA identifies instances of Customer vulnerability; and ● Attempt to resolve the complaint as soon as possible. 5.3 Managing Complaints (Written Responses) Afterpay CA aims to resolve a customer complaint within 15 business days and may be required to provide certain written responses to complaints according to local regulations. 5.4 Timeframes Afterpay acknowledges all complaints within 5 business days and provides an initial response within the same timeframe. Additionally, Afterpay makes every effort to resolve customer complaints within 15 business days. Canada Complaint Policy | Afterpay Canada Ltd. 7 Confidential and Not for Distribution 5.5 Complaint Tier(s) Complaint Tier Description ● Source of the complaint or correspondence is a Government Official, Government Attorney, or Attorney representing customer; ● Source of the complaint is a Regulator, Complaint Authority, or Consumer Advocacy Agency; ● Complaint sent to the legal entity for the business unit, board Tier 1 member, CEO/Executive, or Senior Leader; (High Risk) ● Complaint alleges violation of law or regulation, including privacy violations; ● Complaint alleges IP infringement; ● Complaint alleges Unfair Treatment; and ● Complaint presents a reputational, systematic risk, high risk, or highly complex issue requiring specialized review.1 ● Complaint is a reopened complaint (same or related issue to a Tier 2 prior complaint); and (Medium Risk) ● Complaints escalated to a superior. Tier 3 ● Complaints that do not require escalation or specialized (Low Risk) support to close. 5.7 External Dispute Afterpay CA customers have the right to lodge a complaint with any relevant consumer agency or regulatory body, including but not limited to: 1 Specialized Review within the context of the Program will be applicable to complaints in which an escalated support or specialized knowledge (Legal, Compliance, Risk) is required to properly respond to or resolve the complaint. Customers can file complaints related to consumer transactions, unfair business practices, and other consumer issues. ● Financial Consumer Agency of Canada (“FCAC”): ○ The FCAC is an independent government agency responsible for overseeing financial institutions to ensure compliance with federal consumer protection laws. Introduction This Consumer Protection Policy (“Policy”) outlines Afterpay Canada Ltd.’s (“Afterpay Canada”, “Afterpay CA”) approach to providing a seamless and reliable "buy now, pay later" service while prioritizing consumer protection. Afterpay Canada recognizes the significance of adhering to Canadian consumer protection laws and regulations to ensure the rights and interests of customers are safeguarded. This Policy serves as a comprehensive guide outlining Afterpay CA's commitments, practices, and areas of focus to maintain compliance and protect consumer rights. This Policy shall apply to Afterpay Canada Limited. This Policy has been established in accordance with federal, provincial and territorial consumer protection legislation in Canada and related rules. The Head of Compliance, Afterpay Canada is responsible for management of this Policy. Canada Consumer Protection Policy | Afterpay Canada Ltd. 5 Confidential and Not for Distribution 4. Purpose The purpose of Afterpay CA's Consumer Protection Policy is to ensure a fair, transparent, and secure environment for consumers using Afterpay services in Canada. This Policy aims to protect consumers from unfair practices, provide clear information about their rights and obligations, and foster trust in Afterpay CA's payment platform. It reflects Afterpay CA's commitment to ethical business practices, compliance with Canadian laws and regulations, and dedication to delivering a positive user experience. 5. Scope This Policy is established by Afterpay CA and is applicable across all Afterpay CA business lines within Canada. It outlines the standards and practices that all Afterpay employees, partners, and affiliates within Canada are required to adhere to in order to protect consumer rights and interests. This Policy does not apply to: ● Products that are distributed by third parties ● Bank partners and other financial institutions not directly operated by Afterpay CA. ● Subsidiaries or affiliates operating outside of Canada who are not governed by this Policy and required to adhere to their respective local regulations and policies. 6. Policy Requirements 5.1 Deceptive Acts and Practices An act or practice is deceptive where there is a representation, omission or practice that is likely to mislead consumers who are acting reasonably in the circumstances presented and the representation, omission, or practice is material. Deceptive acts include false statements, misleading claims, bait and switch situations or omitting material information that impacts product choice. Canada Consumer Protection Policy | Afterpay Canada Ltd. 6 Confidential and Not for Distribution 5.2 Unfair Acts or Practices An act or practice is unfair where it causes or is likely to cause substantial injury to consumers which cannot be reasonably avoided and is not outweighed by countervailing benefits to consumers or to competition. The injury may involve a small amount of monetary harm to a large number of people or a significant monetary injury for a select few. Unfair acts may occur when material information is withheld regarding a product or service such that the consumer cannot make an informed choice or when the consumer is coerced into an unwanted product or service. 5.3 Abusive Acts or Practices An act or practice is abusive if it materially interferes with the ability of a consumer to understand a term or condition of a product or service or takes unreasonable advantage of: ● A lack of understanding on the part of the consumer of the material risks, costs or conditions of the product or service; ● The inability of the consumer to protect the interests of the consumer in selecting or using the financial product or service; or ● The reasonable reliance by the consumer on a covered person to act in the interests of the consumer. The term “covered person” means any person that engages in offering or providing a consumer financial product or service. 5.4 Marketing Afterpay Canada’s Legal and Compliance Departments will review and approve all marketing and other consumer-facing materials prior to their usage to ensure: ● The message is complete and accurate; ● The material could not mislead the average customer of the target market; and ● The overall impression of the ad/webpage is accurate, including: ○ The general message is big enough to attract attention ○ Wording and formatting is understandable ○ All information is in a location where consumers can be expected to look. Canada Consumer Protection Policy | Afterpay Canada Ltd. 7 Confidential and Not for Distribution 5.5 New Product/ Feature Review Afterpay Canada’s Legal and Compliance Department will review and approve all new products and features to ensure fairness and transparency to the customer including: ● Considering whether the product and/or feature is (or could be) unfair, deceptive or abusive ● Ensuring that fees charged are not exorbitant. 5.6 Ensuring Transparent Disclosure Afterpay Canada’s Legal and Compliance Department will also review and approve all prospective terms and conditions as well as fee disclosures, including revisions or updates to any previously approved wording, to ensure fairness and transparency to the customer including: Clear Terms and Conditions Afterpay Canada ensures that its terms and conditions are easily accessible, written in plain language, and prominently displayed during transactions. This includes comprehensive details regarding payment schedules, fees, penalties, and cancellation policies to maintain transparency. Full Disclosure of Fees Prior to any transaction, Afterpay Canada explicitly outlines all associated fees, including but not limited to late payment charges or penalties, ensuring customers are well-informed. 5.7 Privacy and Data Security Afterpay Canada complies with the Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA) regulations, implementing robust measures to protect customer data against unauthorized access, breaches, or misuse. Afterpay Canada strictly adheres to regulations governing the collection, use, and protection of personal information. Canada Consumer Protection Policy | Afterpay Canada Ltd. 8 Confidential and Not for Distribution Afterpay Canada obtains explicit consent before collecting or using any personal information, providing customers with control over their data and ensuring compliance with privacy legislation. 5.8 Comprehensive Customer Communication Afterpay Canada shall ensure regular updates to customers regarding payment schedules, outstanding balances, and any changes to terms and conditions. Additionally, Afterpay CA must ensure that customer support is readily
documents.CP-004: Canada's Anti-Spam Legislation CASL Policy Afterpay Last Approved: Dec 8, 2025 Effective Date: Dec 8, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Canada Anti-Spam Legislation (“CASLˮ) Policy ("Policy") outlines Afterpay Canada Limited ("Afterpay Canada") approach to combat spam and regulate electronic communications in Canada. The Head of Compliance, Afterpay Canada, is responsible for overseeing this Policy. This Policy should be read in connection with Block's CANSPAM Policy and if there is any conflict between Block and the applicable local law, this Policy must be followed. Afterpay Canada conducts business in compliance with all applicable laws and regulations in accordance with CASL. The purpose of this Policy is to further Block's protection of Canadians from unsolicited electronic messages while fostering trust and confidence in digital communications. It applies to a wide range of electronic messages sent for commercial purposes, including emails, text messages, and other electronic messaging. CASL not only addresses spam but also governs the installation of computer programs and the alteration of transmission data. This legislation is crucial for Afterpay Canada as it operates within the digital payment services sector, where electronic communications play a vital role in customer engagement and business operations. In short, CASL aims to: ● Protect individuals and businesses from unsolicited electronic messages, spyware, and malware. ● Regulate CEMs to ensure transparency, accountability, and trust in digital communications. ● Facilitate a secure and competitive digital marketplace in Canada. Scope: This Policy is established by Afterpay Canada and is applicable to all Afterpay business lines and jurisdictions within Canada. It applies to Staff Members involved in the marketing, distribution, and communication activities of Afterpay Canada. This Policy does not apply to: ● Products and services that are distributed by third-party partners outside the jurisdiction of Canada. ● Bank partners who are independently responsible for their compliance with CASL. ● Any business activities or communications managed by external marketing agencies that operate under their own CASL compliance policies. Definitions: Terminology Definition CASL Canada Anti-Spam Legislation (“CASLˮ) protects consumers and businesses from the misuse of digital technology, including spam and other electronic threats. CEMs Commercial Electronic Messages (“CEMˮs) are any Email messages the Primary Purpose of which is to advertise or promote for a commercial purpose, a commercial product or service including content on the Internet. An email message would not be considered to be a CEM solely because such a message includes a reference to a commercial entity that serves to identify the Sender or a reference or link to an Internet website operated for a commercial purpose. Sender A person who initiates an email message and whose product, service, or internet website is advertised or promoted by the message. Staff Member(s) References to "Staff Member(s)" throughout this document shall refer to all full-time employees and fixed-term and contingent workers including interns, temps, consultants, vendors, third-party providers, and contractors. 2. Policy Requirements 2.1 Who CASL Protects CASL provides protection to all individuals, businesses, and organizations in Canada from unwanted electronic communications. This includes consumers, businesses, and government entities. CASL's reach extends beyond Canadian borders to anyone sending electronic messages to or from Canada, emphasizing its global impact on digital communications. 2.2 Information requirements in Commercial Electronic Messages CEMs When sending CEMs, Afterpay Canada must include certain information to comply with CASL. This includes: ● Identifying information about the Sender, including name and contact information. ● A clear and functional unsubscribe mechanism that allows recipients to opt out of receiving future messages. Any additional information required by CASL to ensure transparency and accountability in electronic communications. 2.3 Commercial Electronic message CEM Content Requirements Every CEM sent by Afterpay Canada must include: ● Name or business name of the Sender. ● If sent on behalf of another person, the name or business name of that person. ● Contact information (mailing address, phone number, email, or web address) valid for at least 60 days after sending the CEM. ● An unsubscribe mechanism allowing recipients to opt-out of future messages. The unsubscribe mechanism in CEMs must: ● Enable recipients to indicate their wish to stop receiving CEMs at no cost. ● Specify an electronic address or webpage valid for at least 60 days after the CEM is sent. ● Be easily accessible, simple, and quick to use. ● Process unsubscribe requests within 10 business days of receipt. 2.4 Consent CASL mandates that businesses obtain consent before sending CEMs to individuals. Afterpay Canada must ensure that it has the appropriate consent—either express or implied—before sending any CEMs. Express consent requires a clear and affirmative indication of consent, while implied consent may arise from existing business relationships or specific circumstances outlined in CASL. 2.5 General Requirements for Consent When seeking consent for any act regulated under CASL, Afterpay Canada must clearly outline the purpose, identify the requesting party and the party on whose behalf consent is sought, and provide contact information. Consent can be oral or written and must be obtained separately for each type of act specified under CASL. 2.6 Express Consent When seeking express consent to send CEMs, Afterpay Canada must adhere to CASL's prescribed manner for requesting consent. The intended recipient — that is, the consumer or individual to whom Afterpay Canada wishes to send CEMs — must actively indicate their consent. Consent cannot be bundled with general terms and conditions but must be sought separately. The request for consent must clearly state the purpose, name or business name of the Sender, contact information, and the recipient's right to withdraw consent. 2.7 Additional Consent Requirements For acts involving altering transmission data or installing computer programs, Afterpay Canada must comply with specific requirements to allow for withdrawal of consent and assist in removing or disabling the program if requested by the recipient. 2.8 Afterpay Responsibilities Afterpay Canada is committed to upholding the principles of CASL in all its electronic communications activities. Compliance with CASL is essential to maintaining customer trust, safeguarding privacy, and avoiding potential legal and financial consequences. 2.9 Marketing and Compliance Controls Afterpay Canada will implement appropriate compliance and marketing controls to ensure adherence with CASL, including: ● Obtain required explicit consent from individuals before sending CEMs for marketing purposes. ● Maintain and update consent records to ensure compliance with consent requirements under CASL. ● Review all marketing communications to ensure they include required identification information and disclose the purpose of the message clearly. ● Include a clear and functioning unsubscribe mechanism in all marketing communications. ● Process unsubscribe requests promptly and honor opt-out preferences. ● Conduct regular training sessions for marketing team members on CASL requirements and best practices. ● Maintain detailed records of consent obtained from individuals and communication practices. ● Periodically review marketing practices to ensure ongoing compliance with CASL regulations. ● Ensure that any third-party service providers engaged in marketing activities comply with CASL requirements. 2.10 Compliance Measures Afterpay Canada will implement the following compliance measures: ● Staff Training: Where applicable, provide targeted training and awareness programs for relevant Staff Members involved in electronic communications to ensure understanding of CASL requirements and compliance obligations. ● Consent Management: Implement robust controls and oversight mechanisms to manage and track consent from individuals, ensuring that only those who have provided consent receive CEMs. ● Message Content Review: Regularly review the content of CEMs to ensure compliance with CASL's information and unsubscribe requirements. ● Record-Keeping: Maintain detailed records of consent obtained and communication practices to demonstrate compliance with CASL in case of audits or investigations. 2.11 Penalties Non-compliance with CASL can result in severe penalties, including but not limited to substantial fines of up to CAD$ 10 million for businesses and CAD$ 1 million for individuals. Additionally, non-compliance may result in reputational damage and loss of customer trust. Therefore, adherence to CASL requirements is crucial for Afterpay Canada to operate ethically and responsibly in the Canadian digital marketplace. 2.12 Conclusion By adopting and adhering to this CASL compliance Policy, Afterpay Canada demonstrates its commitment to maintaining high standards of ethical conduct in electronic communications. This Policy educates all stakeholders, including Staff Members, about the importance of CASL compliance and its impact on privacy, security, and trust in digital communications within the Canadian context. 2.13 Document Management Document Owner This Policy is owned by the Regulatory Program Manager, Afterpay Canada. 3. Compliance & Enforcement 3.1 Roles and Responsibilities Block, Inc. Roles: ● Head of Compliance, Afterpay Canada: ○ Head of Compliance, Afterpay Canada is Responsible for overall CASL compliance, policy oversight, and reporting to Block, Inc. Board and executive management. ● Compliance Program Manager: ○ The Compliance Program Manager provides advisory support, guidance and oversight on the applicabili
documents.CP-005: Controlling the Assault of Non-Solicited Pornography and Marketing Act (“CANSPAMˮ) Policy Block, Inc. Last Approved: Oct 30, 2025 Effective Date: Oct 30, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: The Controlling the Assault of Non-Solicited Pornography And Marketing Act (“CANSPAMˮ) Policy demonstrates Block, Inc.'s commitment to comply with the CANSPAM Act across all its subsidiaries and operations. This policy establishes comprehensive guidelines governing Block's commercial email practices while maintaining brand-specific flexibility where needed. The CANSPAM Act serves as a crucial regulatory framework enacted to reduce spam and unsolicited pornography by prohibiting senders of unsolicited commercial email messages from disguising the source and content of their messages, while ensuring consumers maintain control over their email communications. CANSPAM requires Block to follow specific procedures when initiating email messages to customers or prospective customers, including obtaining affirmative consent, ensuring message content is not false or misleading, and providing recipients with the ability to opt-out of future correspondence. Scope: This Policy applies to Block, Inc. and its applicable U.S. subsidiaries and affiliates (collectively, "Block") that send commercial email messages. The only exception is Square Financial Services (“SFSˮ), Blockʼs Utah state-chartered Industrial Bank federally regulated by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation, which has its own Compliance Program. It governs all commercial emails sent by or on behalf of Block, including messages with both commercial and transactional content, and third-party email services. This Policy applies to Square, Cash App, and Afterpay's commercial email communications but does not apply to purely transactional messages or internal communications. 1 Definitions: Terminology Definition Affirmative As it relates to commercial electronic mail ("Email") messages, Affirmative Consent Consent occurs when the Recipient expressly consents to receive the message, either in response to a clear and conspicuous request for such consent or at the Recipient's own initiative. If the message is from a party other than the party to which the Recipient communicated such consent, the Recipient must have been given clear and conspicuous notice at the time the consent was communicated that their Email address could be transferred to such other party for the purpose of initiating Commercial Email Messages. Commercial Any Email message the Primary Purpose of which is to advertise or promote Email Message for a commercial purpose, a commercial product or service including content on the Internet. An Email message would not be considered to be a Commercial Email Message solely because such a message includes a reference to a commercial entity that serves to identify the Sender or a reference or link to an Internet website operated for a commercial purpose. Primary Purpose The Primary Purpose of an Email message will be deemed to be commercial if it: ● Contains only the commercial advertisement or promotion of a commercial product or service ● Contains both commercial content and "transactional or relationship" content if either: ○ A Recipient reasonably interpreting the subject line of the Email message would likely conclude that the message contains commercial content; or ○ The Email message's "transactional or relationship" content does not appear in whole or substantial part at the beginning of the body of the message ● Contains both commercial content as well as content that is not transactional or relationship content if a Recipient reasonably interprets either: ○ The subject line of the Email message would likely conclude that the message contains commercial content; or ○ The body of the message would likely conclude that the Primary Purpose of the message is commercial Recipient An authorized user of the Email address to which the message was sent or delivered. 2 Sender A person who initiates an Email message and whose product, service, or Internet website is advertised or promoted by the message. Staff Member(s) References to "staff" throughout this document shall refer to all full-time Employees and fixed-term and contingent workers including interns, temps, consultants, vendors, third-party providers, and contractors. Transactional or An Email message with the Primary Purpose of facilitating, completing, or Relationship confirming a commercial transaction that the Recipient had previously Email agreed to enter into; to provide warranty, product recall, or safety/security information; or subscription, membership, account, loan, or other information relating to an ongoing purchase or use. 2. Block, Inc. Requirements 2.1 General Requirements Block ensures all commercial email communications comply with CANSPAM requirements. This includes emails sent directly by Block or through third parties on Block's behalf. Block maintains appropriate controls to ensure compliance across all brands and communication types. 2.2 Message Classification and Content Requirements Commercial Email Classification Block classifies email messages based on their primary purpose to determine applicable CANSPAM requirements. Messages are evaluated to determine whether they are: ● Commercial messages promoting products or services ● Transactional or relationship messages facilitating existing services ● Mixed-purpose messages containing both commercial and transactional content Content Standards All commercial email messages must include: ● Accurate header information that identifies the sending source ● Subject lines that accurately reflect message content ● Clear identification as advertisements when required ● Valid physical postal address ● Visible and operable unsubscribe mechanism ● Required regulatory disclosures based on message content 2.3 Recipient Rights and Controls Opt-Out Requirements 3 Block maintains comprehensive opt-out processes that: ● Provide clear and conspicuous opt-out mechanisms ● Honor opt-out requests within 10 business days ● Maintain opt-out functionality for 30 days after message delivery ● Prohibit charging fees or requiring additional information for opt-outs ● Prevent sale or transfer of email addresses after opt-out Consent Management Block maintains appropriate controls for: ● Obtaining and documenting affirmative consent ● Managing consent across brands and product lines ● Honoring recipient preferences ● Maintaining records of consent and opt-out requests 2.4 Third-Party Management Block maintains oversight of third parties involved in commercial email communications through: ● Clear contractual requirements for CANSPAM compliance ● Established approval processes for email content and configuration ● Monitoring of third-party compliance with requirements ● Integration of opt-out processes across platforms 2.5 Transactional or Relationship Email Exclusions Block ensures transactional or relationship messages: ● Prioritize transactional content at the beginning of messages ● Maintain clear distinction from commercial content ● Include required account servicing information ● Meet timing requirements for critical notifications 3. Brand-Specific Requirements Block implements CANSPAM requirements across its subsidiaries while recognizing the unique characteristics of each business line's email communications. Detailed procedures for implementation are maintained in subsidiary-specific standards and procedures. 4 3.1 Cash App Cash App's implementation of CANSPAM requirements addresses the specific needs of consumer financial services: Message Classification ● Financial transaction confirmations and security alerts are treated as transactional ● Investment-related communications must meet additional regulatory requirements ● Promotional content must be clearly distinguished from account servicing communications Consumer Protection Requirements ● Essential account and security notifications must be maintained regardless of marketing preferences ● Investment and cryptocurrency promotional content must include required disclosures ● Cross-border payment communications must meet additional regulatory requirements 3.2 Afterpay Afterpay's implementation focuses on the unique aspects of BNPL services: Payment Communications ● Payment schedule and confirmation messages are treated as transactional ● Marketing communications must be clearly separated from payment notifications ● Merchant partnership promotions require clear identification of all parties Consumer Requirements ● Payment-related communications must be maintained regardless of marketing preferences ● Credit-related disclosures must meet additional regulatory requirements ● Shop Directory promotional content must clearly identify merchant relationships The above brand-specific requirements supplement the general compliance TILA requirements outlined in this Policy but are only applicable to their respective brands (i.e., Square Banking requirements apply only to Square Banking operations, Cash App requirements apply only to Cash App operations, and Afterpay requirements apply only to Afterpay operations). 5 4. Compliance & Enforcement 4.1 Roles and Responsibilities: Block, Inc. Roles: ● Compliance Policy Committee (“CPCˮ): ○ Provides oversight and governance of applicable Compliance Policies and is responsible for reviewing and and approving this policy annually to ensure alignment with regulatory requirements and expectations ○ Providing guidance on significant policy matters ○ Ensuring consistency across Blockʼs Compliance Policy Framework ● Block Inc, Chief Compliance Officer (“CCOˮ) ○ Provides assistance and support in implementing this Policy across the organization ○ Establishes appropriate practices designed to ensure Company operations reflect Block's commitment to compliance ○ Ensures all Employees are aware of their compliance responsibilities ○ Monitors and reports on compliance w
documents.CP-006: Electronic Fund Transfer Act Regulation E Policy Block, Inc. Last Approved: Oct 30, 2025 Effective Date: Oct 30, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Electronic Fund Transfer Act Policy (“Policyˮ) outlines Block, Inc.'s U.S. commitment to adhering to regulatory requirements as well as industry standards and best practices in the delivery of its products and services. This Policy is applicable in the United States across all products that involve Electronic Fund Transfers (“EFTˮ) and Remittance Transfers as those transactions are defined under the Electronic Fund Transfer Act (“EFTAˮ) 15 U.S.C. §§ 1693 et seq.) and its implementing regulation, Regulation E 12 C.F.R. Part 1005. This Policy establishes comprehensive guidelines governing Block, Inc. and its U.S. subsidiaries' electronic fund transfer services while ensuring consistent consumer protections across all platforms. The purpose of the EFTA and Regulation E is to establish the basic rights, liabilities, and responsibilities of Consumers who use EFT and Remittance Transfer services and of the financial institutions or other persons that offer these services. The primary objective of the EFTA and Regulation E is the protection of individual Consumers engaging in EFTs and Remittance Transfers. Scope: This Policy applies to Block, Inc. and its subsidiaries (collectively, "Block") in the United States, governing all activities involving EFTs and Remittance Transfers subject to EFTA and Regulation E. The only exception is Square Financial Services (“SFSˮ), Blockʼs Utah state-chartered Industrial Bank federally regulated by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation, which has its own Compliance Program. 1 Definitions: Terminology Definition A card, code, login credentials or other means of access to a Consumer’s Access Device account, or any combination of devices, which may be used by the Consumer to initiate an EFT. This could be the Consumer's debit card, phone, computer/laptop, or login information. Anything that Consumers use to access their funds. A demand deposit (checking), savings, or other consumer asset account Account (other than an occasional or incidental credit balance in a credit plan) held directly or indirectly by a financial institution and established primarily for personal, family, or household purposes. The term includes a Prepaid Account. An individual who is a natural person who has obtained an Account that is Consumer to be used primarily for personal, family or household purposes. Any transfer of funds that is initiated through an Electronic Terminal, Electronic Fund telephone, computer, or magnetic tape for the purpose of ordering, instructing, Transfer (“EFTˮ) or authorizing a financial institution to debit or credit a Consumer's Account. The term includes, but is not limited to: ● Point-of-sale transfers; ● Automated teller machine transfers; ● Direct deposits or withdrawals of funds; ● Transfers initiated by telephone; and ● Transfers resulting from debit card transactions, whether or not initiated through an Electronic Terminal. An electronic device other than a telephone operated by a Consumer, Electronic through which a Consumer may initiate an EFT, including point-of-sale Terminal terminals, ATMs and cash dispensing machines. The term Error means: Error ● Unauthorized EFTs; ● Incorrect or missing transfers to or from a Consumer’s account; ● Omission of a transaction from a periodic statement; ● Computational or bookkeeping Errors; ● Receipt of an incorrect amount from an Electronic Terminal; and ● Requests for documentation (unless requesting a duplicate copy), or requests for information concerning a transaction or clarification to determine whether an “Error” specified above exists. For Remittance Transfers, the term Error also means: ● An incorrect amount paid by a sending customer, unless the remittance disclosures stated an estimate of the amount paid by the 2 Terminology Definition sender and the difference results from application of the actual exchange rate, fees, and taxes, rather than any estimated amount; ● Failure to make available to a designated recipient the amount of currency disclosed (unless certain conditions are met); and ● Failure to make funds available to a designated recipient by the date of availability stated in the disclosure provided to the sender (unless certain conditions are met). A written or oral communication from a Consumer indicating that the Notice of Error Consumer believes there is an Error on their Account, and that: (“NOEˮ) ● Provides sufficient information for Block to identify the Consumer’s name and account number; and ● Indicates why the Consumer believes an Error exists and includes to the extent possible the type, date, and amount of the alleged Error. For Remittance Transfers, a NOE is a written or oral communication from a Sender indicating the Sender believes there is an Error related to a Remittance Transfer sent from their account, and that: Provides sufficient information for to identify the Sender’s name, phone number, and address, the Remittance Transfer recipient’s name, and if known phone number or address; and ● Indicates why the Sender believes an error exists and includes to the extent possible the type, date, and amount of the error of the Remittance Transfer to which the notice of error applies An EFT authorized in advance to recur at substantially regular intervals. Preauthorized EFT In relevant part, an Account that is marketed or labeled as “prepaid” and Prepaid Account that is redeemable upon presentation at multiple, unaffiliated merchants for goods or services or usable at automated teller machines; or an Account that is issued on a prepaid basis in a specified amount or not issued on a prepaid basis but capable of being loaded with funds thereafter; the primary function of which is to conduct transactions with multiple, unaffiliated merchants for goods or services, or at Automated Teller Machines (“ATM”), or to conduct person-to-person transfers, that is not a checking account, share draft account, or negotiable order of withdrawal account. A temporary credit that may be applied to a Consumer’s Account during Provisional their NOE investigation if Block is not able to reach a decision within ten Credit (10) business days. The electronic transfer of funds from a Consumer in the United States to a Remittance recipient in a foreign country. The transfer must be initiated by a remittance Transfer transfer provider and be greater than $15.00 U.S. dollars. 3 Terminology Definition An EFT from a Consumer's Account initiated by a person other than the Unauthorized Consumer without actual authority to initiate the transfer and from which the EFT Consumer receives no benefit. The term does not include an EFT initiated: ● By a person who was furnished the Access Device by the Consumer (i.e., authorized user), unless the Consumer has notified Block that transfers by that person are no longer authorized; ● With fraudulent intent by the Consumer or any person acting in concert with the Consumer; or ● By Block or its employees. 2. Block, Inc. Requirements 2.1 Disclosures and Periodic Statements Block will provide disclosures required by Regulation E to its Consumers in a form that is clear, readily understandable, written in English, and can be kept by Consumers. The scope of disclosures provided by Block to its Consumers includes, but is not limited to:1 ● Initial Disclosures. Provided before a Consumer can make an EFT, including information such as the liability of Block and the Consumer for Unauthorized EFTs, contact information, available transfer types and limitations, applicable fees (including those for ATMs), the Consumerʼs right to documentation and stop payment, and Blockʼs confidentiality agreement. ● Change in Terms Disclosures. Provided at least 21 days before the effective date of any change in a term or condition that was disclosed initially, if the change would result in: increased fees for the Consumer, increased liability for the Consumer, changes in types of EFTs available, or stricter limitations on the frequency or dollar amount of transfers. ● Error Resolution Disclosures. Indicate how a Consumer can contact to file a NOE, provided with the initial disclosure and thereafter either annually or with each periodic statement. ● Receipts. Provided to a Consumer each time the Consumer initiates an EFT at an Electronic Terminal, including the dollar amount of the EFT, date, type, Consumer identification, terminal information, and the third-party where funds were transferred to/from. 1 For disclosure requirements specific to Prepaid Accounts, see section 5.6 – Prepaid Account Requirements for additional requirements. For disclosure requirements specific to gift cards, see section 5.8 – Gift Cards for additional requirements. 4 ● Periodic Statements. Provided monthly to each Consumer that has had an EFT processed on their account during the statement period, including transaction information, account number, fees, account balance, and contact information. Block must explain the consumer's right to stop payment of preauthorized electronic fund transfers and the procedures for initiating such requests. The disclosure must address Block's liability for failure to complete or stop transfers as requested, including the conditions under which Block assumes liability and any applicable limitations. 2.2 Preauthorized Electronic Funds Transfers Block will execute Preauthorized EFTs for its Consumers, upon request and with written authorization, and provide a written notice of transfer within two 2 business days after the transfer occurs (or is rejected) if the Preauthorized EFT is scheduled to transfer at least once every sixty 60 days. Block will stop payment of a Preauthorized EFT when it receives a stop payment request (orally or in writing) at least three 3 business days before the date of the next scheduled transfer, from a Consumer who has in
documents.CP-007: Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act (“ESignˮ) Policy Block, Inc. Last Approved: Oct 30, 2025 Effective Date: Oct 30, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act Policy ("Policy") outlines Block, Inc.'s enterprise-wide approach to compliance with the Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act ("ESign"). The purpose of this Policy is to establish comprehensive guidelines governing Block, Inc. and its U.S. subsidiaries' use of electronic signatures and records, ensuring consistent operations while maintaining brand-specific flexibility. Scope: This Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act Policy (the "Policy") outlines Block, Inc.'s enterprise-wide approach to compliance with the Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act ("ESign"). This Policy establishes comprehensive guidelines governing Block, Inc. and its U.S. subsidiaries' use of electronic signatures and records, ensuring consistent operations while maintaining brand-specific flexibility. The only exception is Square Financial Services (“SFSˮ), Blockʼs Utah state-chartered Industrial Bank federally regulated by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation, which has its own Compliance Program. Definitions: Terminology Definition Consumer An individual who obtains or has obtained a financial product or service that is to be used primarily for personal, family, or household purposes. Electronic Relating to technology having electrical, digital, magnetic, wireless, optical, electromagnetic, or similar capabilities. Electronic A contract or other record created, generated, sent, communicated, Record received, or stored by electronic means. 1 Electronic An electronic sound, symbol, or process attached to or logically associated Signature with a contract or other record and executed or adopted by a person with the intent to sign the record. Record Information that is inscribed on a tangible medium or that is stored in an electronic or other medium and is retrievable in perceivable form. Transaction An action or set of actions relating to the conduct of business, consumer, or commercial affairs between two or more persons. Staff Member(s) All employees and fixed-term and contingent workers who support Block's operations, including interns, temporary workers, consultants, vendors, third-party providers, and contractors. 2. Block, Inc. Requirements 2.1 Fundamental ESign Requirements Block recognizes that electronic signatures and records form the foundation of modern digital commerce. To ensure the validity and enforceability of electronic transactions, Block implements four essential requirements across all its operations: 1 clear demonstration of intent to sign, 2 informed consent and demonstrated ability to access electronic records, 3 reliable association between signatures and signers, and 4 secure record retention. These requirements enable Block to provide information and notices electronically while maintaining compliance with all applicable regulations. ESign serves as an overlay to existing laws and regulations, ensuring that signatures, contracts, or other records relating to a transaction maintain their validity when executed electronically. This framework allows Block to facilitate efficient electronic commerce while preventing deception and fraud in electronic transactions. Block achieves this through comprehensive systems and controls that verify, record, and maintain electronic transactions in a manner that meets or exceeds regulatory standards. 2.2 Consumer Notification and Transparency Before initiating electronic communications, Block ensures transparent communication with Consumers about the electronic relationship. Block provides clear and conspicuous information about the scope of electronic communications, whether for a specific transaction or ongoing relationship. This includes detailed explanations about the types of records that will be provided electronically, such as disclosures, policies, account statements, and other important notices. 2 Block maintains transparency about Consumers' rights regarding paper records, ensuring they understand their options to receive paper copies and any associated fees. Additionally, Block clearly communicates the right to withdraw consent from electronic communications, including straightforward procedures for withdrawal and any resulting consequences. This commitment to transparency extends to keeping Consumers informed about technical requirements and procedures for updating their electronic contact information. 2.3 Consumer Consent Process Block implements a comprehensive consent process that ensures Consumers actively choose to engage in electronic transactions. This process requires affirmative action by the Consumer and demonstrates their ability to access electronic information in the formats we use. The consent process is integrated into our various service platforms, ensuring Consumers understand and agree to electronic communications before proceeding with transactions or account relationships. During the onboarding process, consumers must affirmatively consent to receive electronic disclosures. This consent is obtained in a manner that reasonably demonstrates their ability to access information in the electronic form that will be used. Block achieves this through a process where Consumers actively engage with electronic documents and confirm their ability to receive and access them. Reading and acknowledging the electronic consent form through our platforms serves as sufficient confirmation of this ability. 2.4 Withdrawal of Consent Consumers maintain the right to withdraw their consent to electronic communications at any time. Block provides multiple methods for withdrawal, clearly documented in initial disclosures and readily accessible through Block service platforms. Upon receiving a withdrawal request, Block processes it within a reasonable timeframe and provides confirmation of the withdrawal's effective date in writing, either electronically or in paper form. Block does not impose fees for processing consent withdrawals. However, Block clearly communicates any consequences of withdrawal, such as potential impact on service availability or account features. In cases where electronic communication is essential to service delivery, withdrawal of consent may affect the Consumer's ability to access certain services. Block ensures Consumers understand these implications while maintaining their right to choose their preferred communication method. 2.5 Technical Requirements and Updates Block maintains clear communication about the technical requirements for accessing electronic records. Block provides detailed information about necessary hardware and software before any electronic transaction occurs. This information helps Consumers make informed decisions about their ability to engage in electronic transactions and maintain access to their records. When technical requirements change, particularly in ways that could materially affect access to electronic records, Block implements a comprehensive notification process. This includes: 3 ● Providing clear explanations of new requirements ● Assessing potential impact on record accessibility ● Offering guidance on necessary updates ● Reaffirming consent where appropriate ● Reinforcing the right to withdraw consent without penalty 2.6 Invalid or Inoperable Contact Information Block maintains procedures for handling cases where electronic contact information becomes invalid or inoperable. This includes situations where email addresses or phone numbers malfunction or become invalid. In such cases, Block may treat this as a withdrawal of consent for electronic communications to that specific contact method. Block implements processes to: ● Detect delivery failures ● Attempt alternate contact methods ● Maintain service continuity where possible ● Protect consumer access to important information 2.7 Record Management and Accessibility Blockʼs approach to electronic records emphasizes both security and accessibility. Block maintains records in formats that preserve their integrity while ensuring authorized parties can access them throughout their required retention period. This includes implementing systems for accurate record creation, secure storage, and reliable reproduction when needed. Block's record retention practices align with both internal policies and regulatory requirements. Block maintains comprehensive systems to track retention periods and ensure records remain available for the longer of any applicable retention requirements. This approach balances accessibility needs with security considerations while maintaining regulatory compliance. 2.8 State Law Considerations While ESign provides the federal framework for electronic signatures and records, Block recognizes that state laws may impose additional or more stringent requirements. In jurisdictions where state regulations overlap with ESign requirements, Block applies the more conservative approach to ensure comprehensive compliance. This includes adherence to state-specific versions of the Uniform Electronic Transactions Act UETA and any state-specific consumer protection provisions. When conflicts arise between applicable laws and these requirements, Block consults with Legal Counsel to resolve such conflicts and adjust our practices accordingly. This ensures Block maintains compliance while providing consistent service across our operating jurisdictions. 4 3. Brand-Specific Requirements Block will comply with ESign requirements, which may be detailed further in related Standards or Procedures. This section outlines only the distinct implementations and unique requirements specific to each subsidiary's lending products. 3.1 Cash App: Cash App's implementation of ESign requirements reflects its mobile-first, consumer-focused approach to financia
documents.CP-008: Equal Credit Opportunity Act (“ECOAˮ) Policy Block, Inc. Last Approved: Oct 30, 2025 Effective Date: Oct 30, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Equal Credit Opportunity Act Policy ("Policy") outlines Block's enterprise-wide approach to ensuring fair and equitable lending practices across its credit products. The purpose of this Policy is to establish comprehensive guidelines governing Block, Inc., and its U.S. subsidiaries' compliance with the Equal Credit Opportunity Act ("ECOA"), its implementing Regulation B, and related state laws, while ensuring consistent operations and maintaining brand-specific flexibility, where applicable. Block's Business Product Compliance Lead, Lending & Credit, and/or their designee, is responsible for the management of this Policy. Scope: This Policy applies to all Credit Products offered by Block, Inc., its subsidiaries and affiliates (collectively, "Block") in the United States whether consumer or commercial purpose, as ECOA protects both individual and business credit applicants. The only exception is Square Financial Services (“SFSˮ), Blockʼs Utah state-chartered Industrial Bank federally regulated by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation, which has its own Compliance Program. Definitions: Terminology Definition Adverse Action A refusal to grant credit in substantially the amount or on substantially the terms requested in an Application unless (a) Block makes a counteroffer (to grant credit in a different amount or on other terms) and the Applicant uses or expressly accepts the credit offered; (b) A termination of an account or an unfavorable change in the terms of an account that does not affect all or substantially all of a class of the creditor's accounts; or (c) A refusal to increase the amount of credit available to an applicant who has made an application for an increase. Adverse Action does not include a change in the terms of an account expressly agreed to by an Applicant, or any action or forbearance relating to an account taken in connection with inactivity, default, or delinquency as to that account. 1 Applicant A person (individual or entity) that requests credit or who has received credit from Block, including any person that may become contractually liable for credit provided by Block. Application An oral or written request for an extension of credit made in accordance with procedures used by Block for the type of credit requested. Completed An application that Block has received which contains all the information that Application Block regularly obtains and considers in evaluating applications for the amount and type of credit requested (including, but not limited to, credit reports, any additional information requested from the applicant, and any approvals or reports by governmental agencies or other persons that are necessary to guarantee, insure, or provide security for the credit or collateral). Credit Products Cash App and Afterpay branded consumer loan products for personal, family, or household purposes (including, but not limited to, Cash App Borrow, Cash App Afterpay Pay Over Time, Afterpay on Cash App Card, Cash App Afterpay Online Checkout, Afterpay Pay-in-4, and Afterpay Pay Monthly) and Squareʼs business-purpose credit products. Prohibited Basis ● Race or color ● Religion ● National origin ● Sex ● Marital status ● Age, as long as the applicant is able to contract ● Receipt of income from a public assistance program ● Exercising rights under the Consumer Credit Protection Act in good faith ● Gender/gender identity/gender expression ● Sexual Orientation ● Veteran/servicemember status ● Disability ● Pregnancy ● HIV Status ● Ancestry 2. Block, Inc. Requirements 2.1 Policy Statement Block is committed to ensuring its business activities result in fair, consistent, and responsible treatment of all consumers, and comply with all applicable state and federal laws governing non-discriminatory lending practices. 2 The standards set out in this Policy represent minimum requirements based on applicable legal and regulatory guidance and apply throughout Block's lending operations. These requirements are intended to prevent Block's Employees from violating federal and state regulations related to consumer compliance with respect to fair lending laws, regulations, and practices. 2.2 Fair Lending Requirements Prohibition on Discrimination Block will not discriminate against an Applicant regarding any aspect of a credit transaction on a Prohibited Basis. This prohibition applies to all dealings between the Applicant and Block regarding an Application for credit or for an existing extension for credit throughout the credit lifecycle, including but not limited to: ● The marketing or solicitation of products offered by Block; ● Requests for product information; ● The Application and origination process; ● The standards for evaluating creditworthiness and the terms of credit offered by Block; ● The servicing and collection of existing extensions of credit; ● The furnishing of credit information to consumer reporting agencies. Block requires that policies, procedures, disclosures, customer agreements, communications, marketing strategies, and solicitations be carefully crafted and reviewed to ensure Block adheres to the non-discriminatory and principled standards under ECOA and Regulation B. Each employee must be mindful of providing consistent treatment to all Applicants regardless of any Prohibited Basis. Employees are responsible for interacting with all Applicants and consumers in an ethical, fair, and professional manner in accordance with this Policy. Prohibition on Discriminatory Discouragement Block's prohibition on discrimination extends to all aspects of a credit transaction, which includes the initial offering or marketing of Block's Credit Products and services. Block and its merchants will not discourage any Applicant or prospective Applicant from inquiring about, applying for, or otherwise pursuing a Block Credit Product or service on a Prohibited Basis. Among other things, this means that Block and its employees will not, on a Prohibited Basis: ● Make statements suggesting that a prospective Applicant not apply for Credit Products or services; ● Use words, symbols, or models that suggest a discriminatory preference or policy of exclusion; or ● Withhold assistance or information from prospective Applicants that would ordinarily be extended to other prospective Applicants. Types of Discrimination Under ECOA This Policy extends to the forms of discrimination under ECOA that have been recognized by courts and other federal regulators. These forms of discrimination include: 3 ● Overt Discrimination: Overt discrimination is present where a creditor openly discriminates against an Applicant on a Prohibited Basis or engages in blatant actions or policies based on the prejudice of a Prohibited Basis. ● Disparate Treatment: Disparate treatment is present where differences in treatment are not fully explained by legitimate non-discriminatory factors. It does not require any showing that the treatment was motivated by prejudice or a conscious intention to discriminate against a person beyond the difference in the treatment itself. ● Disparate Impact: Disparate impact is present when a creditor applies a facially neutral policy or practice to all Applicants, but the policy or practice disproportionately excludes or burdens certain Applicants on a Prohibited Basis. Evidence of discriminatory intent is not necessary to establish that disparate impact has occurred. 2.3 General ECOA Requirements Limitations on Information Requests Block will not collect the following information in connection with a credit transaction: ● Information regarding race, color, religion, national origin, or sex of the Applicant or any other person connected with the prospective credit transaction. The Applicant, however, can be asked to designate a title such as Ms., Miss, Mr., or Mrs. although Block must inform the Applicant that providing such designation is optional. ● Information concerning the spouse of an Applicant, unless: ○ The spouse will be permitted to use the account; ○ The spouse will be contractually liable on the account; ○ The Applicant is relying on the spouse's income as a basis for repayment of the credit requested; ○ The Applicant resides in a community property state or is relying on property located in such a state as a basis for repayment of the credit requested; or ○ The Applicant is relying on alimony, child support, or separate maintenance payments from a spouse or former spouse as a basis for repayment of the credit requested. ● Information concerning childbearing or child rearing, including birth control practices, intentions concerning the bearing or rearing of children, or capability to bear children. Block may inquire about the number and ages of an Applicant's dependents or about dependent-related financial obligations or expenditures, provided such information is requested without regard to sex, marital status, or any other Prohibited Basis. Income from Alimony, Child Support, or Separate Maintenance Payments Block may inquire about an Applicant's obligation to pay alimony, child support, or separate maintenance income or whether any part of an Applicant's stated income derives from those sources. However, Applicants are not required to disclose this information and may disclose this income information only if Applicants wish to rely on that particular source of income in the determination of creditworthiness. Block will notify Applicants that they are not required to disclose this information whenever source of income information is requested. 4 Rules for Evaluating Applications In general, Block may consider any information obtained, so long as the information is not used to discriminate against an Applicant on a Prohibited Basis. Block shall not take a Prohibited Basis into account when evaluating the creditworthiness of Applicants except a
documents.CP-009: Fair & Responsible Lending Policy Block, Inc. Last Approved: Oct 30, 2025 Effective Date: Oct 30, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Fair and Responsible Lending Policy (the "Policy") outlines Block, Inc.'s enterprise-wide approach to compliance with the Equal Credit Opportunity Act and its implementing Regulation B (“ECOAˮ and “Reg Bˮ), and the prohibition against Unfair, Deceptive, and Abusive Acts and Practices (“UDAAPˮ). The purpose of this Policy is to establish comprehensive guidelines governing Block, Inc., and its U.S. subsidiaries' compliance with fair lending laws, ensuring consistent operations while maintaining brand-specific flexibility, where applicable. It is important to note that this Policy does not include fair lending components that apply exclusively to Model Risk Management (“MRMˮ), which are governed separately. Block's Business Product Compliance Lead, Lending & Credit, and/or their designee, is responsible for the management of this Policy. Scope: This Policy applies to all consumer Credit Products offered by Block, Inc., its subsidiaries, and affiliates (collectively, "Block"), in the United States, including, both consumer-purpose and commercial-purpose credit. The only exception is Square Financial Services (“SFSˮ), Blockʼs Utah state-chartered Industrial Bank federally regulated by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation, which has its own Compliance Program. Definitions: Terminology Definition Credit Products Cash App and Afterpay branded consumer loan products for personal, family, or household purposes (including, but not limited to, Cash App Borrow, Cash App Afterpay Pay Over Time, Afterpay on Cash App Card, Cash App Afterpay Online Checkout, Afterpay Pay-in-4, and Afterpay Pay Monthly) and Squareʼs business-purpose credit products. 1 Fair and The Manual that sets forth all components of the Fair and Responsible Responsible Lending Program. Lending Program Manual Fair and The Block program that governs all aspects of fair lending, inclusive of Responsible ECOA/Regulation B, state equivalents, and the prohibitions against UDAAP. Lending Program Consumer The examination Modules that the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau FInancial uses to examine supervised entities on ECOA compliance. Protection Bureau ECOA Baseline Review Modules 25 (“Modulesˮ) Equal Credit The Equal Credit Opportunity Act, 15 U.S.C. §§ 16911691f, the law that Opportunity Act prohibits discrimination on the basis of race, color, religion, national origin, (“ECOA/ sex, marital status, age, receipt of public assistance, or good faith exercise of Regulation Bˮ) any rights under the Consumer Credit Protection Act, in addition to imposing disclosure requirements. ECOAʼs implementing Regulation B, 12 CFR Part 1002, elaborates on these and other principles and requirements for fair and non-discriminatory access to credit throughout the credit lifecycle. UDAAP Unfair, Deceptive and Abusive Acts and Practices. For purposes of this Policy, “Unfairˮ means an act or practice that: 1 causes or is likely to cause substantial injury to consumers; 2 is not reasonably avoidable by consumers; and 3 is not outweighed by countervailing benefits to consumers or to competition. Model Risk The umbrella term for treatment of all aspects of the model lifecycle in Management accordance with SR 117. MRM SR 117 Guidance on Model Risk Management from the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System April 2011 that identifies principles of a sound MRM program. 2 Prohibited Basis ● Race or color (also referred to ● Religion interchangeably ● National origin as “Protected ● Sex Classesˮ) ● Marital status ● Age, as long as the applicant is able to contract ● Receipt of income from a public assistance program ● Exercising rights under the Consumer Credit Protection Act in good faith ● Gender/gender identity/gender expression ● Sexual Orientation ● Veteran/servicemember status ● Disability ● Pregnancy ● HIV Status ● Ancestry Disparate Impact The application of facially neutral policies or practices that have an adverse effect or impact on a member of a Protected Class. Disparate A creditor treats a consumer differently based on a Prohibited Basis. Treatment Overt A creditor openly discriminates on a Prohibited Basis. Discrimination Model Any algorithm or calculus consisting of three components: an information input component, which delivers assumptions and data to the model; a processing component, which transforms inputs into estimates; and a reporting component, which translates the estimates into useful business information, including quantitative approaches whose inputs are partially or wholly qualitative or based on expert judgment, provided that the output is quantitative in nature. Models can be first party (developed internally) or third party (obtained from an external source). The same rules, with limited exceptions for self-testing as set forth in the Self-Testing of Models Standard, apply to first and third party models. 2. Block, Inc. Requirements 2.1 Anti-Discrimination Block does not discriminate against any applicant, consumer or customer on a Prohibited Basis regarding any aspect of a credit transaction. 3 ● Aspects of a credit transaction include, but are not limited to, application procedures, criteria used to evaluate creditworthiness, administration of accounts, and treatment of delinquent or slow accounts. ● Discrimination includes Overt Discrimination and Disparate Treatment. ● Block may accept some level of Disparate Impact risk in accordance with, and in adherence to, Section 2.10 of this Policy, the ECOA Self-Test Standard, and the Attributional Proxy Review Standard. 2.2 No Discouragement Block does not make any oral or written statements, in advertising or otherwise, to applicants or prospective applicants that would discourage on a Prohibited Basis a reasonable person from making or pursuing an application. 2.3 Collection of Information As a general matter, Block may request any information in connection with a credit transaction, subject to any Federal or state law regarding privacy, privileged information, credit reporting limitations, or similar restrictions on obtainable information. Block may not collect information related to Prohibited Bases, except under the following circumstances. ● Block may inquire about the race, color, religion, national origin, or sex of an applicant or any other person in connection with a credit transaction for the purpose of conducting a self-test that meets the requirements of Reg B. If Block makes such an inquiry, it must disclose orally or in writing, at the time the information is requested, that: ○ The applicant will not be required to provide the information; ○ Block is requesting the information to monitor its compliance with the Federal Equal Credit Opportunity Act; ○ Federal law prohibits Block from discriminating on the basis of this information, or on the basis of an applicant's decision not to furnish the information; and ○ If applicable, certain information will be collected based on visual observation or surname if not provided by the applicant or other person. ● Block may request that an applicant designate a title on an application form (such as Ms., Miss, Mr., or Mrs.) if the form discloses that the designation of a title is optional.
