Block may only use application forms that are otherwise neutral as to sex. ● Block may not request any information concerning the spouse or former spouse of an applicant, unless: ○ The spouse will be permitted to use the account; ○ The spouse will be contractually liable on the account; ○ The applicant is relying on the spouse's income as a basis for repayment of the credit requested; ○ The applicant resides in a community property state or is relying on property located in such a state as a basis for repayment of the credit requested; or ○ The applicant is relying on alimony, child support, or separate maintenance payments from a spouse or former spouse as a basis for repayment of the credit requested. 4 ● Block may request that an applicant list any account on which the applicant is contractually liable and to provide the name and address of the person in whose name the account is held. Block may also ask an applicant to list the names in which the applicant has previously received credit. ● If an applicant applies for individual unsecured credit, Block may not inquire about the applicant's marital status unless the applicant resides in a community property state or is relying on property located in such a state as a basis for repayment of the credit requested. If an application is for other than individual unsecured credit, Block may inquire about the applicant's marital status, but shall use only the terms married, unmarried, and separated. Block may explain that the category unmarried includes single, divorced, and widowed persons. ● Block may not inquire whether income stated in an application is derived from alimony, child support, or separate maintenance payments unless Block discloses to the applicant that such income need not be revealed if the applicant does not want Block to consider it in determining the applicant's creditworthiness. ● Block may not inquire about birth control practices, intentions concerning the bearing or rearing of children, or capability to bear children. Block may inquire about the number and ages of an applicant's dependents or about dependent-related financial obligations or expenditures, provided such information is requested without regard to sex, marital status, or any other Prohibited Basis. ● Block may inquire about the permanent residency and immigration status of an applicant or any other person in connection with a credit transaction. 2.4 Evaluation of Applications Generally, Block may consider any information obtained, so long as the information is not used to discriminate against an applicant on a Prohibited Basis. Block may not take a Prohibited Basis i
documents.CP-010: Fair Credit Reporting Act (“FCRAˮ) Policy Block, Inc. Last Approved: Oct 30, 2025 Effective Date: Oct 30, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Fair Credit Reporting Act Policy (the "Policy") outlines Block, Inc.'s enterprise-wide approach to consumer credit reporting, information handling practices, and identity theft prevention measures. The purpose of this Policy is to establish comprehensive guidelines governing Block, Inc. and its U.S. subsidiaries' compliance with the Fair Credit Reporting Act (“FCRAˮ) and its amendment, the Fair and Accurate Credit Transactions Act (“FACTAˮ), ensuring consistent operations while maintaining brand-specific flexibility, where applicable. Block's Business Product Compliance Lead, Lending & Credit, and/or their designee, is responsible for the management of this Policy. Scope: This Policy applies to all Credit Products offered by Block, Inc., its subsidiaries and affiliates (collectively, "Block"), The only exception is Square Financial Services (“SFSˮ), Blockʼs Utah state-chartered Industrial Bank federally regulated by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation, which has its own Compliance Program. Definitions: Terminology Definition Adverse Action A denial or revocation of credit, a change in existing credit terms, or a refusal to grant credit in substantially the amount or terms requested. This includes decisions made using information from consumer reports. Consumer Any individual who obtains or seeks to obtain financial products or services primarily for personal, family, or household purposes. Consumer Report Any written, oral, or other communication by a consumer reporting agency bearing on a consumer's creditworthiness, credit standing, credit capacity, character, general reputation, personal characteristics, or mode of living used for credit decisions. 1 Credit Products Cash App- and Afterpay-branded consumer loan products for personal, family, or household purposes (including, but not limited to, Cash App Borrow, Cash App Afterpay Pay Over Time, Afterpay on Cash App Card, Cash App Afterpay Online Checkout, Afterpay Pay-in-4, and Afterpay Pay Monthly) and Squareʼs business-purpose credit products. Credit Score A numerical value derived from a statistical tool or modeling system used to predict credit behavior. Identity Theft A fraud committed using another person's identifying information without authority. Medical Information Information or data related to a consumer's physical, mental, or behavioral health, healthcare provision, or healthcare payment. 2. Block, Inc. Requirements 2.1 Consumer Report Usage and Permissible Purpose Block obtains and uses consumer reports solely for legally permissible purposes as defined under the Fair Credit Reporting Act (“FCRAˮ). Blockʼs permissible purposes for obtaining a consumer report are 1 to use the information in connection with a credit transaction involving the consumer on whom the information is to be furnished and involving the extension of credit to, or review or collection of an account of, the consumer; or 2 to review an account to determine whether the consumer continues to meet the terms of the account. These permissible purposes primarily arise in credit transactions where Block has received explicit authorization from the consumer, either in writing or electronically. This includes situations where the report will support credit-related activities such as extending new credit, reviewing existing accounts, or supporting collection activities. Additionally, Block may obtain consumer reports when working with potential investors or servicers who need to assess credit risks, or in connection with prescreened credit solicitations where the consumer has provided authorization or the transaction constitutes a firm offer of credit and the consumer has not opted out. Block maintains appropriate controls to ensure consumer reports are obtained, used, and maintained in accordance with FCRA requirements. This includes verification of permissible purpose, documentation of authorization, and secure handling of consumer report information, where applicable. 2.2 Credit Decisions and Consumer Notifications Block follows a structured approach to using consumer reports in credit decisions that emphasizes fairness, consistency, and regulatory compliance. Our credit decision process includes: 2 ● Systematic Evaluation ○ Use of established creditworthiness criteria; ○ Documentation of decision factors; ○ Regular review of criteria; ○ Monitoring for potential disparate impact; and ○ Quality control reviews. ● Adverse Action Notifications ○ When taking adverse action based on consumer report information, Block provides written notification within 30 days including: ■ Contact information for the consumer reporting agency; ■ Statement that the agency did not make the decision; ■ Consumer rights regarding report access and disputes; ■ Credit score information when applicable; and ■ Key factors affecting the decision. ● Risk-Based Pricing Notice ○ Block provides appropriate notices explaining: ■ How credit information affected terms offered ■ Consumer rights regarding report access ■ Steps for verifying and correcting information 2.3 Information Furnishing and Accuracy Block maintains comprehensive requirements governing the furnishing of information to Consumer Reporting Agencies. While furnishing practices vary by subsidiary, Block ensures that any furnishing of information adheres to FCRA requirements and maintains appropriate controls regardless of current furnishing status. For subsidiaries that furnish information to Consumer Reporting Agencies, Block requires: ● Accuracy and integrity in furnished information ● Appropriate notice for negative information reporting ● Timely investigation and response to disputes ● Prevention of reporting identity theft-related information ● Proper documentation and record retention For subsidiaries that do not currently furnish information, Block maintains baseline requirements to ensure readiness should furnishing begin. All furnishing activities must comply with applicable regulatory requirements and Block's information accuracy standards. 2.4 Credit Prescreening Block conducts prescreened credit solicitations in compliance with FCRA requirements. When engaging in prescreening activities, Block ensures: ● Firm offers of credit are honored when selection criteria are met ● Required consumer notices are provided in appropriate format ● Opt-out rights are clearly communicated and honored 3 ● Prescreening criteria and selection methods are documented ● Consumer privacy and information security are maintained For subsidiaries that conduct prescreening, Block maintains appropriate controls to ensure compliance with FCRA requirements, including documentation, notice, and opt-out procedures. Subsidiaries that do not currently conduct prescreening must implement required controls before initiating such activities. 2.5 Identity Theft and Prevention and Response Block maintains a comprehensive Identity Theft Prevention Program as required by the Red Flags Rule under FACTA. This program forms an integral part of our commitment to protecting consumers and preventing fraud across our suite of financial products and services. Through this program, Block identifies, detects, and responds to patterns, practices, or specific activities that indicate possible identity theft. Block's approach to identifying and responding to identity theft indicators is guided by the Identity Theft Red Flags Guidelines in Appendix A, which provide a framework for developing specific procedures across brands. The program's oversight rests with the Board of Directors, an appropriate committee, or a designated senior management member which ensures adequate resources, proper implementation, and regular assessment of the program's effectiveness. Day-to-day administration is delegated to designated compliance personnel who develop procedures, coordinate training, and oversee service provider arrangements. Block's approach to identity theft prevention begins with regular risk assessments that consider the nature of our accounts, methods of access, and evolving identity theft risks. These assessments inform our identification of Red Flags across key categories including alerts from consumer reporting agencies, suspicious documents or personal information, unusual account activity, and notices from customers or law enforcement. Blockʼs detection methods are integrated into account opening procedures, existing account monitoring, and transaction processing across all platforms. These methods emphasize early detection while maintaining a seamless customer experience. When potential identity theft is detected, Block responds with measures appropriate to the level of risk, ranging from enhanced monitoring to account closure and law enforcement notification. The Identity Theft Prevention Program undergoes regular updates to address new threats, changes in business operations, and technological advances. These updates incorporate lessons learned from actual identity theft incidents, emerging industry threats, and regulatory guidance. Specific procedures implementing this program are maintained at the brand level to address unique product features and risk profiles. Block requires all service providers performing activities in connection with covered accounts to have reasonable policies and procedures to detect, prevent, and mitigate identity theft. Their 4 performance is regularly monitored to ensure alignment with Block's standards and regulatory requirements. 2.6 Medical Information Protection Block strictly limits the use of medical information in credit eligibility determinations as required by FCRA. We may only use medical information that is: ● Generic in nature and descriptive of the type of medical provider, nature of services provided, or medical status ● Used in a manner and to an extent no less favorable than similar non-
documents.CP-011: Fair Debt Collection Practices Act (“FDCPAˮ) Policy Block, Inc. Last Approved: Oct 30, 2025 Effective Date: Oct 30, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Fair Debt Collection Practices Act Policy (the "Policy") outlines Block, Inc.'s enterprise-wide approach to collections. The purpose of this Policy is to establish comprehensive guidelines governing Block, Inc., and its U.S. subsidiaries' utilization of the Fair Debt Collection Practices Act “FDCPAˮ) framework, ensuring consistent operations while maintaining brand-specific flexibility, where applicable. Block's Business Product Compliance Lead, Lending & Credit, and/or their designee, is responsible for the management of this Policy. This Policy establishes comprehensive guidelines governing Block, Inc., and its U.S. subsidiaries' approach to debt collection practices, ensuring consistent operations while maintaining brand-specific flexibility. Scope: This Policy applies to all Credit Products offered by Block, Inc., its subsidiaries, and affiliates (collectively, "Block"), in the United States, including, but not limited to installment loans, buy-now, pay-later products, and other forms of consumer lending. The only exception is Square Financial Services (“SFSˮ), Blockʼs Utah state-chartered Industrial Bank federally regulated by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation, which has its own Compliance Program. While Block and its subsidiaries do not meet the definition of a Debt Collector under the FDCPA, Block has elected to implement FDCPA guidelines as a best practice when collecting debts or engaging third-party debt collection agencies that may collect debts on behalf of Block. 1 Definitions: Terminology Definition Attempt to Any act to initiate a communication or other contact about a Debt with Communicate any person through any medium, including by soliciting a response from such person. An attempt to communicate includes leaving a limited-content message. Consumer Any natural person, whether living or deceased, obligated or allegedly obligated to pay any Debt. Creditor Any person who offers or extends credit creating a Debt or to whom a Debt is owed. The term Creditor does not include any person who receives an assignment or transfer of a Debt in default solely to facilitate collection of the Debt for another. Credit Products Cash App and Afterpay branded consumer loan products for personal, family, or household purposes (including, but not limited to, Cash App Borrow, Cash App Afterpay Pay Over Time, Afterpay on Cash App Card, Cash App Afterpay Online Checkout, Afterpay Pay-in-4, and Afterpay Pay Monthly) and Squareʼs business-purpose credit products. Debt Any obligation or alleged obligation of a Consumer to pay money arising out of a transaction in which the money, property, insurance, or services are primarily for personal, family, or household purposes, whether or not the obligation has been reduced to judgment. Debt Collector Any person who regularly collects or attempts to collect, directly or indirectly, consumer debts for another person or institution or uses a name other than its own when collecting its own consumer debts. 2. Block, Inc. Requirements 2.1 General Policy Requirements Block is committed to ensuring that collection activity across all Credit Products is conducted in a manner consistent with the FDCPA. The primary purposes of this Policy are to eliminate abusive, deceptive, and unfair debt collection practices, promote fair debt collection, provide consumers with an avenue for disputing and obtaining validation of debt information, and promote consistent state action to protect consumers against debt collection abuses. 2 Where state or local regulations overlap and are stricter than the requirements set out in this Policy, the more conservative approach will be applied. If any applicable requirements conflict with this Policy, Block will consult with appropriate legal counsel to resolve the conflict and adjust policies and procedures to ensure compliance. 2.2 Communication Standards Block maintains strict standards regarding the timing and frequency of consumer communications. Communications with consumers in connection with debt collection may not occur at unusual times, specifically before 800 a.m. or after 900 p.m. in the consumer's time zone, unless Block has knowledge of circumstances indicating that such times would not be inconvenient to the consumer. In alignment with regulatory requirements, Block has implemented specific frequency limitations for telephone communications. Telephone calls to a consumer about a particular debt are limited to no more than seven attempts within a seven-day period. Additionally, once a telephone conversation occurs regarding a debt, Block observes a seven-day waiting period before placing another call, unless the consumer explicitly requests additional contact. When initiating communication about a debt, Block ensures proper disclosure of its identity and purpose. The initial written communication, and if applicable, the initial oral communication, includes a clear statement that Block is attempting to collect a debt and that any information obtained will be used for that purpose. Subsequent communications maintain transparency by consistently identifying the communication as coming from a debt collector. For voicemail communications, Block utilizes the concept of "limited-content messages." These messages include only essential information: a business name that does not indicate debt collection activity, contact information for consumer response, and a request for reply. This approach protects consumer privacy while maintaining effective communication channels. 2.3 Consumer Rights and Protections Block respects and upholds consumers' right to control communication regarding their debt. Upon receiving written notification from a consumer refusing to pay a debt or requesting cessation of communication, Block discontinues further contact, with limited exceptions. These exceptions include advising the consumer that collection efforts are being terminated, notifying the consumer of specific remedies that may be invoked, or informing the consumer of intended remedy implementation. In cases where a consumer has secured legal representation regarding a debt, Block directs all communications to the attorney of record, provided the attorney's contact information is available or can be readily obtained. This protocol remains in effect unless the attorney fails to respond within a reasonable timeframe or explicitly permits direct consumer communication. Block maintains strict protocols regarding workplace contact. Communication with consumers at their place of employment is prohibited when Block knows or has reason to know that the 3 employer prohibits such communication. This protection ensures that debt collection activities do not interfere with consumers' employment relationships. 2.4 Third-Party Communications and Privacy Protection of consumer privacy is important in Block's collection practices. Communication with third parties regarding a consumer's debt is strictly limited. Without prior consumer consent given directly to Block, communication about a debt is restricted to the consumer, their attorney, consumer reporting agencies (if permitted by law), the creditor, and their respective legal representatives. 2.5 Ethical Collection Practices Block prohibits any conduct that could reasonably be considered harassing, oppressive, or abusive in connection with debt collection. This includes refraining from threats of violence, use of obscene language, publication of consumer lists, or coercive advertising of debts. Additionally, Block ensures all communications are truthful and transparent, avoiding any false, deceptive, or misleading representations regarding debt collection. In the handling of consumer debts, Block employs fair and ethical practices. This includes accurate representation of debt amounts, clear communication of legal status, and appropriate handling of disputed debts. Block does not collect amounts unless expressly authorized by the agreement creating the debt or permitted by law, and ensures all collection actions are legally enforceable and intended. 3. Compliance & Enforcement 3.1 Roles and Responsibilities: Block, Inc. Roles: ● Compliance Policy Committee (“CPCˮ): ○ Provides oversight and governance of applicable Compliance Policies and is responsible for reviewing and approving this Policy annually to ensure alignment with regulatory requirements and expectations ○ Providing guidance on significant policy matters ○ Ensuring consistency across Blockʼs Compliance Policy Framework ● Block Inc, Chief Compliance Officer (“CCOˮ) ○ Provides assistance and support in implementing this Policy across the organization ○ Establishes appropriate practices designed to ensure Company operations reflect Block's commitment to compliance ○ Ensures all Employees are aware of their compliance responsibilities ○ Monitors and reports on compliance with this Policy 4 ○ May delegate responsibilities to relevant Senior Management or Compliance Employees who inform and escalate key risks and issues to the CCO ● Head of Consumer Protection ○ Serves as the primary delegate for the CCO in matters relating to this Policy ○ Oversees the development and maintenance of compliance procedures ○ Coordinates policy reviews and updates ○ Manages the policy exception process ● Legal Counsel ○ Provides approval and interpretative advice regarding the applicability and application of policy requirements ○ Ensures legal compliance across all jurisdictions ○ Provides guidance on complex compliance matters ● Employees ○ Understand and comply with all applicable requirements of this Policy ○ Complete required compliance training in a timely manner ○ Report actual or suspected violations of laws or other requirements ○ Follow proper procedures for email communications ○ Maintain awareness of updates and cha
documents.CP-012: Global Complaint Policy Block, Inc. Last Approved: Feb 19, 2026 Effective Date: Feb 19, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: The Global Complaint Policy ("Policy") establishes a standardized complaint management framework across Block. This framework is applicable to all Block, Inc. (“Blockˮ) Businesses that develop and manage financial products and services across all complaint channels and jurisdictions in which Block operates and oversees, excluding Square Financial Services (“SFSˮ), which is governed by a separate complaint management framework due to its chartered banking obligations. The Global Complaint Oversight Lead is responsible for management of this Policy. Block is committed to identifying and resolving Complaints in an effective manner. This Policy has been established to provide guiding principles to be utilized in the development and management of Business-level complaint programs. A full-cycle complaint management program creates a uniform framework for the intake, handling, and resolution of all Complaints. The Global Complaint Oversight function, within Compliance,establishes standard practices by which all Businesses subject to this Policy must comply. Further, Block maintains this Policy to support compliance with legal, regulatory, and contractual requirements governing the handling of customer Complaints for in-scope Block businesses. Scope: This Policy is established by Block and is applicable globally across all Block Business lines, subsidiaries, and jurisdictions, excluding Square Financial Services, Inc. and its subsidiaries, which maintain a separate complaint management framework pursuant to applicable banking regulatory requirements. This includes all directors, officers, and team members of these Block legal entities and any subsidiaries thereof (which for these purposes includes temporary or contract staff and consultants performing work on behalf of these entities). ● Afterpay Australia Pty Ltd ● Afterpay Canada Limited 1 ● Afterpay NZ Limited ● Afterpay US Services, LLC ● ASPIRO AB ● Cash App Investing LLC ● Cash App Taxes, Inc. ● Clearpay Finance Limited ● Square AU Pty Ltd ● Square Canada Inc. ● Square Capital, LLC ● Square K.K. ● Squareup Europe Limited ● Squareup International Limited ● TIDAL Music LLC ● Tidal Serviços de Entretenimento Ltda The entities listed above are collectively referred to as “In-Scope Businessesˮ for purposes of this Policy. It is every team member of the In-Scope Business's responsibility to adhere to this Policy. Definitions: Terminology Definition Complaint An expression of dissatisfaction or grievance (oral or written) by an individual, Business entity, or someone authorized to act on their behalf, with a Block product, process, service, decision, vendor, or interaction regardless of validity. Complaints are more than just the 'words' but also are identified by the tone of and intention behind the customer outreach, as well as sentiments of a breach in the Business' responsibilities Complaints may also include allegations that Block engaged in wrongful or unfair conduct. Complaints do not include Disputes of unauthorized or erroneous electronic funds transfers, which are handled through a distinct process governed by separate policies and procedures. Controls The mechanisms, processes, and procedures designed to minimize risk and provide reasonable assurance regarding the effectiveness and efficiency of operations and compliance with applicable laws and regulations. Dispute A Customer's claim that an electronic funds transfer from their account was unauthorized, incorrect, or not executed as intended. A Dispute is outside the scope of the definition of Complaint. Inquiry A communication (oral or written) that includes general questions, service requests or technical issues that do not have sentiments of a Complaint. 2 Terminology Definition Staff Member All employees and fixed-term and contingent workers who support the operations of Block and/or subsidiaries covered by this Policy, and include interns, temporary workers, consultants, vendors, third-party providers, and contractors. 2. Policy Requirements 2.1 Terms and Definitions The Global Complaint and Inquiry definition must be adopted by all In-Scope Businesses subject to this Policy. Businesses with additional legal, regulatory or contractual requirements not captured within the global definitions may adopt a Business-level complaint definition inclusive of additional requirements to which they are subject. 2.2 Severity Tier Rating A tiered severity model is used to assess and prioritize complaints based on the inherent risk posed by the issue and, in some cases, the source of the Complaint. This model supports consistent Complaint handling, enables efficient resource allocation, and ensures that higher-risk matters receive appropriate attention and escalation. A severity tiering framework must be adopted by all In-Scope Businesses subject to this Policy. If a Business is subject to additional legal, regulatory, or contractual obligations that go beyond what is outlined in this Policy, it must adopt a severity model that meets those heightened requirements while remaining aligned with the core expectations set forth by Block. Detailed tier definitions and criteria are provided in the applicable Complaint Standards and may consider factors such as regulatory impact, reputational harm, systemic issues, or potential consumer harm. 2.3 Complaint Intake Channels Block is committed to providing customers of In-Scope Businesses with multiple accessible avenues for submitting Complaints. Complaints may be received through various intake channels, including but not limited to direct customer communications, company-operated interfaces, regulatory agencies, and authorized third-party service providers acting on behalf of the customer. Intake channels are defined and operationalized in greater detail within the applicable Complaint Standards for each brand or product line. Complaints received by an In-Scope Business are subject to standardized identification, handling, categorization, and resolution, as outlined in this Policy and associated Complaint Standards. 3 2.4 Regulatory Authorities, Rules, and Regulations For In-Scope Businesses operating in jurisdictions with region-or country-specific complaint obligations, such Businesses must comply with applicable local regulatory complaint requirements in addition to the requirements set forth in this Policy and associated Complaint Standards. In-Scope Businesses with contractual obligations established by bank partners must also comply with such obligations in addition to this Policy and associated Complaint Standards. Bank partner requirements are documented in contractual agreements and supporting governance documentation. Block Legal and Compliance functions provide guidance on the integration of bank partner complaint-related requirements into the Business-level complaint governance frameworks and processes. 2.5 Complaint Trend Analysis and Root Cause Analysis Complaint trend analysis provides insight into the types of Complaints raised, the customer journey and experience, and whether information obtained through Complaint handling substantiates customer allegations or identifies emerging patterns of concern. In-Scope Businesses must conduct Complaint trend analysis, where appropriate and commensurate with Complaint severity, volume, and risk. In-Scope Businesses must establish feedback loops to relevant stakeholders, including Product, Operations, Legal, Risk, and Compliance, to assess whether observed trends or volume variations may reasonably be attributed to products, technology, policies, procedures, or customer communications, and whether changes could mitigate the circumstances leading to such Complaints. Trend analysis outputs must be presented through established governance forums to enable further evaluation, prioritization, and treatment decisions. These forums will assess potential root causes, determine whether additional investigation is required, and define appropriate treatment actions, which may include process enhancements, product changes, risk acceptance, or escalation to Issue Management. Root cause analysis may require cross-functional investigation and intervention beyond initial trend analysis. Where an underlying issue is identified that causes material harm to additional customers, Businesses must take appropriate remedial action regardless of whether those customers submitted a Complaint. This includes escalation to Issue Management or incident response processes, where appropriate, to support identification of affected customers, documentation of systemic issues, and remediation tracking. Global Complaint Oversight, as part of the Compliance function, establishes governance expectations for Complaint trend reporting and systemic issue identification. Each month, Global Complaint Oversight reviews RCA outputs and Complaint trends to determine whether issues meet criteria for systemic issue designation, risk acceptance, or other governance actions, and monitors the remediation lifecycle in coordination with Issue Management. 4 3. Compliance & Enforcement 3.1 Roles and Responsibilities Block, Inc. Roles: ● Businesses (“1LODˮ): ○ In-Scope Business teams are subject to this Policy based on the products or services they develop, manage, and oversee. Businesses are responsible for implementing, maintaining, and complying with Policy requirements, including establishing and operating complaint handling processes, controls, and procedures. ○ As the first line of defense, the Business operations team, agent team leaders, complaint handling units, and quality assurance are responsible for designing, implementing, and maintaining internal controls necessary to comply with Global Complaint Policy requirements and applicable contractual, regional, or regulatory obligations. ● Global Complaint Oversight (“2LODˮ): ○ The enter
documents.CP-013: Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act Privacy Policy Block, Inc. Last Approved: Oct 30, 2025 Effective Date: Oct 30, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act Privacy Policy (the "Policy") outlines Block, Inc.'s enterprise-wide approach to protecting consumer privacy in accordance with the Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act ("GLBA") and its implementing regulations. This Policy establishes comprehensive guidelines governing Block, Inc. and its U.S. subsidiaries' collection, use, and sharing of consumer information while ensuring the security and confidentiality of customer data. Scope: This Policy applies to Block, Inc. and its subsidiaries (collectively, "Block") in the United States, governing all activities involving the collection, use, and sharing of nonpublic personal information from consumers and customers. It establishes requirements for protecting customer privacy across all products and services subject to GLBA requirements. The only exception is Square Financial Services (“SFSˮ), Blockʼs Utah state-chartered Industrial Bank federally regulated by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation, which has its own Compliance Program. Definitions: Terminology Definition Affiliate Any company that controls, is controlled by, or is under common control with another company. Clear and A notice is reasonably understandable and designed to call attention to the Conspicuous nature and significance of the information in the notice. Consumer An individual who obtains or has obtained a financial product or service that is to be used primarily for personal, family, or household purposes. Customer A consumer who has a continuing relationship with Block under which Block provides one or more financial products or services. 1 Customer Any record containing Nonpublic Personal Information about a customer, Information whether in paper, electronic, or other form, that is handled or maintained by or on behalf of Block. Nonpublic Personally identifiable financial information that is not publicly available, Personal including: 1 information provided by the consumer to obtain a financial Information product or service; 2 information about a consumer resulting from any (“NPIˮ) transaction involving a financial product or service; or 3 information otherwise obtained about a consumer in connection with providing a financial product or service. Non-affiliated Any person or entity that is not an Affiliate of Block and is not a joint Third Party employee of Block. Personally Information that can be used to distinguish or trace an individual's identity, Identifiable either alone or when combined with other personal or identifying information Information that is linked or linkable to a specific individual. (“PIIˮ) 2. Block, Inc. Requirements 2.1 Policy Statement The Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act has four key requirements for electronic signatures to be recognized as valid under U.S. law: 1 the consumer's intent to sign and be bound to the transaction electronically, 2 the consumer's receipt of required disclosures and affirmative and ongoing consent to conduct business electronically in a manner that reasonably demonstrates the consumer can access information in the electronic form used by the parties, 3 association of the signature with the signer's identity, and 4 record retention. By conforming with these requirements Block is permitted to provide consumers with information and certain notices electronically. 2.2 Consumer Notification Prior to obtaining the consumer's consent to electronic records and disclosures, Block must clearly and conspicuously inform the consumer of several key elements. This includes an explanation of whether the consent applies only to a particular transaction that triggered the disclosure or to specific categories of records that may be provided electronically or made available as part of the consumer relationship, such as disclosures, consents, policies, change-in-terms, and adverse action notices. The notification must address any right or option to receive disclosures in paper form, including the process for requesting a paper copy and whether any fee will be charged. Additionally, the notification must include a statement of the right to withdraw consent, the process to withdraw such consent, and the consequences of doing so. The consumer must be informed of the 2 procedures to update their electronic contact information and the minimum hardware/software requirements needed to access and retain electronic records. 2.3 Hardware and Software Requirements Prior to transmitting records electronically, Block must provide consumers with a disclosure statement of the hardware and software requirements needed for access to and retention of electronic records. The disclosure must ensure that consumers have the technical means to receive records and disclosures electronically. 2.4 Changes to Hardware or Software Requirements When changes occur to hardware or software requirements needed to access or retain electronic records, and these changes create a material risk that the consumer may not be able to access or retain subsequent electronic records, Block must notify consumers of such changes. Additionally, consent must be reaffirmed when there is a change to the hardware and/or software requirements. The notice must include the revised hardware and/or software requirements for access to and retention of electronic records, and a statement that the consumer may withdraw consent without the imposition of any fees or new conditions. 2.5 Consumer Consent Process Consumer consent must be obtained in a manner that demonstrates the ability to access information in the electronic form that will be used. The consumer must affirmatively consent to receive electronic disclosures, and this consent must reasonably demonstrate their ability to access information in the form that will be used to provide the disclosures. 2.6 Withdrawal of Consumer Consumers may withdraw their consent to receive electronic records and communications at any time. Withdrawal can be made using methods established in the disclosures or other supported methods. Upon withdrawal of consent, appropriate alternative methods of communication must be established. The withdrawal of consent to receive electronic records and communications may affect service access or features. No fee may be imposed to process the withdrawal of consent to receive electronic records and communications. Any withdrawal of consent becomes effective after a reasonable period for processing the request, and confirmation of the withdrawal and its effective date must be provided in writing. 2.7 Invalid or Inoperable Contact Information When electronic contact information becomes invalid or inoperable, appropriate procedures must be implemented to handle such situations. This may include treating the provision of invalid contact information or subsequent malfunction of previously valid contact information as a withdrawal of consent for electronic communications to that specific contact method. 3 4.8 State Law Considerations While ESign provides the federal framework for electronic signatures and records, we recognize that state laws may impose additional or more stringent requirements. In jurisdictions where state regulations overlap with ESign requirements, we apply the more conservative approach to ensure comprehensive compliance. This includes adherence to state-specific versions of the Uniform Electronic Transactions Act UETA and any state-specific consumer protection provisions. When conflicts arise between applicable laws and these requirements, we consult with Legal Counsel to resolve such conflicts and adjust our practices accordingly. This ensures we maintain compliance while providing consistent service across our operating jurisdictions. 3. Brand-Specific Requirements Block will comply with GLBA requirements, which may be detailed further in related Standards or Procedures. This section outlines only the distinct implementations and unique requirements specific to each subsidiary's lending products. 3.1 Cash App: Cash App's implementation of GLBA requirements reflects its mobile-first approach to financial services. During the consumer onboarding process, Cash App requires electronic consent to various disclosures, including the Cash App ESign Consent, except for circumstances that allow disclosures to be provided without GLBA compliance. The consumer's electronic consent at onboarding must demonstrate the ability to access information provided by Cash App electronically. A consumer cannot proceed in the application process without consenting to receive information and documents electronically. Reading the electronic consent form on the Cash App website or application and acknowledging the ability to receive and download documents electronically constitutes sufficient confirmation of consent. For withdrawal of consent, Cash App may treat a consumer's provision of an invalid e-mail address or phone number, or the subsequent malfunction of a previously valid e-mail address or phone number, as a withdrawal of consent to receive electronic records and communications via the inoperative contact method. 3.3 Afterpay: Afterpay maintains specific requirements for buy-now-pay-later transactions that address the unique aspects of installment payment relationships. Prior to obtaining consumer consent to electronic disclosures, Afterpay provides clear and conspicuous information about whether the consent applies to a particular transaction or to specific categories of records available as part of the customer relationship. 4 For merchant integration, Afterpay requires specific technical standards for capturing consumer consent during purchase. This includes implementation requirements for checkout flow integration and enhanced record-keeping requirements for installment payment agreements. When hardware and/or software requirements to receive electronic disclosures cha
documents.CP-014: Military Lending Act (“MLAˮ) Policy Block, Inc. Last Approved: Oct 30, 2025 Effective Date: Oct 30, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Military Lending Act Policy (the "Policy") outlines Block's enterprise-wide approach to compliance with the Military Lending Act ("MLA"). The purpose of this Policy is to establish comprehensive guidelines governing Block, Inc., and its U.S. subsidiaries' compliance with the MLA, ensuring consistent operations while maintaining brand-specific flexibility, where applicable. Block's Business Product Compliance Lead, Lending & Credit, and/or their designee, is responsible for the management of this Policy. Scope: This Policy applies to all Consumer Credit products offered by Block, Inc., its subsidiaries, and affiliates (collectively, "Block") in the United States, including, but not limited to installment loans, buy-now, pay-later products, and other forms of consumer lending. The only exception is Square Financial Services (“SFSˮ), Blockʼs Utah state-chartered Industrial Bank federally regulated by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation, which has its own Compliance Program. Definitions: Terminology Definition Consumer Credit Credit offered or extended to a Covered Borrower primarily for personal, family, or household purposes. Creditor A creditor under the MLA is a person who is: ● Engaged in the business of extending consumer credit; or ● An assignee of a person engaged in the business of extending consumer credit with respect to any consumer credit extended. Covered A consumer who, at the time the consumer becomes obligated on a Borrower Consumer Credit transaction or establishes an account for Consumer Credit, is a Covered Member of the armed forces or a Dependent of a Covered Member. Covered Members of the Army, Navy, Marine Corps, Air Force, or Coast Guard Member currently serving on active duty under a call or order that does not specify a period of 30 days or fewer, or such a member serving on Active Guard and Reserve duty. Dependent With respect to a Covered Member, a Dependent means: ● The Covered Member's spouse ● Children under the age of 21 ● Children under the age of 23 enrolled in an accredited institution of higher learning who are dependent on the Covered Member for over half of their support ● Children of any age that are incapable of self-support. Other relationships may qualify as Dependents, and employees should consult compliance. Military Annual The cost of the Consumer Credit expressed as an annual rate, calculated in Percentage Rate accordance with 32 CFR 232.4(c). The MAPR for covered transactions must (“MAPRˮ) not exceed 36 percent. Active Service Also known as "active duty" or "active status." A person who is on active duty in the military full-time for a period of greater than 30 days. They work for the military full-time, may live on a military base, and can be deployed at any time. Such term includes full-time training duty, annual training duty, and attendance, while in active military service, at a school designated as a service school by law or by the Secretary of Defense. 2. Block, Inc. Requirements 2.1 Policy Statement The standards set out in this Policy represent minimum requirements based on applicable legal and regulatory guidance and apply throughout Block's lending operations. These requirements are intended to prevent Block and its employees from violating applicable provisions of the MLA. Block recognizes that MLA violations can present significant legal, reputational, and compliance risk. 2.2 MLA Policy Requirements Military Annual Percentage Rate All transactions covered by the MLA are subject to the Military Annual Percentage Rate ("MAPR"). The MAPR is analogous to the Annual Percentage Rate ("APR") as defined in other consumer protection regulations in that both are calculated in the same manner. However, the MAPR calculation must include certain fees and charges regardless of whether other regulations require those fees or charges to be included in the APR calculation. Specifically, the MAPR calculation must include credit insurance premium or fees, debt cancellation or debt suspension fees, fees for ancillary products sold in connection with the credit transaction, finance charges, and application fees. In accordance with the MAPR cap provided by the MLA, Block will not impose an MAPR greater than 36 percent on transactions with Covered Borrowers. 2.3 MLA Notice and Disclosure Requirements Block will provide the appropriate notice and disclosures to Covered Borrowers, including the following considerations: ● Disclosures will be made clearly and conspicuously in writing and in a form the Covered Borrower may keep. Disclosures will contain a statement of the MAPR applicable to the extension of Consumer Credit as well as the total dollar amount of all charges included in the MAPR. ● If oral disclosures are given, the MAPR, the payment obligation will be provided orally before origination. A toll-free number will be provided with or on the written disclosures that consumers may use to obtain oral disclosure, and oral disclosures are provided when the Covered Borrower contacts Block for that purpose. Where applicable, during the renewal or refinancing of covered transactions, new disclosures will be provided if the transaction is considered a new transaction that would require disclosure under other regulations. 2.4 Prohibited Conduct In accordance with MLA requirements, Block will not condition the extension of Consumer Credit to a Covered Borrower by requiring the Covered Borrower to waive their right to legal recourse under the Servicemembers Civil Relief Act or any other state or federal law. Block will not require the use of a check or other method of access to a deposit, savings, or other financial account maintained by the Covered Borrower, except that, in connection with a Consumer Credit transaction with an MAPR consistent with the regulation, Block may, unless otherwise prohibited by law: require electronic fund transfer for repayment; require a direct deposit of the Covered borrower's salary, as a condition of eligibility; take a security interest in funds deposited after extending credit in an account established in connection with the transaction; or require the Covered Borrower to establish an allotment to repay the obligation. Furthermore, Block will not prohibit the Covered borrower from prepaying Consumer Credit and will not charge the Covered borrower a prepayment penalty fee for prepaying all or part of Consumer Credit. Block will not renew, roll over, repay, refinance, or consolidate any transaction with a Covered Borrower unless the transaction results in more favorable terms to the Covered Borrower. Block will not require a Covered Borrower to submit to arbitration. Block may be subject to certain penalties if MLA is violated; including in certain instances rendering the credit agreement void. 2.5 Identifying Covered Borrowers It is Block's policy to verify the Covered Borrowers' Active Service status via either the U.S. Department of Defense's Defense Manpower Data Center or information contained in a consumer report obtained from a nationwide consumer reporting agency. This verification must occur prior to origination of any Consumer Credit product covered under this Policy. 3. Compliance & Enforcement 3.1 Roles and Responsibilities: Block, Inc. Roles: ● Compliance Policy Committee (“CPCˮ): ○ Provides oversight and governance of applicable Compliance Policies and is responsible for reviewing and approving this policy annually to ensure alignment with regulatory requirements and expectations ○ Providing guidance on significant policy matters ○ Ensuring consistency across Blockʼs Compliance Policy Framework ● Block Inc, Chief Compliance Officer (“CCOˮ) ○ Provides assistance and support in implementing this Policy across the organization ○ Establishes appropriate practices designed to ensure Company operations reflect Block's commitment to compliance ○ Ensures all employees are aware of their compliance responsibilities ○ Monitors and reports on compliance with this Policy ○ May delegate responsibilities to relevant Senior Management or Compliance employees who inform and escalate key risks and issues to the CCO ● Head of Consumer Protection ○ Serves as the primary delegate for the CCO in matters relating to this Policy ○ Oversees the development and maintenance of compliance procedures ○ Coordinates policy reviews and updates ○ Manages the policy exception process ● Legal Counsel ○ Provides approval and interpretative advice regarding the applicability and application of policy requirements ○ Ensures legal compliance across all jurisdictions ○ Provides guidance on complex compliance matters ● Employees ○ Understand and comply with all applicable requirements of this Policy ○ Complete required compliance training in a timely manner ○ Report actual or suspected violations of laws or other requirements ○ Follow proper procedures for email communications ○ Maintain awareness of updates and changes to requirements ● Business Product Compliance Lead, Lending & Credit ○ Implement policy requirements within their respective business units ○ Monitor day-to-day compliance activities ○ Manage training requirements for their business unit ○ Report issues or concerns to the CCO ○ Ensure brand-specific requirements are met while maintaining overall compliance 3.2 Monitoring and Reporting On an ongoing basis, the Policy Owner and/or their designee monitors activities for compliance and objective achievement. As outlined in the Compliance Management System Policy, Compliance will provide regular updates, as applicable, to the Block, Inc. Board and executive management. Reporting may cover specific information related to: ○ Policies and standards ○ Procedures ○ Audit/exam findings ○ Issues management ○ Training ○ Complaints 3.3 Training All relevant employees will receive training at least annually covering appl
documents.CP-015: Servicemember Civil Relief Act (“SCRAˮ) Policy Block, Inc. Last Approved: Oct 30, 2025 Effective Date: Oct 30, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Servicemembers Civil Relief Act Policy (the "Policy") outlines Block's enterprise-wide approach to compliance with the Servicemember Civil Relief Act (“SCRAˮ). The purpose of this Policy is to establish comprehensive guidelines governing Block, Inc., and its U.S. subsidiaries' compliance with the SCRA, ensuring consistent operations while maintaining brand-specific flexibility, where applicable. SCRA governs how financial institutions treat loans when a borrower is called to Active Duty Military Service, and the benefits extended to the Dependents of the impacted Servicemember. Block's Business Product Compliance Lead, Lending & Credit, and/or their designee, is responsible for the management of this Policy. Scope: This Policy applies to all consumer Credit Products offered by Block, Inc., its subsidiaries, and affiliates (collectively, "Block"), in the United States, including, but not limited to installment loans, buy-now, pay-later products, and other forms of consumer lending. The only exception is Square Financial Services (“SFSˮ), Blockʼs Utah state-chartered Industrial Bank federally regulated by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation, which has its own Compliance Program. Definitions: Terminology Definition Active Duty Full-time duty in the active Military Service of the United States, including sustained duty in the Space Force. Such term includes full-time training duty, annual training duty, and attendance, while in the active Military Service, at a school designated as a service school by law or by the secretary of the military department concerned. Active Duty does not include full-time National Guard duty Coverage Period One or more periods of time, no less than 30 consecutive days in length, during which SCRA benefits and protections apply. For regular service members, this period begins on the date of entering military service and ends on the date of discharge or death (plus applicable grace periods). For reservists, the period begins on the date of receipt of orders to report for 1 military service and ends on the date of release from military service or death. Coverage Period may be extended at any time in accordance with SCRA requirements. Credit Products Cash App- and Afterpay-branded consumer loan products for personal, family, or household purposes (including, but not limited to, Cash App Borrow, Cash App Afterpay Pay Over Time, Afterpay on Cash App Card, Cash App Afterpay Online Checkout, Afterpay Pay-in-4, and Afterpay Pay Monthly) and Squareʼs business-purpose credit products. Department of Public database used to verify Servicemember Active Duty status. Defense Manpower Data Center Website ("DMDC" Website) Dependent An individual who may request SCRA relief, including: ● Servicemember's spouse; ● Servicemember's child; and ● An individual receiving more than half their support from a Servicemember for 180 days before SCRA application. Military Service Includes: ● Active Duty members of Army, Navy, Air Force, Marine Corps, Space Force, or Coast Guard; ● Federal Active Duty Reservists; ● National Guard on active service for 30 consecutive days for national emergency; ● Commissioned Officers of the Public Health Service or National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration on active service; ● Absences due to sickness, wounds, leave, or other lawful cause; and ● U.S. citizens serving with allied forces. Period of Military The period beginning the date on which a servicemember enters military Service service, and ending the date on which the servicemember is released from military service or dies while in military service. Servicemember Full-time members of uniformed services, including: ● Army, Navy, Air Force, Marines, Coast Guard, Space Force; ● Public Health Services Officers; ● NOAA commissioned corps; ● Active Duty Reserves; and ● National Guard on > 30-day active service. 2 2. Block, Inc. Requirements 2.1 SCRA Policy Requirements Block is committed to providing comprehensive SCRA benefits and protections to eligible Servicemembers and their Dependants. The SCRA's benefits and protections typically begin on the date that a Servicemember is called to Active Duty Military Service. For reservists and members of the National Guard who are ordered to report to Active Duty, these protections begin on the date they receive their orders calling them to Active Duty Military Service. The fundamental purpose of these protections is to enable Servicemembers to focus on their military duties without the burden of financial concerns. Block recognizes this purpose and implements comprehensive protections that often exceed the minimum requirements of the Act. 2.2 Interest Rate and Fee Benefits When a Servicemember enters Active Duty, Block implements significant interest rate and fee protections. For obligations incurred prior to entering Military Service, Block reduces the interest rate to 6% per annum, inclusive of all fees and charges. Any interest in excess of 6% is permanently forgiven. The interest rate reduction applies from the date the Servicemember enters Active Duty. To qualify for the interest rate reduction, a Servicemember must demonstrate either current Active Duty status or submit their request within 180 days of termination of Active Duty. While Servicemembers may provide military orders or a Department of Defense short form to verify their status, Block takes a proactive approach to verification. Upon receiving notice of potential military service, Block independently verifies eligibility through the Department of Defense Manpower Data Center (“DMDCˮ) Website. If eligibility is confirmed through DMDC, Block automatically applies the benefits without requiring additional documentation from the Servicemember. During the benefit period, Block suspends all fees of any kind on the account. Furthermore, Block will not sell the debt to any third party or initiate legal action against the Servicemember during Active Duty or for 90 days thereafter. These protections ensure comprehensive financial relief during the Servicemember's Period of Military Service. 2.3 Identification and Verification Process Block maintains a thorough yet flexible process for identifying and verifying eligible Servicemembers. While the initial responsibility lies with the Servicemember or their family to notify Block of their military status, Block's verification process is designed to minimize the burden on serving military personnel. Upon receiving notification of military service, Block initiates a verification process that includes checking the DMDC database and reviewing any provided documentation. This documentation may include military orders, a certified letter from a commanding officer, or other official military documentation. Block maintains copies of all supporting documentation in the customer's file for 3 the duration of the period of Military Service and in accordance with record retention requirements. The verification process must be completed within 30 calendar days of the initial notification. During this period, Block takes reasonable follow-up steps if needed documentation is not received, including additional DMDC searches and customer outreach via phone, email, or letter. If verification cannot be completed within the 30-day timeframe, Block accepts the Servicemember's claim as valid pending further documentation. For Military Reservists and those ordered to report for induction, Block recognizes that Military Service begins on the date of receipt of orders, rather than the reporting date. This early recognition ensures that protections are in place before active service begins. 2.4 Default Judgment Protections Block maintains stringent procedures regarding Default Judgments involving potential Servicemembers. Before pursuing any Default Judgment, Block conducts a mandatory search of the DMDC Website to determine the defendant's military status. This search must be documented and included with any court filing. When filing for a Default Judgment, Block must submit an affidavit that either: ● States whether the defendant is in Military Service and provides supporting facts, or ● Indicates that Block is unable to determine the defendant's military status If a defendant is found to be in Military Service, Block complies with all court-ordered protections and stays. During any stay period, Block suspends all penalties and enforcement actions. These protections apply to debts incurred both before and during Active Duty, providing comprehensive protection for Servicemembers involved in legal proceedings. 2.5 Ongoing Account Management Block implements comprehensive account management procedures for SCRA-protected accounts. Each protected account receives special handling status to ensure all benefits and protections are properly applied and maintained. This includes regular reviews of Active Duty status, careful monitoring of interest rates and fees, and special notation to prevent prohibited collection activities. For accounts with SCRA protections, Block maintains detailed records of: ● The date protection began ● The basis for SCRA eligibility ● All rate and fee adjustments ● Communications regarding SCRA benefits ● Scheduled protection end dates ● Post-service protection periods 4 Block conducts periodic reviews of protected accounts to verify continued eligibility and ensure accurate benefit application. When Active Duty status can no longer be verified, Block provides advance notice before removing protections, ensuring a smooth transition from protected status. 2.6 Protection of Rights and Prohibitions Block strictly prohibits practices that could infringe upon or discourage the exercise of SCRA rights. Servicemembers' exercise of SCRA rights will never adversely affect their ability to obtain future financial services from Block. S
documents.CP-016: Telephone Consumer Protection Act (“TCPAˮ) Policy Block, Inc. Last Approved: Oct 30, 2025 Effective Date: Oct 30, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Telephone Consumer Protection Act ("TCPA") Policy ("Policy") outlines Block's obligations for adhering to the TCPA as it pertains to Block's operations. The TCPA restricts and limits how Block ("Company") or a person or entity acting on behalf of Block can contact telephone subscribers via facsimile, telephone, including artificial and prerecorded voice, or message. In general, calls, messages, or facsimiles subject to the TCPA require the recipient's consent. The type and nature of consent required depends on whether the call or text message is placed for Telemarketing purposes or for "informational" purposes. Block has chosen to adhere to certain provisions of the TCPA related to its communication practices across its operations to ensure consistent Called Party protection even though Block asserts that the TCPA may not apply to Blockʼs practices as a matter of law. Block conducts business in compliance with all applicable laws and regulations. The purpose of this Policy is to demonstrate the commitment of the Company to comply with the applicable requirements of the TCPA. It has been created to ensure that Block and relevant personnel are aware of and comply with any applicable obligations under the TCPA. Scope: This Policy is established for Block, Inc. and is applicable to the Block, Inc. business and related products and services. The only exception is Square Financial Services (“SFSˮ), Blockʼs Utah state-chartered Industrial Bank federally regulated by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation, which has its own Compliance Program. This Policy outlines the requirements for adhering to TCPA as they pertain to Block, Inc. and relevant personnel. The TCPA restricts and limits how entities can contact Called Parties via facsimile, telephone, including landline and wireless numbers, and text message. In general, calls, messages, or facsimiles subject to the TCPA require the recipient’s consent. 1 Definitions: Terminology Definition Advertisement Any material advertising the commercial availability or quality of any property, good, or service. Automatic Telephone Dialing System ("ATDS") Equipment that has the capacity to store or produce telephone numbers to be called, using a random or sequential number generator, and to dial such numbers. Called Party The current subscriber or customary user of the telephone number being contacted via telephone, message, or facsimile. Established Business Relationship For purposes of Telephone Solicitations, the term means a prior or existing relationship formed by a voluntary two-way communication between a Block brand (e.g. Square, Cash App, Afterpay, SFS, etc.) or a person or entity acting on behalf of that Block brand and a Called Party, with or without an exchange of consideration, on the basis of the Called Party's purchase or transaction with the Block brand within the 18 months immediately preceding the date of the telephone call, or on the basis of the Called Party's inquiry or application regarding products or services offered by the Block brand within the three months immediately preceding the date of the call, which relationship has not been previously terminated by either party. A Called Party's presence on Block's Internal Do-Not-Call list terminates the Established Business Relationship for the purpose of telephone solicitations. For purposes of facsimile Advertisements, the term means a prior or existing relationship formed by a voluntary two-way communication between a Block 2 Terminology Definition brand or a person or entity acting on behalf of a Block brand and a Called Party, with or without an exchange of consideration, on the basis of an inquiry, application, purchase, or transaction by the Called Party regarding products offered by Block, which relationship has not been previously terminated by either party. Prior Express Written Consent An agreement, in writing, bearing the signature of the person called, that clearly authorizes a Block brand to deliver or cause to be delivered to the person called, Advertisements or Telemarketing messages using an ATDS and/or an artificial or prerecorded voice, and the telephone number to which the signatory authorizes such Advertisements or Telemarketing messages to be delivered. The written agreement must include a clear and conspicuous disclosure informing the person signing that: By executing the agreement, such person authorizes the Block Brand to deliver or cause to be delivered to the signatory, telemarketing calls using an automatic telephone dialing system or an artificial or prerecorded voice; and the person is not required to sign the agreement (directly or indirectly), or agree to enter into such an agreement as a condition of purchasing any property, goods, or services. The "signature" required for prior express written consent includes an electronic or digital form of signature, to the extent that such form of signature is recognized as a valid signature under applicable federal law or state contract law. Seller The person or entity on whose behalf a telephone call or message is initiated for the purpose of encouraging the purchase or rental of, or investment in, property, goods, or services, which is transmitted to any person. 3 Terminology Definition Telemarketer The person or entity that initiates a telephone call or message for the purpose of encouraging the purchase or rental of, or investment in, property, goods, or services, which is transmitted to any person. Telemarketing The initiation of a telephone call or message for the purpose of encouraging the purchase or rental of, or investment in, property, goods, or services, which is transmitted to any person. Telephone Facsimile Machine Equipment which has the capacity to transcribe text or images, or both, from paper into an electronic signal and to transmit that signal over a regular telephone line, or to transcribe text or images (or both) from an electronic signal received over a regular telephone line onto paper. Telephone Solicitation The initiation of a telephone call or message for the purpose of encouraging the purchase or rental of, or investment in, property, goods, or services, which is transmitted to any person. The term does not include a call or message: To any person with that person's prior express permission; To any person with whom the caller has an Established Business Relationship; or By a tax-exempt nonprofit organization. Unsolicited Advertisement Any material advertising the commercial availability or quality of any property, goods, or services which are transmitted to any person without that person's prior express invitation or permission, in writing or otherwise. 2. Block, Inc. Requirements 2.1 General Requirements This Policy outlines the requirements that govern when Block must obtain prior express consent or prior express written consent before contacting Called Parties. The Policy also describes the 4 requirements for Block to evaluate whether a call or SMS can be made to a number that is registered on a do-not-call ("DNC") list, and outlines the restrictions that apply to Advertisements sent via facsimile. 2.2 Outbound Calls to Telephone Numbers The use of an ATDS and/or artificial/prerecorded calls requires prior express consent or Prior Express Written Consent depending on the purpose of the communication and the technology used to initiate it. Therefore, Block must obtain either prior express consent or prior express written consent as necessary before calling the Called Party for communications subject to the TCPA. Exemptions to this requirement may exist and, if applicable, will be identified in subsequent procedures. 2.3 Outbound Text Messages Text messages, including internet-to-phone text messages, require the same type of consent as that required for an equivalent call to a wireless phone number - Prior Express Written Consent for Telemarketing texts and prior express consent for informational text messages. Therefore, Block must obtain either Prior Express Written Consent or prior express consent as necessary before sending such text messages subject to the TCPA. Exemptions to this requirement exist and if applicable will be called out in subsequent procedures. 2.4 Revocation of Consent Called Parties can revoke consent using any "reasonable" method, which includes oral or written revocation. Block will not limit a Called Party's ability to revoke consent by requiring Called Parties to use an exclusive means of revocation. Block will maintain a process to monitor for and honor all Called Party revocations. 2.5 Timing of Telephone Solicitations Block will not initiate any Telephone Solicitation to any residential telephone subscriber (landline) or wireless telephone numbers before the hour of 8 a.m. or after 9 p.m. (local time at the Called Party's location). If applicable local law provides a narrower time window for Telephone Solicitations, Block will adhere to that time window. 2.6 National Do Not Call List Block will comply with the TCPA's restrictions that apply to a Called Party that has registered his or her telephone number on the national DNC registry. The TCPA prohibits Block from initiating Telephone Solicitations to a residential telephone subscriber as well as any wireless telephone subscriber who has registered his or her telephone number on the national DNC registry. This restriction applies to all Telephone Solicitations. Block will honor national DNC registrations indefinitely, or until the registration is canceled by the Called Party or the telephone number is removed from the database. 5 Telephone Solicitation does not include calls or text messages made with the recipient's prior express permission or to any person with whom the caller has an Established Business Relationship with the Company, unless that person is on Block's
documents.CP-017: Truth-in-Lending (“TILAˮ) Policy Block, Inc. Last Approved: Nov 25, 2025 Effective Date: Nov 25, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Truth in Lending Act Policy (the "Policy") outlines Block, Inc.'s approach to ensuring compliance with the Truth in Lending Act ("TILA") and its implementing Regulation Z. The purpose of this Policy is to establish comprehensive guidelines governing Block, Inc., and its U.S. subsidiaries' compliance with the TILA. Block's Business Product Compliance Lead, Lending & Credit, and/or their designee, is responsible for the management of this Policy. Scope: This Policy applies to Block, Inc. and its subsidiaries (collectively, "Block") that engage in closed-end lending activities subject to TILA. The only exception is Square Financial Services (“SFSˮ), Blockʼs Utah state-chartered Industrial Bank federally regulated by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation, which has its own Compliance Program. Definitions: Terminology Definition Annual A measure of the cost of credit expressed as a yearly rate that relates the Percentage Rate amount and timing of value received by the consumer to the amount and (“APRˮ) timing of payments made. Billing Cycle The interval between the days or dates of regular periodic statements. These intervals must be equal and no longer than a quarter of a year. An interval is considered equal if the number of days in the cycle does not vary more than four days from the regular day or date of the periodic statement. Billing Error Written notice received from a borrower that indicates a belief that a billing Notice error exists that is received within 60 calendar days of the first periodic statement that reflects the alleged billing error and includes information sufficient to identify the borrower's name and/or account number. 1 Business Day A day on which creditor's offices are open to the public for carrying on substantially all business functions. Closed-end Consumer credit other than open-end credit. Credit Consummation The time that a Consumer becomes contractually obligated on a credit transaction. Consumer A cardholder or natural person to whom Consumer credit is offered or extended. Open-end Credit Consumer credit extended under a plan in which: ● The creditor reasonably contemplates repeated transactions; ● The creditor may impose a finance charge from time to time on an outstanding unpaid balance; and ● The amount of credit that may be extended during the term of the plan is generally made available to the extent that any outstanding balance is repaid. Periodic Rate A rate of finance charge that is or may be imposed by a creditor on a balance for a day, week, month, or other subdivision of a year. Finance Charge The cost of Consumer credit is represented as a dollar amount, including any charge payable directly or indirectly by the Consumer and imposed directly or indirectly by Block as a condition of the credit. 2. Block, Inc. Requirements 2.1 Policy Statement Block offers closed-end credit products subject to TILA's requirements for credit cards and traditional closed-end installment loans. The requirements set forth below govern both current offerings and potential future products. Block complies with the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau's Interpretive Rule Docket No. CFPB20240017, "Use of Digital User Accounts to Access Buy Now, Pay Later Loans." General Disclosure Requirements Block will make all disclosures required pursuant to Regulation Z in writing, in a form that the Consumer may keep. The disclosures will be provided to the Consumer in a reasonably understandable form and may be provided electronically, subject to compliance with the Consumerʼs consent and other applicable provisions of the Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act (“ESign Actˮ). 2 Content of Disclosures The disclosures will reflect the credit terms to which the parties will be legally bound as of the outset of the credit transaction. Block will disclose the following information as applicable: ● Block's identity ● The amount financed, using that term, and a brief description such as "the amount of credit provided to you or on your behalf" ● A separate written itemization of the amount financed (either proactively or upon request) ● The Finance Charge, using that term, and a brief description such as "the dollar amount the credit will cost you" Block will disclose all required credit terms accurately and clearly, including the amount financed, FInance Charge, APR, payment schedule, and other material terms required by Regulation Z. Block will provide all required TILA disclosures for credit transactions, including but not limited to the Annual Percentage Rate, payment terms, fees, and other material credit terms as required by Regulation Z. All disclosures will use prescribed terminology and follow applicable regulatory guidance and model forms. Treatment of Credit Balances When a credit balance in excess of $1 is created in connection with a transaction, Block will: ● Credit the amount of the credit balance to the Consumer's account ● Refund any part of the remaining credit balance upon written request of the Consumer ● Make a good faith effort to refund to the Consumer by cash, check, money order, or credit to a deposit account any part of the credit balance remaining in the account for more than 6 months Determination of APR Block will calculate the APR in accordance with either the actuarial method or the United States Rule method, following the explanations, equations, and instructions set forth in Appendix J of Regulation Z. The disclosed APR will be considered accurate if it is not more than 1/8 of one percent above or below the APR calculated under Regulation Z. Advertising Requirements Block requires all promotional and advertising materials to meet applicable regulatory requirements. In an oral response to a Consumer's inquiry about the cost of credit, Block will only state the APR. However, Block may also state a simple annual rate or Periodic Rate if it is applied to an unpaid balance. If the APR cannot be determined in advance, Block will state the APR for a sample transaction and may give other cost information for the Consumer's specific transaction. 3 All promotional and advertising materials that state specific terms will state only those terms that actually are or will be arranged or offered by Block. All required disclosures will be made clearly and conspicuously. Any advertisements that state a rate of Finance Charge will state that rate as an APR, using that term. If the APR may be increased after Consummation, the advertisement will state that fact. The advertisement will not state any other rate, except that a simple annual rate or Periodic Rate that is applied to an unpaid balance may be stated in conjunction with the APR.
