Block requires proper disclosures in marketing and advertising materials when the following trigger terms are used: ● The amount or percentage of any down payment ● The number of payments or period of repayment ● The amount of any payment ● The amount of any Finance Charge If any of these triggering terms are included in promotional materials, Block requires the insertion of the following additional disclosures: ● The amount or percentage of the down payment ● The terms of repayment, which reflect the repayment obligations over the full term of the loan, including any balloon payment ● The APR, using that term, and if the rate may be increased after Consummation, that fact For multiple-page or electronic advertisements that provide information in a table or schedule, the table or schedule of terms must include all appropriate disclosures for a representative scale of amounts up to the level of the more commonly sold higher-priced products or services offered. Television or radio advertisements stating any trigger terms must clearly state either each additional disclosure or the information required regarding APR and list a telephone number and reference for Consumers to obtain additional cost information. 3. Partner Bank-Specific Requirements Block will comply with partner bank-specific requirements, which may be detailed further in related Standards or Procedures. This section outlines only the distinct implementations and unique requirements set forth by Blockʼs bank partner(s) for a subset of its products. 3.1 Card Issuance Requirements For certain products, Block may limit card issuance to requested or renewed cards, cap liability for unauthorized use, and allow consumers to assert claims/defenses against the card issuer when disputes with merchants are unresolved, as well as other obligations that may be applicable. 4 4. Compliance & Enforcement 4.1 Roles and Responsibilities: Block, Inc. Roles: ● Compliance Policy Committee (“CPCˮ): ○ Provides oversight and governance of applicable Compliance Policies and is responsible for reviewing and approving this policy annually to ensure alignment with regulatory requirements and expectations ○ Providing guidance on significant policy matters ○ Ensuring consistency across Blockʼs Compliance Policy Framework ● Block Inc, Chief Compliance Officer (“CCOˮ) ○ Provides assistance and support in implementing this Policy across the organization ○ Establishes appropriate practices designed to ensure Company operations reflect Block's commitment to compliance ○ Ensures all Employees are aware of their compliance responsibilities ○ Monitors and reports on compliance with this Policy ○ May delegate responsibilities to relevant Senior Management or Compliance Employees who inform and escalate key risks and issues to the CCO ● Head of Consumer Protection ○ Serves as the primary delegate for the CCO in matters relating to this Policy ○ Oversees the development and maintenance of compliance procedures ○ Coordinates policy reviews and updates ○ Manages the policy exception process ● Legal Counsel ○ Provides approval and interpretative advice regarding the applicabilit
documents.CP-018: Block, Inc. UDAAP/UDAP Policy Last Approved: Oct 30, 2025 Effective Date: Oct 30, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Policy communicates Block, Inc.'s commitment to compliance with both consumer and commercial fairness requirements through: ● The Unfair, Deceptive, or Abusive Acts or Practices (“UDAAPˮ) Standards contained in sections 1031 and 1036 of the Dodd-Frank Act ● The Unfair or Deceptive Acts or Practices (“UDAPˮ) provisions in Section 5 of the Federal Trade Commission Act ● State laws prohibiting similar acts or practices in both consumer and commercial contexts This Policy sets forth comprehensive guidelines governing Block, Inc. and its U.S. subsidiaries, ensuring consistent operations while maintaining appropriate flexibility to address unique business needs. Block's prevention of UDAAP and UDAP violations requires transparency, truthfulness, suitability, and fairness across all transactions and dealings with both consumers and merchants. The distinction between UDAAP and UDAP requirements reflects the different contexts in which Block operates: ● Consumer financial products and services (subject to UDAAP under Dodd-Frank) ● Commercial and merchant services (subject to UDAP under the FTC Act) ● Products with potential dual consumer and commercial applications This comprehensive approach ensures appropriate protections while recognizing the different needs and sophistication levels of consumers and commercial customers. Scope: This Policy applies to Block, Inc. and its U.S. subsidiaries and affiliates (collectively, "Block") and covers: ● Consumer Financial Products and Services UDAAP Block's consumer-focused subsidiaries Cash App and Afterpay) must comply with Unfair, Deceptive, or Abusive Acts or Practices requirements under sections 1031 and 1036 of the Dodd-Frank Act. 1 The only exception is Square Financial Services (“SFSˮ), Blockʼs Utah state-chartered Industrial Bank federally regulated by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation, which has its own Compliance Program. Block maintains appropriate controls for preventing unfair or deceptive practices across all business lines, while applying additional consumer protection requirements where applicable. This Policy establishes requirements for all customer interactions, whether consumer or commercial, while recognizing the different regulatory frameworks that apply to each business context. Definitions: Terminology Definition Commercial A transaction primarily for business, commercial, or agricultural purposes, Transaction including merchant services and business lending products. UDAAP Unfair, Deceptive, or Abusive Acts or Practices as prohibited by sections 1031 and 1036 of the Dodd-Frank Act, which apply to consumer financial products and services. UDAP Unfair or Deceptive Acts or Practices as prohibited by Section 5 of the Federal Trade Commission Act, which apply to both consumer and commercial transactions. Unfair An act or practice that: 1 causes or is likely to cause substantial injury to consumers or merchants; 2 cannot be reasonably avoided; and 3 is not outweighed by countervailing benefits to consumers/merchants or to competition. Deceptive A representation, omission, act or practice that: 1 misleads or is likely to mislead the consumer or merchant; 2 the consumer's or merchant's interpretation is reasonable under the circumstances; and 3 the misleading representation, omission, act or practice is material. Abusive An act or practice that: 1 materially interferes with the ability of a consumer to understand a term or condition of a consumer financial product or service; or 2 takes unreasonable advantage of: (a) a consumer's lack of understanding, (b) inability to protect their interests, or (c) reasonable reliance on a covered person. Applies only to consumer transactions] Consumer An individual who is a natural person who obtains or has obtained a financial product or service that is used primarily for personal, family or household purposes. Commercial A business entity or individual obtaining products or services primarily for Consumer business, commercial, or agricultural purposes. 2 Material Information that is likely to affect a consumer's or merchant's choice of, or Information conduct regarding, a product or service. Staff Member(s) An employee or external worker (e.g., contingent labor, contractors) hired by the Company who supports the operations covered by this Policy. Substantial Injury Financial or other harm that is more than trivial and may include monetary loss, unwarranted health and safety risks, and other forms of economic or social harm. Third-Party Any external entity that performs activities on behalf of Block, including Service Provider vendors, contractors, and service providers who may interact with consumers or merchants. 1.1 Policy Statement Block is committed to offering both consumer and commercial products in a fair and responsible manner. For consumer financial products Cash App and Afterpay), Block complies with UDAAP requirements under the Dodd-Frank Act. For commercial products , Block complies with UDAP requirements under the FTC Act. Block ensures that terms and conditions are communicated clearly and transparently to both consumers and commercial customers. Wherever state or local regulations overlap with, and are stricter than the requirements set out in this Policy, the more conservative or restrictive approach will be applied in those jurisdictions provided such approach is lawful. If any applicable laws conflict with this Policy, Block will consult with appropriate Legal Counsel to resolve the conflict and adjust Blockʼs policies and procedures to ensure compliance. 2. Block-Wide Requirements 2.1 General Standards 2.1.1 UDAAP Standards Block's commitment to preventing unfair, deceptive, or abusive acts or practices is demonstrated through comprehensive standards that apply across all entities and operations. These standards establish the foundation for fair treatment of consumers and merchants throughout their relationship with Block and its subsidiaries. Unfair Acts or Practices: An act or practice is unfair where it causes or is likely to cause substantial injury to Consumers which cannot be reasonably avoided and the injury is not outweighed by countervailing benefits to Consumers or to competition. An act or practice that causes a small amount of harm to a large number of people may be deemed to cause substantial 3 injury. However, an act or practice is not considered to be unfair if Consumers may reasonably avoid injury. Consumers cannot reasonably avoid injury if the act or practice interferes with their ability to make decisions or take action to avoid injury. Public policy, as established by statute, regulation, or judicial decisions may be considered, with other evidence to determine whether an act or practice is unfair and the particular facts in a case are crucial to the determination of unfairness. Block complies with the guidance that discrimination may be considered an unfair act or practice. As such, Block will treat all applicants and Consumers equally, and will not discriminate during the stages of the product lifecycle, including marketing, account opening, servicing, or collections. Deceptive Acts or Practices: An act or practice is deceptive where there is representation omission, or practice that misleads or is likely to mislead Consumers; the Consumer's interpretation of the representation, omission, act or practice is reasonable under the circumstances; and the misleading representation, omission, act or practice is material. A deceptive representation may be an express or implied claim or promise and it may be written or oral. The omission of material information may be deceptive if disclosure of the omitted information is necessary to prevent a Consumer being misled. When representations or marketing practices are targeted to a specific audience, such as the elderly, young people, or financially unsophisticated, the standard is based upon the effects of the act or practice on a reasonable member of that group. Abusive Acts or Practices: An act or practice is abusive if it materially interferes with the ability of a Consumer to understand a term or condition of a product or service or takes unreasonable advantage of: (i) the lack of understanding on the part of the Consumer of the material risks, costs, or conditions in the product or service; (ii) the inability of the Consumer to protect their interests in selecting or using the financial product or service; or (iii) the reasonable reliance by the Consumer on a covered person to act in the interests of the Consumer. 2.1.2 Consumer and Commercial Harm Any unfair, deceptive, or abusive act or practice can result in harm to consumers or merchants. Block seeks to prevent harm through clear, transparent, and accurate delivery of products and services in accordance with applicable federal and state laws and regulations. For consumers, harm may include: ● Actual or potential financial loss ● Non-financial impacts ● Violations of consumer protection laws ● Impacts from compliance management weaknesses 4 For commercial customers, harm may include: ● Business losses or operational impacts ● Disruption to merchant services ● Financial impacts on business operations ● Reputational harm 2.3 Product Design and Delivery 2.3.1 Advertising, Marketing, and Disclosure Guidelines Block maintains comprehensive standards for marketing, advertising, and sales materials across both consumer and commercial products. All communications must present information fairly, clearly, and accurately, with appropriate disclosures of terms, benefits, costs, risks, and material limitations of products or services being offered. This includes any related or optional products and services that may be available to customers. In developing marketing materials, Block prohibits any intentional misrepresentation of terms, bene
documents.CP-019: Lending Identity Theft Prevention/Red Flags Standard Cash App, US Consumer Lending Last Approved: October 30, 2025 Effective Date: October 30, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Lending Identity Theft Prevention/Red Flags Standard (the “Standardˮ) describes the principles, practices, and guidelines for the management and oversight of identity theft compliance risk for Cash Appʼs US Consumer Lending products (“Consumer Lendingˮ). This Standard is designed to ensure that elements of an effective Identity Theft Prevention Program (“Programˮ) are met, and that the Program is appropriate for the size and complexity of Cash App and the nature and scope of the Consumer Lending activities. Scope: This Standard applies to all consumer-purpose Credit Products offered by Cash App. Definitions: Terminology Definition Account A continuing relationship established by a person with a financial institution or creditor to obtain a product or service for personal, family, household or business purposes. Board of In the case of a branch or agency of a foreign bank, the managing official in Directors charge of the branch or agency; and in the case of any other creditor that does not have a board of directors, a designated employee at the level of senior management. Covered An account that a financial institution or creditor offers or maintains, primarily Account for personal, family, or household purposes, that involves or is designed to permit multiple payments or transactions, such as a credit card account, mortgage loan, automobile loan, general consumer loan, margin account, cell 1 phone account, utility account, checking account, or savings account; and any other account that the financial institution or creditor offers or maintains for which there is a reasonably foreseeable risk to customers or to the safety and soundness of the financial institution or creditor from identity theft, including financial, operational, compliance, reputation, or litigation risks. Credit Products Cash App-branded consumer loan products for personal, family, or household purposes (including, but not limited to, Cash App Borrow, Cash App Afterpay Pay Over Time, Afterpay on Cash Card, Afterpay Pay-in-4, Afterpay Pay Monthly). Customer A person that has a covered account with a financial institution or creditor. Identity Theft Fraud committed or attempted fraud using one or more pieces of identifying information of another person without authority. Identifying information means any name or number that may be used, alone or in conjunction with any other information, to identify a specific person, including, but not limited to, any name, social security number, or date of birth. Red Flag A pattern, practice, or specific activity that indicates the possible existence of identity theft. Service Provider The entity or business that provides a service directly to the financial institution or creditor. 2. Cash App Requirements 2.1. Identity Theft Prevention Program Administration In order to mitigate Identity Theft, Cash App will establish a Program that 1 receives initial approval from either the Board of Directors, or an appropriate governing committee; 2 involves a designated employee in the oversight, development, implementation and administration of the Program; 3 trains employees, as necessary, to effectively implement the Program; and 4 ensures appropriate and effective oversight of Service Providers. Cash App must also periodically conduct a risk assessment to determine whether it offers or maintains Covered Accounts that would be subject to the Programʼs oversight. An adequate Program ensures that relevant Identity Theft Red Flags are detected, appropriately responded to, and periodic reviews are conducted such that changing risks and/or market conditions are appropriately accounted for. 2 2.2. Identification of Red Flags Where applicable, Cash App has opted to include the five categories noted below that the Federal Trade Commission (“FTCˮ) has identified as part of its efforts to identify relevant Identity Theft Red Flags and mitigate Identity Theft of its Consumer Lending Customers: ● Alerts, notifications, or warnings from a Credit Reporting Agency (“CRAˮ), which includes fraud or credit alerts, credit freezes, address discrepancies, and/or pattern of activity inconsistent with the history and usual Account activity of the Customer ● Suspicious documents, such as altered or forged documents or identification; ● Suspicious personal identifying information, such as a social security number provided in an application that matches the social security number of other applicants or Customers; ● Suspicious Account activity; or ● Notices from Customers, victims of Identity Theft, law enforcement authorities, or other persons regarding possible Identity Theft in connection with Accounts held by Cash App. 2.3. Detection of Red Flags For the Consumer Lending products, Cash App will address the detection of Red Flags in connection with the opening of new Accounts and existing Accounts by: ● Obtaining identifying information about, and verifying the identity of, a person opening an Account; and, ● Authenticating Customers, monitoring transactions, and verifying the validity of change of address requests, in the case of existing Accounts. 2.4. Mitigation of Identity Theft Cash App is to provide responses to Red Flags detected that are commensurate with the degree of risk posed for the Consumer Lending products. In determining an appropriate response, Cash App is to consider factors that may heighten the risk of Identity Theft, such as a data security incident, or notice that a Customer has provided verified fraudulent information. Cash App may incorporate, as appropriate, its existing policies, procedures, and other arrangements that reasonably control foreseeable risks to Customers or to the safety and soundness of Cash App from Identity Theft. Appropriate responses may include: ● Monitoring an Account for evidence of Identity Theft; ● Contacting the Customer; 3 ● Not allowing a new Account to be opened; ● Changing any passwords, security codes, or other security devices that permit access to a Covered Account; ● Closing an existing Account; ● Filing a Questionable or Suspicious Activity Report in accordance with applicable law and regulation; or, ● Determining that no response is warranted under the particular circumstance. 2.5. Alerts, Notifications, or Warnings from a Consumer Reporting Agency For instances where Cash App uses Consumer Reports to establish its Covered Accounts, it will consider applicable alerts, notifications, and warnings that may indicate Identity Theft, such as a fraud alerts or active duty alerts. 2.6. Potentially Suspicious Activity & Documentation Cash App considers indicators of suspicious documents provided by potential lending Customers. Indicators of suspicious documentation may include, but are not limited to: ● Potentially altered or forged documents; ● Inconsistent photographs or physical descriptions; ● Inconsistent information related to financial institutions or creditors; ● Suspicious personal identifying information; or, ● Other information that is inconsistent with previously provided information. Cash App also considers indicators of suspicious activity related to lending Customers. Suspicious activity may include: ● A new Covered Account being used in a manner commonly associated with known patterns of fraud; or, ● A Covered Account being used in a manner that is not consistent with established patterns of activity on the Account. For example: ○ Nonpayment when there is no history of late or missed payments; ○ A material increase in the use of available credit; or ○ A material change in purchasing or spending patterns. ● A Covered Account that has been inactive for a reasonably lengthy period of time being used; or, 4 ● Cash App is notified of unauthorized activity, or transactions in connection with a Customer's Covered Account. Cash App intends to maintain compliance with all applicable Red Flag guidelines for its Lending products, including the alerts identified above, by adopting and maintaining procedures to ensure compliance. Cash App will: ● Provide a copy of the application and any transaction records available, free of charge, upon receipt of a written claim of Identity Theft that resulted in fraudulent transactions, provided Cash App can confirm the victimʼs allegation(s) are true after the Identity Theft victim: ○ Provides proof of positive identification in the form of a government-issued identification card; and ○ Provides proof of the Identity Theft claim in the form of an Identity Theft affidavit and a copy of a police report; and ● Not sell, transfer, or place in collection a debt that has resulted from Identity Theft. ○ In instances where Cash App engages a third-party debt collector in order to perform collection activities on Consumers who are victims of Identity Theft, in its own name, on behalf of Cash App, Cash App must ensure that the third-party debt collector notifies Consumers that the information may be fraudulent or the result of Identity Theft. 2.7. Account Takeover (“ATOˮ) Account takeover occurs when an unauthorized person has gained access to the Customerʼs Account, allowing the unauthorized person to make unauthorized transactions from their own device. This means that any activity initiated by the unauthorized personʼs device will be reflected on the Customer's Account and device. An example of unauthorized activity is applying for a loan from someone else's account with no intention of repaying it. When a Customer alleges ATO, Cash App will take the necessary steps to 1 correctly identify the ATO victim; 2 identify any unauthorized transactions; and 3 take the necessary precautions to ensure that the fraudulent activity does not continue. 5 3. Compliance & Enforcement 3.1. Roles and Responsibilities: Block, Inc. Roles: ● Compliance Policy Committee (“
documents.CP-020: Customer Conduct & Staff Protection Policy Block, Inc. Last Approved: Mar 25, 2026 Effective Date: Mar 25, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Customer Conduct & Staff Protection Policy (the "Policy") outlines Block, Inc.'s approach to persons whose behavior has been assessed as being abusive. The Consumer Protection Compliance Lead is responsible for management of this Policy. The purpose of this Policy is to establish comprehensive guidelines governing Block's approach to managing unacceptable customer behavior and ensuring consistent operations while maintaining brand-specific flexibility. Scope: This Policy is established by Block, Inc., its subsidiaries, and affiliates (collectively, "Block") and is applicable to all entities within Block. Definitions: Terminology Definition Abusive Behavior Abusive behavior refers to any behavior that is harmful, threatening, or offensive to another person or group. Aggressive Behavior Confrontational, forceful, or intimidating language that exceeds respectful communication boundaries. Examples include berating staff, hostile language, or refusing constructive dialogue. Discrimination Treating someone unfairly based on their race, gender, age, religion, sexual orientation, or other characteristic. Disrespectful Behavior Any behavior that lacks respect, such as mocking, belittling, or ignoring someone's boundaries. Harassment Unwanted or intimidating behavior that creates a confrontational environment. Examples include persistent belittling or demeaning of staff. 1 Immediate Threat Behavior posing direct risk to health, safety, or wellbeing of staff that requires urgent intervention. Persistent Contact Customers who engage in nuisance behavior, by exhibiting persistent contact attempts that are vexatious in nature. Physical Violence Physical aggression or force used against another person, such as hitting, pushing, or using weapons to cause harm. Reasonably Forceful An individual who asserts themselves firmly without resorting to aggressive behavior. This is considered acceptable behavior. Sexual Nature Content, language, or behavior related to sexuality or intimate relationships that is inappropriate for professional interaction. Staff Member All employees and fixed-term and contingent workers who support the operations and/or subsidiaries covered by this Program, and include interns, temporary workers, consultants, vendors, third-party providers, and contractors. Stalking Pattern of repeated unwanted attention, harassment, contact, or behavior that causes staff to feel threatened or unsafe. Threat Expressions of intent to cause harm or negative consequences, such as threatening to harm someone physically or damage their property. Unacceptable Behavior Conduct that deviates from established norms of civility, respect, and cooperation. Unacceptable Public Actions or behaviors that disrupt operations or threaten staff safety in Expression public spaces, including unauthorized recording or photographing of staff. Verbal Harassment Any kind of verbal abuse, such as name-calling, insulting, or making threats. 2. Policy Requirements 2.1. Policy Principles Block upholds the principle that everyone has the right to express their dissatisfaction, provide comments and offer suggestions to contribute to the enhancement of its services. Block also acknowledges that during challenging or distressing situations, people may display behavior inconsistent with their usual conduct when engaging with Block. It is important to note that Block does not categorize assertive or determined approaches as unacceptable or abusive behavior. 2 2.2. Unacceptable and Abusive Behavior Block Staff Members are entitled to a workplace free of abusive, offensive or threatening behavior even when a customer is under stress. Some examples of unacceptable behavior might include: ● Abusive or offensive language directed at Staff Members ● Remarks of a sexual nature directed at Staff Members ● Racist language directed at Staff Members ● Homophobic or other discriminatory remarks directed at Staff Members ● Shouting at Staff Members ● Offensive gestures ● Verbal or physical threats against Staff Members, prominent individuals, Block property or office buildings ● Bullying or intimidating behavior ● Stalking or any other form of harassment 2.3. Unacceptable Persistent Contact Block recognizes that some of its customers cannot or will not accept if Block is unable to assist them further or provide a level of service other than that already provided. Customers may persist in disagreeing with the action or decision taken in relation to their concerns or they may contact Block persistently about the same issue(s) via the same, or a number of contact channels. Examples of unacceptable persistent contact might include: ● Continuing to contact Block Staff Members through illegitimate communication channels, such as LinkedIn ● Persistent contact via email, written mail or other communications to executive leadership ● Persistent refusal to accept a Final Response that has been made in relation to a complaint ● Persistent refusal to accept explanations relating to what Block can or cannot do ● Persistent refusal to follow the proper procedures explained to them in order to pursue their issue/concern ● Making an excessive number of contacts about issues previously addressed ● Continuing to contact Block on the same issue(s) without presenting any new information ● Representing themselves as different people or under different names in order to raise the same issues ● Contacting Block Staff Members outside of the ordinary course of business, such as at a physical office location, or their home location It is not necessarily the manner in which such customers communicate with Block, but their persistence in doing so that goes beyond being reasonable. In certain instances, continuous contact may be considered to be harassment. In scenarios where there is a threat that exceeds any reasonable contact, Staff Members must be escalated to Trust & Safety immediately at go/threatescalation. 3 2.4. Unacceptable Public Expression of Views Block recognizes the importance of striking a balance between providing an opportunity for the lawful expression of views and opinions and unacceptable behavior and actions that affect its Staff Members. Some examples of unacceptable behavior in these instances might include: ● Congregating in and/or obstructing thoroughfares and access points of Block office buildings ● Entering or attempting to enter restricted and/or non public areas of Block office buildings ● Recording or photographing Staff Members without their permission ● Any intentional damage to Block property ● Failing to follow instructions of employees or security Staff Members when instructed to leave the premises ● Making threats of harm or violence against Block, and/or its Staff Members ● Stalking or harassing Block Staff Members ● Making inappropriate contact attempts against Block Staff Members, including the use of foul language, or submitting inappropriate material through support channels All of these behaviours must be escalated to Trust & Safety immediately. The priority for reporting is go/threatescalation if inappropriate contact is made, reporting this to Trust & Safety officers on site immediately if the abusive customer is onsite, and/or reach out to #trust-and-safety on Slack. 2.5. Protocol for Managing Unacceptable and Abusive Behaviors Face-to-Face Contact Staff Members are empowered to ask the abusive individual to leave the premises if they feel the person is being aggressive, abusive or offensive. The Staff Member dealing with the person has the right to make this decision after that person has persisted in their disruptive behavior after being advised that they are behaving unacceptably. In scenarios where Staff Members feel an immediate risk to their personal safety or Block property, they should contact the local Trust & Safety Security Operations officer, if they are in a location where one is present, and the Global Security Operations Center GSOC on Slack (#trust-and-safety) to ask for a private message. If there is no officer on site/duty, or Staff Members have been approached outside of Block, immediately contact the local emergency services number and then contact the Global Security Operations Center on Slack. Local Emergency Services Numbers: ● USA - 911 for Harm and 988 for Self-Harm ● Canada - 911 for Harm and 988 for Self-Harm ● UK - 999 for Harm or 116 123 for Self-Harm Samaritans) ● Ireland - 999 for Harm and 112 for Self-Harm ● Australia - 000 for Harm and 13 11 14 for Self-Harm 4 2.6. Telephone Calls The use of abusive, offensive and harmful language towards Block Staff Members will not be tolerated. Staff Members will end any telephone call if they feel the caller is being aggressive, intimidating, abusive, threatening or offensive. The person taking the call is empowered to make this decision. The caller will be advised that their behavior and/or language is unacceptable. If the person persists and refuses to conclude the conversation, the call will be ended politely. In rare cases, if a warning cannot be issued due to immediate concerns, the Staff Member has the authority to end the call promptly. In some scenarios, individuals may make threats against Block that are severe in nature which might include: ● Death threats ● Threat of gun violence ● Bomb threat ● Threat of self harm ● Cyberattack threat Staff Members are encouraged to keep the caller on the line for as long as possible during which they should take detailed notes and ask as many questions as possible to gather as much information as possible. Once the call has ended, immediately contact a Trust & Safety Security Operations officer, file a ticket at go/threatescalation. 2.7. Correspondence Block Staff Members will not process any abusive correspondence (chat, email, letter or fax). Upon receiving such communications

Denylist Appeals Policy | Cash App 6 Confidential 1. Introduction This Policy explains how Cash App supports Customer appeals when their accounts are denylisted (blocked), except where prohibited by law. The process ensures compliance with consumer protection laws (UDAAP) under the Dodd-Frank Act while maintaining Cash App's commitment to trust. The Head of Consumer Protection owns this Policy. 2. Purpose Cash App conducts business in compliance with all applicable laws and regulations. Its commitment to the prevention of UDAAP-related violations requires transparency, truthfulness, suitability, and fairness in dealings with all Customers. Cash App executes on this commitment to maintain a safe and trusted platform which results in Cash App taking Adverse Action, including Denylisting or applying certain limits on a Customer and their Cash App account(s) when there is a suspected or confirmed violation of Cash App’s Terms of Service, internal policies, or the Customer is engaging Prohibited Activities. The purpose of this Policy is to provide the principles and framework that governs how Cash App operates as it pertains to Customer Appeals related to Denylisting. Compliance with this Policy and the associated laws and regulations is to be achieved through the implementation of risk-based compliance and risk procedures. This Policy is intended to be supplemented by additional documentation, as necessary. 3. Scope This Policy is established by Cash App, U.S., a Line of Business under Block, Inc. and is applicable to Denylisting, a specific type of Adverse Action except where prohibited by law, taken on a Cash App Customer and their account(s). 4. Policy Requirements 4.1 Denylisting Appeals Cash App aims to execute Denylisting, a specific type of Adverse Action, except where prohibited by law, on a Customer and their Cash App account(s) in a targeted and accurate manner to protect Customers and Cash Denylist Appeals Policy | Cash App 7 Confidential App from Prohibited Activity . Cash App maintains an Appeals process specifically for Denylisting actions to ensure Legitimate Customers have an avenue to request review. Cash App adheres to a ten (10) Business Day SLA for Appeals reviews, with intake beginning upon Block's recording of an Appeal Review being requested by the customer and ending with the communication of the Appeals review outcome being sent to the Customer. 4.2 Eligible Denylist Appeals The Cash App Denylist Appeals program is designed to establish a consistent, fair and transparent process for handling a Denylist Appeals review resulting from a Denylisting, a specific type of Adverse Action taken on a Customer and their account(s). There may be circumstances where both Risk and Compliance have denylisted a Customer and their account(s); in these instances, Risk or Compliance Appeals operations teams will review the Customer’s account, including connected user accounts and related denylist reasons, when making a decision to overturn or uphold the denylist.Where Cash App determines the Customer’s activity to be legitimate through the review, Cash App will reinstate any eligible account or assets that had been previously denylisted. 4.3 Sponsored Accounts In order to prevent any potential UDAAP violations, Customers with Sponsored Accounts that are requesting a Denylist Appeals review will be eligible to request such a review and this review from intake to resolution will occur within the ten (10) Business Day SLA. 4.4 Re-review of Dispute Claims Policy Cash App customers who have a successful Denylist Appeal which results in their Cash App account being reinstated but had a Dispute denied while the account was denylisted are eligible for a re-review of their Dispute(s) made during the time p
documents.CP-022: Disputes Standard Cash App Last Approved: Feb 5, 2026 Effective Date: Feb 5, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Disputes Standard (“Standardˮ) outlines the strategy and approach to the intake, investigation, and resolution of Errors for Cash App, a line of business under Block, Inc. Cash App records NOEs and provides resolution by investigating and resolving NOEs in compliance with Electronic Funds Transfer Act (“EFTAˮ) 15 U.S.C. §§ 1693 et seq.) and Regulation E 12 C.F.R. Part 1005 requirements and industry best practices. The Disputes Consumer Protection Compliance Lead is responsible for the management of this Standard. Cash App conducts business in compliance with applicable laws and regulations, maintaining and managing a combination of risk and compliance programs. The purpose of this Standard is to define Cash Appʼs Disputes Program and outline Cash Appʼs strategy to ensure it upholds adequate Dispute resolution in line with industry expectations and compliance with regulatory obligations. The activities in support of this Standard are administered cross-functionally. Scope: This Standard is established for Cash App, a line of business under Block, Inc., and is applicable to the Cash App business and related products and services. The Dispute classifications in scope for this Standard are Cash App Peer to Peer (“P2Pˮ), Account Takeover (“ATOˮ), Cash App Card, Remittances, Automatic Clearing House (“ACHˮ), Deposits, Paper Money Deposits, and Cash App Pay. Definitions: Terminology Definition Account When a third party maliciously gains access to a Consumerʼs account with Takeover (“ATOˮ) the intention of using it for fraudulent purposes. Transactions resulting from an ATO are considered a type of Unauthorized Electronic Fund Transfer. 1 Automated The parameters for leveraging an automated investigation process for Dispute Criteria (“ACˮ) handling. Automatic ACH is a US payment network used by financial institutions to facilitate the Clearing House transfer of money between bank accounts. ACH transactions include outgoing (“ACHˮ) debits and incoming deposits to or from a Consumerʼs Cash App account. Business Day All days of the week except Saturdays, Sundays, and federal holidays. Cardholder CDRN is a chargeback prevention tool that sends real-time notifications to Dispute merchants when a cardholder disputes a transaction. This gives merchants Resolution time to contact the Consumer and resolve the issue before a chargeback is Network filed. (“CDRNˮ) Cashforce 1 A customized version of Salesforce used as Cash Appʼs Consumer (“CF1ˮ) relationship management software. Salesforce) Consumer An individual who is a natural person who obtains or has obtained a financial product or service that is to be used primarily for personal, family or household purposes. Curing Period This initial step in the investigation process is completed prior to running any ACs and is not implemented as an AC itself. The Curing Period does not affect Cash Appʼs adherence to the 10-day timeline requirements under Regulation E. Dispute A dispute is the act of alleging an Error regarding a transaction. In many scenarios, the process for reviewing disputes is governed by regulatory requirements. Dispute Abuse Dispute abuse refers to the behavior where customers file false or exaggerated claims regarding transactions, declaring legitimate transactions as unauthorized in order to seek refunds illegitimately. This type of abuse significantly undermines the integrity of Dispute resolution processes. Dispute Abuse Refers to a specialized review procedure used to manually verify the activity Detection of Consumers flagged for Dispute Abuse. This involves an ehanced review Program by a specialized team. Disputes The Disputes Decision Making Application for Cash App Dispute Decision Making investigations receives a command from the Disputes Platform containing Application basic information about the Dispute. The Disputes Decision Making Application then gathers all the information needed to evaluate ACs and make suggested actions for the Dispute; these actions include suggested 2 queue routing and final Dispute handling. It then sends the investigation result back to the Disputes Platform. Disputes Serves as a central repository for Disputes, enhancing Cash Appʼs oversight Platform of error resolution management. This platform helps track the dispute process from intake to resolution, including monitoring for SLAs. Document dIDV occurs when a Consumer submits an image of their government-issued Identity photo identification document (“IDˮ) as well as a “livenessˮ test (moving Verification selfie) and these submissions are verified with the vendor. (“dIDVˮ) Duress Duress is considered a threat of harm or use of physical harm by a third party that negates the customerʼs free will to voluntarily authorize a transaction. Actions that constitute duress include use of physical harm or violence, false imprisonment, physical constraint, kidnapping, coercion, psychological pressure, or an actual (or perceived) threat. Electronic Fund Any transfer of funds that is initiated through an Electronic Terminal, Transfer (“EFTˮ) telephone, computer, or magnetic tape for the purpose of ordering, instructing, or authorizing a financial institution to debit or credit a Consumer's account. The term includes, but is not limited to: ● Point-of-sale transfers; ● Automated teller machine transfers; ● Direct deposits or withdrawals of funds; ● Transfers initiated by telephone; and ● Transfers resulting from debit card transactions, whether or not initiated through an Electronic Terminal. Electronic The eIDV process matches the identity information collected from the Identity Consumer to identity information returned by external vendors. Verification (“eIDVˮ) Electronic An electronic device, other than a telephone operated by a Consumer, Terminal through which a Consumer may initiate an electronic fund transfer. The term includes, but is not limited to, point-of-sale terminals, automated teller machines (“ATMsˮ), and cash dispensing machines. Error The term Error means: ● Unauthorized EFTs; ● Incorrect or missing transfers to or from a Consumerʼs account; ● Omission of a transaction from a periodic statement; ● Computational or bookkeeping Errors; ● Receipt of an incorrect amount from an Electronic Terminal; and ● Requests for documentation (unless requesting a duplicate copy), or 3 requests for information concerning transaction or clarification to determine whether an “Errorˮ specified above exists. For Remittance Transfers, “Errorˮ also means: ● An incorrect amount paid by a sending Consumer, unless the remittance disclosures stated an estimate of the amount paid by the Sender and the difference results from application of the actual exchange rate, fees, and taxes, rather than any estimated amount; ● Failure to make available to a designated recipient the amount of currency disclosed (unless certain conditions are met); and ● Failure to make funds available to a designated recipient by the date of availability stated in the disclosure provided to the Sender (unless certain conditions are met). Notice of Error The written or oral communication from a Cash App Consumer indicating (“NOEˮ) that the Consumer believes there is an Error on related to their Cash App account, and that: ● Provides sufficient information for Cash App to identify the Consumerʼs name and account number; and ● Indicates why the Consumer believes an Error exists and includes to the extent possible the type, date, and amount of the alleged Error. A NOE is a regulated claim. For Remittance Transfers, a NOE is a written or oral communication from a Sender indicating the Sender believes there is an Error related to a Remittance Transfer sent from their Cash App account, and that: ● Provides sufficient information for Cash App to identify the Senderʼs name, phone number, and address, the Remittance Transfer recipientʼs name, and if known phone number or address; and ● Indicates why the Sender believes an error exists and includes to the extent possible the type, date, and amount of the error of the Remittance Transfer to which the notice of error applies Provisional A temporary credit that may be applied to a Consumerʼs account during a Credit NOE investigation if a decision is unable to be made within ten 10 business days. Regulator A proprietary tool used to view information and take certain actions on a Consumerʼs account. Remittance The electronic transfer of funds from a Consumer in the United States to a Transfer recipient in a foreign country. The transfer must be initiated by a remittance transfer provider and be greater than $15.00 U.S. dollars. 4 Sender (for A Consumer in a State who primarily for personal, family, or household Remittance purposes requests a remittance transfer provider to send a remittance Transfers) transfer to a designated recipient. Service Level The maximum allotted time for intake to resolution of a Dispute review. Agreement (“SLAˮ) Unauthorized An EFT from a Consumerʼs Cash App account initiated by a person other EFT than the Consumer without actual authority to initiate the transfer, and from which the Consumer receives no benefit Account Takeovers are considered to be Unauthorized EFTs). The term does not include an EFT initiated: ● By a person who was furnished the access device to the Consumerʼs account by the Consumer, unless the Consumer has notified Cash App that transfers by that person are no longer authorized; ● With fraudulent intent by the Consumer or any person acting in concert with the Consumer; or ● By Cash App or its Staff Members. 2. Standard Requirements 2.1. Policy Statement Under Regulation E, a NOE refers to a Consumer's notification to a financial institution regarding a suspected Error in an EFT, which the financial institution is required to promptly investigate and determine whether an error occurred within ten 10 business

Document Details 16 Elder Financial Exploitation Policy | Cash App 3 Confidential and Not for Distribution Definitions Terminology Definition Account Take Over (“ATO”) When a third party maliciously gains access to a Consumer’s account with the intention of using it for fraudulent purposes. Brand / Business The Consumer receives an unprovoked external incoming message Impersonation Scam (email, text message, phone call, etc). The message indicates that there is an issue with one of the Consumer’s accounts (e.g. Apple, Amazon, or their local utility) and the Scammer needs the Consumer to pay to resolve the situation (suspicious purchase, past due bill, etc) using their Cash App account. This could also be messaging purporting to be from Cash App. Celebrity Impersonation The fraudster creates a fake online presence impersonating a specific celebrity and makes social media posts offering fans personal connections, VIP access, investment opportunities and the chance to support the celebrity’s favorite charities, but they must pay to participate. Ultimately, the Consumer sends funds via Cash App to the fraudster and receives nothing in return. Charity/Charitable Giving The fraudster creates a fake online presence impersonating a specific Impersonation charity and posts pleas on social media that pull at the Consumer’s heartstrings. The Consumer ultimately sends funds to a Cash App account that they believe is operated by or will benefit the actual charity. Consumer An individual who is a natural person who obtains or has obtained a financial product or service that is to be used primarily for personal, family or household purposes. An Elder is also a Consumer. Elder A Consumer aged sixty (60) years or older. Elder Abuse Any knowing, intentional, or negligent act by a family member, caregiver, advisor, investment entity, or any other person or entity that causes harm or a serious risk of harm to an Elder. An elder may become a victim of Elder Abuse with or without his or her consent or knowledge through trickery, intimidation, forced consent, or his or her inability to give consent due to lack of competency. Elder Abuse includes Elder Financial Exploitation. Elder Financial Exploitation The illegal or improper use of an Elder’s assets, funds, or property. The most common types of Elder Abuse include theft and scams. Elder Financial Exploitation also includes stolen or misappropriated PII, which carries no dollar value threshold to report. Perpetrators can be family, friends, or strangers, and the financial impact can be significant. Elder Financial Exploitation Policy | Cash App 4 Confidential and Not for Distribution Friend/Family The fraudster creates a fake Cash App account using specific Impersonation characteristics of another real Consumer's personal information. The fraudster obtains all the information they need to make the fake profile by either taking over someone’s social media account or by scraping social media accounts that are not private. The fraudster then sends multiple requests to the impersonated person’s contacts, either by knowing $cashtags, phone numbers ahead of time or by searching for profiles in Cash App’s search functionality. The subject lines of the requests indicate the impersonated person is in trouble or needs money urgently. The Consumer ultimately sends funds via Cash App, but eventually realizes the person who is being impersonated is not who is sending the requests. Friend/Family Impersonation refers to the creation of impersonation accounts and is not an indicator that the actual account was fraudulently taken over. Fake Check Scam A Scammer sends the Consumer a check or money order worth more than the amount they are owed and instructs them to return the excess funds back to them via Cash App. The Scammer will portray the situation as urgent and ask the Consumer to front the funds while the bank is processing the check. But the check will ultimately bounce. The Scammers will keep the real money they received. Giveaway/Prize Scam The Scammer pretends to be running an official giveaway from a generally known brand or business. The Consumer will receive outreach in the form of a call, email, or letter saying they won a sweepstakes, lottery, or prize, but are told to pay a small fee via Cash App to get the prize. Ultimately, the prize is never awarded. In this scam, the Consumer may have some familiarity with the brand or business but the outreach is specific to winning a prize - not resolving an issue. This scam can also be an Advanced Fee scam. Goods and Services Scam The Scammer pretends to be a legitimate online seller using a very real looking website and/or is selling a good or service via social media marketplace sites. They offer products or services at prices that are often too good to be true and post fake reviews to corroborate their scam. The Consumer (buyer) will often pay some or all of the cost of the item upfront via Cash App. The seller has no intention to provide the good or service, nor will the Scammer meet face-to-face to facilitate the exchange of a good or service. Government / Public Entity The Consumer will receive an external incoming message (email, text Impersonation message, phone call, etc) from someone who says they work for a government agency (FTC, Social Security Administration, FBI, IRS, etc) or another Public Entity (Banks, Utilities, Businesses, Corporations, etc). The Scammer is often looking for personal information and/or requires a payment via Cash App to resolve an urgent issue. Elder Financial Exploitation Policy | Cash App 5 Confidential and Not for Distribution Identity Theft Identity Theft is when a Consumer’s personal data is used in some way that involves fraud or deception, typically for economic gain. Job Scam These scams prey on the unemployed by promising good jobs and exploit their need for employment through fake job offers. Scammers will ask for money via Cash App to help a Consumer get the job (placement fee), for supplies to start a business at home, for job listings or for a certification that is supposed to get a job. However, after a payment(s) is sent, the Scammer disappears, the job never materializes, and the Consumer is left out of pocket for the amount sent. Money Flip Scam Scammers will claim they can “flip” a Consumer’s money and multiply it as long as the Consumer first sends a payment to the Scammer via Cash App. These scams are too good to be true and the Scammer will accept the funds but never send anything in return. The Scammer may sometimes claim to know someone at Cash App who can quickly make the flip happen. Rental/Housing Scam A Scammer duplicates real rental listings, but changes the contact information and reposts them on several rental sites. Consumers are told to make a deposit or pay fees via Cash App for homes or apartments that the Consumer will never actually buy or rent - the Scammer doesn’t have the authority to sell or rent the places in the listings. Romance Scam A Scammer adopts a fake online identity and uses the illusion of a romantic relationship to gain a victim’s affection and trust. The Scammer uses this relationship to manipulate and steal from the Consumer by having them send money via Cash App. This scam can perpetuate over a long period of time and Consumers may send consistent payments for months or even years before realizing it was all an illusion. Scam A dishonest scheme or fraudulent activity designed to defraud, exploit, or deceive individuals by persuading them to make an authorized transaction under false pretenses. Scammer The perpetrator of a Scam. Technical Support Scam The Consumer receives an external incoming message (email, text message, phone call, etc) and is told that something is wrong with their computer/electronic device and is given a phone number to call for help. The Consumer may also receive a pop-up on their computer due to malware, which instructs them to call a fake technical support number. The technical support agent pretends to deal with their issue and convinces the Consumer to send funds to a Cash App account to resolve it. Elder Financial Exploitation Policy | Cash App 6 Confidential and Not for Distribution Roles and Responsibilities Role Responsibility Cash App Customer Care Cash App CCO is the first line of defense and provides phone, in-app, Organization (“CCO”) messaging, and email support to Cash App Consumers. CCO should consider the behavioral and financial red flags outlined in this Policy when interacting with Elders to identify any potential victims of Elder Financ
documents.CP-024: Loan Loss and Charge-Off Policy Cash App | US Last Approved: Mar 25, 2026 Effective Date: Mar 25, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: As a service provider in the financial industry, it is the policy of Cash App US (“Cash Appˮ) to develop, implement, and maintain this Loan Loss and Charge-Off Policy (“Policyˮ) for its Credit Products Borrow, Pre-Purchase Financing, Retroactive Financing, and Afterpay Pay Monthly). The purpose of this Policy is to describe the terms under which Cash App will charge-off accounts on its servicing platforms. Blockʼs Head of Underwriting & Credit and/or their designee is responsible for the management of this Policy. Cash App conducts business in compliance with applicable laws and regulations. The purpose of this Policy is to document Cash Appʼs loan charge-off process in accordance with the Federal Financial Institutions Examination Councilʼs (“FFIECˮ) Uniform Retail Credit Classification and Account Management Policy, which establishes standards for the classification and treatment of retail credit in financial institutions. Scope: This Policy governs Cash Appʼs Credit Products and has been created to ensure that Cash App and relevant Employees are aware and comply with the expectations of the Policy. Definitions: Terminology Definition Account A continuing relationship established by a person with a financial institution or creditor to obtain a product or service for personal, family, household, or business purposes, including an extension of credit. Charge-Off A determination that an uncollected Account balance is unlikely to be collected. The determination to recharacterize the uncollected Account balance as a charge-off does not discharge the amount of the debt owed by the Account holder. 1 Covered An Account that is primarily for personal, family, or household purposes. Account Credit Products Covered Accounts consisting of Cash App’s lending products (Borrow, Pre-Purchase Financing, Retroactive Financing, and Afterpay Pay Monthly) unless otherwise noted. Customer A consumer who is contractually obligated for repayment of an Account. Employee Staff members, contractors, temporary workers, and executive management. 2. Policy Requirements 2.1. Charge-Off Guidelines The following are guidelines for the timing and circumstances of charged-off Credit Products: ● Accounts originated on or after October 1, 2024 will be charged off no later than upon becoming 90 cumulative days delinquent from the contractual due date. Accounts originated before October 1, 2024 will be charged off no later than upon becoming 120 cumulative days delinquent from the contractual due date. ● Accounts in bankruptcy are charged off no later than 30 days from receipt of notification of the filing of bankruptcy, unless there is clear demonstration and document that repayment is likely to occur. ● Accounts that have been disputed by customers and identified as fraudulent are charged off no later than 30 days of discovery or when loss is determined, whichever is shorter. ● Accounts of deceased persons are charged off no later than 30 days from receipt of notification of the deceased status. Actual credit losses on individual Credit Products should be recorded when Cash App becomes aware of the loss, but in no case should the charge-off exceed the timeframes stated in this policy. A loss is defined as an asset, or portion thereof, that is considered uncollectible, and of such little value that continuance on the books is not warranted. A loss does not mean the asset has absolutely no recovery of salvage value; rather, it is not practical or desirable to defer writing off an essentially worthless asset, or portion thereof, even though partial recovery may occur in the future. When an Account is charged off, Cash App will place a note on the Account in the designated system of record, and Cash App will maintain an appropriate charge-off coding system to ensure a permanent record in its data systems. 2 2.2. Re-Age Guidelines Cash App does not currently re-age any Credit Products. 3. Compliance & Enforcement 3.1. Roles and Responsibilities: ● Head of Underwriting: ○ The Head of Underwriting & Credit will provide assistance and support in implementing this Policy across the organization and shall be responsible for establishing appropriate practices designed to ensure that Company operations reflect the Companyʼs commitment to compliance and that all employees are aware of that commitment and their individual responsibility. ● Counsel: ○ Legal counsel will provide interpretative advice and counsel regarding the applicability and application of the requirements of this Policy to Cash Appʼs business and operational activities. 3.2. Monitoring and Reporting On an ongoing basis, the Policy Owner and/or their designee monitors activities for compliance and objective achievement. As outlined in the Compliance Management System Policy, Compliance will provide regular updates, as applicable, to the Block, Inc. Board and executive management. Reporting may cover specific information related to: ● Policies and standards ● Procedures ● Audit/exam findings ● Issues management ● Training ● Complaints 3.3. Record Retention All Documents are retained according to the longer of: ● Blockʼs Data Classification & Handling Policy ● Applicable regulatory requirements 3.4. Exceptions The Policy Owner or designee will have authority to approve exceptions and/or exemptions to this Policy, provided that the proposed exception or exemption does not violate applicable laws and regulations. Exception requests must be documented in writing, include a reason for a request, and 3 provide a description of the controls that the Company will implement to mitigate risks associated with the exception. Any approved exceptions must be documented in writing and retained with recordkeeping requirements. Any approved exceptions or exemptions should be submitted to the Policy Governance Team. The Document Owner and/or designee will inform Compliance Leadership and applicable committees, as needed, of exceptions. 3.5. Confidentiality This document is confidential and should only be accessed by employees. 4. Questions & Contact Information Questions: For questions or clarifications about this Policy, please contact the Policy Owner. 5. Document Details & Revisions Review and Approval: This document shall be reviewed and approved at least annually and on an ad hoc basis in accordance with the Compliance Document Approval Matrix. documents.CP-025: Fiat P2P Scam Policy Cash App | US Last Approved: Mar 27, 2026 Effective Date: Mar 27, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: Cash App is committed to the establishment of a comprehensive Anti-Fraud program for its peer-to-peer product. An integral part of that program relates to Cash Appʼs efforts to remove a form of fraudulently-induced transactions, also known as Scams, from Cash App's platform. The purpose of this Fiat P2P Scams Policy ("Policy") is to establish and communicate to all employees, Cash App's holistic approach to preventing, deterring, detecting, and reporting Scams, and the circumstances under which Cash App will reimburse its customers for money lost as a result of a Scam. This document defines what a "Scam" is, details how Cash App categorizes Scams, and outlines the responsibilities of employees to protect Cash App customers from being victimized by Scams. Scope: This Policy is established by Cash App US. References to employees throughout this document shall refer to all full-time employees and fixed-term and contingent workers including interns, temps, consultants, vendors, third-party providers, and contractors. Definitions: Terminology Definition Brand / Business The customer receives an unprovoked external incoming message Impersonation (email, text message, phone call, etc). The message indicates that there is an issue with one of the customer's accounts (e.g. Apple, Amazon, or their local utility) and the scammer needs the customer to pay to resolve the situation (suspicious purchase, past due bill, etc) using their Cash App account. 1 Cash App A customer comes into contact via social media with an account Giveaway/Prize purporting to be an official Cash App account, which gives the scammer Scam an added level of legitimacy. The customer is told to pay a small fee via Cash App in order to win a prize. Ultimately, the prize is never awarded. In this scam, the customer may have some familiarity with Cash App but the outreach is specific to winning a prize - not resolving an issue. Cash App The customer receives an external incoming message (email, text Impersonation message, phone call, etc) purporting to be from Cash App. This message often states that a payment needs to be made from the customer's Cash App account to rectify a glitch, verify/secure their account, receive a pending payment, or resolve some other issue. Celebrity The fraudster creates a fake online presence impersonating a specific Impersonation celebrity and makes social media posts offering fans personal connections, VIP access, investment opportunities and the chance to support the celebrity's favorite charities, but they must pay to participate. Ultimately, the customer sends funds via Cash App to the fraudster and receives nothing in return. Charity/Charitable The fraudster creates a fake online presence impersonating a specific Giving Impersonation charity and posts pleas on social media that pull at the customer's heartstrings. The customer ultimately sends funds to a Cash App account that they believe is operated by or will benefit the actual charity. Cleared Recipient A recipient which has not been confirmed as a Scammer through the scam investigative process. Customer A natural or juridical person that opens an account and receives Cash App products and services, or a person opening a new account for someone who lacks legal capacity. Debt/Credit These scams target customers with significant credit card debt by falsely Resolution Scam promising to negotiate with their creditors to settle or otherwise reduce their repayment obligations. These operations often charge cash-strapped customers a large up-front fee via Cash App, but then fail to help them settle or lower their debts – if they provide any service at all. Deception Scams Deception scams occur when the Cash App customer has no familiarity and has no relationship prior to being contacted to the entity the scammer is posing as and is defrauded. Cash App defines the following types of scams as deception scams: Debt/Credit Resolution Scams, Job Scam, Fake Check Scam, Money Flip Scam, Giveaway/Prize Scam, Cash App Giveaway/Prize Scam, Goods and Services Scam, Technical Support Scam, Rental/Housing Scam, and Romance Scam. Fake Check Scam A scammer sends the customer a check or money order worth more than the amount they are owed and instructs them to return the excess funds 2 back to them via Cash App. The scammer will portray the situation as urgent and ask the customer to front the funds while the bank is processing the check. But the check will ultimately bounce, and the bank will require the customer to repay the full amount of the fake check. The scammers will keep the real money they received. Friend/Family The fraudster creates a fake Cash App account using specific Impersonation characteristics of another real customer's personal information. The fraudster obtains all the information they need to make the fake profile by either taking over someone's social media account or by scraping social media accounts that are not private. The fraudster then sends multiple requests to the impersonated person's contacts, either by knowing $cashtags, phone numbers ahead of time or by searching for profiles in Cash App's search functionality. The subject lines of the requests indicate the impersonated person is in trouble or needs money urgently. The customer ultimately sends funds via Cash App, but eventually realizes the person who is being impersonated is not who is sending the requests. Giveaway/Prize The scammer pretends to be running an official giveaway from a Scam generally known brand or business. The customer will receive outreach in the form of a call, email, or letter saying they won a sweepstakes, lottery, or prize, but are told to pay a small fee via Cash App to get the prize. Ultimately, the prize is never awarded. In this scam, the customer may have some familiarity with the brand or business but the outreach is specific to winning a prize - not resolving an issue. Goods and Services The scammer pretends to be a legitimate online seller using a very real Scam looking website and/or is selling a good or service via social media marketplace sites. They offer products or services at prices that are often too good to be true and post fake reviews to corroborate their scam. The customer (buyer) will often pay some or all of the cost of the item upfront via Cash App. The seller has no intention to provide the good or service, nor will the scammer meet face-to-face to facilitate the exchange of a good or service. The identification of this scam depends on the seller's Cash App account characteristics and their behaviors post receipt of the funds from the buyer, (ie. the seller has stopped communicating with the buyer, the seller's Cash App account shows signs of being a scammer). Government The customer will receive an external incoming message (email, text Impersonation message, phone call, etc) from someone who says they work for a government agency FTC, Social Security Administration, FBI, IRS, etc). The scammer is often looking for personal information and/or requires a payment via Cash App to resolve an urgent issue. 3 Imposter Scam Imposter Scams occur when the Cash App customer is familiar with the Impersonation) entity the scammer is posing as (government entity, family member, celebrity, brand, company, etc…). The terms "imposter", "impersonator", and "impersonation" can be used interchangeably. Cash App defines the following types of scams as Imposter: Brand/Business Impersonation, Cash App Impersonation, Celebrity Impersonation, Charity/Charitable Giving Impersonation, Family/Friend Impersonation, and Government Impersonation. Job Scam These scams prey on the unemployed by promising good jobs and exploit their need for employment through fake job offers. Scammers will ask for money via Cash App to help a customer get the job (placement fee), for supplies to start a business at home, for job listings or for a certification that is supposed to get a job. However, after a payment(s) is sent, the scammer disappears, the job never materializes, and the customer is left out of pocket for the amount sent. Money Flip Scam Scammers will claim they can "flip" a customer's money and multiply it as long as the customer first sends a payment to the scammer via Cash App. These scams are too good to be true and the scammer will accept the funds but never send anything in return. The scammer may sometimes claim to know someone at Cash App who can quickly make the flip happen. Recipient A Cash App customer that receives a P2P payment from a Sender. Rental/Housing A scammer duplicates real rental listings, but changes the contact Scam information and reposts them on several rental sites. Customers are told to make a deposit or pay fees via Cash App for homes or apartments that the customer will never actually buy or rent - the scammer doesn't have the authority to sell or rent the places in the listings. Romance Scam A scammer adopts a fake online identity and uses the illusion of a romantic relationship to gain a victim's affection and trust. The scammer uses this relationship to manipulate and steal from the customer by having them send money via Cash App. This scam can perpetuate over a long period of time and customers may send consistent payments for months or even years before realizing it was all an illusion. Scam A "Scam" is a dishonest scheme or fraudulent activity designed to defraud, exploit, or deceive individuals by persuading them to make an authorized transaction sent via the fiat Peer to Peer product under false pretenses. Cash App defines Scams in the following categories: Imposter Scams and Deception Scams. Scammer A recipient which has been confirmed through the scam investigative process. 4 Sender A Cash App customer that initiates a P2P payment to a Recipient.
