documents.CP-027: Senior Managers and Certification Regime SMCR and Conduct Rules Policy Clearpay Finance Limited Last Approved: Mar 23, 2026 Effective Date: Mar 23, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: Clearpay Finance Limited (“CFLˮ) is committed to upholding the highest standards of conduct and governance. This Senior Managers and Certification Regime SMCR and Conduct Rules Policy (“Policyˮ) outlines CFLʼs approach to complying with the Financial Conduct Authorityʼs (“FCAˮ) Individual Accountability regime. The Chief Compliance Officer (“CCOˮ) is responsible for management of this Policy. This Policy relates to CFL only in its operations in the United Kingdom. The purpose of this Policy is to support CFL to comply with the FCAʼs Senior Managers and Certification Regime. This regime aims to reduce harm to consumers and strengthen market integrity by making individuals more accountable for their conduct and competence. The Policy will: ● Establish clear accountability for senior individuals within the firm. ● Ensure that individuals performing key roles meet and maintain standards of fitness, propriety, and competence. ● Embed the FCA's Conduct Rules throughout the organisation, promoting a culture of good conduct. ● Define and signpost the processes for managing SMCR requirements effectively. ● Ensure compliance with relevant sections of the FCA Handbook, primarily SYSC, COCON, and FIT, tailored for a solo-regulated firm. Scope: This Policy applies to all aspects of CFL's business activities regulated by the FCA. It applies to all employees and directors of CFL, with specific requirements for: ● Individuals approved by the FCA as Senior Management Functions SMFs ("Senior Managers"). ● Individuals identified by the Firm as performing Certification Functions ("Certified Persons"). 1 ● All other employees subject to the FCA Conduct Rules ("Conduct Rules Staff"). Definitions: Terminology Definition Senior Manager A role designated by the FCA requiring regulatory approval due to its Function (“SMFˮ) significance. Senior Manager An individual approved by the FCA to perform one or more SMFs. Statement of A document required for each SMF holder detailing their areas of Responsibilities responsibility SoR Prescribed Responsibilities Specific responsibilities defined by the FCA that must be allocated to SMFs. PRs Management A document outlining the Firm's management and governance Responsibilities arrangements, including SMF responsibilities and reporting lines. Map Certification The regime which requires firms to identify staff performing Certification Regime Functions and assess their fitness and propriety annually. Certification A role specified by the FCA that could cause significant harm to the firm or Function its customers, requiring annual certification by the Firm An individual performing a Certification Function who has been certified by Certified Person the Firm as fit and proper High-level standards of behaviour expected by the FCA, applicable to almost Conduct Rules all employees within a regulated firm The assessment criteria (including honesty, integrity, reputation, Fitness and competence, capability, and financial soundness) used to evaluate Propriety F&P individuals for SMF and Certification roles A failure to comply with the requirements of the SMCR, including the Breach Conduct Rules. The reporting of concerns about potential wrongdoing or malpractice within Whistleblowing the Firm. 2 2. Policy Requirements 2.1. Introduction CFL is authorised and regulated by the FCA to enter into regulated consumer credit agreements with customers (“consumersˮ or “customersˮ). CFL expects all its employees to comply with all applicable FCA requirements, specifically for this Policy this refers to the FCAʼs Senior Managers and Certification Regime (“SMCRˮ). 2.2. Governance and Oversight The Board holds the ultimate responsibility for ensuring CFL complies with its regulatory obligations, including SMCR. The Board approves this policy and oversees its implementation. The CCO will provide an overview of the SMCR compliance position annually to the Board, and will provide a quarterly overview of the Certified and SMF populations, training completion rates, and Conduct Rule breaches recorded and reported to the FCA. The CCO holds the relevant SMCR prescribed responsibilities. 2.3. Fitness and Propriety F&P assessments ● Purpose: To ensure individuals appointed to SMF and Certification roles are, and remain, suitable for their positions. ● Scope: Applies to candidates for SMF and Certification roles (pre-appointment) and existing SMFs and Certified Persons (ongoing, at least annually). ● Process: Assessments consider honesty, integrity, reputation, competence, capability, and financial soundness. Associated checks should include, but are not limited to: identity verification, criminal record checks DBS, credit checks, directorship checks, verification of qualifications, regulatory references (mandatory for SMF and Certified roles covering the past 6 years), and internal performance/conduct records. Please see the Clearpay Fitness and Propriety Standard for more detailed information] ● Assessment: Undertaken by the Human Resources Business Partner HRBP for CFL and with oversight from CFLʼs CCO who will hold the SMF16 position under SMCR. A formal record of the assessment and decision should be maintained and must be retrievable. Assessment of the CCO will be overseen by the CFL Board. ● Annual Certification Certified Persons): CFL must formally assess and certify the F&P of all Certified Persons at least annually, issuing an F&P certificate. ● Ongoing Duty: SMFs and Certified Persons have a duty to promptly inform the Firm of any changes that might impact their F&P status. The Firm will reassess F&P if relevant new information emerges between annual checks. ● Record Keeping: All F&P assessments, supporting evidence, and decisions will be documented and retained in accordance with FCA requirements, which is 6 years after the end of the individualʼs employment. 3 2.4. SMF Handover Process A robust and documented handover process is mandatory for all incoming and outgoing Senior Management Functions (“SMFsˮ) to ensure the seamless transfer of responsibilities, preservation of institutional knowledge, and the continued effective discharge of Prescribed and Overall Responsibilities. The outgoing SMF is responsible for providing a comprehensive briefing to their successor, which must include, at a minimum: ● a detailed overview of their Statement of Responsibilities and associated activities; ● key ongoing projects, risks, and regulatory obligations; significant open issues and remediation plans; ● critical internal and external relationships; and relevant management information. The incoming SMF is responsible for actively engaging in this process and satisfying themselves that they have received sufficient information to assume their responsibilities. All handovers must be formally documented, detailing the information shared and any agreed actions. A copy of this handover documentation must be retained by the Compliance function SMF16 and be available for review. This process is designed to support the firm's commitment to effective governance and the individual SMF's Duty of Responsibility. 2.5. Code of Conduct and FCA Conduct Rules CFL adopts the FCA Conduct Rules as a core component of its expectations for employee behaviour which is further laid out in Block Code of Business Conduct and Ethics: ● Tier 1 Individual Conduct Rules): Apply to all employees except those in purely ancillary roles with no connection to regulated activities. ○ Must act with integrity; ○ Must act with due skill, care and diligence; ○ Must be open and cooperative with the FCA, the Prudential Regulation Authority PRA and other regulators; ○ Must pay due regard to the interests of customers and treat them fairly; ○ Must observe proper standards market conduct; ○ Must act to deliver good outcomes for retail customers; ● Tier 2 Senior Manager Conduct Rules): Apply in addition to Tier 1 rules for all SMFs: ○ Must take reasonable steps to ensure that the business of the firm for which Mre responsible is controlled effectively; ○ Must take reasonable steps to ensure that the business of the firm for which they are responsible complies with the relevant requirements and standards of the regulatory system; ○ Must take reasonable steps to ensure that any delegation of their responsibilities is to an appropriate person and that they oversee the discharge of the delegated responsibility effectively; 4 ○ Must disclose appropriately any information of which the FCA or PRA would reasonably expect notice. CFL will provide mandatory training to all relevant staff on the Conduct Rules applicable to their role upon joining and on an ongoing (at least annual) basis. Training completion will be tracked. Failure to comply with the Conduct Rules may result in disciplinary action, up to and including dismissal, and may require notification to the FCA. 2.6. Certification Regime CFL will maintain a list of roles identified as performing Certification Functions based on FCA definitions. This list will be reviewed regularly (quarterly by the Board) and updated as needed. As detailed in Section 2.3, all individuals in these roles will undergo an annual F&P assessment, leading to the issuance (or refusal) of an annual certificate by the Firm. CFL will ensure relevant information about Certified Persons is submitted accurately and promptly to the FCA Directory. 2.7. Whistleblowing and Escalation CFL is committed to fostering an environment where employees feel safe to raise concerns about potential misconduct, including breaches of SMCR requirements or Conduct Rules. The Firm maintains a separate Whistleblowing and Speak-up Policy and Procedure, accessible to all staff, outlining confidential reporting channels and protections against detriment for whistleblower
documents.CP-030: Vulnerable Customer Standard Clearpay Last Approved: Mar 26, 2026 Effective Date: Mar 26, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: The purpose of this Vulnerable Customer Standard (“Standardˮ) is for Clearpay Finance Limited (“Clearpayˮ) to establish a comprehensive framework for engaging with vulnerable customers when providing its regulated products and services. Clearpayʼs aim is to ensure that every customer who has a characteristic or characteristics of vulnerability is treated fairly, and, where possible, in accordance with their needs. This requirement is designed to ensure that vulnerable consumers experience outcomes equal to those of other consumers, and receive consistently good outcomes. This Standard is Clearpay's enterprise vulnerability standard and establishes the primary framework for identifying, categorising, and supporting vulnerable customers across all areas of the business. Whilst this Standard sets out Clearpay's comprehensive approach to vulnerability, Clearpay recognises that vulnerability considerations are relevant throughout the organisation. Accordingly, other Clearpay policies, standards and procedures will reference and incorporate considerations relating to customer vulnerability where appropriate to their scope and purpose. Examples of areas where vulnerability considerations are embedded include, but are not limited to: ● Product Design and Development – ensuring products are designed with vulnerable customers' needs in mind ● Marketing and Financial Promotions – ensuring communications are fair, clear, and not exploitative ● Credit Decisioning and Affordability Assessments – considering vulnerability factors in lending decisions ● Customer Communications – ensuring all communications are accessible and easy to understand ● Collections and Arrears Management – tailoring approaches to vulnerable customers' circumstances ● Complaints Handling – providing enhanced support to vulnerable customers during the complaints process ● Data Protection and Privacy – ensuring sensitive personal information is handled appropriately ● Third Party Management – ensuring outsourced service providers understand and comply with vulnerability requirements ● Staff Training and Competence – ensuring all relevant staff are equipped to identify and support vulnerable customers All Clearpay Employees are responsible for considering vulnerability in their day-to-day work and ensuring that customers are treated fairly, regardless of whether they have been formally identified as vulnerable. This enterprise-wide approach ensures that vulnerability is not treated as an isolated concern but is embedded within Clearpay's culture and business practices. Scope: This Standard applies to Clearpay, all its employees, outsourced service providers, consultants, contractors, suppliers and agents (“Clearpay Employeesˮ). It is the duty of all Clearpay Employees to be familiar with this Standard and any operational routines concerning vulnerable or potential vulnerable customers which are relevant to their work. Definitions: Terminology Definition FCA Financial Conduct Authority Vulnerable The FCA's definition: "someone who, due to their personal circumstances, is Customer especially susceptible to detriment, particularly when a firm is not acting with appropriate levels of care." Retail Customers Customers in the context of Clearpay’s regulated consumer credit agreements. PATHways Clearpay's model used by staff to manage in-depth conversations and Model categorise vulnerability. 2 2. Clearpay Requirements 2.1 Regulatory Framework Clearpay is authorised and regulated by the Financial Conduct Authority (“FCAˮ) to enter into regulated consumer credit agreements with customers (“consumersˮ or “customersˮ). In its Finalised Guidance on the Fair Treatment of Vulnerable Customers (“FG21/1ˮ), the FCA provided guidance on how authorised firms should treat vulnerable customers, the latest version of which is referred to throughout this Standard. Authorised firms are required to pay attention to the fair treatment of vulnerable customers across all products and services. In this Standard, Clearpay demonstrates how its business model, the actions it takes, and its culture ensures the fair treatment of customers. The FCAʼs approach to regulation is outcomes-focused and based on the Principles for Business (“Principlesˮ), with Principle 12Consumer Duty (the delivery of good outcomes for retail customers) underpinning the need for Clearpay to take particular care in the treatment of vulnerable customers. In the context of Clearpayʼs regulated consumer credit agreements, borrowers will be retail customers. Clearpay will also comply with its obligations under the Equality Act 2010, particularly in its approach to vulnerable customers. 2.2 Identifying Vulnerable Customers The FCAʼs definition of a Vulnerable Customer is: “...someone who, due to their personal circumstances, is especially susceptible to detriment, particularly when a firm is not acting with appropriate levels of care.ˮ In order for the needs of vulnerable customers to be correctly addressed, it is important for Clearpay to be able to identify them. The FCA acknowledges that vulnerability is a broad concept and is “such a subjective term that it is hard to define.ˮ Furthermore, when looking at consumerʼs use of credit products, vulnerability can manifest itself in a number of ways. Clearpay will need to consider the increased risk of consumer harm that may be present for vulnerable customers, and should consider the four objectives of the FCAʼs Consumer Duty when considering areas where foreseeable harm may be present for vulnerable customers. The below is not an exhaustive list: ● Products and Services: ○ Customers may choose a product that is not suitable for their needs; ○ Clearpay could provide a product to a customer that is not suitable for their needs, if they have not adequately considered its target market or built vulnerability concerns into its design; ● Price and Fair Value: ○ Customers may choose a high cost product as a result of poorly informed decisions; 3 ○ Customers may incur charges due to being poorly informed of how a product works; ● Consumer understanding: ○ Customers may choose a product that is not suitable for their needs due to lack of understanding; ○ Customers may not fulfil their repayment obligations due to lack of understanding; ● Consumer support: ○ Customers may not be able to access the services offered by Clearpay designed to support customers with specific needs if they are not designed with accessibility considerations; 2.3 Examples of Characteristics of Vulnerability The FCAʼs FG21/1 guidance requires Clearpay to think about vulnerability on a spectrum of risk. Clearpay acknowledges that all customers are at risk of becoming vulnerable and this risk is increased by characteristics of vulnerability related to four key drivers, as set out below. Health – health conditions or illnesses that affect ability to carry out day-to-day tasks, such as: ● Physical disability ● Severe or long-term illness ● Hearing or visual impairment ● Mental health condition or disability ● Addiction ● Low mental capacity or cognitive disability Life events, such as: ● Retirement ● Bereavement ● Income shock ● Relationship breakdown ● Domestic abuse (including economic control) ● Caring responsibilities ● Other circumstances that affect peopleʼs experience of financial services eg. leaving care, migration or seeking asylum, human trafficking or modern slavery, convictions Resilience – low ability to withstand financial or emotional shocks, such as: ● Inadequate (outgoings exceed income) or erratic income ● Over-indebtedness ● Low savings ● Low emotional resilience Capability – low knowledge of financial matters or low confidence in managing money (financial capability), such as: ● Low knowledge or confidence managing finances 4 ● Poor Literacy or numeracy skills ● Poor English language skills ● Poor digital or technology skills ● Learning difficulties ● No or low access to help or support Clearpay recognises that it is a personʼs situation and specific barrier(s) to accessing services that may make them vulnerable, regardless of whether the customer lives with a disability, illness or diagnosis. Vulnerability can be a fluid state and customers may transition in and out of vulnerability during the term of their relationship with Clearpay. Impact of a change in circumstances: A change in personal circumstances can cause a customer to fall into a vulnerable situation - all the more so if the customer already has one or more risk characteristics. People are particularly at risk in their interaction with financial services when they experience a change in circumstances that often leads to financial shock. Examples of a change of circumstances can include: ● A change of job leading to different working patterns, making communication more difficult; ● A change in relationship status; ● An unwell child, relative or dependent; ● A change in physical health leading to reduced working hours; ● Bereavement of a loved one ● Moving house; or ● A change in mental health, making managing finances more difficult Impact of a change of income: A financial shock could be an unexpected large expense or loss of income. A reduction in income, or income volatility, can cause particular problems due to the ongoing commitment presented by many financial products. A reduction in the ability to meet these financial commitments can cause both immediate and longer-term problems. Examples of a change of income vulnerabilities include: ● Zero hours contracts (leading to fluctuating income); ● Redundancy; ● Retirement (leading to a drop in income); ● Statutory sick pay or maternity leave (where not paid in full). With all of this in mind, Clearpay has identified four core risks to customers who have characteristics of vulnerability that could cause harm if it does not act with the appropr

Document Details 32 Consumer Protection Policy | Square | Squareup Europe Ltd 3 Confidential Definitions Terminology Definition Financial Conduct The FCA is a financial regulatory body in the United Kingdom (“UK”), Authority (“FCA”) responsible for issuing SEL licence as an e-Money Institution (“EMI”). Financial Ombudsman The FOS is a government body established to serve as an impartial third Service (“FOS”) party to assess whether a financial institution, such as SEL, has treated a customer fairly within the context of a customer’s Complaint. Consumer Protection Risk Consumer Protection risk is the risk of unfair treatment or detriment to customers arising from a firm’s actions, business model, or practices. Customer For the purposes of this policy, a Customer is defined as all SEL Cash App users and SEL Square merchants, including potential customers, where appropriate. Vulnerable Customer As per FG21/1 guidance published by the FCA, a vulnerable customer is someone who, due to their personal circumstances, is especially susceptible to harm, particularly when a firm is not acting with appropriate levels of care. First Line of Defence The first line of defence comprises operational management responsible for identifying, managing, and reporting Consumer Protection risk concerns, including functions such as Marketing, Sales, Customer Success, and Complaints. Second Line of Defence The second line of defence consists of activities covered by Compliance and Enterprise Risk. This line of defence monitors and facilitates the implementation of effective risk management practices by operational management and assists the first line in reporting adequate risk related information up and down SEL. Complaint The FCA defines a complaint to be ‘any oral or written expression of dissatisfaction, whether justified or not, from, or on behalf of, any person Consumer Protection Policy | Square | Squareup Europe Ltd 4 Confidential about the provision of, or failure to provide, a financial service’, which: ● alleges that the complainant has suffered (or may suffer) financial loss, material distress or material inconvenience; and ● relates to an activity of that respondent or any other respondent with whom a respondent has some connection in providing financial services, which comes under the jurisdiction of the FOS. Note: Potential Customers can make a Complaint to SEL. Regulated Complaint Regulated complaints are issues concerning activity subject to oversight and control by the FCA, such as payment processing and money transfers. For Square, Regulated Complaints could involve issues related to payments processing like deactivation of accounts, frozen funds, fraud, or anything else to do with the processing, settlement of or access to customer’s funds. For Cash, all Complaints would be classed as Regulated Complaints. Unregulated Complaint Unregulated complaints are complaints that do not concern or relate to the provision of regulated financial services. Other services and products provided by Square such as its SaaS services, loans and hardware do not fall under the jurisdiction of a specific regulatory body or authority and are therefore, not regulated Complaints. Conflicts of Interest A Conflict of Interest is a situation where one or more persons or entities have competing interests, and serving one interest may involve detriment to another. A Conflict of Interest includes both an actual Conflict of Interest (i.e. a Conflict of Interest that has arisen) and a potential Conflict of Interest (i.e. a Conflict of Interest that may arise given particular facts and circumstances). It also includes a perceived Conflict of Interest (i.e. a situation which may give rise to the perception of a Conflict of Interest), even where a Conflict of Interest may not in fact exist. Consumer Protection Policy | Square | Squareup Europe Ltd 5 Confidential Roles and Responsibilities Role Responsibility The Board of Directors Responsible for promoting a culture of compliance, positive customer (“Board”) outcomes and providing direction to SEL on the delivery of the strategy and the management of Consumer Protection Risk. Regulatory Governance Responsible for reviewing any Consumer Protection Risk concerns, and Committee (“RGC”) Consumer Protection Key Risk Indicators (“KRI”) and Management Information (“MIˮ). First Line of Defence ● Responsible for effectively identifying Consumer Protection Risk in their respective areas and engaging in mitigating such risks. - ● Understanding consumer protection and embedding and maintaining business processes and controls in the daily operations of the business. ● Informing Compliance and Enterprise Risk of any concerns regarding Consumer Protection that are relevant to their functions in performing their duties. ● Assisting the business with promoting and embedding a customer centric culture. ● Tracking and understanding customer feedback relating to their function and taking appropriate actions accordingly. Staff Members Responsible for complying with this Consumer Protection Policy in their relevant business activities, ensuring that customers are adequately protected and that any concerns in relation to consumer protection are identified, managed, and reported. Compliance Responsible for monitoring compliance with this policy. Compliance will challenge, support and guide the business in the identification of Consumer Protection Risk issues. Consumer Protection Policy | Square | Squareup Europe Ltd 6 Confidential Enterprise Risk Responsible for the assessment, monitoring and reporting on regulatory risk, to include Consumer Protection Risk, on a quarterly basis. The output of such a review will be the Quarterly Risk Register, which is reported to the Board on the same cadence. Consumer Protection Policy | Square | Squareup Europe Ltd 7 Confidential 1. Introduction The Consumer Protection Policy (“Policyˮ) is applicable to Squareup Europe Ltd (“SELˮ) and outlines the fundamental principles to be followed that guide SEL in safeguarding its Customers. This Policy is an integral part of SELʼs risk management framework. Its primary objective is to aid SELʼs Board in assigning responsibilities and implementing controls aimed at mitigating Consumer Protection Risk. This is undertaken with the overarching aim of protecting Customers' interests in accordance with SEL's risk tolerance and regulatory guidelines. The SEL Head of Compliance is responsible for the management of this Policy. Any conflicts between this Policy and SEL's other legal or regulatory obligations should be referred immediately to the Head of Compliance for further assessment. 2. Purpose The purpose of this Policy is to demonstrate SEL's commitment to complying with all applicable laws and regulations concerning consumer protection. This Policy establishes a robust framework for identifying, managing, and mitigating Consumer Protection Risk. It ensures that SELʼs practices align with the standards set forth by the FCA, including the Consumer Duty, as well as industry best practices, to promote fair treatment, transparency, and positive outcomes for all Customers. 3. It outlines the approach to Consumer Protection, ensuring that all Staff Members understand their roles and responsibilities in fostering a culture of compliance and Customer care. 4. Policy Requirements 4.1 Regulatory Framework SEL is authorised by the FCA as an EMI pursuant to the Electronic Money Regulations 2011. Customer For the purposes of this policy, a customer is defined as all Cash App UK users including potential customers, where appropriate. Vulnerable Customer Policy | Cash App UK 4 Confidential and Not for Distribution 1. Introduction The Cash App UK (“Cash App”) service involves the provision of a digital-only peer-to-peer e-money wallet to customers. Cash App is required to ensure that every customer who has a characteristic or characteristics of vulnerability is treated fairly, and where possible, in accordance with their needs. This requirement is designed to ensure that Vulnerable Customers experience outcomes equal to those of other customers, and receive consistently fair treatment. 2. Purpose The purpose of the Vulnerable Customer Policy (the “Policy”) is to define Cash App UK’s obligations in engaging with Vulnerable Customers. It aims to ensure that Cash App delivers fair treatment, offers appropriate assistance, and maintains clear communication to protect and support customers who may be in vulnerable situations. 3. Scope This Policy is established by Cash App UK and is applicable to Cash App UK. No part of this Policy or its supporting documentation should be interpreted as contravening or superseding any other legal or regulatory requirements imposed upon Cash App UK. Any conflicts between this Policy and Cash App's other legal or regulatory obligations should be submitted immediately to the Head of Compliance for further evaluation. 4. Policy Requirements SEL is authorised by the FCA as an EMI pursuant to the Electronic Money Regulations 2011. The FCA’s Finalised Guidance on the Fair Treatment of Vulnerable Customers provides guidance on how authorised firms, including EMIs, should treat their Vulnerable Customers. EMIs are required to pay attention to the fair treatment of Vulnerable Customers across all products and services. In this Policy, Cash App demonstrates how its business model, the actions it takes, and its culture ensures the fair treatment of all customers. Vulnerable Customer Policy | Cash App UK 5 Confidential and Not for Distribution The FCA's approach to regulation is outcomes-focused and based on the Principles for Business (the Principles), with the key Principle underpinning the need for Cash App to take particular care in the treatment of Vulnerable Customers being Principle 6 – Customers' interests: a firm must pay due regard to the interests of customers and treat them fairly. In addition, Cash App is also required to take into account the FCA’s Consumer Duty guidance in its approach to Vulnerable Customers. Cash App should also comply at all times with its obligations under the Equality Act 2010, particularly in its approach to Vulnerable Customers. 4.1 Identifying Vulnerable Customers In order for the needs of Vulnerable Customers to be correctly addressed, it is important to be able to identify them and so Cash App is required to implement processes for Staff to identify vulnerable characteristics / behaviour and encourage disclosure. Cash App is also required to ensure that all relevant Staff receive training to understand the factors that can help to identify a Vulnerable Customer, encourage such disclosure and support the customer appropriately. Characteristics that can help identify a Vulnerable Customer can be divided into four key drivers of vulnerability. The characteristics associated with each driver are summarised below: Health Life Events Resilience Capability ● Physical disability ● Retirement ● Inadequate ● Low knowledge or ● Severe or long-term ● Bereavement (outgoings exceed confidence in illness ● Income Shock income) or erratic managing finances ● Hearing or visual ● Relationship income ● Poor literacy or impairment Breakdown ● Over- indebtedness numeracy skills ● Mental health ● Domestic abuse ● Low savings ● Poor English condition or (including economic ● Low emotional language skills disability control) resilience ● Poor or ● Addiction ● Caring non-existent digital responsibilities skills Vulnerable Customer Policy | Cash App UK 6 Confidential and Not for Distribution ● Low mental ● Other ● Learning difficulties capacity or circumstances that ● No or low access to cognitive disability affect people’s help or support experience of financial services e.g., leaving care, migration or seeking asylum, human trafficking or modern slavery, convictions Living with a disability, illness, or diagnosis does not in and of itself make someone vulnerable. It is a person's situation and barriers to accessing services that may make them vulnerable. Equally, a person may be vulnerable without any disability, illness or diagnosis, for example, if they are recently bereaved or are frail. Vulnerability can take many different forms, be temporary or permanent and the characteristics can be overlapping. Outside of circumstances where a customer directly informs Cash App that they have a condition, diagnosis, or circumstance that places them in a vulnerable position, there are some common customer behaviours which may indicate vulnerability, such as: ● Repeat requests for the same or similar information; (a sign that the customer is not retaining information) ● Difficulty understanding and/or engaging with information presented to them; ● More than average instances where the customer has to reset their Cash App password or other security details. When a customer is identified as vulnerable, Cash App has an obligation to: ● Provide the best possible service to Vulnerable Customer ● Provide reasonable assistance and care when required ● Be able to recognise and respond to the needs of Vulnerable Customers. Vulnerable Customer Policy | Cash App UK 7 Confidential and Not for Distribution 4.2 Understanding Cash App's Target Market Cash App should be aware of the situations and circumstances that may lead to a customer in their target market or customer base becoming vulnerable. Cash App is also generally available to the entire UK consumer base over the age of 18. Here are some generic vulnerability characteristics that may be seen across generally Cash App's target market: ● Financial Hardship: Individuals experiencing financial difficulties, such as those with low income, high debt levels, or who are unemployed or underemployed. ● Digital Literacy: Individuals who are not familiar with using digital financial services, including older adults or those with limited access to technology and the internet. ● Language Barriers: Non-native English speakers or those with limited proficiency in English, which can impact their ability to understand and use the app effectively. ● Cognitive Impairments: Individuals with mental health conditions, cognitive disabilities, or learning difficulties that may affect their ability to manage finances and use digital tools. ● Age: Older adults who might struggle with new technology can both face challenges. ● Health Issues: Individuals with serious or chronic health conditions that result in financial strain and added stress. ● Victims of Fraud or Scams: Those who have been previously targeted by financial fraud or scams, or are particularly vulnerable to such acti
documents.CP-034: Consumer Protection Policy Square | Squareup International Limited Last Approved: Mar 30, 2026 Effective Date: Mar 30, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: The Consumer Protection Policy (“Policyˮ) was established by Squareup International Limited (“SILˮ) as part of its duties as a regulated electronic money institution authorised by the Central Bank of Ireland (“CBIˮ). The primary purpose of the Policy is to facilitate the SIL Board (“the Boardˮ) dissemination of its responsibilities and controls with a view to mitigating Consumer Protection Risk, to ensure Consumersʼ interests are protected within the boundaries of SILʼs risk appetite and to align with regulatory guidance. The objectives of the Policy is to disseminate consumer protection principles throughout SIL, outline responsibilities for consumer protection, and promote a consumer-centric culture. This Policy is an integral part of SILʼs risk management framework. Scope: This Policy is established by SIL and is applicable to all SIL Staff and to outsourced service providers as listed in SILʼs Third Party Service Register. It is vital that all those in scope are aware of, and are committed to complying with the requirements set out in this Policy and the applicable Consumer Protection laws and regulations. Definitions: Terminology Definition Complaint An expression of dissatisfaction by an individual, business entity, or someone authorised to act on their behalf, with a SIL product, process, service, decision, or interaction in which a response or resolution is explicitly or implicitly requested. Complaints may also include allegations that SIL engaged in wrongful or suspicious conduct. Note: Potential Consumers can make a complaint to SIL. Conflicts of A Conflict of Interest is a situation where one or more persons or entities Interest have competing interests and the serving of one interest may involve detriment to another. 1 A Conflict of Interest includes both an actual Conflict of Interest (i.e. a Conflict of Interest that has arisen) and a potential Conflict of Interest (i.e. a Conflict of Interest that may arise given particular facts and circumstances). It also includes a perceived Conflict of Interest (i.e. a situation which may give rise to the perception of a Conflict of Interest), even where a Conflict of Interest may not in fact exist. Consumer(s), or For the purposes of this Policy Consumer(s) are defined as SIL Square Squareup merchants that are receiving regulated services (i.e. payment processing) in International Ireland, including where appropriate former and potential consumers 1 2 Limited SILʼs Consumer(s) Consumer A mistake caused by a person, system issue or process gap that has Impacting Errors impacted or may potentially impact negatively on the SIL Consumer. It can (“Errorsˮ) also result in either a financial or reputational loss for the Company. Consumer The risk SIL poses to its SIL Consumers through its direct interaction with Protection Risk them as well as the risk posed to SIL Consumers stemming from SILʼs strategy, business model, culture, governance, internal structures, systems and processes and behaviours. First Line of First Line of Defence (“1LODˮ): Departmental management and staff Defence responsible for identifying and managing consumer protection risk within their areas of accountability. Controls ensure that risk management and compliance are embedded in all relevant decisions and operations.This includes such functions as Marketing, Sales, Customer Success, Compliance Operations, Credit and Fraud Risk Operations and and Complaints Management. Record A Record means any document, file or information (whether stored electronically or otherwise) and which is capable of being reproduced in a legible form. Second Line of Second Line of Defence (“2LODˮ): The Risk and Compliance teams develop Defence and monitor the implementation of the ERMF. They support the first line of defence in executing risk management responsibilities, ensuring consistency in risk application and measurement. Staff All full-time and part-time employees and fixed-term and contingent workers including interns, temps, consultants, vendors, third-party providers, and contractors. Consumer in A consumer that is a natural person and whose personal circumstances, Vulnerable whether permanent or temporary, make that consumer especially Circumstances susceptible to harm, particularly where a regulated entity is not acting with 1 A cohort of SILʼs Square merchants in Spain that receive regulated services in Spain are afforded certain consumer protection rights. Please see Appendix A Squareup International Spanish Consumer Protection Requirements for further details. 2 SILʼs Square merchants in France do not fall under the definition of Consumer and therefore this Policy does not apply to merchants based in France. 2 the appropriate levels of care. Vulnerable circumstances means the personal circumstances that make someone vulnerable, as described above. 2. Policy Requirements 2.1. Regulatory Requirements The following is an overview of the key conduct and consumer protection related regulations and guidance applicable to SIL. Consumer Protection Code 2025 (‘the Codeʼ) The Code consists of the two regulations: the Standards for Business Regulations, which set out key principles that govern the Code; and the Consumer Protection Regulations, which set out detailed conduct of business rules. SIL must follow the applicable requirements within the Code when providing financial products and services to Consumers in Ireland. In general, these consumer protection requirements should be applied proportionately, with a focus on delivering fair Consumer outcomes. Guidance to Support the Implementation of the CodeThere are three sets of CBI guidance documents to support the implementation of the Codeʼs requirements. 1. Guidance on Securing Consumersʼ Interests sets out what SIL needs to consider, the actions to be taken and importantly the mindset SIL should have towards our Consumers, to effectively comply with our obligations under the Standards for Business to secure consumersʼ interests. 2. Guidance on Protecting Consumers in Vulnerable Circumstances sets out the approach to vulnerability under the Code, and expectations as to how to meet the relevant obligations under the Code. It acts as a support to firms in adopting the right mindset and culture to effectively support Consumers in Vulnerable Circumstances. 3. General Code Guidance helps in the implementation of the broader requirements of the Code. Consumer Protection Risk Assessment (‘CPRAʼ) The PRA model is used by the CBI to assess how firms are managing the risks they pose to Consumers and to ensure firms have appropriate risk management frameworks to deliver the right Consumer outcomes. The CPRA model also forms the basis of SILʼs Consumer Protection Risk Management Framework. Payment Services Directive Council Directive 2015/2366/EC, the Payment Services Directive 2 (“PSD2ˮ) is an EU Directive, administered by the European Commission to regulate payment services throughout the European Union and European Economic Area. The conduct rules set out in PSD2 include transparency of 3 conditions and information requirements. Such requirements are designed to ensure that payment service providers provide merchants with clear information that is proportionate to their needs to enable them to make well informed choices within the payment services market. PSD2 also sets out the rights and obligations in relation to the provision and use of payment services. It regulates the authorisation and execution of payment transactions, the ability to impose charges, requirements relating to refunds, and requirements relating to non-execution or defective or late execution of payment transactions as well as complaints requirements. 2.2. Elements of SILʼs Consumer Protection Risk Management Framework SILʼs framework for consumer protection risk management, available here, is designed to mitigate consumer protection risk in line with SILʼs Risk Appetite Statement. It is a living document and should be reviewed periodically. An image depicting the framework is below. 2.3. Standards for Business - Key Principles SIL must at all times: (a) secure its Consumersʼ interests. This applies to SIL in its overall conduct of its affairs, and is central to its relationship with its Consumers. Read here for more details; 4 (b) act honestly and with integrity. For example SIL must appropriately manage Conflicts of Interest as per Blockʼs Global Code of Business Conduct and Ethics and SILʼs Conflict of Interest Policy; allow Consumers to make free informed decisions without pressure; Staff must operate without bias and must protect SILʼs and its Consumersʼ assets and information from misuse. (c) act with due skill, care and diligence. For example Leads and managers must have appropriate knowledge of activities for which they are responsible and the associated risks; SIL must comply with all applicable financial services legislation. (d) act in the best interests of Consumers and treat them fairly and professionally. This means SIL must provide clear, relevant information to Consumers in a timely manner; gather necessary Consumer information and assess their needs and circumstances; be transparent about the advantages and disadvantages of SILʼs offerings; unfair treatment must be avoided; and maintain reasonable access by Consumers to our services. (e) inform Consumers effectively. For example material features of SILʼs regulated services must be reasonably understandable and key information must be brought to the Consumersʼ attention, such as our fees. SILʼs approach to communication should be less about simply providing information to meet SILʼs regulatory disclosure obligations and more about seeking to support practical understanding by the Consumer. documents.CP-035: Australian Financial Services License AFSL Representatives Standard Square | Square AU Pty Ltd Last Approved: Dec 18, 2025 Effective Date: Dec 18, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: Square AU Pty Ltd (“Square AUˮ) is an Australian Financial Services License (“AFSLˮ) holder License Number: 513929. This AFSL Representatives Standard (“Standardˮ) outlines Square AUʼs approach to oversight and training of Representatives. The CCO Square AU is responsible for management of this Standard. This Standard outlines the requirements and procedures that Square AU has established to ensure the effective oversight and training of its representatives under its AFSL in the provision of its licensed financial services product). Square AU is committed to upholding regulatory standards and maintaining compliance and competency in line with our regulatory obligations. . Square AU acknowledges that its representatives play a critical role in delivering compliant and responsible financial services. This Standard aims to ensure that all representatives are adequately trained, monitored and supervised, enabling them to meet their obligations under the Corporations Act 2001, the conditions of the AFSL, and ASICʼs regulatory standards. Scope: This Standard is established by Square AU and is applicable to Employees of Square AU and sets out the approach to the monitoring, supervision and training of its representatives. Definitions: Terminology Definition Australian A license issued by the Australian Securities and Investments Commission Financial (“ASICˮ) that allows an entity to conduct financial services business in Services Licence Australia. Holders of an AFSL are required to meet specific legal and (“AFSLˮ) regulatory obligations under the Corporations Act 2001. Representatives All full-time and part-time employees and fixed-term and contingent workers of Square AU including interns, temps, consultants, vendors, third-party providers, and contractors.This can encompass anyone acting on behalf of the licensee (“Square AUˮ) in the provision of financial services. Financial Product A recommendation or statement of opinion provided to clients to influence Advice their decision regarding a financial product. Financial product advice can be general or personal, with differing regulatory requirements. Financial Legislation and regulations governing the provision of financial services in Services Law Australia, primarily under the Corporations Act 2001. These laws include requirements for AFSL holders and their representatives to act in the best interests of clients, maintain competency, and ensure compliance. General Financial General product advice refers to information and facts about financial Product Advice products that is not tailored to an individual's specific circumstances. Under Square AUʼs AFSL, representatives are only authorised to provide general financial product advice. Personal Personal financial advice is advice that takes into account one or more of the Financial Product clientʼs objectives, financial situation, or needs — or where it would be Advice reasonable to expect that the adviser has considered these matters. This is prohibited under terms of Square AUʼs AFSL. Responsible A person identified by the AFSL holder as responsible for overseeing specific Manager financial services provided by the organisation. Responsible Managers are subject to compliance and competency standards as defined by ASIC and are accountable for ensuring the organisationʼs adherence to regulatory obligations. Training Register A formal record that tracks training undertaken by representatives, including course details, duration, accreditation, and completion dates. 2. Standard Requirements 2.1 Square AUʼs Responsibilities towards Representatives Square AU is responsible for ensuring that all Representatives are competent, adequately trained, and properly supervised to perform their roles. This includes ensuring Representatives: ● Act efficiently, honestly and fairly in accordance with s912A1a of the Corporations Act; ● Comply with all relevant financial services laws, ASIC Regulatory Guides, and internal policies and procedures; and ● Maintain the skills, knowledge, and competence necessary to discharge their responsibilities effectively. Square AU must establish and maintain a structured training and competency program that ensures Representatives receive initial and ongoing training appropriate to their role and the complexity of the financial services provided. This includes, where relevant, training on topics such as advice and disclosure obligations, financial crimes, data and information security, anti-bribery and corruption, conflicts of interest, complaints handling, and breach reporting. Ongoing oversight and monitoring will be conducted through supervision, quality assurance reviews, and periodic performance reviews to ensure that Representatives continue to meet the required standards. Any performance issues, breaches, or concerns relating to a Representativeʼs conduct or competence will be investigated and addressed promptly in accordance with Block Inc. global Code of Conduct and, if a regulatory breach is identified, Square AUʼs Breach Reporting Policy. 2.2 Representativesʼ Roles in Providing a Licensed Financial Product Representatives play a critical role in upholding our AFSL obligations when interacting with customers, providing information or advice about financial products, and facilitating product access. Every representative must understand the scope of their authority, the limits of the advice they can provide, and the disclosure requirements that apply to each interaction. In particular, representatives must: ● Act efficiently, honestly and fairly when dealing with clients. ● Ensure that any financial service provided is within the scope of the AFSL and their individual authorisation. ● Comply with requirements relating to advice delivery (including general and personal advice distinctions). ● Provide customers with timely and accurate disclosure documents, such as Product Disclosure Statements PDS, when required. ● Escalate or refer any matter that exceeds their authorisation or training to an appropriately qualified person. 2.2.1 General vs Personal Product Advice Under the Corporations Act 2001 Cth, financial product advice may be either general advice or personal advice. ● General advice is financial product advice that does not take into account a customerʼs personal objectives, financial situation, or needs. It may include factual information, opinions, or recommendations about a financial product, but it must be accompanied by a General Advice Warning whenever it is provided to a retail client. This warning makes it clear that the advice is general in nature and that the customer should consider whether it is appropriate for them before acting on it. ● Personal advice is tailored advice that does take into account one or more of the customerʼs personal circumstances (objectives, financial situation, or needs) or would reasonably be expected to do so. Personal advice cannot be provided under Square AUʼs AFSL. Square AU ensures that all Representatives dealing with the provision of licensed financial services understand the distinction between factual information, general advice, and personal advice to prevent inadvertent provision of personal advice without proper authorisation. Where general advice is given, the required general advice warning must be communicated at the time. When giving general advice, the Representative must inform the customer that: ● The advice has been prepared without taking into account the customerʼs objectives, financial situation or needs; and ● The customer should consider whether the advice is appropriate for them and read the relevant disclosure documents (such as a PDS. 2.2.2 Provision of Product Disclosure Statement PDS A Product Disclosure Statement PDS for our licensed financial products is made publicly available on the Square AU website, is agreed to by Squareʼs customers, and can be accessed by customers at any time. Representatives may, where appropriate, direct customers to the online PDS or provide a copy upon request. Square AU ensures that the PDS is readily accessible so customers can review key information about our financial products before making a decision. 2.3 Training of Representatives Square AU is committed to ensuring that all Representatives are competent and adequately trained to perform their roles in compliance with the AFSL and applicable financial services laws. During onboarding, all new Representatives must complete mandatory induction and training covering Square AUʼs AFSL authorisations and obligations under the Corporations Act 2001. The onboarding program also includes training on key compliance policies and procedures such as conduct, conflicts of interest, complaints handling, breach reporting, and customer advice. Representatives are instructed on how to provide general advice and factual information appropriately, with a clear understanding of the distinction between general and personal advice when discussing any licensed financial products with customers or prospective customers. Completion of onboarding training is mandatory prior to engaging in any regulated activity. Representatives also receive training on regulated products or services at onboarding and prior to the launch of any new products. 2.3.1 Continuous Professional Development CPD Square AU maintains a formal annual training and professional development program to ensure that Representativesʼ knowledge and competence remain current. Ongoing training includes annual refresher courses on AFSL obligations and conduct, in addition to other annual compliance training modules. Targeted training is also delivered following any changes to internal policies or procedures, or where risks, incidents, or thematic reviews identify the need for additional capabil
documents.CP-036: Consumer Protection Policy Square AU Last Approved: Dec 18, 2025 Effective Date: Dec 18, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: Square AU Pty Ltd (“Square AUˮ) is committed to the fair treatment of customers and the delivery of positive customer outcomes. This Consumer Protection Policy (“Policyˮ) outlines the principles, responsibilities, and procedures that govern how Square AU manages consumer protection risk in the context of its payment services business in Australia. The Policy forms part of Square AUʼs broader risk and compliance framework and is designed to ensure compliance with Australian Consumer Law, the Corporations Act 2001, the ASIC Act 2001, and ASIC Regulatory Guides. Square AU recognises that the regulatory landscape imposes both legal obligations and best practice standards to ensure that financial products and services are safe, fair, and accessible. This Policy establishes a clear framework for identifying, managing, and mitigating consumer protection risks across all regulated and non-regulated activities under Square AUʼs Australian products and services, outlining the responsibilities of the business, staff, and compliance teams to ensure all customers are treated fairly and in accordance with legislation. Scope: This Policy applies to all Square AU staff members, including employees, contractors, temporary workers, and third-party service providers engaged in delivering services to Australian customers. It covers all regulated activities and interactions where there is potential for consumer detriment, including marketing, sales, customer service, complaints handling, and product development. The Policy does not apply to Square products that are distributed exclusively through third-party arrangements where Square does not control the productʼs features, pricing, or delivery. 1 Definitions: Terminology Definition Australian ASIC is Australia's corporate, markets, financial services and consumer Securities and credit regulator. It is responsible for administering and enforcing the Investments Australian Consumer Law as it applies to financial products and services. Commission ASIC Australian Financial AFCA is the external dispute resolution scheme for financial services in Complaints Australia. It provides fair, independent and effective solutions for financial Authority AFCA disputes between consumers and financial firms. Australian The Australian Consumer Law Schedule 2 to the Competition and Consumer Law Consumer Act 2010) sets out consumersʼ statutory rights and guarantees, ACL prohibits misleading or unfair conduct, and provides remedies for detriment. It underpins Square AUʼs obligations to treat customers fairly. Consumer Consumer Protection risk is the risk of unfair treatment or detriment to protection risk customers arising from a firm's actions, business models, or practices. Customer For the purposes of this policy, a Customer is defined as all Square AU users receiving regulated services (i.e. payment processing) in Australia, including potential customers, where appropriate. Also referred to internally as sellers. Vulnerable A vulnerable customer is someone who, due to their personal customer circumstances, is especially susceptible to detriment, particularly when a firm is not acting with appropriate levels of care. This aligns with ASIC's regulatory guidance on vulnerability. First Line of The first line of defence comprises operational management responsible for Defence identifying, managing, and reporting Consumer Protection risk concerns, including functions such as Marketing, Sales, Customer Success, and Complaints. Second Line of The second line of defence consists of activities covered by Compliance Defence: and Enterprise Risk. This line of defence monitors and facilitates the implementation of effective risk management practices by operational management and assists the first line in reporting adequate risk related information up and down Square AU. Complaint An expression of dissatisfaction made to or about an organization, related to its products, services, staff or the handling of a complaint, where a response or resolution is explicitly or implicitly expected or legally required. 2 Terminology Definition Regulated Activity Regulated activity refers to an activity or service that is subject to regulatory oversight and control by ASIC. For Square AU, this includes financial services provided under its Australian Financial Services License AFSL. Conflict of Interest A Conflict of Interest arises when competing interests could lead to detriment to one party. This includes actual, potential, and perceived conflicts. Consumer Automatic legal rights provided by the ACL. They apply to all eligible Guarantees consumer purchases regardless of what the business says. Warranties Promises made by a business or manufacturer about a productʼs quality or performance, usually for a set period of time. Warranties do not override consumer guarantees. 2. Policy Requirements 2.1 General Principles Under Australiaʼs consumer protection laws and regulatory guidance, financial services providers must act fairly, honestly and efficiently when dealing with customers. These principles underpin all aspects of product design, disclosure, sales, and ongoing customer support to ensure customers are protected from harm and treated with integrity. 2.1.1 A Customer Centric Culture Square AUʼs culture is rooted in its mission to create economic empowerment and a financial system that is accessible, ethical, and customer-focused. Fair treatment of customers is a fundamental aspect of how Square AU conducts business. This culture is embedded in day-to-day decision making, staff training, and the continuous review of products and services to ensure they meet the needs and expectations of customers, fostering trust and long-term relationships. 2.1.2 Delivering Fair Outcomes Square AU prioritises delivering fair, accessible, and inclusive services to all customers. We strive to understand customer needs and design products that meet those needs effectively, while communicating clearly, accurately, and honestly. Our approach emphasises respectful and efficient handling of customer queries and issues, ensuring that services are reliable, high quality, 3 and aligned with customer expectations. We are also committed to resolving complaints promptly and fairly, supporting trust and confidence in Square AUʼs products and services. These commitments extend to all Square AU users, including small businesses, sole traders, and marginalised communities. 2.1.3 Regulatory Framework and Governance Square AU operates within a comprehensive Australian regulatory framework, including the Australian Securities and Investments Commission Act 2001, Corporations Act 2001 (and associated Design and Distribution Obligations), Australian Consumer Law, Payment Systems Regulation) Act 1998, Privacy Act 1988, and Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorism Financing Act 2006. To ensure robust governance, the Square AU Board maintains ultimate responsibility for approving the consumer protection strategy and risk appetite, allocating adequate resources, and overseeing policy implementation. The Board is regularly informed of consumer protection activities, significant complaints, and systemic issues. Governance committees, including a dedicated Risk and Compliance Committee, Product Governance Committee, and Consumer Outcomes Committee, meet on a regular basis and are accountable for reviewing regulatory compliance, consumer outcomes, and internal policy adherence. Square AU applies a “three lines of defenceˮ model to consumer protection risk management. The first line consists of business units and front-line staff tasked with executing controls and delivering products and services in line with policy requirements. The second line comprises Risk and Compliance functions, which independently monitor, provide oversight, and assist in policy development and risk assessment. The third line is Internal Audit, providing independent assurance of system effectiveness and adherence. 2.2 Consumer Guarantees Under the Australian Consumer Law ACL, consumers are automatically entitled to certain guarantees when they buy goods and services. They are designed to ensure fair treatment for consumers and provide remedies when goods or services fail to meet expectations. For goods (such as Square terminals and 3rd Party hardware sold by Square), consumer guarantees include that: ● Products are of acceptable quality (safe, durable, free from defects, acceptable in appearance); ● Products are fit for their intended purpose; ● Products match their description or any demonstration model; ● The business has the right to sell the product (clear title); ● Spare parts and repair facilities are reasonably available. 4 For services (such as the provision of payment processing), guarantees include that: ● Services are provided with due care and skill; ● Services are fit for the purpose the customer made known; ● Services are provided within a reasonable time, if no time was agreed. 2.2.1 Consumer Guarantees and Warranties All warranty and consumer guarantee related claims are assessed on a case-by-case basis, and Square AU is committed to acting in accordance with legislation regarding the handling of these matters. Square AU ensures that customers are provided with clear, accurate and timely information about their rights and available remedies. Where uncertainty arises about the application of consumer guarantees, employees are encouraged to seek internal guidance to ensure consistent, fair and legally compliant outcomes. 2.3 Vulnerable and Hardship-Affected Customers Square AU is committed to identifying and supporting customers who may be vulnerable due to health issues, disability, age, financial stress, family violence, literacy or language barriers, cultural background, or life events like bereavement or unemployment. The Square
documents.CP-037: Responsible Managers Standard Square | Square AU Pty Ltd Last Approved: Dec 18, 2025 Effective Date: Dec 18, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: Square AU Pty Ltd (“Square AUˮ) is an Australian Financial Services License AFSL holder License Number: 513929. This Responsible Managers Standard (“Standardˮ) outlines Square AUʼs approach to managing Responsible Managers. The CCO Square AU is responsible for the management of this Standard. The Responsible Managers Standard for Square AU Pty Limited outlines the policies, processes, and practices established to maintain and enhance the competency of Responsible Managers RMs under the Australian Financial Services Licence AFSL. This document is designed to ensure that Square AU's RMs meet and sustain the educational, experiential, and ethical standards set by ASIC Regulatory Guide 105 RG 105, which governs the organisational competency obligations for financial services providers in Australia. Square conducts business in compliance with all applicable laws and regulations. The purpose of this Standard is to establish a comprehensive framework for the nomination, training, and continuous development of Responsible Managers at Square AU with an objective of ensuring the following: ● Competency Maintenance: Square AUʼs RMs possess and continually enhance the necessary skills, knowledge, and experience to meet regulatory and operational requirements. ● Compliance with RG 105 Compliance with ASICʼs RG 105 organisational competence requirements, providing for Square AUʼs capability to provide financial services as outlined in its AFSL. ● Clear Governance: Defined roles and responsibilities for the Board, Training Officer, RMs, and Compliance Team, supporting a cohesive approach to training and compliance oversight. This Standard serves as a guide to provide all relevant personnel understand their obligations and are equipped to perform their roles effectively in line with regulatory expectations. Scope: This Standard is established by Square and is applicable to relevant employees of Square AU and sets out the approach to training of responsible managers. Definitions: Terminology Definition Responsible A person identified by the AFSL holder as responsible for overseeing Manager (“RMˮ) specific financial services provided by the organisation. Responsible Managers are subject to compliance and competency standards as defined by Australian Securities and Investment Commission (“ASICˮ) and are accountable for ensuring the organisationʼs adherence to regulatory obligations. Organisational The standard of skills, knowledge, and experience required by an AFS Competence licensee to provide the financial services specified in their licence. This is achieved through the appointment of qualified Responsible Managers. Regulatory Guide An ASIC guide detailing the obligations and requirements for AFS licensees 105 RG 105 to demonstrate organisational competence, primarily by appointing suitably qualified Responsible Managers. Australian A license issued by the Australian Securities and Investments Commission Financial Services (“ASICˮ) that allows an entity to conduct financial services business in Licence (“AFSˮ Australia. Holders of an AFSL are required to meet specific legal and Licence) regulatory obligations under the Corporations Act 2001. Key Person A requirement that certain individuals RMs named in an AFS licence Condition application or approval process are necessary for the licence to remain active. Any change in key personnel requires ASIC notification. Financial Services Legislation and regulations governing the provision of financial services in Laws Australia, primarily under the Corporations Act 2001. These laws include requirements for AFSL holders and their representatives to act in the best interests of clients, maintain competency, and ensure compliance. Training and ASIC requirements for the education, training, and experience RMs need to Competency demonstrate. These include qualifications in finance, experience in the Standards relevant financial services, and often, ongoing professional development. Risk Management Identification, analysis, and mitigation of risks in financial services activities. RMs play a central role in ensuring that adequate risk management processes are in place. ASIC Notification Obligations for RMs and AFS licensees to inform ASIC of significant Requirements changes, such as changes in key personnel or new business activities under the licence. 2. Standard Requirements 2.1. Responsible Managers The Board of Square AU must nominate and appoint Responsible Managers on whom it will depend for its organisational competency. In doing so, the Board of Square AU must have regard to the nature, scale and complexity of its business. The Board of Square AU should only nominate and appoint those people who are: most responsible for the provision of services provided by Square AU under the AFSL; and are directly responsible for day-to-day business decisions about the ongoing provision of services by Square AU (including its compliance arrangements). Fit and Proper A Fit and Proper person is an individual who possesses the competence, character, integrity, and judgment necessary to perform the duties of a Responsible Manager RM under an Australian Financial Services Licence AFSL in accordance with the Corporations Act 2001 Cth and ASICʼs regulatory expectations. Being fit and proper means that the individual has demonstrated: ● Honesty, integrity and reputation ● Competence and Capability ● Financial Soundness ● Sound judgement and accountability Suitability of an individual based on the above characteristics can be determined by way of background screening and assessment as detailed in the following section. 2.2 Initial assessment Before Square AU engages a person to become a Responsible Manager, Square AU must obtain or be provided with: ● a copy of the personʼs resume with details of relevant expertise, training and experience, knowledge and skills; and ● a correct copy of the personʼs educational qualifications; and ● Background screening including but not limited to: ○ Criminal History - only if the last check was longer than 12 months ago ○ Global Sanctions, Adverse Media, AML Anti Money Laundering) and PEP Politically Exposed Persons) ○ ASIC Authorised Representative Register ○ ASIC Banned & Disqualified Register ○ APRA Disqualification ○ Bankruptcy Register Search ○ Civil Litigation Search In considering whether there is any reason to believe a person is not of good fame and character, as required by an RM, Square AU must also have regard to: ● any conviction of the person, within 10 years before the application was made, for serious fraud; ● whether the person has previously held an Australian financial service licence that was suspended or cancelled; and ● whether a banning order or disqualification order under the Corporations Act 2001 has previously been made against the person. 2.3 Education and experience requirements In accordance with RG 105, Square AU must ensure that its Responsible Managers collectively demonstrate the appropriate knowledge, skills, and experience to maintain the organisationʼs competence under its AFSL. The combined competency of the Responsible Managers must reflect the nature, scale, and complexity of the financial services provided by Square AU. Each Responsible Manager must possess relevant qualifications and experience in their area of oversight, supported by ongoing professional development to ensure knowledge remains current and aligned with regulatory expectations. ASIC considers that there will normally be a number of options open to a licensee to ensure its Responsible Managers have the education and experience needed for their roles. In summary, the alternatives for the requisite knowledge requirements are outlined below: Option Knowledge component Skills component (qualifications, training, etc.) (experience) Option 1 Meet widely adopted and 3 years relevant experience (see ASIC RG 10555RG relevant industry standards or over past 5 years 105.57 relevant standards set by Australian Prudential Regulatory Authority (“APRAˮ) Option 2 Be individually assessed by an 5 years relevant experience (see ASIC RG 105.58RG authorised assessor as having over past 8 years 105.60 relevant knowledge equivalent to a diploma Option 3 Hold a university degree in a 3 years relevant experience (see ASIC RG 105.61RG relevant discipline and over past 5 years 105.65 complete a relevant short industry course Option 4 Hold a relevant industry- or 3 years relevant experience (see ASIC RG 105.66RG product-specific qualification over past 5 years 105.70 equivalent to a diploma or higher Option 5 If not relying on Options 14, the Responsible Manager must (see ASIC RG 105.71RG have appropriate knowledge and skills for their role. See ASIC 105.74 RG 105.74 for more information. It should be noted that, while foreign qualifications will be relevant, they will almost certainly not have addressed Australian regulatory requirements or features of the financial services industry peculiar to Australia (such as taxation, superannuation and other retirement savings vehicles). To remedy this, ASIC will generally expect Responsible Managers of AFS licensees that wish to provide financial product advice based on foreign qualifications to undertake an approved short industry course to become familiar with Australian requirements. 2.4 Nomination and appointment As at the date of the most recent review of this Standard, the Board has approved and appointed the following persons as Responsible Managers: ● Catherine Katie Cover ● Lance Robert Howard Any removal of RMs or nomination of new RMs will be promptly recorded with ASIC in accordance with RG 105. 2.5 Training Training for all Responsible Managers will be developed and conducted: ● Before Square AU offers a regulated product; and ● within 3 months aft
documents.CP-038: Commercial Telemarketing Policy Square | Square Capital, LLC | Credit Card Last Approved: October 3, 2025 Effective Date: October 3, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: Square Capital, LLC, ("Square Capital" or "the Company") seeks to comply with all requirements that stem from regulations that govern commercial lending activities. This Commercial Telemarketing Policy ("Policy") was created and adopted with the sole focus on Square Capital's commercial credit card program. The Chief Compliance Officer ("CCO") oversees the development and implementation of this Policy and has delegated ownership of this Policy document to the Square Head of Compliance. This Policy is established by the Square Capital Team and is applicable to all commercial credit products offered by Square Capital. All employees, fixed-term and contingent workers who support the operations are covered by this Policy, and including interns, temporary workers, consultants, vendors, third-party providers and contractors. Square Capital conducts business in compliance with all applicable laws and regulations. The purpose of this Policy is to further Square Capital's compliance with the Telephone Consumer Protection Act ("TCPA"), the Truth In Caller ID Act, the Telemarketing and Consumer Fraud and Abuse Prevention Act ("TCFAPA"), and Federal Trade Commission's ("FTC") Telemarketing Sales Rule ("TSR") as applicable. Square Capital is fully committed to complying with these laws and regulations and to upholding business requirements that do not run counter to their requirements. Scope: This Policy applies to all outbound and inbound Telemarketing calls and texts from Square Capital related to Square Capital's commercial lending programs. This also includes certain social media posts and faxes of a marketing or sales nature. Definitions: Terminology Definition Automatic Equipment which has the capacity to store or produce telephone numbers to Telephone be called, using a random or sequential number generator, and to dial such Dialing System numbers. (“ATDSˮ) or Auto Dialer The term can include smartphones, tablets, VoIP phones, calling apps, texting apps, and phones that are not rotary-dialˮ. Clear and A notice that would be apparent to the reasonable consumer, separate and Conspicuous distinguishable from the advertising copy or other disclosures. For facsimiles (faxes), in order to be considered "Clear and Conspicuous " the notice must be placed at either the top or bottom of the facsimile. Established A prior or existing relationship between Square Capital and a residential or Business business subscriber based on the subscriberʼs purchase or transaction with Relationship Square Capital within the 18 months immediately preceding the date of the telephone call or on the basis of the subscriberʼs inquiry or application regarding products or services offered by Square Capital within the three months immediately preceding the date of the call, and neither party has previously terminated the relationship. An individual may reasonably expect that an affiliate is included in an Established Business Relationship based on products offered or the identity of the affiliate. Material Information that is likely to affect a personʼs choice of, or conduct regarding, Information goods or services or a charitable contribution. Prior Express A written agreement, bearing the signature of the person that authorizes Written Consent Square Capital to call the person regarding advertisements or telemarketing (“PEWCˮ) and includes the telephone number to which the authorization is given. The written agreement shall include a Clear and Conspicuous disclosure informing the person signing that: ● By signing the agreement, such person authorizes Square Capital to make telemarketing calls using an automatic telephone dialing system or an artificial or prerecorded voice; and ● The person is not required to sign the agreement or enter into such an agreement as a condition of purchasing any property, goods, or services. The term “signatureˮ includes an electronic or digital form of signature, to the extent that such form of signature is recognized as a valid signature under applicable federal law or state contract law. Telephone The initiation of a telephone call or message for the purpose of encouraging Solicitation the purchase or rental of, or investment in, property, goods, or services, which is transmitted to any person, but such term does not include a call or message: ● to any person with that personʼs prior express invitation or permission; or ● to any person with whom the caller has an Established Business Relationship. There are instances in which the protections and requirements of TCPA do not apply to a Telephone Solicitation, given the existence of one of the above exceptions. For questions about whether one of these enumerated exceptions applies, please consult with the Testing and Monitoring Team (“TMTˮ). Unsolicited Any material advertising the commercial availability or quality of any Advertisement property, goods, or services which is transmitted to any person without that personʼs prior express invitation or permission, in writing or otherwise. 2.
