Square Capital, LLC Requirements The TCPA was implemented by the Federal Communication Commission FCC Restrictions on Telemarketing, Telephone Solicitation and Facsimile Advertising regulation FCC Rule) and addresses the growing number of complaints pertaining to telephone marketing calls. The law protects the privacy interests of residential telephone subscribers by placing certain restrictions on the use of unsolicited phone calls made with automatic dialing services. Square Capital does not engage in these types of phone calls. In addition to regulatory guidance on its applicability, the scope of the TCPA includes mobile phones, SMS texts, marketing phone calls initiated by a person, and calls to both consumers and commercial entities. Additionally, TCPA granted the FTC and the Federal Communications Commission FCC the power to create a national do not call DNC list and related DNC lists. The TCFAPA, enforced through the FTC's TSR, prohibits certain Telemarketing practices, and provides Consumers a right of privacy from unwanted Telemarketing calls. As enacted, the FCC Rule and TSR also apply to sales calls to commercial entities. While the scope of the TSR is strictly confined to interstate telemarketing activities as defined under the TSR, Square Capital will adhere to the applicable provisions across all telemarketing activities. All requirements named in this section apply to any person who engages in covered marketing practices occurring via telephone, cell phone (call or text), social media post, or fax machine, except where noted. 2.1. Sales Calls, Faxes, and Computer Communications It is the policy of Square Capital to comply with all telemarketer requirements, including those outlined by the TCPA, the TCFAPA, and the Truth In Caller ID Act. Accordingly, Square Capital, where engaged in solicitation by telephone, will: ● Provide certain Material Information, in a clear and conspicuous manner, with each telemarketing call prior to a personʼs consent to pay for goods or services offered over the telephone. This includes the following: ○ The total costs to purchase, receive, or use the goods or services that are the subject of the sales offer; ○ Any material restrictions, limitations, conditions to purchase, receive, use the goods, or services that are the subject of the sales offer; or ○ Any material aspect of the performance, efficacy, nature, central characteristics of goods or services that are the subject of a sales offer. ● Give certain prompt oral disclosures in a Clear and Conspicuous manner at the outset of telemarketing calls including: ○ The identity of the seller; ○ That the purpose of the call is to sell goods or services; and ○ The nature of the goods or services being offered; ● Provide, in the event of upselling, the above oral disclosures where: ○ Any of the information is different from those in the initial disclosures; or ○ No disclosures were required based upon the initial reason for the call (i.e. a non-sales customer service call); ● Not in any way misrepresent Material Information; ● Not engage in abusive calling practices including using threats, intimidation, profane or obscene language; ● Not initiate telephone solicitation to any residential telephone number before the hour of 8 a.m. or after 9 p.m. local time at the called partyʼs location; ● Transmit caller identification information for every call covered by this Policy. Caller identification information must include: ○ Either, the calling party number or the automatic numbering information; and ○ When available by the Telemarketerʼs carrier, the name of the Telemarketer. ○ It is permissible to substitute (for the name and phone number used, or billed for making the call) the name of Square Capital and Square Capitalʼs customer service telephone number. The telephone number provided must permit any individual to make a do-not-call request during regular business hours. Square Capital is prohibited from blocking the transmission of caller identification information. ● Not abandon more than three percent of all Telemarketing calls that are answered live by a person, as measured over a 30–day period for a single calling campaign. If a single calling campaign exceeds a 30–day period, the abandonment rate shall be calculated separately for each successive 30–day period or portion thereof that such calling campaign continues. ● Comply with DNC requirements. An individualʼs information must be screened against the following lists before engaging in a marketing or solicitation communication to that individual: ○ Square Capitalʼs DNC List; ○ The National DNC Registry, where the call recipientʼs Business Address is located in California, Arizona, Nevada, or Utah; or ○ The Public Safety Answering Point (“PSAPˮ) Call Registry.
documents.CP-039: Controlling the Assault of Non-Solicited Pornography and Marketing Act Policy Square | Square Capital, LLC | Credit Card Last Approved: October 3, 2025 Effective Date: October 3, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Controlling the Assault of Non-Solicited Pornography and Marketing Act ("CANSPAM") Policy ("Policy") outlines Square Capital, LLC's ("Square Capital" or the "Company") approach to compliance with the CANSPAM (or the "Act") and upholding Square Capital business requirements that do not run counter to the Act. This Policy was created and adopted with the sole focus on Square Capital's commercial credit card program and the Chief Compliance Officer oversees the development and implementation of this Policy document. This Policy establishes the framework by which Square Capital, the Initiator of email advertising communications, ensures that email advertising ("Commercial Email") complies with the requirements of CANSPAM. The purpose of this Policy is to ensure that Square Capital establishes and adheres to a consistent process for crafting email content and subject lines, sending of email advertising, and proper handling of opt-out requests. Background CANSPAM was enacted to prevent abuse in the use of Commercial Emails, and among other things, requires that Commercial Emails provide consumers with a means of opting-out of future solicitations. The Children's Online Privacy Protection Act ("COPPA") prohibits unfair or deceptive acts or practices in connection with the collection, use, and/or disclosure of personal information from and about children, under the age of 13, on the Internet. COPPA provides privacy protections for children and specifically requires operators of websites and email directed to children to provide notice to and obtain consent from parents before collecting data from their children. The Federal Trade Commission ("FTC"), the nation's consumer protection agency, enforces COPPA, which spells out what operators of websites and online services must do to protect children's privacy and safety online. Both of these Acts are implemented by regulations issued by the FTC. While the FTC does not regulate financial institutions, the laws give enforcement authority to banking regulatory agencies. Scope: This Policy is established by Square Capital and is applicable to Square Capital's credit card product. All Square Capital business units, staff, and contractors, including those from vendors and third parties (i.e., Square Inc.), must comply with the aspects of this Policy. Square Capital recognizes a further duty to support the compliance programs of its Bank Partner, where applicable. Definitions: Terminology Definition Affirmative Consent With respect to a commercial electronic mail message ("email"), it means that: ● The recipient expressly consented to receive the message, either in response to a clear and conspicuous request for such consent or at the recipient's own initiative; and ● If the message is from a party other than the party to which the recipient communicated such consent, the recipient was given clear and conspicuous notice at the time the consent was communicated that the recipient's electronic mail address could be transferred to such other party for the purpose of initiating commercial electronic mail messages. Commercial Email Any email that has a primary purpose of commercial advertisement or promotion of a commercial product or service. Initiator Any person, including business entities and nonprofit associations, that initiates commercial e-mail messages must comply with the CANSPAM Act requirements if it either: ● Originates or transmits the e-mail; or ● Procures the transmission of the e-mail, meaning that the business either intentionally pays or provides other consideration to, or induces, another person to transmit the e-mail on its behalf. A person is not an Initiator when their role in initiating the Commercial Email is solely involved in routine conveyance. This is when the Terminology Definition person's actions only relate to the transmission, routing or storage of the message through an automatic technical process and the person is not involved in identifying or providing the recipient's addresses for the message. Sender A Sender is an Initiator whose own product or service, or internet website, is advertised or promoted in the commercial message. A commercial e-mail can have more than one Initiator or Sender. Staff Member(s) All employees and fixed-term and contingent workers who support the operations and/or subsidiaries covered by this Policy, and includes interns, temporary workers, consultants, vendors, third-party providers and contractors. Transactional or See the section entitled "Exclusion: Transactional or Relationship Relationship Email Emails" 2. Policy Requirements 2.1 Email Content Qualifications The FTC has issued qualifying measures to determine if an email meets the threshold of a marketing communication. Square Capital is committed to upholding these requirements by ensuring the following email types are properly handled. Messages Containing Only Advertising Content These messages have a commercial primary purpose. Messages Containing Both Advertising and Transactional or Relationship Content These messages have a commercial primary purpose if either: ● The recipient would interpret the subject line to mean that the message contains commercial advertising; or ● A substantial part of the transactional or relationship content does not appear at the beginning of the message. Messages Containing Both Advertising Content and Other Non-transactional or Non-relationship Content These messages have a commercial primary purpose if either: ● The recipient would interpret the subject line to mean that the message contains commercial advertising; or ● The recipient would determine from the body of the message that the message's primary purpose is commercial advertising. In making this determination, factors for the recipient to consider include: ○ The placement of the commercial advertising at the beginning of the message; ○ The proportion of the message dedicated to commercial advertising; and ○ How prominent the commercial advertising is (for example, highlighted through use of graphics type size and style). Messages Containing Only Transactional or Relationship Content These messages do not have a commercial primary purpose. 2.2 Email Content Requirements CANSPAM sets down seven requirements that every Commercial Email must meet. Square Capital is committed to ensuring all marketing emails comply with these requirements. Under CANSPAM, the Senders of Commercial Email will be deemed to be engaging in legal activity as long as: ● The header of the Commercial Email (indicating the sending source, destination and routing information) does not contain materially false or materially misleading information; ● The subject line doesn't contain deceptive information; ● The email provides "clear and conspicuous" identification that it is an advertisement or solicitation; ● The email includes some type of return email address, which can be used to indicate that the recipient no longer wishes to receive spam email from the Sender (i.e. to "opt-out"); ● The email contains "clear and conspicuous" notice of the opportunity to opt-out of receiving future emails from the Sender; ● The email has not been sent after the Sender received notice that the recipient no longer wishes to receive email from the Sender (i.e., has "opted-out"); and ● The email contains a valid physical postal address for the Sender. Opt-Out Prohibitions There cannot be a fee, nor a requirement that the recipient provide any personally identifying information beyond an email address, nor can there be any steps that the recipient must take other than sending a reply email or visiting a single page on an Internet website as a condition for honoring an opt-out request. Once people advise that they do not want to receive more messages, affected email addresses cannot be sold or transferred, even in the form of a mailing list. The only exception is the addresses may be transferred to a company hired to assist with CAN‑SPAM Act compliance. Allowable Practices There are some practices that are permitted to support these protections: ● #3 The disclosure need not necessarily be in the email heading as long as the notice is "clear and conspicuous" within the email itself. ● #4 This can be a current street address, a post office box registered with the U.S. Postal Service, or a private mailbox registered with a commercial mail receiving agency established under Postal Service regulations. However, it must be of the Sender of the email not the Initiator. ● #5 Craft the notice in a way that's easy for an ordinary person to recognize, read, and understand. Creative use of type size, color, and location can improve clarity. Give a return email address or another easy Internet-based way to allow people to communicate their choice to you. A menu of options to allow a recipient to opt-out of certain types of messages is permitted, but it must include the option to stop all commercial messages. Any spam filter used by Square Capital must not block these opt-out requests. 2.3 Email Delivery Requirements Square Capital is committed to meeting the delivery and content requirements set down by CANSPAM. Square Credit Product Marketing ("Marketing") Team is responsible for the content of the Subject Line and for selecting the audience to whom the email is to be sent. Marketing is charged with the responsibilities to ensure all emails contain properly displayed and functioning unsubscribe links and that they are sent only as permitted under this Policy. To implement these guidelines, the content, structure, and delivery of emails must comply with the following requirements: ● Unsubscribe Compliance: ○ A visible and operable unsubscribe mechanism is present in all emails; ○ Customer opt-out reque
documents.CP-040: Credit Card Privacy Policy Square | Square Capital, LLC | Credit Card Last Approved: October 3, 2025 Effective Date: October 3, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Credit Card Privacy Policy (“Policyˮ) outlines Square Capital, LLCʼs (“Square Capitalˮ or the “Companyʼ) approach to compliance with the Right to Financial Privacy Act (“RFPAˮ) and Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act (“GLBAˮ). This Policy was created and adopted with the sole focus on Square Capitalʼs commercial credit card program and is complementary to Blockʼs wider Privacy program and Privacy Principles. The Chief Compliance Officer (“CCOˮ) oversees the development and implementation of this Policy and has delegated ownership of this Policy document to the Square Head of Compliance. Square Capital conducts business in compliance with all applicable laws and regulations. The purpose of this Policy is to further Square Capitalʼs compliance with RFPA and GLBA. Square Capital is committed to fully complying with RFPA and GLBA and to upholding Square Capital business requirements that do not run counter to the Act. Scope: This Policy is established by Square Capital and is applicable to Square Capitalʼs credit card product. It applies to all activities that involve non-affiliated third parties and the disclosure of nonpublic personal information NPI of customers for Square Capital's credit card product. Square Capital engages with non-affiliated third parties for the carrying out of financial transactions and for marketing of financial products. Such vendors may include, but are not limited to, vendors that verify customer identification information. The use of NPI for these reasons does not require that Square Capital obtain explicit prior consent from the customer. As of the enacting of this Policy, Square Capital may engage with any non-affiliated third party so that the non-affiliate can market Square Capital products, subject to Squareʼs standard privacy due diligence practices of third parties. Definitions: Terminology Definition Customer An individual who has a continuing relationship with Square Capital under which Square Capital provides one or more financial products or services. For example, because Square Capital services the loans, an applicant that receives a loan from Square Capital becomes a customer. Personal Data Information related to an identifiable person or household. Data is considered personal data if it can be used to identify a person, directly or indirectly. Nonpublic Personal The nonpublic portion of personal data, including any customer lists. Information (“NPIˮ) Nonpublic personal information consists of nonpublic information that is collected in connection with providing a financial product or service. Specifically, it means: ● Personally identifiable financial information, which includes: ○ Information a customer provides on an application for a loan or other financial product or service; ○ Account balance information, payment history, overdraft history and credit or debit card purchase information; ○ Information that a customer provides to Square Capital (or an agent thereof) that Square Capital obtains in connection with collecting on a loan or servicing a loan, as the case may be; ○ Any information collected through an Internet “cookieˮ; ○ Information from a customer report; ○ The fact that an individual is or has been a customer of Square Capital or has obtained a financial product or service from Square Capital; and ○ Any information about Square Capitalʼs customer if it is disclosed in a manner that indicates that the individual is or has been a Square Capital customer. ● Any list, description, or other grouping of customers that is derived using any personally identifiable financial information that is not publicly available. Lists include, but are not limited to, any list of individualsʼ names and addresses that is derived in whole or in part using personally identifiable information that is not publicly available, such as account numbers. Nonpublic personal information does not include information that is available from public sources, such as telephone directories or government records. It also does not include aggregate information or blind data that does not contain personal identifiers. Affiliate Any company that controls, is controlled by, or is under common control with another company. Non-Affiliated Third Persons or entities except affiliate(s) and persons jointly employed by a Party financial institution and a nonaffiliated third party. GLBA Privacy Rules restrict information sharing with non-affiliated third parties. Clear and A notice is reasonably understandable and designed to call attention to Conspicuous the nature and significance of the information in the notice. Collect To obtain information that can be organized to retrieve information by the name of an individual or by identifying number, symbol, or other identifying information assigned to the individual, irrespective of the source of the underlying information. 2. Block, Inc. Requirements 2.1. Credit Card Privacy 2.1.1. How Square Capital Collects Information Information about customers is accumulated in various ways including, but not limited to, via loan applications and information from a customer report. 2.1.2. Information Square Capital is Allowed to Share The law allows Square Capital to share information with Square Capitalʼs affiliate(s), to the extent it is allowable for Squareʼs affiliate(s) to have that information to act as required by or for Square Capital. The law further allows sharing as permitted for customer credit report information as established by the Fair Credit Reporting Act FCRA. In addition, Square Capitalʼs current practice is to only share information about transactions and experiences with its affiliate(s). Please see the Square Capital Fair and Accurate Transactions Act Policy for more information. Prior to sharing information with third parties, Square Capital must have a permissible purpose and must receive approval to share the information with third parties via Squareʼs standard third party risk management practices. Square Capital may share information with other parties under any of the following conditions: ● To market Square Capitalʼs own financial products or services; ● Where third parties are performing services or functions on behalf of Square Capital, including marketing Square Capitalʼs own products or services; ● Products and services offered pursuant to a joint agreement between Square Capital and a third party financial institution, provided that Square Capital: ○ discloses that arrangement, and ○ enters into an agreement with the third party to maintain the confidentiality of the information. ● The customer consents; ● It is necessary to effect, administer, or enforce a transaction requested or authorized by the customer; ● To process and service transactions the customer requests or authorizes; ● To protect against potential fraud or unauthorized transactions; ● To protect the confidentiality or security of Square Capitalʼs records; ● For risk control purposes or for resolving customer disputes; ● To persons holding a legal or beneficial interest relating to the customer; ● To persons acting in a fiduciary capacity on behalf of the customer; ● To law enforcement agencies, if permitted or required under other provisions of law; ● To respond to judicial process, attorneys, accountants and auditors; ● To respond to governmental authorities for examination, compliance, or other lawful purposes; ● To a customer reporting agency in accordance with the FCRA; or ● To comply with federal state or local laws. 2.1.3. OptOut Provision Although Square Capital does not currently engage in this practice, if the company does share nonpublic personal information with nonaffiliated third parties other than as defined above, Square Capital may offer customers the opportunity to “opt-outˮ of the information sharing process. Prior to any such sharing and if choosing to offer an opt-out provision, Square Capital will ensure that the customer is provided with the right to opt-out or limit the sharing by notifying Square Capital of the intent to “opt-outˮ through the use of a mail-in form or other permissible means. 2.1.4. Privacy Notice to New Customers Square Capital is required to provide a copy of the Privacy Notice when it enters into a customer relationship with a customer. A customer relationship means a continuing relationship between a customer and Square Capital, and is established when Square Capital provides one or more financial products or services to the customer that are to be used primarily for personal, family, or household purposes. Square Capital provides a copy of its Privacy Notice to all customers prior to origination and requires the customer to acknowledge receipt of the privacy notice as a necessary step to obtaining a loan with Square Capital. 2.1.5. Annual Privacy Notice Square Capital may elect to annually provide a Privacy Notice to applicable customers, as long as there is a continuation of the customer relationship, subject to the below qualifiers: ● Annually means at least once in any period of 12 consecutive months during which that relationship exists; and ● Square Capital may post the Privacy Notice on its webpage (e.g., Square Capital website or consumer dashboard) and send an email to all applicable customers notifying them of the location of the Privacy Notice. Alternatively, as a business decision, Square Capital may elect not to send a Privacy Notice, which may be appropriate in particular where both of the following are true: ● Nonpublic personal information is only shared in the manner and to the extent outlined above; and ● The policies and practices regarding the disclosure of nonpublic personal information have not changed from those that were disclosed in the most recent privacy notice that was sent. 2.1.6. Changes to the Privacy Notice Square Capital does not di
documents.CP-041: Electronic Signature Policy Square | Square Capital, LLC | Credit Card Last Approved: October 3, 2025 Effective Date: October 3, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Electronic Signature (“ESignˮ) Policy (“Policyˮ) outlines Square Capital, LLCʼs (“Square Capitalˮ or the “Companyʼ) approach to compliance with the Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act. This Policy was created and adopted with the sole focus on Square Capitalʼs commercial credit card program. The Chief Compliance Officer (“CCOˮ) oversees the development and implementation of this Policy and has delegated ownership of this Policy document to the Square Head of Compliance. Square Capital conducts business in compliance with all applicable laws and regulations. The purpose of this Policy is to further Square Capitalʼs compliance with ESign. Square Capital is committed to fully complying with ESign and to upholding Square Capital business requirements that do not run counter to the Act. Scope: This Policy is established by Square Capital and is applicable to Square Capitalʼs credit card product. Definitions: Terminology Definition Electronic Relating to technology having electrical, digital, magnetic, wireless, optical, electromagnetic, or similar capabilities. Electronic A contract or other Record created, generated, sent, communicated, Record received, or stored by Electronic means. Electronic An Electronic sound, symbol, or process, attached to or logically associated Signature with a contract or other Record and executed or adopted by a person with the intent to Sign the Record. Information Data, text, images, sounds, codes, computed programs, software, databases, or the like. Record Information that is inscribed on a tangible medium or that is stored in an Electronic or other medium and is retrievable in perceivable form. Staff Member(s) All employees and fixed-term and contingent workers who support the operations and/or subsidiaries covered by this Policy, and includes interns, temporary workers, consultants, vendors, third-party providers and contractors. Transaction An action or set of actions relating to the conduct of business, consumer, or commercial affairs between two or more persons, including any of the following types of conduct: ● The sale, lease, exchange, licensing, or other disposition of: ○ Personal property, including goods and intangibles; ○ Services; and ○ Any combination thereof; or ● The sale, lease, exchange or other disposition of any interest in real property, or any combination thereof. 2. Block, Inc. Requirements 2.1. Policy Statement Square Capital adheres to the requirements of ESign for all of its product offerings. Federal laws, regulations, and guidelines related to ESign are regulated by the Federal Trade Commission, providing lending Records and disclosures electronically. The Companyʼs goal is to implement procedures that: ● Facilitate e-commerce and make legally valid Electronic Transactions; ● Prevent deception and fraud in Transactions involving Electronic Records; and ● Ensure that customers can access Information Square Capital sends electronically. 2.2. Background ESign allows Square Capital to facilitate e-commerce and provide disclosures and Records that are typically required to be “in writingˮ in an Electronic format. The Company ensures that prior to engaging in Transactions in which it provides disclosures and Records Electronically, that the customer has provided affirmative consent and demonstrates the ability to access the disclosure and/or Record. ESign only applies to consumer product offerings, but Square Capital has elected to apply its requirements to its credit card product. The Uniform Electronic Transactions Act (“UETAˮ) establishes the legal equivalence of Electronic Records and Signatures with paper writings and manually-Signed Signatures. Forty-seven states, the District of Columbia, Puerto Rico, and the Virgin Islands have adopted UETA, which was promulgated by the Uniform Law Commission. By adopting the official version of UETA, states have the authority to modify, limit or supersede some ESign provisions, including its consumer protection provisions. ESign governs in the absence of a state law or where states have made modifications to UETA that are inconsistent with ESign. 2.3. ESign Requirements Disclosures Prior to transmitting Records Electronically, Square Capital provides a clear and conspicuous disclosure statement informing the customer of: ● The availability of paper Records; ● The scope, effect, and applicability of consent; ● The right to withdraw consent; ● Procedures to withdraw the consent; ● Procedures to update the Electronic Information, such as contact email address; and ● Hardware and software requirements. Hardware and Software Requirements As stated above, prior to transmitting Records Electronically, Square Capital provides customers with a statement of the hardware and software requirements for access to and retention of Electronic Records. The disclosure ensures that customers have the technical means to receive lending Records and disclosures Electronically. Required Consent Square Capital has implemented a control that allows a customer to give consent Electronically. This also demonstrates that the customer can access Information in the Electronic form that the Company will use to provide Information. Square Capital is required to explain that the consumer has the right to withdraw consent and provide the procedures to withdraw consent as well as the consequences of withdrawing consent, such as fees, termination of the relationship, loss of preferred pricing or having to switch account types. Changes to Hardware and Software Requirements Square Capital also notifies customers of a change to hardware or software requirements needed to access or retain their Electronic Records where the change in requirements creates a material risk that the customer will not be able to access or retain subsequent Electronic Records that were the subject of the initial consent. Additionally, consent must also be re-affirmed when there is a change to the hardware and/or software requirements. When Square Capital provides such notice, the notice includes: ● The revised hardware and software requirements for access to and retention of the Electronic Records; and ● A statement that the customer may withdraw consent without the imposition of any fees or the imposition of any new condition or consequence resulting from the withdrawal. 3. Compliance & Enforcement 3.1. Roles and Responsibilities: ● Chief Compliance Officer (“CCOˮ) : ○ The CCO will provide assistance and support in implementing this Policy across the organization and shall be responsible for establishing appropriate practices designed to ensure that Company operations reflect the Companyʼs commitment to compliance and that all employees are aware of that commitment and their individual responsibility. ○ The CCO is responsible for monitoring and reporting on compliance with this Policy. The CCO delegates the responsibilities in this section to the Square Head of Compliance along with the authority to adopt, develop and implement procedures and reporting that support this Policy. ● Counsel: ○ The Companyʼs legal Counsel provides interpretative advice and counsel regarding the applicability and application of the requirements of this Policy to the Companyʼs business and operational activities. ● Square Capital Board of Managers (“SCBMˮ): ○ Reviews and approves this Policy annually. ● Square Head of Compliance, or Square Consumer Protection Program Lead as designee: ○ Owns this Policy document, reviews and approves exception requests. 3.2. Monitoring and Reporting On an ongoing basis, the Policy Owner and/or their designee monitors activities for compliance and objective achievement. As outlined in the Compliance Management System Policy, Compliance will provide regular updates, as applicable, to the Block, Inc. Board and executive management. Reporting may cover specific information related to: ● Policies and standards ● Procedures ● Audit/exam findings ● Issues management ● Training ● Complaints 3.3. Training All relevant personnel will receive training at least annually covering applicable requirements and guidance to implement this Policy effectively. All training related activities are monitored by the Compliance Team and are outlined in the Compliance Training Program. 3.4. Record Retention All Documents are retained according to the longer of: ● Blockʼs Data Classification & Handling Policy ● Applicable regulatory requirements 3.5. Exceptions The Policy Owner or designee will have authority to approve exceptions and/or exemptions to this Policy. Policy exception requests must be documented in writing, include a reason for a request, and provide a description of the controls that the Company will implement to mitigate risks associated with the exception. Any approved exceptions must be documented in writing and retained with recordkeeping requirements. Any approved exceptions or exemptions should be submitted to the Policy Governance Team at go/policyhelp. The Policy Owner and/or designee will inform Compliance Leadership and applicable committees, as needed, of exceptions. 3.6. Confidentiality This document is confidential and should only be accessed by disclosed individuals within Square Capital, LLC and on a need-to-know basis. 4. Questions & Contact Information Questions: For questions or clarifications about this Policy, please contact the Policy Owner. Review related documents: ● Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act 5. Document Details & Revisions Review and Approval: This document shall be reviewed and approved at least annually and on an ad hoc basis in accordance with the Compliance Document Approval Matrix. Approval History: Approver Title Type Description Date N/A Credit Card Creation New policy March 3, 2022 Compliance intended for the Comm
documents.CP-042: Equal Credit Opportunity Act Policy Square | Square Capital, LLC | Credit Card Product Last Approved: October 3, 2025 Effective Date: October 3, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Equal Credit Opportunity Act (“ECOAˮ) Policy (“Policyˮ) outlines Square Capital, LLCʼs (“Square Capitalˮ or the “Companyˮ) approach to compliance with the Equal Credit Opportunity Act (“ECOAˮ or the “Actˮ) and to upholding requirements that do not run counter to the Act. The Policy was created and adopted with the sole focus on Square Capitalʼs commercial credit card program. The Chief Compliance Officer (“CCOˮ) oversees the development and implementation of this Policy and has delegated ownership of this Policy document to the Square Head of Compliance. Square Capital conducts business in compliance with all applicable laws and regulations. The purpose of this Policy is to outline Square Capitalʼs compliance with ECOA and to uphold Square Capital business requirements that do not run counter to the Act. Scope: This Policy is established by Square Capital and is applicable to Square Capitalʼs credit card product. All Square Capital business units, Staff Members, and contractors, including those from vendors and third parties (i.e., Square, Inc.) must comply with all aspects of this Policy. Square Capital recognizes a further duty to support the compliance program of its Bank Partner, where applicable. Definitions: Terminology Definition Adverse Action The term means: ● A refusal to grant credit in substantially the amount or on substantially the same terms as requested in an Application; ● A termination of an account or an unfavorable change in the terms of an account that does not affect all of substantially all of a class of Square Capitalʼs accounts; or ● A refusal to increase the amount of credit available to an applicant to who has made an Application for an increase. Application Means a request for an extension of credit for the type of credit offered. There are two types of Applications: ● Complete: An Application is complete once Identity Verification Service (“IDVˮ) screening is successfully passed and Square Capital has received all information needed to evaluate an Application, as defined by Square Capital and rendered a credit review decision. ● Incomplete: An Application is incomplete when Identity Verification Service (“IDVˮ) screening is successfully passed but Square Capital has not received all information needed to evaluate an Application, as defined by Square Capital, and has not rendered a credit decision. Inquiries and pre-qualification requests related to credit are not Applications. However, an inquiry or pre-qualification request becomes an Application if Square Capital evaluates information about a person, determines the person does not or does not qualify for a credit card or pre-qualification order, and communicates that decision to that person. This still applies whether the communication of a decision is intentional or inadvertent. Examples of responses that can turn an inquiry into an Application: ● “We can approve your Application for at least the minimum offer amount because you have high GPV and no chargebacks.ˮ ● Your credit score qualifies so go ahead and submit an Application and we will approve you.ˮ Creditor The person or entity that is intending to grant a credit extension. In the credit card program, the Creditor is Celtic Bank and Square Capital is considered the servicer to this program. Discrimination Means to treat an applicant less favorably than other applicants. Against an Applicant Prohibited Basis Means race, color, religion, national origin, sex, sexual identity or sexual orientation, marital status, or age (provided that the applicant has the capacity to enter into a binding contract); the fact that all or part of the applicantʼs income derives from any public assistance program; or the fact that the applicant has in good faith exercised any right under the Consumer Credit Protection Act or any state law upon which an exemption has been granted by the Federal Reserve Board of Governors. Staff Member(s) All employees and fixed-term and contingent workers who support the operations and/or subsidiaries covered by this Policy, and includes interns, temporary workers, consultants, vendors, third-party providers and contractors. 2. Block, Inc. Requirements 2.1. Policy Statement Square Capital manages its lending programs so as to not discriminate nor to discourage applicants from applying for credit based on any Prohibited Basis or, once origination, for any aspect of a credit transaction on a Prohibited Basis. Additionally, Square Capital provides applicants for credit all disclosures and notifications required under ECOA, its implementing rule, Regulation B, and other applicable fair lending laws. 2.2. Background ECOA and its implementing rule, Regulation B, make it illegal for Creditors to discriminate, on a Prohibited Basis, against qualified individuals seeking credit and on any aspect of a credit transaction,as long as they are found to be creditworthy. Under ECOA, it is illegal to discriminate against an applicant because of their: ● Race or color; ● Religion; ● National origin; ● Sex; ● Gender identity or sexual orientation; ● Marital status; ● Age (provided the applicant has the capacity to contract); ● The applicantʼs receipt of income derived from any public assistance program; or ● The applicantʼs exercise, in good faith, of any right under the Consumer Credit Protection Act. ECOA prohibits Creditors from discriminating on a Prohibited Basis in virtually all aspects of a lending transaction, including: ● Accepting credit Applications; ● Processing and evaluating credit Applications; ● Notifying the applicant of the credit decision; ● Servicing the credit transaction; ● Collection activities; ● Advertising; ● Compensation programs; and ● Complaint handling. 2.3. ECOA Requirements 2.3.1. Prohibitions ECOA contains two basic and comprehensive prohibitions against discriminatory lending practices: 1. A Creditor shall not discriminate against an applicant on a Prohibited Basis regarding any aspect of a credit transaction. 2. A Creditor shall not make any oral or written statement, in advertising or otherwise, to applicants or prospective applicants that would discourage, on a Prohibited Basis, a reasonable person from making or pursuing an Application. By making it illegal to discriminate in this manner, ECOA ensures that all customers have an equal and fair opportunity to obtain credit, as long as they are found to be creditworthy. Under ECOA and applicable state law, it is illegal to discriminate against an applicant because of their: ● Race or color; ● Religion; ● National origin; ● Sex; ● Marital status; ● Gender identity or sexual orientation; ● Age (provided the applicant has the capacity to contract); ● The applicantʼs receipt of income derived from any public assistance program; or ● The applicantʼs exercise, in good faith, of any right under the Consumer Credit Protection Act. Square Capital structures its product offerings and controls to ensure that it prevents the following three types of discrimination: 1. Overt Discrimination: Intentional discrimination on a Prohibited Basis. An example of overt discrimination is having a policy not to issue credit to persons based on one of the prohibited bases. 2. Disparate Treatment: A form of discrimination that may arise when a lender treats an applicant less favorably than a similarly situated applicant on the basis of a prohibited characteristic, and there is no credible, non-discriminatory explanation for the difference in treatment. An example of disparate treatment is providing a different rate, term or duration, information, or level of service to an applicant because of a prohibited factor, such as the race or gender of the borrower. 3. Disparate Impact: A neutral policy or practice is implemented uniformly, but the policy or practice disproportionately affects a protected class, and there is no credible, non-discriminatory business justification for the policy. An example of disparate impact is a prohibition on issuing credit to a particular type of business may result in a disparate impact if the prohibition leads to the exclusion of credit to businesses owned predominantly by members of protected classes. 2.3.2. Accepting Credit Applications The credit Application process is neutral in nature and of a type applicable to every applicant desiring the same kind and amount of credit. Square Capital employees, contractors, or third parties involved in the lending transaction may not consider any Prohibited Basis as a factor in taking or processing Applications for credit, except to the extent allowed by federal and state law. All disclosures required to be given under ECOA are to be made in a clear and conspicuous manner. 2.3.3. Processing and Evaluating Credit Applicants Square Capital Data Science (“Data Scienceˮ) creates and maintains the heuristic model that underwrites customer applicants to determine whether they are eligible for a credit card. Square performs periodic fair lending analysis on its underwriting models and processes to identify and mitigate potential disparate impact, with results reviewed by Compliance and Legal Square Capital may elect to utilize an automated onboarding review process based on the above heuristics or conduct review manually for all Applications. Regardless, once that review is completed one of the following then occurs: ● The automated heuristic model or the Capital Team approves the submitted Application and notifies the customer of the approval in writing; or ● The automated heuristic model of the Capital Team does not initially approve the Application then the following occurs: ○ The Application is not approved and the customer is notified in writing of the declined Application. Note that manual reviews are not conducted today. The Capital Team plans to implement a manu
documents.CP-043: Fair and Accurate Credit Transactions Act Policy Square | Square Capital, LLC | Credit Card Last Approved: October 3, 2025 Effective Date: October 3, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Fair and Accurate Credit Transactions Act ("FCRA") Policy ("Policy") outlines Square Capital, LLC's ("Square Capital" or the "Company") approach to compliance with the FCRA as amended by the Fair and Accurate Credit Transactions Act ("FACTA"). The Chief Compliance Officer ("CCO") oversees development and implementation of this Policy and has delegated ownership of this Policy document to the Square Head of Compliance. The purpose of this Policy is to establish the responsibilities of Square Capital to comply with the requirements set forth in the FCRA, as amended. Scope: This Policy is established by Square Capital and is applicable to Square Capital. This Policy applies to Square Capital's credit card product, whether conducted by the Company's employees or through outsourced service providers. Square Capital recognizes a further duty to support the compliance programs of its Bank Partner, where applicable. Definitions: Terminology Definition Adverse Action A denial or revocation of credit, an unfavorable change in the terms of an existing credit arrangement, or a refusal to grant credit in substantially the amount or on substantially the terms that the applicant requested. It does not include an "accepted counteroffer," where the applicant requested particular credit card terms, such as a specific down payment or rate, but accepted the Company's offer for different terms, such as a higher down payment or rate. Affiliate Any Person or entity that is related by common ownership or common corporate control with another Person or entity. Consumer Any individual. Consumer Any written, oral, or other communication of any information by a Consumer Report Reporting Agency bearing on a Consumer's creditworthiness, credit standing, credit capacity, character, general reputation, Personal characteristics or mode of living. Consumer Generally refers to a credit bureau. If Square Capital shares information Reporting about Consumers with a third-party, the Company could become a Agency Consumer Reporting Agency. Covered Any account the Company offers or maintains that involves or is designed to Account permit multiple payments or transactions, and any other account the Company offers or maintains for which there is a reasonably foreseeable risk to Consumers or the safety and soundness of the Company from Identity Theft, including financial, operational, compliance, reputation or litigation risks. Creditor Any Person who regularly extends, renews, or continues credit; any Person who regularly arranges for the extension, renewal, or continuation of credit; or any assignee of an original Creditor who participates in the decision to extend, renew, or continue credit. Eligibility Includes any information that per the above definition would constitute a Information Consumer Report, plus the following: ● A report containing information solely as to transactions or experiences between the Consumer and the Person making the report; ● A communication of that information among Persons related by common ownership or Affiliated by corporate control; or ● A communication of other information among Persons related by common ownership or Affiliated by corporate control, if it is clearly and conspicuously disclosed to the Consumer that the information may be communicated among such Persons and the Consumer is given the opportunity, before the time that the information is initially communicated, to direct that such information not be communicated among such Persons. Eligibility information does not include aggregate or blind data that does not contain Personal identifiers such as account numbers, names, or addresses. Firm Offer of An offer that will be honored if the Consumer meets the specific criteria used Credit to select him/her for the offer, except the offer may be conditioned on one or both of the following: ● The Consumer meets any pre-established credit worthiness criteria based on the application, as long as such other criteria are established in advance; and ● The Consumer's continued eligibility and application information can be verified after the Consumer accepts the offer. Identity Theft A fraud committed or attempted using the identifying information of another Person without authority. Medical Information or data, whether oral or recorded, in any form or medium, Information created by or derived from a health care provider or the Consumer, that relates to: ● The past, present, or future physical, mental, or behavioral health or condition of an individual; ● The provision of health care to an individual; or ● The payment for the provision of health care to an individual. Person Any individual, partnership, corporation, trust, estate cooperative, association, government or governmental subdivision or agency, or other entity. Red Flag A pattern, practice, or specific activity that indicates the possible existence of Identity Theft. Staff Member(s) All employees and fixed-term and contingent workers who support the operations and/or subsidiaries covered by this Policy, and includes interns, temporary workers, consultants, vendors, third-party providers and contractors. 2. Policy Requirements 2.1. Policy Statement Square Capital seeks to comply with the FCRA, which establishes requirements related to the use and sharing of Consumer credit information, information about credit reports and credit scoring, protections for victims of Identity Theft, and protections for Consumer Medical Information. 2.2. FCRA Background The FCRA was enacted to provide Consumers with greater control and insight into their credit reports and how those reports are used to grant extensions of credit or other considerations addressed by the rule. In addition, the FCRA requires entities who extend credit based on usage of those reports as well as the providers of those reports to meet certain reporting and usage requirements, regardless of whether the credit is for commercial or Consumer purposes. In general, the FCRA states that its primary goals are to: ● Provide fair and accurate credit reporting. Inaccurate credit reports directly impair the efficiency of the banking system, and unfair credit reporting methods undermine the public confidence, which is essential to the continued functioning of the banking system; ● Create an efficient mechanism for investigating and evaluating the credit worthiness, credit standing, credit capacity, character, and general reputation of Consumers; ● Provide Consumer Reporting agencies with regulatory guidance in assuming a vital role in assembling and evaluating Consumer credit and other information on Consumers; and ● Ensure that Consumer Reporting agencies exercise their grave responsibilities with fairness, impartiality, and a respect for the Consumer's right to privacy. To implement those goals, the FCRA regulates the following: ● Obtaining and using Consumer Reports; ● Providing applicable disclosures; ● Furnishing information; ● Adverse action (as it pertains to the usage of credit report information); ● Sharing information with both Affiliates and non-Affiliates; ● Reporting accurate information and correction of errors; ● Identity theft; ● Medical information; ● Free annual Consumer Reports; ● Coverage of business transactions; and ● Risk based pricing. The FCRA contains significant responsibilities for business entities that are Consumer Reporting agencies and lesser responsibilities for those that are not. Generally, financial institutions and providers of financial services are not considered to function as Consumer Reporting agencies. In addition to the requirements related to entities acting as Consumer Reporting agencies, the FCRA requirements also apply to businesses that operate in the following capacities: ● Procurers and users of information (for example, as credit grantors, purchasers of dealer paper, or when opening deposit accounts); ● Furnishers and transmitters of information (by reporting information to Consumer Reporting agencies or other third parties, or to Affiliates); ● Marketers of credit or insurance products; or ● Employers. 2.3. Requirements This Policy governs Square Capital's guidelines to align with the requirements within the FCRA when Consumer Reports are used. Square Capital: ● Does not furnish information to a Consumer Reporting Agency that the Company knows is inaccurate; ● Does not submit information to a Consumer Reporting Agency if notified by a Consumer that the information is inaccurate and the Company subsequently determines the information is inaccurate; ● Enacts policies and procedures designed to enable Square Capital to form a reasonable belief that a Consumer Report relates to the Consumer about whom it has requested the report, when Square Capital receives a notice of address discrepancy; ● Only uses Consumer Reports for permissible purposes as defined within the FCRA; and ● Provides disclosures to Consumers, as required by law. 2.3.1. Obtaining Consumer Reports The FCRA requires any prospective user of a Consumer Report to have a legally permissible purpose to obtain a report. The Company may request a Consumer Report only for one of the following defined permissible purposes: ● The Consumer provides authorization in writing; ● The report will be used in connection with a credit transaction involving the Consumer and involving the extension of credit to, or review or collection of an account of the Consumer; ● A prescreened solicitation in connection with any credit transaction that is not initiated by the Consumer only if: ○ The Consumer authorizes the Consumer Reporting Agency to provide such a report; or ○ The transaction consists of a Firm Offer of Credit and the Consumer has not opted out of receiving such solicitations. 2.3.2. Medical Information The FCRA prohibits
documents.CP-044: Red Flags Policy Square | Square Capital, LLC | Credit Card Last Approved: October 3, 2025 Effective Date: October 3, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Red Flags Policy ("Policy") outlines Square Capital, LLC's ("Square Capital" or the "Company") approach to compliance with the Red Flags provision of the Fair and Accurate Credit Transactions Act ("FACTA"). The Chief Compliance Officer ("CCO") oversees the development and implementation of this Policy and has delegated ownership of this Policy to the Head of Business Product Compliance and Partnerships or Lending Product Compliance Lead as designee. This Policy is designed to effectively detect, prevent and mitigate Identity Theft pursuant to the rules and regulations established under FACTA known as the "Red Flag Rules." It applies to all customer accounts owned or maintained by Square Capital. Scope: This Policy is established by Square Capital and is applicable to Square Capital, specific to the credit card product. This Policy applies to Square Capital's lending products and associated activities, whether conducted by the Company's employees or through outsourced service providers, including when Square Capital acts as a service provider for a Bank Partner that originates lending products. Definitions: Terminology Definition Covered An account that a creditor offers or maintains for which there is a reasonably Account foreseeable risk to customers or to the safety and soundness of the creditor from Identity Theft, including financial, operational, compliance, reputation, or litigation risk. Identity Theft Fraud committed or attempted using one or more pieces of identifying information of another person without authority. Identifying information means any name or number that may be used, alone or in conjunction with any other information, to identify a specific person, including, but not limited to, any name, social security number, or date of birth. Red Flag A pattern, practice, or specific activity that indicates the possible existence of Identity Theft. Staff Member(s) All employees and fixed-term and contingent workers who support the operations and/or subsidiaries covered by this Policy, and includes interns, temporary workers, consultants, vendors, third-party providers and contractors. 2. Policy Requirements 2.1. Background Identity Theft is a problem for consumers who must contend with their identities being used to engage in unauthorized or fraudulent activities including illegitimate credit transactions and the negative impact that usage can render to a person's consumer credit score, and for creditors, who must absorb or otherwise write off transactions that result from stolen identities which can affect liquidity and material operations. Because Identity Theft is so closely linked to consumer credit reports, FACTA was passed as an amendment to the Fair Credit Reporting Act ("FCRA"). FACTA primarily addresses: ● Identity Theft Prevention and Credit History Restoration; ● Improvements in Use of and Consumer Access to Credit Information; ● Enhancing the Accuracy of Consumer Report Information; ● Limiting the Use and Sharing of Medical Information in the Financial System; ● Financial Literacy and Education Improvement; and ● Protecting Employee Misconduct Investigations. The regulatory guidance for Identity Theft prevention suggests using 26 specific criteria that are either linked to consumer credit reports or that are normally associated with credit applications. Triggering one or more of those criteria in a credit transaction, regardless of whether a consumer credit report is used or not, is a "Red Flag" that the identity being used in the transaction may be compromised which then requires the creditor to determine an appropriate course of action. This is why the term "Red Flag" is used to describe a creditor's program that seeks to prevent the usage of stolen identities. Usage of the 26 Red Flags is not required, but if not used it is expected that a creditor explain in their program why one or more of the Red Flags have not been used. It is further required that creditors add to their Red Flag program any new criteria that are self-identified as helping to prevent Identity Theft. Due to the complexity required to implement a robust Red Flags program, the Red Flags Program requirements are contained fully within this Policy. The remainder of FACTA does not apply to Square Capital's lending operations either because it is not applicable to a commercial lending program or because consumer credit reports are not currently used by this program. It should be noted that instances of stolen identity used to gain access to the Square Credit Card product are exceedingly unlikely to occur. This is because in order to access the Square Credit Card product, a customer must first be onboarded as a seller and must have sufficient payment processing volume to qualify for the product. FACTA Red Flags should not be confused with Bank Secrecy Act Red Flags which are associated with identifying money laundering risks, not with preventing Identity Theft. 2.2. Identification of Red Flags Square Capital identifies which Red Flags, singly or in combination, are relevant to detecting the possible risk of Identity Theft to customers or the safety and soundness of Square Capital. When identifying relevant Red Flags for Covered Accounts, Square Capital includes relevant Red Flags from the following categories, as appropriate, and documents which are not used along with a justification: ● Alerts, notifications, or other warnings received from consumer reporting agencies or service providers, such as fraud detection services, including third party vendors used for fraud monitoring; ● The presentation of suspicious documents; ● The presentation of suspicious personal identifying information; ● The unusual use of, or other suspicious activity related to, a Covered Account; and ● Notice from customers, victims of Identity Theft, law enforcement authorities, or other persons regarding possible Identity Theft in connection with Covered Accounts. Square Capital considers the following risk factors in identifying relevant Red Flags for Covered Accounts: ● The types of Covered Accounts offered or maintained; ● The methods provided to open Covered Accounts; ● The methods provided to access Covered Accounts; and ● Its previous experience with Identity Theft. Square Capital adds relevant Red Flags from sources such as: ● Incidents of Identity Theft previously experienced; ● Methods of Identity Theft that reflect changes in risk; and ● Applicable supervisory guidance. The list of Red Flags Square Capital considers and their applicability to Square Credit Card are detailed in Appendix I. 2.2.1. Detection of Red Flags Square Capital addresses the detection of Red Flags in connection with the opening of Covered Accounts and existing Covered Accounts, by: ● Obtaining identifying information about, and verifying the identity of, a person opening a Covered Account; and ● Authenticating customers, monitoring transactions, and verifying the validity of change of address requests in the case of existing Covered Accounts. 2.2.2. Response Square Capital provides appropriate responses to detected Red Flags. The response is commensurate with the degree of risk posed. Appropriate responses may include: ● Monitoring a Covered Account for evidence of Identity Theft; ● Contacting the customer; ● Changing any passwords, security codes or other security devices that permit access to a Covered Account; ● Reopening a Covered Account with a new account number; ● Not opening a new Covered Account; ● Closing an existing Covered Account; ● Notifying law enforcement proactively on a case-by-case basis; ● Determining no response is warranted under the particular circumstances; ● Requiring the customer to pass identity verification questions; and ● Requiring the customer to provide documentary identification, such as a driverʼs license, state ID, or passport. In addition, pursuant to Section 609(e) of the Fair Credit Reporting Act, Square Capital will, upon notice from a customer regarding possible Identity Theft and appropriate written request, provide the customer (and law enforcement if requested by the customer) with copies of transaction records relating to the theft of his or her identity within 30 days of receiving the written request to the following address: Square, Inc. Attention: Square Capital LLC 1955 Broadway, Suite 600, Oakland, CA 94612 Incidents of ID theft are verified, tracked and communicated/responded to pursuant to the process defined in the Square Capital Credit Card Closure SOP. Square Capital may request of a customer claiming an instance of ID theft 1.) proof of identity, including a copy of a driverʼs license, government-issued ID card or other identification document and 2.) proof of the claim of ID theft, including a police report regarding the ID theft or completed and signed FTC Identity Theft Report or other affidavit of fact detailing the claim of ID theft. Law enforcement requests for records related to incidents of identity theft are handled pursuant to the requirements set forth in the Credit Card Privacy Policy. 2.2.3. Service Providers Square Capital maintains oversight of service providers that perform activities related to identity verification, fraud detection, or customer authentication. Contracts require service providers to have policies and procedures in place to detect, prevent, and mitigate Identity Theft, consistent with Square Capitalʼs Red Flags Program. 2.2.4. Updating the Program Square Capitalʼs Red Flag Program is updated periodically to reflect changes in risks to customers or to the safety and soundness of the organization from Identity Theft based on factors such as: ● The experiences of the organization with Identity Theft; ● Changes in methods of Identity Theft; ● Changes in methods to detect, prevent and mitigate Identity Theft; and ● Chang
documents.CP-045: Servicemembers Civil Relief Act Policy Square | Square Capital, LLC | Credit Card Last Approved: October 3, 2025 Effective Date: October 3, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Servicemembers Civil Relief Act ("SCRA") Policy ("Policy") outlines Square Capital, LLC's ("Square Capital" or the "Company") approach to compliance with the Servicemembers Civil Relief Act of 2003 (the "SCRA" or the "Act"). The Chief Compliance Officer ("CCO") has adopted this Policy for Square Capital and the CCO oversees the development and implementation of this Policy. The CCO has delegated ownership of this Policy document to the Square Head of Compliance. The purpose of this policy is to facilitate compliance with the SCRA and to uphold Square Capital business requirements that do not run counter to the Act. Scope: This Policy is established by Square Capital and is applicable to Square Capital, specific to the Credit Card Product. Square Capital recognizes a further duty to support the compliance programs of its Bank Partner, where applicable. Definitions: Terminology Definition Active Duty Means full-time duty in the active Military Service of the United States. Such term includes full-time training duty, annual training duty, and attendance while in the active Military Service at a school designated as a service school by law or by the Secretary of the military department concerned. Benefits Refers generally to all reliefs afforded to eligible Servicemembers and qualifying Spouses, children, and dependents. Child For purposes of identifying a dependent child, a person who is unmarried and: Who is under the age of eighteen years; Who, before attaining the age of eighteen years, became permanently incapable of self-support; or Who, after attaining the age of eighteen years and until completion of education or training (but not after attaining the age of twenty-three years), is pursuing a course of instruction at an approved educational institution; AND Who is a legitimate child, A legally adopted child, A stepChild who is a member of a veteran's household or was a member at the time of the veteran's death, Or an illegitimate Child but, as to the alleged father, only if acknowledged in writing signed by him, or if he has been judicially ordered to contribute to the child's support or has been, before his death, judicially decreed to be the father of such child, or if he is otherwise shown by evidence satisfactory to the Secretary to be the father of such child. Coverage Period The amount of time in days for the application of SCRA Benefits and protections. A Coverage Period may be one or more periods of time of no less than 30 consecutive days in length, and may be extended at any time. Interest Includes the money paid regularly at a particular rate for the use of money lent and service charges, renewal charges, fees or any other charges (except bona fide insurance) with respect to a credit obligation or liability. Military Service Is the primary requirement for the Benefits and protections, which is defined as (any): Active Duty, for members of the Army, Navy, Air Force, Marine Corps, or Coast Guard; In the case of a member of the National Guard, includes service under a call to active service authorized by the President or the Secretary of Defense for a period of more than 30 consecutive days under section 502(f) of title 32 for purposes of responding to a national emergency declared by the President and supported by Federal funds; Active service, in the case of a Servicemember who is a commissioned officer of the Public Health Service or the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration; or Any period during which a Servicemember is absent from duty on account of sickness, wounds, leave, or other lawful cause. Servicemember Includes individuals from the following branches of uniformed services: Army; Navy; Air Force; Marine Corps; Coast Guard; Reservists or the National Guard; as well as Commissioned officers of either the Public Health Service or the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration engaged in active service. Spouse The person to whom the Servicemember is legally married. Staff Member(s) All employees and fixed-term and contingent workers who support the operations and/or subsidiaries covered by this Policy, and includes interns, temporary workers, consultants, vendors, third-party providers and contractors. 2. Policy Requirements 2.1. Policy Statement Square Capital is committed to providing the Benefits and protections required by SCRA, as applicable. The objective of this Policy is to ensure that Square Capital provides the Benefits and protections afforded to eligible military personnel and their qualifying family members as prescribed under SCRA, including but not limited to: ● Interest rate waiver on existing debt obligations; ● Fines and penalties under borrower agreement; ● Credit Protection; and ● Prohibitions. 2.2. SCRA Background The SCRA was originally titled the "Soldiers' and Sailors' Civil Relief Act," and was first enacted by Congress in 1918. SCRA in its present form was enacted in 2003 50 U.S.C. Chapter 50, which substantially revised and expanded upon the existing protections. The aim of the SCRA is to enable men and women in the armed services to devote their entire energies to the defense needs of the nation by giving them Benefits and protections regarding certain financial obligations they incur prior to being called into Military Service. SCRA protections apply not only to qualifying Servicemembers but also to their Spouses, children, and dependents as prescribed. 2.3. Operational Elements 2.3.1. Identification of Eligible Servicemember It is the responsibility of the borrower to notify the Company to trigger the application of SCRA Benefits and protections. In order to be eligible for the Benefits and protections available under SCRA, the borrower must satisfy each of the following requirements: ● Be classified as one of the types of Military Service under SCRA or be a qualified Spouse, child, or dependent of such a person; ● Notify Square Capital via phone call, email, or letter that SCRA Benefits are being requested. When the requester is a Servicemember's Spouse, child, or dependent, the requestor must additionally supply: ○ The legal name of the Servicemember; and ○ Statement of relationship to the Servicemember ● The request does not have to specify which SCRA benefit is being requested. ● Required Documentation ○ Square Capital does not require, but the Servicemember or family member may include, along with the request: ■ The Department of Defense (“DODˮ) letter calling for Military Service; or ■ A letter on official letterhead from the office of the Servicememberʼs superior certifying the call to Active Duty status and the dates that it applies; and ■ Provide all of the above documentation no later than 180 days after the date of the Servicemember's termination or release from Military Service. ○ A request that conforms with the above is valid whether it comes from the Servicemember or his/her commanding officer. Military Reservists and Orders to Report for Induction For members of a reservist component ordered to report for Military Service or for persons ordered to report for induction under the Military Selective Service Act, the Servicemember enters "Military Service" on their date of the receipt of the order and exits on the date the Servicemember reports for Military Service (or the date of induction, in the case of such orders). If this service is deemed as Military Service then the Benefits and protections of SCRA may continue to apply. This provision also applies to a qualified Spouse, child, or dependent of an activated Military Reservist. 2.3.2. Verification of Eligible Servicemember Proof of Military Service can be evidenced through a copy of military orders or any other appropriate indicator of Military Service, including a certified letter from a commanding officer. Proof of Military Service can also be evidenced independently by searching the Defense Manpower Data Center (“DMDCˮ) website. All supporting documentation should be retained in the customerʼs file and available upon request. If utilizing the DMDC website to verify Active Duty, results of the search should be printed, dated and retained in the customerʼs file. Proof of Military Service can be provided by the Servicemember. However, if military orders (or similar document(s) as described above) are not received, SCRA protection should not be declined without a reasonable effort to follow-up. Reasonable effort can be demonstrated by any of the following: ● Searching the DMDC website to independently verify Active Duty; and ● Following up with the customer by phone, email, or letter. In accordance with 50 U.S.C. § 3957, SCRA benefits, including the interest rate cap, are triggered by a written request from the Servicemember along with a copy of military orders or other appropriate documentation. Square Capital may elect to apply benefits upon verbal request as a courtesy, but such application does not waive the legal requirement for written documentation The verification of eligibility and the determination of benefit must be finalized and communicated to the customer within 30 calendar days. When confirmed, the applicable Benefits due are to be applied beginning on the start date of the Coverage Period. If confirmation is not performed in this manner, then Square Capital accepts the Servicemember's claim as valid. 2.3.3. Term of Benefits Once verified as an eligible Servicemember, the rights granted to a Servicemember or qualified Spouse, child, or dependent are valid as described below: ● The Coverage Period starts on the first date of Military Service; and ● The Coverage Period and all associated Benefits end when the earlier of the following occurs: ○ The credit card account is closed and paid in full; or ○ Military Service ends. In addition: ● There may be more than one Military Service
documents.CP-046: UDAAP/UDAP Policy Square | Square Capital, LLC | Credit Card Last Approved: Feb 19, 2026 Effective Date: Feb 19, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Policy communicates Square Capital, LLC's commitment to compliance with both consumer and commercial fairness requirements through: ● The Unfair, Deceptive, or Abusive Acts or Practices (“UDAAPˮ) Standards contained in sections 1031 and 1036 of the Dodd-Frank Act ● The Unfair or Deceptive Acts or Practices (“UDAPˮ) provisions in Section 5 of the Federal Trade Commission Act ● State laws prohibiting similar acts or practices in both consumer and commercial contexts This Policy sets forth comprehensive guidelines governing Square Capital, LLC, ensuring consistent operations while maintaining appropriate flexibility to address unique business needs. Square Capital's prevention of UDAAP and UDAP violations requires transparency, truthfulness, suitability, and fairness across all transactions and dealings with both consumers and merchants. The distinction between UDAAP and UDAP requirements reflects the different contexts in which Square Capital, LLC operates: ● Consumer financial products and services (subject to UDAAP under Dodd-Frank) ● Commercial and merchant services (subject to UDAP under the FTC Act) ● Products with potential dual consumer and commercial applications This comprehensive approach ensures appropriate protections while recognizing the different needs and sophistication levels of consumers and commercial customers. Scope: This Policy applies to Square Capital, LLC (collectively, "Square Capital"). This policy does not apply to Square Financial Services (“SFSˮ), Block, Inc.ʼs Utah state-chartered Industrial Bank federally regulated by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation. Definitions: Terminology Definition Abusive An act or practice that: 1 materially interferes with the ability of a consumer to understand a term or condition of a consumer financial product or service; or 2 takes unreasonable advantage of: (a) a consumer's lack of understanding, (b) inability to protect their interests, or (c) reasonable reliance on a covered person. Applies only to consumer transactions] Commercial A business entity or individual obtaining products or services primarily for Consumer business, commercial, or agricultural purposes. Commercial A transaction primarily for business, commercial, or agricultural purposes, Transaction including merchant services and business lending products. Consumer An individual who is a natural person who obtains or has obtained a financial product or service that is used primarily for personal, family or household purposes. Deceptive A representation, omission, act or practice that: 1 misleads or is likely to mislead the consumer or merchant; 2 the consumer's or merchant's interpretation is reasonable under the circumstances; and 3 the misleading representation, omission, act or practice is material. Material Information that is likely to affect a consumer's or merchant's choice of, or Information conduct regarding, a product or service. Staff Member(s) An employee or external worker (e.g., contingent labor, contractors) hired by the Company who supports the operations covered by this Policy. Substantial Injury Financial or other harm that is more than trivial and may include monetary loss, unwarranted health and safety risks, and other forms of economic or social harm. Third-Party Any external entity that performs activities on behalf of Square Capital, Service Provider including vendors, contractors, and service providers who may interact with consumers or merchants. UDAAP Unfair, Deceptive, or Abusive Acts or Practices as prohibited by sections 1031 and 1036 of the Dodd-Frank Act, which apply to consumer financial products and services. UDAP Unfair or Deceptive Acts or Practices as prohibited by Section 5 of the Federal Trade Commission Act, which apply to both consumer and commercial transactions. 2 Unfair An act or practice that: 1 causes or is likely to cause substantial injury to consumers or merchants; 2 cannot be reasonably avoided; and 3 is not outweighed by countervailing benefits to consumers/merchants or to competition. Policy Statement Square Capital is committed to offering both consumer and commercial products in a fair and responsible manner. For consumer financial products, Square Capital complies with UDAAP requirements under the Dodd-Frank Act. For commercial products, Square Capital complies with UDAP requirements under the FTC Act. Square Capital ensures that terms and conditions are communicated clearly and transparently to both consumers and commercial customers. Wherever state or local regulations overlap with, and are stricter than the requirements set out in this Policy, the more conservative or restrictive approach will be applied in those jurisdictions provided such approach is lawful.
