If any applicable laws conflict with this Policy, Square Capital will consult with appropriate Legal Counsel to resolve the conflict and adjust Square Capitalʼs policies and procedures to ensure compliance. 2. Requirements 2.1 General Standards 2.1.1 UDAAP Standards Square Capital's commitment to preventing unfair, deceptive, or abusive acts or practices is demonstrated through comprehensive standards that apply across all entities and operations. These standards establish the foundation for fair treatment of consumers and merchants throughout their relationship with Square Capital. Unfair Acts or Practices: An act or practice is unfair where it causes or is likely to cause substantial injury to Consumers which cannot be reasonably avoided and the injury is not outweighed by countervailing benefits to Consumers or to competition. An act or practice that causes a small amount of harm to a large number of people may be deemed to cause substantial injury. However, an act or practice is not considered to be unfair if Consumers may reasonably avoid injury. Consumers cannot reasonably avoid injury if the act or practice interferes with their ability to make decisions or take action to avoid injury. Public policy, as established by statute, regulation, or judicial decisions, may be considered with other evidence to determine whether an act or practice is unfair and the particular facts in a case are crucial to the determination of unfairness. 3 Square Capital complies with the guidance that discrimination may be considered an unfair act or practice. As such, Square Capital will treat all applicants and Consumers equally, and will not discriminate during the stages of the product lifecycle, including marketing, account opening, servicing, or collections. Deceptive Acts or Practices: An act or practice is deceptive where there is representation omission, or practice that misleads or is likely to mislead Consumers; the Consumer's interpretation of the representation, omission, act or practice is reasonable under the circumstances; and the misleading representation, omission, act or practice is material. A deceptive representation may be an express or implied claim or promise and it may be written or oral. The omission of material information may be deceptive if disclosure of the omitted information is necessary to prevent a Consumer being misled. When representations or marketing practices are targeted to a specific audience, such as the elderly, young people, or financially unsophisticated, the standard is based upon the effects of the act or practice on a reasonable member of that group. Abusive Acts or Practices: An act or practice is abusive if it materially interferes with the ability of a Consumer to understand a term or condition of a product or service or takes unreasonable advantage of: (i) the lack of understanding on the part of the Consumer of the material risks, costs, or conditions in the product or service; (ii) the inability of the Consumer to protect their interests in selecting or using the financial product or service; or (iii) the reasonable reliance by the Consumer on a covered person to act in the interests of the Consumer. 2.1.2 Consumer and Commercial Harm Any unfair, deceptive, or abusive act or practice can result in harm to consumers or merchants. Square Capital seeks to prevent harm through clear, transparent, and accurate delivery of products and services in accordance with applicable federal and state laws and regulations. For consumers, harm may include: ● Actual or potential financial loss ● Non-financial impacts ● Violations of consumer protection laws ● Impacts from compliance management weaknesses For commercial customers, harm may include: ● Business losses or operational impacts ● Disruption to merchant services ● Financial impacts on business operations 4 ● Reputational harm 2.2 Product Design and Delivery 2.2.1 Advertising, Marketing, and Disclosure Guidelines Square Capital maintains comprehensive standards for marketing, advertising, and sales materials across both consumer and commercial products. All communications must present information fairly, clearly, and accurately, with appropriate disclosures of terms, benefits, costs, risks, and material limitations of products or services being offered. This includes any related or optional products and services that may be available to customers. In developing marketing materials, Square Capital prohibits any intentional misrepresentation of terms, benefits, costs, risks, or material limitations, whether through direct statements or by omission. Headlines and promotional language must be truthful and supported by clear, conspicuous disclosures rather than relying on fine print or inconspicuous disclaimers to clarify potentially misleading statements. All representations made in communications must have a reasonable factual basis and be appropriately documented. Square Capital ensures that key terms and conditions are prominently displayed and easily understood, enabling customers to make informed decisions about pr
documents.CP-047: Complaints Standard Afterpay Australia Pty Ltd and Afterpay New Zealand Limited Last Approved: Jan 27, 2026 Effective Date: Jan 27, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This ANZ Complaints Standard (the "Standard") outlines Afterpay Australia Pty Ltd and Afterpay New Zealand Limited's (collectively, "Afterpay ANZ") approach to complaints management and resolution. The purpose of this Standard is to establish comprehensive guidelines governing Afterpay ANZ's complaints management processes, ensuring consistent operations across Australia and New Zealand while maintaining compliance with applicable regulatory requirements. This Standard aligns with the Block Global Complaints Policy and the Block Global Complaints Standard, ensuring that all complaints are identified and resolved in an effective, consistent, and compliant manner. This Standard is designed to provide specific regulatory requirements for managing complaints for Afterpay ANZ and is to be read together with the requirements set out in the complaints handling procedures. Scope: This Standard applies to: ● Afterpay Australia Pty Ltd and Afterpay New Zealand Limited (collectively, "Afterpay ANZ"); ● All directors, officers, and team members of Afterpay ANZ • All full-time employees and fixed-term and contingent workers including interns, temps, consultants, vendors, third-party providers, and contractors; ● All employees and affiliates acting for or on behalf of Afterpay ANZ, across all areas supporting Afterpay ANZ operations; and ● All complaints related to products and services offered by Afterpay ANZ in Australia and New Zealand This Standard is applicable globally across all Afterpay business lines, subsidiaries, and jurisdictions for transactions that apply to Australia and New Zealand. 1 Definitions: Terminology Definition Afterpay Afterpay Australia Pty Ltd and Afterpay New Zealand Limited that offers, among other things, a 'Buy Now Pay Later' facility. Australian Credit A license issued by the Australian Securities and Investments Commission Licence ACL ASIC for credit activities. Afterpay An internal governance committee made up of senior management Governance, responsible for the oversight of Afterpay's compliance with applicable laws Risk and and its key risks. Compliance Committee GRC Committee) Block, Inc. Block, Inc., is a Delaware corporation and the ultimate parent of Afterpay. Board The Board of Directors of Afterpay. Business Units Organisations within Afterpay responsible for the products and services offered by Afterpay. Complaint An expression of dissatisfaction made to or about an organisation, related to its product, services, staff or the handling of a complaint, where a response or resolution is explicitly or implicitly expected or legally required. In accordance with RG 271 and AS/NZS 100022014. Complaint Tier Classification of complaints based on risk level: Tier 1 High Risk), Tier 2 Medium Risk), Tier 3 Low Risk). Dispute Chargebacks or allegations of an unauthorized transaction. Disputes are handled by specialized teams and not via the complaint process, unless the customer expresses dissatisfaction about the dispute process itself. External Dispute External schemes for resolving disputes: AFCA Australia) and FDRS New Resolution EDR Zealand). Financial Registration status in New Zealand with the New Zealand Companies Office. Services Provider FSP Hardship A complaint relating to financial hardship, treated as urgent matters requiring Complaint resolution within 21 calendar days. 2 Internal Dispute Afterpay's internal process for managing complaints in compliance with Resolution IDR regulatory standards. Procedure Obligations The actionable requirements within applicable laws, rules and regulations, and enforceable regulatory guidance for Afterpay products and services. Staff Member(s) An employee or external worker (e.g., contingent labor, contractors) hired by the Company who supports the operations covered by this Standard. 2. Afterpay-Specific Requirements AFTERPAY'S COMPLAINTS MANAGEMENT OBLIGATIONS: 2.1. Afterpay Australia In Australia, Afterpay is: ● A holder of an Australian Credit Licence ACL with the Australian Securities and Investments Commission ASIC. As such, Afterpay must have an internal dispute resolution IDR procedure that complies with standards and requirements made or approved by ASIC in accordance with the regulations and covers disputes in relation to credit activities engaged in by itself or its representatives. ● Accredited as a Code Compliant Member of the Buy Now Pay Later Code of Practice BNPL Code). Under the BNPL Code, Afterpay has obligations to ensure that the Complaints Policy is visible and easily accessible on the Afterpay website and to comply with ASIC's standards and requirements in relation to complaint resolution. ● A member of an external dispute resolution EDR scheme in Australia and is a member of the Australian Financial Complaints Authority AFCA, membership number 45772. 2.2. Afterpay New Zealand In New Zealand, Afterpay is: ● Registered as a Financial Services Provider FSP with the New Zealand Companies Office. ● A member of an external dispute resolution EDR scheme in New Zealand and is a member of the FDRS (membership number FM4499. 3 2.3. Definition of Complaint In accordance with RG 271 and AS/ NZS 10002 2014, Afterpay defines a complaint as the following: “An expression of dissatisfaction made to or about an organisation, related to its product, services, staff or the handling of a complaint, where a response or resolution is explicitly or implicitly expected or legally requiredˮ . 2.4. Afterpay’s IDR procedure Afterpay maintains an IDR procedure that complies with the standards and requirements required by regulation. This procedure is maintained by the Operations team and is reviewed on a periodic basis. This procedure complies with the obligations set out in ASIC Regulatory Guide RG271, Part B of the Buy Now Pay Later Code of Practice BNPL, AFCA Rules; ASIC RG96 Debt Collection Guidelines and is in place to support Afterpay in meeting the timeframe and response requirements for effectively managing complaints. An overview of these requirements are provided in Annexure A - IDR Timeframes and Responses. 2.5. External Dispute Resolution Membership Renewal of the annual membership to both AFCA and the FDRS is performed by the EDR team and is reported by the Operations team to the GRC Committee on an annual basis. 2.6. Complaints Resolution Complaint resolutions operate in accordance with the delegations set out within the Goodwill Refunds - Complaint Resolution document. This document is reviewed on an annual basis by the Afterpay Global Complaints Leader. 2.7. Customer Remediation Customer remediation requirements are outlined in the Afterpay Compliance Incident and Breach Management Program. Any customer remediation for a complaint must follow these requirements and include: ● 9 key principles to ensure Afterpayʼs remediation process is effective when delivering outcomes to our customers; and ● A tailored customer remediation approach for Reportable Situations that takes into account Regulatory Guide 277 Customer Remediation requirements. 2.8. Ongoing Monitoring and Governance Complaints are monitored on an ongoing basis by the Operations Team. Reporting of complaints is performed by the Operations Team to ANZ senior management on a monthly basis, quarterly to the GRC Committee and annually to the Board. 4 In addition, the Internal Testing and Monitoring Team performs periodic reviews to confirm that Afterpay is managing complaints in line with its regulatory requirements. 3. Compliance & Enforcement 3.1. Roles and Responsibilities: Block, Inc. Roles: ● Global Complaints Leader: ○ Responsible for overseeing global complaints management program ○ Provides monthly complaint reports on volumes, trends, and insights to Afterpay ANZ leadership ○ Serves as point of contact for complaint data and supporting commentary for Board reports ● Global Head of Afterpay Lending Operations: ○ Responsible for oversight of customer service operations ○ Escalates complaint trends and volumes as appropriate ○ Oversight for root cause analysis and remedial action decisions Afterpay Roles: ● Head of Compliance, ANZ ○ Document Owner for this Standard ○ Responsible for approving exceptions to this Standard ○ Approves non-material changes to this Standard ○ Ensures appropriate practices are in place to support compliance ○ Monitors and reports on compliance with this Standard ● Operations Team: ○ Maintains the IDR procedure ○ Manages the Complaint Log ○ Monitors complaints on an ongoing basis ○ Reports complaints monthly to ANZ senior management ○ Reports quarterly to the GRC Committee and annually to the Board ○ Performs EDR membership renewal reporting ● Internal Testing and Monitoring Team: ○ Performs periodic reviews to confirm Afterpay is managing complaints in line with regulatory requirements 3.2. Monitoring and Reporting 5 On an ongoing basis, the Head of Compliance, ANZ and/or their designee monitors activities for compliance and objective achievement. As outlined in the Compliance Management System Policy, Compliance will provide regular updates, as applicable, to the Block, Inc. Board and executive management. Reporting may cover specific information related to: ● Policies and standards ● Procedures ● Audit/exam findings ● Issues management ● Training ● Complaints 3.3. Training All relevant personnel will receive training at least annually covering applicable requirements and guidance to implement this Standard effectively. All training-related activities are monitored by the Compliance Team and are outlined in the Compliance Training Program. New hires must receive complaint training before servicing complaints and must complete annual training thereafter. 3.4. Record Retention All complaint logs and associated data must be retained for a mini

Canada’s Anti-Spam Canada's anti-spam legislation (CASL) protects consumers and Legislation (“CASL”) businesses from the misuse of digital technology, including spam and other electronic threats. Consumer Protection Act An Act to provide for better protection of the interests of (“CPA”) consumers Staff Members All employees and fixed-term and contingent workers who support the operations and/or subsidiaries covered by this Program, and include interns, temporary workers, consultants, vendors, third-party providers, and contractors Canada Marketing Standard | Afterpay Canada Limited 4 Confidential and Not for Distribution 1. Introduction This Marketing and Deceptive or Abusive Acts and Practices Standard ( "Standard") has been adopted by the board of Afterpay Canada Inc. (the "Board") to ensure compliance with applicable law in all of Afterpay Canada Ltd.'s ("Afterpay") activities including laws and regulations relating to Misleading, Deceptive, Abusive Acts or Practices and the Canadian Anti-Spam Legislation ("CASL"). 2. Purpose Afterpay is committed to ensuring that its management, employees, officers, directors, and third-party vendors both refrain from acts or practices that could be considered deceptive or abusive and comply with CASL. Afterpay is committed to structuring financial products and conducting marketing in such a way as to achieve compliance with this objective. If any applicable laws are in conflict with this Standard, employees and management must report the issue to the Head of Compliance, Afterpay Canada. 3. Scope This Standard applies to Afterpay Canada Ltd. and all subsidiary and affiliate companies over which it exercises control.It is every Staff Member’s responsibility to adhere to this Standard. 4. Standard Requirements 4.1 General Principles The Canadian Competition Act and various provincial laws define Deceptive or Abusive Acts and Practices. Deceptive acts and practices are those material representations, omissions, acts, or practices that mislead or are likely to mislead a consumer. Structuring Financial Products It is Afterpay’s Standard to structure any financial product in such a way as to ensure that such products could not be considered deceptive or abusive and the delivery of such products and services are compliant with all applicable Canadian laws including CASL. In order to achieve this objective, Afterpay will abide by the following principles: Canada Marketing Standard | Afterpay Canada Limited 5 Confidential and Not for Distribution ● Afterpay will not assess any hidden fees not disclosed to the consumer in advance; ● Afterpay will monitor its portfolio and address its underwriting criteria as appropriate to ensure that consumers are making responsible use of its financial products; and ● In order to ensure compliance with this directive, all new product structures or changes in the structure of any financial product offered by Afterpay and related communication or marketing will be subject to the review and approval of Afterpay’s legal and compliance team. Marketing and Advertising Ensuring that consumers adequately understand financial products is an important consideration for avoiding deceptive or abusive acts and practices. Afterpay is committed to ensuring fair, accurate, and transparent representation of its products in all consumer communications. It is Afterpay’s Standard to review in advance all consumer-facing marketing material for potential concerns. Afterpay has implemented a review program led by Afterpay’s compliance team with appropriate controls to ensure that each marketing piece used by Afterpay and the associated channel and placement is reviewed and approved by Afterpay’s legal and compliance team, prior to use. The creator of any customer-facing content, whether marketing or customer communication, is responsible for ensuring the actions set out in this Standard are completed, including the approval process being complied with. No content is to be published or sent to customers (directly or through mass marketing) without obtaining the required Compliance and Product Counsel approvals. In addition, certain merchant partners will independently market and/or advertise the Afterpay product on that merchant’s website or through other marketing efforts. Afterpay will ensure that all merchants are contractually obligated to use only marketing material approved by Afterpay. Merchants will be provided with pre-approved marketing material reviewed and approved by Afterpay’s legal and compliance team. Afterpay’s marketing team will instruct merchants to submit any marketing material that deviates from pre-approved materials to Afterpay for review and approval prior to first use. Afterpay’s legal and compliance team will review and approve all merchant marketing materials prior to giving approval to merchants. Canada Marketing Standard | Afterpay Canada Limited 6 Confidential and Not for Distribution CASL Afterpay is committed to ensuring compliance with CASL obligations, including the requirement of obtaining consent for sending Commercial Electronic Messages (“CEMs”) in any electronic form, including email, text messages, instant messages and social media. As part of Afterpay’s marketing material review process, Canada Compliance will review all materials to ensure messages deemed CEMs are CASL-compliant and will include the ‘unsubscribe’ language and mechanism. 4.2 Fair Lending and Complaint Management Appropriate team members will receive training on the Canadian Consumer Protection Act on an annual basis as part of Afterpay’s training curriculum. 4.3 Social Media Afterpay maintains multiple social media accounts for the purposes of marketing and community engagement. These social media accounts may allow third parties to add publicly visible comments or materials (“Social Media Comments”) which become associated with Afterpay. Afterpay will regularly monitor Social Media Comments and take action where such comments could result in a material misunderstanding or misperception. 4.4 Record Retention In order to ensure compliance with this Standard and applicable law, Afterpay will maintain records of reviewed and approved marketing material for a minimum period of two (2) years. 4.5 Training All Afterpay Canada personnel will receive training on Deceptive or Abusive Acts and Practices as part of Afterpay’s training curriculum during an Afterpay onboarding session and on an annual basis as part of Afterpay’s training curriculum. Canada Marketing Standard | Afterpay Canada Limited 7 Confidential and Not for Distribution 5. Quebec Specific Regulations Afterpay recognizes the importance of adhering to Quebec-specific regulations concerning marketing and advertising practices. Quebec has distinct consumer protection laws, notably the Consumer Protection Act (“CPA”) and the Charter of the French Language (“Bill 101”), which impose additional requirements on businesses operating within the province. 5.1 Compliance with Consumer Protection Act Under the CPA, businesses operating in Quebec must ensure that all marketing and advertising materials are clear, accurate, and transparent. This includes providing comprehensive information about products and services offered by Afterpay, including any fees, terms, and conditions associated with financial products. Afterpay will ensure that all marketing materials targeting consumers in Quebec comply with the requirements outlined in the CPA. 5.2 Compliance with the Charter of the French Language (Bill 101) The Charter of the French Language mandates that businesses operating in Quebec use French as the primary language in all commercial communications, including advertising and marketing materials. Afterpay will ensure that all marketing and advertising materials distributed in Quebec comply with the language requirements set forth in Bill 101. This includes ensuring that French language versions of marketing materials are readily available and accessible to consumers in Quebec. 5.3 Ongoing Compliance Monitoring Afterpay will implement procedures for ongoing compliance monitoring to ensure that all marketing and advertising materials distributed in Quebec adhere to the requirements outlined in the CPA and Bill 101. This includes regular audits of marketing materials, as well as monitoring consumer feedback and complaints related to marketing practices in Quebec. 5.4 Coordination with Quebec-Specific Legal Counsel A

French Language Standard | Afterpay Canada Limited 4 Confidential and Not for Distribution 1. Introduction Afterpay Canada Limited (“Afterpay Canada”) is committed to providing an inclusive and compliant experience for all its customers, including those in the Province of Quebec. The French Language Standard (the “Standard”) outlines the importance of operating with knowledge of the French language and the necessary steps to ensure compliance with Quebec's French Language laws. 2. Purpose Afterpay Canada's French Language Standard is crucial for ensuring legal compliance and enhancing customer experience in Quebec. By aligning its operations with Quebec's language laws, Afterpay Canada can strengthen its market position and demonstrate its commitment to inclusive and respectful business practices. Importance of French Language Compliance Legal Requirements in Quebec In adherence to the Charter of the French Language (Bill 101), businesses in Quebec must recognize French as the primary language for commercial activities. This extends to contractual agreements, advertisements, and communications. Failure to comply with these legal requirements could lead to severe consequences, including fines and legal actions. Ensuring consistent compliance is paramount for Afterpay Canada to operate seamlessly in Quebec and build a positive reputation. Consumer Expectations Understanding and respecting the linguistic preferences of consumers in Quebec is crucial for building trust and maintaining positive customer relationships. 3. Scope This Standard is established by Afterpay Canada and is applicable to all Staff Members. 4. Standard Requirements Afterpay Canada is committed to fostering a positive and inclusive experience for all customers, and adherence to Quebec's French language laws is a key component of this commitment. By French Language Standard | Afterpay Canada Limited 5 Confidential and Not for Distribution prioritizing compliance and providing French language services, Afterpay Canada aims to meet the linguistic preferences of customers in Quebec. 4.1 French Language Laws in Quebec Charter of the French Language (Bill 101) This legislation establishes French as the official language of Quebec and outlines specific requirements for businesses regarding the use of French in various aspects of their operations. Consumer Protection Act The Consumer Protection Act enforces regulations related to consumer transactions and includes provisions regarding language rights. Regulations Respecting Language of Commerce and Business These regulations provide additional guidance on the use of French in commercial activities, including requirements for contracts and advertising. 4.2 Ensuring Compliance with French Language Laws Language of Contracts and Agreements Afterpay Canada understands the importance of language in legal documents. As mandated by Quebec's legal framework, contracts must be available in French. In case of any differences between English and French versions, the French version takes precedence. Failure to comply with this requirement can lead to legal disputes, potentially harming Afterpay Canada's reputation and finances. Customer Communication Afterpay Canada prioritizes effective communication with its customers. This includes ensuring that emails and notifications are available in French. By default, customers have the option to receive communications in French. For instance, Afterpay Canada enables language preferences in customer profiles, allowing users to set their preferred language for communications. Website and Mobile App Afterpay Canada’s digital platforms are vital for customer interaction. It is not just a legal obligation but also crucial for enhancing customer experience that Afterpay Canada’s website and mobile app French Language Standard | Afterpay Canada Limited 6 Confidential and Not for Distribution are fully accessible in French. Inadequate language support on these platforms can result in customer frustration, loss of trust, and reduced user engagement. Therefore, Afterpay Canada prioritizes localization of the user interface and content to align with the linguistic preferences of the Quebec market. Customer Support Afterpay Canada ensures that its customer support services are available in French. This includes providing French-language support through phone assistance, live chat, and email communication. For instance, Afterpay Canada employs bilingual customer support representatives or provides access to dedicated French-speaking support teams. 4.3 Providing French Language Services French Language Customer Support Customer support is often the frontline for consumer interaction. In Quebec, offering dedicated French language customer support services is not just a legal obligation; it's an essential aspect of customer satisfaction. Inadequate French language support may lead to misunderstandings, dissatisfaction, and potential regulatory scrutiny. Therefore, the availability of proficient French-speaking support agents and clear communication channels in French is crucial. Marketing and Promotional Materials All marketing and promotional materials targeting customers in Quebec must be available in French. Example: Create French-language versions of promotional materials, advertisements, and campaigns tailored to the Quebec market. 4.4 Training and Awareness Employee Training Train employees on the importance of French language compliance and the specific requirements outlined in Quebec's language laws. Example: Conduct regular training sessions for customer support representatives on the use of French in customer interactions. French Language Standard | Afterpay Canada Limited 7 Confidential and Not for Distribution Internal Communication Establish internal communication practices that reinforce the significance of French language compliance. Example: Distribute internal guidelines and reminders to all employees emphasizing the use of French in official communications. 4.5 Regular Audits and Updates Compliance Audits Conduct regular audits to ensure ongoing compliance with Quebec's language laws. Example: Schedule periodic audits of customer communications, contracts, and marketing materials to identify and rectify any language discrepancies. Updating Policies and Practices Stay informed about changes to language laws and update internal policies and practices accordingly. Example: Regularly review and update language policies based on amendments to Quebec's language laws. 4.6 Consequences of Non-Compliance Non-compliance with Quebec's language laws can have severe consequences. Fines and penalties are not only financial burdens but also tarnish the reputation of the business. Additionally, legal actions resulting from non-compliance may lead to protracted disputes that can be resource-intensive and time-consuming. It is imperative that all employees are aware of the potential repercussions of not adhering to French language laws and that compliance is treated with the utmost seriousness. Regular training and updates should reinforce this awareness throughout the organization. French Language Standard | Afterpay Canada Limited 8 Confidential and Not for Distribution 4.7 Reporting For questions or concerns regarding Afterpay Canada's French Language Compliance, please contact the Afterpay Canada Compliance Team. 5. Document Management 5.1 Document Owner This document is owned by the Head of Compliance Canada or their designee. 5.2 Scheduled Review & Ad-Hoc Maintenance This document shall be reviewed and approved at least annually and on an ad-hoc basis in accordance with the Compliance Document Approval Matrix. 6. Exceptions Exceptions to this document must be pre-approved by the Head of Compliance Canada or their designee. Staff members may submit exception requests for review at go/policyhelp. 7. documents.CP-052: Acquiring Chargebacks Standard Cash App Last Approved: Aug 8, 2025 Effective Date: Aug 8, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: Cash App's Acquiring Chargebacks Standard explains how Cash App processes Chargebacks received from our Customers' banks. When a Customer uses their credit or debit card for a transaction, they might later question or dispute that payment with their bank. This can happen for various reasons, such as: ● Unauthorized card use ● Unhappiness with a product or service ● Other transaction concerns The Chargeback Process Protocol outlines at a high level how this process works. 1. The cardholder initiates a dispute through their financial institution Issuing Bank) 2. Upon review, the Issuing Bank may initiate a formal chargeback procedure through the card network 3. The chargeback notification is transmitted to Cash App through established network channels 4. As the merchant of record, Cash App evaluates the chargeback and may: a. Conduct a transaction review b. Submit a response to validate the original transaction c. Accept Chargeback liability The Chargeback process adheres to the standardized protocols established by card network regulations to ensure proper dispute resolution. Scope: This document is relevant for Acquiring Chargebacks related to Cash App transactions conducted through debit or credit card, excluding Cash App Pay. Cash App is a line of business under Block, Inc., and is applicable to Cash App U.S. As of July 2025, true fraud evaluations (see 2.1.1 New Chargebacks) are not supported for AmEx or any payments still processed through the Cash Service Franklin. Addressing AmEx and migrating the remaining Franklin payments to new services is planned for Q3 '25. This means by Q4'25 all transaction types should be supported. Acquiring Chargebacks Standard ┃Cash App 1 Definitions: Terminology Definition Acquirer An Acquiring or Merchant bank. A financial institution that provides services to Merchants that enable them to accept branded credit and debit cards for payment. Arbitration The final stage of the Chargeback lifecycle where the Network makes a binding decision to resolve a Chargeback when all prior resolution attempts have failed. Chargeback It is a financial mechanism in which a debit or credit card transaction is reversed by the cardholder's issuing bank. The Merchant who processed that transaction may accept the liability or represent the Chargeback with evidence of a valid transaction. Chargeback Cancellation This is the return of funds from a cardholder's issuing bank after a Chargeback was thought to be resolved as Merchant liability. Chargeback Reversed The Issuer accepts the Merchant Chargeback Representment as valid or otherwise does not continue the Chargeback; funds remain or are returned to Merchant; often referred to as "Won" by Merchant. Chargeback Upheld The Issuer continues the Chargeback process after receipt of the Merchant Chargeback Representment and maintains Merchant liability; generally referred to as "Lost" by Merchant. Clawback A term that refers to Cash App retrieving funds from a Consumer to cover the cost of a Chargeback that was received. Clawback Reversal / The return of funds retrieved from a Consumer to cover the cost Reimbursements of a Chargeback that was received after a Chargeback Cancellation event. First Party Fraud / This occurs when a consumer uses their own information to Friendly Fraud commit fraudulent activities, such as falsely disputing a legitimate transaction or misrepresenting information. Issuer This is the financial institution, and member of the Card network, such as Visa, who issues a prepaid, debit or credit card. Merchant A business or sole proprietor that accepts payments for goods or services. Acquiring Chargebacks Standard ┃Cash App 2 Terminology Definition Network The entities that facilitate the movement of funds into and out of debit and credit card products and establish rules that other players must adhere to. Pull Transactions A customer initiates a purchase or funding transaction from their linked debit or credit card into the Cash ecosystem. Push Transactions Transfers from one account to another account that are initiated from the account sending funds. Peer To Peer (“P2Pˮ) A Peer-to-Peer transaction refers to a payment from one individual to another individual. Representment The opportunity for an Acquirer/Merchant to respond to a received Chargeback with details about the customer and/or transaction. Template / Chargeback The documentation an Acquirer/Merchant provides in response to Response a Chargeback. 2. Standard Requirements 2.1 Chargeback Initiation When a Chargeback is initiated by a cardholder with their issuing bank, it first enters the Cash App ecosystem via Acquirer reports (like AmEx) or network gateways (like VROL, Mastercom and Discover Direct). This ultimately lands in the Cash Finplat Disputes CFD service which acts as the initial intake and validation layer. This service performs several critical checks, including validating the Chargeback details and ensuring proper formatting. The system processes various types of Chargebacks-related notifications, from initial Chargeback alerts to resolution updates. The Chargeback then flows to the Chargeback handling service Payback, which is responsible for the core decision-making process. Payback checks if the payment exists in the system and validates its details. 2.1.1 Chargeback Handling Once a Chargeback notification has been received by Payback, the Cash App process for handling the Chargeback takes place. This section covers handling for Cash App Pull Transactions. ● Auto-defended Chargebacks Sometimes, our Acquiring partners may action an incoming Chargeback on our behalf by responding with Representment documentation. Generally, this is when a Chargeback occurs on a transaction that is already refunded, or when the Chargeback received is out of compliance with network rules. In these instances, we receive notice of the Chargeback and that it has already been defended, and updates later on about the final outcome. Acquiring Chargebacks Standard ┃Cash App 3 ● New Chargebacks When a new Chargeback is received, first, for Chargebacks with a fraud reason code, a call is made to our risk service to evaluate whether the Chargeback is likely to be a result of truly unauthorized cardholder use (as opposed to Friendly or First Party Fraud). The risk evaluation utilizes an ML Model to determine if the transaction was 1. the result of stolen card usage or 2. as the result of an account takeover. If the ML model determines either of these scenarios exist, Cash App will accept the Chargeback and no further action is taken. If not, the Chargeback will continue down the processing flow. For Chargebacks deemed to not be the result of true fraud, the next step in the process is attempting a Clawback according to the existing policies (see section 2.7 Clawback Policies). If a Clawback is successful, the Chargeback will be accepted and no further action is taken. If a Clawback is not successful, the processing will continue to the next step. Payback will then evaluate whether the Chargeback meets any other auto-accept criteria. Remaining auto-accept criteria is generally based on reason code, Issuers, networks and transaction amounts, representing Cash App business logic that is subject to change based on business needs. Any Chargebacks that meet the current auto-accept criteria will be accepted. At this point, any remaining Chargeback is meant to be responded to. ● Chargeback Response Template Selection Umpire, our Chargeback Representment service, determines what Template to utilize based on transaction details, Chargeback reason code, and available evidence. The evidence might include details about the customer's account, transaction records, or other documentation that supports the transaction's validity and will be useful for the issuing banks' assessment of their customers' claim (see more details in section 2.8. Chargeback Response Details). Umpire ensures all challenge responses are properly formatted. ● Manually Handled Chargebacks There is only one exception to the automated Template selection, which is AmEx Chargebacks. At this time, there is no automated handling for AmEx Chargeback Responses, so the response Template selection and processing is handled manually by members of the Disputes Operations team. Other actions Chargeback updates and record keeping, Clawback policies, and monitoring) are handled the same. ● Timelines The Chargeback process overall has timelines governed by the card networks and procedures of the different parties involved (issuing banks, card schemes, and Merchant banks). However, for Cash App, the automated processes take 48 hours to run and respond each time a Chargeback notification is received. This ensures Cash App remains in compliance with all network response time requirements and gives Issuers ample time to review responses on their end for follow up with their cardholder. Acquiring Chargebacks Standard ┃Cash App 4 Manually handled Chargebacks have due dates visible to the Disputes Operations team handling responses, corresponding to the network's timeframe requirements for Merchant responses. Any failure to respond to an incoming Chargeback Response on the Cash App side in time, or at all, would result in Cash App accepting liability for the Chargeback. 2.2 Updates to Existing Chargebacks Once an Issuer receives the Cash Chargeback Response, they will evaluate the details provided along with their internal records and determine whether they accept the response and reverse the Chargeback, or would like to continue the Chargeback. 2.3 Chargeback Reversed In instances where Issuers accept the Cash App response, Cash App considers the Chargeback Reversed Cash App "Won") as the funds remain or are returned to Cash App. No further actions are taken. 2.4 Chargeback Upheld In instances where Issuers would li

Cash App adheres to the requirements outlined in the Block, Inc. Global Complaint Policy and Program. Complaints provide valuable insight into the operations of an organization and often help identify and raise issues involving consumer harm from UDAAP violations, discrimination, or other regulatory violations. documents.CP-055: Fraud Risk Management Standard Cash App | U.S. Last Approved: Jan 12, 2026 Effective Date: Jan 12, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Fraud Risk Management Standard ("Standard"), established by Cash App, U.S., a Line of Business under Block, Inc., outlines Cash App's strategy to detect, prevent, deter, and report on individuals who attempt to defraud Cash App Customers, gain access to Customer accounts, or move money using Cash App with fraudulent intent. The Consumer Protection Compliance Program Lead and/or their designee is responsible for management of this Standard. This Fraud Risk Management Standard is established by Compliance. References to "staff members" throughout this document shall refer to all employees or external workers (e.g., contingent labor, contractors) hired by Block, Inc. who supports the operations covered by this Standard. Cash App conducts business in compliance with applicable laws and regulations, maintaining and managing a combination of risk and compliance programs. The purpose of this Standard is to define the Fraud Risk Management Program and outline Cash App's strategy to ensure it upholds adequate risk and fraud mitigation strategies in line with industry expectations and relevant regulatory obligations. The activities in support of this Standard are administered cross-functionally and are designed to prevent and mitigate fraudulent activity on Cash App. Scope: This Standard is established by Cash App and is applicable to the Cash App business in the U.S. It defines the categories of fraudulent events, outlines the responsibilities of Staff to mitigate fraudulent activities and remove perpetrators of fraud from Cash App. Definitions: Terminology Definition Account Takeover ("ATO") When a third party maliciously gains access to a Consumer's account with the intention of using it for fraudulent purposes. Chargebacks When a cardholder files a dispute with their card issuing bank for a transaction funded by a linked debit or credit card. Consumer Consumer refers to an individual who is a natural person who obtains or has obtained a financial product or service that is to be used primarily for personal, family or household purposes. Customer Any individual, entity, or organization that has engaged in a transaction, purchased goods or services, or interacted with Block in a manner that establishes a direct relationship or affiliation. Denylisting The process by which Cash App accounts are closed after a determination or suspicion of involvement in harmful, fraudulent or illegal activity on Cash App. Fraud Any intentional act to obtain access to a Consumer's funds by deception, deceit, misrepresentation, or without authority. Fraudster An individual who commits an intentional act to gain access to or take Consumer funds without authority. Inauthentic Identities A label applied when Cash App has a reasonable level of knowledge that the underlying account holder is not the same as the identity that was used to successfully pass the identity verification process. Know Your Customer ("KYC") A legal requirement designed to prevent and monitor for identity theft, money laundering, terrorist financing, and other types of illegal activity by identifying a Consumer and understanding the purpose of the Consumer's account. Terminology Definition Machine Learning Model A machine learning model is a data transformation that has been ("MLM") trained to recognize certain types of behaviors. A model is trained on a set of data, providing it an algorithm that it can use to reason over and learn from. Fueled by data, machine learning models are the mathematical engines of artificial intelligence. Scam A dishonest scheme or activity designed to deceive individuals by persuading them to make an authorized transaction sent under false pretenses. Scammer A type of Fraudster that specializes in perpetrating Scams. Staff Members An employee or external worker (e.g., contingent labor, contractors) hired by the Company who supports operations covered by this Standard. Synthetic Identity A fake identity that is used to create a new, untraceable identity made up of various data points that do not correlate to a real identity. 2. Standard Requirements 2.1 Consumer Education Cash App believes that providing proactive education to Consumers about the risk of Fraud and Scams, among other measures, is effective in helping to prevent fraud on Cash App. Cash App provides education using a variety of communication methods and platforms on the most common types of Fraud and Scams and informs Consumers about ways they can protect themselves and keep their money safe. Consumer education is updated when necessary to ensure that the information is current and relevant and includes emerging types of fraudulent activity. 2.2 Consumer Onboarding Consumer Onboarding is a critical component of Cash App's Fraud Risk Management strategy as it serves as the primary process for ensuring that Cash App knows who it is allowing to use its platform and protects Consumers from having an account created as a result of identity theft. Know Your Customer ("KYC") allows for the reasonable understanding of the types of transactions a Consumer may engage in and the determination of when transactions may be suspicious and need further investigation. Cash App's KYC Policy documents the core components that influence Cash App's fraud risk management strategy which include: ● Customer Risk Rating ("CRR"): During account onboarding, as well as on an ongoing basis, accounts receive a Customer Risk Rating, which allows for assessing the risks associated with the Consumer, such as identifying those who may be at a higher risk for perpetrating illegal and/or prohibited activity on Cash App. ● Customer Due Diligence ("CDD") and Enhanced Due Diligence ("EDD"): Cash App performs ongoing CDD to understand the nature and purpose of the Customer relationship. As part of this program, EDD is performed on certain higher risk accounts and Customers. During CDD and EDD reviews, Compliance specialists conduct holistic reviews of accounts, which includes reviewing the Customer's identity information (e.g. IDV information, documents, source of funds / source of wealth, display names and $cashtag). As part of these reviews, if there are indicators of fraudulent activity, specialists may denylist the account, or escalate the account to the Financial Crimes team for further review. ● KYC Refresh: Cash App maintains a KYC refresh program which helps to ensure, using a risk-based approach, that up-to-date Customer information is maintained on transacting, active Cash App users. Cash App also maintains an Identity Verification Standard, which outlines Cash App's strategy for verifying Customer's identity through the Identity Verification ("IDV") process on Cash App. The IDV process allows Cash App to maintain compliance with the KYC requirements under the Bank Secrecy Act ("BSA") and its implementing regulations. It also serves as a process reasonably designed to prevent Inauthentic Identities from engaging on Cash App. Core components supporting Cash App's management of fraud on the platform include: ● Social Security Number ("SSN") Denylisting: Cash App maintains an SSN denylist, which is an internal list of SSNs that are associated with accounts that have had certain illicit or prohibited activity on Cash App. Once an SSN is added to the SSN denylist and is used again during IDV, that account is automatically denylisted. ● Inauthentic/Stolen Identities: In addition to collecting and verifying identity information electronically, and in some cases via documentation, Cash App maintains machine learning models designed to detect inauthentic or stolen identities. These models may block IDV attempts, suspend accounts, or escalate to manual review based on suspicion of fraud. ● Deceased Identities: To further prevent identity theft, as part of its IDV process, Cash App automatically denylists accounts (along with their SSN in the event the vendor returns a signal that the SSN is associated with a deceased individual. ● Transaction Limits: Cash App has a risk-based, graduated identity verification process. When a Customer signs up for a Cash App account, they can send (or Cash App Pay) and receive P2P at lower amounts prior to going through identity verification. Payments may only be sent or withdrawn using a linked financial instrument from a U.S. depository institution (i.e., can not send P2P from stored balance). If a Customer chooses to engage more with Cash App, which includes access to other products and features, being able to send money from stored balance and higher transaction limits, Customers are required to successfully pass full IDV. ● Elder Abuse Reviews: To manage the risks associated with Elder Financial Exploitation, Cash App maintains dedicated processes that focus on the proactively identifying instances of fraud/exploitation of elders, and takes action accordingly. Cash App manually reviews accounts where criteria entered in the IDV process meets defined thresholds. This enhanced review process helps to ensure that Cash App is identifying, deterring and escalating instances of elder abuse on the platform, inclusive of fraud and identity theft. 2.3 Transaction Mitigation Measures Cash App maintains processes and controls designed to reduce the likelihood that Consumers are involved in fraudulent activity. These processes and controls are based on signals associated with an account, as well as the underlying activity within the account, such as prior Scam reports, links to denylisted accounts, suspicious payment notes or abnormal Customer transaction activity. Cash App employs mitigating actions to help disrupt fraudulent transactions as outlined below. ● Consumer Fraud Warning: If a transaction has potential signals related to a Scam, a Consumer Fraud Warning for the sender will alert about the possibility of transferring funds to a Scamme
