documents.CP-058: Customer Communication Standard Squareup International Limited Last Approved: Dec 17, 2025 Effective Date: Dec 17, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Customer Communication Standard (“Standardˮ) outlines Squareup International Limitedʼs (“SILˮ) approach for designing, testing, and monitoring customer communications. The Head of Compliance is responsible for management of this Standard. The purpose of this Standard is to ensure that SIL communicates with customers in a manner that is clear, fair, and not misleading, supporting good outcomes and consumer understanding while meeting applicable laws and regulatory expectations. Scope: This Standard applies to all oral and written customer communications across SILʼs channels, including but not limited to: ● Website and app content ● Onboarding and welcome communications ● Account updates ● Marketing and promotional materials ● Product disclosures (e.g., terms, pricing, and fees) ● Sales and Customer support interactions ● Social media/Telephone communications ● Complaint responses This Standard does not apply to: ● Internal employee communications or training materials ● Communications related to supplier or partner agreements ● Automated transaction confirmations (e.g., receipts) where no decision-making is involved. It covers both new and existing communications, ensuring legacy content is also brought up to Standard. 1 Definitions: Terminology Definition Staff Member(s) An employee or external worker (e.g., contingent labor, contractors) hired by the Company who supports the operations covered by this Standard. Central Bank of The CBI is a financial regulatory body in Ireland(“IEˮ), responsible Ireland (‘CBIˮ) for issuing the SIL license as an e-Money Institution (“EMIˮ). Customer For the purposes of this document, a Customer is defined as all SIL Square merchants, including potential Customers, where appropriate. Vulnerable The CPC, 2025 defines a consumer in vulnerable circumstances" as a Customer consumer that is a natural person and whose personal circumstances, whether permanent or temporary, make that consumer especially susceptible to harm, particularly where a regulated entity is not acting with the appropriate levels of care, and "vulnerable circumstances" shall be construed accordingly. Complaint A "complaint" means, subject to Regulation 260, an expression of grievance or dissatisfaction by a consumer, either orally or in writing, in connection with - (a) the provision or the offer of the provision of a financial service to a consumer by a regulated entity, or(b) the failure or refusal of a regulated entity to provide a financial service to a consumer; Note: Potential Customers can make a Complaint to SIL. 2. Standard Requirements The Standard mandates that all SIL communications must: ● Be clear, fair, and not misleading; ● Highlight key information customers need to make decisions; ● Present information in an accessible and understandable manner; and ● Be tested, monitored, and refined based on actual customer outcomes. 2 2.1. Communication Design All customer communications must be designed and delivered in alignment with the following principles: 1. Language and Clarity ● Use plain, everyday language appropriate for the target audience; avoid technical jargon where possible. ● Ensure key terms, risks and pricing are prominent, understandable, and easy to locate. ● Present content in a clear, concise, and factually accurate manner, consistent across all channels. 2. Accessibility and Inclusion ● Design communications to be accessible to all customer groups, including those with characteristics of vulnerability. ● Use a readable, user-friendly layout with bullet points, clear headings, summaries, diagrams, and infographics, where helpful. ● Offer information through multiple accessible formats and channels (e.g., email, phone, chat, video tutorials, alternative formats). 3. Channel Effectiveness and Support ● Assess and select communication channels based on their ability to deliver clear, timely, and accessible information to different customer segments. ● Monitor customer engagement across channels to identify gaps and determine whether additional support or alternative formats are required. 4. Journey Mapping and Friction Reduction ● Map the customer journey across all channels to identify pain points where users experience frustration, confusion, delays, or obstacles that impede customer understanding, decision-making, or task completion. Use these insights to eliminate unnecessary friction and create a smoother, more intuitive experience. ● Tailor communications to reflect customer needs, preferences, and their stage in the journey. ● Use a layered approach where appropriate, offering key information upfront with options to access more detailed explanations. ● Ensure proactive delivery of important information at the right time at key decision points (e.g., post-sale) to help the customers assess their options, understand associated risks, and make choices that align with their needs. 5. Regulatory Disclosure and Transparency ● Clearly distinguish between regulated and unregulated products, and explicitly communicate the regulatory status and associated protections of each. ● Include the following regulatory disclosure, in a durable medium, in all regulated customer-facing documentation (e.g., letters, emails, marketing content, websites) “Squareup International Limited, trading as Square is regulated by the Central Bank 3 of Ireland.ˮ Translate to where applicable) 2.2. Telephone Communication and Personal Visits Square may, from time to time, need to contact existing, potential or former customers via telephone or by visiting their business premises. Permitted contact with a customer by telephone or by visit, must be made only between 9.00 a.m. and 9.00 p.m. Monday to Saturday, excluding public holidays, unless otherwise agreed in writing with the consumer. When making a personal visit or initiating communication by means of telephone call, advocates must, and in the following order provide: ● their name and the company name ● the purpose of the telephone call or visit ● inform the consumer that telephone contact is being recorded, if this is the case ● establish if the consumer wishes the personal visit or telephone call to proceed and, if not, end the contact immediately Example - Hi, my name is John, calling from Square about X, before proceeding i need to inform you, this call is recorded for training and verification purposes, is that OK? Telephone Calls with Existing Customers SIL may make telephone calls with existing customers only when one or more of the following specific conditions is met: ● The consumer holds a product which requires SIL to maintain contact with the consumer in relation to that product ● The contact must directly relate to that existing product ● SIL has provided that consumer with a financial service similar to the one that is the purpose of the contact within the previous 12 months ● The similarity of services must be clearly documented and justifiable ● Records must be maintained showing the previous service relationship and timing. ● The necessity for ongoing contact must be clearly established and documented ● The consumer has given written consent to being contacted by telephone 4 Telephone Calls with potential or former Customers SIL may initiate telephone calls with potential customers or former customers only if one of the following specific conditions is met: ● The contacted person has provided written consent for SIL to contact them for a specific purpose within the previous 12 months ● The contact must directly relate to that specified purpose ● Consent must be documented with clear purpose specification and date ● The contacted person has made their contact details public in Ireland through Business listing section of a current telephone directory or classified telephone directory Trade or professional directories or a website, software app, or other digital technology operated by a business (with the person's consent) ● The contacted person is a director of a company or partner in a firm with an entry in the directories or digital platforms referenced above. Contact must be made via the business contact details of the company or firm and must be in connection with their role as director or partner All telephone communications must ensure that the extent of communications is proportionate, reasonable, and not excessive. SIL must take into account the circumstances of the consumer, including, contact frequency, timing and duration of calls. Personal Visits SIL may make a personal visit to a consumer only if that consumer has provided written consent covering all of the following elements: ● Clear specification of the purposes for which the personal visit is to be made ● For sales and marketing visits, explicit identification of the product to be discussed during the personal visit ● documentation of the specific business objectives and intended outcomes ● Agreed time, date, and location for the personal visit ● If applicable, clear disclosure of any fee that SIL proposes to charge for the personal visit 5 All written consent must be obtained prior to the visit, properly documented, and retained in accordance with record retention requirements. 2.3. Review and Approval The first line of defence must ensure that new customer communications, or updates to existing communications, undergo a structured review and approval process before deployment. The review must cover three key dimensions to ensure content is accurate, clear, and compliant: ● Customer Understanding Review: Ensure that the content meets the design principles outlined in Section 2.1, with a focus on clarity, consistent branding, accessibility, and ease of understanding for the target audience. ● Subject Matter Expert (“SMEˮ) Review: Relevant SMEs - Product, Marketing, Risk, Customer Success, etc must validate the content for
documents.CP-059: Hardship Support Standard Square | Square AU Pty Ltd Last Approved: Feb 9, 2026 Effective Date: Feb 9, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: At Square AU Pty Ltd, we understand that customers may experience financial hardship due to unforeseen events or changing circumstances. As a responsible financial services provider, we are committed to supporting customers during difficult times with empathy, fairness, and transparency. Our approach is guided by legal and regulatory obligations, including the Corporations Act 2001, Australian Consumer Law ACL and relevant ASIC guidance on hardship support, complaints handling, and fair treatment. We offer timely, flexible, and practical assistance tailored to individual needs, with clear and accessible communication channels. We also recognise that financial hardship can intersect with broader forms of vulnerability, and we are committed to providing support that is inclusive, respectful, and responsive. This policy outlines our commitment to helping customers in financial difficulty and ensuring they are treated with care and dignity. Scope: This Standard applies to all Square AU employees, contractors, and representatives who interact with customers, including but not limited to: ● Square sellers (merchants using Square products and services) ● Individuals receiving customer service, onboarding assistance, or technical support ● Individuals lodging complaints, disputes, or requests for help ● Business owners or operators disclosing business or personal hardships The Standard applies across all products and services offered by Square AU, including Square Loans, payments, point-of-sale, online commerce, business software, and associated support functions. Definitions: Terminology Definition Financial A situation where a customer is willing but temporarily unable to meet Hardship financial obligations due to events such as illness, job loss, natural disaster, or family violence. Vulnerable A customer who, due to personal circumstances, requires special care or Customer support, such as individuals with cognitive impairment, mental illness, or language barriers. 2. Square AUSpecific Requirements 2.1. Understanding Financial Hardship Financial hardship refers to a situation where a customer is willing but temporarily unable to meet their financial obligations due to unforeseen circumstances. These circumstances may include, but are not limited to, loss of employment or reduced income, serious illness or injury, domestic or family violence, the impact of natural disasters, a relationship breakdown, increased cost of living pressures, or the death of a family member. Square AU acknowledges that financial hardship can be short-term or long-term in nature and may affect individuals differently depending on their personal and financial situation. We are committed to assessing each case with sensitivity and fairness. 2.2. Regulatory and Legal Context As an Australian Financial Services Licence AFSL holder and payment services provider, Square AU is subject to several key obligations: ● Corporations Act 2001 (s912A): We must act efficiently, honestly, and fairly, maintain adequate resources, and implement compliant dispute resolution procedures. ASIC expects licensees to proactively support customers in hardship, including tracking hardship complaints and ensuring systems and staff are equipped to respond. ● ASIC Expectations RG 271 and Report 782 ASIC expects accessible hardship channels, tailored responses based on individual circumstances, appropriate staff training, additional support for vulnerable customers, and internal tracking of hardship data. ● Australian Consumer Law ACL We must avoid unconscionable or misleading conduct. This includes treating hardship customers fairly and ensuring any representations made about support options are accurate. ● Australian Financial Complaints Authority AFCA As a member of AFCA, we are required to handle hardship disputes fairly, avoid delays, and document all hardship-related communications. AFCA may award compensation if we fail to meet these obligations. 2.3. Requesting Hardship Support Customers who are experiencing financial hardship are encouraged to contact Square AU as early as possible to discuss their situation. Requests for hardship support can be made through our dedicated support channels, including email, phone, or our online customer portal. At the initial point of contact, customers will not be required to provide supporting documentation. However, in order to assess the request accurately and to provide tailored assistance, we may reserve our right to request reasonable evidence of the customerʼs financial situation—such as a medical certificate, a recent payslip, or a bank statement. Our goal is to make the hardship application process as accessible and respectful as possible, while ensuring we have adequate information to make informed decisions. We accept hardship support requests from customers or from their authorised representatives, including those acting under a Power of Attorney or other recognised authority. Each request is assessed on a case by case basis and handled appropriately internally. 2.4. Assessment and Communication Square AU aims to acknowledge hardship requests as soon as possible. We will assess all hardship applications promptly, fairly, and on a case-by-case basis, with the aim of providing an outcome in a timely manner. Throughout the process, we will maintain open and transparent communication with the customer, clearly explaining the assistance options available and any decisions made. Where a hardship request also involves a complaint, the complaint will be resolved within the mandated timeframe of 21 days. If a request for hardship assistance is declined, we will provide the customer with a clear explanation of the reasons for the decision and inform them of their right to lodge a complaint through our Internal Dispute Resolution IDR process or escalate the matter to the Australian Financial Complaints Authority AFCA. The below table is a summary of a high level assessment of Hardship factors our customers may face, however each instance is considered on a case by case basis and handled appropriately by our customer facing teams in line with their own procedures: Assessment Category Indicators of Hardship Severity Level Appropriate Action Financial Position - Declining Low/ Moderate / High Assess severity level transaction volumes and apply appropriate - Increased hardship measures chargebacks - Overdue payments - Reliance on Government Support - Reduced or No income Payment Processing - Hold on funds Moderate/ High Assess severity level Status - Restrictions on and apply appropriate Square account hardship measures, - Compliance holds consider escalation relating to transfer internally to assist in of account removal of account ownership or KYC restrictions where appropriate; mindful of tipping off where an SMR potentially lodged Hardware/ Square - Technical issues Moderate/ High Device Considerations with Square terminal - Connectivity issues with device Life Events - Bereavement Moderate/ High Prioritise privacy and - Illness or injury safety; fast track - Family Breakdown support; consider - Domestic or family alternate arrangements violence Business event/ - Loss of key staff Moderate Offer tailored Employment Status - Premises issues repayment options; - Supply chain issues consider pausing or - Recently reducing fees where unemployed appropriate - Casual or unstable work - Reduced hours/ income Health Factors - Mental health Moderate/ High Engage with empathy; - Disability may require internal - Chronic illness escalation to handle hardships; external escalation to offer additional support if appropriate Natural Disaster/ - Impacted by flood, High Expedite support Emergency fire, pandemic etc options, may trigger automatic hardship relief Dependents or Carer - Sole Carer Low/ Moderate Consider flexible Responsibilities - Caring for someone arrangements, with high needs acknowledge additional financial strain Customer Behaviour/ - Distress during call Any level Use soft enquiry and Cues - Avoiding contact supportive language, - Expressing shame escalate as or anxiety appropriate, consider offering hardship assistance proactively 2.5. Available Assistance Options Square AU offers a range of assistance options to support customers experiencing financial difficulty. The type of assistance provided is tailored to the customerʼs individual circumstances and may include payment deferrals, the restructuring of payment arrangements into more manageable installments, the temporary suspension of fees and charges, fee waivers or refunds where appropriate, or other bespoke arrangements. In some cases, we may also refer customers to independent financial counselling or community support services. We are committed to working collaboratively with each customer to determine the most appropriate form of support and will ensure that all solutions are fair, reasonable, and sustainable. 2.6. Supporting Vulnerable Customers Square AU is committed to supporting customers who may be experiencing vulnerability in addition to financial hardship. Vulnerability may arise from a range of factors, and may be temporary or permanent. In line with our Vulnerable Customer Standard and broader customer care principles and procedures, we take extra care to identify indicators of vulnerability and apply appropriate protocols to ensure these customers receive the additional support they may need. We recognise the importance of treating vulnerable customers with heightened sensitivity, confidentiality and respect at all times. 2.7. Monitoring and Continuous Improvement Square AU is committed to continuously improving the way we support customers in financial hardship. We maintain records of all hardship cases and monitor the effectiveness of the support provided through regular internal reviews, r
documents.CP-060: Vulnerable Customers Standard Square | Square AU Pty Ltd Last Approved: Feb 4, 2026 Effective Date: Feb 4, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: Square AU is committed to treating all customers fairly, with empathy, and in line with its legal and ethical obligations. We recognise that vulnerability or hardship can affect a personʼs ability to access services, understand information or manage their affairs. Our approach is guided by key regulatory frameworks, including: ● Australian Consumer Law, which prohibits unconscionable conduct and promotes fair treatment, especially for vulnerable consumers. ● ASIC RG 271, which sets expectations for handling complaints from vulnerable and disadvantaged individuals. ● ASIC Report 782, which highlights the importance of proactive support for small businesses in hardship. We also uphold federal anti-discrimination laws, including the Age, Disability, Sex, and Racial Discrimination Acts, and the Australian Human Rights Commission Act, to ensure inclusive, respectful service. Vulnerability can arise beyond traditional lending contexts. Many Square sellers are small businesses facing external pressures, and while Square AU may not cause that vulnerability, we recognise our role in supporting these customers with flexible and compassionate solutions. This Standard sets out how we identify and assist vulnerable customers to meet regulatory expectations and reflect our values of fairness, accessibility, and economic empowerment. Scope: This Standard applies to all Square AU employees, contractors, and representatives who interact with customers, including but not limited to: ● Square sellers (merchants using Square products and services) ● Individuals receiving customer service, onboarding assistance, or technical support ● Individuals lodging complaints, disputes, or requests for help ● Business owners or operators disclosing business or personal hardship The Standard applies across all products and services offered by Square AU, including payments, point-of-sale, online commerce, business software, and associated support functions. Definitions: Terminology Definition Vulnerability A state which, due to personal circumstances or characteristics, an individual is especially susceptible to detriment or harm, particularly when a firm is not acting with appropriate levels of care. Vulnerability can be temporary, recurring, or permanent. Vulnerable An individual who, due to their personal circumstances, is especially Customer susceptible to harm or disadvantage, particularly if Square AU fails to act with appropriate levels of care. This includes customers who may have difficulty accessing services, understanding information, making decisions, or managing their financial affairs effectively. Hardship A situation where a customer is willing but unable to meet their financial obligations due to illness, unemployment, or other reasonable cause. This includes both personal and business-related financial difficulties. 2. Square AU Specific Requirements Square AU recognises that customer vulnerability can affect a person's ability to effectively engage with our products, services, or support channels. In line with our obligations under Australian Consumer Law, we are committed to avoiding conduct that is misleading, deceptive, or unconscionable, particularly where a customer is at a disadvantage. Additionally, under ASIC Regulatory Guide 271 RG 271, we are expected to identify and appropriately support vulnerable customers—especially throughout the complaints process. This Standard outlines the minimum expectations for recognising and responding to customer vulnerability across all interactions, including in hardship, complaint handling, and everyday service delivery, to ensure fair and inclusive outcomes. 2.1. Definition of a Vulnerable Customer A vulnerable customer is an individual who, due to personal circumstances or characteristics, is at increased risk of detriment, harm, or disadvantage, particularly when accessing or using financial products or services. Vulnerability may be temporary, recurring, or permanent, and may not always be disclosed by the customer. Square AU recognises that vulnerability is dynamic and may arise at any point in a personʼs life. In Australia, while no legal definition of a vulnerable customer exists, the Australian Securities and Investments Commission ASIC and Australian Consumer Law require financial service providers to exercise due care in their treatment of individuals deemed vulnerable. Vulnerability can be broken down into four key drivers: health, life events, capability and resilience. The four drivers are intended to: ● Help firms identify common root causes of vulnerability ● Encourage dynamic, judgment-based responses rather than rigid checklists ● Guide training, policies, and frontline service deliver To simplify the approach to identifying these vulnerabilities, they can be grouped into four key drivers: Vulnerability Definition Examples Health Health-related vulnerability arises when a customer is - Serious illness or injury - Disabilities (intellectual, mental, impacted by a physical or mental health condition that physical, sensory, neurological, or affects their ability to engage with financial services. learning) – e.g. autism, cognitive impairment - Substance abuse or addiction issues, such as gambling - Caring responsibilities for a person experiencing any of the above Life events Life events refer to significant personal or external - Family or domestic violence experiences that may cause emotional, psychological, or - Recent bereavement or trauma financial disruption, reducing a customerʼs ability to - Changes in family or relationship circumstances manage their financial affairs or make informed - Natural disasters decisions. These events may be sudden or ongoing. - Victims of human trafficking - Victims of financial crime or fraud Resilience Resilience-based vulnerability relates to a customerʼs - Financial hardship, including low ability to withstand or recover from financial shocks or income, job loss, or homelessness pressures. Low financial or emotional resilience may leave - Financial abuse situations - Individuals facing homelessness or the customer particularly exposed to harm when facing housing insecurity adversity. - Macroeconomic conditions Capability Capability vulnerability refers to limitations in a customerʼs - English as a second language ability to understand, access, or act on financial - Low literacy or numeracy information and services. This includes challenges related - Limited digital or financial literacy - Age – particularly very young or to literacy, language, digital skills, or social and cultural older adults context that impede effective engagement. - Cultural or linguistic diversity, including varied expectations, behaviours, or communication norms - Living in remote or regional areas These factors may affect a customerʼs ability to understand, access, or act on financial information, make informed decisions, or seek help when needed. 2.2. Identifying Vulnerability Square AU staff are not expected to diagnose or confirm a customerʼs vulnerability. Instead, they must exercise sound judgment, empathy and caution when potential indicators of vulnerability are present. Staff should remain alert to verbal, behavioural, or circumstantial cues that suggest a customer may be experiencing hardship, distress, confusion or difficulty engaging with our services. All frontline employees are provided with practical guidance on how to identify vulnerability and manage sensitive situations respectfully. This includes recognising when a customer may require additional support, alternative communication methods or referral to specialised assistance. Where a customerʼs experience of vulnerability results in dissatisfaction, hardship, or service issues, staff should escalate the matter internally and, where appropriate, initiate a complaint in accordance with Square AUʼs Internal Dispute Resolution IDR process. All complaints involving vulnerable customers must be handled with heightened care and resolved within the prescribed timeframes under ASIC RG 271 of 21 calendar days where Hardship exists. 2.3. Responding to Vulnerable Customers When a customer is identified or reasonably suspected to be experiencing vulnerability, Square AU staff must take proactive, respectful steps to ensure their experience is safe, supportive and fair. A resolution- and customer-focused approach is paramount in these situations and staff should prioritise practical support. The focus should be on understanding the customerʼs needs and removing barriers to access or service wherever possible. Key principles when responding to vulnerable customers: ● Clear, Compassionate Communication: Staff must communicate using plain, respectful language, listen empathetically without assumptions and offer follow-up or clarification to ensure the customer fully understands. ● Reasonable Accommodations: Staff must offer practical adjustments—such as interpreters, extended time, alternative formats or support persons—tailored to the individual needs of each customer. ● Respect for Privacy and Confidentiality: All personal information relating to a customer's vulnerability must be handled with informed consent, strict confidentiality and in line with the Privacy Act and APPs. ● Cultural Awareness and Sensitivity: Staff must engage customers with cultural respect, avoid assumptions and provide flexible, culturally safe support where needed. ● Commitment to Positive Outcomes: All interactions must reflect Square AUʼs commitment to treating customers with respect, dignity, and fairness, supporting informed decision-making, removing access barriers, resolving issues with care and meeting all legal and regulatory obligations. 2.4. Escalation and Internal Support All escalations must be documented, handled sensitively, and managed in line with relevan

documents.EE-002: Global Modern Slavery Policy Block, Inc. Last Approved: October 3, 2025 Effective Date: October 3, 2025 1. Overview At Block, we are committed to supporting the freedom, dignity, and well-being of all people connected to our ecosystem. Respect for human rights is at the center of our culture and operations. This commitment means we take action to foster equitable working places for our employees and business partners, and eradicate the existence of involuntary labor and exploitation in our business and extended supply chain. Block strictly prohibits all forms of modern slavery in our operations. Our supply chain may include business in jurisdictions or industries known to present a risk of human and labor exploitation. Block must take proactive steps to ensure our operations do not cause, contribute to, or have links to exploitation and modern slavery. Modern slavery affects millions of victims worldwide and takes many forms across industries. Means of compulsion can be violent and visible; they can also be subtle, as in the cases of debt bondage, extortion, or seizure of travel documents. Below are the most common forms of Modern Slavery covered by this Policy: - Forced labor is any work or service extracted from a person without consent and under threat of penalty. This includes some forms of state-imposed labor and criminal servitude that involve punishment on the basis of political, social, religious, or ethnic identity. - Child labor includes all uses, purchasing, or offering services of children for illicit or illegal exploitation, both commercial and private. - Debt bondage is the use of debt to secure labour or services below the fair value of that labour or where debt cannot be repaid. Debt bondage commonly takes the form of steep recruitment fees or transportation fees levied by employers that are never repaid. - Sexual exploitation includes the use, procurement, or offering of forced sexual labor as part of commercial exploitation, including all forms of commercial sexual exploitation of children. - Domestic servitude forces victims to reside inside of private homes, often without pay, and are subject to compelled labor as well as limitations on mobility and communication. - Human trafficking involves the recruitment, transportation, or harboring of individuals through coercive means, and also falls under modern slavery for the purpose of exploitation and profit. Our commitment to combat modern slavery aligns with various international conventions, principles, and standards, including the United Nationsʼ Universal Declaration of Human Rights, its Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights (“GPBHRˮ), and the International Labour Organizationʼs (“ILOˮ) core labor standards. These sources uphold the rights to life, liberty, security, fair treatment at work, and direct all companies to eliminate any vestiges of forced labor, child labor, and exploitation in respect of employment and occupation. Scope: This Policy applies to Block, Inc., its subsidiaries, and affiliates (collectively, “Blockˮ), including all Reporting Subsidiaries. Definitions: Terminology Definition Block Supplier Any third party involved in producing or delivering a product or service to Block, from raw materials to end consumers. Block Suppliers include direct and indirect suppliers, contractors, contingent workers, vendors, consulting firms, staffing agencies, and licensees. Staff Member(s) An employee or external worker (e.g., contingent labor, contractors) hired by the Company who supports the operations covered by this Policy. Reporting Block subsidiaries that are subject to Modern Slavery Statement consultation Subsidiary and reporting requirements under local laws, including Australiaʼs Modern Slavery Act 2018, the U.K. Modern Slavery Act 2015, and Canadaʼs Fighting Against Forced Labour and Child Labour in Supply Chains Act, SC 2023. Criteria for Reporting Subsidiaries are specifically set forth under local statute, and Officers and Directors of Reporting Subsidiaries may have additional responsibilities as set forth by local laws for the evaluation and reporting of modern slavery risk. 2. Block, Inc. Requirements 2.1. General This Policy prohibits Block employees, contractors, officers, and directors from engaging in business practices that cause, contribute to, or are tied to modern slavery. This includes indirect activities of third parties that Block Staff knowingly engage with. All Block Staff Members must: ● Maintain a safe, fair, and respectful workplace free from exploitation and harassment. ● Never cause, contribute to, or tolerate human rights violations or modern slavery abuses. ● Follow this Policy and other Block internal policies and procedures to identify, respond to, and mitigate human rights and modern slavery risks in our operations. ● Speak up with any concerns including through our confidential Ethics Line (https://ethics.block.xyz/), hotline, or directly with managers, HR, or the Modern Slavery Policy Owner. 2.2. Examples of prohibited activities and red flags Under this Policy, we do not engage in or align ourselves with the following practices: ● Using force, compulsion, or threat of harm in the performance of work. ● Engaging in conduct that restricts workerʼs freedom of movement or right to take alternative work, including restricting access to worker passports, visas, and work permits and mandatory overtime practices. ● Using child labor, which means hiring workers below the local legal minimum age for such work, the age of completion of compulsory education, or 15 years old, whichever is greater. ● Use of bonded or indentured labor, including conditions where workers are charged recruitment fees, placement fees, or other in-kind charges for employment. ● Retaliation of any kind against workers who raise grievances, report abuses, or participate in workers unions. Red flags and indicators include, but are not limited to: ● Any complaints by workers of abuse or exploitation. ● Frequent use of workers from vulnerable communities, including migrant workers who do not know local languages or laws. ● Deceptive recruitment practices. ● Hiring workers without formal agreements. ● Social isolation of workforce. ● Fees and penalties levied against workers. ● Unclear responsibility for worker treatment (i.e., workers “employedˮ by third party). ● Heavy presence of security or third party agencies directing workers. ● Employer access to worker bank accounts or garnishing worker pay. ● Retention of worker documents or belongings. ● Use of workforce under “cultural improvementˮ or “rehabilitationˮ programs. ● Large transfers of workforce between locations. Please contact the Policy Owner if you have any questions about the applicability of this Policy in day to day operations. 2.3. Maintain ethical business relationships Block expects Staff Members, Suppliers, and business partners to actively identify, prevent and mitigate all forms of modern slavery in our extended operations. These expectations are communicated through our Code of Business Conduct and Ethics and Supplier Code of Conduct, among other Block policies and procedures. Block reserves the right to terminate or refuse to enter into a business relationship that Block determines has failed to meet Blockʼs requirements under this Policy. 2.4. Establish and maintain internal controls and procedures Block will establish and maintain policies, procedures, controls, and systems reasonably designed to ensure compliance with this Policy. Such controls may consist of specialized screening and diligence procedures for direct and indirect suppliers, supplier risk tiering systems, transactional review, contractual requirements, and ongoing monitoring activities including site visits. 2.5. Perform regular risk assessments Block performs modern slavery risk assessments on a periodic basis to assess and prioritize Blockʼs risks in relation to modern slavery practices. Risk assessment procedures and findings will be documented and result in a detailed risk matrix that maps modern slavery risks to existing policies and controls. Risk assessments under this section will conform with Blockʼs overarching Enterprise Risk Management process. 2.6. Escalate grievances Any potential violations of this Policy must be escalated to ensure prompt and timely investigation, remediation, and reporting. Block maintains clear and safe channels and resolution procedures that can be used by individuals, workers, and other third parties around the globe to report concerns about modern slavery and exploitation, including: ● Blockʼs Ethics Line (ethics.block.xyz or go/speakup). ● Blockʼs hotline, 18004639330, which is operated by an independent third party. If outside the U.S., a list of local access numbers is available at ethics.block.xyz) ● Reporting directly to oneʼs manager, HR, or to the Policy Owner. Reporters using our Ethics Line or telephone hotline may identify themselves or remain anonymous. Block respects the confidentiality and anonymity of all concerns reported through these channels. Block investigates modern slavery concerns and issues in a timely, appropriate, and effective manner. Where modern slavery is identified, Block is committed to taking meaningful action. Our first priority is to ensure the safety and well-being of victims of modern slavery. 2.7. Annual Modern Slavery Statement The Boards of Directors for Block and Reporting Subsidiaries will review Blockʼs modern slavery risks and compliance under this Policy and formally approve Blockʼs annual Modern Slavery Statement (“Statementˮ) as required by laws and regulations in jurisdictions in which Block operates. These requirements may include mandatory consultation with entities owned or controlled by Reporting Entities. 3. Compliance & Enforcement 3.1. Roles and Responsibilities: ● Block and Reporting Subsidiary Board and Executive Oversight ○ Provides consultation and approval for the annual Modern Slavery
