Document Details 13 Global Anti-Bribery & Anti-Corruption Policy | Block 2 Confidential and Not for Distribution Definitions Terminology Definition Bribery or Bribe Anything of value offered, given, or received, including any financial or other advantage, in order to improperly influence a decision, action, or exercise of discretion by a Government Official or commercial party. Corruption Dishonest or fraudulent conduct, including the misuse or abuse of one’s power, influence, or position for personal benefit. Some common types include kickbacks, bribery, influence peddling, nepotism, handling the proceeds of a crime, bid rigging, and extortion. Facilitation Anything of value, typically a small payment or gift, offered or provided to Payment a Government Official to facilitate or expedite routine clerical or administrative, non‐ discretionary actions such as inspecting goods in transit, issuing permits and/or licenses, processing visas or providing police protection. Government Also known as Public Official, a Government Official is any elected or Official appointed officer, candidate for political office, employee, or other person performing service on behalf of a government, government agency, state‐owned or ‐controlled entity, military, political party, or public international organization (e.g. United Nations). This definition includes both U.S. and non‐U.S. Government Officials. Family members of government officials and individuals working at the express or implied direction of Government Officials, such as contractors, should be treated as Government Officials. Staff Includes all Block full-time employees and fixed-term and contingent workers, including interns, consultants, vendors, third party providers, and contractors. Global Anti-Bribery & Anti-Corruption Policy | Block 3 Confidential and Not for Distribution 1. Introduction This Anti-Bribery & Anti-Corruption Policy (“Policy”) outlines Block, Inc.’s (“Block”) approach to combating Bribery and Corruption risks within its business. Block’s Anti-Bribery and Corruption Program Lead is responsible for the management of this Policy. 2. Purpose Block’s mission requires it to act ethically and transparently everywhere it operates. Block does not tolerate bribery or corruption of any sort. Block, including members of its Board of Directors, officers, and staff, must maintain the highest ethical standards of business conduct and comply with all applicable anti-corruption and anti-bribery laws and regulations, including the U.S. Foreign Corrupt Practices Act (“FCPA”) and the U.K. Bribery Act, as well as the anti-corruption and anti-bribery laws of the countries where Block is doing business. Block’s global policy with respect to bribery and corruption is based on four key principles: ● Do not offer, give, or accept bribes or improper payments, gratuities, or remuneration in any form; ● Be ethical and transparent in interactions with Government Officials and third parties, including pre-clearance of any gifts or entertainment with Government Officials at go/disclose or ethics@; ● Speak up. Ask questions and escalate concerns regarding potential Bribery in connection with any of Block’s business dealings; and ● Block will not retaliate against anyone who reports, in good faith, acts of actual or suspected corruption. Broken down, Block’s Policy is simple: Global Anti-Bribery & Anti-Corruption Policy | Block 4 Confidential and Not for Distribution Don’t offer or accept any bribe to or from anyone, anytime, for any reason. Open and candid disclosure is essential to protect Block staff from corrupt actors. Involvement in Bribery or Corruption can result in lasting damage to Block, its brand, reputation, and mission. It can also result in termination of business relationships, costly investigations and litigation, multi-million-dollar fines and penalties, as well as jail time for participants. 3. Scope This Policy is established by Block and is applicable globally across all Block business lines, subsidiaries, and jurisdictions. 4. Policy Requirements 4.1 No Bribes Block and its staff cannot offer, give, or accept Bribes from any individual, regardless of whether that individual is a Government Official or a private party. “Anything of value” includes cash, cash equivalents such as gift cards, gifts, cryptocurrencies, meals, travel and entertainment, charitable or political contributions, loans or forgiveness of debt, and any other benefits, including those of nominal value. It can include a promise, authorization, or guarantee of something of value. Anything of value can also include job offers, consulting opportunities, or internships for individuals or their family members. Block does not offer employment of any kind, including paid or unpaid internships and contract positions, in order to obtain or retain business or gain a business advantage. Reasonable and customary business gifts, meals, and hospitality provided for a legitimate business purpose may be permissible if they comply with applicable international and local anti-corruption laws and are provided in accordance with Block policy. Please refer to Block’s Code of Business Conduct & Ethics, Gift and Entertainment Policy, and Gifts Page for more details and guidance, including thresholds. Global Anti-Bribery & Anti-Corruption Policy | Block 5 Confidential and Not for Distribution If you are confronted with a demand to pay a Bribe, you must refuse. Explain that these types of payments are against Block policy, and report the incident to the Compliance team at ethics@ or use the Ethics Line at ethics.block.xyz. 4.2 No Kickbacks Kickbacks are a type of Bribery and occur when a person is offered money or something of value in exchange for providing something to a third party. The third party may be requesting sensitive or non-public information, a discount, the award of a contract, preferential treatment, or a favor. Like all other Bribes, kickbacks may be in the form of cash or the equivalent, gifts, meals, and entertainment. Kickbacks are not permissible and are strictly prohibited by Block. If you are confronted with a demand to pay a kickback or are offered a kickback, you must refuse. Explain that these types of payments are against Block policy, and report the incident to the Compliance team at ethics@ or use the Ethics Line at ethics.block.xyz. 4.3 Act Ethically with All Government Officials Interactions with Government Officials pose a heightened risk to Block from a reputational and compliance perspective. Block generally prohibits business gratuities involving Government Officials. Any potential gifts, meals, entertainment, and travel including Government Officials must be pre-cleared by the Compliance team via go/disclose, prior to an exchange occurring. “Government Official” is defined broadly to include any person who is paid with government funds or serves in a public function, as well as their family members. This includes individuals—both U.S. and foreign—who work for a local, state/provincial or national government, First Nations, political party officials or candidates, or a public international organization such as the United Nations, as well as employees of public (government-owned or operated) schools, hospitals, and state-owned enterprises. In some instances, it may be difficult to determine if an individual is a Government Official, especially if Block is interacting with them in a non-governmental capacity. Interactions regarding the following subjects likely involve Government Officials: Global Anti-Bribery & Anti-Corruption Policy | Block 6 Confidential and Not for Distribution ● Negotiations/agreements/renewals with governmental agencies or bodies, state-owned or state-controlled entities, including through a third party, such as a sales agent; ● Applying for or renewing licenses, permits, or public grants; ● Interactions with customs or immigration officials in connection with customs clearance/payment of customs fees or processing visa applications; ● Representing the company in judicial or administrative proceedings; ● Security services provided by police or military personnel (even if off-duty); and ● Payment or refund of taxes and any tax-related disputes. Failure to recognize and act appropriately with Government Officials can have substantial consequences for Block and its staff . If you are unsure, please contact the Compliance team at ethics@. 4.3.1 State-Owned Enterprises Special care and consideration should be taken when dealing with state-owned enterprises. It may not always be clear that a company is state-owned. Companies such as public universities, airlines, manufacturing companies, banks, sovereign wealth funds, hospitals, telecoms, and others are commonly partially or wholly owned by government entities. All employees of these organizations are considered Government Officials regardless of title or position. 4.3.2 Facilitation Payments Facilitation Payments, also known as “grease payments,” are typically a small payment or gift offered or provided to a Government Official to expedite routine clerical or administrat
documents.EE-004: Global Gifts and Entertainment Policy Block Inc. Last Approved: Mar 25, 2026 Effective Date: Mar 25, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: The Global Gifts and Entertainment Policy (the “Policyˮ) covers Block Inc.ʼs requirements for managing business exchanges with Third Parties. Appropriate exchanges, such as gifts, meals, or entertainment, can help build and maintain relationships. However, when excessive, they may create unnecessary risk or conflicts for the organization. This Policy helps Staff Members avoid risks such as corruption, bribery, influence peddling, conflicts of interest, and kickbacks. Block is committed to maintaining the highest standards of business and personal ethics, as set forth in the Block Code of Conduct. References to “Staff Memberˮ throughout this document include all full-time employees and fixed-term and contingent workers, including interns, temps, consultants, vendors, Third Party providers, and contractors. This Policy is designed to help prevent conflicts of interest, undue influence, and situations that may be perceived as corruption. Complying with this Policy protects Blockʼs reputation for conducting business with integrity and safeguards Staff Members and their personal integrity. Scope: This Policy applies to Block Inc. (“Blockˮ) and its subsidiaries globally. It extends to Block officers, directors, temporary workers, third parties representing Block, and Block Staff Members. If Staff Members have any questions about a situation not covered in this Policy, they should contact Compliance at gifts@. Definitions: Terminology Definition Approval Formal, written, permission to offer, give, or receive a gift or entertainment. 1 Disclosure Form An online form, available at go/disclose, that Staff Members must use to disclose and seek approval for gifts and entertainment offers as outlined in this policy. Government All staff of national, state, provincial, and local governments and all their Officials departments and agencies, from high-level officials to the low-level staff. Government Officials also include staff of government-owned or state-controlled entities (“SOEsˮ) – that is, staff of companies and organizations that may not explicitly be a part of the government but that are owned or controlled by the government. Staff Member An employee or external worker (e.g., contingent labor, contractors) hired by the Company who supports the operations covered by this Policy. Third Party Any person or business that has a perspective, potential, current business relationship with Block or our subsidiaries. 2. Policy Requirements 2.1. Key Considerations for Business Exchanges This Policy is designed to help prevent conflicts of interest, undue influence, and situations that may be perceived as corruption. Complying with this Policy protects Blockʼs reputation for conducting business with integrity and safeguards Staff Members and their personal integrity. Careful consideration should be given to the following factors when Staff Members are offered gifts, meals, or entertainment: ● Whether the exchange creates a potential conflict of interest or undue influence. ● The appropriateness of the offer in the business context. ● Whether the offer exceeds the established thresholds. If Staff Members are unsure if a business exchange is appropriate, they must: ● Consult with their manager; ● Seek advice from Compliance; and ● If there is not enough time to obtain advice or approval, Staff Members must politely decline. 2 Disclose any gift or entertainment offer valued over $250 USD1 at go/disclose For guidance and questions, reach out to the Compliance at gifts@. 2.2. Types of Business Exchanges 2.2.1 Gifts Items of value exchanged between Block and a Third Party. In general, acceptable gifts include: ● Merchandise (e.g., gift baskets, wine, clothing, pens, hospitality bags); and ● Reasonably priced tickets to sporting, cultural, or business events where Block Staff Members and the Third Party do not attend together. 2.2.2 Entertainment Experiences or events exchanged between Block and a Third Party, including: ● Appetizers, entrees, drinks, desserts, and other food/beverages; and ● Reasonably priced tickets to sporting, cultural, or business events where Block Staff Members and the Third Party attend together. 2.3. Disclosure and Approval Requirements Any gift or entertainment offer valued over $250 USD, or multiple gifts in one year from the same Third Party where the total value exceeds $250, must be disclosed and approved. Additionally, any offer that meets the criteria of the Restricted Exchanges section below must be disclosed and approved prior to accepting, regardless of value. Staff Members must submit their disclosure using the online form at go/disclose in a timely fashion to provide for review and approval. In urgent cases, where prior approval isnʼt possible, Staff Members must follow up with a disclosure immediately after the event. Such situations should be infrequent and disclosed in good faith. For meals or entertainment accepted without prior approval (e.g., spontaneous invitations during meetings), Staff Members must report it, in a timely manner, if it exceeds the $250 threshold or seems excessive. 1 Or equivalent value in local currency. Visit go/gifts for more information and a currency converter tool. 3 2.4. Exclusions 2.4.1 Exchanges Related to Marketing Programs Proposed gifts or payments related to customer marketing, research, incentive, or rewards programs are not covered by this Policy. These programs require separate review and approval by Legal and Compliance due to their complexity and risks. 2.4.2 Contractually Stipulated Exchanges Proposed gift or entertainment offers that are established as provision of the terms of a contract between Block and a Third Party are not covered by this Policy. Examples of these types of exchanges included mandatory training, networking, business events, promotional tickets to events where the Third Party or Block are a sponsor and receive an allocated number of tickets as part of the arrangement. Such exchanges are governed by the terms of the contract agreed to between Block and the Third Party. 2.4.3 Exchanges Between Block Staff Members Offers between Block Staff Members are not subject to this policy. 2.5. Prohibited and Restricted Exchanges While business exchanges can foster strong relationships, certain types of offers and interactions create risks that may compromise Block's integrity and reputation. To maintain high ethical standards and avoid potential conflicts of interest, the following exchanges are strictly prohibited or subject to additional restrictions, reviews, and approvals. These rules apply to both offers made by Block Staff Members and those received from third parties. 2.5.1 Prohibited Exchanges ● Exchange of cash or cash equivalents (e.g., pre-paid general use debit cards); ● Soliciting any gift or entertainment exchange from a Third Party; ● Expenses related to Third Party payment or reimbursement for Staff Members travel and accommodations; ● Lavish or excessive gifts and entertainment offers must be politely declined. Compliance, in its sole discretion, will determine if a gift or entertainment offer is considered excessive or inappropriate given the circumstances surrounding the offer; 4 ● Favorable pricing, preferential treatment, or business offers not available to the general public; and ● Any offer, regardless of value, that is intended as a facilitation payment, bribe, or kickback. 2.5.2 Restricted Exchanges Some offers present heightened risk factors that require additional levels of due diligence and review to ensure that offers that could give rise to the appearance of a conflict are done so transparently, ethically, and with the best interests of Block in mind. The following exchanges must always be disclosed, regardless of value. ● Any offer valued over $250 USD or exchanges with the same third party where the aggregate value exceeds $250 USD in a 12 month period; ● Gifts, meals, or entertainment offered to Government Officials are subject to additional restrictions. Staff Members must submit a disclosure and consult with Compliance before offering anything to Government Officials; and ● Any offer with a Third Party who is currently negotiating a contract with Block must be submitted. Staff Members must not accept any gifts, meals, or entertainment from Third Parties if they have signature authority on the behalf of Block or have influence over business negotiations. 2.5.3 Return or Donation of Prohibited Gifts If Staff Members receive an inappropriate or prohibited gift, they must immediately report it to Compliance. For guidance on handling such offers, Staff Members can contact Compliance to determine whether a gift should be returned, donated, or otherwise managed. 2.5.4 Attendance by Non-Block Staff Members Generally, non-Block Staff Members (e.g., the partner, spouse, friend, or child of a Block Staff Members member) may not attend events provided by a Third Party. Some exceptions may exist if the guest is necessary to assist Staff Members with the business purpose of the event. Please check with Compliance if Staff Members believe this to be the case. 2.6. Guidelines for Conference and Event Participation Offers to pay for conference fees by Third Parties must be disclosed and approved in advance in accordance with the guidance above. Meals and entertainment during business-related events must 5 comply with the policy guidelines. Travel and accommodations must always be booked through Blockʼs travel system and cannot be paid for by Third Parties. 3. Compliance & Enforcement 3.1. Monitoring and Reporting On an ongoing basis, the Policy Owner and/or their designee monitors activities for compliance and objective achievement. As outlined in the Compliance Management System Policy, Compliance will provide regular updates, as applicable, to the Block, Inc. Board and executive man
documents.EE-005: Global Conflicts of Interest Policy Block Inc. Last Approved: Mar 25, 2026 Effective Date: Mar 25, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Global Conflict of Interest Policy (“Policyˮ) is designed to help Block employees avoid even the appearance of a Conflict of Interest and recognize when to get help or report a concern. It is crucial that we operate our business with the highest level of integrity. That means we must all act openly, honestly, and transparently. Block, Inc. and its subsidiaries (collectively, “Blockˮ or the “Companyˮ) are committed to conducting business with honesty and integrity. If you suspect or believe that a situation creates a Conflict of Interest, disclose it by using the reporting channels described below: Disclose your potential Conflict of Interest by visiting: go/oar for Outside Activity Requests go/disclose for all other Conflicts of Interest For guidance and questions, reach out to Compliance at ethics@; and, employment-legal@ for questions regarding OARs. Scope: This Policy applies to Block and extends to employees of Block. Employees who have questions about a situation not covered in this Policy should contact Compliance at ethics@. While this policy does not apply to external workers at Block, external workers are expected to disclose certain conflicts of interest to their employer of record or to Block as part of their agreement. Directors and officers of Block, Inc. should follow the Conflict of Interest policies set forth in the Companyʼs Code of Conduct, Corporate Governance Guidelines and related policies. 1 Most Restrictive Policy Governs Where applicable laws or regulations differ from this policy, team or role requirements, employees are expected to follow the most restrictive applicable requirement. Definitions: Terminology Definition Close Personal Includes family members (e.g., spouse, domestic partner, parent, Relationship son/daughter, sibling, grandparent, aunt/uncle, niece/nephew, cousin, step/half/or in-law relation, etc.), individuals living in the same household, individuals with whom an individual has a personal relationship of an intimate or romantic nature, or any other person with whom the individual has such a close bond that could impair, or appear to impair, the individualʼs objectivity. Conflict of A Conflict of Interest occurs when your Personal Interests are in potential Interest conflict with your role and work at Block. Personal Interest Means a Block employee's situation or circumstances outside Block that could create, potentially create or appear to create a financial or other benefit to the employee or their Close Personal Relationship. Third Party Any non-Block employee, including customers, prospective customers, suppliers, prospective suppliers, service providers, government officials, and any person with whom Block engages with, does, or may do business. 2. Policy Requirements 2.1. About Conflicts of Interest A Conflict of Interest occurs when your Personal Interests are in potential conflict with your role and work at Block. Always act in the best interests of Block, and donʼt let Personal Interests conflict, or appear to conflict, with the Companyʼs interests. Conflicts of interest can arise in many situations, including personal relationships with family and friends, activities outside of work (such as secondary employment), financial interests in or board/advisory positions with companies that do business or compete with Block, and business opportunities that employees may learn about through their position at Block. Conflicts of interest can be actual, perceived or potential: ● Actual: these are conflicts of interest where there is an actual conflict between an employeeʼs duties or responsibilities to Block and any external interest. 2 ● Perceived: these are conflicts of interest where a reasonable person would view that, based on available information, there may be a Conflict of Interest. ● Potential: these are conflicts which may materialize in the future based on current circumstances or forecasted changes in circumstances. 2.1.1 Obligation to Disclose Employees are required to disclose conflicts of interest, as described in this policy, as soon as possible. This includes any situation in which an employee is involved, or plans to become involved, which could result in an actual, perceived, or potential Conflict of Interest. Once the conflict has been disclosed, no action should be taken until the disclosure has been reviewed and a decision is provided. Employees, their manager, or leadership team may not make an approval decision or determination on the existence of a Conflict of Interest. It must be disclosed as described above and reviewed by Compliance, Employment Counsel, and other relevant stakeholders as appropriate. 2.1.2 Responsibility and Accountability In every disclosure, approval is contingent upon: 1. Submission of a disclosure at go/disclose or go/oar as appropriate; 2. Blockʼs review and approval; 3. Acknowledgement of, and adherence to, any conditions that may be required by Block in connection with the conflict; and, 4. Prompt communication with Block should the nature of the conflict change after approval. Failure to disclose a Conflict of Interest or abide by conditions placed on an approval may result in disciplinary action up to, and including, termination. 2.1.3 Retention of Disclosures Disclosures are maintained in Blockʼs relevant systems of record for the duration of employment + 4 years. 3 2.2. Situations that Require Disclosure Conflicts of Interest come in many shapes and sizes and are often highly situational. Blockʼs business is broad in scope and continues to evolve, which means the factors considered in evaluating a Conflict of Interest may change. 2.2.1 Outside Activities Block supports and encourages appropriate activities outside of your employment with Block. Outside activities include (but are not limited to) other employment, consulting, payment for services rendered, serving on a board or in another advisory capacity, teaching and course work, and certain types of volunteer work. Before you engage in any such outside activity during your employment or engagement with Block, you must submit a formal request to engage in the activity. Additional requirements, restrictions, and/or considerations may be required for roles that are subject to regulatory requirements or for workers who are on a visa. New hires wishing to continue outside activities after joining Block are subject to this policy. You should submit your outside activity request as soon as possible, as it may require review from a number of potential stakeholders. Furthermore, if you are seeking approval for multiple outside activities, you must submit separate requests to go/oar so that each request can be reviewed and evaluated separately. Outside Activities that Require Disclosure: ● Activities that could interfere with Block work, e.g., because they interfere with work hours; ● Activities that relate to Blockʼs current or anticipated business (to determine competitiveness); and, ● Activities that pose a risk of disclosure of confidential business information, IP or trade secrets, including but not limited to activities that may crossover with the requestorʼs role at Block and mentoring roles. Additional Responsibilities You are required to identify and disclose any documents you are asked to sign with any entity involved in your outside activity, including but not limited to any agreement pertaining to confidential information or intellectual property. Block may require that you and the outside entity sign an addendum to any such agreement before you begin the outside activity. 4 Furthermore, if your outside activity at any point changes from the description you originally provided, you must update your request as soon as possible. To update a request that was already approved, please submit a new request at go/oar. Block also reserves the right to modify or revoke its approval of any outside activity request particularly if, for instance, Block's business were to expand into a competitive space. Use of Block Property, Facilities, and Tools In connection with any outside activity, you are prohibited from using Blockʼs property, facilities, equipment, systems, time, or brand; disclosing Blockʼs confidential, proprietary, and trade secret information; or otherwise violating the terms of your Confidential Information and Invention Assignment Agreement with Block or any other written agreement between you and Block.1 Unpaid Volunteer Activities Practically speaking, some unpaid volunteer activities do not implicate our policy (for instance, a one day cancer walk outside of work hours and entirely unrelated to Blockʼs business). However, the following unpaid volunteer activities should be submitted for approval: ● Activities that could interfere with Block work, e.g., because they interfere with work hours; ● Activities that relate to Blockʼs current or anticipated business (to determine competitiveness); ● Activities that cross over with the requestorʼs role at Block (because of the risk that confidential business information, IP or trade secrets will be disclosed); and ● Activities that otherwise pose a risk that confidential business information, IP or trade secrets will be disclosed (e.g., mentoring roles). If youʼre unsure if a particular activity needs to be submitted for approval under this policy, please submit via go/oar. 2.2.2 Personal Investment Activities Employees should avoid any personal investment that could create a real or perceived conflict or otherwise affect your decision-making on behalf of Block. This is especially true of investments in 1Non-exempt employees may not use paid working time for participation in outside activities. 5 current or prospective vendors when the investor has a relationship with the vendor or has signing authority on behalf of Block to continue, renew, or terminate contracts wi
documents.EE-006: Global Speak Up Policy Block, Inc. Last Approved: Mar 25, 2026 Effective Date: Mar 25, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: Block, Inc. and its subsidiaries (collectively, “Blockˮ) are committed to conducting business with honesty and integrity. Block expects all of its Employees, officers, directors, non-employees, representatives, and any other person working on Blockʼs behalf (collectively, “Staffˮ) to act ethically and in accordance with the Blockʼs Global Code of Business Conduct and Ethics (“the Codeˮ) or Supplier Code of Business Conduct and Ethics (“Supplier Codeˮ) depending on the nature of their engagement with Block, as well as applicable policies and procedures. If you suspect or observe any conduct by Block and/or its representatives that is inconsistent with the Code, Block policies, or the law you may report this by using the reporting channels described below. By doing so, you give us the opportunity to learn about the issue and take appropriate action. To Speak Up, Visit www.ethics.block.xyz or Call 18004619330. If outside the United States, local contact numbers are available in the appendix of this policy. This Global Speak Up Policy (“Policyˮ) sets out how Staff can speak up and express their concerns without fear of retaliation. Scope: This Policy is established by Block and is applicable globally across all Block businesses, subsidiaries, and jurisdictions. This Policy applies to Staff and to other individuals who raise concerns to Block relating to the above, including but not limited to Block Employees. This Policy provides globally-applicable general guidance on reporting concerns and related processes. This Policy may be supplemented by region- and/or entity-specific policies and procedures. No part of this Policy should be interpreted as contravening or superseding any other legal or regulatory requirements imposed upon Block. If there is any conflict between the Policy and applicable laws or regulations, the more restrictive requirements should apply. 1 Definitions: Terminology Definition Employee Any person employed by Block, including Block officers and directors, at all levels. Ethics Line One of the reporting channels available to Blockʼs Employees and third parties. It is operated by an independent third-party provider. The Ethics Line can be used to ask an ethics and compliance related question, or to report possible or suspected misconduct. It is available in multiple languages and allows Reports to be made online, by SMS, or by telephone on an anonymous basis, if desired. Non-Employees Including, but not limited to, external workers, vendors, suppliers, and others that do business with Block but are not employed by Block Inc. directly. Report A concern disclosed by an Employee or other party. Staff An employee or non-employee (e.g., contingent labor, contractors) hired by the Company who supports the operations covered by this Policy. 2. Policy Requirements Block is committed to high standards of business conduct. In line with this commitment, Block encourages Staff (including, but not limited to, employees, non-employees, vendors, and others that do business with Block) to raise good faith concerns about Blockʼs and/or its representativesʼ conduct that may be inconsistent with the Code, Block policies, or the law without fear of retaliation in any form. Individuals are not expected to substantiate a concern prior to reporting. Nothing in this Policy, the Code, or other Block policies restricts your ability to engage in communications or actions protected by applicable law, as described in the Companyʼs Protected Communications policy, available upon request and contained in the Companyʼs Employee Handbook. 2.1. Non Retaliation It is important that Staff feel comfortable raising their concerns. Block prohibits retaliation against individuals who raise concerns in good faith. 2 Individuals who have any concerns after making a Report can engage their Lead or manager, HRBP, the Compliance or Legal Teams, or the investigator looking into the concern. 2.2. Reporting Concerns 2.2.1 What Should be Reported You should report conduct that you believe violates Blockʼs Code or Blockʼs policies, or that you believe is unethical or unlawful. This includes, but is not limited to, the below conduct: ● Fraud; ● Irregularities regarding accounting, internal accounting controls, or auditing matters; ● Insider trading; ● Corruption and bribery; ● Money laundering or terrorist financing; ● Collusion with competitors or other violation of competition laws; ● Intellectual property violations; ● Theft or misuse of Block assets; ● Harassment; ● Discrimination; ● Unsafe workplace; ● Retaliation; ● Deliberately concealing violations or withholding relevant information concerning a violation of law; and ● Any other potential violations of Blockʼs Code or policies, or applicable laws or regulations. 2.2.2 Where to Report a Concern Block provides a variety of channels for individuals to report concerns. You should feel comfortable reporting a concern using whatever channel or resource you prefer. If you desire to report your concern anonymously, you may use Blockʼs dedicated Ethics Line. External or contingent workers should report concerns through the Ethics Line, their external engagement partner, or their Block Contact Lead. Manager go/myprofile ethics@ Compliance Team Human Resources go/myhrbp Employee Relations erpb@ Legal employment-legal@ 3 Ethics Line Online: ethics.block.xyz Phone: 18888298316. Phone Outside the United States: visit the appendix for local number 2.3. Confidentiality & Anonymity You are encouraged to provide your identity when making a Report, as this information can be helpful to the investigation; however, you may choose to remain anonymous by reporting concerns through the Ethics Line. Regardless of whether you provide your information or remain anonymous, Block will examine the Report based on its own merits and take appropriate actions, which may include an investigation. While Block cannot guarantee complete confidentiality of a reporting party, we treat all such information with a high degree of discretion, consistent with Block's need to investigate the matter, and our compliance with applicable laws and regulations. 2.4 Investigations Concerns that are reported to Block will be timely reviewed and addressed as appropriate. Due to considerations such as confidentiality and ensuring the integrity of investigations, we may not be able to share with you the details of such investigations. Subject to applicable laws, all Employees are required to cooperate with investigations and provide complete, accurate and truthful information. Retaliating against someone who has made a Report or participated in an investigation in Good Faith is not tolerated. 3. Compliance & Enforcement 3.1. Monitoring and Reporting On an ongoing basis, the Policy Owner and/or their designee monitors activities for compliance and objective achievement. As outlined in the Compliance Management System Policy, Compliance will provide regular updates, as applicable, to the Block, Inc. Board and executive management. Reporting may cover specific information related to: ● Policies and standards ● Procedures ● Audit/exam findings ● Issues management ● Training ● Complaints 4 3.2. Training All relevant personnel will receive training at least annually covering applicable requirements and guidance to implement this Policy effectively. All training related activities are monitored by the Compliance Team and are outlined in the Compliance Training Program. 3.3. Record Retention All Documents are retained according to the longer of: ● Blockʼs Data Classification & Handling Policy ● Applicable regulatory requirements 3.4. Exceptions Exceptions to this Policy are not allowed. 3.5. Confidentiality This document is confidential and should only be accessed by employees. 4. Questions & Contact Information Questions: For questions or clarifications about this Policy, please contact the Policy Owner. Review related documents: ● Block, Inc. | Global Code of Business Conduct and Ethics 5. Document Details & Revisions Review and Approval: This document shall be reviewed and approved at least annually and on an ad hoc basis in accordance with the Compliance Document Approval Matrix. Approval History: Approver Title Type Description Date John Alfano Head of Global Initial Appro… Initial creation January 6, 2025 Policy & Governance Compliance Compliance Scheduled R… Annual review; March 25, 2026 5 Policy Policy Committee updated Committee document to new template, no material updates Document Details: Business Block Scope Global Compliance Domain Ethics & Employee Conduct Document Owner Global Ethics Lead Approved By Compliance Policy Committee Document Tier Tier 2 6 Appendix: Intʼl Ethics Line Phone Numbers Country Phone Number Argentina 08003330588 Australia 1800138156 Austria 0800296431 Belgium 080026063 Brazil 08007610139 Bulgaria 080030027 Canada 18338243445 Chile 800914229 China 4000281735 Colombia 018009145004 Croatia 0800200436 Czech Republic 800040866 Denmark 80820965 Finland 0800412167 France 0805080398 Germany 08001813682 Greece 008008481314 Hong Kong 800969664 Hungary 0680088040 India 0008001004501 Ireland 1800816839 Israel 1809480122 Italy 800142807 Japan 0120 679161 Korea South 03084910151 Malaysia 1800812708 Mexico 8002833469 Netherlands 08000200750 New Zealand 0800002148 Norway 80024714 Philippines 180013220508 Poland 00800009075 Portugal 800180296 Puerto Rico 18442283491 Romania 0800630115 Russian Federation 88001006355 7 Saudi Arabia 8008912139 Serbia 800190758 Singapore 8003211712 Slovakia 800010001 Slovenia 080488873 South Africa 0800172989 Spain 900933292 Sweden 020881680 Switzerland 0800561428 Taiwan 00801128123 Tunisia 31300364 Turkey 08006212475 UAE 80006512023 Ukraine 0800801216 U
documents.EE-008: Squareup Europe Limited Conflicts of Interest Policy Last Approved: Sep 29, 2025 Effective Date: Sep 29, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: Squareup Europe Limited (“SELˮ) is an authorised Electronic Money Institution, regulated and supervised by the Financial Conduct Authority (“FCAˮ) under Firm Reference Number 900846. The indirect and ultimate parent company is Block, Inc., a Delaware corporation, and a merchant services aggregator and mobile payments company based in the United States of America. This Conflicts of Interest Policy (“Policyˮ) outlines SELʼs approach to identifying, managing, and mitigating conflicts of interest within the firm. SEL is dedicated to adhering to the principles of fair treatment and good outcomes for Customers, conducting itself with honesty, fairness, and professionalism, and acting in the best interests of its Customers. An important element of these principles is that SEL has appropriate arrangements in place to prevent Conflicts of Interest from adversely affecting the interests of SELʼs Customers. This Policy provides a clear statement of the standards around identifying, documenting, escalating and managing or preventing Conflicts of Interest. The objective of this Policy is to communicate SELʼs requirements regarding Conflicts of Interest and to advise all Staff Members of their obligation to disclose Conflicts of Interests and maintain high ethical standards. SEL and its Staff Members must maintain the highest possible standards of integrity in business relationships, both inside and outside the organization. Staff Members must: ● Reject any business practice which might be deemed improper (including practices that might benefit SEL where there is a Conflict of Interest); ● Never use authority or position for personal gain; ● Act at all times with impartiality, independence, and integrity; and ● Avoid being, or giving the appearance of being, in a position which may result in actual or perceived detriment to SEL's reputation and/or interests. Staff Members must take all reasonable and appropriate steps to prevent or avoid situations where a Conflict of Interest may happen, potentially arise, or have been perceived to have happened. It is not enough to rely solely on the disclosure and recording of (actual, potential or perceived) conflicts. Proactive steps must be taken to prevent Conflicts of Interest from occurring in the first place and to ensure that personal interests do not conflict with the wider interests of SEL or its Customers at any time. Staff are personally accountable for disclosing Conflicts of Interest and/or seeking direction on any issues which could potentially conflict with Staff responsibilities to SEL as they arise in line with the Policy Requirements Section 3 of this Policy. Where Conflicts of Interest are not properly managed, there may be serious consequences for SEL and for all those involved. In extreme cases, Conflicts of Interest may facilitate fraud, corruption, or other serious issues. Furthermore, they have the potential to negatively impact the business and tarnish the reputation of SEL. The person responsible could be subject to disciplinary action internally with SEL, or criminal sanctions externally (e.g. if it is deemed to be bribery or corruption). Scope: This Policy is established by SEL and is applicable to all SEL Staff Members. SEL requires all Staff Members to adhere to the standards set out in this Policy and any other SEL and Block Inc., standards which seek to prevent and manage Conflicts of Interest. Failure to comply with this Policy may result in disciplinary and/or legal action. As a member of the Block Inc. Group, SEL is subject to the Block, Inc. Global Code of Business Conduct and Ethics (“the Codeˮ) and Global Conflicts of Interest Policy and should read this Policy in connection with the Code and Global Conflicts of interest. If there is any conflict between the Code and applicable local law, the most restrictive requirement must be followed. Additionally, SEL requires Outsource Service Providers (“OSPˮ) to communicate and make their Staff aware of their responsibilities under this Policy and to monitor the implementation of this Policy. Definitions: Terminology Definition Conflict of Interest Is a situation where one or more persons or entities have competing interests and the serving of one interest may involve detriment to another. In terms of Block Inc, Conflicts of Interest occur when Personal interests are in potential conflict with an employeeʼs role and work at Block. Outsource Service Are considered as a third-party entity that is undertaking an outsourced Providers (“OSPˮ) process, service or activity, or parts thereof, under an outsourcing arrangement on behalf of SEL. The term refers to both external third party service providers and intra/inter group service providers. Staff Member(s)/ All employees and fixed-term and contingent workers who support the Staff operations and/or subsidiaries covered by this Policy, and include interns, temporary workers, consultants, vendors, third-party providers, and contractors. An employee or external worker (e.g., contingent labour, contractors) hired by SEL who supports the operations covered by this Policy. Close Personal Includes family members (e.g., spouse, domestic partner, parent, Relationship son/daughter, sibling, grandparent, aunt/uncle, niece/nephew, cousin, step/half/or in-law relation, etc.), individuals living in the same household, individuals with whom an individual has a personal relationship of an intimate or romantic nature, or any other person with whom the individual has such a close bond that could impair, or appear to impair the individual's objectivity. Personal Interest Means a Block employee's situation or circumstances outside Block that could create, potentially create or appear to create a financial or other benefit to the employee or their Close Personal Relationship. Customers A Customer is defined as all SEL Cash App users and SEL Square merchants, including potential Customers, where appropriate. 2. Policy Requirements 2.1 Conflicts of Interest A Conflict of Interest occurs when competing interests impair the ability to make an objective, unbiased decision that impacts professional duties or SELʼs interests. Conflicts of interest can arise in many situations, including Personal Relationships with family and friends, activities outside of work (such as secondary employment), financial interests in or board/advisory positions with companies that do business or compete with SEL or Block, and business opportunities that employees may learn about through their position in SEL or Block. Conflicts of Interest under this Policy includes the following: ● Actual: these are Conflicts of Interest where there is an actual conflict between an employeeʼs duties or responsibilities to SEL or Block and any external interest; ● Potential: these are conflicts which may materialise in the future based on current circumstances or forecasted changes in circumstances; ● Perceived: these are Conflicts of Interest where a reasonable person would view that, based on available information, there may be a conflict; and ● Where direct Conflicts of Interest may not in fact exist. To assist in the identification of Conflicts of Interest, Annex 1 includes non-exhaustive lists of (i) relationships where Conflicts of Interest may arise and (ii) Conflicts of Interest scenarios, as well as (iii) a sample of specific examples of circumstances in which these Conflicts of Interest arise. 2.2 Regulatory Requirements Conflicts of Interest represent a key element of conduct risk and consumer protection risk. The identification, monitoring, management and mitigation of Conflicts of Interest are critical to the development of a robust conduct risk environment. SEL has a number of regulatory requirements pertaining to Conflicts of Interest, including regulations such as the “FCA Handbook SYSC 10 Conflicts of Interestˮ, and “European Banking Authority Guidelines on Outsourcing Arrangementsˮ, and as such SEL is expected to have established processes and controls in place for Conflicts of Interest management. 2.3 Identification and reporting Employees are required to disclose Conflicts of Interest, as described in this policy, as soon as possible. This includes any situation in which an employee is involved, or plans to become involved, which could result in an actual, perceived, or potential Conflict of Interest. Once the conflict has been disclosed, no action should be taken until the disclosure has been reviewed and a decision is provided. ● All Conflicts of Interest, or transactions or relationships that could give rise to Conflicts of Interest must be disclosed immediately. This requirement extends to any interest or relationships involving all Staff, their immediate family members or any other individual with whom they have Close Personal Relationships. All Staff are expected to exercise good judgement and common sense in anticipating situations that may give rise toConflicts of Interest. Staff and other persons working on behalf of SEL who believe they may have Conflicts of Interest, become aware of a potential Conflicts of Interest with others, or are uncertain as to whether the potential for a Conflict of Interest actually exists must disclose it using the reporting channels described below: Disclose your potential Conflict of Interest by visiting: go/oar for Outside Activity Requests go/disclose for all other Conflicts of Interest For guidance and questions, reach out to Compliance at ethics@; and, employment-legal@ for questions regarding OARs. ● Once a Conflict of Interest has been disclosed, Staff are to take no further action until the disclosure has been reviewed and approval is provided by one or more of the following, depending on the type of conflict involved: the Ethics team, the Legal team, HR Business Partner or the Outsourcing Oversight Committee (“OC

Introduction This Speak Up Policy (“Policyˮ) outlines SELʼs approach to Protected Disclosures. SEL has a culture to encourage staff to raise concerns of wrongdoing without fear of retaliation. Staff are to adhere to this Policy along with Block, Inc.ʼs (“Blockˮ) Global Code of Business Conduct and Ethics (the “Codeˮ) if they become aware of information which suggests that wrongdoing (as described below) may have happened or may happen if steps are not taken to address it. The UK Head of Compliance and MLRO is responsible for the management of this Policy. 2. Purpose SEL is committed to maintaining the highest standards of honesty, openness and accountability and recognises that all staff have an important role to play in achieving this goal. SEL strives to conduct its business with honesty and with integrity at all times, and expects all staff to maintain high standards in accordance with our Block, Inc.'s Block Global Code of Business Conduct and Ethics (the "Code"). However, all organisations face the risk of things going wrong from time to time, or of unknowingly harbouring illegal or unethical conduct. A culture of openness and accountability is essential in order to prevent such situations occurring and to address them when they do occur. The main purpose of this policy is: ● To encourage staff to report suspected wrongdoing as soon as possible, in the knowledge that their concerns will be taken seriously and investigated as appropriate, and that their confidentiality will be respected; ● To provide staff with guidance as to how to raise those concerns; and ● To reassure staff that they should be able to raise genuine concerns without fear of reprisals, even if they turn out to be mistaken. 3. Scope This Policy is established by SEL and applies to all Staff, who acquire information in respect of a relevant wrongdoing (as described in Section 4.1 below) in a work-related context. Speak Up Policy | Square | Squareup Europe Limited 6 Confidential 4. Policy Requirements 4.1 Protected Disclosure A Protected Disclosure is a disclosure made by staff of relevant information in accordance with law or this Policy, which in the staffʼs reasonable belief, tends to show one or more relevant wrongdoings (as explained below). A "Relevant Wrongdoing" in relation to the workplace means that: ● An offence has been committed or is being or is likely to be committed; ● A person has failed, is failing or is likely to fail to comply with any legal obligation (other than an obligation arising under the worker's contract of employment or contract of services); ● A miscarriage of justice has occurred, is occurring or is likely to occur; ● The health and safety of an individual has been, is or is likely to be endangered; ● The environment has been, is or is likely to be damaged; ● An unlawful or improper use of funds or resources of a public body or other public money has occurred or is likely to occur; ● That a “Breachˮ has occurred- for the purpose of this Policy, a breach means a breach of the laws governing the following matters (the below is a non-exhaustive list): ○ Financial services, products and markets, and prevention of money laundering and terrorist financing; ○ Protection of privacy and personal data, and security of network and information systems; and ○ Consumer protection. ● A Relevant wrongdoing also includes information tending to show that any of the above matters has been, is being or is likely to be concealed or destroyed or an attempt has been, is being or is likely to be made to conceal or destroy such information. ● A Relevant Wrongdoing can include a Relevant Wrongdoing that occurred, occurs or would occur in the United Kingdom UK or anywhere else in the world. 4.2 What is Not a Protected Disclosure Generally, a matter or a concern may not be a Protected Disclosure under this Policy if it is an interpersonal grievance exclusively affecting Staff (for example, a complaint about bullying, or other type Speak Up Policy | Square | Squareup Europe Limited 7 Confidential of personal dispute with a colleague or manager which concerns Staff exclusively) or relates to a Staff member's individual contract of employment. 5. Reporting a Concern 5.1 General Principles Staff are encouraged to report relevant information in relation to Relevant Wrongdoings as quickly as possible to prevent escalation and to help SEL to contain and reverse any adverse consequences. Staff must provide as much detail as possible including the times, dates, sequence of events, locations and factual details of the matter or concern when reporting a concern. SEL will keep confidential the identity of the Reporter and any third party mentioned in the report, to the extent reasonably possible, consistent with the need to adequately investigate the information, and our compliance with applicable laws and regulations.
