documents.EE-010: Conduct Standards Policy Square | Squareup International Limited Last Approved: Mar 30, 2026 Effective Date: Mar 30, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: Squareup International Limited (“SILˮ or “the Companyˮ) is authorised by the Central Bank of Ireland (the “CBI ˮ) as an e-money institution (“EMoney Firmˮ) pursuant to Regulation 9 of the European Communities Electronic Money) Regulations 2011 (as amended) (the “EMoney Regulationsˮ). The Company was incorporated on 26 May 2016 under registration number 583287 and was authorised as an EMoney Firm by the CBI on 18 June 2020 (with CBI reference number: C184117. The Central Bank Individual Accountability Framework) Act 2023 (the “Actˮ) provides for the introduction of the Individual Accountability Framework (“IAFˮ), which is designed to: ● Improve governance, performance, and accountability in regulated firms by establishing a framework of enhanced clarity as to who is responsible for what within these firms; ● Clarify the standards to be met by individuals holding these responsibilities; ● Promote the embedding of a culture of ethical compliance in regulated firms; and ● Achieve better outcomes for consumers and users of financial services and for the ongoing functioning of the economy. The four key areas covered by the Act are noted below and it is the first area, Conduct Standards which is governed by this Policy: ● Conduct Standards, which set out the standards of behaviour the CBI expects of firms and certain individuals working within them; ● A Senior Executive Accountability Regime (otherwise known as “SEARˮ) which ensures clearer accountability by imposing obligations on in-scope firms and senior managers within them to set out clearly where responsibility and decision-making lies for their business. Currently this requirement does not apply to SIL; ● Enhancements to the Fitness & Probity F&P Regime; and 1 ● Enhancements to CBIʼs Administrative Sanctions Procedure, including a key change regarding the CBIʼs ability to take enforcement directly against individuals for breaches of their obligations rather than only for their participation in breaches committed by a firm. The Conduct Standards, as a key element of the IAF and guidance on the IAF (the “Guidanceˮ) came into force in December 2023. As a regulated EMoney Firm, the Company and individuals performing certain roles in and on behalf of SIL, are subject to the Conduct Standards, and related provisions in the Act and the Guidance. The purpose of this Conduct Standard Policy (“Policyˮ) is to facilitate the Companyʼs compliance with these obligations. This Policy sets out the Conduct Standards that are applicable to SIL and its Controlled Functions CFs and Pre-Approval Controlled Functions PCFs and the related obligations under the Act and the Guidance. This Policy is an integral part of SILʼs internal IAF, and promotes the integration of the Conduct Standards into the culture and conduct of the affairs of SIL, in order to drive the right behaviours and standards in SIL. This Policy should be read in conjunction with the Companyʼs Conduct Standards procedure, and Fitness and Probity policy and procedures. The Companyʼs HR Business Partner can be contacted to obtain a copy of any of these documents, if needed. Scope: This Policy is established by SIL and is applicable to individuals that hold CFs or PCFs within SIL and individuals that perform CF or PCF roles on behalf of SIL. Definitions: Terminology Definition Controlled A CF is a function performed with respect to a regulated financial service Functions provider, like Squareup International Limited (“SILˮ), which is prescribed by (“CFsˮ) the CBIas a CF. A full list of CBI-prescribed CFs can be found here. CF1 A CF1 is a type of controlled function that has the ability to exercise significant influence on the conduct of a regulated financial services providerʼs affairs. Pre-Approval A PCF is a subset of CFs. PCFs are key roles within a firm and by virtue of Controlled the nature of the role, require pre-approval from the CBI. A full list of PCFs Functions can be found here on pages 129 - 130. (“PCFsˮ) Staff All SIL workers including current and former employees, officers, directors, consultants, independent contractors, trainees, interns, work experience trainees, part-time and fixed-term workers, casual and agency staff, volunteers and executive and non-executive directors of the board of SIL. This includes those who are in a recruitment or other pre-contractual negotiation with SIL. 2 2. Policy Requirements 2.1. Conduct Standards Introduction to the Conduct Standards The Conduct Standards act as a benchmark to guide regulated firms and CFs and PCFs in those firms, individually and collectively, as to the standards of behaviour expected of them. The Conduct Standards are the basic standards that underpin the provision of financial services and the relationships of trust that are central in this area. Importantly, the Conduct Standards impose positive, enforceable legal obligations on CFs and PCFs to act in accordance with a single set of standards of expected behaviour. That being said the Conduct Standards reflect the sound values to which all Staff should already hold themselves. The Conduct Standards are comprised of: ● The Common Conduct Standards, which apply to all CFs and PCFs; ● The Additional Conduct Standards, which apply to PCFs and CF1 only; and ● The Business Standards, which apply to SIL as an entity. These standards were developed by the CBI in conjunction with the CBIʼs review of the Consumer Protection Code, which comes into effect in March 2026. Please see SILʼs Consumer Protection Policy for more details. The Conduct Standards apply to CFs and PCFs that perform their role in Ireland or abroad, whether or not the role is outsourced and the Conduct Standards are to be adhered to whether or not the CF/PCF is dealing with customers based in Ireland or abroad. Reasonable Steps CFs and PCFs must take reasonable steps to discharge their responsibilities and duties under the Conduct Standards. CFs and PCFs should consider what steps would be considered reasonable in particular circumstances and whether they have taken such reasonable steps. Below are some of the factors the CBI may consider when determining if an individual took such reasonable steps: ● The nature of SILʼs business, including its scale and complexity; ● The functions of the CF/PCF role, and the level of knowledge and experience that a person with such functions could reasonably be expected to have (one such factor would be the steps taken to ensure their awareness of key risks and developments, to include regulatory developments, that may impact the area of business they are responsible for); ● The level of knowledge and experience of the person (this includes taking account of learning curves if a person is moving into a different or more senior role); ● The existence and application (or otherwise) of— ○ appropriate and effective systems (including risk management systems, internal control mechanisms and governance arrangements); ○ effective oversight of any delegation of responsibilities and effective safeguards against any inappropriate delegation; and 3 ○ appropriate and effective procedures for identifying and remedying problems. ● The extent to which any matter in the above bullet point was within the control or influence of the person; and ● Any guidelines published by the CBI in this regard. Discharging responsibilities and duties under the Conduct Standards should not be a tick box exercise. CFs and PCFs are expected to be experienced, competent professionals and should be in a position to: ● Fully understand the area of the business for which they are responsible; ● Fully understand their own responsibilities, the inherent responsibilities of their PCF role and how they relate to the responsibilities of others in SIL; ● Fully understand risks and consequences of their decisions and actions and behaviours, on SIL, its Staff, its customers and the wider market; ● Take the necessary steps required to ensure that the area of the business for which they are responsible is managed appropriately e.g. by questioning/challenging, being thorough, making informed decisions, delegating appropriately and being in a position to discharge their duties; ● Satisfy themselves with the quality of any input provided by other Staff that is required for them to carry out their responsibilities; and ● Understand their role in the effective running of the business more broadly, including their obligations in relation to effective collective decision-making and seek to create and/or contribute to an overall environment where the risk of a regulatory breach is minimised. Please see the section on Collective Decision Making below, for more details. Collective Decision Making CFs and PCFs must be fully aware of matters for which they are collectively responsible, even if other individuals are responsible for those specific areas, so that they can actively contribute to and challenge relevant decisions. SIL Board members and other senior managers should also be familiar with obligations that apply under the corporate governance framework and Companies Acts in respect of their role and responsibilities in decision making at the firm. To ensure that SILʼs collective decision making avoids barriers to effective decisions such as groupthink, herd behaviour or domineering individuals, CFs and PCFs must exercise due care, skill and diligence when partaking in collective decision making. This includes, for example CFs and PCFs: ● Ensuring sufficient attention to and awareness of their own behaviour, cognitive biases of participants and the group dynamics that influence the results; ● Providing input and constructive challenge, in such a way that recognises both the value and limitations of their own knowledge, skills and experience; ● Contributing in an engaging and inclusive man
documents.EE-011: Conflicts of Interest Policy Square | Squareup International Limited Last Approved: Mar 30, 2026 Effective Date: Mar 30, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: SIL is an authorised Electronic Money Institution, regulated and supervised by the Central Bank of Ireland (“the CBIˮ) under Firm Reference Number C184117. The direct parent company is Block, Inc., a Delaware corporation, and a merchant services aggregator and mobile payments company based in San Francisco, California in the United States of America. It is registered as a Money Services Business with the Financial Crimes Enforcement Network. This Conflicts of Interest Policy (“Policyˮ) is established by SIL and is applicable to SIL. SIL is committed to abiding by the principles of treating customers fairly and acting honestly, fairly and professionally in the best interests of its customers. An important element of these principles is that SIL has appropriate arrangements in place designed to prevent Conflicts of Interest from adversely affecting the interests of SILʼs customers. This Policy provides a clear statement of the standards around identifying, documenting, escalating and appropriately managing or preventing Conflicts of Interest. The objective of this policy is to communicate SILʼs requirements in respect of Conflicts of Interest; and to advise all Staff Members of their obligation to disclose Conflicts of Interest and maintain high ethical standards SIL and its Staff Members must take all reasonable and appropriate steps to prevent or avoid situations where a Conflict of Interest may happen, potentially happen or have been perceived to have happened. It is not enough to rely on the disclosure and recording of (actual, potential or perceived) conflicts. Proactive steps must be taken to prevent a Conflict of Interest from occurring in the first place and to ensure that our personal interests do not conflict with the wider interests of SIL or its customers at any time. Staff Members are all personally accountable for disclosing conflicts of interests,and/or seeking direction on any issues which could potentially conflict with Staff Memberʼs responsibilities to SIL, . Where conflicts of interests are not properly managed, there may be serious consequences for SIL 1 and for all those involved. In extreme cases, conflicts of interests may facilitate fraud, corruption, or other serious issues. In addition, they can damage the business and harm SILʼs reputation. The person responsible could be subject to disciplinary action internally with SIL, or criminal sanctions externally e.g. if it is deemed to be bribery or corruption. Scope: This Policy applies to all SIL Staff Members, Officers, and Directors (collectively; Staff Members). SIL requires all Staff Members to adhere to the standards set out in this Policy and any other SIL and Block Inc., standards which seek to prevent and manage Conflicts of Interest. As a member of the Block Inc. Group, SIL is subject to the Block, Inc. Global Code of Business Conduct and Ethics (“the Codeˮ). This Policy should be read in connection with the Code and if there is any conflict between the Code and applicable local law, the most restrictive requirement must be followed. Additionally, SIL requires OSPʼs to communicate and make their staff aware of their responsibilities under this Policy and to monitor the implementation of this Policy. Definitions: Terminology Definition Outsource Are considered as a third-party entity that is undertaking an outsourced Service process, service or activity, or parts thereof, under an outsourcing Providers arrangement on behalf of SIL. The term refers to both external third party (“OSPˮ) service providers and intra/inter group service providers. Staff Member(s) All employees and fixed-term and contingent workers who support the or Staff operations and/or subsidiaries covered by this Policy, and include interns, temporary workers, consultants, vendors, third-party providers, and contractors. This includes Officers and Directors of SIL. An employee or external worker (e.g., contingent labour, contractors) hired by Squareup International Limited who supports the operations covered by this Policy. 2. Policy Requirements 2.1. Conflict of Interest A Conflict of Interest is a situation where one or more persons or entities have competing interests and the serving of one interest may involve detriment to another.A Conflict of Interest under this Policy includes: ● An actual Conflict of Interest (i.e. a Conflict of Interest that has arisen); ● A potential Conflict of Interest (i.e. a Conflict of Interest that may arise given particular facts and circumstances); and ● A perceived Conflict of Interest (i.e. a situation which may give rise to the perception of a Conflict of Interest) even where a Conflict of Interest may not in fact exist. 2 To assist in the identification of Conflicts of Interest, Annex 1 includes non-exhaustive lists of (i) relationships where Conflicts of Interest may arise and (ii) Conflicts of Interest scenarios, as well as (iii) a sample of specific examples of circumstances in which these Conflicts of Interest arise. 2.2. Regulatory Requirements Conflicts of Interest represent a key element of conduct risk and consumer protection risk. The identification, monitoring, management and mitigation of conflicts of interest are critical to the development of a robust conduct risk environment. SIL has a number of regulatory requirements pertaining to conflicts of interest, including the CBIʼs Consumer Protection Code and European Banking Authority Guidelines on Outsourcing Arrangements, and as such SIL is expected to have established processes and controls in place for Conflicts of Interest management. 2.3. Identification and Reporting ● All Conflicts of Interest, or transactions or relationships that could give rise to a Conflicts of Interest must be disclosed immediately. This requirement extends to any interest or relationships involving Staff members, their immediate family members or any other individual with whom they have close personal relationships. All Staff members are expected to exercise good judgement and common sense in anticipating situations that may give rise to a Conflicts of Interest. Staff members and other persons working on behalf of SIL who believe they may have a Conflict of Interest, become aware of a potential Conflicts of Interest with others, or are uncertain as to whether the potential for a Conflict of Interest actually exists must disclose it through the available channels detailed on the Conflicts of Interest page. ● Once the Conflict of Interest has been disclosed, Staff members are to take no further action until the disclosure has been reviewed and approval is provided by one or more of the following, depending on the type of Conflict of Interest involved: the Ethics team, the Legal team, HR Business Partner or the Outsourcing & Resilience Oversight Committee (‘OROCʼ) ● Staff members must promptly take action to eliminate a Conflict of Interest if SIL asks Staff members to do so. When necessary, SIL will take appropriate action to manage the conflict based on the circumstances. ● If Staff members seek guidance from their Lead, the Lead is to direct their Staff members to the correct resource. If there is any violation or suspected violation of this Policy, to include non-disclosures, the Code or applicable local law that a Lead becomes aware of, the Lead must promptly report this. Leads may be held responsible if they donʼt report misconduct; ● Potential Conflicts of Interest arising from potential outsourcing arrangements, to include intra-group outsourcing, should be identified via the completion of due diligence. Please refer to SILʼs Outsourcing Policy for more details. All such identified Conflicts of Interest should be disclosed through the available channels detailed on the Conflicts of Interest page. ● Any Director who is in any way directly or indirectly personally interested in a transaction with SIL must disclose the nature and extent of that interest to the Board at the Board meeting and the conflict shall be recorded in the minutes of the meeting. Such Director will abstain from voting on any such transactions. 3 2.4. Evaluation and Management of Conflicts of Interest The Ethics team is required to: ● Maintain a record of Conflicts on Interest in a case management system1 ● Evaluate any Conflicts of Interest reported to them. Other relevant stakeholders may be included in this evaluation in order to opine on whether a Conflict of Interest exists. These additional stakeholders may include HR, Trust and Safety, Legal, Employee Relations, and Outside Counsel, and the reporter's lead, depending on the nature of the Conflict of Interest; ● Make a recommendation, based on the above mentioned evaluation and recommendations from other relevant stakeholders as described above, as to the best course of action to resolve, manage or avoid the Conflict of Interest, including further escalation to a higher management authority where necessary, to include SILʼs OROC and or SILʼs Board. These recommendations may include placing any required conditions on the approval of a Conflict of Interest, such as the reporter recusing themselves from contract negotiations, performance evaluations etc., where a Conflict of Interest exists ; and ● Maintain a record of all the above including the output of the Conflicts of Interest evaluation. SILʼs OROC is required to: ● Assess and manage Conflicts of Interest with regard to its outsourcing arrangements. Such Conflicts of Interest are to be reported by Staff members to the Ethics team, for onward escalation to the OROC. ● Assess and make a determination about any reported Conflicts of Interest with respect to OSP, together with any recommendations from the Ethics team as to how to appropriately manage/ prevent/ resolve the Conflicts of Interest provided by the Ethics team. ● Escalate a reported Confli
documents.EE-012: Fitness and Probity Policy Square | Squareup International Limited Last Approved: Mar 24, 2026 Effective Date: Mar 24, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: Squareup International Limited (“SILˮ or referred to as “the Companyˮ) is authorised by the Central Bank of Ireland (the “CBI ˮ) as an e-money institution (“EMoney Firmˮ) pursuant to Regulation 9 of the European Communities Electronic Money) Regulations 2011 (as amended) (the “EMoney Regulationsˮ). The Company was incorporated on 26 May 2016 under registration number 583287 and was authorised as an EMoney Firm by the CBI on 18 June 2020 (with CBI reference number: C184117. In 2010, the Central Bank Reform Act 2010 (as amended) (the “Actˮ) created a new unified structure for financial regulation within Ireland and established the CBI as a single fully integrated structure with a unitary board – the CBI Commission. The Act enhances the system of regulatory control and confers additional powers on the CBI and its Governor to support the stability of the financial system and to protect users of financial services. As part of this, the Act harmonised a statutory system for the regulation of persons performing CFs or PCFs in regulated financial service providers. This allowed the CBI to introduce their F & P Regime. This regime has been enhanced by the Central Bank Individual Accountability Framework) Act 2023 (the “IAFˮ). The core aim of the F & P Regime is to ensure that persons in senior positions within firms are competent and capable, honest, ethical and of integrity, and also financially sound. As a regulated EMoney Firm, the Company is subject to the F & P Regime and the provisions of the Act. The purpose of this Policy is to facilitate the Companyʼs compliance with its obligations under the Act. This Policy also provides an explanation of the F & P Regime and the relevant requirements the Company must adhere to, in order to ensure CFs and PCFs are competent and capable, honest, ethical, of integrity and financially sound. 1 This Policy should be read in conjunction with the Companyʼs Fitness and Probity procedures and F & P Framework. The Companyʼs HR can be contacted to obtain a copy of any of these documents, if needed. Scope: This Policy is established by SIL and is applicable to persons that hold CF or PCF roles within SIL and persons that perform CF or PCF roles on behalf of SIL. Definitions: Terminology Definition Controlled A CF is a function performed with respect to a regulated financial service Functions provider, like Squareup International Limited (“SILˮ), which is prescribed by (“CFsˮ) the Central Bank of Ireland (“CBIˮ) as a CF. A full list of CBI-prescribed CFs can be found in the CBIʼs Guidance on the Fitness and Probity Standards.The designation as a CF is dependent on the functions performed by the individual rather than their job title or physical location in the organisation structure. CF1 A CF that has the ability to exercise significant influence on the conduct of the affairs of a regulated financial service provider, such as SIL CF2 A CF that ensures, controls or monitors compliance by a regulated financial service provider with its relevant obligations Pre-Approval A PCF is a Pre-Approval Controlled Function and is a subset of CFs. PCFs are Controlled key roles within a firm and by virtue of the nature of the role, require Functions pre-approval from the CBI. (“PCFs)ˮ 2. Policy Requirements 2.1. The Fitness and Probity Regime Description of Controlled Functions Controlled Functions (“CFˮ) The CFs and PCFs that are relevant to the Company and the holders of those functions are set out in the Companyʼs PCF and CF registers. The Companyʼs CF and PCF Registers are maintained by the Companyʼs HR. Outsourced Functions If PCF and CF roles are outsourced to an ‘unregulated entityʼ (like Block, Inc.), certain conditions must be met under the F & P regime and prior CBI approval is required for any PCF role that is 2 outsourced to an ‘unregulated entityʼ. In all cases, the relevant persons performing the outsourced role must comply with the applicable F & P Standards. The following requirements will apply when the Company is outsourcing a PCF or CF role to an “unregulated entityˮ: Outsourcing PCFs The outsourcing agreement must identify the person who will perform the outsourced PCF function. The Company will obtain the CBIʼs prior written approval for the appointment of that person to the PCF. Any outsourcing arrangement must also identify the person in the Company who is responsible for the performance of the PCF. The Company will complete the certification of the compliance process in respect of each PCF. Outsourcing CFs SIL will put a written agreement/contract in place between it and the outsourced service provider that performs CFs., It is not necessary to identify and list in writing each person performing the CF in that agreement. The outsourced service provider should incorporate the F & P Standards into its own HR process. The outsourced service provider must: (i) be able to identify the persons who will perform the CFs and assess whether those persons are compliant with the F & P Standards; and (ii) assess whether the CFs are compliant with the F & P standards and (iii) obtain those personsʼ agreement to abide by the F & P Standards. The outsourced service provider should, if requested, provide written confirmation to the Company that the persons performing CFs are compliant with the F & P Standards and have agreed to abide by them. The Company remains responsible for satisfying itself on reasonable grounds that persons performing CFs comply with the F & P Standards and that those persons have agreed to abide by the F & P Standards the Company remains responsible for the certification of compliance process in respect of each CF. 2.2. The Fitness and Probity Standards (“F & P Standardsˮ) The F & P Standards set the benchmark for considering the fitness, probity and financial soundness of people appointed to a PCF or a CF and may form the basis for refusing to approve the appointment of a person to a PCF or for removing or prohibiting someone from a CF if they do not meet those standards. The F & P Standards require PCFs and CFs to: ● Be competent and capable; ● Act honestly, ethically and with integrity; and ● Be financially sound. The Company undertakes appropriate due diligence to satisfy itself on reasonable grounds that its PCFs and CFs comply with the F & P Standards and be in a position to certify this. To satisfy the reasonable grounds test, the Company undertakes the required due diligence, prior to appointment, as set out in the sections below. 3 Compliance with this requirement is a continuing obligation and is not a one-off obligation that is discharged once due diligence has been undertaken prior to the appointment of the CF or PCF. The Company will conduct due diligence on persons in CF and PCF roles on an on-going basis. Please see further information on this in the on-going compliance section. The majority of due diligence will be assessed by the Company itself in the case of PCFs and CF1CF2. The Company cannot permit a person to perform a CF or PCF role, or allow them to continue in such a role, unless it is satisfied that the person meets the F & P standards as set out in Appendix 1, a certificate of compliance with the F & P standards is in force in relation to the person; and the person has agreed in writing to comply with any such standards. Certain CF roles may be exempt from the application of these F&P Standards. This does not mean these roles are exempt from the F&P Regime in full i.e. they will remain subject to Part 3 of the Act (e.g. the powers of investigation, suspension and prohibition) and the Conduct Standards within the IAF (“the Conduct Standardsˮ). Appropriate Level of Due Diligence When Assessing Fitness Persons performing a PCF or a CF must be competent and capable. The Company will determine the particular demands of a CF or a PCF, i.e., what qualifications, experience, and knowledge are commensurate with the requirements of the role and other relevant factors will make a person fit to perform that function and meet the responsibilities of the role. The inherent responsibilities of certain PCF roles have been set out in the CBI Guidance on the Fitness and Probity Standards (“the GuidanceˮThe due diligence considered suitable to determine a personʼs fitness to perform a CF or a PCF includes the following measures: ● Evidence of professional qualification(s), where relevant (to be performed as part of initial due diligence and on an ongoing basis, if applicable); ● Professional body checks, where relevant, (to be performed as part of initial due diligence and on an ongoing basis, if applicable; ● Evidence of CPD, where relevant (to be performed as part of initial due diligence and on an ongoing basis, if applicable); ● Record of interview and application, where applicable; ● References (to be performed as part of initial due diligence); ● Record of previous experience. If the level of experience and knowledge set out in the Guidance is met or exceeded, it can be presumed that the individual has sufficient experience/knowledge unless there is an indication to the contrary. An individual who does not hold the years of experience set out could still be considered fit and proper where the Company can justify this; ● Record of experience gained outside of Ireland, where relevant (to be performed as part of initial due diligence); and ● Concurrent responsibilities (to be performed as part of initial due diligence and on an ongoing basis, if applicable). Time commitments must be assessed, which take account of the nature, scale and complexity of the Company, and the individualʼs other mandates such as directorships, other CF/PCF roles. Individuals holding multiple CF roles must demonstrate competency for each,sufficient capacity and the absence of unmanageable 4 conflicts of interest. If
documents.EE-013: Speak Up Policy Squareup International Limited Last Approved: December 8, 2025 Effective Date: December 8, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Speak Up Policy ("Policy") outlines SIL's approach to Protected Disclosures. Staff are to adhere to this Policy along with Block, Inc.'s ("Block") Global Code of Business Conduct and Ethics (the "Code") if they become aware of information which suggests that wrongdoing (as described in Section 2.1 may have happened or may happen if steps are not taken to address it. The EU Chief Compliance Officer ("CCO") is responsible for the management of this Policy. SIL conducts business in compliance with all applicable laws and regulations. The purpose of this Policy is to further SIL's commitment to maintaining the highest standards of honesty, openness and accountability and recognises that all Staff have an important role to play in achieving this purpose. SIL strives to conduct its business honestly and with integrity at all times. SIL culture is to encourage Staff to raise concerns of wrongdoing without fear of retaliation. This Policy and the Code guide Staff on how to raise any matters of genuine concern. SIL will not tolerate retaliation against or penalisation of anyone who uses this Policy correctly to raise concerns. Scope: This Policy is established by SIL and applies to all Staff, who acquire information in respect of a relevant wrongdoing (as described in Section 2.1 in a work-related context. Definitions Term Definition Staff All Squareup International Limited (“SILˮ) workers including current and former employees, officers, directors, consultants, independent contractors, trainees, interns, work experience trainees, part-time and fixed-term workers, casual and agency Staff, volunteers and executive and non-executive directors of the board of SIL. This includes those who are in a recruitment or other pre-contractual negotiation with SIL. 1 Term Definition Protected A Protected Disclosure is a disclosure made by Staff of relevant Disclosure information in accordance with law or this Policy, which in the Staff's reasonable belief, tends to show one or more relevant wrongdoings. Designated Person The EU Chief Compliance Officer is the Designated Person for the purposes of this Policy and, as such, is responsible for responding to reports of matters or concerns under this Policy. Reporter A Reporter is a member of Staff that reports matters or concerns under this Policy via internal reporting channels. 2. Policy Requirements 2.1 Protected Disclosures Protected Disclosures involve the disclosure of relevant information, which in the Staff's reasonable belief, tends to show one or more relevant wrongdoings. A Relevant Wrongdoing in relation to the workplace means that: ● An offence has been committed or is being or is likely to be committed; ● A person has failed, is failing or is likely to fail to comply with any legal obligation (other than an obligation arising under Staff's contract of employment or contract of services); ● A miscarriage of justice has occurred, is occurring or is likely to occur; ● The health and safety of a person has been, is or is likely to be endangered; ● The environment has been, is or is likely to be damaged; ● An unlawful or improper use of funds or resources of a public body or other public money has occurred or is likely to occur; ● A "Breach" has occurred - for the purposes of this Policy, a breach means a breach of the laws governing the following matters (the below is an non-exhaustive list): ○ financial services, products and markets, and prevention of money laundering and terrorist financing; ○ protection of privacy and personal data, and security of network and information systems; and ○ consumer protection. ● A Relevant Wrongdoing also includes information tending to show that any of the above matters has been, is being or is likely to be concealed or destroyed or an attempt has been, is being or is likely to be made to conceal or destroy such information. ● A Relevant Wrongdoing can include a Relevant Wrongdoing that occurred, occurs or would occur in Ireland or anywhere else in the world. 2.2 What is Not a Protected Disclosure Generally, a matter or a concern may not be a Protected Disclosure under this Policy if it is an interpersonal grievance exclusively affecting Staff (for example, a complaint about bullying, or other type of personal dispute with a colleague or manager which concerns Staff exclusively) or relates to a Staff member's individual contract of employment. 2 3. Reporting a Concern 3.1 General Principles Staff are encouraged to report relevant information in relation to Relevant Wrongdoings as quickly as possible to prevent escalation and to help SIL to contain and reverse any adverse consequences. To Speak Up visit www.ethics.block.xyz or call 18888298316. If outside Ireland, local contact numbers are available at www.ethics.block.xyz. Staff must provide as much detail as possible including the times, dates, sequence of events, locations and factual details of the matter or concern when reporting a concern. SIL will keep confidential the identity of the Reporter and any third party mentioned in the report, to the extent reasonably possible, consistent with the need to adequately investigate the information, and SILʼs compliance with applicable laws and regulations. There are however limited circumstances where confidentiality cannot be maintained including for example where: ● The person to whom the protected disclosure was made or referred shows that they took all reasonable steps to avoid so disclosing any such information; or ● The person to whom the Protected Disclosure was made or referred reasonably believes that Staff are consenting to the disclosure of any such information; or ● The person to whom the Protected Disclosure was made or referred reasonably believes that disclosing any such information is necessary for: ○ The effective investigation of the Relevant Wrongdoing concerned; or ○ The prevention of serious risk to the security of the State, public health, public safety or the environment; or ○ The disclosure is otherwise necessary in the public interest or is required by law. In such instances, SIL will ensure that information is handled sensitively. 3.2 Internal Reporting Staff are strongly encouraged to report any matter or concern promptly after it comes to their attention in a work-related context using internal reporting channels. SIL's internal reporting channels operate in a secure manner that ensures the confidentiality of the Reporter and anyone mentioned in the report. It is a protected reporting channel which prevents access by anyone other than authorised persons. 3 SIL takes any allegation of violations, malpractice or breach of obligations very seriously, regardless of who may have committed them. SIL has appointed its Chief Compliance Officer as the Designated Person to respond to reports of matters or concerns under this Policy. The Designated Person is impartial and is trained to respond to these reports. The Designated Person will be the Reporter's point of contact throughout the process. Reports may be made upon request by the Reporter by means of a physical meeting with the Designated Person, within a reasonable period from making of the request. When such a request is made, the Designated Person shall ensure, subject to the consent of the Reporter, that complete and accurate records of the meeting are kept in a durable and retrievable form. The Designated Person may document the meeting by way of i) audio recording, or ii) accurate minute taking. Where the Reporter has disclosed their identity, the Designated Person shall offer the Reporter the opportunity to check, verify and agree the minutes of the meeting by signing them. Where a matter or concern is reported in accordance with this Policy, the report will be acknowledged in writing no later than 7 days after its receipt. The Designated Person will remain in communication with the Reporter as far as is reasonably possible, will follow up in relation to the concern and where necessary will ask Staff for further information. The Designated Person will carry out a diligent follow-up to the report which will include at least the following ("Diligent Follow Up"): - The Designated Person will carry out an initial assessment to establish if there is preliminary evidence that a Relevant Wrongdoing may have occurred. If the Designated Person comes to the conclusion that no preliminary evidence exists, the procedure will be closed or referred to other applicable internal procedure mechanisms. Reporters will be notified in writing of the decision and the reason for it as soon as possible. - Where the Designated Person concludes that preliminary evidence exists of a Relevant Wrongdoing, appropriate actions will be taken to address the wrongdoing, having regard to the nature and seriousness of the matter. - The Designated Person will maintain communication with the Reporter, will inform the Reporter of how they propose to investigate the matter, and will keep the Reporter informed of actions, where possible. However it is important to note that sometimes the need for confidentiality and legal considerations may prevent the Designated Person from giving the Reporter specific details of an investigation. The Designated Person will inform the Reporter of the likely timescales in regard to the steps being taken where possible. The Designated Person will provide feedback to the Reporter no later than 3 months after acknowledgement of receipt of the report or, where no such acknowledgement was sent, not more than 3 months from the date of expiry of the period of 7 days after the report was made. If the Reporter requests further feedback, the Designated Person will provide feedback to the Reporter at 3 month intervals until the investigation is closed. 4 Anonymous Reports As outlined in the Code, Staff who prefer to remain ano
documents.EE-014: Conflicts of Interest Policy Square Australia Last Approved: Dec 18, 2025 Effective Date: Dec 18, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Conflicts of Interest Policy ("Policy") sets out Square AU's approach to identifying, managing, and monitoring conflicts of interest, reflecting our commitment to strong corporate governance and ethical business conduct. Operating in a regulated environment, effective conflict management is essential to maintaining trust and integrity across all our activities. Under the Corporations Act 2001, Square AU is required to have arrangements in place to manage conflicts arising in connection with the provision of financial services. This Policy provides a clear framework to address potential, actual, and perceived conflicts, outlining the responsibilities of all representatives to adhere to these guidelines and promptly report any conflicts. The Chief Compliance Officer (“CCOˮ) has primary responsibility for overseeing and implementing this Policy. Through these measures, Square AU seeks to protect the interests of customers, employees, and stakeholders, ensure regulatory compliance, and promote an ethical culture throughout the organisation. The purpose of this Conflicts of Interest Policy is to provide a clear and structured framework for identifying, assessing, escalating, and managing conflicts of interest within Square AU. It ensures that all conflicts are addressed in a way that upholds Square AUʼs integrity and ethical standards, meets regulatory requirements, and safeguards the interests of customers, employees, and stakeholders. This Policy forms an integral part of Square AUʼs broader compliance and risk management framework, demonstrating our commitment to the highest standards of professional conduct and corporate responsibility. Scope: This Conflicts of Interest Policy is established by Square AU Pty Ltd ("Square AU") and is applicable to all employees, officers, directors, contractors, interns, contingent workers and representatives of Square AU. The Policy sets out the approach to identifying, managing, and mitigating conflicts of interest within the context of Square AU's operations. This Policy applies specifically to the activities and responsibilities of Square AU and its subsidiaries directly involved in the provision of financial services. It governs the conduct of all employees and representatives in relation to their professional duties and external engagements that may give rise to conflicts of interest. This document must be read in conjunction with Block Inc Policies and programs. Square AU will make reasonable attempts to align with Block Inc Policies unless regulatory obligations differ. Definitions: Terminology Definition Conflict of Interest ● An actual Conflict Of Interest (i.e. a Conflict Of Interest that has arisen); ● A potential Conflict Of Interest (i.e. a Conflict Of Interest that may arise given particular facts and circumstances); ● A perceived Conflict Of Interest (i.e. a situation which may give rise to the perception of a Conflict Of Interest); and ● Where a direct Conflict Of Interest may not in fact exist Actual Conflict of Interest A Conflict of Interest that is currently happening. For example, if an advisor recommends a product that benefits them financially, even though it may not be in the best interest of the client. Potential Conflict of Interest A situation where a Conflict of Interest may arise in the future. For example, an advisor who could benefit from the sale of a particular financial product in the future, though they haven't recommended it yet. Perceived Conflict of Interest A situation where it appears to others that a Conflict of Interest exists, even if there is no actual conflict. This is often based on appearances and can erode trust, even if the advisor is acting impartially. Managing Conflicts of Interest The process by which financial services licensees identify, assess, and address conflicts of interest to ensure that they do not adversely affect clients. This can involve disclosure, avoidance, or control measures. Disclosure of Conflicts The practice of informing clients about any conflicts of interest that may affect the advice or services provided. This is a crucial part of managing conflicts and ensuring transparency. Avoidance of Conflicts Taking steps to eliminate a Conflict of Interest altogether. This may involve not engaging in certain activities or declining to provide advice where a conflict exists. Terminology Definition Control of Conflicts Implementing measures to manage conflicts of interest that cannot be avoided, ensuring that they do not negatively impact clients. This may include setting up barriers between different parts of a business to prevent conflicts from influencing advice. Staff Member(s) or Staff All employees and fixed-term and contingent workers who support the operations and/or subsidiaries covered by this Policy, and include interns, temporary workers, consultants, vendors, third-party providers, and contractors. An employee or external worker (e.g., contingent labour, contractors) hired by Squareup International Limited who supports the operations covered by this Policy. 2. Policy Requirements 2.1 Guiding Principles By implementing this Policy, Square AU seeks to: ● Promote Ethical Conduct: Foster a culture of transparency and ethical behaviour across all levels of the organisation by ensuring that conflicts of interest are addressed promptly and effectively. ● Facilitate Regulatory Compliance: Comply with the obligations set forth under the Corporations Act 2001 and relevant Regulatory Guide 181 - Licensing: Managing conflicts of interest, particularly those related to the management of conflicts of interest in financial services. ● Protect Stakeholder Interests: Safeguard the interests of our customers, employees, and other stakeholders by mitigating the risks associated with conflicts of interest that could compromise the quality and fairness of our services. ● Enhance Corporate Governance: Strengthen Square AU's governance framework by providing clear guidelines and responsibilities for the identification, reporting, and resolution of conflicts of interest. ● Support Informed Decision-Making: Ensure that decisions made within the organisation are free from bias and undue influence, thereby maintaining the trust and confidence of all parties involved in or affected by our business activities. 2.2 Mechanisms for managing conflicts of interest The Board has considered and adopted, among other arrangements, the three mechanisms for managing conflicts of interest prescribed by ASIC in RG 181 in preparing this section of the compliance manual. Under the arrangements set out in this document, Square AU has put in place processes for: ● controlling conflicts of interest; ● avoiding conflicts of interest; and ● disclosing conflicts of interest. These mechanisms are defined in the definition section of this Policy. 2.3 Disclosure Obligations All personnel must self-disclose any actual, potential, or perceived conflicts of interest by following the Conflicts of Interest disclosure process, details of which can be found on the Conflicts of Interest page. Once the potential conflict has been disclosed, no action may be taken until the disclosure has been reviewed by a Manager and approval is provided if required. 2.4 Assessment of Conflicts Square AU will assess business activities, including relationships to identify any potential conflicts of interest. In order to control conflicts of interest, Square AU has put a program place to: ● identify the conflicts of interest relating to the business of Square AU (if any); ● assess and evaluate those conflicts; and ● decide upon, and implement, an appropriate response to those conflicts. Assessment of the business level conflicts are conducted at least annually by the CCO and included in Annexure A. Data on conflicts of interest is reported to the Square AU Board. Examples of what may constitute a conflict which may require disclosure include activities outside of work such as a secondary employment position, personal investments, or engaging friends or family in a business relationship with Square AU. 2.5 Mitigation Strategies Once identified, Conflicts of Interest must be managed through appropriate mitigation strategies, which may include but is not limited too: ● Removing the individual from decision-making related to the conflict; ● Segregation of duties; ● Disclosing the conflict to relevant stakeholders; ● Implementing information barriers or ethical walls; and ● Divesting conflicting interests. 2.6 Breaches of the Policy In the event of a Conflict of Interest breach, Square AU requires mandatory reporting, followed by a confidential investigation to assess the breach's impact. Corrective actions, including removal from conflicting situations and potential disciplinary measures, may be taken. Square AU is to ensure internal transparency through communication of outcomes and, if necessary, external communication to affected stakeholders. Post-incident reviews where applicable may be conducted to evaluate the response and enhance the Conflict of Interest policy where necessary. Enhanced monitoring may be implemented to prevent future breaches, ensuring the organisation's integrity is maintained. 3. Compliance & Enforcement 3.1 Roles and Responsibilities Square AU operates a Three Lines of Defence (“3LoD") Model. All Staff Members are expected to comply with this Policy, including the Anti-Bribery and Corruption element of Blockʼs Code of Business Conduct and Ethics (the Code) at all times. Square AU is required to demonstrate that it took all reasonable steps and exercised all due diligence in order to avoid Conflicts of Interest and the commission of a bribery or corruption offence. Therefore, it is essential that all Staff Members and each line of defence understands its roles and responsibilities in respect to this Policy. Firs
documents.EE-015: Communications Requirements Standard Cash App | Cash App Investing Last Approved: August 22, 2025 Effective Date: August 22, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Cash App Investing Communications Requirements Standard ("Standard") outlines Cash App Investing's approach to acceptable communications practices for Block employees. Cash App Investing conducts business in compliance with all applicable laws and regulations. The purpose of this Standard is to outline Cash App Investing's communications requirements. The Chief Compliance Officer for Cash App Investing, or their designee, is responsible for management of this Standard. Note: For any inquiries regarding what may be considered investing-related, Cash App Investing Compliance can be contacted by email (comms@cashappinvest.com) or Slack at #comms-policy. Scope: This Standard is established by Cash App Investing and is applicable to Block employees. Definitions: Terminology Definition Investing-related All communications relating to Cash App Investing LLC's ("CAI" or communication "Cash App Investing") stocks and exchange-traded funds ("ETFs") services are considered "investing-related". Examples include communications regarding troubleshooting a stock-related bug, discussing investing advertising and designing an investing screen for the app. Conversely, communications that are not related to CAI or CAI's services are excluded. Examples include human resources queries, information technology support requests, anything 100% bitcoin related, Employee Stock Purchase Plan questions, Jack Dorsey's "Ask Me Anything" channel, or other communications unrelated to CAI services. Employees Employees refer to all Block employees and fixed-term and contingent workers who support the operations and/or subsidiaries covered by these Standard, and include interns, temporary workers, consultants, vendors, third-party providers, and contractors. 2. Program Requirements 2.1 Approved and Forbidden Platforms 2.1.1 Instant Messaging Employees are only permitted to use a Slack Investing workspace account to communicate, or must communicate in a direct message ("DM") or channel that is connected to the Slack Investing workspace. The use of Cash App or other Block workspaces (e.g. Square, Block, Tidal) is strictly prohibited unless the communication occurs in a DM or channel where at least one Slack Investing account is connected. For more information, please refer to the How to be Compliant with Slack section below. Text messaging, iMessages, Google Chat, Google Meet Chat, Discord, Signal, Messenger, WeChat, WhatsApp, QQ, Youni, ICQ, Skype, Signal, Wickr, Confide, and any other messaging platform are prohibited. Employees may use a prohibited platform to direct the recipient to an approved platform but must not include any details that are investing-related in the message. For example, a Slack message may be sent that says: "I need to discuss something with you. I have invited you to a channel on another workspace, please check your email for the invite." 2.1.2 Slack Employees who regularly communicate about Investing are required to have a Slack Investing account. There are several options for Employees who do not regularly communicate about Investing to use Slack in a compliant way. Refer to Appendix A for instructions on how to use Slack with and without an Investing account. 2.1.3 Email The sender or at least one of the recipients must be a @cashappinvest.com address. For example, if all participants have only an @block.xyz email address, this would be prohibited. The use of any personal email addresses is strictly prohibited. If no party to the email has a @cashappinvest.com email address, Cash App Investing Compliance (comms@cashappinvest.com) must be contacted before sending the email. Requesting an @cashappinvest.com Address: Employees are required to obtain a @cashappinvest.com email address if they regularly send or receive investing-related emails. Cash App Investing Compliance (comms@cashappinvest.com) must be contacted if an @cashappinvest.com address is required. 2.2 Reporting Communications on Forbidden Platforms If a Staff Member has communicated on forbidden platforms they are required to follow the steps below to capture the communications and ensure they are reported to Compliance. If there are a large number of messages and following the steps below would be overly burdensome, Cash App Investing Compliance (comms@cashappinvest.com) must be contacted to determine if there are other technical solutions available to capture the communications. The required process for reporting communications on forbidden platforms: ● Take a screenshot of the messages that have been received or sent; Ensure that each screenshot captures the date and time of each message, message contents and message participants. If only relative dates show like "Today," include the current date and time from the computer's clock. ● Download all attachments (if applicable). ● Send an email with the screenshots and attachments (if applicable) to comms@cashappinvest.com. Use go/SendSafely for large or sensitive documents. ● Keep all messages, screenshots, and attachments until further instructions are provided. 2.3 Physical Communication Requirements If physical mail is received, Front Office Operations (front-office-operations@cashappinvest.com) must be contacted as soon as possible. Brokerage Operations employees should follow established procedures for sending and receiving physical mail. If planning to produce and/or distribute physical communications such as banners, posters, physical letters, brochures, etc., pre-approval is required and must be obtained from Cash App Investing Compliance (comms@cashappinvest.com). 2.4 Public Appearance Requirements Prior to participating in an investing-related public appearance such as a seminar, forum, radio, or television interview, Employees must contact Cash App Investing Compliance (comms@cashappinvest.com). This applies to any appearance intended for retail or institutional investors. Investors including customers, prospective customers, and the general public at large. A public appearance does not include a private Google Meet meeting with a vendor or potential vendor. It would, however, include a live research interview with customers or prospective customers. Unless otherwise stated by the Compliance team, there is no obligation to record the video and/or audio of a public appearance. If a recording is made, the Cash App Investing Compliance (comms@cashappinvest.com) must be contacted with a go/sendsafely link to the recording. 2.5 Social Media Pre-Approval Requirements Employees are strictly prohibited from publicly communicating anything relating to CAI's services online, including activity on social media unless they have received pre-approval. The only activity permitted is posting resume information on a website such as LinkedIn, as long as there is no promotional content. For example, listing the services and products of Cash App Investing on a LinkedIn profile is forbidden, as it could be misconstrued as a communication relating to Cash App Investing's services and products. In general, Employees are permitted to re-post an employment opportunity as long as the content does not promote the products or services of Cash App Investing. Discussing any investing-related topics online that are unethical or illegal in nature is forbidden. For example, Employees are forbidden from promoting a stock online. Cash App Investing Compliance also generally does not advise that Employees discuss any investing-related topics online. For example, Employees may not: ● Submit a comment on an online newspaper article stating that "Cash App's round-ups for stocks really helped me." ● Post a Cash App screenshot of great stock returns on a Snapchat story. ● Post a blog article where an explanation for how an engineering problem was solved relating to an investing feature on Cash App. ● Post on Reddit that Cash App's investing feature is the easiest to use. ● Respond to a journalist and provide a quote about a new investing feature. It is to be noted that this is not an exhaustive list. If an Employee is unsure about the communication related to any investing-related topics, they must contact Cash App Investing Compliance (comms@cashappinvest.com) to obtain the required pre-approval. 2.6 General Prohibition on Communicating with Customers Employees are prohibited from sending investing-related communications to customers, prospective customers, or members of the public unless it has been pre-approved by the Compliance team or are a member of the Front Office Brokerage Operations team. Employee cooperation is paramount to maintaining compliance with industry rules and regulations. If there are any questions about the requirements listed in this document, Cash App Investing Compliance must be contacted by email (comms@cashappinvest.com) or Slack at #comms-policy. 3. Compliance & Enforcement 3.1 Roles and Responsibilities ● Cash App Investing Chief Compliance Officer ("CAI CCO"): The CAI CCO or their designee will provide assistance and support in implementing this Program across the organization and shall be responsible for establishing appropriate practices designed to ensure that Company operations reflect the Company's commitment to compliance and that all employees are aware of that commitment and their individual responsibility. The CAI CCO is responsible for monitoring compliance with this Standard. The CAI CCO may delegate to others the authority to adopt, develop, and implement procedures and reporting that support this Standard. ● Front Office Brokerage Operations: Front Office Brokerage Operations provides customer-facing service to Cash App Investing customers as well as support for CAI processes and procedures that relate to the customer experience with the Cash App Investing product. ● Cash App Investing Compliance: Cash App Investing Complia

Anti-Money Laundering & Countering the Financing of Terrorism Policy| Block 6 Confidential and Not for Distribution 1. Introduction The Anti-Money Laundering & Countering the Financing ofTerrorismPolicy(“Policy”)outlinesBlock, Inc.’s and its subsidiaries (“Block” or the “Company”) commitment to compliance with anti-money laundering (“AML”) and countering the financing of terrorism (“CFT”) laws and regulations (“Local Laws”) in all jurisdictions in which they operate, and to preventthemisuseofservicesandanyother related involvement in criminal or unlawful activity. Money laundering and terrorist financing are serious financial crimes with potentially serious social andfinancialeffects.TheU.S.governmenthas passed laws that impose affirmative obligations upon covered entities in order to prevent, detect, andreporttheseactivities. 2.Purpose This Policy is designed in compliance with applicable Local Laws and establishes Block’s commitment to a culture of AML/CFT compliance. Because the business activity of each Block subsidiary and jurisdiction varies, the application of individual AML/CFT compliance requirements may also vary. This Policy establishes a framework for satisfying relevant AML/CFT compliance requirements and is intended to be supplemented byadditionalpolicies,programs,andprocedures where necessary, including but not limited to the Global Sanctions Compliance Policy. Compliance with Local Laws is to be achieved through the implementation of arisk-basedcomplianceprogram thatisapprovedbytherelevantgoverningboardofdirectors,whererequired. 3. Scope This Policy is established by Block and is applicable to Covered Products globally across all Block businesslines,subsidiaries,andjurisdictions. Introduction ThisGlobalSanctionsCompliancePolicy(“Policy”)outlinesBlockInc.’s(“Block”)approachtoensuring compliance with applicable Economic Sanctionslawsandregulations.TheGlobalHeadofSanctions isresponsibleformanagementofthisPolicy. 2.Purpose The Policy sets forth Block’s commitment to comply with applicable sanctionslawsandregulations in all jurisdictions in which it does business, including but not limited to those implemented and enforced by (i) theUnitedNations;(ii)theUnitedStates;(iii)Canada;(iv)theEuropeanUnionandany relevant member state thereof; (v) the United Kingdom; (vi) Australia; (vii) New Zealand; and (viii) Japan. This Policy is intended to be supplemented by additionaldocumentationasnecessary. 3. Scope This Policy is established by Block and is applicable globally across all Block business lines, subsidiaries,andjurisdictions. 4.Policy Requirements 4.1 Prohibitions This Policy prohibits activity involving certain jurisdictions and persons, asdescribedindetailbelow. This Policy further prohibits any direct or indirect dealings that would otherwise violate any applicablesanctionslawsandregulations. 4.1.1 Prohibited Jurisdictions To ensure compliance with regulatory requirements in the jurisdictions in which it operates, Block has made a commercial decision pursuant to this Policy to prohibit any directandindirectdealings with, involving, or for the benefit of the following jurisdictions, which Block hasdeterminedpresent Global Sanctions Compliance Policy | Block 5 Confidential anelevatedlegal,business,and/orreputationalrisktothecompany: ● Cuba ● Iran ● NorthKorea ● Syria ● TheCrimeaRegionofUkraine ● So-calledDonetskPeople’sRepublic(DNR)regionofUkraine ● So-calledLuhanskPeople’sRepublic(LNR)regionofUkraine Staff Members are also prohibited from working from and bringing work materials (e.g., laptops, work-relatedmobileapplications)intothesejurisdictions. 4.1.2 Prohibited Persons The Policy also prohibits all direct and indirect dealings with any “sanctioned persons,” defined as any individuals or entities (“Persons”), (a) located, domiciled, resident, or organized in any of the jurisdictions listed above; (b) targeted by any applicable sanctions authority; and/or (c) majority owned or controlled, individually or in the aggregate, directly or indirectly, by one or more persons referredtoin(a)and(b). 4.1.3 Exceptions Block may make exceptions to these prohibitions where dealings with prohibited jurisdictions or persons are permissible under applicable law. Global Sanctions Compliance Policy | Block 6 Confidential 4.3 Internal Policies, Procedures, and Controls The Policy requires Block to implement and maintain policies, procedures, controls, processes, and systems reasonably designed to ensure compliance with all applicable sanctions laws and regulations. Controls may consist of, but are not limited to, customer screening and due diligence, transaction screening, and geoblocking. Block maintains a risk-based approach to implementing controls that is commensurate with sanctions risks posed by Block’s business model, geographic operations,andproductofferings. 4.4 Risk Assessment The Policy requires Block to conduct sanctions risk assessments in order to identify potential sanctionsrisksandtoinformthedesignandprioritiesoftheProgram. 4.5 Independent Testing The Policy requires Block to be subject to independent, periodic risk-based audits on the overall adequacyandeffectivenessofthePrograminaccordancewithregulatoryrequirements. 4.6 Regulatory Reporting The Policy requires Block to comply with sanctions reporting obligations required by relevant regulatory authorities, including but not limited to annual reports ofblockedorfrozenpropertyand ad-hocreportingofcertainblocked,frozen,orrejectedtransactions. 4.7 Duty to Report/Non-Retaliation Any potential violations of this Policy or any sanctions-related law or regulation must be reported immediately to the Global Head of Sanctions or by making a report—including anonymously—at ethics.block.xyz. Report AML/CTF Compliance Program Australia | Afterpay Australia Pty Ltd 7 Confidential 2. Introduction 2.1 Afterpay overview Afterpay Australia Pty Ltd (“Afterpay”) is a subsidiary of Afterpay Pty Ltd, an indirect wholly owned subsidiary of Block, Inc.1 Afterpay is registered as a Reporting Entity with AUSTRAC and is a ‘non-bank lending and financier, non-deposit taking lender (NBNDTL)’, i.e., a non-bank institution that offers loans, but does not hold a banking licence. Afterpay offers a ‘buy now, pay later’ (“BNPL”) service allowing Consumers to make interest free purchases at participating Merchants and pay for them over four instalments. Consumers fund the repayment of the instalments to Afterpay from debit or credit cards. Afterpay pays the Merchant the purchase price of the goods (less a fee), and assumes all Consumer non-payment risk. Afterpay settles these funds to the Merchant’s nominated bank account by providing instructions to its local bank partner to make those payments. There is no charge to the Consumer for the use of Afterpay BNPL as long as payments are made in accordance with their payment schedule. Afterpay’s BNPL service can also be accessed at Merchant stores who are Customers of Afterpay (known as in-network), via the Afterpay and Afterpay Plus Cards, or at Merchant stores who are not Customers of Afterpay (known as out-of-network), via the Afterpay Plus Card. Afterpay also allows Australian Consumers to purchase goods and services from eligible Merchants who are customers of Afterpay in other markets where Afterpay is located. For Australian Consumers, this is limited to Canada, New Zealand, the United Kingdom and the United States. Reciprocally, an eligible Australian Merchant can sell goods and services to Consumers in those same markets. This payment mechanism is called Cross Border Trade (“CBT”). Payments to fund purchases and settle funds to eligible Merchants in these markets are conducted through payment services provided by Afterpay’s local bank. For payments relating to CBT transactions, Afterpay’s Consumers continue to repay their instalments to Afterpay, which are made via the Customers Debit or Credit card via our payment acquiring partners. 1 As at 1 February 2022. AML/CTF Compliance Program Australia | Afterpay Australia Pty Ltd 8 Confidential Afterpay does not conduct IFTIs (International Funds Transfer Instruction), cash transactions nor other transactions that would be subject to specific Designated Services or other prescribed reporting obligations. 2.2 Afterpay’s AML/CTF Program This Anti Money Laundering and Counter Terrorism Financing Program (“AML/CTF Program”) is a standard AML/CTF program as defined by section 84(1) of the AML/CTF Act. It applies to Afterpay Australia Pty Limited ACN 169 342 947 and its directors, officers, employees, contractors and third parties. This AML/CTF Program applies to all areas of Afterpay's business that are involved in the provision of a Designated Service. This includes any function that is carried out on Afterpay's behalf by a third party. Afterpay adopts this AML/CTF Program from the date of approval by the Board of Directors. The AML/CTF Program is divided into two parts, Part A and Part B. This document is Part A. 2.2.1 Part A Part

Part B - Customer Identification and Verification | Afterpay Australia Pty Limited 6 Confidential 2. Overview This document sets out Afterpay’s Part B of the Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorism Financing Program for the purposes of section 84 of the Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorism Financing Act 2006. The Customer identification and verification procedures specified in this Part B (including all of its Schedules) outline how Afterpay collects and verifies KYC Information (as that term is defined in the AML/CTF Rules) about Customers so that Afterpay can be reasonably satisfied that the Customer exists and is who they claim to be. This document includes references to Afterpay’s Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorism Financing Program Part A. 3. Customer Identification and Verification Procedures 3.1 Timing of Customer identification and verification Afterpay performs the ACIP in respect of each Customer in accordance with this Part B before providing any of the Designated Services to a Customer for the first time, and in any other circumstances required by the AML/CTF Laws or this AML/CTF Program. 3.2 Customer types Afterpay’s Customers fall into only one category , which are domestic individuals.2 Afterpay will amend this Part B if it decides to provide Designated Services to other Customer types in the future. 3.3 Customers that cannot provide satisfactory evidence of identity A. Where a Customer cannot provide satisfactory evidence of identity, in accordance with this AML/CTF Program, due to exceptional circumstances, the matter will be referred to the AML/CTF Compliance Officer (or delegate) to determine whether the Customer may be able 2 Afterpay assumes that the Customer and the Beneficial Owner are one and the same, unless there is reasonable grounds to consider otherwise (Rule 4.12.2 of the AML/CTF Rules). Part B - Customer Identification and Verification | Afterpay Australia Pty Limited 7 Confidential to provide alternative documents to establish proof of identity in accordance with the exception process in Part A of Afterpay’s AML/CTF Program. B. The exceptional circumstances referred to in (a) above include, but are not limited to: i. individuals whose birth was not registered; ii. undocumented arrivals in Australia; iii. people living in remote areas; iv. people who are transgender or intersex; v. people affected by natural disasters; vi. people with limited access to identity documents (for example because they were raised in institutional or foster care); and vii. people over 18 who have not established a ‘social footprint’ in the community. C. Afterpay will not rely on alternative documents to establish proof of identity if it knows or has reason to believe that it is incorrect or misleading. 4. Verification Methods 4.1 Verification from electronic data A. Afterpay primarily uses electronic data to verify information about Customers and determines the verification procedures using electronic data to be reliable and independent, considering the following factors: i. the accuracy of the data; ii. how secure the data is; iii. how the data is kept up-to-date; iv. how comprehensive the data is; v. whether the data has been verified from a reliable and independent source; vi. whether the data is maintained by a government body or pursuant to legislation; and vii. whether the electronic data can be additionally authenticated. B. Verification of information collected in accordance with the Schedules to this Part B will, as far as possible, be based on reliable and independent electronic data. C. Afterpay has assessed: i. what reliable and independent electronic data it will use for the purpose of verification; ii. its predefined tolerance levels for matches and errors; and iii. whether, and how, to confirm information collected about a Customer by independently initiating contact with the person the Customer claims to be. Part B - Customer Identification and Verification | Afterpay Australia Pty Limited 8 Confidential D. Afterpay has determined the electronic data contained in Schedule 3 of this Part B to be reliable and independent in accordance with the provisions set out in Section 4.10.2(1) of the AML/CFT Rules.
