documents.FC-005: Afterpay Canada Limited Anti-Money Laundering Program Afterpay Canada Limited Last Approved: Dec 8, 2025 Effective Date: Dec 8, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Afterpay Canada Limited (“Afterpay Canadaˮ, “Afterpay CAˮ) Anti-Money Laundering Program (“Programˮ) outlines Afterpay Canadaʼs approach to anti-money laundering, counter-terrorism financing, and sanctions compliance. The purpose of this Program is to establish comprehensive guidelines governing Afterpay Canada's operations to identify, mitigate, and manage money laundering and terrorism financing risks, while ensuring consistent operations and maintaining regulatory compliance. Afterpay Canada Limited, a British Columbia incorporated entity, offers "buy now, pay later" installment products in Canada. As a subsidiary of Afterpay Australia Pty., Afterpay Canada maintains this Program to meet subsidiary obligations under Australian AUSTRAC requirements, partner expectations from banking and financial institutions, and to implement voluntary best practices aligned with Canadian AML/CTF frameworks. Scope: This Program applies to Afterpay Canada Limited, its employees, contractors, and agents engaged by Afterpay Canada, or acting on Afterpay Canada's behalf, for the purpose of performing services for the benefit of Afterpay Canada. Definitions: Terminology Definition CRR/MRR Afterpay Consumer Customer Risk Rating framework that evaluates consumer behavior and profile to assign ML/TF risk ratings Consumer An individual end-consumer who utilizes, or seeks to utilize, Afterpayʼs “buy now, pay laterˮ services Customer In the context of this AML Program, the term “Customerˮ comprises both Consumers and Merchants. EDD Enhanced Due Diligence procedures applied to high-risk customers Consumers and/or Merchants) Employee Includes full-time, part-time and casual employees, contractors and agents engaged by Afterpay, or acting on Afterpayʼs behalf, for the purpose of performing certain services for the benefit of Afterpay. Financial The Financial Transactions and Reports Analysis Centre of Canada is Transactions and an independent agency of the Government of Canada responsible for Reports Analysis the administration of the Proceeds of Crime Money Laundering) and Centre of Canada Terrorist Financing Act (“PCMLTFAˮ or “PC Actˮ) and its implementing (“FINTRACˮ) regulations. IDV Identity Document Verification - Afterpay Canada's identity verification program Know Your The “Know Your Customerˮ framework implements procedures and Customer (ˮKYC“) controls related to identifying a person or entity to mitigate ML/TF and Sanctions risk. Material Meaningful inconsistency in beneficial ownership information that Discrepancy could impact proper identification or conceal individuals who own or control a corporation. Merchant Individuals, partnerships, and companies with whom Afterpay has an arrangement to pay the value of purchases made by a Consumer using Afterpayʼs “buy now,pay laterˮ loan product and who offer goods or services for sale to Consumers. Money Laundering The risk that Afterpay may reasonably face that its provision of and Terrorism Designated Services may involve or facilitate money laundering or Financing terrorism financing. (“ML/TFˮ) Risk PC Act or PCMLTFA The Proceeds of Crime Money Laundering) and Terrorist Financing Act is Canada's primary anti-money laundering and counter-terrorist financing law whose aim is to detect and deter ML/TF while protecting the Canadian financial system. Risk Assessment Afterpayʼs assessment of its ML/TF and Sanctions Risk that underpins the AML/CTF Program. Suspicious A type of report submitted to FINTRAC when a financial transaction Transaction Report occurs, or is attempted, where there are reasonable grounds to (“STRˮ) suspect the transaction is related to the commission or the attempted commission of a money laundering or terrorist activity financing offence.1 Standard Operating An SOP is a set of step-by-step instructions compiled to help staff Procedure (“SOPˮ) perform routine operations. 2. Block, Inc. Requirements 2.1. Parent Company Obligations As a subsidiary of Block, Inc., Afterpay Canada operates within Block's global compliance framework while maintaining entity-specific requirements. This Program aligns with Block's Anti-Money Laundering & Counter-Terrorist Financing Policy and incorporates applicable global standards adapted for Canadian operations. 2.2. Cross-Border Regulatory Framework Afterpay Canada maintains dual compliance obligations: Australian AUSTRAC Requirements: As a subsidiary of Afterpay Australia, Afterpay Canada complies with applicable AUSTRAC obligations for foreign subsidiaries, including maintaining this AML/CTF program and reporting requirements. Canadian Regulatory Framework: While Afterpay Canada is regulated by the Bank of Canada under the Retail Payment Activities Act RPAA for providing payment services, this Program implements voluntary AML/CTF best practices aligned with Canadian frameworks and partner expectations. 1 Though Afterpay Canada is not directly regulated by FINTRAC, nor subject to the PC Act, this Program highlights the entityʼs commitment to identifying and managing ML/TF and other criminal activity on the platform as a best practice. Afterpay Canadaʼs MLRO or their delegate will submit voluntary STRs to FINTRAC where warranted. 2.3. AML/CTF Legal Framework In Canada, the anti-money laundering/counter terrorism financing ("AML/CTF") regime is set out in the Proceeds of Crime Money Laundering) and Terrorist Financing Act ("PC Act") and certain implementing regulations. While Afterpay Canada is not subject to the requirements of, or a reporting entity regulated under the PC Act, Afterpay Canada is nonetheless committed to identifying, mitigating and managing indicators of ML/TF, sanctions evasion or other criminal activity as a best practice. In this regard, Afterpay Canada undertakes certain KYC, risk assessment and related procedures in order to ensure that Afterpay Canada does not establish a relationship with or transact with individuals or entities that could be linked to money laundering, terrorist activities, sanctions evasion or that can use Afterpay Canada to facilitate such activities. As a subsidiary of an Australian entity, Afterpay Canada may also develop an AML/CTF policy relevant to any obligations under Australian law. As a British Columbia incorporated entity, Afterpay Canada is subject to compliance requirements under the Canadian sanctions laws. To this end, Afterpay Canada maintains this Program which is designed to ensure that it complies with and does not directly or indirectly assist others to contravene such laws. 3. Afterpay Canada-Specific Requirements 3.1. Governance and Oversight 3.1.1 Board Approval This Program is to be approved and adopted by the Afterpay Canada Board. This Program should be endorsed for approval by the Canada Anti-Money Laundering Compliance Officer prior to being presented to the Board. 3.1.2 Compliance Officer Afterpay Canada will appoint an Anti-Money Laundering Compliance Officer, who is referred to as the Money Laundering Reporting Officer (“MLROˮ), whose appointment must be approved by Senior Management. The MLRO will be appropriately knowledgeable in AML-related matters, and will understand Afterpay Canadaʼs products, services, Customers and geographies services and the potential risks associated with such products, services, Customers and geographies. The MLRO is responsible for implementing this Program, overseeing Afterpay Canadaʼs AML Program, and ensuring regulatory compliance. As necessary, the MLRO may also serve as a liaison between Afterpay Canada and law enforcement officials, government regulators, internal or external auditors, service providers, and others regarding issues related to Afterpay Canadaʼs AML Program, and reviewing and updating Afterpay Canadaʼs AML Program consistent with any new statutes, regulations, or regulatory and industry guidance. The MLRO will provide periodic reports to Senior Management, as needed, at least on a biannual basis. Such reports may include information about any instances of non-compliance with this Program, any feedback received from external stakeholders including any regulatory bodies, the results of any review conducted, and any legal or regulatory developments bearing on the AML Program. The MLRO may delegate duties to other responsible Employees as appropriate. 3.1.3 Independent Review This AML Program may be independently reviewed every two 2 years. The Independent Review will be performed by suitably qualified internal or external personnel. The purpose of this review is to assess the effectiveness of the AML Program design, whether it has been effectively implemented and whether Afterpay Canada is complying with it. The Independent Review will focus on either all or parts of the AML Program, using a risk based approach. The results of the Independent Review will be provided to the Afterpay Canada Board. 3.2. Risk Assessment Afterpay Canada conducts regular ML/TF and sanctions risk assessments considering: ● Products & Services risk ● Customer risk ● Geographic risk ● Distribution channel risk ● Operational and Employee risk ● Emerging risk ● Sanctions risk This AML Program is informed by the outcome of the Afterpay risk assessment. The latest risk assessment was conducted at a local (regional), brand and enterprise-wise in conjunction with Block, Inc. levels covering the timeframe from January to December 2024. The Afterpay Canada Risk Assessment will be reviewed annually or where one of the risk types changes. Overview of the AML/CTF Program 1.1 About Afterpay CA Afterpay Canada Limited BN - BC1246445 (Afterpay CA, we, or us) is a reporting entity enrolled on the Reporting Entity Roll managed by AUSTRAC for the purposes of AML/CTF Laws. Afterpay CA is a subsidiary of Afterpay Pty Ltd, an indirect wholly owned subsidiary of Block, Inc. It is a non-bank institution that offers short-term loans (also referred to as ‘Low Cost Credit Contractsʼ or ‘Buy Now Pay Later Contractsʼ under the National Credit Code), but does not hold a banking licence. Afterpay CA offers a ‘Buy Now, Pay Laterʼ (BNPL) product service that allows a Customer to make an interest free purchases using a short-term, low value loans at a participating merchant and to repay the loan to Afterpay CA for them over four fortnightly instalments. Although Afterpay CA is registered, and operates in Canada (CA) and is subject to CA laws, the AML/CTF Law extend to the activities of a subsidiary of an Australian resident company insofar as that subsidiary provides a Designated Service at or through a permanent establishment of that subsidiary in a country other than Australia. Afterpay CA is a subsidiary of Afterpay Pty Ltd ACN 618 280 649, an Australian resident company, and it provides certain Designated Services at or through its permanent establishment in CA. Customers ordinarily fund the repayment of the instalments to Afterpay CA from debit or credit cards. Afterpay CA directly pays the merchant for the purchase price of the goods (less a fee), and assumes the credit risk. Afterpay CA settles these funds to the merchantʼs nominated bank account by providing instructions to its local bank partner to make those payments. Afterpay CAʼs BNPL product is available exclusively online (not in-store) at participating merchants and can only be accessed through an Afterpay account. 1.2 Afterpay CA AML/CTF Program The Afterpay CA AML/CTF Program comprises: ● the ML/TF Risk Assessment of Afterpay CA,1 as summarised in Schedule 1; and ● these AML/CTF Policies. These AML/CTF Policies have been designed to: o appropriately manage and mitigate the risks of ML/TF/PF that Afterpay CA may reasonably face in providing its Designated Services. Each Designated Service that Afterpay CA provides (or may provide) to Customers are set out in Schedule 2;2 o ensure that Afterpay CA complies with the obligations imposed by the AML/CTF Laws;3 and 1 AML/CTF Act, ss 26C – 26E. 2 AML/CTF Act, s 26F(1)(a). 3 AML/CTF Act, s 26F(1)(b). 1 o be appropriate to the nature, size and complexity of the Afterpay CA business.4 Afterpay CA must comply with these AML/CTF Policies.5 1.3 Application of these AML/CTF Policies The Afterpay CA AML/CTF Program, including these AML/CTF Policies, was formally approved and adopted by the Governing Body on 26 March 2026 and Senior Manager of Afterpay CA on 30 March 2026. The full amendment and approval history of these AML/CTF Policies can be found at the beginning of this document. Afterpay CA keeps a record of these approvals. The Designated Services provided by Afterpay CA are listed in Schedule 2. 1.4 Enrolment with AUSTRAC Afterpay CA is enrolled with AUSTRAC as a Reporting Entity.6 The AML/CTF Compliance Officer will ensure that the AUSTRAC CEO is notified of any changes to enrolment details within 14 days of the change occurring.7 As a Reporting Entity enrolled with AUSTRAC, we will advise the AUSTRAC CEO of any changes to the enrolment details which were provided to AUSTRAC when we are enrolled as a Reporting Entity.8 This includes information about our Designated Services,9 and information relating to us.10 It also includes updating our annual earnings where earnings of $100 million or more occur. The AML/CTF Compliance Officer will ensure that the AUSTRAC CEO is notified of any changes to the above enrolment details within 14 days of the change occurring.11 2. Updates to the AML/CTF Program 2.1 Updates to the ML/TF Risk Assessment The ML/TF Risk Assessment will be kept under review and updated from time to time as described in Section C of these AML/CTF Policies. Updates to the ML/TF Risk Assessment: ● may be made with the approval of a Senior Manager with authority to do so. Senior Managers with such authority are specified in Schedule 3;12 ● must be notified, in writing, to the Governing Body of Afterpay CA as soon as practicable after the update is made; and13 ● will result in a review and update of these AML/CTF Policies in accordance with 2.2.14 2.2 Updates to AML/CTF Policies These AML/CTF Policies will be kept under review and updated from time to time including: ● as a result of any need identified as part of the AML/CTF Compliance Officerʼs review as described in section 2.3; 4 AML/CTF Act, s 26F(1)(c). Senior Managers with such authority are specified in Schedule 3.15 2.3 AML/CTF Compliance Officer review The AML/CTF Compliance Officer will ensure that a review of the AML/CTF Policies is conducted at least annually.16 This review will cover: ● our compliance with these AML/CTF Policies; ● the extent to which our AML/CTF Policies are appropriately managing and mitigating the ML/TF/PF risk that we may reasonably face in providing our designated services; and ● our compliance with the AML/CTF Laws.17 Following this review, the AML/CTF Compliance Officer must report to the Governing Body on the findings of this review in accordance with section 5.3. 2.4 Documenting updates to the AML/CTF Program If we update the AML/CTF Program (including these AML/CTF Policies or the ML/TF Risk Assessment), we will document the AML/CTF Program (as updated) within 14 days after the update occurs.18 2.5 Role of supporting systems and processes These AML/CTF Policies will be supported by internal procedures, systems and controls to ensure that Afterpay CA implements its obligations consistently with these AML/CTF Policies. Provided that the supporting internal procedures, systems and controls operate consistently with these AML/CTF Policies, changes to those processes, systems and controls may not require the approval of a Senior Manager. Afterpay NZ is exempted from the need to comply with a range of obligations under the AML/CTF Laws that would otherwise apply if the Designated Services were being provided at or through a permanent establishment in Australia; and B. this Australian AML/CTF Program is required to consider only minimal additional systems and controls to those already applicable to Afterpay NZ within NZ, as reflected in the remainder of this document. Australian AML/CTF Program | Afterpay NZ Limited 7 Confidential 3. Key Concepts 3.1 What is money laundering Money Laundering is a serious crime. Money Laundering involves hiding, disguising or legitimising the true origin and ownership of money used in or derived from committing crimes. It is an extremely diverse activity that is carried out at various levels of sophistication and plays an important role in organised crime. The AML/CTF Act defines “money laundering” as conduct that amounts to: (a) an offence against Division 400 of the Criminal Code; or (b) an offence against a law of a State or Territory that corresponds to an offence referred to in paragraph (a); or (c) an offence against a law of a foreign country or of a part of a foreign country that corresponds to an offence referred to in paragraph (a). Introduction This Global Sanctions Compliance Program Standard (“Standard”) outlines Block’s approach to complying with applicable sanctions laws and regulations in the jurisdictions in which it operates. This Standard is intended to supplement Block’s Global Sanctions Compliance Policy (“Policy”). The Global Head of Sanctions is responsible for management of this Standard. 2. This Standard is also designed to satisfy and operationalize the Policy, which establishes Block’s requirements for complying with all applicable sanctions laws and regulations. This Standard is intended to be holistic and sustainable, providing a reasonable level of assurance that Block is effectively managing economic sanctions risks and related exposure. 3. Scope This Standard is established by Block and is applicable globally across all Block business lines, subsidiaries, and jurisdictions. 4. Standard Requirements 4.1 Management Commitment Senior management is committed to robust oversight of Block’s global sanctions compliance program and Block’s compliance with all applicable sanctions laws and regulations of the Global Sanctions Compliance Program Standard | Block 8 Confidential jurisdictions in which Block operates. documents.FC-010: Risk and Compliance Adverse Actions Policy Cash App Last Approved: Feb 4, 2026 Effective Date: Feb 4, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Risk and Compliance Adverse Actions Policy ("Policy") outlines Cash App's approach to actioning accounts and customers that violate Cash App's Terms of Service (herein referred to as "platform violations"), internal policies, or engage in suspicious or illegal activity (together, referred to as "prohibited activity"). This framework is designed to ensure Cash App has reasonable measures and appropriate methods to manage accounts and customers who present higher risk to Cash App, and to ultimately mitigate and manage the risks associated with potential money laundering, terrorist financing, fraud and other financial crimes in line with its BSA/AML and CTF obligations. The BSA/AML Program Office Lead is responsible for management of this Policy. Cash App conducts business in compliance with all applicable laws and regulations. The purpose of this Policy is to establish a holistic set of standards and requirements for which Cash App operates as it pertains to offboarding and/or putting certain limitations on customers and accounts. Scope: The scope of this Policy includes Risk and Compliance teams and processes that take action to accounts and customers on Cash App. Definitions: Terminology Definition Adverse Action Controls in place to limit customer access to Cash App as a result of prohibited activity. Account The structure that is used by Cash App customers to log in, manage their personal information, and send or receive money or engage in other transactions. Terminology Definition Customer A person that opens an account and receives Cash App products and services, or a person opening a new account for someone who lacks the legal capacity. Denylisting Accounts The process by which Cash App accounts are prevented from continued use, with the exception of liquidating assets and withdrawing funds after a determination or suspicion of involvement in prohibited, fraudulent or illegal activity on Cash App. Denylist Propagation The act of denylisting more than one account or identity-based asset with the intention of offboarding all accounts and assets linked to the account of a perpetrator of bad activity. Employee Identification A list of EINs updated and maintained by Compliance. Once an EIN Number EIN Denylist is added to the EIN denylist, that prevents a business using that same EIN from successfully passing Know Your Business KYB in the future. Identity Based Assets An alias or attribute that a customer links to an account that may be associated with an identity (i.e., social security number, phone number, email address, financial instrument, device). Financial Instrument Linked external debit, prepaid, credit card and bank account Identity Verification The process of verifying a customer's identity, which may be ("IDV") triggered by a number of actions including but not limited to ordering a Cash Card, exceeding peer-to-peer sending and receiving limits, buying bitcoin or enabling bitcoin withdrawals and deposits, signing up for a Cash App Investing account, and sending from a stored balance Cash App Card Refers to the deactivation of a customer's Cash App Card and all Termination associated services, including the direct deposit feature. This ensures that the card cannot be reactivated or used for any future transactions. Risk Suspension A degraded customer experience state where certain features of Cash App are unavailable to the account until the customer or Cash App takes a particular action. Applied when certain risky customer behaviours are detected. Terminology Definition Suspicious Activity Report A document that financial institutions file with the Financial Crimes ("SAR") Enforcement Network FinCEN to report suspected violations of law or suspicious activity Sponsor Account A parent or guardian of a teen on Cash App. All Sponsors have successfully passed IDV. Sponsored Account Accounts operated by individuals between the age of 1317 years old that require authorization and sponsorship from their parent or guardian Sponsorship Strike A negative indicator based on the 1 and 2 strike policy applicable to a Sponsor Account's SSN if one of the accounts they've sponsored has been actioned (e.g. denylisted) Sponsorship Warning A notification sent to a Sponsor Account when a Sponsored Account has been denylisted to notify the Sponsor of the Adverse Action taken 2. Policy Requirements 2.1 Adverse Actions Cash App is responsible for establishing the appropriate processes, criteria and corresponding action on accounts and customers upon the detection or suspicion of prohibited activity on Cash App. In general, these processes and criteria are designed to be commensurate with the customer and account's activity, and take into account factors such as length of the customer relationship, number of counterparties involved, and the overall risk associated with the customer, to the extent that such decisions do not conflict with applicable law. Cash App offboards accounts and relationships with customers who it identifies to be engaging in prohibited activity. Cash App also maintains processes and controls to prevent the re-onboarding of customers previously denylisted. 2.2 Monitoring & Decision Making Prohibited relationships and activity are determined and driven by the Cash App Terms of Service, internal policy, risk assessments, partnership requirements, and applicable regulations. The Risk and Compliance teams have established monitoring to ensure prohibited customer accounts and relationships do not persist on Cash App. Additionally, Risk and Compliance teams maintain relevant procedures to ensure consistent application of the framework described in this Policy. When determining whether a customer account or relationship should be exited, a comprehensive and holistic review of the account and relationship specific behavior is conducted. Reputational risk exposure and overall business impact may also be considered when determining if an account or relationship should be exited. 2.2.1 Assessing Customer Risk Customer denylist decisions are to be determined based on the holistic risk exposure posed to Cash App and reputational risk exposure. Input from the Brand and Reputational Risk Committee may be sought prior to a final decision to retain a high risk relationship. Additionally, the overall impact of the customer's relationship to the business will be assessed ahead of a determination to exit a relationship. Enhanced due diligence may be leveraged to mitigate risk exposure; however, if the risk exposure of the relationship exceeds Cash App's risk tolerance, the relationship will be exited. Any customer relationship that is subject to Adverse Action will involve a review that considers some or all of the aforementioned factors. 2.3 Account Denylisting Account denylisting is executed when an account is found to be engaging in activity that violates Cash Appʼs Terms of Service (including, without limitation engaging in fraudulent activity) or internal policies (collectively referred to as “bad activityˮ herein). The action may happen either manually or automatically, and revokes all account functionality, with the exception of: ● Logging in ● Contacting customer support ● Accessing account statements ● Selling or transferring stocks ● Selling bitcoin ● Paying back loans (check only) ● Linking a financial instrument ● Receiving pending direct deposits and check deposits ● Withdrawing available fiat funds to a linked financial instrument Once an account is denylisted, if applicable, the customerʼs corresponding account with Marqeta is also closed, which deactivates their Cash App Card and prevents any future direct deposits. Account level denylisting is executed by Compliance and Risk, while the denylisting service and underlying technology is managed by Trust (“Safeguardsˮ). The technological service is “guardrailsˮ. 2.3.1 Manual Account Denylisting In general, account level denylisting happens manually, when Compliance or Risk Specialists review an account that has alerted (e.g., due to triggering a Risk or Compliance machine learning model or heuristic) and the Specialist suspects bad activity1. In the context of this Policy, the definition of “suspectsˮ is - once the Specialist has conducted a thorough review of the account and activity, and has determined based on the underlying procedures that the activity warrants a denylist. Specialists may also escalate the account to the Financial Crimes program for further investigation and actioning (as determined by applicable procedures). 2.3.2 Automated Account Denylisting Account denylisting may also happen automatically. When applied automatically, Cash App has a high degree of confidence in the existence of bad activity on the account. Typically, account denylisting happens automatically either when Cash App has identified an account (or group of accounts) through a high precision model or heuristic rule that is designed to detect certain types of bad activity, as part of a remediation effort (e.g., denylisting a population of networked fraud with various bad actors), or during an automated account denylist propagation. Automated account denylist propagation occurs when Cash App has a high degree of confidence that accounts connected to a confirmed bad actor are operated by the same individual due to their shared account attributes, such as social security number SSN, device, and more (see more in the ‘Customer Denylistʼ section below for more details). 2.3.4 Account Denylist Reasons Account denylisting may happen for a variety of reasons across Risk and Compliance. In general, a denylist may be categorized into one (or more) of the following categories: ● Financial Crime & Abuse (e.g., Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing, and Securities Law Violations) ● Fraud & Identity Abuse (e.g., Identity Theft, Acc
documents.FC-011: Cash App Financial Crime Program Squareup Europe Limited SEL Last Approved: September 29, 2025 Effective Date: September 29, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: Squareup Europe Limited (“SELˮ) is an authorised Electronic Money Institution, regulated and supervised by the Financial Conduct Authority (“FCAˮ) in the United Kingdom UK, under Firm Reference Number 900846. The direct parent company is Squareup International Limited, registered in the Republic of Ireland; both companies are wholly owned subsidiaries of Block, Inc. SELʼs two key product offerings are (i) Square Managed Payments and (ii) Cash App (in Winddown since October 2024. Further information on the products and services offered by SEL in the UK are located in Appendix 1. This Program document relates to the Cash App business. Block, Inc. Block, Inc., a Delaware corporation, is a merchant services aggregator and mobile payments company based in San Francisco, California in the United States of America. It is registered as a Money Services Business with the Financial Crimes Enforcement Network (“FinCENˮ). SELʼs primary Cash App product offering is a peer-to-peer money transfer service, allowing individuals, through a mobile software application, to transfer funds via stored balance or from a debit card issued by a UK-based financial institution to another individualʼs Cash App account. A feature of Cash App is the ability to make cross-currency transactions (send and receive funds from other jurisdictions where Cash App is available). This feature enables individual Cash App users to transfer funds between Cash App accounts e.g., a US individual Cash App customer can transfer US dollars from their stored value balance or linked debit card to a UK individual customerʼs Cash App account where it will appear in her GBP stored value balance, and vice versa. Scope: This Financial Crime Program document applies to Cash App products available in the UK. Definitions: Terminology Definition Money Laundering Money Laundering is the process by which individuals or organisations conceal the origins of illegally obtained funds, making them appear legitimate. In the context of SEL, Money Laundering typically involves disguising the proceeds of crime through legitimate payment channels, including merchant transactions and card processing services. Criminals may attempt to exploit financial institutions to move, layer or integrate illicit funds into the financial system. As a regulated entity under the UK Money Laundering Regulations, SEL has a legal and ethical responsibility to detect and prevent such activities by implementing robust Anti-Money Laundering (“AMLˮ) controls, including customer due diligence (“CDDˮ), transaction monitoring and the reporting of suspicious activity. Terrorist Financing Terrorist Financing involves the collection or provision of funds with the intention or knowledge that they will be used to support terrorist acts, individuals or organisations. Unlike Money Laundering, the origin of the funds used for terrorist financing may be legitimate or illicit, making detection more complex. For SEL, there is a heightened risk that payment processing services could be misused to raise, move or store funds that support terrorism, often through seemingly legitimate merchants or transactions. Under the Terrorism Act 2000 (“TACTˮ) and related UK regulations, SEL must implement effective controls to identify and report suspicious activity, conduct enhanced due diligence (“EDDˮ) where necessary and ensure systems are in place to prevent its services from being exploited for terrorist financing purposes. Proliferation Financing Proliferation Financing refers to the act of providing funds or financial services that are used to support the development, acquisition or transfer of weapons of mass destruction (“WMDsˮ), including nuclear, chemical or biological weapons. This type of financing is often linked to sanctioned individuals, entities or countries and may involve complex networks designed to evade international controls. For SEL, the risk lies in unintentionally facilitating transactions that contribute to proliferation activities through merchant relationships or payment processing. Under UK regulations and international obligations such as UN and UK sanctions regimes, SEL is required to implement robust screening, due diligence and monitoring systems to detect and prevent any involvement in Proliferation Financing, ensuring compliance with all applicable sanctions and export control laws. Financial Crime A written document that sets out the primary components the Program company uses to identify, mitigate, and manage potential risks of its products or services being misused to facilitate ML, TF or PF. The components of the program and documentation must be developed and implemented and proactively maintained. Beneficial Owner Refers to the natural person(s) who ultimately owns or controls a customer or the natural person on whose behalf a transaction is being conducted. It also includes those persons who exercise ultimate effective control over a legal person or arrangement. Customers An individual or company that uses products and services offered by SEL. Host Country A country where a company that is based in another country has business activities. Merchant A business that offers products and services related to SELʼs primary product - Square Managed Payments. Suspicious Activity A document that a financial institution must file with the relevant Report SAR Financial Intelligence Unit following a suspected incident of ML, TF, PF or fraud. Staff/Employee All full-time employees, fixed-term employees, outsourced employees and contingent workers including interns, temps, consultants, vendors, third-party providers, and contractors. 2. Legal & Regulatory Framework The UK Money Laundering Regulations, applying to financial institutions, date from 1993, the current Regulations being the Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017 (as amended) Money Laundering Regulations 2017. The offence of Money Laundering was contained in various acts of parliament (such as the Criminal Justice Act 1988 and the Drug Trafficking Offences Act 1986. The Proceeds of Crime Act 2002 (“POCAˮ) consolidated, updated and reformed the law relating to money laundering to include any dealing in criminal property. Specific obligations to combat terrorist financing were set out in the Terrorism Act 2000. SEL takes into account legislation such as the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002, the Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017 (as amended) , the Counter Terrorism Act 2008, the Electronic Money Regulations 2011 and the Payment Services Regulations 2017. As an e-money institution, SEL must also comply with the relevant parts of the FCA handbook for firms and take into account the FCAʼs Approach Document. SELʼs business is subject to a wide range of laws and regulations within the UK and the US, including Anti-Money Laundering and Counter Terrorist Financing and and Counter Proliferation Financing AML/CTF/CPF legislation as well as US Treasury Departmentʼs Office of Foreign Assets Control (“OFACˮ) and US sanctions requirements. SEL also follows the international framework proposed by the Financial Conduct Authority (“FCAˮ), Financial Action Task Force (“FATFˮ) and guidance produced by Joint Money Laundering Steering Group (“JMLSGˮ) in the UK. 3. Financial Crime Program Summary This document outlines SELʼs comprehensive Financial Crime Program, which is designed to identify, assess and mitigate risks related to Financial Crime. It encompasses the key components of SELʼs framework to prevent and detect Money Laundering (“MLˮ), Terrorist Financing (“TFˮ) and Proliferation Financing (“PFˮ). SEL is committed to developing, implementing, and maintaining an effective, risk based Financial Crime Program, which is reasonably designed to prevent Cash App from being used to facilitate ML, TF or PF activities. This Financial Crime Program document applies to all the Cash App products available in the UK. The objectives of this Financial Crime program for the UK are: ● To develop and implement a risk-based Financial Crime program for all Cash App activities; ● To develop risk-based AML/CTF/CPF control procedures for all Cash App activities; ● To promote awareness of applicable AML/CTF/CPF laws and regulations; ● To detect, prevent, monitor, and report any suspicious activity and TFtransactions; and ● Fully cooperate with all law enforcement, regulatory agencies, and financial intelligence units to the extent required and permitted under applicable law. It is vital that all SEL personnel1 are aware of and committed to achieving compliance with all applicable requirements of the Financial Crime program, laws, and regulations. All SEL employees and support staff, including employees of other Block entities who support Cash App in the UK, are expected to follow the protocols and processes outlined in this Program document, as well as any related procedures, to ensure compliance with all related legal and regulatory requirements. 4. Governance and Oversight Board of Directors and Senior Management Senior Management of SEL, including the Board of Directors (the Board), have responsibility for managing identified ML, TF and PF risks by demonstrating active engagement in SELʼs approach to effectively mitigating such risks. The Board meets quarterly and follows a terms of reference which details information including the Boardʼs role, its standing agenda, and its decision making process. This Financial Crime Program and any changes to it must be approved, in the first instance, by the Money Laundering Reporting Officer (“MLROˮ) who has been appointed by the Board of SEL to coordinate and manage SELʼs Financial Crime Program and secondly, by the Board itself. documents.FC-012: Know Your Customer Policy Cash App US Last Approved: Feb 19, 2026 Effective Date: Feb 19, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: The KYC Policy (“Policyˮ) is established by Cash App, U.S., a Line of Business under Block, Inc., and documents how Cash App knows the identity of its customers, understands the nature and purpose of customer relationships to develop a customer risk profile, reasonably predicts the types of transactions that a customer is likely to engage in, and determines when transactions are potentially suspicious. Cash App is required to adopt and implement a comprehensive KYC program to identify customers who may present a higher risk for money laundering, terrorist financing, reputational concern, and other types of illegal activity. This Policy is established by Cash App Compliance. References to “staffˮ throughout this document shall refer to all full-time employees and fixed-term and contingent workers including interns, temps, consultants, vendors, third-party providers, and contractors. Cash App conducts business in compliance with all applicable laws and regulations. The purpose of this Policy is to document Cash Appʼs requirements and framework for satisfying relevant KYC compliance obligations, and is intended to be supplemented by additional Policies, Standards, and Procedures where necessary. Further, it provides policy requirements around identity verification, risk-based due diligence and cadence, and periodic KYC reviews. Know Your Customer KYC compliance is integral in the prevention of identity theft, money laundering, terrorist financing, and other types of illegal activity, and begins with understanding a customerʼs identity and assessing the risks associated with the customer. Cash App employs a risk-based approach in order to form a reasonable belief that it knows the true identity of each customer. Scope: The Bank Secrecy Act, as amended and supplemented by the USA Patriot Act and other laws (collectively, the “BSAˮ), obligates covered financial institutions to implement anti-money laundering programs reasonably designed to prevent the institutions from being used to facilitate money laundering and the financing of terrorist activities. An essential component of a compliant AML program are policies and procedures designed to ensure the financial institution knows its customer. Therefore, the KYC Policy includes Cash Appʼs requirements for knowing and verifying the identity of customers, understanding the nature and purpose of customer relationships for the purpose of developing a customer risk profile, and conducting ongoing monitoring to identify and report suspicious transactions and, maintaining and updating customer information. Because Cash App engages in the transmission of funds, it is covered by the BSAʼs definition of “financial institutionˮ and subject to BSA requirements, including KYC requirements. Definitions: Terminology Definition Account The structure that is used by Cash App customers to log in, manage their personal information, and send or receive money or engage in other transactions. Bank Secrecy Act Passed in 1970 and amended several times, a U.S. law requiring financial institutions to assist U.S. government agencies in detecting and preventing money laundering. Customer A natural or juridical person that opens an account and receives Cash App products and services, or a person opening a new account for someone who lacks the legal capacity. A customer must receive services to be subject to the KYC Policy. Customer Due Diligence The information and documentation maintained by Cash App on its CDD or Enhanced Due accounts, customers, or relationships. Diligence EDD Profile Financial Institution Each agent, agency, branch, or office within the U.S. of any person doing business in one or more capacity as a bank, a broker or dealer in securities, a money service business, a casino, a commodities broker or dealer, among other things. Know Your Customer A legal requirement designed to prevent and monitor for identity KYC theft, money laundering, terrorist financing, and other types of illegal activity by identifying customers and understanding the purpose of customer accounts. Money Service Business Any person doing business, whether or not on a regular basis or as an organized business concern, in one or more of the following capacities: dealer in foreign exchange, check casher, issuer or seller of traveler's checks or money orders, provider or seller of prepaid access, a money transmitter, and the U.S. Postal Service. Note that exemptions may apply. USA Patriot Act Passed in 2001, this amendment to the Bank Secrecy Act was enacted to deter and punish terrorist acts in the United States and around the world, to enhance law enforcement investigatory tools, and other purposes which include measures to prevent, detect, and prosecute international money laundering and financing of terrorism; to require all appropriate elements of the financial services industry to report potential money laundering; and to strengthen measures to prevent use of the U.S. financial system for personal gain by corrupt Terminology Definition foreign officials and facilitate repatriation of stolen assets to the citizens of countries to whom such assets belong. Staff All full-time employees and fixed-term and contingent workers including interns, temps, consultants, vendors, third-party providers, and contractors 2. Policy/Program Requirements 2.1 Customer Identification Program CIP A CIP is a foundational component of AML programs and requirements, as it allows financial institutions to form a reasonable belief that the institution knows the identity of its customers. This requirement applies specifically to customers, which includes, 1 an individual who opens a new account, and 2 an individual who opens a new account for an individual who lacks legal capacity, such as a minor or an entity that is not a legal person. Additional exceptions may apply, for example, when a customer maintains a Restricted Account. 2.1.1 Customer Identity Verification Cash App's CIP must include written, risk-based procedures for verifying the identity of each customer to the extent reasonable, practicable, and where required by this Policy. Verification may be conducted through documentary or through non-documentary methods. A combination of documentary and non-documentary verification may be required for higher-risk relationships or to access certain products and features. Customers who want access to certain products and features, to send money from their stored balance, or who want to access higher payment limits are required to pass identity verification IDV. Where identity verification is required, Cash App will obtain the following: Personal Account ● Legal name; ● Date of birth; ● Social Security Number; ● Physical address; and, ● In some cases, unexpired government ID (required for document identity verification) and selfie. Business Account ● Business name; ● Business structure; ● Business category; ● Business address; ● Employer Identification Number EIN, if used as their taxpayer identification number ITIN If identity verification is required, and the customer either has not provided the necessary information to affirm their identity, or Cash App is not able to affirm the customer's identity with the information provided, Cash App will continue to restrict the customer's access to certain products, features, and higher limits, as well as the ability to send from stored balance. Cash App may also end its relationship with the customer based on information provided during IDV. 2.1.2 Lack of Verification Cash App must establish procedures for circumstances in which it cannot form a reasonable belief that it knows the true identity of a customer. At a minimum, these procedures must describe: ● Circumstances in which Cash App should not open an account or should exit the relationship with the customer; ● The terms under which a customer may use an account while Cash App attempts to verify the customer's identity; ● When Cash App should close an account after attempts to verify a customer's identity have failed; and, ● When Cash App should file a suspicious activity report SAR or similar applicable reports with a bank partner. 2.1.3 Adequate Customer Notice Cash App is required to provide customers with adequate notice that Cash App is requesting information to verify their identity. The notice must generally describe Cash App's identification requirements and be provided in a manner that is reasonably designed to allow a customer to view the notice or otherwise receive the notice before the account is opened. 2.2 Customer Risk Rating Cash App recognizes that customers who pose higher risks for money laundering or terrorist financing present increased exposure. Accordingly, Cash App has developed machine learning-based models, the Activation Customer Risk Rating (aCRR) and the Continuous Customer Risk Rating (cCRR), to risk score accounts. New accounts must receive a risk score at onboarding, as well as continuously throughout their lifecycle on the platform. Based on the account risk score, accounts are assigned a risk rating of high, medium, or low. Additionally, there are criteria that will override the machine learning model score to "high" which will result in the customer receiving EDD on an ongoing basis. The additional criteria include association with high risk adverse media, high risk PEP, or certain high risk Merchant Category Code MCC exposure. The purpose of customer risk rating is to understand account populations with elevated risk exposure, and to identify accounts and customers who pose a higher risk to Cash App and therefore may require enhanced due diligence or enhanced monitoring. Accounts and customers should be monitored in a manner that is commensurate with the level of risk the account poses to Cash App. If the risk profile of the relationship changes,
documents.FC-013: UK Consumer Due Diligence Standard Clearpay Finance Limited Last Approved: 27 February 2026 Effective Date: 27 February 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This UK Consumer Due Diligence (“Standardˮ) outlines Clearpay Finance Ltd.ʼs (“Clearpayˮ, “the Companyˮ) approach to procedural requirements for customer due diligence of customers who are consumers and apply for Clearpay services in compliance with anti-money laundering regulations (including the Money Laundering Regulations 2017 and international sanctions regimes. The Money Laundering Reporting Officer (“MLROˮ) is responsible for management of this Standard. Clearpay conducts business in compliance with all applicable laws and regulations. The purpose of this Standard is to further Clearpayʼs Consumer Due Diligence process by ensuring Clearpay carries out proper due diligence for customers who are consumers and apply for Clearpay services in compliance with anti-money laundering regulations (including the Money Laundering Regulations 2017 and international sanctions regimes. Scope: This Standard is established by Clearpay UK, is applicable to Personnel of Clearpay Finance Ltd and sets out the approach to Consumer Due Diligence. Definitions: Terminology Definition Consumer The Customer who is a natural person and who utilises or seeks to utilise Clearpayʼs BNPL Service. Customer A customer, as defined in the The Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017, who utilises Clearpayʼs BNPL service. Personnel All individuals whose work is relevant to Clearpay's compliance with AML/CTF/CPF requirements or is otherwise capable of contributing to the identification or mitigation of money laundering, terrorist financing, or proliferation financing risks, or the prevention or detection of such activities. 1 This includes employees, contractors, agency workers, and individuals working for Clearpay, Block, third-party service providers or agents who perform functions on behalf of Clearpay. 2. Standard Requirements 2.1. Customer Identification and Verification 2.1.1 Timing Clearpay performs the customer identification and verification prior to providing any service to consumers for the first time, and at other times as specified in this Standard, including: ● in response to an increase in money laundering ML risk; and ● as part of an AML investigation. 2.1.2 Customer Types Clearpay only provides its services to UK-resident natural persons. Clearpayʼs consumer customers include politically exposed persons (“PEPsˮ). 2.1.3 Identification and Verification Schedule 1 outlines the information and documentation that Clearpay collects from customers to identify them, and the methods it uses to verify them. Schedule 2 describes in detail the tiered procedure followed for the escalation of identity verification methods. 2.1.4 Customers that cannot provide satisfactory evidence Where a customer cannot provide satisfactory evidence of identity in accordance with this Standard due to exceptional circumstances, the case may be referred to the MLRO to determine whether the customer may be able to provide alternative documents to establish proof of identity. No alternative documents may be accepted as evidence of identity without approval from the MLRO. 2.1.5 Verification from reliable and independent electronic data Clearpay considers the verification procedures and underlying data sources used for eIDV of customers to be sufficiently extensive, reliable, accurate, independent of the customer, and capable of providing an appropriate level of assurance that the person claiming a particular identity is in fact that person. This assessment is on the basis that: 2 ● LexisNexis is a reputable third party provider of IDV services such as IDU ● Clearpay has configured IDU to use a wide range of information sources independent of the customer including, and telephone directory data. ● For customers posing particularly low ML/TF risk, Clearpay has configured IDU to always require matches to at least one stronger source (electoral roll or credit bureau data). ● For all other customers, Clearpay has configured IDU to always require matches to at least two sources that include at least one stronger source. ● Clearpay has configured IDU to use negative sources to reject customer applications for identities flagged as deceased. 2.1.6 Verification of documentation Where Clearpay is unable to verify information using reliable and independent electronic data, Clearpay verifies information collected in accordance with Schedule 2 by using reliable and independent documentation. When using reliable and independent documentation to verify KYC Information for individuals, Clearpay satisfies itself that the document has not expired. 2.1.7 Support by external service providers Clearpay has appointed third party ID verification service providers to assist with the provision of identity verification. They are as follows (see Schedule 2 for details): ● Ekata for risk assessment of customer for differential application of Tier 1 eIDV ● LexisNexis IDU for Tier 1 Soft Electronic ID Verification ● Onfido for the verification of ID documents for Tier 2 Hard Electronic ID Verification ● Lexis Nexis Bridger for PEP and sanctions screening of customers. 2.2. Politically Exposed Persons and Sanctions Screening 2.2.1 Politically Exposed Persons Clearpay implements risk-based procedures to determine whether an individual customer is a PEP, or a relative or close associate of a PEP (referred to as a PEP in the rest of this document), before the provision of the service to the customer or as soon as practicable after the services have been provided, as required by law. Consumers are re-screened once per quarter to identify any new PEP as detailed in the Lexis Nexis Bridger Procedure. Clearpay uses a third-party vendor to screen the full name collected from an individual customer against PEP lists to determine whether they are a PEP and potential matches are reviewed manually by Clearpayʼs KYC/KYB Operations Team. 3 All confirmed PEPs are reviewed and approved by the MLRO or their delegate. The money laundering risk of each PEP customer is assessed on a case-by-case basis (taking into account factors such as their status as a domestic vs foreign PEP, and either: A. Approved without additional AML controls; B. Approved on the basis of additional AML controls; or C. Rejected as outside the risk appetite of Clearpay. Consumer Domestic PEP true matches are accepted as within Clearpayʼs risk appetite provided they do not present particularly high money laundering risk (e.g., they are implicated through adverse media in bribery and corruption). Foreign PEP true matches are rejected as outside Clearpayʼs risk appetite, notwithstanding that they may be a UK-resident. 2.2.2 Source of Wealth and Source of Funds UK Money Laundering Regulations require Clearpay to undertake EDD on all PEPs which must include taking adequate measures to establish the Source of funds (“SOFˮ) and Source of Wealth (“SOWˮ) which are involved in the proposed business relationship or transactions. SOF refers to the origin of the funds in the business relationship. SOF looks at the money used in a specific transaction and answers the question, “where did this money come from?ˮ SOW refers to how a customer or beneficial owner acquired their total wealth over time. Both SOF and SOW checks could include employment income. If the PEP is deemed to be lower risk, e.g. UK domestic PEP with no material relevant adverse news, and the product or service provided is lower risk, the approach to SOF and SOW identification can be tailored to be less intrusive and exhaustive. Clearpayʼs approach to conducting SOF and SOW checks for consumers is informed by the ML risk of the BNPL service. Clearpayʼs BNPL service has been assessed to be low risk given that consumers are required to list a UK card as their payment method and a low initial spend limit £450 is applied at on-boarding. Clearpay only supports lower risk PEPs, because consumers identified as non-UK PEPs are rejected as outside Clearpayʼs risk appetite. This approach is maintained through initial and ongoing PEP and adverse news screening that informs risk assessment of PEP consumers. Clearpay undertakes SOF/SOW checks at onboarding and on an ongoing basis for all PEPs. 2.2.2.1 Source of Funds Process SOF checks must be completed for all PEPs at onboarding and on an ongoing basis. SOF analysis must include: confirming that initial funds are received from a UK card or account in the customerʼs name, and should include public source checks to establish their income (e.g. employment) unless relevant public sources are not available in which case other methods (such as contacting the consumer) should be considered. 4 These checks must be applied at onboarding, and on an ongoing basis as part of the activity review step of Enhanced Ongoing Monitoring as outlined in the Enhanced Ongoing Monitoring Procedure. As part of this, checks should be undertaken to determine whether payments are received from third parties. If payments are received from third parties, this may be a red flag and would warrant further checks to establish SOF that must be appropriately documented. This may involve reaching out to the consumer to ask questions, depending on the volume of funds involved and assessment of the risk of the situation. 2.2.2.2 Source of Wealth Process SOW checks must be completed for all PEPs at onboarding and on an ongoing basis. SOW analysis should consider publicly available information to confirm if a PEP is in a role that is remunerated and whether it can be inferred that SOW is from that role. On the basis that the SOF analysis has identified payments are made from a UK card or account in the Consumer/PEPs name, and in the absence of any red flags, this SOW analysis will generally be adequate for a low risk PEP using Clearpayʼs low risk BNPL serv
documents.FC-015: UK Anti-Money Laundering, Counter-Terrorist Financing, Counter-Proliferation Financing, and Sanctions Policy Clearpay Finance Limited Last Approved: Dec 11, 2025 Effective Date: Dec 11, 2025 1. Overview This Policy establishes Clearpay Finance Limited's framework for Anti-Money Laundering, Counter-Terrorist Financing, Counter-Proliferation Financing and financial sanctions compliance. The Policy applies to all Clearpay products, Personnel, and operations, and reflects Clearpay's commitment to compliance with UK legal and regulatory requirements for anti-money laundering, counter-terrorist financing, and counter-proliferation financing. Clearpay adopts a risk-based approach to managing financial crime risks, applying measures that are proportionate to the risks identified through comprehensive risk assessments. The Board of Directors and senior management provide governance oversight of the framework and are responsible for ensuring its effectiveness. 2. Definitions The following terms are used throughout this policy: Terminology Definition AML Anti-Money Laundering. Measures and controls designed to prevent, detect, and report ML as required by Regulation 19 of the MLRs. Block Block, Inc. NYSE SQ and its direct and indirect subsidiaries. BNPL Buy Now Pay Later. Clearpayʼs lending product as defined in 5.1 Products. CDD Customer Due Diligence. The process of identifying and verifying customer identity, understanding the purpose and intended nature of business relationships, and assessing money laundering, terrorist financing, and proliferation financing risks in accordance with the Regulations 27 to 30 of the MLRs. Clearpay or CFL Clearpay Finance Limited Confidential and Not for Distribution Terminology Definition Consumer An individual who is an actual or prospective customer for Clearpay's BNPL loans. CTF Counter-Terrorist Financing. Measures to prevent, detect, and disrupt terrorist financing activities, including systems and controls to identify and report suspicious activity related to TF, as required under TACT and the MLRs. CPF Counter-Proliferation Financing. Measures to prevent, detect, and disrupt the financing of the proliferation of weapons of mass destruction and their means of delivery, as required by Regulation 19A of the MLRs. Customer A Consumer, Direct Merchant, or PSP. DAML Defence Against Money Laundering. Consent obtained from the NCA to proceed with a transaction or activity that would otherwise constitute a ML or TF, as provided for by sections 335 and 336 of POCA and section 21D of TACT. Direct Merchant An individual or entity in receipt of Clearpay's Merchant Services Direct product to enable BNPL as a payment method for sales they make to Consumers. The term also refers to prospective Direct Merchants. EDD Enhanced Due Diligence. Additional due diligence measures and enhanced ongoing monitoring applied when the CDD process identifies elevated ML, TF, or PF risks, required for PEPs, high-risk jurisdictions, and other elevated-risk scenarios, as defined in the Regulations 33 to 35 of the MLRs. FCA The Financial Conduct Authority. The UK's financial services regulator established under the Financial Services and Markets Act 2000, which supervises and enforces compliance with financial crime requirements, including AML/CTF/CPF obligations under the MLRs. Merchant A Direct Merchant, PSP Sub-merchant, or SUP Merchant. Merchant As defined in section 5.1 Products. Services Direct Merchant As defined in section 5.1 Products. Services PSPs Merchant As defined in section 5.1 Products. Services SUP ML Money Laundering. The concealing, disguising, converting, transferring, or removing criminal property or entering into an arrangement concerning criminal property, as defined in sections 327, 328 and 34011 of POCA. MLRs The Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017 SI 2017/692, as amended. The regulations that set out the UK's UK Anti-Money Laundering, Counter-Terrorist Financing, Counter-Proliferation Financing & Sanctions Policy | Clearpay Finance Ltd Confidential and Not for Distribution Terminology Definition regulatory requirements for preventing and detecting money laundering, terrorist financing, and proliferation financing. MLRO Money Laundering Reporting Officer. The individual who is appointed as nominated officer and senior manager responsible for compliance with the MLRs, as required by Regulation 21 of the MLRs. NCA The National Crime Agency. The UK law enforcement agency who performs the functions of the financial intelligence unit and to whom SARs are submitted under POCA and TACT obligations. OFSI The Office of Financial Sanctions Implementation. The UK Treasury office responsible for implementing and enforcing financial sanctions regimes under the Sanctions and Anti-Money Laundering Act 2018. PEP Politically Exposed Person. An individual entrusted with prominent public functions (other than as a middle-ranking or more junior official), including their family members (spouse, civil partner, children and their spouses/civil partners, and parents) and known close associates (individuals with joint beneficial ownership or close business relations with a PEP, or sole beneficial ownership of entities set up for the benefit of a PEP, as defined in MLR 2017, Regulation 3512. Personnel All individuals whose work is relevant to Clearpay's compliance with AML/CTF/CPF requirements or is otherwise capable of contributing to the identification or mitigation of money laundering, terrorist financing, or proliferation financing risks, or the prevention or detection of such activities. This includes employees, contractors, agency workers, and individuals working for Clearpay, Block, third-party service providers or agents who perform functions on behalf of Clearpay. PF Proliferation Financing. The provision of funds or financial services for use in connection with chemical, biological, radiological or nuclear weapons, their means of delivery, and related goods and technology, in contravention of a relevant financial sanctions obligation, as defined in the MLRs, Regulation 16A9. POCA The Proceeds of Crime Act 2002 UK legislation creating criminal offences for money laundering activities and establishing obligations for reporting suspicious activity, with tipping-off prohibitions protecting investigation integrity PSP Payment Service Provider. A merchant acquirer in receipt of Clearpay's Merchant Services PSPs product to enable BNPL as a payment option for their own merchant customers. The term also refers to prospective PSPs. PSP A merchant that a PSP has enabled to offer BNPL as a payment option using the Sub-merchant Merchant Services PSP product. The term also refers to prospective PSP Sub-merchants. SAR Suspicious Activity Report. A report filed with the NCA regarding suspicious activity that may indicate ML, TF, or other financial crime, as required under POCA and TACT. UK Anti-Money Laundering, Counter-Terrorist Financing, Counter-Proliferation Financing & Sanctions Policy | Clearpay Finance Ltd Confidential and Not for Distribution Terminology Definition SUP Merchant A merchant that Clearpay has enabled Consumers to shop at via the Merchant Services SUP product. The term also refers to prospective SUP Merchants. TACT The Terrorism Act 2000. The UK legislation that addresses terrorist financing offences and reporting requirements, establishing obligations for reporting suspicious activity that may indicate terrorist financing. TF Terrorist Financing. The provision, collection, or use of funds or other property with the intention, or reasonable cause to suspect, that they will be used for the purposes of terrorism, as criminalised under sections 15 to 18 of TACT. UK Anti-Money Laundering, Counter-Terrorist Financing, Counter-Proliferation Financing & Sanctions Policy | Clearpay Finance Ltd Confidential and Not for Distribution 3. Introduction and Purpose 3.1 Policy Purpose and Scope Clearpay Finance Limited Firm Reference Number is 826341 is a wholly owned subsidiary of Block, Inc NYSE XYZ. This Policy sets out Clearpay's strategic approach, governance structures, and high-level mechanisms for compliance with applicable legal and regulatory obligations related to AML/CTF/CPF and financial sanctions This Policy applies to all Clearpay products, including BNPL, Merchant Services Direct, Merchant Services PSPs, and Merchant Services SUP. The Policy applies to all Personnel and is supported by detailed standards, procedures, and guidance documents that provide operational detail for implementing the framework. Together, these documents enable Clearpay to meet its regulatory obligations under the MLRs, POCA, TACT, and financial sanctions legislation. 3.2 Key Policy Principles Clearpay's AML/CTF/CPF framework is guided by the following principles: Risk-based approach: Clearpay applies measures that are proportionate to the ML, TF, and PF risks identified through comprehensive risk assessments. Higher-risk customers and relationships are subject to enhanced due diligence and monitoring. Compliance with regulatory requirements: Clearpay meets all applicable legal and regulatory obligations under the MLRs, POCA, TACT, and sanctions legislation, and has regard to guidance issued by the FCA, JMLSG, and OFSI. Governance and accountability: Clear ownership and oversight of the AML/CTF/CPF framework exists at all levels of the organisation, from the Board of Directors to operational teams, with defined responsibilities and escalation pathways. Continuous improvement: Clearpay regularly assesses, tests, and enhances the framework to ensure its effectiveness in light of evolving risks, regulatory requirements, and industry best practices. Training and awareness: All Personnel understand their obligations under this Policy and receive training appropriate to their roles, enabling them to recognise and report suspic
documents.FC-016: Payment Service Provider Due Diligence Standard Clearpay Finance Limited Last Approved: Mar 2, 2026 Effective Date: Mar 2, 2026 1. Regulatory Framework 2 2.1 CDD Treatment of PSPs 2 2.2 Policy Integration 3 2.3 Supporting Framework and Core Principles 3 3. PSP Risk Assessment Framework 4 3.1 Risk-Based Approach 4 3.2 Due Diligence and Risk Indicator Identification 4 3.3 Ongoing Risk Assessment Requirements 4 4. PSP Onboarding Standards 5 4.1 Mandatory Eligibility Criteria 5 4.2 Due Diligence Standards 5 4.4 Execution and Approval Standards 6 5. Ongoing Monitoring Standards 6 5.1 Review Scheduling 6 5.2 Event-Driven Review Standards 6 5.3 Transaction Monitoring Standards 7 5.4 Watchlist Screening Standards 7 6. Compliance & Enforcement 8 7.1 Roles and Responsibilities 8 7.2 Monitoring and Reporting Requirements 8 7.3 Training Requirements 8 7.4 Record Retention Requirements 9 8. Documentation Standards 9 8.1 Documentation Quality Requirements 9 8.2 Documentation Retention and Management 9 8.3 Audit Trail Requirements 9 9. Technology and System Standards 10 9.1 System Requirements Framework 10 10. Exceptions 10 11. Document Management 10 11.1 Questions & Contact Information 10 11.2 Related Documents 10 11.3 Review and Approval 11 1. Overview 1.1 Introduction and Purpose This Clearpay UK Payment Service Providers PSP Due Diligence Standard (the "Standard") establishes mandatory requirements for Payment Service Provider due diligence and risk management within Clearpay Finance Limited's Merchant Services PSPs product offering. Clearpay Finance Limited ("Clearpay UK") operates as a subsidiary of Block, Inc. ("Block"), providing Buy Now Pay Later BNPL services in the UK market. Clearpay serves three distinct customer categories: consumers receiving BNPL loans, merchants utilising Clearpay payment options, and Payment Service Providers partnering with Clearpay to offer services to their merchant customers. This Standard defines specific, measurable requirements that must be achieved to ensure effective PSP oversight. The requirements establish comprehensive control frameworks, compliance metrics, technology standards, reporting obligations, exception management protocols, and quality assurance measures. 1.2 Scope This Standard applies to Clearpay UK and governs all PSP relationships supporting the Merchant Services PSPs product offering, including: ● Traditional merchant acquirers ● Payment facilitators ● Embedded finance providers ● MLevel partners The Standard establishes requirements for due diligence, ongoing monitoring, risk management, and compliance oversight activities for all PSPs that partner with Clearpay to offer Clearpayʼs BNPL service to their merchant customers. Clearpay UK Payment Service Provider Due Diligence Standard | Clearpay Finance Ltd. 1 1.3 Definitions Terminology Definition A third-party financial services entity that integrates with Clearpay's Merchant Services product to enable Clearpay as a payment option for its Payment Service merchant clients. PSPs maintain direct contractual relationships with Provider PSP merchants and are responsible for merchant onboarding, due diligence, transaction processing, and compliance oversight. A PSP relationship where the PSP acts as Merchant of Record, assuming MLevel Partner enhanced responsibilities for merchant onboarding and transaction processing while enabling Clearpay services through their platform. Money A country or territory designated by HM Treasury (or an equivalent The designated senior individual within Clearpay Finance Limited who holds Laundering statutory responsibility for oversight of compliance with UK Money Reporting Officer Laundering Regulations and maintains ultimate approval authority for PSP MLRO onboarding decisions and periodic review outcomes. Comprehensive additional due diligence measures applied to High-risk Enhanced Due PSPs, encompassing enhanced documentation requirements, beneficial Diligence EDD ownership verification, control testing, and increased monitoring frequency. Block Merchant The specialised Block team responsible for executing PSP due diligence and Network reviews, ongoing monitoring activities, and compliance oversight on behalf Compliance of Clearpay while leveraging Block's enterprise compliance infrastructure. MNC 2. Regulatory Framework 2.1 CDD Treatment of PSPs PSPs are not customers under the Money Laundering Regulations because the services they receive from Clearpay are not listed activities under the MLRs. Nevertheless, Clearpay applies standards equivalent to customer due diligence under the Money Laundering Regulations to PSPs to mitigate the risk of regulatory challenge, meet broader financial crime obligations, and ensure comprehensive risk management. Clearpay does not apply customer due diligence to PSP Sub-merchants because the firm has no direct business relationship with these entities. However, Clearpay remains exposed to the ML/TF/PF and Sanctions risk that processing their transactions poses. Therefore, Clearpay applies enhanced due diligence measures to all PSPs given their role in facilitating relationships with PSP Sub-merchants and Clearpayʼs reliance on the controls of PSPS to mitigate the risks posed by their sub-merchants. Clearpay UK Payment Service Provider Due Diligence Standard | Clearpay Finance Ltd. 2 2.2 Policy Integration This Standard complies with Clearpayʼs AML/CTF/CPF and Sanctions Policy and maintains alignment with Block's global compliance framework while establishing Clearpay-specific requirements. Where conflicts exist between this Standard and Block's global standards, UK policy and regulatory requirements take precedence. The Standard builds on the Block Standards for Payment Service Provider Oversight and Indirect Merchant Relationships, incorporating the global framework while addressing Clearpay's business model, Clearpayʼs AML/CTF/CPF and Sanctions policy requirements, and local regulatory expectations. 2.3 Supporting Framework and Core Principles This Standard operates within a comprehensive compliance framework supported by established Block processes and standards. The Block Standards for Compliance Outreach and Escalations to PSPs provides the operational foundation for managing PSP compliance communications, establishing structured timelines, escalation pathways, and accountability measures that ensure effective oversight execution. Core Principles of this Standard: Risk-Based Approach: All PSP oversight activities are calibrated to assessed risk levels, ensuring proportionate resource allocation and appropriate control measures. Regulatory Primacy: UK regulatory requirements take precedence over global standards where conflicts exist, ensuring full compliance with applicable laws. Comprehensive Due Diligence: PSPs must demonstrate robust compliance capabilities through comprehensive verification and ongoing assessment processes. Continuous Monitoring: Ongoing oversight through transaction monitoring, due diligence refresh and event-driven assessments ensures sustained compliance effectiveness. Operational Integration: Seamless coordination between Clearpay compliance functions and Block's enterprise infrastructure ensures consistent and effective oversight. Technology-Enabled Oversight: Automated systems and real-time capabilities enhance monitoring effectiveness and compliance efficiency. Clearpay UK Payment Service Provider Due Diligence Standard | Clearpay Finance Ltd. 3 3. PSP Risk Assessment Framework 3.1 Risk-Based Approach All PSP oversight activities operate under a comprehensive risk assessment framework informed by relevant risk indicators. This approach ensures that all PSPs meet Clearpayʼs enterprise risk appetite, regulatory and network expectations, and operational oversight standards from onboarding through the full lifecycle of the relationship. The risk assessment framework evaluates multiple risk indicators identified through the due diligence process to determine appropriate oversight measures, monitoring frequencies, and control requirements while maintaining consistently elevated standards across all PSP relationships as part of Clearpay's PSP due diligence program. 3.2 Due Diligence and Risk Indicator Identification PSPs undergo comprehensive due diligence processes that identify and assess risk indicators through structured information requests and documentation review. The due diligence process encompasses: Information Request Process: PSPs complete comprehensive Due Diligence Questionnaires DDQs and provide supporting documentation that enables assessment of their business model, operational structure, and internal controls. This process identifies risk indicators related to business complexity, jurisdictional exposure, ownership structures, and compliance capabilities. Risk Indicator Evaluation: Through the due diligence process, various risk indicators are identified and evaluated, including but not limited to their operations, jurisdictional risk exposure, beneficial ownership, regulatory compliance history, proliferation financing risk exposure and control effectiveness. These indicators inform the intensity and scope of ongoing oversight requirements. Enhanced Assessment: All PSPs are subject to enhanced due diligence measures appropriate to their identified risk profile, with additional assessment requirements applied based on the nature and extent of risk indicators identified during the initial and ongoing due diligence processes. 3.3 Ongoing Risk Assessment Requirements The Block MNC must submit active PSPs and their risk assessments for review and approval by the MLRO at least annually, and following material changes in PSP circumstances, regulatory developments, or identified concerns.
