documents.FC-017: Financial Crime Program Squareup Europe Limited SEL Last Approved: September 29, 2025 Effective Date: September 29, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: Squareup Europe Limited (“SELˮ) is an authorised Electronic Money Institution, regulated and supervised by the Financial Conduct Authority (“FCAˮ) in the United Kingdom (“UKˮ), under Firm Reference Number 900846. The direct parent company is Squareup International Limited (“SILˮ), registered in the Republic of Ireland; both companies are wholly owned subsidiaries of Block, Inc (“Blockˮ). SELʼs two key product offerings are (i) Square Managed Payments and (ii) Cash App (in Winddown since October 2024. Further information on the products and services offered by SEL in the UK are located in Appendix 1. This Program document relates to the Square business. Block, Inc. Block, Inc., a Delaware corporation, is a merchant services aggregator and mobile payments company based in San Francisco, California in the United States of America. It is registered as a Money Services Business with the Financial Crimes Enforcement Network (“FinCENˮ). SELʼs primary product offering includes a combination of card acceptance hardware, made up of the Square Reader (a device used with an Apple iOS device or an Android mobile device), a Square Stand and an accompanying software application to provide the technical interface between the hardware and the merchant acquiring banking infrastructure. Collectively, this payment solution is referred to as "Square Managed Payments". Square Managed Payments reads debit, credit, gift, and prepaid card data and enables a merchant (an individual or a company) to accept non-cash payments using their iPhone/iPad/Android mobile device and send a digital receipt to their customer (or a printed receipt if compatible hardware is installed). Scope: This Financial Crime Program document applies to SEL products. Definitions: Terminology Definition Money Laundering Money Laundering is the process by which individuals or organisations conceal the origins of illegally obtained funds, making them appear legitimate. In the context of SEL, Money Laundering typically involves disguising the proceeds of crime through legitimate payment channels, including merchant transactions and card processing services. Criminals may attempt to exploit financial institutions to move, layer or integrate illicit funds into the financial system. As a regulated entity under the UK Money Laundering Regulations, SEL has a legal and ethical responsibility to detect and prevent such activities by implementing robust Anti-Money Laundering (“AMLˮ) controls, including customer due diligence (“CDDˮ), transaction monitoring and the reporting of suspicious activity. Terrorist Financing Terrorist Financing involves the collection or provision of funds with the intention or knowledge that they will be used to support terrorist acts, individuals or organisations. Unlike Money Laundering, the origin of the funds used for terrorist financing may be legitimate or illicit, making detection more complex. For SEL, there is a heightened risk that payment processing services could be misused to raise, move or store funds that support terrorism, often through seemingly legitimate merchants or transactions. Under the Terrorism Act 2000 (“TACTˮ) and related UK regulations, SEL must implement effective controls to identify and report suspicious activity, conduct enhanced due diligence (“EDDˮ) where necessary and ensure systems are in place to prevent its services from being exploited for terrorist financing purposes. Proliferation Proliferation Financing refers to the act of providing funds or financial Financing services that are used to support the development, acquisition or transfer of weapons of mass destruction (“WMDsˮ), including nuclear, chemical or biological weapons. This type of financing is often linked to sanctioned individuals, entities or countries and may involve complex networks designed to evade international controls. For SEL, the risk lies in unintentionally facilitating transactions that contribute to proliferation activities through merchant relationships or payment processing. Under UK regulations and international obligations such as UN and UK sanctions regimes, SEL is required to implement robust screening, due diligence and monitoring systems to detect and prevent any involvement in Proliferation Financing, ensuring compliance with all applicable sanctions and export control laws. Financial Crime A written document that sets out the primary components the company Program uses to identify, mitigate, and manage potential risks of its products or services being misused to facilitate ML, TF or PF. The components of the program and documentation must be developed and implemented and proactively maintained. Beneficial Owner Refers to the natural person(s) who ultimately owns or controls a customer or the natural person on whose behalf a transaction is being conducted. It also includes those persons who exercise ultimate effective control over a legal person or arrangement. Customers An individual or company that uses products and services offered by SEL. Host Country A country where a company that is based in another country has business activities. Merchant A business that offers products and services related to SELʼs primary product - Square Managed Payments. Suspicious A document that a financial institution must file with the relevant Financial Activity Report Intelligence Unit following a suspected incident of ML, TF, PF or fraud. SAR Staff/Employee All full-time employees, fixed-term employees, outsourced employees and contingent workers including interns, temps, consultants, vendors, third-party providers, and contractors. 2. Legal & Regulatory Framework The UK Money Laundering Regulations, applying to financial institutions, date from 1993, the current Regulations being the Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017 (as amended) Money Laundering Regulations 2017. The offence of money laundering was contained in various acts of parliament (such as the Criminal Justice Act 1988 and the Drug Trafficking Offences Act 1986. The Proceeds of Crime Act 2002 (“POCAˮ) consolidated, updated and reformed the law relating to money laundering to include any dealing in criminal property. Specific obligations to combat terrorist financing were set out in the Terrorism Act 2000. SEL takes into account legislation such as the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002, the Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017 (as amended) , the Counter Terrorism Act 2008, the Electronic Money Regulations 2011 and the Payment Services Regulations 2017. As an e-money institution, SEL must also comply with the relevant parts of the FCA handbook for firms and take into account the FCAʼs Approach Document. 3. Financial Crime Program Summary This document outlines SELʼs comprehensive Financial Crime Program, which is designed to identify, assess and mitigate risks related to Financial Crime. It encompasses the key components of SELs framework to prevent and detect Money Laundering (“MLˮ), Terrorist Financing (“TFˮ) and Proliferation Financing (“PFˮ). SELʼs business is subject to a wide range of laws and regulations within the UK and the US, including Anti-Money Laundering, Counter Terrorist Financing and Counter Proliferation Financing AML/CTF/CPF legislation as well as US Treasury Departmentʼs Office of Foreign Assets Control OFAC and US sanctions requirements. SEL also follows the international framework proposed by the Financial Conduct Authority FCA, Financial Action Task Force FATF and guidance produced by Joint Money Laundering Steering Group JMLSG in the UK. SEL is committed to developing, implementing, and maintaining an effective, risk based Financial Crime Program, which is reasonably designed to prevent SEL from being used to facilitate money laundering,terrorist or proliferation financing activities. This Financial Crime Program document applies to all the Square products available in the UK. The objectives of this Financial Crime Program for the UK are: ● To develop and implement a risk-based Financial Crime Program for all Square activities; ● To develop risk-based AML/CTF/CPF control procedures for all Square activities; ● To promote awareness of applicable AML/CTF/CPF laws and regulations; ● To detect, prevent, monitor, and report any suspicious activity and any terrorist financing transactions; and ● Fully cooperate with all law enforcement, regulatory agencies, and financial intelligence units to the extent required and permitted under applicable law. It is vital that all SEL personnel1 are aware of and committed to achieving compliance with all applicable requirements of the Financial Crime Program, laws, and regulations. All SEL employees are expected to follow the protocols and processes outlined in this Program document, as well as any related procedures, to ensure compliance with all related legal and regulatory requirements. 4. Governance and Oversight Board of Directors and Senior Management Senior Management of SEL, including the Board of Directors (the Board), have responsibility for managing identified ML/TF/PF risks by demonstrating active engagement in SELʼs approach to effectively mitigating such risks. The Board meets quarterly and follows a terms of reference which details information including the Boardʼs role, its standing agenda, and its decision making process. This Financial Crime Program and any changes to it must be approved, in the first instance, by the Money Laundering Reporting Officer (“MLROˮ) who has been appointed by the Board of SEL to coordinate and manage SELʼs Financial Crime Program and secondly, by the Board itself. The document is updated as needed and at least on an annual basis and reaffirmed by SELʼs senior management and the Boar
documents.FC-018: Financial Crime Program Squareup International Limited SIL Last Approved: September 25, 2025 Effective Date: September 25, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Financial Crime Program (“Programˮ) is established by Squareup International Limited (“SILˮ). SIL is committed to developing, implementing, and maintaining an effective, risk-based AML/CFT program, which is reasonably designed to prevent SIL from being used to facilitate ML and the financing of terrorist activities. This Program document has been designed to meet the following objectives: ● To develop and implement a risk-based AML/CFT/FS program for all SIL activities in the European Union (“ EUˮ); ● To inform the development of risk-based AML/CFT/FS control procedures for all SIL activities in the EU; ● To promote awareness of applicable AML/CFT/FS laws and regulations; ● To detect, prevent, monitor and and report suspicious activity and any terrorist financing transactions; and ● To fully cooperate with all law enforcement, regulatory agencies, and financial intelligence units to the extent required and permitted under applicable law. A regional EU country Annex provides an overview of the country regulations SIL adheres to in the countries in which it currently operates. This annex will be updated as and when SIL begins to provide regulated activities in other EEA countries. Scope: This program is established by SIL and is applicable to Seller. It is vital that all Staff members that support SIL are aware of, and are committed to complying with, all applicable requirements of this Program and relevant AML/CFT/FS laws and regulations. All Staff members are expected to maintain the standards specified within this Program. In the context of SIL, ML typically involves disguising the proceeds of crime through legitimate payment channels, including merchant transactions and card processing services. Suspicious STRs are documents that a financial institution must file with the Transaction relevant Financial Intelligence Unit (“FIUˮ) and the Revenue Reports (“STRˮ) Commissioners following a suspected incident of money laundering, terrorist financing or fraud. Staff/ Staff All full-time and part-time employees and fixed-term and contingent Members workers including interns, temps, consultants, vendors, third-party providers, and contractors. Terrorist Financing Terrorist Financing involves the collection or provision of funds with the intention or knowledge that they will be used to support terrorist acts, individuals or organisations. Unlike Money Laundering, the origin of the funds used for terrorist financing may be legitimate or illicit, making detection more complex. SIL must implement effective controls to identify and report suspicious activity, conduct enhanced due diligence (“EDDˮ) where necessary and ensure systems are in place to prevent its services from being exploited for terrorist financing purposes. 2. Program Requirements 2.1. Legal & Regulatory Framework SIL has obligations under Part 4 of the Criminal Justice Money Laundering and Terrorist Financing) Act 2010 (as amended) Criminal Justice Act “CJAˮ 2010 and under its requirements to adhere to international sanctions. SIL will also have regard to the Anti-Money Laundering and Countering the Financing of Terrorism Guidelines for the Financial Sector (“The Guidelinesˮ) introduced by CBI in September of 2019, other guidance and papers issued by CBI, and other EBA Guidelines. Part 4 of the CJA Act 2010 obliges SIL to put in place an effective, risk-based AML/CFT framework, which includes the application of a risk-based approach, Customer due diligence (“CDDˮ) measures, reporting of suspicious transactions, governance, policies and procedures, recordkeeping, and training. The retention of such records will be tested as part of Blockʼs global risk based approach to monitoring and testing. The CBI is the competent authority for the monitoring of credit and financial institutionsʼ compliance with the CJA 2010 and is responsible for taking reasonable measures to secure such compliance. 2.1.1 Money Laundering Money laundering means an offence as set out under Section 7 of the CJA 2010. It involves the intentional or reckless conversion of property, generated from “criminal conductˮ, so that the criminal origin of the property is difficult to trace. Money Laundering Section 71 of the CJA 2010 provides that a person commits a money laundering offence in the State if: (a) the person engages in any of the following acts in relation to property that is the proceeds of criminal conduct: (i) concealing or disguising the true nature, source, location, disposition, movement or ownership of the property, or any rights relating to the property; (ii) converting, transferring, handling, acquiring, possessing, or using the property; (iii) removing the property from, or bringing the property into, the State; and (b) the person knows or believes (or is reckless as to whether or not) the property is the proceeds of criminal conduct. Section 72 of the CJA 2010 provides that a person who attempts to commit an offence under subsection 1 commits an offence. 2.1.2 Criminal Contact “Criminal conductˮ is defined in Section 6 of the CJA 2010 as “Conduct that constitutes an offence or conduct occurring in a place outside the State that constitutes an offence under the law of the place and would constitute an offence if it were to occur in the State. This definition encompasses all offences whether minor or serious, summary, or indictable. 2.1.3 Proceeds of Criminal Conduct “Proceeds of criminal conductˮ is defined in Section 6 of the CJA 2010 as “any property that is derived from or obtained through criminal conduct, whether directly or indirectly or in whole or in part, and whether that criminal conduct occurs before, on or after the commencement of this Partˮ. 2.1.4 Terrorist Financing Terrorist financing means an offence under Section 13 of the Criminal Justice Terrorist Offences) Act 2005 CJA 2005. Part A Overview of the Square AU AML/CTF Program 1. Overview of the AML/CTF Program 1.1 About Square AU Square AU Pty Ltd ACN 167 106 176 (Square AU, we, or us) is a Reporting Entity enrolled on the Reporting Entity Roll, and to be registered on the Remittance Sector Register managed by the Australian Transaction Reports and Analysis Centre (AUSTRAC) for the purposes of AML/CTF Laws. Square AU is a wholly owned subsidiary of Block, Inc (Block). Square AU is the provider of a managed payments business and the issuer of business loans to Australian Customers. This document is the AML/CTF Policies for Square AU and applies to all areas of Square AUʼs business relating to the provision of the Designated Services. We recognise that there is a risk that our services may be used to facilitate money laundering, terrorism and proliferation financing. The Square AU ML/TF Risk Assessment is summarised in Schedule 1. By having procedures to more effectively identify the Customers of our Designated Services, and policies to manage and mitigate ML/TF/PF risk, we provide both a deterrent to persons considering the misuse of our services but also generate records that provide an audit trail that may be relied upon by law enforcement agencies entitled to access the information. Square AU acknowledges that from 31 March 2026, Reporting Entities must demonstrate compliance with the updated CDD, SCDD, ECDD, sanctions, outsourcing and proliferation finance prevention requirements introduced under the AML/CTF Acts and associated AML/CTF Rules. This AML/CTF Program incorporates those expanded obligations. 1.2 Square AU AML/CTF Program The Square AU AML/CTF Program comprises: ● the ML/TF Risk Assessment of Square AU,1 as summarised in Schedule 1; and ● these AML/CTF Policies. These AML/CTF Policies have been designed to: o appropriately manage and mitigate the risks of ML/TF/PF that Square AU may reasonably face in providing its Designated Services. Each Designated Service that Square AU provides (or may provide) to Customers are set out in Schedule 2;2 o ensure that Square AU complies with the obligations imposed by the AML/CTF Laws;3 and o be appropriate to the nature, size and complexity of the Square AU business.4 1 AML/CTF Act, ss 26C – 26E. 2 AML/CTF Act, s 26F(1)(a). 3 AML/CTF Act, s 26F(1)(b). 4 AML/CTF Act, s 26F(1)(c). AML/CTF Act, s 26F(1)(d) also requires the Reporting Entity to develop and maintain AML/CTF Policies that deal with certain matters in respect of transfer of value. The Designated Services we provide do not trigger this obligation. 1 Square AU must comply with these AML/CTF Policies.5 1.3 Application of these AML/CTF Policies The previous version of the Square AU AML/CTF Program was formally approved and adopted by the Governing Body (i.e. the board of directors of Square AU on 11 December 2024. This version is approved and adopted by the Governing Body and a Senior Manager on 30 March 2026. The full amendment and approval history of these AML/CTF Policies can be found at the beginning of this document. Square AU keeps a record of these approvals. The Designated Services provided by Square AU are listed in Schedule 2. 1.4 Enrolment and registration with AUSTRAC Square AU is required to enrol with AUSTRAC no later than 28 days after commencing to provide a Designated Service as a Reporting Entity.6 Square AU will be registered with AUSTRAC as described in section 31. As a Reporting Entity enrolled with AUSTRAC, we will advise the AUSTRAC CEO of any changes to the enrolment details which were provided to AUSTRAC when we are enrolled as a Reporting Entity.7 This includes information about our Designated Services,8 and information relating to us.9 It also includes updating our annual earnings where earnings of $100 million or more occur.10 As an independent remittance provider registered with AUSTRAC, we will advise the AUSTRAC CEO of any change in circumstances that could materially affect our registration,11 and changes12 to our registrable details, changes to criminal or civil proceedings relating to us or our key personnel, changes to information regarding other person assisting, changes to foreign country registrations or licences, us ceasing to provide registrable services, changes to the details of Beneficiary Owners, and changes to persons located in foreign countries.13 The AML/CTF Compliance Officer will ensure that the AUSTRAC CEO is notified of any changes to the above enrolment or registration details within 14 days of the change occurring.14 2. Updates to the AML/CTF Program 2.1 Updates to the ML/TF Risk Assessment The ML/TF Risk Assessment will be kept under review and updated from time to time as described in Part C of these AML/CTF Policies. Updates to the ML/TF Risk Assessment: ● may be made with the approval of a Senior Manager with authority to do so. Senior Managers with such authority are specified in Schedule 3; 15 ● must be notified, in writing, to the Governing Body of Square AU as soon as practicable after the update is made; and16 ● will result in a review and update of these AML/CTF Policies where required in accordance with section 2.3.17 5 AML/CTF Act, ss 26G(1). 6 AML/CTF Act section 51B. 7 AML/CTF Act, s 51F. 8 AML/CTF Rules, r 3-9(1) which specifies information in r 3-2. 9 AML/CTF Rules, r 3-9(1) which specifies information in r 3-3. 10 AML/CTF Rules, r 3-9(2) which specifies information in r 3-4(2). 11 AML/CTF Act, s 75M(1)(d). 12 AML/CTF Act, s 75M(1)(e). 13 AML/CTF Rules, r 4-34. 14 AML/CTF Act, ss 51F(2)(a) and 75M(4)(a). 15 AML/CTF Act, s 26P(1) 16 AML/CTF Act, s 26P(2) 17 AML/CTF Act, s 26F(3)(c)(i). 2 2.2 Updates to AML/CTF Policies These AML/CTF Policies will be kept under review and updated from time to time including: ● as a result of any need identified as part of the AML/CTF Compliance Officerʼs review as described in section 2.3; ● in response to AUSTRAC feedback and guidance as described in section 3; ● following an independent evaluation as described in section 4; and ● in response to a review of the ML/TF Risk Assessment as described in section 9. Changes to the AML/CTF Policies must be approved by a Senior Manager with authority to do so. Senior Managers with such authority are specified in Schedule 3. 18 2.3 AML/CTF Compliance Officer review The AML/CTF Compliance Officer will ensure that a review of the AML/CTF Policies is conducted at least annually.19 This review will cover: ● our compliance with these AML/CTF Policies; ● the extent to which our AML/CTF Policies are appropriately managing and mitigating the ML/TF/PF risk that we may reasonably face in providing our designated services;
documents.FC-020: Anti-Money Laundering & Anti- Terrorist Financing Policy Square | Square Canada, Inc. Last Approved: Mar 11, 2026 Effective Date: Mar 11, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: Square Canada, Inc. (“Square Canadaˮ or “the Companyˮ) is a wholly owned subsidiary of Block, Inc., a Delaware corporation, which is a merchant services aggregator and mobile payments company in San Francisco, California. Square Canada is a money services business MSB offering payment processing solutions which allow customers (“Sellersˮ) to accept credit, debit and gift card payments in exchange for goods and services (“Managed Payments Service"). Square Canadaʼs Managed Payments Service includes a variety of product features, each of which is ancillary to the payment card processing solution1. Included within Square Canadaʼs Managed Payments Service are “crowdfunding platform servicesˮ, as defined under the PCMLTFA, which allow Sellers to raise funds from their respective customers. Crowdfunding platform services may only be used by Sellers to raise funds on their own behalf, and cannot be used to raise funds on behalf of persons or entities specified by them. Square Canada also offers prepaid business expense cards (where the source of funds is derived exclusively from payment processing activities accessed via Managed Payments Service), commercial loans, and merchant cash advances (collectively, “Financial Servicesˮ). Square Canada is committed to preventing its services from being used for illicit purposes such as money laundering and terrorist financing activities. Square Canada has established an anti-money laundering (“AMLˮ) and anti-terrorist financing (“ATFˮ) compliance program (“AML/ATF Programˮ) to ensure compliance with applicable AML and ATF laws and regulations. Accordingly, Square Canada has adopted this Anti-Money Laundering and Counter Terrorist Financing Policy (“AML/ATF Policyˮ, “this Policyˮ) and will conduct business activities in compliance with the principles found herein. 1 Managed Payments Service features include, but are not limited to: (i) Point of Sale; (ii) Invoices; (iii) Virtual Terminal; (iv) eCommerce; (v) Funds transfers to a sellerʼs external linked bank account; (vi) crowdfunding platform services; and (vii) Square Gift Cards. 1 Scope: This Policy is established by Square Canada and is applicable to Square Canadaʼs activities within Canada. Definitions: Terminology Definition Anti-money Implementing controls and measures to stop criminals and terrorists from laundering abusing the financial system for the purposes of money laundering. (“AMLˮ) Customer Verifying the identity of the customer using independent and reliable due-diligence documentation, data or information. (“CDDˮ) Anti-terrorist Implementing controls and measures to stop criminals and terrorists from financing (“ATFˮ) abusing the financial system for the purposes of financing terrorism or terrorist acts. Crowdfunding A website or an application or other software that is used to raise funds or Platform virtual currency through donations. Crowdfunding The provision and maintenance of a crowdfunding platform for use by other Platform persons or entities to raise funds or virtual currency for themselves or for the Services persons or entities specified by them. Enhanced Collecting and/or verifying additional information or documentation about the due-diligence customerʼs identity including, but not limited to: source of funds and source (“EDDˮ) of wealth. Electronic funds The transmission - by any electronic, magnetic or optical means - of transfer instructions for the transfer of funds, including a transmission of instructions (“EFTˮ) that is initiated and finally received by the same person or entity. It does not include a transmission or instructions for the transfer of funds: (i) that involves the beneficiary withdrawing cash from their account; (ii) that is carried out by means of a direct deposit or pre-authorized debit; (iii) that is carried out by cheque imaging and presentment; (iv) that is both initiated and finally received by persons or entities that are acting to clear or settle payment obligations between themselves; or (v) that is initiated or finally received by a person or entity referred to in paragraphs 5(a) to (h.1) of the PCMLTFA for the purposes of internal treasury management of their financial assets and liabilities, if one of the parties to the transaction is a subsidiary of the other or if they are subsidiaries of the same corporation. Financial Canadaʼs financial intelligence unit Transactions and Reports Analysis Centre of Canada 2 (“FINTRACˮ) Head of an A person who, at a given time, holds - or has held within 5 years before that international time - the office or position of head of: (i) an international organization that is organization established by the governments of states; (ii) an institution of an organization (“HIOˮ) referred to in paragraph (i); or (iii) an international sports organization. Know your The process of identifying customers and verifying customer information in customer accordance with regulations and guidance aimed at preventing criminals (“KYCˮ) from accessing the financial system. Money An act, or attempted act, to disguise the source of money or assets derived Laundering from criminal activity. Money laundering is the process whereby “dirty (“MLˮ) moneyˮ – value produced through criminal activity – is transformed into “clean moneyˮ. There are three recognized stages of money laundering: (i) Placement: placing the proceeds of crime into the financial system; (ii) Layering: converting the proceeds of crime into another form and creating complex layers of financial transactions to disguise the trail and the source and ownership of funds; and (iii) Integration: integrating the laundered proceeds back into the economy to create the perception of legitimacy. Terrorist Financing Act (“PCMLTFAˮ) Politically A person who, by nature of their being entrusted with a prominent public exposed person function or position of influence, is more susceptible to bribery or corruption. (“PEPˮ) Reasonable Steps taken to achieve a desired outcome, even if they do not result in the Measures desired outcome. Reasonable measures include, but are not limited to: (i) asking the customer; (ii) conducting open source searches; or (iii) consulting commercially available information. Regulations Regulations Implementing the United Nations Resolutions on the Suppression Implementing of Terrorism the United Nations Resolutions on the Suppression of Terrorism (“RIUNRSTˮ) Suspicious A type of report that must be submitted to FINTRAC by a reporting entity if transaction there are reasonable grounds to suspect that a financial transaction that report (“STRˮ) occurs or is attempted in the course of its business activities is related to the commission or the attempted commission of a ML/TF offence. 3 Terrorist The use of funds, property or other services to encourage, plan, assist or financing (“TFˮ) engage in acts of terrorism, where the primary motivation is not financial gain. Listed Person or A type of report that must be submitted to FINTRAC by a reporting entity if it Entity Property has property in its possession or control that it knows or believes is owned or Report controlled by or on behalf of a terrorist or a terrorist group or it knows or (“TPRˮ) believes that the property is owned or controlled by or on behalf of a listed person or entity. Virtual currency Virtual currency means (i) a digital representation of value that can be used (“VCˮ) for payment or investment purposes that is not a fiat currency and that can be readily exchanged for funds or for another virtual currency that can be readily exchanged for funds; or (ii) a private key of a cryptographic system that enables a person or entity to have access to a digital representation of value referred to in paragraph (i). 2. Policy Requirements 2.1. Key Concepts 2.1.1. Money Laundering Money laundering is an act, or attempted act, to disguise the source of money or assets derived from criminal activity. Money laundering is the process whereby “dirty moneyˮ, or money generated through criminal activity (the proceeds of crime), is transformed into “clean moneyˮ, the source of which is difficult to trace or verify. Criminals aim to convert the proceeds of crime into a form which appears to have a legitimate source; and if carried out successfully, money laundering allows criminals to maintain control of their proceeds of crime while concealing the true origin. There are three primary stages to money laundering: 1. Placement: the placing of the proceeds of crime into the financial system; 2. Layering: converting the proceeds of crime into another form of value and creating complex layers of financial transactions to disguise the audit trail, source and true ownership of the proceeds of crime; and 3. Integration: integrating the proceeds of crime back into the economy in such a way so as to create the perception of legitimacy. A money laundering offence generally requires that an individual knows or believes that the proceeds of crime was obtained or derived from the commission of another offence, known as a predicate offence. Predicate offences include a variety of crimes including drug dealing, theft, bribery, smuggling, corruption, fraud, embezzlement, counterfeiting, arson, extortion or murder. 2.1.2. Terrorist Financing 4 Terrorist financing is knowingly collecting or giving property (such as money) to carry out terrorist activities. This includes the use and possession of any property to help carry out terrorist activities. With terrorist financing, using funds to carry out a terrorist act is the ultimate goa

Bank Secrecy Act The Bank Secrecy Act of 1970, also known as the Currency and Foreign (“BSAˮ) Transactions Reporting Act, is a U.S. law requiring financial institutions in the United States to assist U.S. government agencies in detecting and preventing money laundering. Customer CIP represents the minimum standard of KYC and comprises two elements: Identification a) the collection of customer information Identity Collection); and b) Program (“CIPˮ) verification Identity Verification) using reliable and independent sources to reasonably establish the identity of the customer. Enhanced Due EDD includes CDD plus additional collection and/or verification of customer Diligence (“EDDˮ) information where the risk of the customer is potentially high. Local Laws may specify circumstances where EDD must be performed and the measures required. Know Your KYC includes the process of establishing risk-based controls to reasonably Customer (“KYCˮ) identify and verify the identity of Blockʼs customers. The term KYC may also be referred to as Know Your Business KYB when dealing with non-individual entities or business customers. Financial Crimes A bureau of the U.S. Department of Treasury that collects and analyzes Enforcement information about financial transactions in order to combat domestic and Network (“FinCENˮ) international money laundering, terrorist financing, and other financial crimes. Integration As the final stage of money laundering, integration occurs when the criminal receives the proceeds of what now appear to be legitimate payments. The criminal can then use the funds as they wish. Layering As the second stage of money laundering, layering is generally the use of many complex transactions to obscure the source of the criminal proceeds and make them appear legitimate. Credit Card Bank Secrecy Act/Anti-Money Laundering & Countering the Financing of Terrorism Program Standard | Square | Square Capital, LLC 4 Confidential Money Laundering Money laundering is the process of making criminal proceeds ('dirty' money) appear to have come from a legitimate source ('clean' money). Criminals do this by moving those proceeds through different entities, accounts and transactions where the funds are less likely to attract attention but are readily accessible when required. Office of Foreign The Office of Foreign Assets Control is a financial intelligence and Assets Control enforcement agency of the U.S. Department of Treasury. Unlike money laundering, which always involves the proceeds of a crime, funds to finance terrorism are often diverted from legitimate sources. Introduction This Credit Card Bank Secrecy Act/Anti-Money Laundering & Countering the Financing of Terrorism Program Standard (the “Standardˮ) is designed to support compliance with relevant U.S. money laundering, terrorist financing, and sanctions regulations applicable to Square Capital, LLC (“Square Capitalˮ or the “Companyˮ) under its Credit Card Program (as described in more detail below), offered in partnership with Celtic Bank (the “Bank Partnerˮ). The Bank Secrecy Act, as amended by Title III of the USA PATRIOT Act, and its implementing regulations (collectively, the "BSA"), obligates financial institutions to implement anti-money laundering (“AMLˮ) programs and requires the U.S. Department of Treasury to issue regulations specifying the requirements for such programs. The U.S. Department of Treasury issues such regulations through the Financial Crimes Enforcement Network FinCEN. Under the Credit Card Program, Square Capital is not subject to BSA requirements because it serves as a credit card servicer and not a financial institution.1 However, it is subject to certain Bank Partner requirements. Square Capital adheres to the Block, Inc. ("Block") Global AML Policy. At a minimum, the Bank Partner requires the Credit Card Product to have a Standard. This Standard also aligns with the Block Global AML Policy and also forms part of Blockʼs Anti-Financial Crime domain under the Block Compliance Policy Governance Framework. 2. Purpose This Standard is designed to ensure compliance by Square Capitalʼs Credit Card product with applicable BSA and AML requirements and applies to all relevant activities conducted by Square Capital, including lending activities undertaken as part of servicing a credit card program. This document does not apply to Square Capital's other products or programs outside of the Credit Card Program. It should be noted that certain controls are administered by Block, which are specified throughout this Standard. Anti-Social Forces Management Policy | Square | Square K.K. 4 Confidential and Not for Distribution 1. Introduction Japan has stringent regulations against Anti-Social Forces (“ASFˮ) to ensure business integrity and safety. Compliance with these regulations, mandated by the Government policies and prefectural authorities, is essential. All entities in Japan must adhere strictly to these standards, ensuring a secure and ethical business environment, by prohibiting any relationships with these Anti-Social members. 1.はじめに 反社会的勢力に対しては、企業の健全性と安全性を確保するために日本国内で厳格な規制が敷かれています。これらの規 制は政府方針および各都道府県当局によって義務付けられており、遵守は必須です。日本国内のすべての事業体は、反社 会的勢力との一切の関係を禁止することで、安全かつ倫理的な事業環境を確保し、これらの基準を厳守しなければなりませ ん。 2. Purpose The purpose of this Policy is to establish necessary measures for addressing ASF, ensuring proper administrative operations in its implementation, in order to firmly sever and eliminate any relationships with ASF and prevent harm from such forces. 2.目的 本規程は、Square株式会社（以下、「当社」という。）が反社会的勢力による被害を防止するために、断固として、反社会的 勢力との関係を遮断し、排除すべく実施にあたって適正な事務運営を確保できるよう、反社会的勢力への対応にあたって必 要な事項を定めることを目的とします。 3. Scope This Policy applies to all operations of the Company and is applicable to all Employees engaged in the Company's operations (including Board members, auditors, staff, secondees, contract employees, temporary employees, etc., hereinafter referred to as "officers and employees"). 3. 対象 本規程は、当社のすべての事務を対象とし、当社の事務を行うすべての社員（取締役、監査役、職員、出向者、契約社員、 派遣社員等を含む。以下「役職員」という。）に適用する。 Anti-Social Forces Management Policy | Square | Square K.K. 5 Confidential and Not for Distribution 4. Definitions In these regulations, the definition of ASF is as specified in the following items: 1. Organized Crime Group: Refers to groups where members (including those from constituent organizations of the group) are likely to collectively or habitually engage in violent illegal activities. 2. Organized Crime Group Member: Refers to members of an organized crime group and those who have not surpassed five years since ceasing to be a member. 3. Quasi-Member of an Organized Crime Group: Refers to individuals who, although not members of an organized crime group, have relationships with them and are likely to engage in violent illegal activities using the power of the organized crime group, or those who supply funds, weapons, etc., to such groups, thereby cooperating with or being involved in their maintenance or operation. 4. Organized Crime Group-Related Company: Refers to companies where organized crime group members are substantially involved in management, companies managed by quasi-members or former organized crime group members that actively cooperate with or are involved in the maintenance or operation of organized crime groups by providing funds or other means, or companies that actively utilize organized crime groups in the execution of their business, thereby supporting their maintenance or operation. 5. Corporate Racketeers: Refers to individuals, such as corporate racketeers or "sokaiya," who threaten public safety by potentially engaging in violent illegal activities to seek illicit gains from companies. 6. Individuals Posing as Social Movements, etc: Refers to individuals who disguise or pose as engaging in social or political activities but threaten public safety by potentially engaging in violent illegal activities to seek illicit gains. 7. Special Intelligence Violence Groups: Refers to groups or individuals, other than those listed in items 1 through 6, who use the power of organized crime groups or have financial ties with them, forming the core of structural corruption. 8. Others Equivalent to the Above: Refers to individuals or entities that are equivalent to the aforementioned categories. 4. 定義 本規程において、反社会的勢力の定義は、以下の各号に定めるものとします。 Anti-Social Forces Management Policy | Square | Square K.K. 6 Confidential and Not for Distribution 1. 暴力団（その団体の構成員（その団体の構成団体の構成員を含む）が集団的にまたは常習的に暴力的不法行 為等を行うことを助長するおそれがある団体をいう。） 2. 暴力団員（暴力団の構成員および暴力団の構成員でなくなった日から五年を経過しない者をいう。） 3. 暴力団準構成員（暴力団以外の暴力団と関係を有する者であって、暴力団の威力を背景に暴力的不法行為等 を行うおそれがあるもの、または暴力団もしくは暴力団員に対し資金、武器等の供給を行うなど暴力団の維持 もしくは運営に協力し、もしくは関与するものをいう。） 4. 暴力団関係企業（暴力団員が実質的にその経営に関与している企業、準構成員もしくは元暴力団員が経営す る企業で、暴力団に資金提供を行うなど、暴力団の維持もしくは運営に積極的に協力しもしくは関与する企業、 または業務の遂行等において積極的に暴力団を利用し、暴力団の維持もしくは運営に協力している企業をい う。） 5. 総会屋等（総会屋や会社ゴロ等、企業等を対象に不正な利益を求めて暴力的不法行為等を行うおそれがあり、 市民生活の安全に脅威を与える者をいう。） 6. 社会運動等標ぼうゴロ（社会運動もしくは政治活動を仮装し、または標ぼうして、不正な利益を求めて暴力的不 法行為等を行うおそれがあり、市民生活の安全に脅威を与える者をいう。） 7. 特殊知能暴力集団等（第１号から第６号までに掲げる者以外の、暴力団との関係を背景にその威力を用い、ま たは暴力団と資金的なつながりを有し、構造的な不正の中核となっている集団、または個人をいう。） 8. その他前各号に準ずる者 5. Policy Requirements 5.1 Declaration of Policy on the Exclusion of Anti-Social Forces The Company recognizes that decisively severing and excluding relationships with ASF is essential to maintaining public trust and social credibility, as well as ensuring the appropriateness and soundness of our operations. We publicly declare the following policy regarding the exclusion of ASF both internally and externally. 1. The Company will not establish any relationships with ASF and will ensure the safety of Employees who deal with unjust demands from such forces. 2. To prevent damage caused by ASF, the Company will collaborate with external professional organizations such as the police, the Center for the Elimination of Violence, and lawyers, responding in an organized and appropriate manner. 3. The Company will not comply with any unjust demands from ASF and will take a resolute legal approach in response. 4. The Company will not provide funds to or engage in illicit transactions with ASF. documents.FC-023: U.S. Identity Verification Policy Square Last Approved: Dec 30, 2025 Effective Date: Dec 30, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This U.S. Identity Verification ("IDV") Policy ("Policy") is established by Square and applies to its U.S. operations. The Policy outlines Square's approach to IDV for each customer of Square's Financial Products. The KYC Program Manager or delegate is responsible for managing this Policy, with final approval from the Customer Onboarding and Appeals Program Lead. The purpose of this Policy is to facilitate compliance within all aspects of Block, Inc.'s Bank Secrecy Act ("BSA")/Anti-Money Laundering ("AML") Program, across Square's financial products, as well as its U.S. Know Your Customer ("KYC") Program Standard. Square's U.S. KYC Program Standard outlines the company's approach to ensuring the business reasonably knows its customers and assessing the risks involved in conducting business with them, including adherence to the Customer Identification Program ("CIP"). The IDV Policy details how customer information is collected and verified to establish identity as required by the CIP component of the KYC Program. Scope: This Policy is established by Square and is applicable to Square's Covered Products in the U.S. market. This Policy does not apply to products offered by other legal entities, including Square Financial Services, or other business units within Block, Inc., including Cash App, and Afterpay. Additionally, developer test accounts are outside the scope of this Policy. 1 Definitions: Terminology Definition Identity Verification ("IDV") Identity Verification ("IDV") is a risk-based method of collecting and confirming identity information to enable Square to form a reasonable belief that it knows the true identity of each customer. Covered Products Square defines its Covered Products and activities as products and services offered by Square that are required, whether due to regulatory, partner, or other obligations, to comply with Block, Inc.'s Bank Secrecy Act ("BSA")/Anti-Money Laundering ("AML") Program. Customer Identification A Customer Identification Program ("CIP"), a component of the Program ("CIP") Know Your Customer Program, is a requirement for United States ("U.S.") financial institutions to verify the identities of customers (both individuals and business entities) when they open new accounts. Beneficial Owner ("BO") Beneficial Owners ("BO") are individuals who either exercise substantial control over a legal entity customer or, directly or indirectly, own/control at least 25% of its ownership interests. 2. Policy Requirements Square is both directly and indirectly regulated in various jurisdictions according to the types of financial services and products offered and is subject to all applicable BSA/AML obligations. For the purposes of Square's U.S. products that do not require money transmission licenses, including its managed payments business, Square is not directly regulated. However, even where Square is not directly regulated, it is required to meet the same expectations of a regulated entity in commitment to its contractual obligations with acquiring partners. In order to fulfill these requirements with acquiring partners, Square must collect and verify certain information from its customers. Square's electronic IDV is performed automatically via direct application programming interface ("API") calls with vendors. 2 2.1 IDV Overview Before customers can use Square's financial products, Square verifies their identity using a combination of electronic identity verification ("eIDV") and document identity verification ("dIDV"), which are performed automatically via direct API calls with vendors. After Square verifies the customer's identity, their account is activated for payments processing. Only verified Square accounts can process payments and use Square's Financial Products. 2.2 Verification of Individuals and Sole Proprietors 2.2.1 Information Collection For identity verification of individuals or sole-proprietors, Square obtains the following information directly from customers, vendors, or a combination of both any information obtained from vendors will be pre-filled and confirmed by the customer for accuracy prior to submission). ● Individual's full legal name; ● Physical residential address; ● Social Security Number ("SSN") or Individual Taxpayer Identification Number ("ITIN"); and ● Date of birth ("DOB"). 2.2.2 Electronic Identity Verification (eIDV) To conduct comprehensive Electronic Identity Verification ("eIDV"), Square partners with select vendors to assess the information that Square collects from customers via attribute matching. Attribute matching compares the customer information collected during onboarding (legal name, DOB, SSN or ITIN, and address) with data provided by our vendors: LexisNexis, IDology, and Veriff. These vendors aggregate customer information records from local and international public and proprietary records. A match is determined by leveraging the data provided by the vendor to determine if the information provided matches a record. Strategy for Risk Mitigation and Controls Square uses the following controls to manage the risk associated with fraud, identity theft, and other illicit activity for eIDV 1. Vendor Verification - Square maintains a set of rules and configurations with its third-party vendors, which are designed to ensure Square takes a risk-based approach to verify the identity of the customer. The following scenarios result in a customer failing eIDV, based on the information collected from the customer: a. SSN is reported as deceased; b. Identity is reported as deceased; c. SSN was issued prior to DOB; and d. Individual is 13 or under. 3 2. Internal IDV Rules and Configurations - Square maintains a set of internal rules and configurations that are applied post-vendor approval. This allows Square to override the external vendor approval and deny the customer verification if needed. This could happen under the following circumstances: a. Collected age is less than 13 or greater than 113; or b. SSN not valid - SSN is a sequence of 4 or 9 digits, is not invalid 111111111, and does not start with three digits commonly used for ITINs 666, or numbers falling between 900999 3. Hardblock - Square has developed a process such that, once customer information (bank account, name, SSN, DOB, and address) are added to the hardblock database, any future account attempting to onboard with the same information is automatically deactivated. Information related to an account is typically added to the hardblock database when the account is deactivated by compliance for Terms of Service Violations or AML concerns. 2.2.3 Document Identity Verification (dIDV) If a customer's identity is not verified through eIDV, the customer may be prompted to go through Document Identity Verification ("dIDV"). A customer may also be prompted to go through dIDV if they did not meet the eIDV attribute matching criteria, if vendor data quality is low, or if risk is perceived as high. Document identity verification consists of the collection of government-issued photo identification and a live selfie, processed through a third party vendor. Strategy for Risk Mitigation and Controls Square uses the following controls to manage the risk associated with fraud, identity theft, and other illicit activity for dIDV 1. Vendor Verification - Square uses a third-party vendor, Veriff, to support the execution of the dIDV process. During the process, the customer is required to provide photos of their government-issued photo identification, as well as a live selfie. Veriff has risk mitigation features, including the ability to identify natural movements in the selfie and biometric analysis to account for common changes in an individual's appearance. 2. Internal dIDV Rules and Configurations - Square has created rules and policies to ensure the identity information provided during dIDV is appropriately matched to the name provided during eIDV. 2.2.4 Manual Verification Depending on the product, customers may be eligible for manual verification if they do not pass via dIDV or eIDV. Manual reviews may be available for other customers, at the discretion of the Compliance Operations Lead. If manual verification is not possible or is not successful, the customer account is not opened. 4 2.3 Business Verification 2.3.1 Information Collection Square requests the following information from business entity customers (i.e. those who are not individuals, sole proprietors, or single-member LLCs without an EIN ● Legal Business Name; ● Business Address (no PO boxes); ● Business Entity Type; ○ Corporation ○ LLC ○ Partnership ○ Charities ● Employer Identification Number ("EIN"); ● Trading name or doing business as ("DBA") name; and ● Personal information Name, Address, SSN, DOB for relevant Beneficial Owners, Directors or Account Holders 2.3.2 Verification of Business Entities Square partners with select vendors, like Persona and Dun & Bradstreet, to verify that the business information provided by a customer during onboarding Business Name, Address, and EIN matches an existing record. Beneficial Owners, Directors and Account Holders associated with a business are validated using the eIDV and dIDV methods described in Section 2.2.2 Electronic Identity Verification (eIDV). Strategy for Risk Mitigation and Controls Square has developed processes and the following controls to manage the risk associated with fraud, identity theft, and other illicit activity for Business Verification: 1. Internal IDV Rules and Configurations - Square maintains a set of internal rules and configurations. If the following criteria is met, the data is not sent to vendors: 1. EIN not valid - EIN must be 9 digits, and cannot be an invalid sequence of numbers 111111111. 2. Hardblock - Once a customer's information—such as account holder name, SSN, date of birth, and address—is ent
documents.FC-024: Bank Secrecy Act/Anti-Money Laundering & Countering the Financing of Terrorism Program Standard Block, Inc Last Approved: August 12, 2025 Effective Date: August 12, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: Money Laundering and terrorist financing are serious financial crimes with potentially serious social and financial effects. The laws of the U.S. government impose affirmative obligations on covered entities to implement systems and processes to prevent, detect, and report on a range of illicit activity, including money laundering, terrorist financing, and other criminal acts. Block, Inc. and its subsidiaries (hereinafter referred to as "Block" or the "Company") are committed to complying with anti-money laundering ("AML") and countering the financing of terrorism ("CFT") laws and regulations in all jurisdictions in which they operate, and to preventing the misuse of services and any other related involvement in criminal or unlawful activity. This Bank Secrecy Act/Anti-Money Laundering & Countering the Financing of Terrorism Program Standard ("Standard") is designed to operationalize requirements under U.S. laws and regulations and meet the minimum standards set out under Block's Global AML/CFT Policy. This Standard also forms part of Block's Anti-Financial Crime domain under the Block Compliance Policy Governance Framework. Block complies with a number of AML/CFT laws and regulations globally including, but not limited to, the Bank Secrecy Act (“BSAˮ). The U.S. Financial Crimes Enforcement Network (“FinCENˮ), a bureau of the U.S. Department of the Treasury, issues and enforces regulations under the BSA. Among other things, the BSA requires financial institutions, such as money services businesses (“MSBsˮ), to develop, implement, and maintain an effective BSA/AML Compliance Program (“Programˮ or “BSA/AML Programˮ). Scope: This Standard is applicable to Block and all its U.S. subsidiaries. The only exception is Square Financial Services which is a Utah state-chartered Industrial Bank federally regulated by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation and has its own Compliance Program, which includes its own BSA/AML Program, and Cash App Investing LLC, a licensed money transmitter and registered money service business subject to the Bank Secrecy Act ("BSA") and USA PATRIOT Act. Cash App Investing maintains its independent AML/CFT Program while leveraging the resources, technology, and sophistication of the enterprise-wide Block AML Program. Definitions: Terminology Definition Anti-Financial Crime A set of regulatory rules (including the BSA, USA PATRIOT Act, CTA, AMLA, etc.) and investigative techniques aimed at preventing and combating various illegal activities related to the financial system, including (but not limited to): Money Laundering; Terrorist Financing; Fraud; Identity Theft; Corruption; Sanctions Violations; Cybercrime; Tax Evasion. Customer Identification Program CIP comprises two elements: a) the collection of customer ("CIP") information Identity Collection); and b) verification Identity Verification) using reliable and independent sources to reasonably establish the identity of the customer. Covered Products Block defines its Covered Products as the products and services that Block offers pursuant to a license or registration and that are subject to BSA/AML related laws and regulations. Certain other products may also be considered Covered Products, such as where required under partner bank expectation and/or contractual obligation. Each Business Line is responsible for maintaining a documented and up-to-date list of products and services that meet this definition. Customer Due Diligence Including CIP, CDD involves the collection and risk-based ("CDD") verification of additional information from customers at onboarding, account setup, or on an ongoing basis. Enhanced Due Diligence ("EDD") EDD includes CDD plus additional collection, verification, or corroboration of customer or transactional information. This information is used to enhance the overall understanding of the customer and the activities the customer is engaged in where the customer has demonstrated potential higher AML risk. Local Laws may specify circumstances where EDD must be performed and the measures required. Know Your Customer ("KYC") KYC includes the process of establishing risk-based controls to identify and apply a risk-based approach to verifying the identity of Block's customers. The term KYC may also be referred to as Know Your Business KYB when dealing with non-individual entities or business customers. Integration As the final stage of money laundering, integration occurs when the criminal receives the proceeds of what now appear to be legitimate payments. The criminal can then use the funds as they wish. Terminology Definition Layering As the second stage of money laundering, layering is generally the use of many complex transactions to obscure the source of the criminal proceeds and make them appear legitimate. Money Laundering Money Laundering is the process of making criminal proceeds ('dirty' money) appear to have come from a legitimate source ('clean' money). Criminals do this by moving those proceeds through different entities, accounts and transactions where the funds are less likely to attract attention but are readily accessible when required. Money Transmitter A person that provides "money transmission services", which are generally defined under the BSA to mean acceptance of currency, funds, or other value that substitutes for currency from one person and the transmission of currency, funds, or other value that substitutes for currency to another location or person by any means. Placement As the first stage of money laundering, placement is the introduction of criminal proceeds into the financial system. Provider of Prepaid Access A participant in a prepaid program that agrees to serve as the principal conduit for access to information from its fellow program participants in which "prepaid access" is defined as access to funds or the value of funds that have been paid in advance and can be retrieved or transferred at some point in the future through an electronic device or vehicle, such as a card, code, electronic serial number, mobile identification number, or personal identification number. Terrorist Financing Terrorist financing is the solicitation, collection or delivery of funds to support terrorist acts or organizations. Unlike money laundering, which always involves the proceeds of a crime, funds to finance terrorism are often diverted from legitimate sources. Terrorists may also rely on laundered funds derived from criminal enterprises. 2. About Block 2.1 Blockʼs Money Services and Virtual Currency Business Activities Block engages in money services business activity by acting as a Money Transmitter and Provider of Prepaid Access. Block is registered as an MSB with FinCEN. Block has established procedures for the maintenance of the MSB registration with FinCEN and the state regulators. These procedures include, but are not limited to, registration renewal timelines and filing requirements for significant events. 2.2 Cash App Cash App is a Business Line that provides its customers access to a range of financial services, which may be accessed through its mobile application (and for some features, its website). Among other features, Cash App allows its customers to receive, transfer, and hold funds. Cash App customers may also have the ability to buy, sell, hold, receive, and transfer Bitcoin. Cash App partners with banks to enable certain features, such as a Visa Prepaid Debit Card ("Cash App Card") and loans. Customers are required to meet certain eligibility criteria in order to access many of these features. 2.3 Square Square is a merchant payment services platform that enables businesses of all sizes ("Sellers") to manage how they accept payments, bank, manage inventory and appointments, pay vendors and employees, send invoices, message customers, access credit such as loans and cash advances, organize multiple locations, and, among other things, engage in e-commerce. Sellers have access to Square's products through mobile and desktop applications and through use of Square's hardware products. Square partners with banks to enable certain products, such as Square Checking (business checking account) and Square Card (a debit card offered to Square Checking customers). 3. Mitigating Controls Block mitigates the money laundering risks associated with its business in several ways, including but not limited to: ● Assigning customer risk ratings and performing risk-based KYC, customer due diligence, and enhanced due diligence, as applicable, based on the potential AML/CFT risk that the customer presents; ● Prohibiting certain customers from processing payments due to unacceptable money laundering, reputational, or other risks and outlining these prohibitions in its terms of service and internal compliance policies; ● Blocking transactional activity for accounts with connections to other accounts, individuals, or devices with a known history of illicit activity within the Block system; ● Monitoring transactions using proprietary algorithms, including heuristic and machine-learning methodologies, to identify and prevent patterns of transactional behavior suggestive of money laundering or other suspicious activities; ● Monitoring transactional activity related to card payments associated with high risk jurisdictions, which may not otherwise be subject to the AML controls of U.S. financial institutions subject to the BSA; ● Screening customer and account data at onboarding and other points in the account lifecycle to prevent the onboarding or continued use of the product by previously identified bad actors; and ● Maintaining procedural controls that can identify and terminate high-risk accounts in real time or following appropriate investigations, including blocking any corresponding inflows or outf

Introduction Block, Inc., including its subsidiaries (herein referred to as “Block” or “Company”), has an obligation to ensure it is operating in accordance with the economic Sanctions laws and regulations of the jurisdictions in which it operates, including those administered by applicable Sanctions Administrators. Economic Sanctions is a broad term describing a foreign policy toolbox used to coerce, constrain, and signal bad behavior. Governments enact a myriad of Sanctions regimes through individual asset freezes, travel bans, export and import restrictions, financial prohibitions, and technical assistance prohibitions. Although the requirements of each Sanctions-related regulatory program is unique, regulations generally prohibit transacting with any embargoed or Sanctioned jurisdiction, individual, group, or entity. Scope All Cash Compliance personnel should be knowledgeable in Program content and are responsible for compliance with this Program in their related business activities. documents.FC-027: Anti-Money Laundering & Counter Terrorist Financing Standard Cash App Investing LLC Last Approved: Mar 27, 2026 Effective Date: Mar 27, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Anti-Money Laundering & Counter Terrorist Financing Program (“Programˮ) outlines Cash App Investing LLCʼs (“CAI,ˮ “Broker-Dealer,ˮ or “Firmˮ) approach to comply with applicable laws, regulations, and guidelines to monitor, detect, prevent, and report possible money laundering, financial crimes and terrorist financing. The BSA/AML Compliance Officer (“Officerˮ) is responsible for management of this Program. This Program ensures compliance with regulations administered by the Financial Industry Regulatory Authority (“FINRAˮ), the Bank Secrecy Act (“BSAˮ), and the Treasury Departmentʼs Office of Foreign Assets Control (“OFACˮ). This Program is risk-based and designed to address the risks of money laundering and terrorist financing as they relate to the characteristics of the customers CAI serves, where CAI customers are located, and the types of services CAI offers. CAI is a subsidiary of Block, Inc. (“Blockˮ), a licensed money transmitter and registered money service business subject to the Bank Secrecy Act (“BSAˮ) and USA PATRIOT Act requirements to establish and maintain an anti-money laundering (“AMLˮ) compliance program. While CAI maintains this independent Program, CAI leverages the resources, personnel, technology, and sophistication of the enterprise-wide Block AML program. The Firm is committed to substantive AML monitoring and continuous improvement in its AML program, including adequate oversight of Block enterprise-wide AML services. In both money laundering and terrorist financing, criminals may attempt to exploit loopholes and other weaknesses in the financial system to launder criminal proceeds or conduct other illegal activities. Both money laundering and terrorist financing are serious financial crimes with potentially devastating social and financial effects. Anti-Money Laundering and Counter Terrorist Financing Program | Cash App Investing LLC CAI reserves the right to investigate any known or suspected violation of this Program. Failure to comply with this Program may result in disciplinary action, including termination. Staff must be mindful that they may be subject to criminal liability if they are willfully blind to suspicious activity or should have known that an activity was suspicious. Willful blindness (deliberately ignoring facts that a reasonable person would consider suspicious) does not require active involvement in a money laundering scheme in order to create liability. CAI designed this Program to provide a framework for a robust AML program and to communicate at an organizational level CAIʼs commitment to a culture of effective AML compliance. This Program is intended to be supplemented by additional policies, programs and procedures where necessary. Some policies, programs or procedures are maintained by Block. References to “staff members'' throughout this document shall refer to all employees of CAI and/or Block and fixed-term and contingent workers who support the operations and/or subsidiaries covered by this Program, and include interns, temporary workers, consultants, vendors, third-party providers, and contractors. Scope: To address the money laundering concerns outlined above, the U.S. government has passed laws that both make money laundering a crime and additionally impose affirmative obligations upon covered entities. The Bank Secrecy Act, as amended by Title III of the USA PATRIOT Act (collectively, the “BSAˮ), relies on U.S. financial institutions to assist U.S. government agencies in detecting and preventing money laundering and terrorist financing.1 To accomplish these objectives, the BSA and FINRA Rule 3310 require broker-dealers to implement written risk-based AML programs that are reasonably designed to ensure compliance with all applicable BSA and FINRA requirements, while further imposing certain recordkeeping and reporting requirements.2 In addition to BSA and FINRA regulations, OFAC administers and enforces economic and trade sanctions against targeted countries, agencies, instrumentalities, controlled entities, and nationals of those countries, as well as other designated individuals, such as terrorists or narcotics traffickers.3 Although the specific requirements of each OFAC-related regulatory program are unique, all programs generally prohibit a U.S. person4, broadly defined, from transacting business with any embargoed or sanctioned country, individual, or entity. Further, OFAC guidance instructs financial institutions such as CAI to develop a risk-based approach to economic sanctions screening that is commensurate with the sanctions risks that are currently or will be posed.5 Definitions Terminology Definition AML Anti-Money Laundering BSA Bank Secrecy Act Report of International Transportation of Currency or Monetary CMIR Instruments FBAR Report of Foreign Bank and Financial Accounts 1 31 U.S.C. § 5311 2 31 U.S.C. § 5318(h) 3 31 C.F.R. § Part 500 et. seq. 4 31 C.F.R. § 560.314 5 See https://www.treasury.gov/resource-center/sanctions/Documents/matrix.pdf and OFAC FAQ 560. Apex also maintains an anti-money laundering/counter-terrorist financing and Apex sanctions compliance program. DriveWealth, LLC (“DriveWealthˮ). DriveWealth also maintains an anti-money laundering/counter-terrorist financing and DriveWealth sanctions compliance program. The process of raising money to carry out terrorist activities, the funds of which may be derived from unlawful or legitimate sources, such as personal donations and profits from businesses and charitable Terrorist Financing organizations. For Denylisted Cash App accounts with a CAI account, the customer is prevented from making further securities purchases, but is Denylist permitted to sell existing holdings. Anti-Money Laundering and Counter Terrorist Financing Program | Cash App Investing LLC 2. Program Requirements 2.1. Customers and Product 2.1.1. Customers The Firm is an introducing broker-dealer that enables customers to trade securities on a purely self-directed basis. The Firm makes its services available through an application available to customers of Cash App, a financial services platform offered by Block. Existing Cash App customers who wish to engage in securities transactions sign up for an account with the Firm through the Cash App mobile application. CAI only permits U.S. residents to open brokerage accounts, and limits brokerage accounts to one per customer by ensuring that the Social Security Number obtained is not already affiliated with another CAI account, with certain exceptions6. 2.1.2. Products and Securities CAI enables customers to buy or sell hundreds of equities on a self-directed basis. Specifically, the broker-dealer provides access to purchase highly liquid stocks, exchange traded funds (“ETFsˮ) and American Depository Receipts (“ADRsˮ) listed on the New York Stock Exchange (“NYSEˮ) or the National Association of Securities Dealers Automated Quotations System (“NASDAQˮ). The menu of securities available to CAI customers is further limited by additional established criteria;. please see CAIʼs Written Supervisory Procedures for full details. The CAI platform is characterized by relatively small accounts and transactions in highly liquid securities. CAI does not offer retirement accounts, joint accounts, or any account type other than individual brokerage accounts. CAI does not carry customer accounts or custody of any funds or securities, but instead introduces accounts on a fully disclosed basis to clearing firm partner(s), who are registered carrying broker-dealers and members of FINRA (“CAIIntroduced Accountsˮ). CAIIntroduced Accounts never contain free credit balances, and customers cannot directly transfer funds to or from these accounts. Rather, customers only fund securities transactions from their Cash App Balance7 held outside CAI and clearing firm partner(s) in the Cash App digital wallet and only receive proceeds from securities sales in this external Cash App Balance. All funds notionally flowing to and from CAIIntroduced Accounts represent the exact amounts to fund securities purchases and provide proceeds of sales. All actual movements of funds related to securities purchases and sales occur on an aggregate basis between CAIIntroduced Accounts and the Cash App Wallet. 2.2.
