Document Details 9 Bitcoin Travel Rule Program | Cash App 2 Confidential and Not for Distribution Definitions Terminology Definition Bank Secrecy Act (“BSA”) Passed in 1970 and amended several times, a U.S. law requiring financial institutions to assist U.S. government agencies in detecting and preventing money laundering. Eligible Customer To be eligible for increased limits, customers must meet defined criteria which has been approved in writing by the Head of Product Compliance. Financial Action Task The Financial Action Task Force (“FATF”) is the global money laundering Force (“FATF”) and terrorist financing watchdog. It sets international standards that aim to prevent these illegal activities and the harm they cause to society. Financial Institution Each agent, agency, branch, or office within the U.S. of any person doing business in one or more capacity as a bank, a broker or dealer in securities, a money services business, a casino, a commodities broker or dealer, among other things. Financial Crimes FinCEN is a bureau of the U.S. Department of the Treasury. FinCEN’s Enforcement Network mission is to safeguard the financial system from illicit use and combat (“FinCEN”) money laundering and promote national security through the collection, analysis, and dissemination of financial intelligence and strategic use of financial authorities.1 Staff Member All employees and fixed-term and contingent workers who support the operations and/or subsidiaries covered by this Program, and include interns, temporary workers, consultants, vendors, third-party providers, and contractors. Travel Rule BSA rule [31 C.F.R. 1010.410(f)]—often called the “Travel” rule—requires financial institutions to pass on certain information to the next financial institution, in certain funds transmittals involving more than one financial institution. Travel Rule Universal A platform that allows virtual asset service providers to securely send Solution Technology customer information as required by the Travel Rule. (“TRUST”) Virtual Asset A digital representation of value that can be digitally traded, or transferred, and can be used for payment or investment purposes. Virtual 1 https://www.fincen.gov/what-we-do Bitcoin Travel Rule Program | Cash App 3 Confidential and Not for Distribution assets do not include digital representations of fiat currencies, securities and other financial assets.2 Roles & Responsibilities Role Responsibility Product Compliance Provide advisory support to product and compliance to devise a strategy Management for compliance with the Travel Rule. Regulatory Counsel Provide legal requirements as it pertains to the Travel Rule to ensure compliance with applicable laws and regulations. Compliance Operations Own and execute the procedure to enable compliance with the Travel Rule. 2https://www.fatf-gafi.org/content/dam/fatf-gafi/guidance/Updated-Guidance-VA-VASP.pdf.coredownload.in line.pdf Bitcoin Travel Rule Program | Cash App 4 Confidential and Not for Distribution 1. Introduction Cash App is committed to complying with applicable laws, regulations, and guidelines to prevent, monitor for, detect and report possible money laundering, financial crimes, and terrorist financing. The Bitcoin Travel Rule Program outlines the strategy and method by which Cash App meets the Financial Crimes Enforcement Network (“FinCEN”) and other regulators’ expectations set forth by the Funds Transfer Rule, often referred to as the “Travel Rule” 31 C.F.R. 1010.410(f). 2. Purpose Cash App conducts business in compliance with all applicable laws and regulations. The framework for this Program applies to the use of virtual currency (e.g. Bitcoin) on Cash App, including where the virtual assets are transmitted to a Virtual Asset Service Provider (herein referred to as “VASP”) in the amount of $3,000 or more. Cash App has adopted this framework as a matter of risk management with respect to destinations for withdrawals at or above the Travel Rule threshold. 3. Scope This Program is established by Cash App, U.S., a line of business under Block, Inc., and is applicable to its Bitcoin services. The Travel Rule requires that financial institutions, including money transmitters, collect, retain, and transmit information on funds transferred to another financial institution. Specifically, the rule requires that the information must be sent in the transmittal order. FinCEN guidance clarifies that the Travel Rule applies to virtual currency transactions.3. FinCEN has stated its expectation that VASPs comply with the Funds Travel Rule 31 CFR 1010.410(f), which requires firms like Cash App to collect and retain the specified information from customers for transmissions of virtual assets that are equivalent to $3,000 or more. Information to be sent with transmittal order: ● Sender’s name; 3 FinCEN 2019 Virtual Currency Guidance. Bitcoin Travel Rule Program | Cash App 5 Confidential and Not for Distribution ● Sender’s account number; ● Sender’s physical address/ID number/wallet address/date and place of birth; ● Amount of transaction; ● Execution date; ● Identity of recipient’s financial institution; and ● To the extent Cash App receives the following information: ○ Recipient’s name; ○ Recipient’s physical address; ○ Recipient’s account number if available. 4. Program Requirements 4.1 Travel Rule Applicability: On-Chain Bitcoin Withdrawals to VASPs Cash App allows customers to purchase, sell, send (peer-to-peer), withdraw and deposit Bitcoin via Cash App. To enable Bitcoin withdrawals and deposits, customers must first undergo document identification verification. Cash App maintains a set of withdrawal limits that reduces the applicability of the Travel Rule to its products and features; most customers may only withdraw up to $2,000 per day. Eligible Customers are subject to higher limits, and Cash App has integrated with the Travel Rule Universal Solution Technology (“TRUST”) to facilitate Travel Rule compliance for such higher limit transactions. TRUST is a Travel Rule solution platform that facilitates the sharing of customer information with other member (and non-member) VASPs. By integrating with TRUST, when transmitting a withdrawal, the receiving VASP, also a TRUST member, confirms ownership of the wallet address, which then triggers the secure sending of the requisite customer information in a message to the receiving VASP. In turn, when Cash App receives a Bitcoin deposit from a TRUST member, that transaction is accompanied by the sender’s personally identifiable information. FinCEN expects VASPs like Cash App to maintain Travel Rule documentation with regard to self-hosted wallets, but Cash App is not required to transmit the information anywhere, because there is no other financial institution involved in the transaction. For withdrawals to another VASP, FinCEN expects the sending institution (Cash App) to transmit Travel Rule information to the receiving institution. Bitcoin Travel Rule Program | Cash App 6 Confidential and Not for Distribution 4.2 On-Chain Bitcoin Withdrawals to Self-hosted Wallets Travel Rule reporting obligations in the U.S. are typically understood to not apply to transactions involving self-hosted wallets. Since self-hosted wallets do not typically meet the definition of financial institution,4 Cash App has determined that, while the reporting aspect of the Travel Rule may not apply to transfers to self-hosted wallets,5 the recordkeeping aspects may still apply, so Cash App must retain information, even when not transmitted onwards.6 Cash App will evaluate, on an ad hoc basis, requests from customers who intend to transmit more than $10,000/daily in Bitcoin. Cash App’s Compliance Operations team is responsible for implementing and maintaining the process to comply with this Travel Rule program. Compliance Operations maintains a dedicated email address for Travel Rule correspondence with the receiving VASP. Please refer to the Bitcoin (BTC) Funds Transfer EDD Procedure for more details on the process. 5. Document Management 5.1 Document Owner This document is owned by the CashApp Bitcoin Product Compliance Manager or its designee. 5.2 Review and Approval This document shall be reviewed and approved at least annually and on an ad-hoc basis in accordance with the Compliance Document Approval Matrix. 6. Training All relevant personnel will receive training at least annually covering applicable requirements and guidance to implement this Program effectively. All training activities are monitored by the Compliance Team and are outlined in the Compliance Training Program. 4 31 CFR 1010.100(t) and 31 CFR 1010.410(f). 5 Although FinCEN has not specifically addressed the Travel Rule in the context of self-hosted wallets, the global standard setting body, the Financial Action Task Force, released a standard generally mirroring the Travel Rule for transactions over $1,000 involving VASPs like Cash App. FATF guidance states that VASPs need not transmit such information when sending digital assets from VASPs to unhosted wallets. Even if not required to transmit that information, however, the FATF guidance advises that the VASP should otherwise “adhere[] to the requirements” of its version of the Travel Rule—that is, the VASP should obtain and retain accurate originator and beneficiary information, and make it available on req
documents.FC-029: Compliance Real Time Transaction Blocking Program Cash App | US Last Approved: Aug 19, 2025 Effective Date: Aug 19, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: Cash App is committed to complying with applicable laws, regulations, and guidelines to monitor, detect, prevent, and report possible money laundering, financial crimes, and terrorist financing. The Real-time Transaction Blocking ("RTB") Program ("Program") is an additional component to the existing Transaction Monitoring ("TM") and Unacceptable Use Policy ("UUP") Programs. This Program provides a mechanism to evaluate attempted transactions in real-time, automatically preventing or blocking them if specific compliance concerns are detected. This is a 'preventive' program that complements the 'detective' TM and UUP programs. Any transactional activity not assessed by the RTB process will still be subject to detection by the downstream TM and UUP monitoring rules. This Program is established and maintained to help proactively reduce the overall risk of the Cash App platform by preventing suspected illicit, suspicious and/or terms of service violations activity from occurring on the platform. Cash App utilizes rules that consist of proprietary algorithms (machine-learning, heuristics or some combination thereof) to evaluate and block transactions in real-time. These rules are established and maintained by the Risk Machine Learning team. Any new rules or recommended changes to the existing rules are reviewed and approved by the Financial Crimes Detections program and in accordance with the Model Change Management Process. The Program is applicable to transaction blocking rules targeted at Compliance Concerns. Scope: This Program is established by Cash App and is applicable to all Cash App products in the United States. Definitions: Terminology Definition Real time Transaction Blocking A system that evaluates a transaction in flight and makes a ("RTB") System decision to block transaction based on the rule output. Good Customer Any user of the platform that is not involved in perpetrating fraud, scam, illegal/suspicious activity or Terms of Service violations in any form or fashion. Good Transaction Any transaction on the platform that is not involved in perpetrating fraud, scam, illegal/suspicious activity or Terms of Service violations in any form or fashion. Customer) Insult Any negative action on a good customer account or action that ends up negatively impacting that customers' experience or ability to transact on the platform. Compliance Concern A type of bad activity that Compliance actively monitors/seeks to prevent. (e.g., illegal drug sales, money laundering, adult services). Staff Member All employees and fixed-term and contingent workers who support the operations and/or subsidiaries covered by this Program, and include interns, temporary workers, consultants, vendors, third-party providers, and contractors. 2. Program Requirements 2.1 Process Presentation Fig. 1 - Real-time transaction evaluation and blocking system Fig. 2 - Offline rule performance measurement loop 2.2 Transaction Evaluation, Blocking and Attribution RTB is specific to a particular payment type (e.g., a fiat peer to peer payment or a fiat remittance payment type). Additionally, different rules may be targeted at one or more Compliance Concerns (e.g., Prostitution prevention rule or a Drug trafficking prevention rule) for the same payment type. Multiple rules may even target the same Compliance Concern. Therefore, this Program will include multiple rules across multiple payment types and Compliance Concerns combined. Each payment type may have multiple rule evaluations and each rule can make a decision to block independently of other rules. When multiple rules evaluate a payment type, the decision to block will be true if any of the rules decide to block the payment (aka OR mechanism). If multiple rules make a decision to block, the block will be attributed to the rule corresponding to the highest risk Compliance Concern for metrics tracking purposes. 2.3 Quality Control RTB rules aim to maximize the number of 'bad' transactions blocked; however, these rules are likely to have false positives that result in 'good' transactions (aka 'insults') getting wrongly blocked. Therefore, it is also important to track the false positive rates of these rules on an ongoing basis to take any corrective actions as necessary. 2.4 Key Metrics The Program seeks to maximize the RTB rules' Recall performance ( aka the true positive rate). Since the rules have to balance against 'insults', Recall is typically optimized over iterative rule improvements and tuning. Each rule (or set of rules) is also designed (and continuously monitored) to keep insult rates within a predetermined tolerance level. The key metrics are - Recall = number of True Positives / number of True Positives + False Negatives) Insult Rate = 1 - Precision) * Block Rate Block Rate = number of transaction attempts blocked / total number of transactions Precision = number of True Positives / number of True Positives + False Positives) The insult tolerance for a specific rule (or set of rules) is determined on a case by case basis in consultation with Compliance Detections Program and Business leadership and is set based on the risk severity of the Compliance Concern being targeted by the rule. 2.5 Compliance Specialist Review The Program will rely on Compliance Specialists to conduct reviews to track rule performance on an ongoing basis. Since the rule actions are automated (in real time), the reviews performed by Compliance Specialists will be conducted periodically through offline processes (typically weekly, biweekly or a monthly cadence). Depending on the risk severity of the Compliance concern being targeted, either a representative sample or one hundred percent of the transaction blocks are reviewed by humans. The decisions from these reviews will form the basis for generating true and false positive labels required to assess the performance of a specific rule (or set of rules) on an ongoing basis. Reviews may also include "below the line" ("BTL") alerts (i.e. transactions that were not actually blocked by the rule) required to estimate model Recall. As part of the review, the agent will take all the necessary actions including escalation to the SAR team, as appropriate. 2.6 Tuning Criteria Tuning criteria is trigger based (as opposed to cadence based). The rule performance is tracked on an ongoing basis and failing to meet set performance targets will result in a review and possible rule re-tuning (which may include an underlying machine learning model rebuild). Additionally, a rule may be pro-actively re-tuned to improve the target metric (i.e. Recall) iteratively even if the insult rates are below the predetermined tolerance levels. 2.7 Rule Audit Every transaction that is scored by a Program rule will log input signal values (this includes both the heuristics and machine learning model input signals) and the rule output (score and decision). This is maintained for replayability of the decision for audit reasons. 3. Compliance & Enforcement 3.1 Roles and Responsibilities ● Compliance Operations: ○ Review (sample of) alerts and investigate associated activity; and Decision alerts and escalate activity as necessary. ● Risk Machine Learning: ○ Maintenance of the Program document; Establish and maintain rules, parameters and thresholds; Monitor rule performance on an ongoing basis; Emergency rule shut off; Retune the rule as necessary; and Provide reporting to senior leadership on model performance and enhancement. Help determine the insult tolerance for the rules; and Act in a consultative capacity around best practices for RTB rules and systems. ● Detections Program: ○ Oversight of the program and process for the RTB rules 3.2 Monitoring and Reporting On an ongoing basis, the Program Owner and/or their designee monitors activities for compliance and objective achievement. As outlined in the Compliance Management System Policy, Compliance will provide regular updates, as applicable, to the Block, Inc. Board and executive management. Reporting may cover specific information related to: ● Policies and standards ● Procedures ● Audit/exam findings ● Issues management ● Training ● Complaints 3.3 Record Retention All Documents are retained according to the longer of: ● Block's Data Classification & Handling Policy ● Applicable regulatory requirements 3.4 Exceptions Exception requests must be pre-approved by the Head of Automation ML - Risk, Block or their designee. Staff Members may submit exception requests for review at go/policyhelp. 4. Questions & Contact Information Questions: For questions or clarifications about this Program, please contact the Program Owner. Review related documents: ● Transaction Monitoring Modeling Program ● Block Change Management Procedure 5. Document Details & Revisions Review and Approval: This document shall be reviewed and approved at least annually and on an ad hoc basis in accordance with the Compliance Document Approval Matrix. Megan Gonzales Head of Financial Scheduled No material Aug 19, 2025 Crimes Review updates. documents.FC-031: Identity Verification Standard Cash App US Last Approved: Dec 15, 2025 Effective Date: Dec 15, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Identity Verification Standard (“Standardˮ) is established by Cash App, and is applicable to the Cash App business in the United States. This Standard is designed to define Cash Appʼs strategy to obtain and verify a customerʼs identity. Identity Verification (“IDVˮ) is a process that allows Cash App to maintain compliance with its Know Your Customer (“KYCˮ) Policy, which establishes Cash Appʼs requirements for complying with applicable laws, regulation, and guidelines to monitor, detect, and prevent identity theft, money laundering, terrorist financing, and other types of illegal or illicit activity. In order to protect customers, prevent identity theft, and combat other fraud risk, Cash App should not allow the continued use of a Cash App account if there are concerns related to the authenticity of the identity submitted during the IDV process. Concerns around authenticity may include but are not limited to: synthetic identities, the fraudulent use of another personʼs identity, and use of fraudulent, forged, or inauthentic documents. If these red flags are identified after onboarding and verification, the account should be handled with the appropriate corresponding adverse action. Cash App conducts business in compliance with all applicable laws and regulations. The purpose of this Standard is to document Cash Appʼs strategy to ensure adherence to laws and regulations as outlined in the Cash App KYC Policy. Scope: This Standard is established by Cash App and is applicable to all subsidiaries used for the Cash App business, or Cash App subsidiary in the United States. Definitions: Terminology Definition Electronic Identity Verification Customer submitted information is checked against data (“eIDVˮ) sources in a waterfall method of third party eIDV vendors to determine if the identity information collected can be verified Document Identity Verification dIDV involves the collection of a government-issued picture (“dIDVˮ) ID and live selfie that is compared to: 1 the likeness of the selfie to the picture ID to ensure it is the same individual; and 2 the submitted eIDV information to ensure it is the same individual. Business Identity bIDV involves the collection and verification of customer Verification (“bIDVˮ) business information to comply with Know Your Business guidelines and onboard the platform Merchant Category Code A code assigned to businesses during the Know your (“MCCˮ) Business (“KYBˮ) process, serving as a basis for evaluating customer risk. A standardized code that classifies the types of goods and services a merchant sells and is a key piece of info for payment processing. Social Security Number A unique 9-digit number that identifies an individual or natural person in the United States. Taxpayer Identification Number A unique 9-digit number used by the Internal Revenue Employer Identification Number Service in the administration of tax laws. (“EINˮ) A unique 9-digit federal tax ID number for businesses. Staff Members Employees and fixed-term and contingent workers who support the operations and/or subsidiaries covered by this Standard, and include interns, temporary workers, consultants, vendors, third-party providers, and contractors. 2. Cash App Requirements 2.1. Cash App Account Onboarding To sign up for a Cash App account, a prospective customer is prompted to provide: ● Legal first and last name; ● Phone number or email address; ● Date of birth; ● ZIP code1 ; ● Cash Tag; ● PIN; and ● Debit card (optional) 1 If the customer is prompted to complete IDV during account onboarding, Cash App follows its address collection method through the IDV process and does not additionally prompt the customer for their ZIP code. The customer is required to verify the phone number or email address through a one-time passcode sent by Cash App. 2.2. Graduated IDV Process Cash App verifies customersʼ identity through a combination of electronic IDV (“eIDVˮ) and document IDV (“dIDVˮ) and maintains a risk-based, graduated approach to the IDV process. 2.3. Restricted Accounts & Unrestricted Accounts Prior to going through IDV, a Cash App customer may maintain a “Restricted account.ˮ With a Restricted account, a customer may send and receive peer-to-peer (“P2Pˮ) payments at lower limits, and use Cash App Pay prior to going through IDV. Restricted accounts may send P2P and Cash App Pay up to $1,000 and receive P2P payments up to $1,000 within any 30 day period, and up to $1,500 in their lifetime. Restricted accounts may not send money from their stored balance; payments must be sent from and withdrawn to a linked financial instrument from a U.S. depository institution. The $1,000 send and receive limit is aggregated by a customerʼs mobile device and linked financial instrument. A customer is considered to have an “Unrestricted accountˮ when the customer successfully passes IDV. Unrestricted accounts may send money from their stored balance, have access to a broader set of products and features, and have higher transaction limits compared to Restricted accounts. 2.4. Electronic Identity Verification 2.4.1 Customer Information Obtained As part of the eIDV process, Cash App obtains the following identification information: ● Legal first and last name; ● Date of Birth (“DOBˮ); ● Social Security Number (“SSNˮ) or Individual Taxpayer Identification Number (“ITINˮ); and ● Address Cash App may also obtain a photo of an accepted, unexpired government issued ID, as well as a selfie in cases where the customerʼs identity is not verifiable through electronic methods, or when required to use certain Cash App features (e.g., Bitcoin deposits and withdrawals or wires). 2.4.2 Waterfall Method with Third Party Vendors To support its eIDV process, Cash App deploys a waterfall methodology using four vendors. This process compares the customerʼs personal information collected in-app (legal name, DOB, SSN or ITIN with data provided by Cash Appʼs vendors: IDology, Transunion (“TLOˮ)2, LexisNexis, and Socure (including the use of the Social Security Administrationʼs (“SSAˮ) data source, Electronic 2 TLO does not support ITINs. For TIN attempts where the last-4 digits are provided, all vendors are called. However, for full-9 digit attempts, in which validation confirms an ITIN, TLO is skipped in the waterfall. Consent Based Social Security Number Verification (“eCBSVˮ). These vendors aggregate customer information records from public and proprietary records across sources such as credit bureaus, telecom customer data, and higher education institutions, with exception of the eCBSV which is completed via a call to the SSA by way of Socure. This process consists of determining a canonical set of verified data about a customer based on a hierarchy of vendors. Cash App does not mix and match responses from multiple vendors to complete verification. A match is determined by leveraging the data provided by the vendor to determine if the information provided matches a record. 2.4.3 Strategy for Risk Mitigation and Controls Cash App has developed processes and controls to manage the risk associated with fraud, identity theft, and other illicit activity through the design of the eIDV process. 1. Vendor Verification - Cash App maintains a set of rules and configurations with its third-party vendors, which are designed to ensure Cash App adequately manages the risk of fraud, identity theft and other illicit activity through its eIDV process. The following scenarios result in a customer failing eIDV based on the information collected from the customer, and are ineligible for dIDV, but may maintain a Restricted account: ● SSN or ITIN matched to a deceased identity - If the SSN or ITIN matches a deceased identity on a last 4 SSN or ITIN attempt, along with the Name and DOB, the account and the full 9 SSN or ITIN are denylisted. If the SSN or ITIN matches with a deceased identity on a full 9 SSN or ITIN attempt, the account is denylisted (along with the SSN even if the name and DOB do not match. This restriction is appealable by the customer. ● SSN or ITIN mismatch - vendor match on legal name and DOB but mismatch on full 9 SSN or ITIN. Any of the following will result in automatic failure of eIDV, and the customer may be eligible for dIDV, if they do not attempt or cannot pass dIDV, they may maintain a Restricted account: ● Vendor match on legal name and SSN or ITIN, but mismatch on DOB3; ● Vendor match on DOB and SSN or ITIN, but mismatch on name; or ● Customer inputs a valid SSN or ITIN that is not associated with a vendor maintained identity. 2. Internal IDV Rules and Configurations - Cash App maintains a set of internal rules and configurations that operate in addition to the vendor verification process. These rules and configurations are applied post-vendor approval, which allows Cash App to override the external vendor approval and deny the customer verification if needed. For example: a. Collected age is less than 18 or greater than 13 b. SSN or ITIN not valid - SSN or ITIN, a sequence of 4 or 9 digits, is not invalid (e.g.,111111111, 3 In this case, if the customer does not pass the SSA check, they will not be eligible for dIDV. c. SSN Denylist 3. Vendor Validation - Cash Appʼs third-party identity verification vendors return response codes and raw data to Cash App. The codes and the raw data are ingested for comparison of the customer provided information. Cash App conducts a synchronous validation check of vendor response codes during identity verification by completing a check on the code returned by the vendor, the raw data returned by the vendor, and the customer provided information. If the synchronous check fails, meaning the response code does not match the raw data (e.g., response code indicates an SSN or ITIN exact match, but the raw data and customer input SSN or ITIN do not match) Cash Ap

documents.FC-033: Know Your Customer Standard Cash App US Last Approved: Dec 23, 2025 Effective Date: Dec 23, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: In accordance with the Block, Inc. (herein referred to as the "Company" or "Block") BSA/AML Policy, the US Cash App ("Cash") Know Your Customer ("KYC") Standard has been established to ensure that Cash App reasonably understands who its customers are, and the risks posed by those customers. The Customer Onboarding and Management team ("COM") has been established to oversee implementation and monitoring of KYC processes, controls and procedures. Compliance is responsible for implementing procedures to ensure proper information or documentation is obtained and stored for each customer file. A risk-based approach is used to ensure customers identified as posing a heightened risk are subject to Enhanced Due Diligence ("EDD"), allowing the business to further understand the risks the customer poses and establish processes to mitigate those risks. The cornerstone of a strong BSA/AML compliance program is the adoption and implementation of risk-based due diligence policies, procedures, and processes for all customers, particularly those that present a higher risk for money laundering and terrorist financing. In order to achieve this, Cash App has designed and implemented a customer risk rating program that assesses a customer's profile, assigns risk ratings, and monitors the customers' risk levels. The customer risk rating ("CRR") program enables Cash App to apply the appropriate risk-based controls to mitigate exposure to money launderers, terrorist financing, and financial crime. Improper assessment of a customer's risk can have a cascading effect, creating deficiencies in multiple areas of internal controls and resulting in an overall weakened AML compliance program. The KYC program captures Customer Due Diligence ("CDD"), Enhanced Due Diligence ("EDD"), Periodic Due Diligence, and Ongoing Due Diligence to ensure Cash App has a clear understanding of its customer base and continuously evaluates risks associated with money laundering, terrorist financing and other financial crimes. It also establishes consistent risk-based monitoring controls on the platform, including KYC, Know Your Business ("KYB"), Politically Exposed Person ("PEP"), Source of Wealth/ Source of Funds ("SoW/SoF"), Adverse media screening, KYC Refresh and Section 314(a) screening. Scope: All Cash App Compliance personnel should be knowledgeable in program content and are responsible for compliance with this Program in their related business activities. Cash App employees should have access to policies that affect their jobs and understand their responsibilities to perform their work in an ethical and legally compliant manner. Business units engaged in the development of new products and services, whether resulting from internal product development or merger and acquisition activities, must consider this Program. Standard Requirements 2.1 Customer Onboarding Controls Cash App maintains a comprehensive inventory of product controls documented within its controls library that help mitigate risks across its platform. These controls extend across multiple customer touchpoints within Cash Appʼs platform including account creation, financial instrument linking, account access, transactions, investments. Additionally, Cash App has implemented onboarding controls and ongoing monitoring to reasonably understand who the customer is, the nature and purpose of activity on the platform, and to identify and mitigate reputational and financial crimes related risk. During onboarding, customers provide an alias1 (phone number or email address), legal name (first and last name, which is also used as the customerʼs display name), zip code2, and a $cashtag. A customer may also link a debit card to their account. When a debit card is linked to the customers account through Plaid, Cash App collects the customers bank account name, mailing address, phone number and email address which is surfaced for specialists during reviews. At onboarding, customers are prompted for their legal name (referred to as “Unverified Legal Nameˮ). This name is used during the customerʼs first attempt at electronic identity verification (eIDV). If the customer does not pass eIDV on their first attempt, they are provided the opportunity to change their name on the second attempt, and any subsequent eIDV attempts after it resets3. However, the customer is only allowed to make minor changes like adding, changing, or deleting up to three characters. If the change is greater than three characters, they are prompted for dIDV. The name on their document must match the name provided by the customer in any identity verification (“IDVˮ) attempt where they provided their full SSN. Cash App has introduced additional controls for restricted/unverified accounts. To mitigate risk exposure, these controls include aggregating monthly transaction limits for accounts linked to the same device. Additionally, unverified customers are restricted to using a maximum of five active devices for transactions, and collectively these accounts cannot exceed $1,000 in transactions within a 30 day rolling basis. If the account limit is exceeded, customers must undergo IDV to 1 The alias must be verified using a one-time passcode (OTP) 2 For sign-ups in Florida customers provide their full address 3 60 day reset period complete transactions. Furthermore, restricted/unverified accounts4 have a lifetime limit of $1,500, applied individually rather than aggregated across devices. If identity verification is not completed once the lifetime limit is met, access to the account will be restricted to only allow the withdrawal of funds. These measures prevent perpetual unverified states and promote a higher number of verified accounts in the Cash App ecosystem. Customers seeking access to certain products are required to successfully pass the IDV flow. During this process, Cash App obtains the customerʼs legal name, date of birth, the last four digits of their Social Security Number or Individual Tax Identification Number5 and physical address. If the customer is required to be verified through document identity verification (dIDV), an unexpired government issued ID and selfie is required. A customer can be verified through eIDV or document identity verification processes. eIDV involves electronically validating customer-provided information against vendor sources6 to verify their identity and dIDV7 requires the collection and verification of official documents, such as a government-issued ID, and a selfie to verify the information provided during onboarding. Additional sign-up controls prohibit the use of VoIP phone numbers, device emulators, and block account creation from outside the US (“VPNˮ) as follows: ● Accounts may not be created using a Voice over Internet Protocol (“VOIPˮ) phone number; ● An emulated device can not be used to create a new account or log into an existing account; and ● Cash App blocks account creation if device signals indicate that the customer is outside the US (e.g., IP address, device time zone, device SIM card). Cash App has instituted measures to remove bad actors from the platform and prevent them from bypassing existing controls through denylist propagation. This control extends across customer assets. Those assets include: social security number or individual tax identification number, financial instrument, mobile device, phone number, and email address. To mitigate risk exposure, a social security number or individual tax identification number can be linked to a maximum of 5 active accounts. A financial instrument can also be linked to a lifetime maximum of 5 accounts.

documents.FC-035: Anti-Money Laundering & Counter-Terrorism Financing Risk Awareness Training Program Square | Square AU Pty Ltd Last Approved: Mar 26, 2026 Effective Date: Mar 26, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: The Anti-Money Laundering and Counter Terrorism Financing AML/CTF Risk Awareness Training Program (the “Programˮ) is a mandated part of Square AU Pty Ltdʼs (“Square AUˮ) AML/CTF Program , as required by AUSTRAC under the AML/CTF Act. It is central to Square AUʼs effort to protect its business from being used to facilitate money laundering, terrorism financing and proliferation financing (“PFˮ). The purpose of this Program is to equip Employees with the necessary knowledge and understanding to effectively identify and manage risks associated with money laundering, terrorism financing and proliferation financing. Scope: This Program is established by Square AU and applicable to the Square AU products through which Square AU provides Designated Services defined under the AML/CTF Act, as specified in the Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorism Financing Program. This Program is managed and maintained by the Square AU Compliance team and sets out the approach to design and execute Square AUʼs AML/CTF Risk Awareness Training Program. The Square AU Compliance Team and the Block Compliance Training Team collectively administrate the AML/CTF Risk Awareness Training Program, and ensure timely completion and tracking of the training. Definitions: Terminology Definition Anti-Money A set of regulatory rules and investigative techniques aimed at preventing Laundering and combating various illegal activities related to the financial system (“AMLˮ) 1 Counter - A set of regulatory rules and investigative techniques designed to detect, Terrorism prevent and disrupt the flow of funds of terrorist organisations and activities. Financing (“CTFˮ) Proliferation The act of providing funds or financial services used, in whole or in part, for Financing (“PFˮ) the manufacture, acquisition, possession, development, export, trans-shipment, brokering, transport, transfer, stockpiling or use of nuclear, chemical or biological weapons and their means of delivery. The Anti-Money As part of its broader regulatory compliance obligations, Square AU Pty Ltd Laundering and (“Square AUˮ) is required to comply with the Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terroris Counter-Terrorism Financing Act 2006 Cth, as amended from time to time. m Financing Act The AML/CTF Act is designed to deter, detect and disrupt Money Laundering 2006 (“AML/CTF (“MLˮ) and Terrorism Financing (“TFˮ). Actˮ) AUSTRAC The Australian Transaction Reports and Analysis Centre (“AUSTRACˮ) performs a dual role as AML and CTF regulator and financial intelligence unit. Employees (also Means an individual who is directly employed by Square AU under a contract referred as Staff) of employment (whether full-time, part-time, or casual), and who performs duties for or on behalf of Square AU. Personnel Means any individual employed or otherwise engaged by Square AU, including persons deployed, seconded, contracted or supplied through an Outsourced Service Provider, who perform or will perform functions relevant to Square AUʼs obligations under the AML/CTF Act. Service Level Defines the expected review period for cases and other responsibilities of Agreements the Compliance Australia Analysts working on alerts. (“SLAˮ) 2. Program Requirements 2.1. Overview The AML/CTF Risk Awareness Training program provides training at appropriate intervals, tailored to the specific ML/TF risks faced by Square AU, and is updated regularly to incorporate current obligations and regulatory changes. It is also tailored to Square AUʼs product offerings. Training is risk-based, role-specific and proportionate to the level of ML/TF/PF exposure associated with the Employeeʼs role. The Program is reviewed at least annually and updated to reflect: ● Legislative amendments ● AML/CTF Rule changes 2 ● AUSTRAC regulatory guidance ● Internal risk assessment updates ● Emerging ML/TF/PF typologies ● Enforcement outcomes and regulatory expectations Specifically, the Program aims to help Employees to understand: ● What is money laundering, terrorism financing and proliferation financing risks and obligations ; ● Politically Exposed Person PEP requirements and enhanced customer due diligence ECDD triggers; ● Tipping-off prohibitions under the AML/CTF Act; ● Suspicious Matter Reporting SMR obligations; ● Regulatory regimes in Australia, including AML/CTF and Sanctions; ● Designated Services Square provides; ● Key elements of the AML/CTF Program; ● Square AUʼs obligations under the AML/CTF Act and Rules; ● Consequences of non-compliance; ● Type of ML/TF Risks that Square AU might face and the potential consequences of those risks; ● Square AUʼs AML/CTF policies and procedures; and ● Processes and procedures that are relevant to an employeeʼs responsibility for AML/CTF compliance. The Program comprises three key components to create a comprehensive learning approach that caters to different employee levels. 2.2. Enterprise-Wide Mandatory Compliance Training This foundational component of the Program ensures that all Employees, regardless of their roles, receive comprehensive AML/CTF training. The enterprise-wide mandatory compliance training is administered through Blockʼs Learning Management System (“LMSˮ), supported by the Block Compliance Training Team. The training content is tailored to reflect Square AUʼs designated services, ML/TF/PF risk assessment outcomes, and relevant obligations applicable. 2.2.1. Initial AML/CTF Risk Awareness Training An initial AML/CTF risk awareness training is administered by the Block Compliance Training Team. Key aspects of this initial training are listed below: 3 ● It is delivered online to all new employees, including full time Employees, interns and contractors; ● It is Intended to provide a general introduction and exploration of Square AUʼs AML/CTF obligation and legislative requirements; and ● It must be completed within 30 calendar days of the employeeʼs start date. 2.2.2. Periodic AML/CTF Risk Awareness Training Session Periodic AML/CTF risk awareness training is delivered annually online to all existing Block full time Employees, interns and contractors. 2.2.3. Training Records of the Enterprise-wide Mandatory Compliance Training Training records of the enterprise-wide mandatory compliance training are maintained and stored in accordance with Blockʼs record retention standards. Training records include: ● Training content, including versions of training material; ● Records of training completion from LMS; and ● Where applicable, records of training assessment and corresponding results. ● Records are stored within the LMS and secured shared drives managed by the Block Compliance Training Team. Prior to 2018 and to the implementation of online learning and LMS, the general training was delivered by the Square AU CCO in-person with completion acknowledged by trainees and recorded in a centralised register electronically. These records are still retained for at least 7 years. 2.3. Targeted Training for Senior Manager and AMLCO In addition to enterprise-wide mandatory AML/CTF training, the appointed Senior Manager and AMLCO must undertake enhanced, role-specific AML/CTF training commensurate with their statutory accountability and oversight obligations. Training may be delivered through a range of methods appropriate to the role, including industry seminars, regulatory briefings, professional certifications, workshops, or other targeted learning formats. Training must be completed upon appointment to the role and refreshed periodically, including following material legislative or regulatory change. 2.4. Board of Directors AML/CTF Training Session 4 The Board of Directors plays a crucial role in overseeing the companyʼs AML/CTF Program. Specific AML/CTF training sessions are provided to the Board to enhance their understanding of their obligation under the AML/CTF Act. The Board of Directors AML/CTF training session is: ● Delivered in-person by the Square AU CCO or their delegates to the Board of Directors of Square AU; ● Delivered annually in one board meeting; ● Intended to help each board member to understand the regulatory requirements and ramifications of non-compliance; and ● Tracked for completion in board meeting minutes. Additional training may be provided as needed, considering changes in AML/CTF regulations, at the discretion of the Square AU CCO. The completion is tracked electronically in the board meeting minutes and retained for at least 7 years. 2.5. Compliance Operational Training Program Beyond Blockʼs Enterprise-wide Mandatory Compliance Training, Square AU administers an additional training program tailored specifically for its Compliance team, ensuring team members have the knowledge and capability required to effectively perform their AML/CTF responsibilities. The program is delivered to new employees and those transferring into the Square AU Compliance Team, providing enhanced guidelines and procedures on AML/CTF compliance required for day-to-day functions. Further detail can be found in Square AU Compliance Operational Procedure. Key elements of Compliance Operational Training includes: ● New Hire Training: Provides an introduction to compliance responsibilities, systems, tools and procedures, to ensure personnel understand their AML/CTF obligations and are prepared for their roles ● Role-Specific and Ongoing Training: Training is tailored to the nature of the role and responsibilities and is commensurate with the ML/TF/PF risks associated with the functions they perform. This includes shadow sessions and group training and workshops to address emerging topics and knowledge gaps. ● Where personnel perform Square AU-related functions on a temporary or incidental basis (for example, to provide short-term coverage), targeted on-the-job training may be pro
documents.FC-036: Employee and Personnel Due Diligence Program Square | Square AU Pty Ltd Last Approved: Mar 26, 2026 Effective Date: Mar 26, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: The Employee and Personnel Due Diligence Program (the “Programˮ) details Square AUʼs risk based approach to screen employees and personnel to minimise the risks that they may commit, or assist others in committing money laundering, financing of terrorism (“ML/TFˮ) or other financial crime offences. The purpose of this Program is to establish and maintain an effective framework for assessing and managing the risks associated with employing or engaging individuals who may be involved in activities that could facilitate money laundering, terrorist financing, or proliferation financing. This program is designed to ensure that all personnel , particularly those in roles that influence AML/CTF outcomes or have access to sensitive financial information or decision-making authority over high-risk activities, undergo thorough background checks and risk-based ongoing monitoring. By identifying and mitigating potential risks early, the program helps protect Square AU from reputational, legal, and financial harm, while ensuring compliance with the AML/CTF Act and supporting the ongoing effectiveness of Square AUʼs AML/CTF controls. Scope: The Program applies to all employees (including full time, part time and casual workers) and directors (collectively known as “employeesˮ) of Square AU, and personnel (including individuals employed or otherwise engaged by Square AU who perform functions relevant to its AML/CTF obligations). Where individuals are engaged through employment agencies or other third-party arrangements, Square AU satisfies its personnel due diligence obligations through contractual screening requirements and oversight mechanisms agreed with the relevant agency or provider. Due diligence procedures, including background checks appropriate to the AML/CTF risk of the role, are conducted by the employment agency pursuant to the applicable services agreement. Square AU reserves the right to review, test and audit the agencyʼs screening procedures and requires hiring managers to attest that screening proportionate to the risk of the role has been completed prior to commencement. The Program applies when Square AU transfers contractors into permanent roles as direct hires of Square AU. Program Requirements Square AU adopts a risk-based approach to Employee and Personnel Due Diligence. Personnel performing functions relevant to Square AUʼs AML/CTF obligations are subject to formal due diligence requirements proportionate to their level of financial crime risk, regulatory exposure, and decision-making authority. In addition, to address risks arising from broader organisational structures and cross-functional responsibilities, Square AU applies baseline due diligence measures across all employees to support consistent risk management, governance, and oversight. Definitions: Terminology Definition Anti-Money Set of laws, regulations and procedures designed to detect and deter Laundering (“AMLˮ) Money Laundering (“MLˮ), Terrorist Financing (“TFˮ), and any other financial crimes. AUSTRAC The Australian Transaction Reports and Analysis Centre (‘AUSTRACʼ), which performs a dual role as AML and CTF regulator and financial intelligence unit. Counter-Terrorist Set of laws, regulations and measures designed to detect, prevent and Financing (“CTFˮ) disrupt the flow of funds to terrorist organisations and activities. Employee and The process by which businesses conduct background checks and ongoing Personnel Due monitoring of employees and personnel to ensure they are not involved in Diligence (“EPDDˮ) money laundering, terrorist financing, or other illegal activities. The Anti-Money As part of its broader regulatory compliance obligations, Square AU Pty Ltd Laundering and (“Square AUˮ) is required to comply with the Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorism Counter-Terrorism Financing Act 2006, as amended from time to time. The Financing Act 2006 AML/CTF Act is designed to deter, detect and disrupt money laundering and (“AML/CTF Actˮ) terrorism financing. Personnel Due Diligence Program | Square | Square AU Pty Ltd 2 Politically Exposed A PEP is an individual who holds a prominent public position or role in a Persons (“PEPsˮ) government body or international organisation, either in Australia or overseas. Immediate family members and/or close associates of these individuals are also considered PEPs. Employee Means an individual who is directly employed by Square AU under a contract of employment (whether full-time, part-time, or casual), and who performs duties for or on behalf of Square AU. Personnel Means any individual employed or otherwise engaged by Square AU, including persons deployed, seconded, contracted or supplied through an Outsourced Service Provider, who perform or will perform functions relevant to Square AUʼs obligations under the AML/CTF Act. Designated Service A service that is listed in section 6 of the AML/CTF Act. Roles and Responsibilities Role Responsibility Square Australia Acts as the designated Senior Manager for approvals required under the (“AUˮ) Chief AML and CTF Act and Rules, including approving the Employee and Compliance Officer Personnel Due Diligence Program and any material updates to it. (“CCOˮ) Square AU Act as the AML/CTF Compliance Officer to oversee and coordinate the Regulatory Program operational functions of the Employee and Personnel Due Diligence Program Manager in compliance with local regulatory requirements, ensures that all due diligence processes, including onboarding, screening, and ongoing checks, are implemented effectively and consistently, and is responsible for reporting. The Trust & Safety Responsible for protecting employees and personnel, assets and information team of Block. Personnel Due Diligence Program | Square | Square AU Pty Ltd 3 2. Role-Level Risk Assessment Framework Square AU applies a role-level risk assessment methodology to determine the appropriate level of Personnel Due Diligence (“PDDˮ) required for Personnel. This assessment recognises that roles present varying levels of exposure to money laundering and terrorism financing (“ML/TFˮ) risk based on their access, authority, decision-making capacity and influence over AML/CTF controls. Square AU conducts personnel due diligence both prior to employment or engagement and on an ongoing basis during employment. This approach is designed to ensure that personnel due diligence is risk-based, proportionate to the AML/CTF risk exposure of the role, and aligned with Square AUʼs broader AML/CTF compliance obligations. The purpose of the role-level assessment is to ensure that personnel due diligence measures are proportionate to: ● The level of decision-making authority exercised by the role; ● The roleʼs ability to influence or design AML/CTF controls; ● Access to regulatory reporting functions; ● Access to sensitive customer or business information; ● The potential impact on Square AUʼs AML/CTF compliance obligations if the role fails. The role-level assessment applies to all Personnel performing AML/CTF functions across Square AU, including oversight, investigations, onboarding, detection, escalation and customer-facing functions where relevant. The outcomes of this assessment inform the allocation of roles into the appropriate EDD Screening Package and are documented in Appendix A. 2.1. Roles and Methodology 2.1.1. Identification of Personnel Performing AML/CTF Functions Square AU identifies all personnel who perform, or are responsible for overseeing, influencing or supporting functions relevant to Square AUʼs obligations under the AML/CTF Act. Personnel Due Diligence Program | Square | Square AU Pty Ltd 4 This includes people who: ● Hold AML/CTF governance roles, such as governing body, senior manager(s) and AML/CTF compliance officer; ● Have responsibilities under the AML/CTF program; ● Work in roles that could be exposed to ML/TF risks; ● Perform any other functions or roles that support or help perform your AML/CTF obligations. Only roles identified as performing AML/CTF functions are subject to role-level AML/CTF risk assessment under this Program. 2.1.2. Methodology Square AU applies a structured role-level risk assessment to determine the AML/CTF exposure associated with each identified AML/CTF role. The assessment evaluates the nature and extent of the roleʼs authority, control influence and potential regulatory impact. In particular, Square AU considers whether the role: ● Designs, approves or materially influences AML/CTF policies, procedures or frameworks; ● Holds authority to approve or escalate high-risk customers; ● Has access to and/or authorises regulatory reporting submissions; ● Has the ability to override or bypass internal controls; ● Configures or influences transaction monitoring rules or detection parameters; ● Amends customer risk ratings or audit trails; ● Implements process exemptions or manual workarounds; ● Manages outsourcing or third-party arrangements impacting AML/CTF controls; ● Has access to highly sensitive business or customer information. Each role is assessed by these factors and assigned an Overall Risk Rating that can be found in Appendix A. Personnel Due Diligence Program | Square | Square AU Pty Ltd 5 The Overall Risk Assessment reflects the highest level of authority, systemic influence or regulatory exposure exercised by the role. This methodology ensures that the level of personnel due diligence applied is proportionate to the AML/CTF risk presented by the role. 2.1.2.1. Determination of High Risk Roles Roles classified as High risk are those that: ● Hold concentrated AML/CTF decision-making authority; ● Exercise systemic control influence over AML/CTF frameworks; ● Have direct responsibility for regulatory compliance oversight; or ● Have the capacity to create material regulatory or systemic expos
documents.FC-037: Enhanced Customer Due Diligence Program Square | Square AU Pty Ltd Last Approved: Mar 25, 2026 Effective Date: Mar 25, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: Under the AML/CTF Act, Square AU Pty Ltd (“Square AUˮ) has enacted obligations to perform multiple AML controls that include its Enhanced Customer Due Diligence ECDD Program (“Programˮ). The Program defines the triggers of ECDD and the actions Square AU takes when ECDD is performed. It also documents additional requirements from Acquirers and Payment Networks. The program sets out the ECDD measures applied in response to specific ML/TF risks. It also describes how the effectiveness of ECDD measures is monitored and reviewed, and outlines how Square AU manages its tipping-off obligations when conducting ECDD. Scope: This Program is established by Square AU and is applicable to the Square AU products through which Square AU provides Designated Services as defined under the AML/CTF Act. This Program is implemented, managed and maintained by the Square AU Compliance team and sets out the approach to design and execution Square AUʼs ECDD Program. Definitions: Terminology Definition Service Level Defines the expected review period for cases and other responsibilities of Agreements the Analysts working on filing PEP watchlist alerts. (“SLAˮ) Suspicious A report that a reporting entity must submit under AML/CTF Act if they have Matter Report reasonable grounds to suspect that a transaction may be related to money (“SMRˮ) laundering, terrorism financing, tax evasion, proceeds of crime or any other serious crimes under Australian law. An SMR must also be submitted if the Reporting Entity has reasonable grounds to suspect the customer or an 1 agent of the customer is not who they say they are. Reporting Entity Providers of Designated Services as that term is defined under the AML/CTF Act. Tipping Off The conduct of disclosing any information about an SMR, or inferring that Square AU has submitted or will submit an SMR. Designated A service that is listed in section 6 of the AML/CTF Act. Service 2. Program Requirements 2.1. Customer Risk Rating Model Square AU has established its ECDD program based on its Continuous Customer Risk Rating Program. All new and/or active customers are risk-rated daily using a Machine Learning-based Continuous CRR (cCRR) model. This model defines the risk rating for customers, which is used in part to determine the customers that may require enhanced due diligence by Square AU. 2.1.1. Empirical Customer Risk Square uses machine learning models to assess the risk of each customer. These assessments of customer risk include a variety of sources of information about a customer, including, but not limited to, the customerʼs payment behaviour, bank behaviour, casing history, account attributes and other. These models are trained using historical data. Square AU refers to model-based assessments of customer risk as empirical risk. Square uses two machine learning models to assess empirical risk across the life of a customer: the Activation CRR and Continuous CRR models. 2.1.2. Activation Customer Risk Rating (aCRR) Every customer who is onboarded is evaluated by the aCRR machine learning model and given a score. A customerʼs aCRR score is stored and used as an input into the cCRR model. Additional details on aCRR can be found in the Block Compliance Activations CRR ML Program. 2 2.1.3. Continuous Customer Risk Rating (cCRR) Every customer is evaluated daily by the cCRR machine learning model and given a score. The cCRR model leverages a wide range of information about a customer and their behavior. This score should be considered a current assessment of the ML/TF risk posed by a customer given the information available as of the evaluation date. 2.1.4. Intrinsic Customer Risk There are certain characteristics a customer displays that Square AU believes should lead them to be considered as high risk, regardless of their empirical model scores. Square AU refers to this as Intrinsic Customer Risk, and the specific attributes that lead to this assessment are referred to as intrinsic risk factors. A customer is considered to be intrinsically “high riskˮ if they display one or more intrinsic risk factors. Intrinsically high risk customers are subjected to regular monitoring equal to or exceeding that of empirically high risk customers. The intrinsic high risk factors for Square AU are defined as follows: ● Sanctions: The individual, beneficial owner, or the business is identified as having sanction related concerns. ● Suspicious Matter Report SMR: Customer included in an SMR filing and the account is placed on enhanced monitoring without deactivation after review. ● High-Risk Politically Exposed Person HRPEP: The customer,, beneficial owner, or the person on whose behalf the customer (such as a beneficiary of a trust or foreign equivalent) is identified as a confirmed high-risk PEP. ● High-Risk Jurisdiction: Square data indicates that the customer has geographic linkage to any of the high-risk countries listed in 2025 Country Risk Rating v1.0, which include the high-risk jurisdiction that Financial Action Task Force FATF has called for ECDD to be applied. 2.2. ECDD Scenarios Square AU applies its ECDD measures in the below scenarios. 2.2.1. Sanctions If Square AU identifies that an individual, beneficial owner, or the business has sanction-related concerns, but decides to maintain the relationship after a review and evaluation of risk-appetite, the customer will be subject to an ECDD review as laid out in the ECDD Review Procedures. 3 2.2.2. Suspicious Matter Report SMR When the SMR obligation arises, in addition to submitting the SMR by following the SMR procedures, Square AU will also determine whether to continue the business relationship with the customer depending on the ML/TF risk. During this process, Square AU will ensure that a “Tipping offˮ offence is not committed. If Square AU discontinues the relationship, Square AU will close the customerʼs accounts. If Square AU continues the relationship, Square AU will: ● Follow the review process in ECDD Review Procedures for initial review; ● Monitor the customerʼs future activities and examine the customerʼs account(s)s, subject to the Transaction Monitoring Program; and ● Monitor the customerʼs future activities and examine the customerʼs account(s) at least yearly, according to the ECDD Program and subject to ECDD review following ECDD review procedures. 2.2.3. High-Risk Politically Exposed Person HRPEP If a customer, a beneficial owner of a customer or the person on whose behalf the customer is determined to be a high ML/TF risk PEP or the individual is a foreign PEP, Square AU will follow the review process outlined in the ECDD Review Procedures to determine whether to continue the business relationship with the customer. If Square AU discontinues the relationship, Square AU will: ● Close the customerʼs account(s); and ● Consider whether a SMR should be submitted. If Square AU continues the relationship, Square AU will: ● Establish on reasonable grounds the source of funds and source of wealth; ● Obtain explicit approval from the Square AU CCO; ● Monitor the customerʼs future activity and examine the customerʼs account(s), subject to the Transaction Monitoring Program; and ● Monitor the customerʼs future activities and examine the customerʼs account(s) at least yearly, according to the ECDD Program and subject the account(s) to ECDD review following ECDD review procedures. 4 2.2.4. High-Risk Jurisdiction If Square AU data indicates that the customer has geographic linkage to any countries identified as high risk in 2025 Country Risk Rating v1.0, according to Blockʼs 2024 Global Geographic Risk Ratings Methodology & Program, Square AU will subject the customer to an ECDD review as laid out in the ECDD Review Procedures. 2.2.5. High ML/TF Risk Customers Eligible for Review When circumstances arise, Square AU will perform an ECDD review following the ECDD Review Procedures. During initial onboarding and throughout ongoing CDD, including KYC updates and re-verification, Square AU must identify, document, and maintain the customerʼs source of funds and source of wealth when this information is relevant to the customerʼs high ML/TF risk. 2.3. ECDD Measures The Square AU Compliance Analyst reviews alerts based on ECDD Review Procedures that are routinely reviewed and updated to align with regulatory requirements. In general, the ECDD measures used must be appropriate to the situation, and informed by the reason why the customer was identified as high ML/TF risk and designed to manage and mitigate the ML/TF risk. ECDD measures including a range of measures below: ● Obtain additional information, for example the reason for certain transactions; ● More detailed analysis of customer information; ● Collect, verify or re-verify more KYC information about the customer ● Collect and/or verify information about the customer or beneficial ownerʼs source of funds and/or source of wealth; ● More detailed analysis and monitoring of transactions; ● Take additional measures to better understand the background, ownership (if relevant) and financial situation of the customer, and other parties to the transaction; ● Cease providing designated service where this falls out of Square AUʼs risk appetite; ● Escalate and get approval from senior management. 2.4. Additional Requirements Square AU may apply additional ECDD measures in additional circumstances, for example under the requirement from Acquirers and Payment Networks, following ECDD Review Procedures. 5 2.5. Record keeping Requirements The Square AU Compliance Analyst must keep clear, accurate records of any enhanced CDD applied to a customer, including the reasons for its application, the information collected and verified, any analysis, ML/TF risk assessments, and any resulting decision or actions taken to manage or mitigate risk.
