documents.FC-038: Financial Crimes Transaction Monitoring Program Square Australia Last Approved: Mar 25, 2026 Effective Date: Mar 25, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: The Financial Crimes Transaction Monitoring Program TMP is a key component of Square AU Pty Ltd's ("Square AU") Anti-Money Laundering & Counter-Terrorism Financing Program, designed to meet the obligations set out by the Australian Transaction Report and Analysts Centre AUSTRAC. The Financial Crime Transaction Monitoring Program for Square AU is overseen by the Square AU CCO, who provides approval and ensures regulatory compliance. The AML/CTF Compliance Officer is responsible for managing and updating the program when required, ensuring it adheres to local regulatory requirements. In accordance with Square AU Anti-Money Laundering & Counter-Terrorism Financing Program, the TMP establishes methods to monitor, detect, and prevent possible money laundering, financial crimes, terrorist financing and proliferation financing for Square AU's customer transactions in Australia. The program is designed to identify, mitigate and manage money laundering, terrorism financing and proliferation financing risks in compliance with AUSTRAC's requirements and the AML/CTF Act. Its primary objective is to identify and report suspicious transactions, including but not limited to those that deviate from a customer's normal behaviour, involve high-risk jurisdiction, or trigger specific red-flag indicators. Scope: This Program is established by Square AU Pty Ltd Square AU and applicable to Square AU products and services, such as Square Point of Sale ("POS") and other financial products that process transactions which are deemed to fall within the scope of this Program. It is implemented, managed and maintained by the Square Australian AU Compliance team and sets out the approach to design and execution Square AU's Transaction Monitoring program. 1 Definitions: Terminology Definition Anti-Money Laundering Set of laws, regulations and procedures designed to detect and ("AML") deter money laundering, terrorist financing, and any other financial crimes. Counter-Terrorist Financing Set of laws, regulations and measures designed to detect, ("CTF") prevent and disrupt the flow of funds of terrorist organisations and activities. The Anti-Money Laundering As part of its broader regulatory compliance obligations, Square and Counter-Terrorism AU Pty Ltd Square AU is required to comply with the Financing Act 2006 Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorism Financing Act ("AML/CTF Act") 2006 Cth, as amended from time to time. The AML/CTF Act is designed to deter, detect and disrupt money laundering and terrorism financing. AUSTRAC The Australian Transaction Reports and Analysis Centre ('AUSTRAC') performs a dual role as AML and CTF regulator and financial intelligence unit. Transaction Monitoring The process of overseeing and analysing financial transactions to detect suspicious or unusual activity that may indicate money laundering, terrorism financing, or other illicit activities. Suspicious Matter Report A report that a reporting entity must submit under AML/CTF Act ("SMR") if they have reasonable grounds to suspect that a transaction may be related to money laundering, terrorism financing, tax evasion, proceeds of crime or any other serious crimes under Australian law. An SMR must also be submitted if the reporting entity has reasonable grounds to suspect the customer or an agent of the customer is not who they say they are. Typology Assessment The assessment of Square's product conducive to automated anti-money laundering Transaction Monitoring, mapping those products to risk indicators, Transaction Monitoring rules, then conducting a gap assessment to determine whether those products are covered by a Transaction Monitoring rule (also called "Coverage Assessment". Service Level Agreements Defines the expected review period for cases and other ("SLA") responsibilities of the Compliance Australia Analysts working on Transaction Monitoring queues. 2 Terminology Definition Quality Assurance The quality assurance process of reviewing decisions made by an analyst during TM reviews by a Quality analyst. Regulator A proprietary tool which houses all customer data and transactions, to perform monitoring of customer transactions across all Square products. Square Point of Sale ("POS") Square's payment processing system offered to merchants. AML/CTF & Sanction Risk Systematic process for identifying and measuring the risks Assessment which could jeopardise an organisation's objects, as well as the effectiveness of the control environment established to mitigate those risks. 2. Program Requirements The Transaction Monitoring program is designed and maintained based on the annual AML/CTF & Sanction Risk Assessment conducted by Compliance Governance and Reporting AML/CTF & Sanctions Risk Assessment Team CGRRA. This program undergoes an annual review to assess its effectiveness, address any significant findings, and implement necessary improvements. Additional reviews may be conducted following events such as identified issues or significant regulatory changes. In addition, the program is subject to regular testing and independent auditing to evaluate its performance, accuracy, and compliance with AUSTRAC requirements. This program also outlines the compliance controls that are used for the evaluation and management of Square's program as it relates to money laundering, terrorist financing, proliferation financing and financial crime related risk, as well as how to identify suspicious customer transactions. These controls include, but are not limited to: ● Coverage Assessments; ● Model Governance and Monitoring; and ● Transaction Monitoring Alert Review Procedures and Processes. 2.1 Coverage Assessments Square conducts annual Anti-Money Laundering AML and Transaction Monitoring TM Risk Coverage Assessments. These assessments, known as Typology Assessments, help identify any gaps and inform the design of the program. At a high level, these assessments involve identifying key inputs, compiling risk indicators and regulatory requirements, aligned to covered markets and products. The assessments include reviewing documentation, conducting Subject Matter Expert SME interviews, and assessing 3 current manual and automated monitoring controls. A coverage analysis is performed to identify gaps ensuring comprehensive risk coverage and regulatory compliance. The Square AU Compliance team is involved in the coverage assessment process across all phases, with involvement from the Square AU CCO, AML/CTF Compliance Officer, Compliance AU Program Manager and Square APAC Transaction Monitoring Lead. These phases include the creation of documentation, gathering and finalising risk rating, drafting, and approving management action plans. 2.2 Model Governance and Queue Parameters Square utilises various proprietary algorithms to create cases for the Compliance analysts to review. The creation, review and governance of these models is covered by the Block | Model Governance Policy and Block | Model Change Management Procedure. These include automated case queues and periodic or manual reports for review. Models cover multiple typologies to detect anomalous activity as outlined in the Coverage Assessment, including, but not limited to: ● Structuring; ● Profiling; ● Velocity; ● Lack of Economic Purpose; ● Patterning; ● High Risk Geography; and ● Funnelling. Square AU's Transaction Monitoring queues are established and maintained by the Compliance Engineering & Technology team. This team will evaluate effectiveness of the alerts generated by queues (i.e. productivity) and make recommendations for tuning the models, adding further models or changing existing models. Detailed rules of Square AU's Transaction Monitoring queues are maintained in Square | AU TM Rulebook. Any new models or recommended changes to the existing models are reviewed and approved in accordance with the Block | Model Change Management Procedure. To ensure oversight of the Transaction Monitoring queues, Square has implemented change management and model validation policies, managed and executed by the Compliance Engineering & Technology team, in accordance with the Block | Model Change Management Procedure.. Any changes applicable to Square AU must be approved by both the AU Compliance Program Approver and the AU Compliance Operations Approver, ensuring continued local oversight. 2.3 Transaction Monitoring Alert Review Procedures and Processes The Analyst reviews alerts based on written procedures that are routinely reviewed and updated. In general, the investigation of a case follows the steps outlined below: 1. The Analyst claims a new alert case in Regulator and notes the typologies, alert type, and product; and 4 2. The Analyst reviews customer information in conjunction with account activity, web presence, and potentially adverse media in order to make a determination of an appropriate case action. Following that, the analyst can take one of the following actions: ● Clear: Account activity represents typical product use with no adverse or potentially unusual activity identified; ● Clear and escalate: Account activity represents typical product use with no adverse or unusual activity identified, however potential terms of service violations or sanctions concerns exist and require an escalation in a timely manner to relevant queues; ● Escalate to ECDD team for an ECDD review when: ○ The merchant has no apparent economic or legal purpose; or ○ The merchant has made unusually complex or large transactions; or ○ Would involve an unusual pattern of transactions; ● Request for more information: In instances where The Analyst is not able to confidently take a “clearˮ or “deactivationˮ action type, the Analyst may request more information from the merchant. Once this information is received from the merchant, the Analyst will re-
documents.FC-039: Politically Exposed Persons Watchlist Program Square | Square AU Pty Ltd Last Approved: Mar 26, 2026 Effective Date: Mar 26, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: As per Australiaʼs Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorism Financing Act 2006 AML/CTF Act), Square AU Pty Ltd (“Square AUˮ) is required to determine whether its customer or a beneficial owner of the customer is a Politically Exposed Person (“PEPˮ) before providing services or as soon as practical after services have been provided. Under section 5 and 28 of the AML/CTF Act and the AML/CTF Rules, reporting entities must implement appropriate risk-based systems and controls to identify and manage the risks associated with customers who are PEPs, including their family members and close associates. The PEP Watchlist Program for Square AU (“Programˮ) is overseen by the Square AU CCO, who provides necessary approval and ensures regulatory compliance. The Square AU Regulatory Program Manager is responsible for managing and updating the Program when required, ensuring it adheres to local regulatory requirements. In accordance with Square AU Anti-Money Laundering & Counter-Terrorism Financing Policies, the PEP Watchlist Program established methods and controls to identify PEPs and assess the risk posed by them. The purpose is to ensure that Square AU appropriately mitigates the potential risk of money laundering,terrorism financing and proliferation financing associated with PEPs. Square AU applies a risk-based approach to the identification and management of PEP relationships, including enhanced due diligence and ongoing monitoring where appropriate. Scope: This Program is established by Square AU and applicable to the Square AU products through which Square AU provides Designated Services as defined under the AML/CTF Act, as specified in the Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorism Financing Policies. This Program is implemented, managed and maintained by the Square AU Compliance team and sets out the approach to design and execution Square AUʼs PEP Watchlist Program. This program forms part of Square AUʼs AML/CTF Program and supports Square AUʼs obligations to identify, assess and 1 manage money laundering and terrorism financing risks associated with Politically Exposed Persons. Definitions: Terminology Definition Anti-Money A set of regulatory rules and investigative techniques aimed at preventing Laundering and combating various illegal activities related to the financial system (“AMLˮ) Counter-Terrorist A set of regulatory rules and investigative techniques designed to detect, Financing prevent and disrupt the flow of funds of terrorist organisations and activities. (“CTFˮ) Proliferation The act of providing funds or financial services used, in whole or in part, for Financing (“PFˮ) the manufacture, acquisition, possession, development, export, trans-shipment, brokering, transport, transfer, stockpiling or use of nuclear, chemical or biological weapons and their means of delivery. The Anti-Money As part of its broader regulatory compliance obligations, Square AU Pty Ltd Laundering and Square AU is required to comply with the Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terroris Counter-Terrorism Financing Act 2006, as amended from time to time. The m Financing Act AML/CTF Act is designed to deter, detect and disrupt money laundering and 2006 (“AML/CTF terrorism financing. Actˮ) The Anti-Money The AML/CTF Rules are subsidiary legislative instruments made under the Laundering and AML/CTF Act. The AML/CTF Rules provide the detail for the broader Counter-Terroris obligations set out in the AML/CTF Act. m Financing Rules Instrument 2007 No. 1 (“AML/CTF Rulesˮ) AUSTRAC The Australian Transaction Reports and Analysis Centre (‘AUSTRACʼ) performs a dual role as AML and CTF regulator and financial intelligence unit. Politically A PEP is an individual who holds a prominent public position or role in a Exposed government body or international organisation, either in Australia or Persons (“PEPsˮ) overseas. Immediate family members and/or close associates of these individuals are also considered PEPs. Enhanced Enhanced Customer Due Diligence ECDD involves carrying out extra Customer Due checks on a customerʼs identification, collecting additional information and Diligence doing additional verification, according to Enhanced Customer Due Diligence 2 (“ECDDˮ) Program. Suspicious A report that a reporting entity must submit under AML/CTF Act if they have Matter Report reasonable grounds to suspect that a transaction may be related to money (“SMRˮ) laundering, terrorism financing, tax evasion, proceeds of crime or any other serious crimes under Australian law. An SMR must also be submitted if the reporting entity has reasonable grounds to suspect the customer or an agent of the customer is not who they say they are. Designated A service that is listed in section 6 of the AML/CTF Act. Service Service Level Defines the expected review period for cases and other responsibilities of Agreements the Analysts working on filing PEP watchlist alerts. (“SLAˮ) 2. Program Requirements 2.1. Definition of PEP A PEP is someone who holds a prominent public position in a government or international organisation. Through their position, they may have power and influence, and access to and control over money and resources. This definition also extends to their immediate family members and close associates. There are three categories of PEP, as defined in the AML/CTF Rules 2007 ● Domestic PEP Someone who holds a prominent public position or role in an Australian government body; ● Foreign PEP Someone who holds a prominent public position or role with a government body in a country other than Australia, this would include foreign PEPs working or residing in Australia; ● International Organisation PEP Someone who holds a prominent public position or role in an international organisation, such as the United Nations UN, the World Trade Organisation WTO or the North Atlantic Treaty Organisation NATO. Immediate family members and close associates of a PEP are also considered PEPs for the purposes of this Program and must be assessed under Square AUʼs risk-based framework. 2.2. PEP Risk Assessment All Foreign PEPs must be treated as high risk automatically. Domestic PEPs and International Organisation PEPs may also be considered as high risk depending on the circumstances. Square AU 3 implements a risk-based approach and conducts risk assessment on domestic and international organisation PEPs to determine their risk levels. When deciding the risk levels and whether to apply our Enhanced Customer Due Diligence measures, we consider the following factors which include but are not limited to: ● Type of business/business nature; ● Purpose/Use of account; ● Transaction size and pattern; ● Level of PEP positions; ● Potential risk posed by PEPs; and ● Adverse media. Where Square AU becomes aware that an existing customer or beneficial owner has become a Politically Exposed Person during the course of the business relationship, Square AU will reassess the customerʼs ML/TF risk rating and determine whether enhanced due diligence measures and senior management approval are required. If Square AU determines that a person was previously a PEP, the same controls as for current PEP will be applied, taking into account: ● Whether the individual still has influence over public policy or expenditure decisions; ● The length of time since the individual held PEP status; ● Whether the individual remains prominent or politically connected; and ● Any other information suggesting the individual may continue to pose a high ML/TF risk due to their former PEP status. Senior management approval is required where a former PEP is assessed as presenting a high ML/TF risk. 2.2.1. High Risk PEP If a customer or a beneficial owner of a customer is determined to be a high ML/TF risk PEP or is a Foreign PEP, Square AU must undertake the following steps: ● Seek information from the customer and/or perform detailed analysis of the business to clarify the nature of the customerʼs ongoing business with us such as the purpose of the account. ● Take reasonable measures to establish the source of the customerʼs wealth and funds and follow the review process outlined in Square AUʼs ECDD review procedures for initial review. In this context, reasonable measures are measures that are practical, necessary and in line with identified ML/TF risk. ● Determine whether to establish or continue the business relationship with the customer, which could potentially result in the following outcomes: ○ Discontinue relationship 4 ■ Close the customerʼs account(s); and ■ Consider whether a SMR should be submitted ○ Establish or continue relationship ■ Obtain explicit approval from the Square AU CCO, acting as the Senior Manager responsible for AML/CTF oversight, before establishing or continuing the business relationship. ■ Monitor the customerʼs future activities and examine the customerʼs account(s), subject to the Transaction Monitoring Program; and ■ Monitor the customerʼs future activities and examine the customerʼs account(s) at least yearly, according to the ECDD Program and subject the account(s) to ECDD review following ECDD review procedures 2.2.2. Medium or Low Risk PEP If a customer or a beneficial owner of a customer is a domestic PEP or an international organisation PEP and determined to be Medium or Low ML/TF risk, Square AU must undertake the following steps: ● Seek information from the customer and/or perform detailed analysis of the business to clarify the nature of the customerʼs ongoing business with Square AU,such as the purpose of the account(s). ● Determine whether to continue the business relationship with the customer, which could potentially result in the following outcomes: ○ Discontinue relationship ■ Close the customerʼs account(s); and ■ Consider whether a SMR should be submitted ○ Continue relationship:
documents.FC-040: Suspicious Matter Report Program Square | Square AU Pty Ltd Last Approved: Mar 25, 2026 Effective Date: Mar 25, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: In order to fulfil our reporting obligations under Australiaʼs Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorism Financing Act AML/CTF Act), Square Australia Pty Ltd (“Square AUˮ) has developed the Suspicious Matter Report Program (“Programˮ) as part of Square AUʼs Anti-Money Laundering & Counter-Terrorism Financing Policies, to prevent, detect, and report suspicious activities as they pertain to the Designated Services Square AU provides. The purpose of the Program is to establish a structured process within Square AU for identifying, escalating, and reporting suspicious activities to AUSTRAC in compliance with the AML/CTF Act. The Program ensures that all relevant Staff Members understand their obligations, including the need to identify suspicious transactions or behaviour, perform enhanced due diligence, and submit accurate and timely reports to AUSTRAC when required without tipping off. Actˮ) Enhanced Enhanced Customer Due Diligence ECDD involves carrying out extra Customer Due checks on a customerʼs identification, collecting additional information and Diligence doing additional verification, according to Enhanced Customer Due Diligence (“ECDDˮ) Program. Suspicious A report that a reporting entity must submit under AML/CTF Act if they have Matter Report reasonable grounds to suspect that a transaction may be related to money (“SMRˮ) laundering, terrorism financing, tax evasion, proceeds of crime or any other serious crimes under Australian law. An SMR must also be submitted if the reporting entity has reasonable grounds to suspect the customer or an agent of the customer is not who they say they are. Financial Act that imposes certain obligations on solicitors and entities known as “cash Transaction dealersˮ under the FTR Act. Reports (“FTRˮ) Act Designated A service that is listed in section 6 of the AML/CTF Act. Service 2 2. Program Requirements 2.1. Employee Obligations 2.1.1. Tipping Off “Tipping offˮ is a criminal offence prohibited by AML/CTF Act and can result in a penalty of up to two years imprisonment, 120 penalty units, or both. The tipping off offence applies to the following persons (current or former): ● A Reporting entity; ● An officer, employee or agent of a reporting entity; ● A person that has been required to give information or produce documents under sections 491 or section s49B1 of the AML/CTF Act. Tipping off is disclosing certain types of information to another person, where it would or could reasonably be expected to prejudice an investigation. This includes: ● Information about SMRs ○ Information that establishes that an SMR has been submitted, or that a requirement to submit an SMR has been triggered. ○ A report made or prepared for the purposes of meeting SMR obligations, including any copies. ○ any document purporting to set out information contained in an SMR. ● Information relating to notices issued under sections 49 and 49B of the AML/CTF Act. ○ That a requirement was or is in place to provide information or produce a document in response to a notice. ○ that you have given information or produced a document in response to a notice. Appropriate controls will be implemented to prevent the improper disclosure of protected Information, including when sharing within the reporting group or with third parties. Information held will be identified, and circumstances where disclosure could prejudice an investigation will be assessed, with measures applied such as restricting access on a genuine need-to-know basis, maintaining confidentiality and security safeguards, and ensuring appropriate protections when sharing Information externally. Regular employee training and appropriate due diligence processes will be maintained to support compliance with tipping off obligations. 2.1.2. Notification and escalation There are three broad channels through which a member of the Square AU Compliance Team may become aware of activity requiring further investigation to determine if it warrants a SMR submission. These channels include: ● Automated account monitoring that has been established to identify potentially suspicious activity. 3 ● Internal referrals from Block Staff Members (e.g., Compliance, Risk, Capital Operations, Afterpay or Customer Success teams) ● External referrals from third parties (e.g., banking partners, other financial institutions, law firms, law enforcement or government agencies) The investigation will be conducted within a time frame set internally by Square AU. An internal tracking system is set up to ensure the time frame is strictly followed. If any Square AU Staff Member is aware of any activity that may involve money laundering, terrorist financing, proliferation financing, and/or other criminal activity that they believe should be investigated immediately by the Square AU Compliance Team for reporting purposes, the Square AU Compliance Team shall be contacted immediately via compliance_au@squareup.com. 2.2. When to Submit SMR If unusual activity has been identified and the Square AU Compliance Team is notified, Enhanced Customer Due Diligence ECDD must be conducted initially to determine if Square AU have reasonable grounds for suspicion. Square AU is required to submit an SMR to AUSTRAC when Square AU forms a suspicion on ‘reasonable groundsʼ, which means, that if a reasonable person (e.g. Square AU InvestigationsAnalyst) would conclude from the facts, circumstances and all the information available about the matter that an SMR should be submitted, Square AU must report it. Square AU must also explain why Square AU has formed a suspicion in the SMR. A SMR must be submitted if Square AU has reasonable grounds to suspect that the customer or transaction is related to one or more of the following offences or scenarios: ● Terrorism financing; ● Money laundering; ● An offence against a Commonwealth, state or territory law; ● Proceeds of crime; ● Tax evasion; ● Someone is not who they claim to be; and ● Could be the victim of a crime. A SMR must be submitted each time a new suspicion is formed on reasonable grounds. SMRs only relate to matters involving Designated Services. This includes when Square AU starts providing Designated Services, or when Square AU is asked about providing Designated Services as part of our normal business activities. 4 2.3. Deadline for SMR The SMR reporting deadlines start once Square has formed its suspicion, which may be at any time in Square AUʼs business relationship with its customers. A suspicion is only ‘formedʼ after the Square AU Compliance team has conducted ECDD and when the Square AU Investigation team determines that there are reasonable grounds for suspicion. Once a suspicion on reasonable grounds has been formed after investigations, the Square AU Investigation team must submit an SMR about the matter (subject to ensuring a "Tipping Off" offence is not committed) within: ● 24 hours after forming the relevant suspicion if the suspicion relates to terrorism financing; or ● 3 business days after forming the suspicion in all other cases; or ● 5 business days after the day the suspicion is formed if Square AU claim the SMR include information protected by Legal professional privilege LPP. It does not apply if suspicion was relevant to terrorism financing. The Square AU Compliance team leverages an internal mechanism to track the above deadline is strictly followed to avoid any late submission. 2.4. After Submitting SMR 2.4.1. Relationship with suspicious customers Square AU is not required to end the business relationship or stop p

Anti-Social Forces ASF Management Program | Square | Square K.K. Confidential and Not for Distribution 1. Introduction Japan has stringent regulations against Anti-Social Forces (“ASFˮ) to ensure business integrity and safety. Compliance with regulations against ASF mandated by the Japanese Government policies and prefectural authorities, is essential. All entities in Japan must ensure a secure and ethical business environment, by prohibiting any relationships with these anti-social members. This “Anti-Social Forces Management Programˮ (“Programˮ) has been established to ensure the proper implementation of Square Kabushiki Kaishaʼs (“Square K.K.ˮ or the “Companyˮ) Anti-Social Forces Management Policy. Japan Chief Compliance Officer (“JP CCOˮ) is responsible for the operation and management of this Program. 1. 目的 日本では、企業の健全性および安全性を確保するため、反社会的勢力（以下「ASF」という。）に対する厳格な規制が敷 かれています。政府の方針および都道府県当局が定めるASF関連規制の遵守は不可欠です。国内のすべての事業体 は、これら反社会的勢力との一切の関係を禁止し、安全かつ倫理的な事業環境を確保しなければなりません。 本「反社会的勢力対応管理プログラム」（以下「本プログラム」という。）は、Square株式会社（以下「Square K.K.」また は「当社」という）の「反社会的勢力対応規程」の適切な運用を確保するため策定されました。本プログラムの運用管理 は日本エンティティのチーフコンプライアンスオフィサー（以下「JP CCO」という。）が担当します。 2. Purpose The purpose of the Program is to describe the detailed measures for implementing Square K.K.'s ASF Policy (“ Policyˮ), ensuring proper administrative operations at Square K.K. 2. 目的 本プログラムは、「反社会的勢力対応規程」に基づき、当社が、反社会的勢力との関係を遮断し、排除するために必要と なる事項の詳細および具体的な手続き等に関し遵守すべき事項等を定めることを目的とします。 3. Scope This Program is applicable to all Employees engaged in the Company's operations (including Board members, auditors, staff, secondees, contract employees, temporary employees, etc., hereinafter referred to as "Employees") and has the same scope of application as the Policy. Anti-Social Forces ASF Management Program | Square | Square K.K. Confidential and Not for Distribution 3. 対象 本規程は、「反社会的勢力対応規程」と同じく、当社のすべての事務を対象とし、当社の事務を行うすべての社員（取締役、 監査役、職員、出向者、契約社員、派遣社員等を含む。以下「役職員」という。）に適用する。 4. Standard Requirements 4.1 Internal Systems and Executive Reporting Pursuant to Article 5.2 of the Policy, the JP CCO and the Compliance Team is responsible for establishing and maintaining a framework to block and eliminate any ties with Anti-Social Forces, implementing necessary measures, and ensuring that all employees fully understand the requisite knowledge and regulations concerning ASFs. The JP CCO is required to deliver an appropriate report and conduct training on the Company's measures against Anti-Social Forces to the Board annually. In the event of the JP CCO's absence or when necessary for the efficient execution of duties, the JP CCO may appoint a suitable delegate to carry out their responsibilities. The delegate appointed under the previous provision shall be the APAC Alert Monitoring Lead. 4.1 内部体制および経営層への報告 規程5.2に基づき、JP CCOおよびコンプライアンスチームは、反社会的勢力との関係を遮断し排除するための体制整備と 取組みの実施、および全従業員が反社会的勢力に関する必要な知識・規制を十分に理解できるよう責任を負います。 JP CCOは当社の反社会的勢力対策について、年1回取締役会へ適切な報告を行い、併せて研修を実施しなければなりま せん。 また、JP CCOが不在の場合や職務遂行上必要と判断した場合には、JP CCOは代行者を指名してその職務を代行させる ことができます。現在の代行者はAPAC Alert Monitoring Leadとします。 4.2 Collaboration with External Agencies In accordance with Article 5.3 of the Policy, the JP CCO regularly, or as needed, evaluates collaboration with necessary external agencies and the use of databases related to ASF, as well as managing membership rights and related matters. 4.2 外部機関との連携 Anti-Social Forces ASF Management Program | Square | Square K.K. Confidential and Not for Distribution 規程5.3に従い、JP CCOは反社会的勢力対応において必要となる外部機関との連携状況や、関連データベースの利用・会 員権等の管理状況を定期的にまたは必要に応じて評価します。 4.3 Information Collection and Database Management According to the Article 5.4 of the Policy, the Japan Compliance Team is tasked with gathering information and maintaining databases to prevent making relationships with ASF. A comprehensive database with following information about ASFs, ensuring timely additions, deletions, or modifications; 1. Information collected by our ASF response actions from previous screenings; 2. Information gathered by external partners; 3. Information shared by Block Inc. and its group companies; 4. Information provided by external experts, such as the police, the Center for the Elimination of Violence, lawyers, and the Japan Credit Association; and 5. Any other relevant information similar to the above categories. JP CCO and JP Compliance Team establish strong working relationships with external experts, such as the Police and the Center for the Elimination of Violence, to ensure effective communication and cooperation if ASF issues arise. When outsourcing pre-screening, post-verification, or similar evaluations related to ASF, JP Compliance Team will use the external vendors in line with Article 4.11. of this Program. The JP Compliance Team will regularly, or as needed, verify that these vendors' databases are updated accurately and promptly. 4.3 情報収集とデータベース管理 規程5.4に基づき、JP Complaince Teamは反社会的勢力との関係構築を防止するため、以下の情報を網羅的に収集・登 録し、追加・削除・修正を適時行います。 1. 当社のこれまでのスクリーニング対応で得た情報 2. 外部パートナーから取得した情報 3. Blockおよびそのグループ会社から共有された情報 4. 警察、暴力追放運動推進センター、弁護士、日本クレジット協会など外部専門家から提供された情報 5. 上記に類するその他の関連情報 JP CCOおよびJP Complaince Teamは、警察や暴力追放運動推進センター等の外部専門家と強固な協力関係を構築 し、反社会的勢力問題発生時の迅速かつ適切な連携・対応を確保します。 Anti-Social Forces ASF Management Program | Square | Square K.K. Confidential and Not for Distribution また、反社会的勢力に関する事前審査、事後確認または同等の評価業務を外部委託する場合は、本プログラム4.11に基づ き委託先を選定します。日本コンプライアンスチームは、当該委託先のデータベースが正確かつ迅速に更新されているかを 定期的または必要に応じて確認します。 4.4 Anti-Social Forces Exclusion Clauses Pursuant to Article 5.5 of the Policy, the JP Legal Team is responsible for incorporating clauses excluding Anti-Social Forces into various contracts and agreements related to the Company's operations to proactively prevent transactions with such entities. 4.4 反社会的勢力排除条項 JP Legalは規程5.5に基づき、当社の事業活動に関連するすべての契約・合意書等に反社会的勢力排除条項を原則として 盛り込み、取引開始前に当該勢力との関与を未然に防止する責任があります。 4.5 Onboarding Screening According to Article 5.6 of the Policy, the JP Compliance Team is responsible for conducting pre-screening to prevent transactions with ASF when necessary. This involves using information managed in the database specified in Article 5.4, to ensure no involvement with ASF before entering into the following transactions: ● Individuals being considered for employment by the Company; ● Merchant users of the Companyʼs services; ● External vendors, including individuals; or ● Any other transactions similar to the above. Additionally, if the Japan Compliance Team identifies any individuals suspected of being associated with ASF during the onboarding-screening process, they must document and retain evidence of these findings. For our new hires, Block's Talent Team will conduct a preliminary screening to ensure that individuals with ties to ASF are not employed, and will determine employment eligibility accordingly. For external vendors with whom our company enters into agreements, Bloc
documents.FC-043: U.S. Know Your Customer Standard Square | US Last Approved: Jan 6, 2026 Effective Date: Jan 6, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: The U.S. Know Your Customer ("KYC") Standard ("Standard") outlines Square's KYC Program ("KYC Program") to ensure the business reasonably knows its customers and the associated risks of conducting business with its customers. The KYC Program Lead is responsible for managing this Standard, in accordance with Block, Inc.'s Bank Secrecy Act ("BSA")/Anti-Money Laundering ("AML") Program. Square is committed to maintaining full compliance with the laws and regulations that govern its business. The U.S. KYC Standard covers Square's approach to Customer Identification, Customer Due Diligence ("CDD"), and Enhanced Due Diligence ("EDD"). The Standard is committed to identifying customers who may present a higher risk for money laundering, terrorist financing, identity theft, reputational risk, or other types of illegal activity. Square's U.S. KYC Program Standard enables the business to: ● Know and verify the identity of its customers; ● Understand the nature and purpose of customers' account activity and apply a Customer Risk Rating (“CRRˮ) score; ● Apply enhanced due diligence to higher risk customers; ● Understand the customer's business and expected transactions for each customers' profile; and ● Conduct ongoing customer due diligence ("OCDD") to confirm if customerʼs information is accurate. Square conducts business in compliance with all applicable laws and regulations, in addition to network, partnership requirements and Square's Terms of Service and Payment Terms. The purpose of the U.S. KYC Standard is to ensure that Square reasonably knows its customers and the associated risks of conducting business with its customers and adheres to the Block AML & CTF Policy and related Standard. Scope: This Standard is established by Block, Inc. and is applicable to Square's U.S. products and services. Square uses risk-based controls to evaluate a broad range of customers, which are businesses. This includes sellers that use Square for merchant payment services, including, e-commerce, inventory, appointments, invoicing, customer messaging, and products offered in conjunction with banks such as Square Checking and Square Card. Definitions: Terminology Definition Activation Customer Risk A customer's initial risk rating, which is assigned to new accounts Rating ("aCRR") during onboarding. Appeals The process for reviewing and potentially reactivating accounts that have been frozen or deactivated. Continuous Customer Risk A customer's ongoing risk rating, which is based on a range of Rating ("cCRR") factors including the customer's transacting activity, location, onboarding attributes, connection to other Square accounts, linked instruments and evaluated on a daily basis. Customer Due Diligence The process through which Square reviews seller accounts to ("CDD") understand the nature and purpose of the customer's account activity. CDD reviews include a customer's onboarding information, transactional behavior, linked accounts, web presence, etc. Customer Identification Identity verification ("IDV") via the collection and verification of Program ("CIP") customer information (i.e., name, DOB, address, SSN/TIN, etc). Customer Risk Rating The methodology, created by the Machine Learning ("ML") team, ("CRR") used to assess a customer's profile and assign a risk rating. Declined- Unverified The status assigned to customers when Square is unable to verify Identity their identity. Enhanced Due Diligence EDD reviews are thorough assessments of high-risk customer ("EDD") accounts, which were identified by their CRR or other risk factors. Escalations The process of referring cases to other Square teams under specific circumstances. Terminology Definition Hardblock An automated way of blocking previously deactivated customers from creating new unit-level accounts. Know Your Customer Processes and procedures to reasonably know and understand ("KYC") Program Square customers, their business activities, and associated risks. This includes CIP, CDD and EDD, as described below. The term KYC may also be referred to as Know Your Business KYB when dealing with non-individual entities or business customers. Identity Verification ("IDV") This refers to the procedures Square uses to verify the identity of a customer. Merchant Category Code This code classifies a business by the types of goods or services it ("MCC") provides and assigns a 4-digit number based on the primary offering. Ongoing Customer Due OCDD Reviews are performed at a frequency determined by the Diligence ("OCDD") seller's risk level or triggered by other specific events. Politically Exposed Persons Politically Exposed Persons are individuals who hold or have held ("PEPs") prominent public positions, and, therefore, have a higher risk for money laundering, corruption, bribery, and terrorist financing. Quality Control & The process of reviewing decisions made by an analyst during Assurance CDD and EDD reviews based on procedural guidance and processes applicable to the review and reporting the results to senior leadership. Square Secure Profile A secure question sent to customers to request additional ("SSP") information and/or documentation. Terms of Service Violations To violate Square's Terms of Service, or engage in business ("TOSV") activities prohibited by Square's network partners. 2. Program Requirements 2.1 Overview The U.S. Square KYC Program Standard applies to customers using various Square payments as either an Individual operating a business, or a Business entity. The following compliance controls are used to know and verify the identity of its customers, inclusive of beneficial owners, including but not limited to: ● Customer Identification Program ("CIP") ○ Identity Verification ("IDV") ■ Electronic Identity Verification ("eIDV") for Individuals ■ Documentary Identity Verification ("dIDV") for Individuals ■ Know You Business (“KYBˮ) Entity Verification ● Manual “EIN Onboardingˮ process is used in limited circumstances, generally for large, enterprise merchants that are onboarded via Square Sales channels. Company documentation and information is collected by the Square Sales representatives and sent to Compliance Operations for manual review, verification, and onboarding. ● Due Diligence Reviews ○ CDD ○ Adverse Media ○ EDD ○ Ongoing Customer Due Diligence ("OCDD") ● Sanctions Screening 2.2 Customer Identification Program ("CIP") and Customer Due Diligence ("CDD") CIP enables financial institutions to form a reasonable belief that the institution has established the identity of its customers. During onboarding in the U.S., Square provides all customers with adequate notice of its customer identity verification procedures in the Terms of Service. Square uses electronic and/or documentary IDV procedures to verify the identity of each customer. Square may find it necessary, using a risk-based approach, to implement additional measures for verifying the identity of a customer, or its associated business or business owners, utilizing document-based identity verification and business account verification procedures. 2.2.1 Customer Identification Square's customers create accounts online using an electronic, self-sign up process. Customers provide key information Identity Collection) that is then instantly assessed against third party vendor databases Identity Verification) to ensure Square can reasonably verify the identity of its customers. For sole proprietorships, this process is often referred to as Know Your Customer ("KYC"). The key information collected and verified is: ● Individual's full name; ● Physical residential address; ● Social Security Number ("SSN") or Individual Taxpayer Identification Number ("TIN"); and ● Date of birth. For entities, this process is often referred to as Know Your Business ("KYB"). The key information collected and verified is: ● Legal business name; ● Registered business address; ● Entity type; ● Employer Identification Number ("EIN"); ● Trading name or doing business as ("DBA") name; and ● Personal information for relevant Beneficial Owners, Directors or Account Holders per the KYC flow above. If an individual in the sole proprietorship flow, or individuals attached to an entity, fails electronic verification, they are offered a documentary verification flow. An entity is subject to business verification in addition to the verification of the individuals listed on the business. Also see the “EIN Onboardingˮ process referenced above.) When a customer's identity is satisfactorily verified, multiple additional screenings occur (e.g., evaluation under the Activation Customer Risk Rating model and sanctions screening is conducted) and information is collected about the business they will operate on Square to complete onboarding. Only verified customers can access payments and other products to transfer money. A customer that wishes to only use the Payroll (or Staff) product has separate entity onboarding. A full description of the Customer Identification Program is outlined in the Identity Verification Policy. 2.2.2 Customer Due Diligence Customers provide information about the business they will operate on Square during the onboarding phase to complete Customer Due Diligence. For both individuals and entities, email address, phone number, website (if available) and business type (i.e. products or services offered and against which the Merchant Category Code is assigned), and business structure (i.e. sole-proprietorship, LLC, corporation, etc.) are collected. For individuals, the business name, business address, EIN if available and business type (i.e. products or services offered and against which the Merchant Category Code is assigned) are also collected. This information helps in understanding the nature of the business relationship with Square and the expected behavior of our customers. Square may
documents.FC-044: Square Financial Crimes Compliance: U.S. Transaction Monitoring Program Standard Square | U.S. Last Approved: Mar 27, 2026 Effective Date: Mar 27, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Transaction Monitoring (“TMˮ) Program (“Standardˮ) covers Square, as a business line within Block Inc. (“Blockˮ), which is registered as a money service business (“MSBˮ) with the Financial Crimes Enforcement Network (“FinCENˮ), a bureau of the United States Department of the Treasury. Square is committed to maintaining full compliance with the laws and regulations that govern its business. This Standard covers Squareʼs approach to Transaction Monitoring, a vital component of ensuring BSA/AML compliance as it provides a mechanism for evaluating suspicious activity and transactions across Squareʼs products. The Global Transaction Monitoring & Investigations Program is responsible for managing this Standard and its implementation in accordance with Blockʼs Global Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorist Financing Policy. The purpose of this Standard is to outline Squareʼs approach to monitor, detect, and prevent possible money laundering, financial crimes and terrorist financing for Squareʼs products and customer transactions. Scope: This Standard is established by Block and is applicable to Squareʼs United States (“U.S.ˮ) products and services, such as Square Point of Sale (“POS") and other financial products that process transactions, such as Square Checking, a business checking account, and Square Card, a debit card for Square checking customers, in addition to other products deemed to fall within the scope of this Standard. It should also be noted that certain products within Cash App are also within scope, such as Cash App Invest. More information about these products/queues can be found here with an applicable user guide located here. 1 Definitions: Terminology Definition Transaction Squareʼs dedicated analysts who perform reviews on transaction monitoring Monitoring alerts in accordance with this Standard, procedures and processes. (“TMˮ) Team Suspicious Reports submitted to the Financial Crimes Network (“FinCENˮ) when Activity Report suspicious activity is detected that may be linked to fraud, money laundering, (“SARˮ) terrorist financing, or any other financial crime. Money Service Defined as being a regulated entity that provides financial services outside of Business traditional banking. (“MSBˮ) Financial Crimes The bureau of the U.S. Department of the Treasury responsible for Enforcement safeguarding the financial system from illicit activities. FinCEN collects, Network analyses, and shares financial intelligence to combat these threats and (“FinCENˮ) enforce compliance with regulations designed to protect the financial system. United States One of the many laws in which Square adheres to. This was designed to Bank Secrecy prevent money laundering and other financial crimes. Act (“BSAˮ) Anti-Money Set of laws, regulations and procedures designed to detect and deter money Laundering laundering, terrorist financing, and any other financial crimes. (“AMLˮ) Counter-Terrorist Set of laws, regulations and measures designed to detect, prevent and Financing disrupt the flow of funds of terrorist organizations and activities. (“CTFˮ) Service Level Defines the expected review period for alerts and other responsibilities of the Agreements TM team. (“SLAˮ) Square Point of Squareʼs payment processing system offered to merchants. Sale (“POS") Quality The process of reviewing decisions made by an analyst post TM decisions Assurance by a Quality analyst. Staff References to “staffˮ throughout this document shall refer to all full-time employees and fixed-term and contingent workers including interns, temps, consultants, vendors, third-party providers, and contractors. 2 2. Standard Requirements 2.1. Overview This Standard outlines the compliance controls that are used for the evaluation and management of Squareʼs program as it relates to money laundering, financial crime, and terrorist financing related risks. These controls include, but are not limited to: ● Coverage Assessments; ● Model Governance and Monitoring; ● Transaction Monitoring Alert Review Procedures and Processes; and ● Escalations for Potentially Suspicious Activity. 2.2. Coverage Assessments On an annual basis, Square conducts an annual Anti-Money Laundering (“AMLˮ) and Transaction Monitoring (“TM") Coverage Assessments, also known as Red Flag Assessments. The framework would cover the following steps of coverage assessment: ● Identify and confirm in-scope products, product features, geographies. ● Evaluate mapping of regulatory red flags to AML typologies and associated products. ● Identify the triggering events that would initiate a review of the coverage assessment. ● Product and control lineage over AML Red flags/Typologies. ● Identification and evaluation of potential coverage gaps. ● Document the remediation of identified coverage gaps, as well as the formal acceptance of risks that cannot be reasonably remediated or do not warrant monitoring coverage, including the supporting rationale and justification. The Technology Enablement team will evaluate the results of the Coverage Assessment in partnership with Compliance Engineering & Technology and ensure sufficient coverage and monitoring is implemented in accordance with Model Governance and Monitoring below for program effectiveness. 2.3. Model Governance and Monitoring Square utilizes various proprietary algorithms to create alerts for the Transaction Monitoring team to review. The creation, review and governance of these models is covered by the Model Governance Program and Model Change Management Process. These models can include generating automated alerts in queues and periodic or manual reports for review. Models cover multiple typologies to detect anomalous activity as outlined in the Coverage Assessment, including, but not limited to: ● Structuring; ● Profiling; ● Velocity; 3 ● Lack of Economic Purpose; ● Patterning; ● High Risk Geography; and ● Funnelling. Squareʼs Transaction Monitoring team queues are established and maintained by the Compliance Engineering & Technology team. This team will evaluate effectiveness of the alerts generated by queues (i.e. productivity) and make recommendations for tuning the models, adding further models or changing existing models. Any new models or recommended changes to the existing models are reviewed and approved in accordance with the Model Change Management Process. The current set of models maintained by the Compliance Engineering & Technology team are contained within the Square - US/CA TM Rulebook. To maintain oversight over the Transaction Monitoring team queues, Square has implemented change management and model validation policies that are managed by the Model Risk Management team and executed by the Compliance Engineering & Technology team. In accordance with requirements issued by U.S. and international laws, Square uses a Block - Model Change Management Procedure to establish a standardized process for creating, modifying, and deprecating compliance-related models and heuristics (e.g. queues). This includes the following requirements, which Square has incorporated into its Model Governance Program with respect to transaction monitoring models and technologies: ● On an annual basis, the BSA/AML & Sanctions team will at a minimum, review the TMCA for completeness and accuracy. The Transaction Monitoring Coverage Assessment supports Block in assessing the completeness and appropriateness of its automated transaction monitoring systems in accordance with regulatory guidance based on relevant typologies (or red flags) appropriate for the size, nature, and product set that Square offers; ● Periodic reviews and testing of the accuracy of Squareʼs filtering systems and transaction monitoring systems to policy and program level documentation; ● Maintenance of documentation relating to the configuration of Squareʼs filtering systems and transaction monitoring rules; ● Approval processes for changes to configurations within the filtering systems and transaction monitoring systems, including approval from delegates of the BSA/AML Compliance Officer. JIRA tickets are created to document approvals for model changes as required under the Compliance Change Management Procedure; and ● The completion of an independent assessment to support the BSA/AML Compliance Officerʼs annual NYDFS Regulation 504 certification concerning Blockʼs monitoring and filtering programs. 2.4. Transaction Monitoring Alert Review Procedures and Processes The Financial Crimes Transaction Monitoring team establishes and maintains procedures and processes for conducting manual reviews of the alerts generated. Using the procedures and processes, the team provides initial assessments of alerted or referred merchant activity, and serves as the first level triage function within the Compliance Operations team. The team evaluates the alerted or referred merchant activity to determine if further investigation is required by the SAR team. The Transaction Monitoring program operates by reviewing alerts within SLAs). Alerts are 4 investigated in accordance with the Transaction Monitoring teams standard operating processes and procedures. A Transaction Monitoring analyst reviews alerts based on written procedures that are routinely reviewed and updated. Based on these procedures, the Transaction Monitoring team investigates a case by taking the following ʼs case investigation review process is as follows: ● Analyst claims a new alert case in the Case Management System and notes typologies, alert type, and product; and ● Analyst reviews customer information in conjunction with account activity, web presence, and potentially adverse media in order to make a determination of an appropriate case action. Following that, the Transaction Monitoring analyst can take one of the following actions: ● Clea
documents.GOV-001: Controlling the Assault of Non-Solicited Pornography and Marketing Act (“CANSPAMˮ) Policy Block, Inc. Last Approved: Oct 30, 2025 Effective Date: Oct 30, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: The Controlling the Assault of Non-Solicited Pornography And Marketing Act (“CANSPAMˮ) Policy demonstrates Block, Inc.'s commitment to comply with the CANSPAM Act across all its subsidiaries and operations. This policy establishes comprehensive guidelines governing Block's commercial email practices while maintaining brand-specific flexibility where needed. The CANSPAM Act serves as a crucial regulatory framework enacted to reduce spam and unsolicited pornography by prohibiting senders of unsolicited commercial email messages from disguising the source and content of their messages, while ensuring consumers maintain control over their email communications. CANSPAM requires Block to follow specific procedures when initiating email messages to customers or prospective customers, including obtaining affirmative consent, ensuring message content is not false or misleading, and providing recipients with the ability to opt-out of future correspondence. Scope: This Policy applies to Block, Inc. and its applicable U.S. subsidiaries and affiliates (collectively, "Block") that send commercial email messages. The only exception is Square Financial Services (“SFSˮ), Blockʼs Utah state-chartered Industrial Bank federally regulated by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation, which has its own Compliance Program. It governs all commercial emails sent by or on behalf of Block, including messages with both commercial and transactional content, and third-party email services. This Policy applies to Square, Cash App, and Afterpay's commercial email communications but does not apply to purely transactional messages or internal communications. 1 Definitions: Terminology Definition Affirmative As it relates to commercial electronic mail ("Email") messages, Affirmative Consent Consent occurs when the Recipient expressly consents to receive the message, either in response to a clear and conspicuous request for such consent or at the Recipient's own initiative. If the message is from a party other than the party to which the Recipient communicated such consent, the Recipient must have been given clear and conspicuous notice at the time the consent was communicated that their Email address could be transferred to such other party for the purpose of initiating Commercial Email Messages. Commercial Any Email message the Primary Purpose of which is to advertise or promote Email Message for a commercial purpose, a commercial product or service including content on the Internet. An Email message would not be considered to be a Commercial Email Message solely because such a message includes a reference to a commercial entity that serves to identify the Sender or a reference or link to an Internet website operated for a commercial purpose. Primary Purpose The Primary Purpose of an Email message will be deemed to be commercial if it: ● Contains only the commercial advertisement or promotion of a commercial product or service ● Contains both commercial content and "transactional or relationship" content if either: ○ A Recipient reasonably interpreting the subject line of the Email message would likely conclude that the message contains commercial content; or ○ The Email message's "transactional or relationship" content does not appear in whole or substantial part at the beginning of the body of the message ● Contains both commercial content as well as content that is not transactional or relationship content if a Recipient reasonably interprets either: ○ The subject line of the Email message would likely conclude that the message contains commercial content; or ○ The body of the message would likely conclude that the Primary Purpose of the message is commercial Recipient An authorized user of the Email address to which the message was sent or delivered. 2 Sender A person who initiates an Email message and whose product, service, or Internet website is advertised or promoted by the message. Staff Member(s) References to "staff" throughout this document shall refer to all full-time Employees and fixed-term and contingent workers including interns, temps, consultants, vendors, third-party providers, and contractors. Transactional or An Email message with the Primary Purpose of facilitating, completing, or Relationship confirming a commercial transaction that the Recipient had previously Email agreed to enter into; to provide warranty, product recall, or safety/security information; or subscription, membership, account, loan, or other information relating to an ongoing purchase or use. 2. Block, Inc. Requirements 2.1 General Requirements Block ensures all commercial email communications comply with CANSPAM requirements. This includes emails sent directly by Block or through third parties on Block's behalf. Block maintains appropriate controls to ensure compliance across all brands and communication types. 2.2 Message Classification and Content Requirements Commercial Email Classification Block classifies email messages based on their primary purpose to determine applicable CANSPAM requirements. Messages are evaluated to determine whether they are: ● Commercial messages promoting products or services ● Transactional or relationship messages facilitating existing services ● Mixed-purpose messages containing both commercial and transactional content Content Standards All commercial email messages must include: ● Accurate header information that identifies the sending source ● Subject lines that accurately reflect message content ● Clear identification as advertisements when required ● Valid physical postal address ● Visible and operable unsubscribe mechanism ● Required regulatory disclosures based on message content 2.3 Recipient Rights and Controls Opt-Out Requirements 3 Block maintains comprehensive opt-out processes that: ● Provide clear and conspicuous opt-out mechanisms ● Honor opt-out requests within 10 business days ● Maintain opt-out functionality for 30 days after message delivery ● Prohibit charging fees or requiring additional information for opt-outs ● Prevent sale or transfer of email addresses after opt-out Consent Management Block maintains appropriate controls for: ● Obtaining and documenting affirmative consent ● Managing consent across brands and product lines ● Honoring recipient preferences ● Maintaining records of consent and opt-out requests 2.4 Third-Party Management Block maintains oversight of third parties involved in commercial email communications through: ● Clear contractual requirements for CANSPAM compliance ● Established approval processes for email content and configuration ● Monitoring of third-party compliance with requirements ● Integration of opt-out processes across platforms 2.5 Transactional or Relationship Email Exclusions Block ensures transactional or relationship messages: ● Prioritize transactional content at the beginning of messages ● Maintain clear distinction from commercial content ● Include required account servicing information ● Meet timing requirements for critical notifications 3. Brand-Specific Requirements Block implements CANSPAM requirements across its subsidiaries while recognizing the unique characteristics of each business line's email communications. Detailed procedures for implementation are maintained in subsidiary-specific standards and procedures. 4 3.1 Cash App Cash App's implementation of CANSPAM requirements addresses the specific needs of consumer financial services: Message Classification ● Financial transaction confirmations and security alerts are treated as transactional ● Investment-related communications must meet additional regulatory requirements ● Promotional content must be clearly distinguished from account servicing communications Consumer Protection Requirements ● Essential account and security notifications must be maintained regardless of marketing preferences ● Investment and cryptocurrency promotional content must include required disclosures ● Cross-border payment communications must meet additional regulatory requirements 3.2 Afterpay Afterpay's implementation focuses on the unique aspects of BNPL services: Payment Communications ● Payment schedule and confirmation messages are treated as transactional ● Marketing communications must be clearly separated from payment notifications ● Merchant partnership promotions require clear identification of all parties Consumer Requirements ● Payment-related communications must be maintained regardless of marketing preferences ● Credit-related disclosures must meet additional regulatory requirements ● Shop Directory promotional content must clearly identify merchant relationships The above brand-specific requirements supplement the general compliance TILA requirements outlined in this Policy but are only applicable to their respective brands (i.e., Square Banking requirements apply only to Square Banking operations, Cash App requirements apply only to Cash App operations, and Afterpay requirements apply only to Afterpay operations). 5 4. Compliance & Enforcement 4.1 Roles and Responsibilities: Block, Inc. Roles: ● Compliance Policy Committee (“CPCˮ): ○ Provides oversight and governance of applicable Compliance Policies and is responsible for reviewing and and approving this policy annually to ensure alignment with regulatory requirements and expectations ○ Providing guidance on significant policy matters ○ Ensuring consistency across Blockʼs Compliance Policy Framework ● Block Inc, Chief Compliance Officer (“CCOˮ) ○ Provides assistance and support in implementing this Policy across the organization ○ Establishes appropriate practices designed to ensure Company operations reflect Block's commitment to compliance ○ Ensures all Employees are aware of their compliance responsibilities ○ Monitors and reports on compliance w
