Definitions Terminology Definition Afterpay Afterpay Australia Pty Ltd ABN 15 169 342 947, Australian Credit Licence 527911 Afterpay BNPL Afterpay’s buy now, pay later, product. Afterpay credit Afterpay’s internal credit rating for each existing customer which is profile primarily based on Afterpay’s internal data on that customer. Customers are only allocated an Afterpay credit profile 43 days after they are onboarded by Afterpay to use Afterpay BNPL. Afterpay’s internal data (and therefore the Afterpay credit profile) is refreshed on a daily basis. Afterpay internal As defined in section 4.4.2 of this policy. assessment Afterpay internal Includes a number of data points about the customer and their use of data Afterpay BNPL, including the following: ● tenure using Afterpay BNPL (calculated from the date of the customer’s first order); ● number of on-time payments within the previous 180 days; ● late payments, including number of late payments and number of days late; ● orders declined for insufficient funds on linked card at time of first payment; and ● average utilisation of Afterpay BNPL within the previous 30, 60, 90, 180 days. Afterpay Plus An additional feature of Afterpay BNPL that allows a customer to use Afterpay BNPL almost anywhere Apple Pay, Google Pay, or Samsung Pay is accepted, online or in-store. Afterpay As defined in section 4.4.1 of this policy. vulnerability indicators comply with In respect of the financial obligations of the customer, includes the substantial customer complying by requiring an unrealistic or unachievable reduction hardship in outgoings to cover the cost of living. consumer lease As defined in section 204 of Schedule 1 of the NCCPA (National Credit Code). credit decision As defined in section 4.2 of this policy. Terminology Definition credit report A credit report provided by a credit reporting body containing credit information in relation to an Afterpay customer (or prospective Afterpay customer). credit reporting As defined in section 5(1) of the NCCPA. body customer A customer of Afterpay. LCCC or low cost As defined in the Afterpay General Terms. credit contract MACC or medium As defined in section 204 of Schedule 1 of the NCCPA (National Credit amount credit Code). contract NCCPA National Consumer Credit Consumer Act 2009 (Cth), as amended by the Treasury Laws Amendment (Responsible Buy Now Pay Later and Other Measures) Act 2024 (Cth). NCCPR National Consumer Credit Protection Regulations 2010 (Cth), as amended by the National Consumer Credit Protection Amendment (Low Cost Credit) Regulations 2025 (Cth). new to bureau As defined in section 4.3.1 of this policy. report or NTB report policy This Unsuitability Assessment Policy. SACC or small As defined in section 5 of the NCCPA. amount credit contract spend limit As defined in the Afterpay General Terms. unsuitability The process required by sections 128 and 131 of the NCCPA, to determine assessment whether a low cost credit contract or credit limit increase would be unsuitable for a customer. $ References to dollar ($) amounts in this policy are references to Australian Dollars. 2. Purpose This policy outlines Afterpay’s approach to conducting unsuitability assessments in accordance with the NCCPA and the NCCPR. 3. Scope This policy applies to Afterpay BNPL, as a low cost credit contract under the NCCPA, in relation to all unsuitability assessments conducted by or on behalf of Afterpay. 4. Unsuitability assessment framework Afterpay has a structured framework for completing unsuitability assessments. 4.1. General scope of unsuitability assessment The NCCPA provides that a credit contract or credit limit increase is unsuitable if, at the time of the assessment: ● the customer will be unable to comply with their financial obligations under the credit contract, or could only comply with substantial hardship; or ● the contract does not meet the customer’s requirements or objectives. 4.2. Timing of unsuitability assessment In this policy, the following actions are each referred to as a credit decision: ● entering into a low cost credit contract with a customer (onboarding); or ● increasing a customer’s spend limit under an existing Afterpay low cost credit contract (limit increase). No more than 120 days before making a credit decision, Afterpay must: ● make inquiries and take steps in accordance with section 130 of the NCCPA, as set out in this policy; and ● complete an unsuitability assessment based on those inquiries and steps. Where Afterpay completes an unsuitability assessment for a customer, Afterpay may rely on the inquiries made, and steps taken, on which the unsuitability assessment was based, to complete more than one unsuitability assessment. 4.3. Reasonable inquiries and reasonable steps 4.3.1. Reasonable inquiries - financial situation Afterpay seeks to obtain the following information about a customer’s financial situation: ● Income and expenses: Afterpay collects information about the customer’s income and expenses. This information is obtained directly from the customer through the onboarding flow and / or through an in-app flow, depending on the circumstances. Afterpay will not proceed with an unsuitability assessment unless this information has been obtained. ● Existing credit obligations: Afterpay collects information about the customer’s low cost credit contracts, small amount credit contracts, and consumer leases. This information is obtained from the customer’s credit report. Where Afterpay has obtained a credit report (including a credit report that shows the customer has no reported credit information (a NTB report)) Afterpay will proceed with an unsuitability assessment . ● Credit check: Afterpay seeks to obtain a ‘negative’ credit report from a credit reporting body, illion.1 Where Afterpay is unable to seek to obtain a credit report due to technical difficulties (e.g. bureau outage) Afterpay will not proceed with an unsuitability assessment. 4.3.2. Reasonable inquiries - requirements and objectives Afterpay seeks to obtain information about the customer’s requirements and objectives by presenting the Afterpay BNPL key product information and having the customer confirm that the product is right for them. This information is obtained directly from the customer through the onboarding flow and / or through an in-app flow, depending on the circumstances. Afterpay will not proceed with an unsuitability assessment unless this information has been obtained. Afterpay does not rely on the rebuttable presumption available for LCCCs under section 133BXF of the NCCPA. 4.3.3. Reasonable steps - verification Afterpay takes reasonable steps to verify the information obtained about a customer’s financial situation by leveraging the information available in the customer’s credit report. 4.4. Unsuitability assessment decisions 4.4.1. Credit decision at onboarding Afterpay will assess a credit decision as unsuitable at onboarding in the following situations: ● The customer’s income is less than their expenses. 1 Afterpay does not offer Afterpay LCCCs or limit increases on existing Afterpay LCCCs over $1,999. ● The customer has one or more of the Afterpay vulnerability indicators present on their credit report. The Afterpay vulnerability indicators are: ○ a bankruptcy within the previous 12 months; ○ more than four SACC inquiries within the previous 6 months; ○ more than four BNPL inquiries within the previous 6 months; ○ more than four equipment hire (i.e. consumer lease) inquiries within the previous 6 months; or ○ a default within the previous 3 months. ● Afterpay is otherwise aware of information that evidences that: ○ the customer will be unable to comply with their financial obligations under the contract, or could only comply with substantial hardship; or ○ the contract will not meet the customer's requirements or objectives (even if the customer has confirmed that the product is right for them during onboarding). 4.4.2. Credit decision for a spend limit increase Afterpay completes an internal assessment to determine whether a customer is eligible for a spend limit increase (Afterpay internal assessment). Afterpay only completes unsuitability assessments where a customer has consented to limit increases and passed an Afterpay internal assessment. Under an Afterpay internal assessment, a customer will not be eligible for a spend limit increase if the customer: ● has a current spend limit above $1900; ● has a frozen Afterpay account; ● has requested a spend limit decrease (and this request has not expired or been revoked); ● has had a late payment in the previous 30 days; ● has had a late payment exemption applied due to goodwill within the previous 30 days; ● has moved a repayment due date in the previous 30 days; ● has had a spend limit increase in the previous 30 days; ● does not have a high enough Afterpay credit prof
documents.GOV-003: Incident Response Management Plan Afterpay Canada Last Approved: August 8, 2025 Effective Date: August 8, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: Afterpay Canada (“Afterpayˮ) is a British Columbia incorporated entity that offers “buy now, pay laterˮ installment products in Canada. Afterpay Canada is registered as a Payment Service Provider (“PSPˮ) with the Bank of Canada and subject to the Retail Payment Activities Act (“RPAAˮ). Additionally, Afterpay maintains a credit license in Nova-Scotia, New Brunswick and Saskatchewan. This document outlines Afterpay Canada Afterpay)ʼs internal processes for detecting, responding to and recovering from incidents and reporting of incidents that may affect the confidentiality, integrity, or availability of systems, services and operations involved in the delivery of Afterpayʼs retail payment activities and in accordance with the RPAA. This document is to be read together with the Block Incident Management Standard , the Block Issue Management Program, the Privacy Incident Runbook and the Afterpay Incident Management Policy and sets out the plan to be followed to identify and respond to threats and incidents in a coordinated and efficient manner, assess and determine if an incident is material and reportable to the Bank of Canada (“BoCˮ) in accordance with Afterpayʼs obligations as a PSP and subject to the RPAA and while ensuring that remediation is completed with minimal disruption in accordance with stipulated SLAs. Scope: This document applies to all Afterpay related payment services, systems, staff, and third party vendors that support any Afterpay related payment activities within the scope of the RPAA. References to "staff" throughout this document shall refer to all full-time employees and fixed-term and contingent workers including interns, temps, consultants, vendors, third-party providers, and contractors engaged to work on Afterpay matters. Definitions: Terminology Definition The Retail Payment Activities The Retail Payment Activities Act is a Canadian federal law that Act establishes a regulatory framework for the oversight of retail payment service providers (“PSPsˮ) Terminology Definition Bank of Canada Bank of Canada is the supervisory authority under the RPAA and acts as the regulator for retail payment service providers in Canada. Block, Inc. Block, Inc., a Delaware corporation and the ultimate parent of Afterpay Canada. Governance Risk and An internal governance committee made up of Senior Compliance Committee Management responsible for the oversight of Afterpay's compliance with applicable laws. Incident Any event or series of related events that is unplanned, unexpected or adverse and that results in or could reasonably be expected to result in the reduction, deterioration or breakdown of any retail payment activity that is performed by Afterpay Canada and/or the systems, data and information involved in the Afterpay Canada's performance of its retail payment activities. Reportable Incident An incident that has a material impact on an end user, Afterpay Canada's operations. 2. Objectives ● Outline RPAA Operational Risk Incident Management and Reporting requirements ● Map compliance to existing processes ● Define roles & responsibilities ● Document data sources and validation procedures ● Establish a workflow to efficiently and effectively report a major RPAA Operational Risk Incident, inclusive of identification and classification of an incident, internal and external communication channels, escalation procedures, and notification timeline requirements ● Align with Block's existing Security IRP and Block's Information Security Response plan ● Ensure compliance with detection, classification, notification and reporting obligations under the RPAA 3. Overview Under RPAA, incidents are categorised broadly into four types: security, operational, fraud and non-compliance.. Security incidents include events like cyberattacks, data breaches, or any unauthorized access that compromises data authenticity, integrity or confidentiality. Operational incidents refer to disruptions that affect service continuity and availability, such as system outages or failures in critical infrastructure. Fraud include events involving actual or attempted fraudulent activities, misappropriation of funds, identity theft affecting end-user accounts. Non-compliance includes events that could impact Afterpayʼs compliance status such as violations of RPAA requirements or applicable privacy laws. All categories of incidents include incidents caused by Afterpay or its third-parties or vendors. Only incidents that have been assessed and deemed to have a material impact on Afterpayʼs operations and end users are reportable to the Bank of Canada under RPAA. Afterpay leverages Block-foundational resources to respond to, contain, remediate and resolve a Security Incident. Blockʼs Detection and Response Team (“DARTˮ) supports security incidents, while Site Reliability Engineering (“SREˮ) supports operational incidents (also known as SEVs) and the Compliance Issue Management Team supports fraud and non-compliance related incidents. This document will often refer back to each teamʼs respective incident handling procedures and will only outline the RPAA-specific requirements. 4. Background The RPAA is a Canadian regulation introduced by the Bank of Canada to establish a regulatory framework for the oversight of PSPs and to protect the integrity of payment services, and the interest of end users. As a PSP operating under RPAA, Afterpay is required to comply with these requirements and by implementing this plan, Afterpay demonstrates its commitment to operational resilience, end-user protection, and regulatory compliance. 5. Prerequisite Reading This document does not replace Block's existing incident plans and runbooks, rather it is to be used as an extension of existing processes to ensure Afterpay's alignment with the RPAA. It is recommended that DRIs directly or indirectly impacted by this plan familiarize themselves with the following: ● BLOCK INFORMATION SECURITY INCIDENT RESPONSE PLAN ● Security Incident Response Runbook ● [Engineering] Incident Management at Square (go/sev) ● Incident Severity Definitions & Thresholds ● Incident Management Roles & Responsibilities ● Counsel SEV Runbook - Privileged and Confidential ● Afterpay Incident Management Policy ● Privacy Incident Runbook 6. Program Requirements 6.1 Identification of Incidents Incidents may be identified through any of the following sources: ● Automated monitoring systems ● End-user reports or escalations ● Internal staff ● Third-party notifications ● Compliance, Regulatory or law enforcement alerts Incidents will be assessed by the Afterpay Compliance Team, Risk and Legal Counsel as to whether they are deemed reportable incidents under the RPAA. Refer to the Afterpay Canada incident materiality threshold guidance Appendix A. Block Incident Identification ● Security and Operational incidents are identified in a variety of ways. We will continue following the identification methods listed in Blockʼs IR and Square Engineering Incident Management. plans. ● Incidents impacting Afterpayʼs critical or important functions – where malicious unauthorized access to network and information systems is identified, or where there is an adverse impact on the availability, authenticity, integrity, or confidentiality of data – will be assigned a criticality of P0/P1/SEV0/SEV1. Designations will consider other factors such as trend and impact assessments. For more information on security incident classifications, refer to Blockʼs incident response plans and runbooks. ● All P0, P1 and SEV0, SEV1 incidents impacting Afterpay International and/or international Afterpay users, regardless of impact to Afterpayʼs CIF, will be escalated to the Afterpay Incident Team. Security Incidents ● Analysis of past security incidents affecting Afterpay suggests material impacts on Afterpay's CIFs and generally P1 incidents. ● While P2 incidents generally do not meet the reportable criteria thresholds, incident teams will share information as it becomes available, but no later than post-containment, to ensure readiness in the event that criticality increases. The escalation channel will be done via Slack at #afterpay-rpaa-incident-team on Slack. ● Incidents with a P0/P1 designation will be escalated to the Afterpay Incident Team via Slack #afterpay-rpaa-incident-team on Slack. Operational Incidents SEVs ● Operational incidents, also known as Engineering SEVs, ALERTs, or just SEVs, are managed differently from security incidents. These are typically created and managed by the engineering teams that support the impacted service. ● Go/sevlevels requires a minimum designation of SEV1 for incidents with reputational or regulatory concerns. ● Incidents with a SEV0/SEV1 designation impacting the Afterpay will be escalated to the Afterpay Incident team via the Incident.io and PagerDuty integration, prompting an email, text, and phone call. ● While SEV2 incidents generally do not meet the reportable criteria thresholds, Incident.io will still notify the Afterpay Incident team when a SEV2 impacting Afterpay is declared to ensure readiness, in the event that criticality increases. ● The first 24 hours of the incident are the most uncertain. Engineering teams will have more information on impact upon stabilization 4hrs from incident start time). Fraud and Non-Compliance Incidents ● All fraud and non-compliance incidents are promptly reported within 48 hours in accordance with the Block Issue Management Program. These categories of incidents must be promptly identified, assessed, and escalated to the issue management team in coordination with AP Canada compliance, legal and risk teams to ensure containment, preservation of evidence and where applicable, regulatory reporting to the Board of Canada (refer to Reporting Incidents section). documents.GOV-004: Afterpay Third Party Risk Management Policy Afterpay Canada Limited Last Approved: Aug 8, 2025 Effective Date: Aug 8, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Afterpay Third Party Risk Management Policy ("Policy") outlines Afterpay Canada Limited's approach to managing third-party risks across its operations. Afterpay Canada Limited ("Afterpay") is a registered Payment Service Provider (“PSPˮ) operating under the Retail Payment Activities Act (“RPAAˮ) and regulated by the Bank of Canada. As part of its obligations under the RPAA, Afterpay must ensure that its third-party risk management practices are robust, proportionate to risk, and aligned with the expectations set forth under the RPAA. This policy establishes Afterpay's framework for identifying, assessing, managing, and monitoring risks associated with third-party vendors, with particular focus on Third-Party Service Providers (“TPSPsˮ) as defined under the RPAA. The purpose of this Policy is to establish a comprehensive framework for managing third-party risks across Afterpay's operations. It sets out the principles, responsibilities, and controls required to ensure that all TPSP arrangements are assessed and governed in a manner that is consistent with regulatory expectations and industry best practices. Scope: This document is established by Afterpay and is applicable globally across all business lines or functions that support Afterpay. References to "staff" throughout this document shall refer to all Afterpay full-time employees and fixed-term and contingent workers including interns, temps, consultants, vendor personnel, third-party provider personnel, and contractors. This document applies to all employees and affiliates acting for or on behalf of Afterpay, as well as to all functions that support Afterpay's operations. Definitions: Terminology Definition Afterpay The consumer-facing product provided by Afterpay Canada Ltd that offers, among other things, 'Buy Now Pay Later' functionality. Terminology Definition Bank of Canada Regulator responsible for administering and enforcing the RPAA, including registering PSPs and supervising their compliance with the legislation. Block, Inc. Block, Inc., a Delaware corporation and the ultimate parent of Afterpay. TPSP Service Services provided by a TPSP that impacts the provision, availability or performance of one or more of Afterpay's retail payment activities. Third Party Service Provider A TPSP is any external third party or intra-group entity (“TPSPˮ) that provides Afterpay with services supporting Afterpay's retail payment activities, as defined under the Retail Payment Activities Act. RPAA Retail Payment Activities Act Service Arrangements A contractual agreement between Afterpay and a third-party provider outlining the specific services, deliverables, and performance expectations for the service being provided. Third Party Vendors Afterpay Third Party Vendors are non-TPSPs that provide services which are unrelated to Afterpay's provision of retail payment services. TPSP Register A document that contains the risk profiles of each TPSP for Afterpay, including TPSP's inherent risks, controls, findings and ongoing monitoring and performance results. TPSP Determination Assessment An assessment tool used to determine applicability of third-party arrangements that support Afterpay's retail payment functions and operations. 2. Policy/Program Requirements 2.1 RPAA Obligations Under the RPAA, a TPSP means an individual or entity that, under a contract, provides a PSP with a service related to a payment function and that is not an employee or agent or mandatary of the payment service provider. If Afterpay receives services from a third party service provider, Afterpay must assess each third-party service provider supporting retail payment-related functions at least annually and prior to entering into, renewing, extending, or significantly amending any contract. These assessments must evaluate the provider's data protection capabilities, system connection security, operational risk management practices, change notification procedures, and performance monitoring mechanisms, including breach reporting protocols. Afterpay must maintain records of the scope, timing, and findings of these assessments. Responsibilities between Afterpay and each third-party service provider must be clearly defined, particularly regarding the ownership, confidentiality, integrity, and availability of data and information. 2.2 Afterpay TPSP Arrangements TPSP arrangements may involve either external vendors or Afterpay affiliates that support payment-related functions. All third-party arrangements must first undergo an assessment to determine whether they meet the definition of a TPSP under the RPAA. If determined to be a TPSP, the arrangement will then be subject to applicable RPAA requirements. For all TPSPs, adherence to RPAA obligations must be maintained throughout the full lifecycle of the TPSP arrangement—from initial assessment and onboarding through to ongoing monitoring, renewal, and termination. This Policy describes Afterpay's standard requirements to manage the third party risks arising from TPSP activities, both to external third party vendors and Afterpay intra-group arrangements with Afterpay affiliates. Implementation of the standards laid out in this policy will ensure that Afterpay has a robust framework to ensure the following: ● The Afterpay Board is responsible for overseeing the CCO and senior management in the execution of their responsibilities related to all third-party arrangements, associated risks, and activities. The Board also ensures that this Policy remains aligned with Afterpay's risk appetite and strategic objectives. ● A risk management and internal control framework is embedded to enable effective oversight of TPSP activities by the CCO, who also serves as the Designated Senior Officer, along with other risk DRIs; ● That the impact of any TPSP arrangement on Afterpay's retail payment activities is fully considered and understood; and that Afterpay retains the competence and ability to assess the TPSPs performance; ● That all TPSP Arrangements are formalised in a TPSP Contract between Afterpay and the TPSP, that sets out all respective roles, responsibilities, rights, and obligations and meets the requirements set out in this Policy; ● That the TPSP has adequate risk management and internal control systems in place, and that Afterpay incorporates the oversight of the TPSP's functions and activities into its own risk management framework; ● That the TPSP has sufficient resources in place in terms of quality, quantity, and availability to perform the contracted functions and activities; ● That the TPSP is compliant with relevant confidentiality and sensitive data provisions and has adequate contingency arrangements in place; ● That Afterpay notify the relevant authorities, including the Bank of Canada (“BoCˮ) in a timely manner and in line with regulatory notification requirements of any major operational or security incidents involving the TPSP; ● That Afterpay has access to perform audit and/or assurance reviews of the TPSP and the TPSP will be cooperative with this audit/assurance review; and ● That retail payment related services provided by a TPSP do not undermine the continuous service to Afterpay end-users. 2.3 Risk-Based Approach Afterpay adopts a risk-based approach to third-party risk management, ensuring that the depth and frequency of oversight is proportional to the level of risk of each arrangement and whether a third party vendor is a TPSP or not. All third-party services are assessed using relevant Afterpay and Block TPRM risk assessment methodologies. This includes but is not limited to evaluating the potential for operational risk, service disruption, reliance on subcontractors, the business continuity and disaster recovery plan of the provider, and alignment with regulatory requirements under the RPAA and other applicable regulatory requirements. TPSP determination assessments are conducted using an assessment tool to identify third-party arrangements that support Afterpay's retail payment activities and related operations. These assessments inform the classification of arrangements and the level of due diligence, monitoring, and contractual control required. Periodic reviews ensure reassessments remain current with the evolving operational risk landscape. 2.4 TPRM Process 2.4.1 TPSP Determination Assessment Not all Afterpay Third Party Vendors will be regarded as TPSPs that are subject to the RPAA requirements. To identify TPSPs that provide retail payment related services to Afterpay, Afterpay has developed a 'TPSP Determination Assessment Tool'. This tool is completed for any prospective third party vendor or intra-group arrangement at the initial onboarding stage and periodically. The Governance and Oversight Lead is responsible for initiating the TPSP Determination Assessment, which must be completed by the Service Recipient to assess whether a third party vendor meets the definition of a TPSP under this policy. The Service Recipient must complete the assessment and submit it for review and approval when: 1. Entering into a Third Party Vendor or intra-group arrangement; 2. Scheduled annual review of a TPSP occurs; 3. Afterpay plans to make a substantive change to their use of a particular Third Party Vendor or a TPSP; or 4. There is a change in service provision. The output of any TPSP Determination Assessment, once completed, is sent to the Afterpay Governance, Risk and Compliance Committee (“GRCˮ) and other relevant committees for review and approval. 2.4.2 Due Diligence Before engaging any Third Party Vendor, Afterpay conducts a comprehensive due diligence review to assess the provider's capability, regulatory compliance framework, and risk controls. The due diligence evaluates both qualitative and quantitative factors, including business reputation, experience, regulato
documents.GOV-005: Record Retention Policy Afterpay Canada | Square Canada Last Approved: Sep 8, 2025 Effective Date: Sep 8, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Record Retention Policy (the “Policyˮ) outlines Afterpay Canada and Square Canadaʼs approach to recordkeeping requirements in accordance with the Retail Payment Activities Act (“RPAAˮ) and applies to all documents, records, data and communications, whether paper or electronic, held and managed by Afterpay Canada Limited (“Afterpayˮ) and Square Canada Incorporated (“Squareˮ), and its affiliates for the purposes of complying with the RPAA requirements. The purpose of this Policy is to establish record retention guidelines governing Afterpay and Square, and its affiliates, ensuring consistent operations while maintaining brand-specific flexibility and in compliance with the RPAA. Scope: This Policy applies to Afterpay and Square, including all business areas supporting both entities. References to “staffˮ throughout this document shall refer to all full-time employees and fixed-term and contingent workers including interns, temps, consultants, vendors, third-party providers, and contractors engaged to work on Afterpay and/or Square matters. This Policy does not provide exhaustive, detailed guidance on the technical implementation of data retention; instead it provides retention periods and general guidance aimed at supporting individuals and teams who own and manage RPAA relevant records and/or the systems that house them. It is the responsibility of all staff to ensure these obligations, including with partners and vendors are adhered to in accordance with relevant policies and standards. Definitions: Terminology Definition An employee or external worker (e.g., contingent labor, contractors) hired by Staff Member(s) the Afterpay and/or Square, who supports the operations covered by this Policy. Terminology Definition Retention Relates to the continued possession, use, or control of data, and where it is archived or beyond use. Data is no longer retained when it is disposed of (destroyed or erased). Raw facts and statistics collected for reference or analysis and can be Data processed to generate meaningful information. For example, a name, a payment amount, or an account of a meeting that occurred. Data can be in structured (like tables in a relational database) and unstructured systems (like text files) and housed in databases, media devices (e.g. a USB, and in hard copy. It can take on different forms, such as an email, a document, a visual photo, or metadata. Data that is classified as a record that serves as evidence of Afterpay and Record(s) Squareʼs business activities, decisions, and operations, with a defined lifecycle and retention schedule required by the RPAA, ensuring they are preserved or disposed of systematically and in accordance with applicable regulatory requirements. Data that is reasonably identifiable or about an individual as defined under Personal Data our applicable privacy laws. For example, a name, contact and billing details, transaction history, or behaviour profiling. Refer to Section 5 of this Policy for relevant policies and standards related to personal data retention requirements. 2. Record Retention Requirements Under the RPAA Under Section 40 of the RPAA, Payment services providers are required to retain all records related to their compliance with the RPAA requirements especially as it relates to the following obligations. ● Operational risk management and incident response (per RPAA subsection 171 ● Incident notification procedures (per RPAA sections 18 & 19 ● Requirements for holding end-user funds (per RPAA paragraph 201a–c ● All reporting obligations, including: ● Annual reports ● Notices of significant change or new activity ● Registration applications and any updates ● Special audits and related information requests ● Information and assessment fee-related responses To ensure compliance with Section 40 of the RPAA and its supporting regulations, Afterpay and Square will implement and maintain robust recordkeeping practices that align with regulatory expectations and internal standards. The following commitments apply: 2.1. Retention of Records Afterpay and Square will retain all records required under the RPAA for at least five 5 years following the date on which they cease to demonstrate compliance with a current RPAA obligation, unless a longer retention period is required by applicable law or policy. 2.2 Clear and intelligible records Afterpay and Square shall take reasonable measures to ensure that all relevant records are in a format that is clear and intelligible, and maintained in a sufficient manner such that it is readily available and easily retrievable from secured systems of record within a short period of time, and capable of being reviewed by the Bank of Canada or other regulatory authorities upon request; This includes all relevant records that are managed by Afterpay, Square, external vendors, third-party service providers and/or intra-group entities. 2.3. Record Protection and Integrity Afterpay and Square take reasonable measures to retain the records required under the RPAA to prevent loss and destruction, prevent their falsification, detect and correct any inaccuracies contained in them, and prevent unauthorized persons from accessing or using the information contained in them. 2.4. Third Party Service Provider Record Where RPAA-related records are created or stored by an agent, mandatary, Third-Party Service Provider as defined under the RPAA TPSP, or intra-group entity: ● Afterpay and Square will take reasonable measures to ensure that such records remain accessible to Afterpay and Square at all times, and can be made available to the Bank of Canada upon request. ● Afterpay and Square will take reasonable measures to ensure that these records are retained in accordance with the RPAA requirements, including format, retention period, and protective measures. ● Contracts with TPSPs will include recordkeeping clauses, in line with Afterpay and Squareʼs Third Party Risk Management Policy, to ensure compliance with these obligations. 2.5. Governance and Oversight To ensure appropriate governance, accountability, and compliance with the RPAA, Internal Audit and Compliance will periodically review recordkeeping practices as part of the broader RPAA compliance monitoring program. These reviews are intended to ensure: ● Documented procedures are in place to classify, store, and retrieve RPAA-related records effectively; ● Controls for record accessibility, accuracy, and protection are tested periodically to confirm operational effectiveness; ● Third-party oversight is maintained, ensuring external vendors, TPSPs, and intra-group entities meet their contractual recordkeeping obligations in accordance with Section 40 of the RPAA; ● Records are readily available to internal stakeholders, auditors, and regulatory authorities upon request. These reviews support Afterpay and Squareʼs commitment to data integrity, regulatory readiness, continuous improvement of its compliance framework and ongoing compliance with RPAA obligations. 2.6. Retention Schedule All relevant RPAA records must be retained for a period of 5 years following the date such records cease to demonstrate a PSPʼs current compliance with the Act and Regulations. The five-year retention period begins when a record is superseded or no longer used for current compliance, not when it is created. For example, if a policy is updated in 2026, the previous version must be kept until 2031. Record Classification Retention Period Record Type Operational Risk 5 years Required Frameworks, policies and Management Framework procedures under the RPAA ● Operational Risk Management Framework, Policies and Standards ● Incident Response Management Framework, policies and plans ● Third Party Risk Management Framework, policies, procedures and supplementary documents ● Record retention policy and procedures Evidence of document review and approval by relevant approvers including senior officers and the relevant entityʼs Board of Directors where applicable. Applicable only to Square. Safeguarding of End-user 5 years funds ● Daily ledger records per user (including contact information and balances) ● Tracking of funds not yet placed in safeguarding accounts ● Retention of trust agreements, declarations, account agreements, or insurance documentation ● Safeguarding evidence ● Daily reconciliation evidence Evidence of document review and approval by relevant approvers including senior officers and the relevant entityʼs Board of Directors where applicable. Incident Response and 5 years Incident response files Notifications ● records detailing the incident, affected systems, materiality assessment, impact, cause, actions, incident logs and remediation. Internal Escalation ● Evidence of internal reporting paths and decision-making processes for incident classification. Regulator Notifications ● Copies of all incident notifications sent to the Bank of Canada, including timelines and rationale. User & Partner Communications ● Records of notifications sent to impacted users or PSPs, including what was communicated and when. Post-Incident Reviews ● Record lessons learned, process changes, and any updates made to controls or classification criteria. Annual Report Ongoing Reporting 5 years Annual Report) and ● Copies of the annual attestation or Significant Change update submitted under RPAR Notices Section 23 ● Documentation of how data for the update was compiled (e.g., transaction volume, risk profile) ● Internal sign-offs from Compliance, Risk, or the Board, depending on governance structure Significant Change ● All notices of significant change or new activity filed with the Bank of Canada under RPAA Section 15 ● Internal impact assessments or change rationale for submitted updates ● Copies of follow-up communications with the Bank of Canada regarding changes or

documents.GOV-009: Custody and Liquidity Management Policy Block Inc. Last Approved: Mar 25, 2026 Effective Date: Mar 25, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Policy outlines the principles and practices for the custody and liquidity management of Supported Digital Assets at Block, Inc. ("Block"). The implementation of these principles and practices ensure that Block both abides by the laws and regulations that its Supported Digital Assets products are subject to and adopts industry-leading standards and best practices. The development, approval, implementation and ownership of these principles and practices are primarily the responsibility of the Supported Digital Assets Platform Team (“BitPlatˮ Team). The core standards and best practices are anchored in five key areas: Infrastructure, Security, Trading, Operations and Integrity. Each area is managed by dedicated teams with specialized expertise in their respective domains. In collaboration with the BitPlat Team and Business Partners, the Block Risk and Controls Team has memorialized the principles and practices documented in this Policy. Block is required to conduct business in compliance with all applicable laws and regulations. The purpose of this Policy is to document Blockʼs principles and practices so as to evidence compliance with such applicable laws, regulations, industry standards and agreed upon best practices. Scope: The Policy sets out Blockʼs approach for and is applicable to the custody and liquidity management of Supported Digital Assets. 1 Definitions: Terminology Definition Cold Storage Cold Wallets are self-custodial and used to store digital assets offline. Also known as Cold Wallets) Custody The act of safeguarding digital assets. Gateway Wallet Gateway Wallet is a hot wallet that we refer to as “gatewayˮ because it's only used for transfer out of Cold Storage. The wallet is used by Treasury to interimly hold funds with the intention of eventually moving some or all funds to the Hot Wallet. Hot Storage Hot Wallets are connected to the internet and are used to store, send and Also known as receive digital assets. Hot Wallet) Joiners, Movers A process that manages the entire lifecycle of an organizationʼs employee. and Leavers This consists of new hires Joiners), internal transitions Movers and (“JMLˮ) departures Leavers). Staff Members An employee or external worker (e.g., contingent labor, contractors) hired Also referred as by Block, Inc. who supports the operations covered by this Policy. Staff) Supported Supported Digital Assets are limited to cryptocurrencies that are supported Digital Asset and operationally managed by Blockʼs Bitcoin Platform Team in coordination with its partners. 2. Policy Requirements 2.1. Custody Management In accordance with relevant laws and regulations; Block is responsible for deploying technology infrastructure, security and operational mechanisms that ensure responsible processing, handling and safekeeping of information within its ecosystem. Processing, handling and safekeeping of Supported Digital Assets, within Blockʼs ecosystem, is managed with technology components, security prevention and detection methods and operational support and enforcement. 2 2.1.1 Infrastructure Technology infrastructure, which is composed of hardware, software and networks, enables systems, staff and customers to perform operational actions across Blockʼs ecosystem. The Platform Engineering Team designs and implements the necessary infrastructure to participate across both Block and Supported Digital Assetsʼs ecosystems collaboratively and responsibly. The teamʼs remit includes the following: ● Build, implementation and maintenance of infrastructure in accordance with policies and standards; ● Build, implementation and maintenance of Supported Digital Assets storage options: Cold Storage, Gateway Wallet and Hot Storage; ● Development, implementation and maintenance of code, which enforces business logic rules regarding: ○ Asset Segregation ○ Custody, withdrawal and transfer ○ Settlement ○ Reconciliation ○ Liquidity Management and; ● Ensures the availability and reliability of the infrastructureʼs components. 2.1.2 Security Engineering Cybersecurity teams are responsible for protecting Blockʼs technology ecosystem and the sensitive data that it intakes, processes and stores. This is accomplished by implementing security measures that ensure technical and operational standards and practices are in place to immediately mitigate potential breaches to Blockʼs infrastructure. The Security Engineering Team designs, implements and maintains safeguards, specific to Blockʼs Supported Digital Assets products, that mitigates potential breaches and suspicious activity. At times, the Security Engineering Team relies on or collaborates with other internal cybersecurity teams to design, implement and maintain security measures. The team is responsible for: ● The creating, storing and management of cryptographic keys; ● Designing, implementing and maintaining code that enforces business logic for user roles and their access; ● Continuous monitoring of infrastructure to identify potential security threats, unusual behaviors and/or unauthorized access and; ● Penetration Testing; which allows Block to proactively find and fix security issues before malicious attacks can occur. 2.1.3 Custody Operations The Business Operations Teams, at Block, manage the day-to-day operations of their assigned remit. These teams are typically responsible for developing and operationalizing processes that 3 support, enforce and maintain adherence to relevant laws and regulations, Blockʼs Policies and Standards and business goals. The Custody Operations Team is responsible for the operational handling of Supported Digital Assets stored and transferred across Blockʼs ecosystem. This includes: ● Develop processes and business rules that support and enforce the operational handling of Supported Digital Assets; including storage and transfer of Supported Digital Assets across wallets and Service Level Agreements SLAs; ● Ensure wallets maintain a sufficient amount of Supported Digital Assets, as identified in documented policies, procedures and/or processes; ● Identify, train and monitor Operators; ● Manage notifications, to relevant teams, of Leavers, Joiners and Movers JML)and; ● Lead engagement of internal and external auditors and compliance professionals. 2.2. Liquidity Management Supported Digital Assets trading involves the buying and selling of cryptocurrency. Assets allocated for buying and selling activities are stored in Blockʼs hot wallets. These wallets are constantly connected to the internet and are primarily used for quick access; which makes it ideal for frequent trading and everyday transactions. The Trading Operations Team is responsible for monitoring buy and sell activities. By monitoring these activities, it allows the team to ensure hot wallets have sufficient liquidity to accommodate both Blockʼs and its customerʼs trading activities. In partnership with Blockʼs Treasury Team, the Trading Team is responsible for ensuring hot walletsʼ liquidity by developing and maintaining processes and procedures that: ● Identify and facilitate onboarding of liquidity providers. These partners are engaged so that Block can buy and sell Supported Digital Assets. Liquidity partners typically are OTC desks, market makers or prime brokers; ● Ensure sufficient liquidity is maintained in the hot wallets; ● Ensure trades are settled in a timely fashion; ● Document audit and reconciliation activities and reporting of Block and customer owned Supported Digital Assets and; ● Mitigate pricing risks. 2.3. Business Partners In order to satisfy the requirements documented in this Policy, some reliance may be placed on other internal Block teams to support and enforce the operational activities, continuity and availability of Blockʼs Supported Digital Assets ecosystem. 4 2.3.1 Funding The Treasury Team supports the processing of transfers, withdrawals and both the reconciliation and settlement of funds. Operational support comes in the form of funding wallets, reconciliation and settlement activities. The roles, responsibilities and support activities are documented in Treasuryʼs policies, processes and procedures. The Supported Digital Assets operational support that falls in Treasuryʼs remit includes: ● Wallet funding and funding management protocols and enforcement; ● Reconciliation of wallet and ledger balances and; ● Settlement of transactions with liquidity providers. 2.3.2 Continuity and Reliability To ensure both operational continuity and the reliability of infrastructure components, in addition to complying with Blockʼs obligations under Title 23 of the New York Codes, Rules and Regulations (“NYCRRˮ) 200.17, operational and technical teams are required to participate in annual business impact analysis BIA processes; owned by Blockʼs Global Business Resilience Team. These assessments are a key input into providing assurances that Block has the necessary capabilities to recover in cases of disruption. Blockʼs Supported Digital Assets core and support teams adhere to the policies, processes and procedures set forth by Blockʼs Business Resilience teams, which provide assurance of operational continuity and disaster recovery capabilities; in cases of operational disruption. Related policies include, but are not limited to Blockʼs Disaster Recovery Policy and Blockʼs Global Business Resilience Policy. 2.3.2.1 Business Continuity To ensure operational continuity; operational functions across Block are required to participate in the Business Impact Assessment BIA process. The process, which is documented and owned by the Business Continuity Team, assesses operational teamsʼ capabilities to continue in event of disruption. Blockʼs operational teams participation frequency, assessment and documentation of risks, gaps and responses are owned and driven by the Business Co
documents.GOV-010: Escalation Framework for Non-Completion of Compliance Training External Workers) Block, Inc. Last Approved: Mar 30, 2026 Effective Date: Mar 30, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: Block, Inc (“Blockˮ) requires external workers to complete applicable mandatory Compliance training. This Escalation Framework for Non-Completion of Compliance Training External Workers) (“Frameworkˮ) defines the approach taken by Block for External Workers who do not complete mandatory compliance training, as required. For mandatory Compliance training, the Standard outlines the end-to-end Training Campaign Cycle, which generally gives Learners 30 days to complete the assigned mandatory training on time, followed by automated escalating communications post-due date aimed at driving completion of compliance training. Select mandatory Compliance trainings may follow an adjusted timeline as outlined in the Compliance Mandatory Training Standard. This Framework establishes a three-level escalation process for External Workers who remain non-compliant after receiving standard automated notifications: ● Level 1 & 2 Automated written and final warnings delivered via Block Academy ● Level 3 Case-by-case review by EWP and relevant stakeholders to determine appropriate action This Framework takes effect when External Workers have not completed mandatory Compliance training at the conclusion of the automated notification period. Scope: This Framework is established by Block and is applicable across all Block business lines and subsidiaries. It applies to all external workers including BPO resources, Magnit-managed temporary workers, independent contractors, vendor workers, and temporary agency workers outside of Magnit. 1 Definitions: Terminology Definition Call-to-Action Action required to be completed within a specified timeframe. Disciplinary Disciplinary Action may include verbal or documented coaching, written or Action final warning, system access restrictions, or termination of contract, as a result of a policy violation. Training The end-to-end management of deploying a mandatory compliance training Campaign Cycle module that generally allows 30 days for completion and a subsequent post-due date escalation and notification period. Select mandatory compliance training may follow an adjusted timeline as outlined in the Compliance Mandatory Training Program Standard. Consequences for Non-Completion of Compliance Training by External Workers Block partners with the External Workforce Program Team to partner with External Workforce suppliers so that external workers (non-full time employees) complete required training. If an external worker does not complete the training within the Training Campaign Cycle, Block will implement a three-level escalation process that includes automated warnings followed by case-by-case review, which could result in the end of the assignment at Block. Compliance & Enforcement 3.1. Monitoring and Reporting 4 On an ongoing basis, the Escalation Framework Owner and/or their designee monitors activities for compliance and objective achievement. As outlined in the Compliance Management System Policy, Compliance will provide regular updates, as applicable, to the Block, Inc. Board and executive management. Reporting may cover specific information related to: ● Policies and standards ● Procedures ● Audit/exam findings ● Issues management ● Training ● Complaints 3.2. Exceptions The Framework Owner or designee will have authority to approve exceptions and/or exemptions to this Standard, provided that the proposed exception or exemption does not violate applicable laws and regulations. Exception requests must be documented in writing, include a reason for a request, and provide a description of the controls that the Company will implement to mitigate risks associated with the exception. documents.GOV-012: Model Governance Policy Block Published November 2023 Table of Contents Table of Contents 2 Introduction 3 Definitions 4 Roles & Responsibilities 5 Scope 5 Key Regulatory Guidance 6 Typology Coverage Assessment 6 Model Development 6 Model Change Management 7 Model Maintenance 7 Model Validation 7 Document Management 8 Document Owner 8 Scheduled Review & Ad-Hoc Maintenance 8 Exceptions 8 Related Documents 8 Revision & Approval History 8 Document Details 8 Model Validation Policy | Block 2 Introduction Block Inc. and its subsidiaries (collectively “Block”) uses certain technologies to support its management of financial crimes-related risks. These technologies include certain filtering and monitoring systems and models to detect and prevent money laundering, terrorist financing, and sanctions evasion activities. Block will comply with the New York State Department of Financial Services’s (NYDFS) Part 504 provisions as well as other international laws, regulations, and expected standards and accordingly maintain this Model Validation Policy (the “Policy”), which will oversee Block’s model governance framework and provide guidance on how Block will incorporate the following practices, for the anti-money laundering (AML) transaction monitoring models and watchlist screening and filtering technologies: ● On an annual basis, Block elects to perform a coverage assessment on applicable key financial crimes models to test against expected levels of conceptual soundness; ● Block performs periodic reviews and testing of the accuracy of its transaction monitoring and filtering and screening systems as well as their alignment to the Global AML and Counter-Terrorist Financing Policy and the Global Sanctions Policy, and their corresponding key programs; ● Block maintains key model, system, and data-related documentation for Block’s AML transaction monitoring system and watchlist screening and filtering systems1; ● Block maintains robust oversight through mandated review and approval processes for any changes to its transaction monitoring system and watchlist screening and filtering systems, from the relevant money laundering reporting officers (MLROs) and delegates of the Bank Secrecy Act (“BSA”)/AML Compliance Officer; and ● Block elects to conduct an independent assessment to assist the BSA/AML Compliance Officer in accurately attesting for the annual NYDFS Part 504 certifications. This Policy is established by Block and is applicable globally across all Block business lines, subsidiaries, and jurisdictions. References to “staff” throughout this document shall refer to all full-time employees and fixed-term and contingent workers including interns, temps, consultants, 1 Separately, Block maintains appropriate documentation relating to the procedures for the adjudication, investigation and escalating of outputs from our model systems and processes. Model Validation Policy | Block 3 vendors, third-party providers, and contractors. Definitions Model A quantitative method, system, or approach that applies statistical, economic, financial, or mathematical theories, techniques, and assumptions to process input data into quantitative estimates. A model consists of three components: an information input component, which delivers assumptions and data to the model; a processing component, which transforms inputs into estimates; and a reporting component, which translates the estimates into useful business information. Model Tuning A subset of model validation, which is a process to determine that a defined model is optimized to deliver effective results, per predefined performance metrics. Model Validation Set of standards for independent validation of Block’s BSA/AML and OFAC Sanctions program to identify areas for improvement and increase confidence of Block’s TM and Sanctions models, and determine that a defined model is working as designed and to documented and approved specifications. Money Laundering The process by which individuals attempt to conceal and disguise the true nature of illegal proceeds through phases known as placement, layering, and integration. Terrorist Financing The process of raising money to carry out terrorist activities, the funds of which may be derived from unlawful or legitimate sources, such as personal donations and profits from businesses and charitable organizations. Watchlist Screening Watchlist screening is the process of checking individuals and entities against global watchlists, including but not limited to sanctions, Politically Exposed Persons (PEPs), and adverse media. Model Validation Policy | Block 4 Roles & Responsibilities BSA/AML Compliance Officer The BSA/AML Compliance Officer is a suitably experienced and qualified person who is appointed by the Block Board of Directors and who is responsible for oversight of day-to-day AML compliance. Global Policy and Governance The Global Policy and Governance Team oversees the Policy and manages its corresponding requirements for key compliance models. Model Risk Management Team (MRM) The Model Risk Management team is responsible for operational oversight of in-scope models and performs a review of changes during the validation stage. BU Compliance and Operations Teams BU Compliance and Operations teams are end users of the in-scope models and provide business requirements and feedback to the model output. Technology Owners Technology Owners maintain and manage the technical requirements and specifications and ensure the in-scope models reflect the business requirements from the BU Compliance and Operations Teams. Scope The scope of this document includes the key models related to financial crimes risk detection, monitoring, and reporting: ● AML Transaction Monitoring System; ● Watchlist Screening and Filtering Systems; ● Customer Risk Rating Models; and ● Other key detection, monitoring, and reporting systems or models. In accordance with NYDFS 504 provisions, other international laws and best practices, Block maintains a model risk management program to oversee and manage its use of these filtering systems and models. In particular, Block complies with NYDFS 504 in managing and maintaining its AML transaction monitoring system and watchlist screening and filtering systems. Model Validation Policy | Block 5 Key Regulatory Guidance Block complies with the following domestic and international laws and regulatory guidelines as related to Model Validation and Model Risk Management. Office of the Comptroller of the Currency Model Risk Management, Comptroller's (“OCC”) Handbook New York State Department of Financial NYSDFS Part 504 Provisions Services Federal Reserve Bank Interagency Statement on Model Risk Management for Bank Systems Supporting Bank Secrecy Act/Anti-Money Laundering Compliance (April 9, 2021) Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) A Framework for OFAC Compliance Commitment Typology Coverage Assessment Block performs a Typology Coverage Assessment on its AML transaction monitoring rules to ensure coverage of the risk flags and corresponding typologies for suspicious activities indicative of money laundering and terrorist financing. The Typology Coverage Assessment is performed annually, and the corresponding results from the analyses are documented in a report along with a remediation plan to address any risk coverage gaps found in the assessment. While the Typology Coverage Assessment is owned by the appropriate BU Compliance and Operations Teams, it is closely related as input to the models considered to be governed by this Policy. Model Development When a need is identified to design, develop, and implement a new model or system, Block first assesses the risk coverage and purview of the model and bases its design on the business or regulatory requirements. At the point of development of the model, key model development considerations are translated into technical and functional requirements, and the model is subject to review by the appropriate stakeholders, and may only be implemented after required Model Validation Policy | Block 6 pre-implementation and post-implementation testing are conducted and any issues have been identified and remediated. Necessary approval must be obtained before the model go-live. Model Change Management Block’s model change management framework covers the holistic life cycle of the in-scope models and consists of 4 major stages: Creation, Analysis, Execution, and Validation. It is accompanied by strict requirements for audit trail to ensure material changes to a given model are captured, logged, and properly communicated to the appropriate stakeholders. Detailed process requirements are included in the Model Change Management Procedure. Model Maintenance All in-scope models are maintained through performance monitoring, model tuning and optimization, periodic testing, and evaluations. Relevant results are reported on a periodic basis to the financial crimes management team and other appropriate stakeholders. All issues found in ongoing monitoring of model performance are tracked for timely remediation and closure. The testing focuses on the accuracy and validity of the AML transaction monitoring system and watchlist filtering and screening systems against the expectations set within the appropriate policies and programs. Any findings or issues identified in the testing are documented and tracked along with a remediation plan to bring them to closure. Model Validation Where required, an independent party will perform model validation to assess and verify Block’s AML transaction monitoring and watchlist screening and filtering models to identify areas of potential improvement and to increase confidence in such models and subsequently Block’s ability to monitor and detect suspicious activities for money laundering and terrorist financing or potential sanctions violations. The independent party will verify the model performance based on expectations from regulatory requirements, business requirements, Bl
documents.GOV-013: Business Activity Monitoring Policy Block, Inc. | Cash App Last Approved: Aug 12, 2025 Effective Date: Aug 12, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: The Business Activity Monitoring Policy (“Policyˮ) defines Cash App's approach to identifying business activity conducted in Personal Accounts, along with corresponding action taken. This Policy is critical to ensuring our customers are using the correct account type based on their intended use of Cash App. The Cash App Product team is responsible for complying with this Policy. Scope: This Policy is established by Cash App and is applicable to all Block entities used for the Cash App business. Cash App facilitates peer-to-peer ("P2P") payments, enabling customers to send fiat currency from one account to another. Payments can be funded by the customerʼs Cash App balance or linked instruments, such as debit or credit cards. Cash App accounts can generally be used for either personal or business use. To remain compliant with this Policy, customers must use the appropriate account type based on the nature of their transactions. This is critical to ensuring we are able to continuously and effectively monitor customer accounts and transactions, understand our relationship with our customers, and ensure we are able to maintain compliance with relevant tax reporting obligations. Personal accounts are for personal, non-commercial use, while payments for goods and services require a C4B account. Definitions: Terminology Definition Business BAM is a process designed to monitor, detect, and if necessary action Activity personal accounts operating as a Business. Monitoring (“BAMˮ) BAM Team Employee(s), which includes Engineering, Product, Compliance, Legal, Design, and Risk Machine Learning, who support the BAM program. Business A Business is generally defined as an individual or entity accepting payments for goods and services. Cash App for A C4B account is designed to support certain types of businesses providing Business goods or services to their consumers. In order to open a C4B account, users (“C4Bˮ) are required to provide certain information about the business in order to Account verify the identity of the business. Personal A Personal Account is designed to allow Cash App customers to send or Account receive funds for personal, non-commercial purposes. 2. Policy Requirements 2.1. Cash for Business Accounts Generally, a Business can be defined as an individual or entity providing a good or service to consumers in exchange for money. On Cash App, if a customer uses their account for selling goods or services, they must create a C4B account. In order to open a C4B account, a user is required to provide certain information about themselves and their Business in order to verify and open the account. To mitigate the risk of customers using their personal account for business activity, Cash App has established a Business Activity Monitoring (“BAMˮ) system. 2.2. BAM System The BAM system is a coordinated effort across Product, Engineering, Design, Risk and Compliance designed to monitor, detect, and if necessary, take action on personal accounts primarily being used for business purposes. This approach may leverage a combination of machine learning models, heuristics, and/or other data intelligence to identify suspected business operating personal accounts and take action commensurate with the level of confidence in the underlying accountʼs activity. The outcome of these detections may result in directing potentially non-compliant personal accounts toward C4B onboarding, provided their business activities are legally permissible and do not fall within Section “4. Unsupported Industries" of the Cash For Business Payment Terms. 2.3. Implementation Date and Transition Period To enforce this policy, Cash App has developed a risk-based Business Account Migration BAM system with the requirements outlined in this Policy effective September 2025. All Block entities used for the Cash App Business are expected to be in full compliance with requirements of this document no later than six 6 months after the approval date of this document. 3. Compliance & Enforcement 3.1. Roles and Responsibilities: ● Product Counsel ○ Product Counsel provides legal advice to Product teams building, designing and implementing BAM. Additionally, Product Counsel provides interpretative advice and counsel regarding the applicability and application of the requirements of this Policy to Cash Appʼs business. ● Business Product Compliance ○ Business Product Compliance provides Compliance advisory support to enable BAM, inclusive of potential regulatory, operational, and/or consumer protection implications. Business Product Compliance owns the Policy and ensures adherence. ● Product Manager ○ The Product Manager is responsible for ensuring Cash Appʼs BAM process is designed and implemented to abide by the Policy described within. 3.2. Monitoring and Reporting On an ad hoc basis, the Policy Owner and/or their designee will monitor Policy related activities to evaluate adherence to the Policy and determine whether outputs adhere to established objectives. 3.3. Training While no explicit training is required to relevant BAM personnel for the implementation of this system, outside of their usual business training considerations, Product Compliance will provide an annual notification to the BAM Team to review the Business Activity System Policy and verify the BAM System is adhering to the Policy. 3.4. Record Retention All Documents are retained according to the longer of: Blockʼs Data Classification & Handling Policy Applicable regulatory requirements 3.5. Exceptions The Policy Owner or designee will have authority to approve exceptions and/or exemptions to this Policy, provided that the proposed exception or exemption does not violate applicable laws and regulations. Exception requests must be documented in writing, include a reason for a request, and provide a description of the controls that the Company will implement to mitigate risks associated with the exception. Any approved exceptions must be documented in writing and retained with recordkeeping requirements. Any approved exceptions or exemptions should be submitted to the Policy Governance Team. The Document Owner and/or designee will inform Compliance Leadership and applicable committees, as needed, of exceptions. 3.6. Confidentiality This document is confidential and should only be accessed by employees. 4. Questions & Contact Information Questions: For questions or clarifications about this Policy, please contact the Policy Owner. Review related documents: ● Definition of Business Activity ● Business Activity Risk Program Overview Doc ● Cash App | Know Your Business Standard ● Cash App | Know Your Customer Standard 5. Document Details & Revisions Review and Approval: This document shall be reviewed and approved at least annually and on an ad hoc basis in accordance with the Compliance Document Approval Matrix. EMD Individual Regime Policy| Square | Squareup Europe Limited 6 Confidential and Not for Distribution 1. Introduction Squareup Europe Limited (“SELˮ or referred to as “the Companyˮ) is authorised by the Financial Conduct Authority (“FCAˮ) as an Electronic Money Institution (“EMIˮ) pursuant to the Electronic Money Regulations 2011 ( “EMoney Regulationsˮ). A condition of EMI authorisation is that SEL maintains compliance with the FCAʼs Electronic Money Directive Individual Regime (“EMDRˮ, or referred to as “the Regimeˮ) and that the FCA is satisfied (on an ongoing basis) that directors and persons responsible for the management of the Companyʼs e-money and payment services business (referred to by the FCA as "EMD Individuals") are of "good repute and possess appropriate knowledge and experience to issue electronic money and provide payment services" Regulation 66b of the EMoney Regulations). The EMDR aims to encourage a culture of staff at all levels taking personal responsibility for their actions, ensuring that firms and staff can clearly understand and demonstrate where responsibility lies. The fitness and propriety requirements imposed on firms under the EMDR are designed to ensure that persons in senior positions within firms are competent and capable, honest, ethical and of integrity. As an EMI, SEL has an obligation to notify the FCA of any "significant change" in circumstances relevant to the EMI's conditions for authorisation Regulation 37 of the of the EMoney Regulations), and one of the conditions of an EMI's authorisation relates to the reputation, knowledge, and experience of EMD Individuals. Any change to EMD Individuals (including the removal and appointment of directors) should therefore be notified to the FCA. 2. Purpose This Policy sets out how SEL maintains compliance with its obligations under the EMDR relating to the fitness and propriety of specified individuals. This Policy also provides an explanation of the Regime and the relevant requirements that SEL must adhere to, in order to ensure the EMD individuals are competent and capable, honest, ethical, and of integrity. EMD Individual Regime Policy| Square | Squareup Europe Limited 7 Confidential and Not for Distribution 3. Scope This Policy is applicable to persons who are regarded as EMD Individuals for SEL. This Policy aims to ensure that SELʼs EMD Individuals comply with applicable fitness and propriety requirements. 4.
