documents.GOV-015: Outsourcing Policy Squareup Europe Limited Approved: September 29, 2025 Effective Date: September 29, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Outsourcing Policy is established by Squareup Europe Limited (“SELˮ). SEL is an authorised Electronic Money Institution, regulated and supervised by the Financial Conduct Authority (“FCAˮ) under Firm Reference Number 900846. The direct parent company is Squareup International Limited (“SILˮ), registered in the Republic of Ireland; both SEL and SIL are wholly owned subsidiaries of Block, Inc (“Blockˮ). Block Inc. a Delaware corporation, and a merchant services aggregator and mobile payments company based in San Francisco, California in the United States of America. It is registered as a Money Services Business with the Financial Crimes Enforcement Network (“FinCENˮ). Outsourcing involves the use of a third party service provider in any number of operational functions to perform ongoing activities that would normally be undertaken by SEL personnel. Outsourcing Arrangements generate risks for SEL. These risks must be identified and relevant controls applied prior to SEL entering into the arrangement, and these risks must be effectively managed throughout the outsourcing lifecycle. The purpose of this Policy (“the Policyˮ, “this Policyˮ) is to document SELʼs approach to doing this, aligned with applicable legal and regulatory requirements and relevant supervisory guidance. As a member of the Block Inc. Group, SEL is subject to the Third Party Risk Management Policy, which ensures that all third party vendors are reviewed and assessed in accordance with such policy at the Block level. This Policy should be read in conjunction with the Block Third Party Risk Management Policy. Where there is a conflict between the Block Third Party Risk Management Policy and this Policy, this Policy will prevail. SELʼs Senior Management and the Board recognise that the delegation of activities under an Outsourcing Arrangement does not result in any delegation of responsibility, and that ultimate responsibility for outsourced processes, services, or activities remains with SEL management and the SEL Board at all times. The purpose of this Policy is to set out SELʼs approach to the planning, implementation, monitoring and management of ‘Outsourcingʼ of activities by SEL to Outsourcing Service Providers (“OSPsˮ). Scope: This Policy is established by SEL and is applicable to all SEL Outsourcing Arrangements and SEL Staff Members. Definitions: Terminology Definition Critical Activity Functions that are necessary to perform core business lines or critical business functions, unless the institutionʼs assessment establishes that a failure to provide the outsourced function or the inappropriate provision of the outsourced function would not have an adverse impact on the operational continuity of the core business line or critical business function1. Intra-group Outsourcing Refers to an Outsourcing Arrangement between SEL and any entity within Block. Outsourcing Arrangement An arrangement between SEL and a vendor (whether intra-group or an external third party) by which the service provider performs a process, a service or an activity, or parts thereof, that would otherwise be undertaken by SEL, even if SEL has not performed that process, service or activity in the past. Outsourcing Framework/Program Any references to “Outsourcing Frameworkˮ or “Outsourcing Programˮ refer to this Policy, the SEL Outsourcing Procedures documents and the SEL Outsourcing Strategy. The terms “Outsourcing Programˮ and “Outsourcing Frameworkˮ are used interchangeably across this document. Outsourcing Risk Management Refers to the framework in place to identify, assess and manage risks associated with Outsourcing Arrangements. Outsourced Service Provider Are considered as a third-party entity that is undertaking ("OSP") an outsourced process, service or activity, or parts thereof, under an outsourcing arrangement on behalf of SEL. The term refers to both external third party service providers and intra/inter group service providers. Outsourcing Register The Outsourcing Register forms part of the ‘Outsourcing and Third Party Registerʼ and contains details of all outsourced processes, services, or activities. This register is maintained by the Third Party and Outsourcing Risk Manager. 1 EBA Guidelines on Outsourcing Arrangements, Section 4 Terminology Definition Service Recipient An individual at SEL that is responsible for the delivery of the process, service, or activity and the performance of the OSP. Sub-outsourcing Sub-outsourcing occurs when an OSP engaged by SEL has themselves further outsourced the relevant process, service or activity, or parts thereof to another OSP. For the avoidance of doubt, this includes where SEL has an intra-group Outsourcing Arrangement with another Block entity and that Block entity has sub-outsourced to any other individual or entity. Staff Member(s) or Staff All employees and fixed-term and contingent workers who support the operations and/or subsidiaries covered by this Policy, and include interns, temporary workers, consultants, vendors, third-party providers, and contractors. An employee or external worker (e.g., contingent labour, contractors) hired by Squareup Europe Limited who supports the operations covered by this Policy. 2. Policy/Program Requirements 2.1 What is Outsourcing? SEL defines a vendor relationship as ‘an arrangement between SEL and any other individual or entity that provides products or services to SEL via an ongoing relationshipʼ. Vendor arrangements can be either ‘Outsourcingʼ or ‘Non-Outsourcingʼ arrangements, and vendors can be either external third parties or other Block Inc. entities. SEL defines outsourcing as ‘an arrangement between SEL and a vendor (whether intra-group, or an external third party) by which the vendor performs a process, a service or an activity, or parts thereof, that would otherwise be undertaken by SEL, even if SEL has not performed that process, service or activity in the pastʼ. Not all activities will be Outsourcing (an analysis will be required on a case-by-case basis). This Policy describes SELʼs minimum requirements to manage the risks arising from outsourcing activities, both to external third parties and within Block. Implementation of the standards laid out in this Policy will ensure that SEL has a robust framework to ensure the following: ● That the SEL Board remains fully responsible for all outsourced activities; ● That a risk management and internal control framework is embedded which allows for sufficient oversight of the outsourced activities; ● That the impact of any Outsourcing Arrangement is fully considered and understood; and that SEL retains the competence and ability to assess the OSPs performance; ● That all Outsourcing Arrangements are formalised in an outsourcing contract between SEL and the OSP, that sets out all respective roles, responsibilities, rights, and obligations and meets the requirements set out in this Policy; ● That the OSP has adequate risk management and internal control systems in place, and that SEL incorporates the oversight of outsourced functions and activities into its own risk management framework; ● That the OSP has sufficient resources in place in terms of quality, quantity, and availability to perform the outsourced functions and activities; ● That the OSP is compliant with relevant confidentiality and sensitive data provisions and has adequate contingency arrangements in place; ● That SEL notify the competent authority, the FCA, in a timely manner and in line with regulatory notification requirements of any major operational or security incidents; ● That SEL notifies the FCA in a timely manner and in line with regulatory notification requirements of any critical functions or activities to be outsourced; ● That SEL has full access to perform audit and/or assurance of the OSP and the OSP will be fully cooperative with this audit / assurance review; andUK ● That the outsourcing of critical functions and activities does not undermine the continuous and satisfactory service to SEL stakeholders. 2.2 Intra-Group Arrangements Outsourcing services provided by Block entities to SEL also fall in scope of this Policy (“Intra-Group Outsourcingˮ). The Block service provider will be regarded as an OSP for the purpose of this Policy. SEL applies the same requirements to Intra-group Outsourcing as to Outsourcing Arrangements with external third parties. SEL recognises that Intra-group Outsourcing Arrangements can present unique risks. For Intra-group Outsourcing, SEL shall ensure that the arrangement is organised on an ‘armʼs lengthʼ basis. The selection of the intra-group entity must be based on objective reasons, any conflicts of interest inherent in the Outsourcing Arrangement must be identified and managed in line with the Conflicts of Interest Policy, and the arrangement must not impair SELʼs ability to comply with its regulatory obligations. SEL shall ensure that it is in a position to exert sufficient influence on the intra-group arrangements and that any necessary remediation of outsourced services, processes, or activities that impacts on SEL are given appropriate prioritisation by Block. 2.3 Outsourcing to Third Countries SELʼs approach to outsourcing is governed by a principle of consistency, ensuring that all outsourcing arrangements, regardless of the geographic location of the OSP, are subject to the same rigorous governance framework. SEL does not differentiate its due diligence, risk assessment, and ongoing monitoring standards based on whether an OSP is located within the UK or in a third country. 2.4 Sub-Outsourced Services Sub-outsourcing occurs when an OSP engaged by SEL has themselves further outsourced the relevant process, service or activity, or parts thereof to another OSP. Intra-Group Outsourcing can often involve sub-Outsourcing. The OSP must undertake appropriate due dilig

documents.GOV-017: Breach Reporting Policy Square | Square AU Pty Ltd Last Approved: Dec 18, 2025 Effective Date: Dec 18, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: Square AU Pty Ltd (“Square AUˮ) is an Australian Financial Services License AFSL holder License Number: 513929. This Breach Reporting Policy (“Policyˮ) outlines Square AUʼs approach to reporting a breach. The Chief Compliance Officer (“CCOˮ) Square AU is responsible for management of this Policy. This Policy is reviewed at least annually, and after any major regulatory change or incidents, by the Square AU Compliance team and approved by the Square AU Board. The Policy applies to all Square AU operations, Employees, and relevant third party service providers. In accordance with ASIC's Regulatory Guide 78 RG 78 and Square AU Pty Ltd's obligations under the Corporations Act, this Breach Reporting Policy outlines the framework for identifying, assessing, escalating, and reporting breaches within the organisation. As an Australian Financial Services AFS licensee, Square AU is mandated to report certain incidents, known as "Reportable Situations," to ASIC. This policy details the roles and responsibilities across the organisation, providing clear guidance on breach significance, timing, documentation and escalation to ensure compliance and protect stakeholders. Square AU conducts business in compliance with all applicable laws and regulations. The purpose of this Policy is to further Square AUʼs Breach reporting practices by providing for processes and guidelines to manage breaches within Square AU. Scope: This Policy, which sets out Square AUʼs approach to breach reporting under the Australian Financial Services Regime, is established by Square AU and is applicable to the Employees of Square AU. It also applies to: ● All persons engaged by Square AU (whether permanent, contract, or via third-party service providers), ● All relevant breaches affecting Square AUʼs regulated product or service footprint in Australia, and ● Any reporting required by contractual obligation to banking or business partners where regulatory breach reporting is specified. Definitions: Terminology Definition Reportable A ‘Reportable Situationʼ can fall into two categories: Situation ● Category 1 A ‘Core Reportable Situationʼ arises in any of the following scenarios: ○ Square Au Pty. Ltd. (“Square AUˮ) has breached a ‘Core Obligationʼ and the breach is ‘Significantʼ; ○ Square AU is no longer able to comply with a ‘Core Obligationʼ and the breach, if it occurs, will be ‘Significantʼ; ○ Square AU conducts an ‘Investigationʼ1 into whether there is a Reportable Situation, and the Investigation continues for more than 30 days2; or ○ an Investigation discloses there is no Reportable Situation ● Category 2 Gross negligence or fraud: A Category 2 Reportable Situation may arise if: ○ in the course of providing a financial service, Square AU has engaged in conduct constituting gross negligence; ○ Square AU has committed serious fraud3; or ○ any other circumstances prescribed by the regulations for the purposes of this paragraph (none are prescribed). Core Obligation ‘Core Obligationsʼ mean the obligations imposed on Square AU as an AFS licensee in ss 912A and 912B of the Corporations Act4 Investigation Square AU conducts an ‘Investigationʼ5 if: ● there has been some information gathering or human effort applied by Square AU to determine whether a breach has occurred or will 1 The term ‘Investigationʼ is not defined in the legislation and has its ordinary meaning. What constitutes an Investigation is likely to vary significantly depending on the size of the licenseeʼs business, their internal systems and processes, and the type of breach ((para 11.43 of the Explanatory Memorandum to the Financial Sector Reform Hayne Royal Commission Response) Bill 2020 EM. 2 While Investigations about the additional Reportable Situations are not expressly required to be reported to Australian Investments and Securities Commission (“ASICˮ) as part of the additional Reportable Situations, they may still need to be reported if they amount to a Core Reportable Situation. That is, if the Investigation can be characterised as an Investigation conducted by Square AU into whether there has been or will be a Significant Breach of a Core Obligation, and the Investigation continues for more than 30 calendar days (para 11.54 of EM. 3 ‘Serious fraudʼ is defined in s 9 of the Corporations Act to mean an offence involving fraud or dishonesty, against any law, that is punishable by imprisonment for a period of at least 3 months. 4 In respect of the obligation under paragraph 912A1c, so far as it relates to provisions of the Corporations Act or the ASIC Act referred to in paragraph (a), (b), (ba), and (c) of the definition of ‘financial services lawʼ in section 761A. Therefore, in relation to financial services, it is broad enough to capture obligations under Chapter 7 of the Corporations Act and Division 2 of Part 2 of the Australian Securities and Investments Commission Act 2001 Cth ASIC Act). 5 Even where Square AU does not conduct an Investigation, there may still be a Reportable Situation if Square AU has reasonable grounds to believe that they have breached, or will breach a Core Obligation and the breach is or will be Significant. In other words, there may be circumstances where a Significant and reportable breach should be clear to a licensee, even without an Investigation being conducted (para 11.49 of the EM. occur such as: ○ communicating with representatives or staff of Square AU who may have been involved in the relevant conduct; ○ communicating with potentially affected clients; or ○ seeking specialist or technical advice6; or ● Square AU outsources the Investigation, or if a related entity (eg Square Inc) conducts the Investigation Note: Merely entering a suspected compliance issue into a risk management system is unlikely to amount to an Investigation, although this will depend on the circumstances in each case. We consider the time at which an Investigation commences is at the time the matter is raised or identified by Square AU. Significant A breach of a ‘Core Obligationʼ will be ‘Significantʼ if it falls into one of these Breach two categories: ● Category 1 Deemed significance: A breach of a ‘Core Obligationʼ is taken to be ‘Significantʼ if: ○ the breach is constituted by the commission of an offence under any law and the commission of the offence is punishable on conviction by a penalty that may include imprisonment for a maximum period of: ○ If the offence involves dishonesty – 3 months or more; or ○ in any other case – 12 months of more; ○ the breach is constituted by the contravention of a civil penalty provision under any law, other than a civil penalty provision prescribed by the regulations for the purposes of this paragraph; or ○ the breach is constituted by a contravention of subsection 1041H1 of this Act or subsection 12DA1 of the ASIC Act (misleading or deceptive conduct in relation to a financial product or a financial service); or ○ the breach results, or is likely to result, in material ‘loss or damageʼ to Square AUʼs clients (which includes both retail and wholesale clients); ○ any other circumstances prescribed by the regulations (none are prescribed). ● Category 2 Significant based on factors: A breach of a ‘Core Obligationʼ is ‘Significantʼ having regard to the following: ○ the number or frequency of similar previous breaches; ○ the impact of the breach or likely breach on Square AUʼs ability to provide financial services covered by the AFS licence; ○ the extent to which the breach or likely breach indicates that Square AUʼs arrangements to ensure compliance with those obligations as a licensee are inadequate. Examples of breaches that may be Significant: ● Failure to maintain professional indemnity PI insurance or an 6 Para 11.4411.45 of the EM. appropriate level of PI cover. ● Failure to prepare cash flow projections. ● Previously undetected breach of s 912A or 912B of the Corporations Act. ● Representatives give inappropriate advice (occurred with sufficient degree of regularity). ● Representatives operating outside the scope of Australian Financial Services License (“AFSLˮ) authorisations. For breaches that arise from data security breaches or service failures, Square AU will participate in the steps set out in Block, Inc.ʼs Incident Response Plan (“IRPˮ). The determination of whether such breaches of ‘Core Obligationsʼ occurred and whether it is Significant according to the above factors, will be made by the persons outlined in the IRP. Any reportable breaches will be reported to ASIC in accordance with the IRP and consistent with this Policy. Incident An incident refers to any unplanned event or occurrence that results in, or has the potential to result in, harm, disruption, non-compliance, or loss to Square AU, its customers, employees, or other stakeholders. Employees (also All full-time and part-time employees and fixed-term and contingent workers referred as Staff) including interns, temps, consultants, vendors, third-party providers, and contractors. 2. Policy Requirements 2.1. Obligation to report to ASIC If there are ‘reasonable grounds to believeʼ that a ‘Reportable Situationʼ has arisen, Square AU must lodge a report with ASIC within the mandated timeframe, as outlined in section 2.3. ‘Reasonable grounds to believeʼ ensures the breach reporting obligation is clearly objective. There will be reasonable grounds to believe a Reportable Situation has arisen if there are facts and/or evidence to induce, in a reasonable person, a belief that a Reportable Situation has arisen. Square AU will treat each Reportable Situation as separate from one another7. This means, for example, that there will be two separate obligations to lodge a report with ASIC if Square AU is no longer able to comply with a Core Obligation and the breach will be Significant if it occurs8, and t
documents.GOV-018: Complaints Policy Square | Square AU Pty Ltd Last Approved: Dec 18, 2025 Effective Date: Dec 18, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Complaints Policy (“Policyˮ) outlines Square AU Pty Ltdʼs (“Square AUˮ) approach to managing complaints in accordance with applicable Australian legislation, Australian Financial Complaints Authority (“AFCAˮ) Rules and Australian Securities and Investments Commission (“ASICˮ) Regulatory Guides. The Chief Compliance Officer CCO is responsible for overseeing this Policy and ensuring compliance with all relevant legal and regulatory obligations. This Policy supplements Block Incʼs global Complaints Management framework by setting out specific requirements for managing complaints within Square AU. Where any inconsistencies arise, the requirements of local policies and standards take precedence. Square AU is committed to fair, transparent, and timely complaint handling. The regulated Internal Dispute Resolution (“IDRˮ) process provides customers with a fair opportunity to have their concerns addressed internally before escalating a complaint to AFCA. Scope: This Policy is established by Square, sets out its approach to managing complaints and is applicable to all its Employees. Square AU deals with Complaints in accordance with the procedures set out in this Policy. Definitions: Terminology Definition IDR Instrument An Internal Dispute Resolution (“IDRˮ) instrument refers to Australian Securities and Investments Commission (“ASICˮ) Corporations, Credit and Superannuation Internal Dispute Resolution) Instrument 2020/98. 1 RG 271 ASIC Regulatory Guide 271 Internal dispute resolution which outlines the mandated approach for AFSL holders in handling and resolving complaints. Complaint An expression of dissatisfaction made to or about an organisation, related to its products, services, staff, or the handling of a complaint, where a response or resolution is explicitly or implicitly expected or legally required. Complainant A Complainant (also referred to as a Consumer/Customer) refers to Square AUʼs retail client which includes a small business. Systemic Issue A systemic issue is a matter that affects, or has the potential to affect, more than one consumer. AFCA Australian A free and independent ombudsman service that resolves disputes between Financial consumers, small businesses, and financial firms in Australia. Where External Complaints Dispute Resolution EDR is required, complaints are referred to AFCA. Authority) Front Line Refers to any team or staff member who has direct contact with Customers Representatives and is able to receive a complaint. This includes Risk, Compliance, Sales, Account Management, Customer Success, Complaints Management, Application Support Engineering, Square Advanced Support, Winback, and all other teams and individuals where direct Customer contact happens, either intentionally or incidentally. 2. Policy Requirements 2.1 Regulatory Framework Square AU, as an Australian Financial Services Licence AFSL holder and a member of the Australian Financial Complaints Authority AFCA, is required to comply with a robust regulatory framework governing complaint handling. This framework ensures that all complaints are managed fairly, promptly, and consistently in accordance with applicable laws, regulations, and industry best practice. 2 Key components of the regulatory framework include: ● Corporations Act 2001 Cth, particularly the obligations under section 912A relating to efficient, honest, and fair management of financial services. ● ASIC Regulatory Guide 271 – Internal Dispute Resolution RG 271, providing guidance on the requirements and expectations for complaint handling by AFSL holders. ● AFCA Rules and Terms of Reference, which govern external dispute resolution and outline the rights of complainants to escalate unresolved matters. Square AU is also subject to mandatory reporting obligations, including the submission of complaints data to ASIC on a six-monthly basis, in line with regulatory requirements. Compliance with this framework ensures that Square AU maintains transparency, accountability, and a customer-focused approach to complaint management. 2.2 Guiding Principles The guiding principles of this Complaints Policy ensure that all complaints are managed in a fair, transparent, and accountable manner. Square AU is committed to fairness and impartiality, ensuring all complaints are handled objectively and without bias. The complaints process is accessible and visible, allowing customers to easily lodge complaints through multiple channels and receive appropriate support. Confidentiality is maintained at all times to protect customer information and privacy. Complaints are addressed with timeliness, acknowledging and resolving matters as quickly as possible in line with regulatory requirements. The process also emphasises systemic issue identification, using complaint insights to detect broader issues that may affect multiple customers. Finally, Square AU is dedicated to continuous improvement, regularly reviewing complaints data and feedback to enhance its products, services, and customer experience. 2.3 What is a Complaint? Square AU considers the Australian legislative definitions and guidelines when considering whether or not a matter constitutes a complaint. AS/NZS 100022014 sets out the following definition of ‘complaintʼ: An expression] of dissatisfaction made to or about an organization, related to its products, services, staff or the handling of a complaint, where a response or resolution is explicitly or implicitly expected or legally required. Complaints may be received in a variety of forms e.g. in a letter, by email or by telephone and through posts on a social media channel / account owned or controlled by Square AU where the author is identifiable and contactable and where the post meets the definition of complaint. A complaint may also include complaints about a matter that is the subject of an existing remediation program or about the remediation program itself (e.g. delays, lack of communication). The following is an excerpt from RG 271, regarding matters not considered to be complaints: For avoidance of doubt, we do not consider the following to be ‘complaintsʼ: 3 (a) employment-related complaints raised by financial firm staff; (b) comments made about a firm where a response is not expected, such as: (i) feedback provided in surveys; or (ii) reports intended solely to bring a matter to a financial firmʼs attention—for example, that an automatic teller machine ATM is damaged; (c) hardship notices or requests to postpone enforcement proceedings, unless the customer raises issues that meet the definition of complaint; and (d) reports of unauthorised transactions under the ePayments Code and disputed transactions under a chargeback process. However, we consider that a complaint has been made if the consumer raises separate issues related to the transaction that meet the definition of a complaint, or expresses dissatisfaction with the outcome or handling of the unauthorised or disputed transaction. 2.4 Complaints Handling Process Square AU ensures all complaints are managed in a timely, fair, and consistent manner in accordance with regulatory requirements. The below diagram is an example of how this process is handled at Square AU, from receipt of complaint through to resolution and closure: 4 The complaint handling process more specifically includes the following key stages: Acknowledgement and Recording – If a customer lodges a complaint through any channel, Square AU will confirm receipt of their complaint within 24 hours (or one business day), or as soon as practicable in the event of a delay. Where possible, acknowledgement will be provided using the same method the complaint was received, or by another method as agreed with the customer. The complaint is documented within the appropriate platform and a record of these complaints is maintained. Assessment and Investigation – Each complaint is promptly assigned for review and investigation. Relevant facts are gathered, documented, and assessed to ensure an objective and evidence-based outcome. Response – Where a complaint is not resolved within 5 days of receipt of the complaint, a written Internal Dispute Resolution IDR response is required. The IDR must be provided within 30 calendar days for financial services complaints, or within 21 calendar days where the complaint involves financial hardship. Where the deadline for an IDR falls on the weekend, all endeavours must still be made to respond within the mandated timeframe. Resolution – The response must clearly outline the outcome of the complaint and any remedial actions taken, such as a correction, refund, apology, or explanation. All endeavours must be made to resolve the complaint internally and as soon as possible after receipt of complaint, however IDR responses must also advise the complainant of their right to escalate the complaint to AFCA. Employees must follow internal procedures for escalating complaints that cannot be resolved at the frontline or that raise potential regulatory, reputational, or systemic risks. More details on internal escalation are contained with section 2.10 of this Policy. 2.5 Internal Dispute Resolution Response Requirements Where a complaint cannot be resolved within 5 days, an IDR response is required to be provided to the complainant. IDR responses are also required for all complaints relating to financial hardship. Each final IDR response issued by Square AU must meet the requirements set out in ASIC Regulatory Guide 271 – Internal Dispute Resolution and the ASIC Corporations, Credit and Superannuation Internal Dispute Resolution) Instrument 2020/98. An IDR response must: ● Provide a clear explanation of the outcome of the complaint and the reasons for the decision, including reference to any relevant evidence or Terms and Conditions; ● Inform the complainant o
documents.GOV-019: Compliance Management System CMS Policy Square AU Last Approved: Dec 18, 2025 Effective Date: Dec 18, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Compliance Management System CMS Policy (the Policy) outlines Square AU Pty Ltdʼs (Square AU) approach to identifying, assessing, managing, monitoring, and reporting regulatory and ethical compliance obligations and risks across its Australian operations. The CMS promotes integrity, accountability, transparency, and continuous improvement and is aligned to global compliance practices. Scope: This Policy applies to all Square AU operations, officers, employees, and contractors engaged in regulated and supporting business activities. 1 Square AU operates within a broad and dynamic Australian regulatory environment that includes financial services, financial crime, consumer protection, and payments obligations. While the CMS considers this entire regulatory landscape, it applies a risk-based approach to prioritise programs and controls proportional to the nature, scale, and complexity of Square AUʼs operations. This approach ensures resources are directed to the areas of greatest regulatory, operational, and reputational risk, while maintaining visibility of broader obligations and emerging developments. Key areas of focus under this Policy include: ● AFSL and Conduct Obligations under the Corporations Act 2001 Cth, ASIC Regulatory Guides and Anti Bribery and Corruption; ● Financial Crime Compliance under the Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorism Financing Act 2006 Cth and AUSTRAC Rules and Autonomous Sanctions Act 2011Cth; ● Consumer Protection under ASIC, ACCC, and relevant industry codes; ● Complaints Handling, including requirements under ASIC Regulatory Guide 271 and the AFCA Rules; and ● Payments Industry Compliance, encompassing scheme participation, network standards, and relevant prudential or operational guidelines. Square AU operates as an independent Australian licensee within the Block group, aligning its local CMS with Blockʼs enterprise-wide compliance standards and programs to ensure consistency, accountability, and effective global governance. Global Alignment (summary): Square AUʼs CMS is supported by Block Inc.ʼs global compliance ecosystem (e.g., Global Monitoring & Testing Program, Issue Management Program, PoP, TPRM Standard). This ensures enterprise consistency and shared tooling while Square AU retains full accountability to Australian regulators and its Board. Definitions: AFSL Australian Financial Services Licence): A licence granted under the Corporations Act 2001 Cth authorising entities to provide specified financial services in Australia. The AFSL framework establishes the licenseeʼs obligations to maintain organisational competence, manage conflicts, ensure compliance, and act efficiently, honestly, and fairly. Responsible Manager: An individual nominated under the AFSL to demonstrate the organisationʼs competence and integrity in meeting its licensing obligations (refer ASIC Regulatory Guide 105). Responsible Managers play a key role within the compliance management system by ensuring controls, governance, and oversight arrangements align with licence conditions and regulatory expectations. 2 Compliance Management System CMS: A structured and integrated framework encompassing governance, risk management, compliance policies, procedures, controls, monitoring, training, reporting, and assurance mechanisms designed to ensure and evidence adherence to legal, regulatory, and ethical obligations. The CMS supports proactive identification and management of compliance risks, continuous improvement, and a culture of integrity across the organisation. Issue: A deficiency, weakness, or non-conformity in a process, control, or compliance obligation that may impair compliance effectiveness. Issues are logged, risk-rated, and tracked to remediation within the CMS to ensure accountability and timely resolution. Significant Breach: A breach that meets the materiality or impact criteria under s912D of the Corporations Act 2001 Cth or other applicable laws. Such breaches must be reported to ASIC within prescribed timeframes (generally within 30 days). The CMS should facilitate timely escalation, assessment, and documentation of significant breaches. Three Lines of Defence: A governance model underpinning the CMS ● 1st Line: Business and operational functions own and manage risks and controls. ● 2nd Line: Compliance and Risk functions provide oversight, guidance, and challenge to ensure effective implementation of the CMS. ● 3rd Line: Internal Audit provides independent assurance over the design and effectiveness of compliance and risk management frameworks. 2. Square AU Specific Requirements Compliance Governance and Regulatory Ecosystem Square AUʼs CMS ensures local accountability while aligning to global practices. It integrates governance, monitoring, and improvement mechanisms to meet regulatory expectations and maintain a culture of integrity and transparency. 2.1 Regulatory Context Primary regulatory stakeholders include (non-exhaustive): ASIC, AUSTRAC, OAIC, APRA/RBA (payments), AFCA, and industry schemes. 3 2.2 Board and Management Oversight Leadership and Commitment Square AUʼs Board and Senior Management team are actively involved in shaping the compliance culture. The Chief Compliance Officer CCO for Square AU has a dotted reporting line to the Square AU Board. Leadership commitment is demonstrated by: - Regular compliance and risk updates to the Square AU Board and Senior Management Committees - Allocation of dedicated Compliance resources across key risk domains Financial Crime, Regulatory, Licensing, Consumer Protection and Operations) - Regular gap analyses and risk assessments. - Organisational Roles, Responsibilities, and Authorities Responsibilities are communicated through role descriptions, governance documents, and training. Authority and accountability for compliance outcomes are embedded in governance structures across the Three Lines of Defence. Management Review The Chief Compliance Officer CCO leads an Biennial Management review of the Compliance Framework. This review assesses the continuing suitability, adequacy, and effectiveness of the Compliance Framework, taking into account audit results, compliance metrics, material breaches, regulatory changes, and emerging risks. Outcomes from the management review feed directly into Board-level compliance reporting and inform Square AUʼs broader regulatory risk assessment and 4 strategic compliance planning processes. 2.3 Policies, Standards & Procedures Square AU maintains a suite of compliance policies, standards, and procedures that define the controls and expectations for managing regulatory and conduct risk across the business. Each document supports the operation of the CMS by ensuring obligations are clearly articulated, consistently implemented, and regularly reviewed for relevance and effectiveness. All compliance documents are developed, approved, and maintained in accordance with the Block Compliance Policy on Policies PoP and the Compliance Document Approval Matrix, which establish governance requirements for creation, ownership, review, and retirement of compliance documents. Under these frameworks: ● Each policy or standard has an identified Document Owner responsible for its accuracy, currency, and alignment with Square AUʼs risk appetite and legal obligations; ● Documents undergo formal review at least biennially, and ad hoc updates are triggered by material regulatory changes, emerging risks, or internal audit findings; ● All approved versions are stored in a controlled central repository with version control, audit trails, and access restrictions to ensure authenticity and integrity; ● Changes are communicated to relevant staff through targeted updates, training, and operational change processes; and ● Retired or superseded policies are archived in line with record retention requirements to preserve regulatory evidence. This structure ensures that compliance documentation remains current, traceable, and effective, while maintaining alignment with both Block Inc.ʼs global governance standards and Square AUʼs local regulatory obligations. 5 The below table outlines the roles and responsibilities of individuals within both Block Inc. and Square AU in terms of the current suite of Compliance Policies, Standards and Program Documents: Chief Block Inc. Square AU Square AU Compliance Document Cycle Compliance Chief Risk Global Board Officer) Officer Square AU Risk Management Annually I A R Strategy and Policy Compliance Framework Biennially I A R I AML/CTF Program Annually I A R AML/CTF Risk Awareness Annually A,R Training Program Employee Due Diligence Annually A,R Enhanced Customer Due Annually A,R Diligence Program Financial Crimes Transaction Annually A,R Monitoring Program Politically Exposed Persons Annually A,R Watchlist Program Suspicious Matter Report Program Annually A,R Sanctions Policy Annually A A R Breach Reporting Policy Annually A R Complaints Policy Annually A R Outsourcing Policy Annually A R AFSL Representative Standard Annually A R Regulatory Change Annually A,R Management Program Responsible Managers Standard Annually A R Key Persons Fit and Biennially A R Proper Standard Conflicts of Interest Policy Annually A R Privacy Policy (global) Annually A I Information Security Policy (global) Annually A I I Anti Bribery and Annually A A R Corruption Policy (global) A Approve, R Review, S Support, I = Informed 6 2.4 Training & Awareness Square AU recognises that a strong compliance culture relies on the capability and awareness of its people. To achieve this, the Compliance Management System CMS embeds a structured and risk-based training and competency program designed to ensure that all employees understand their regulatory obligations and perform their roles in accordance with legal, ethical, and internal standards.
documents.GOV-020: Third Party Risk Management TPRM Policy Square | Square AU Pty Ltd Last Approved: Dec 18, 2025 Effective Date: Dec 18, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: Square AU Pty Ltd (“Square AUˮ) is an Australian Financial Services License AFSL holder License Number: 513929 and is a reporting entity enrolled on the Reporting Entity Roll managed by the Australian Transaction Reports and Analysis Centre (“AUSTRACˮ) for the purposes of AML/CTF Law . This Third Party Risk Management TPRM Policy (“Policyˮ) outlines Square AUʼs approach to outsourcing. The Chief Compliance Officer (“CCOˮ) for Square AU is responsible for the management of this Policy. The Policy describes the outsourcing arrangements that Square AU has in place in relation to the conduct of various aspects of its activities. Square AU remains fully accountable for compliance with legislation, regardless of any outsourcing. In accordance with AUSTRACʼs guidance on Outsourcing, all outsourced AML activities must align with Square AUʼs AML/CTF program, policy, standard and related procedures. This Policy is modelled on the processes set out in the Block. Inc, Third Party Risk Management TPRM Policy. Scope: This Policy applies to all employees, officers, and contingent workers of Square AU involved in any aspect of outsourcing or vendor management, including intra-group outsourcing across the Block group. Definitions: Terminology Definition Outsourcing The practice of engaging a third party to perform services or functions typically conducted in-house, ensuring compliance with all regulatory standards, especially those mandated by Australian Investments and Securities Commission (“ASICˮ) and Australian Transaction Reports and Analysis Centre (“AUSTRACˮ) Employees All full-time and part-time employees and fixed-term and contingent (also referred workers including interns, temps, consultants, vendors, third-party as Staff) providers, and contractors. Vendor (third A non-affiliated third party engaged to provide products or services, party service inclusive of intra-group outsourcing where services are provided by provider / affiliated entities. outsourced service provider) Responsible An individual responsible for maintaining effective oversight and Manager compliance of outsourced activities, ensuring adherence to regulatory (“RMˮ) requirements. Third-Party A program managing the identification, assessment, monitoring, and Risk mitigation of risks associated with third-party vendors. Management (“TPRMˮ) Enterprise A role responsible for implementing the TPRM program locally, including Risk Manager monitoring Vendor performance, assessing risks, and reporting to the (“ERMˮ) Board. Third-Party The primary point of contact responsible for managing the Vendor Relationship relationship and supporting TPRM program components. Owner (“TPROˮ) Due Diligence The process of evaluating prospective Vendors to ensure they are competent, experienced, and capable of meeting regulatory requirements and service standards. Vendor A preliminary assessment that helps determine the inherent risk level and Engagement guides further due diligence requirements as part of the Vendor Request contracting and engagement process. (“VERˮ) Risk Subject Specialists who provide expertise on various risk areas, advising on Due Matter Diligence, contract inclusions, and residual risks. Experts (“Risk SMEsˮ) Business A program evaluating Vendors' ability to maintain or restore critical Continuity-Dis functions following disruptions, ensuring continuity in outsourced aster services. Recovery (“BCDRˮ) Monitoring Continuous assessment of a Vendor's compliance with service levels, performance, and financial health, documented and reported periodically to stakeholders. Breach Any non-compliance or failure by the Vendor to meet contractual or regulatory obligations, requiring reporting and potentially corrective actions. Contingency A process to ensure continuity or transfer of critical services if a Vendor Planning relationship is terminated, mitigating business impact. 2. Policy Requirements 2.1. Guiding Principles The guiding principles upon which this Policy is implemented by Square AU are as follows: ● Accountability: Square AU remains fully responsible for compliance with all AFSL and AML/CTF obligations, regardless of outsourcing. ● Proportionality: Risk management controls are commensurate with the materiality, complexity, and regulatory importance of the outsourced service. ● Regulatory Compliance: Outsourcing must not diminish Squareʼs ability to meet obligations under AFSL, AML/CTF, Privacy Act, or other applicable laws. ● Due Diligence & Ongoing Monitoring: All service providers are subject to appropriate due diligence, risk assessment, and periodic performance reviews. ● Contractual Safeguards: Outsourcing agreements must include rights of access, audit, data security, and termination provisions aligned with ASIC and AUSTRAC guidance. ● Continuity & Contingency: Plans must exist to ensure continuity of critical functions in the event of vendor failure or termination. 2.2. Planning Process for engaging vendors Square AUʼs board of directors (“Boardˮ), Employees and the TPROʼs are ultimately responsible for identifying the services to be outsourced to third parties and for ensuring compliance with its obligations as an AFSL holder and an AUSTRAC-regulated entity. As part of this process, Square AUʼs management team will conduct the following activities (and report to the Board for review and approval of material Outsourcing arrangements): ● identify all material risks associated with the Outsourcing proposal (the Block Vendor Management Policy outlines the risks that may be created by an Outsourcing relationship); ● ensure that appropriate Due Diligence has been conducted in relation to prospective outsource providers; and ● consider relevant documentation, including the necessity for service level agreements. Documented justification for all outsourcing of critical or customer-impacting functions must be maintained in accordance with record keeping requirements. 2.3. Due Diligence and Third Party Selection Initial Due Diligence Square AU will conduct appropriate initial Due Diligence in relation to prospective third party / Vendor in order to: ● review the experience and capabilities of those providers; and ● ensure that they are suitable to perform the outsourced functions. Initial Due Diligence is driven by the inherent risk and measures the residual risk by assessing a third partyʼs ability to satisfy Square AUʼs requirements for the proposed products and services, while maintaining appropriate controls to ensure compliance with our policies and regulatory requirements. When due diligence is required, Square AU requests the third party to complete topic-based questionnaires (such as information security and privacy) and provide relevant supporting documentation. Risk SMEs review the responses to identify any gaps or control weaknesses and determine appropriate mitigations. TPRM, Procurement, and relevant teams — including Legal and Information Security — then collaborate with the business and the third party to assess risks, agree on remediation actions, and ensure controls are in place before engagement proceeds. The frequency of Due Diligence on vendors is outlined in the Block Inc global Vendor Management Policy. 2.4. Process for the selection of Vendors Planning and assessing risk ● Planning begins when the Square TPRO or Business Unit (“BUˮ) defines business requirements that involve a third party. Square AU selects and manages third parties using a value- and risk-based approach, initiated through a VER to capture engagement details and determine inherent risk, in accordance with the Block Inc global Vendor Management Policy. Vendor selection considers the reputation, financial stability, performance history, and operational capability of the provider. Square AU must be satisfied that each vendor is: ● competent, experienced, and qualified to provide the outsourced service; ● familiar with Square AUʼs business and regulatory obligations; and ● of good fame, character, and integrity. All use of cloud computing, data hosting/storage, and international vendors must undergo additional due diligence to assess cyber and data risks. 2.5. Conflicts of Interest Square AU is committed to managing conflicts of interest to ensure all decisions and interactions are fair, transparent, and in the best interests of customers and the business. A conflict may occur when personal interests, relationships, or external obligations could influence, or appear to influence, professional judgement. Employees must promptly disclose any potential or actual conflicts so they can be assessed and managed appropriately — through disclosure, recusal, segregation of duties, or other controls. All conflicts must be handled in line with the Square AU and Block Inc. Conflicts of Interest Policies. Conduct that prioritises personal or commercial benefit over integrity or customer fairness is not tolerated. 2.6. Contracts Management and Negotiation Square AU will ensure that all third-party engagements are governed by written contracts that align with the requirements of the Block global TPRM policy. During negotiation and throughout the contract lifecycle, agreements must address key risk areas including data privacy, security controls, regulatory compliance, and the right to audit. Contracts will be reviewed in consultation with relevant stakeholders to confirm appropriate risk mitigation measures are included and that third parties remain accountable for their obligations. Regular reviews and updates of contractual terms will be conducted to maintain alignment with evolving business, legal, and regulatory requirements. 2.7. documents.GOV-022: Compliance Management System CMS Policy Square | Square Capital, LLC | Credit Card Last Approved: October 3, 2025 Effective Date: October 3, 2025 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Compliance Management System ("CMS") Policy ("Policy") outlines Square Capital, LLC's ("Square Capital" or the "Company") commitment to implementing and maintaining an effective CMS. The Head of Block Consumer Protection Compliance and/or designee is responsible for management of this Policy. The purpose of this Policy is to set forth the expectations of the Square Capital Board of Managers ("SCBM") for managing and mitigating applicable regulatory compliance risk as it pertains to the Square Credit Card product. This Policy sets forth the framework within which Square Capital implements its CMS. The SCBM recognizes that maintaining an effective CMS is critical for mitigating the risks arising from violations of law and regulations and the potential for customer harm and is necessary to maintain a high level of public confidence in Square Capital's safe and sound operating practices. A critical aspect of managing and mitigating regulatory compliance risk is the development and maintenance of a CMS that is integrated into the overall framework for product design, delivery, and administration of all product and service life cycles. In general, Square Capital achieves this standard by ensuring a sound compliance structure is part of daily operations and that the Company identifies Issues and initiates appropriate and timely remediations. Square Capital's commitment to consumer protection is underscored by its dedication to accountability, transparency, and business ethics. It is Square Capital's policy to comply with all applicable federal state laws and regulations. Pursuant to this Policy, Square Capital will take prudent and reasonable steps to ensure compliance, including careful consideration of applicable commercial credit card laws and regulations. Scope: This Policy is applicable to the Square Capital Credit Card product only. Definitions: Terminology Definition Issue An Issue is defined as a deficiency gap, or weakness in the design, performance, or operating effectiveness. Staff Member An employee or external worker (e.g., contingent labor, contractors) hired by Square Capital who supports the operations covered by this Policy. 2. Policy Requirements 2.1 Requirements for the CMS The SCBM recognizes that sound compliance risk management, like other areas of operational management, depends on Square Capital establishing a comprehensive program of risk controls and reviews. Square Capital requires the CMS to be tailored to Square Capital's organizational needs, business strategy, operational complexity, product diversity, market circumstances, regulatory environment, and risk profile. Because these essential criteria are not static, the SCBM also requires that the CMS have a strong infrastructure while remaining dynamic in its ability to respond to changes. The SCBM mandates that Square Capital's CMS ● Define roles and responsibilities for regulatory compliance activities covered by Square Capital's CMS; ● Require the creation and maintenance of policies, procedures, and controls that ensure that Square Capital conducts business in compliance with applicable laws and regulations and in a manner that meets customer service goals; ● Set forth internal supervision of Square Capital systems in order to deliver consistent and on-going execution of CMS standards; ● Conduct an annual compliance risk assessment to evaluate inherent risks, controls, and the resulting residual risks to Square Capital in order to guide the execution of the CMS; ● Create a monitoring and testing program, and separate independent audit requirements to identify operational and programmatic deficiencies; ● Establish change management and corrective action programs; ● Outline training requirements for all Staff, including the SCBM; ● Distribute compliance reporting for internal and external stakeholders; and ● Reinforce the concepts set forth in Square Capital's CMS Policy as integral to identifying potential violations of law and correcting internal control deficiencies; ● Policies and programs approvals, including those by the Compliance Policy Committee, must be evidenced in Square Capital's meeting minutes. 2.2 The Compliance Management System 2.2.1 Board and Management Oversight and Reporting The Block Lending Product Compliance Team will provide quarterly compliance updates to the SCBM and Senior Management related to the operation of the CMS, customer complaints, monitoring and testing results, corrective action updates, training results, and regulatory findings/exams. 2.2.2 Regulatory Change Management The Legal Team will maintain a regulatory change management process to first identify, monitor, and track relevant material regulatory changes that may impact Square Capital products, services, and operations. The Legal Team will conduct an applicability analysis for certain regulatory changes and then, where necessary, notify key compliance and product stakeholders. The Head of Business Product Compliance and Partnerships, or their designee, with the support of a cross-functional group, will develop and communicate to impacted business teams an implementation plan, as needed, and the impacted business teams will be responsible for executing on that implementation plan. 2.2.3 Risk Assessment Risk assessments are a process for evaluating the compliance risk arising from: (i) Square Capital's operating environments; (ii) the regulatory landscape; (iii) emerging industry and regulatory trends; (iv) the Company's size, complexity, and product offerings; and (v) the effectiveness of internal controls. Risk assessments assist Square Capital in identifying additional control needs, prioritizing compliance resources, and adjusting the CMS to meet new and emerging risks. On an annual basis, the Head of Business Product Compliance and Partnerships, or their designee will conduct a risk assessment with the support of a cross-functional group of stakeholders across product, legal, and compliance. The risk assessments will be used to: ● Understand the risk of specific regulatory requirements to Square Capital; ● Inform monitoring, testing, and training plans; ● Identify opportunities to enhance existing controls or to institute additional controls, as needed; and ● Evaluate resource levels needed to manage compliance risks. The risk assessment framework will assign inherent risk ratings, identify mitigating controls and determine the resulting residual risk rating (which is derived from assessing the likelihood and impact of risk occurrence after considering Square Capital's control activities). Compliance will present the results of the annual risk assessment to the CCO, SCBM and Senior Management. The Block Global Compliance Monitoring and Testing team will conduct monitoring and testing as needed to verify that regulatory changes are incorporated into Square Capital's CMS program. 2.2.4 Policies, Standards, and Procedures Square Capital requires that compliance-related policies, standards, and procedures be documented in written form, in sufficient detail to implement compliance requirements, and are formally approved, adopted, or recognized as outlined in the Compliance Document Approval Matrix. Policies, standards, and procedures are updated periodically by their respective owners, and are reviewed and approved as outlined in the Block, Inc. Compliance Policy on Policies ("PoP"), as required. All policies and standards are posted in a shared location, as outlined in the PoP. The PoP establishes the document hierarchy and life cycle of compliance policies, standards, and procedures, as well as the roles and responsibilities for keeping Square Capital's compliance document inventory reliable and compliant. 2.2.5 Monitoring and Testing Compliance will develop a monitoring and testing plan annually based on the size, scale, and complexity of the Company's operations and business offerings. The annual plan will reflect the risk profile of Square Capital, including relevant third-party risk, as well as compliance risk impacts from new products, regulatory changes, previous testing findings, management action plans and due dates, audit coverage areas, and any bank partner requests. The testing program will support adherence to regulatory compliance requirements and the frequency of testing for applicable regulations and each business unit will depend on Square Capital's risk profile. Corrective action to any noted deficiencies will be implemented efficiently and documented accordingly. Details on this process can be found in Blockʼs Global Compliance Monitoring and Testing Program. 2.2.6 Change Management Block, Inc. has implemented procedures to review new or materially changed business practices (namely marketing materials and customer communications, products or features) using a combination of Legal and Compliance personnel, who will determine whether new or updated controls are required. Square Capital Staff is responsible for engaging these functions and escalating any such changes for review. The results of reviews will be documented and maintained. Business practice changes for Square Capital will also be reviewed, documented and maintained, following these procedures. 2.2.7 Issue Management Block, Inc. maintains a documented Issue Management Program applicable to Square Capital that defines its standardized approach to the identification, assessment, prioritization, treatment, and reporting of Issues. A standardized approach is necessary to allow Senior Management the ability to make effective decisions concerning risk management. Square Capital will track and monitor deficiencies identified through monitoring and testing, complaints, audits, and regulatory exams. Square Capital will record and track the nature of the Issues, a description of the pl

's legal team provides interpretative advice, conducts regulatory management and counsel regarding the applicability and application of the requirements of this Policy to the Companyʼs business and operational activities. Compliance Management System CMS Policy | Square | Square K.K. 5 Confidential and Not for Distribution 1. Introduction Square Kabushiki Kaisha (“Square K.K.ˮ or “Companyˮ) is a Payment Service Provider (“PSPˮ) in Japan that is not at this point directly regulated or supervised by any authorities in Japan. However, as a company that partners with international credit card brands, card issuers, QR-code money transfers, and acquirers, Square K.K. is responsible for satisfying various regulations and guidelines that must be adhered to. The parent company of Square K.K. is Block, Inc. (“Blockˮ), a merchant services aggregator and mobile payments company based in Oakland, California, USA. Block is registered as a Money Services Business with the Financial Crimes Enforcement Network (“FinCENˮ). Compliance is critical for Square K.K. as it ensures the company can sustain its vital partnerships and function effectively within the payment services industry. By adhering to relevant regulations and guidelines, Square K.K. mitigates the risk of liabilities occurring when operating in Japan. Furthermore, a robust compliance framework fosters trust and credibility with customers and partners, enhancing the company's reputation. Compliance also ensures that Square K.K. aligns with the strategic objectives of its parent company, Block, promoting consistent and reliable operations across various markets. This Compliance Management System (“CMSˮ) Policy (“Policyˮ) specifically addresses financial regulatory compliance, focusing on adhering to the standards and requirements set by local and foreign financial authorities and industry bodies. The CMS Policy of Square K.K. demonstrates the commitment of the JP CCO and compliance team to implementing and maintaining effective compliance management to meet the expected compliance standards. This commitment helps maintain strong relationships with partners and customers and supports the Companyʼs goal of providing reliable and innovative payment services. 1. はじめに Square株式会社（以下、「Square K.K.」または「当社」という。）は、日本における決済サービスプロバイダー（以下、「PSP」 という。）であり、現時点では日本の当局による直接の規制や監督を受けていません。しかし、各国際カードブランド、カード発 行会社、QRコードを利用した資金移動業者、アクワイアラーと提携する企業として、Square K.K.は遵守すべきさまざまな規 制やガイドラインを満たす責任があります。また、当社の親会社は、カリフォルニア州オークランドに本社を置くマーチャント サービスアグリゲーターおよびモバイル決済会社であるBlock, Inc.（以下、「Block」という。）であり、金融犯罪取締ネット ワーク（FinCEN）にマネーサービスビジネスとして登録されています。 Compliance Management System CMS Policy | Square | Square K.K. 6 Confidential and Not for Distribution コンプライアンスの維持は、Square K.K.にとって重要です。なぜなら、それが当社の重要なパートナーシップを維持し、決 済サービス業界内で効果的に機能することを保証するからです。関連する規制やガイドラインを遵守することで、Square K.K.は日本で運営することで生じる様々な責任のリスクを軽減します。さらに、堅牢なコンプライアンスフレームワークは、顧 客やパートナーとの信頼と信用を築き、当社の評判を高めます。コンプライアンスは、親会社であるBlockの戦略的目標と一 致し、さまざまな市場での一貫した信頼性のある運営を促進します。 このコンプライアンス管理システム（以下、「CMS」という。）ポリシー（以下、「当ポリシー」という。）は、特に金融規制コンプラ イアンスに焦点を当てており、国内外の金融当局および業界団体が定めた基準と要件を遵守することに重点を置いていま す。当ポリシーは、当社に期待されるコンプライアンス基準を満たすために、Square K.K.のチーフコンプライアンスオフィ サー（以下、「JP CCO」という。）およびコンプライアンスチーム（以下、「JP Compliance Team」という。）が効果的なコン プライアンス管理を実施および維持することへのコミットメントを示しています。このコミットメントは、パートナーや顧客との強 力な関係を維持し、信頼性が高く革新的な決済サービスを提供するという当社の目標をサポートします。 2. The Board recognizes that maintaining an effective CMS is crucial for mitigating risks arising from legal and regulatory violations, as well as preventing potential harm to customers and partners. It is also essential for sustaining a high level of public confidence in Square K.K.'s safe and sound operating practices. Confidential and Not for Distribution Definitions Terminology Definition Business Continuity Business continuity risk is the risk that a third party, providing services or Risk products to Block, experiences a disruptive incident, such as a cyber-attack, natural disaster, or technology failure, that deteriorates the quality or availability of the outsourced service or product beyond acceptable levels, as defined by predetermined impact tolerances, including Recovery Time Objectives (“RTOsˮ) and Recovery Point Objectives (“RPOsˮ), or leads to a complete failure of the service. Compliance Risk The risk to current or projected financial condition and resilience arising from violations of laws, rules, or regulations, or from non-conformance with prescribed practices, policies and procedures. Contract Any agreement that binds or obligates Block legally or financially. Contracts may include but are not limited to Master Services Agreements, Statements of Work, Order Forms, Online Terms of Use, Subscription Agreements, Change orders, etc. Critical Vendor A Critical Vendor for Block is a vendor who provides products or services that are essential to day-to-day operations and whose failure to provide those products or services, either willfully or inadvertently, will cause material impacts to Block or its customers. Regulatory Change Management Standard | Afterpay US, Inc. 5 Confidential and Not for Distribution 1. Introduction This Regulatory Change Management Standard (“Standard”) outlines Afterpay US, Inc.’s (“Afterpay”) approach to identifying changes to . The Product Compliance Manager is responsible for the management of this Standard. 2. Purpose The purpose of the Regulatory Change Management Standard is to direct effective change management that will ensure all impacted LOB revise and implement procedures and controls to mitigate Afterpay’s legal, compliance, and reputation risks related to any regulatory changes. The purpose of this document is to outline the roles and responsibilities and workflow for processing regulatory changes. The scope of the Standard is designed to direct the implementation of regulatory changes in an efficient and effective manner by providing regulatory guidance to Directly Responsible Individuals (“DRIs”) to mitigate legal, compliance and reputation risks. The Lending Regulatory Counsel Team (“Lend Reg”) and Product Compliance (“Compliance”) will coordinate with multiple groups within Afterpay to drive the change process and package implementation material for the impacted LOBs. Generally, the level of detail captured herein is intended to provide experienced Managers and associates with an overview and understanding of the subject process and underlying activities for implementing regulatory change, and it should serve as a resource to such individuals. The regulatory change management process will: ● Identify and research regulatory changes; ● Evaluate the regulatory change to determine the impacted LOBs; ● Create and manage a project plan for the regulatory change; ● Effectively communicate and provide legal and compliance support for the regulatory change to the impacted LOBs; ● Update Afterpay policies impacted by the regulatory change and ensure that the impacted LOBs update their policies and procedures as needed; ● Designate training, as needed, for associates impacted by the regulatory change; and Regulatory Change Management Standard | Afterpay US, Inc. 6 Confidential and Not for Distribution ● Verify the regulatory change has been implemented by the impacted LOBs prior to the final effective date of the change. 3. Scope This Standard is applicable to Afterpay’s employees and sets out the approach to Regulatory Change Management. This Standard has also been created to ensure that Afterpay and relevant personnel are aware of and comply with expectations regarding Regulatory Change Management, as detailed below. 4. Standard Requirements 4.1.1 Identify and Research Regulatory Changes Lend Reg will identify regulatory changes using reputable industry resources. When changes are identified that will impact Afterpay’s processes and practices, Lend Reg will review and research the proposed change. There are several resources that will be used by Lend Reg to identify and research regulatory changes including but not limited to the following: ● Reg Counsel Monthly Updates meeting; ● Fiscal Note Report; ● Bi-weekly Policy Counsel Meeting; ● Policy Reg Counsel Slack Channel; ● Communications from the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (“CFPB”); ● Communications from outside counsel; and ● Communications from other Federal banking agencies. In addition to these resources, other reputable industry resources (bulletins, regulator news updates, etc.) or RegTech solutions may be used. When changes are identified where a Notice of Proposed Rulemaking (“NPRM”) has been issued by the regulatory agencies, Lend Reg will create a Notion change event with impacts to a LOB and update it throughout the implementation process. Regulatory Change Management Standard | Afterpay US, Inc. 7 Confidential and Not for Distribution 4.2 Evaluate the Regulatory Change to Determine the Impacted LOBs Lend Reg and Compliance will evaluate the proposed regulatory change to determine the impacted LOBs that will be responsible for implementing the change. At this time, Lend Reg will create a Notion change event. LOBleadership will determine DRIs and assign the appropriate associate(s) to create a Project Team. During the process of building the Notion change event, the Project Team will then evaluate and formulate their respective role in the Change Management Process. 4.3 Create and Manage a Project Plan for the Regulatory Change Once regulatory agencies issue a final rule with an effective date for a proposed regulatory change, the Project Team will develop the project plan. The project plan will include the following: ● Assignment of DRIs for the impacted BU(s); ● State the project scope; ● Prepare a plan to monitor the progress of the implementation for the regulatory change; ● Determine a Cost Management Plan or Budget, if applicable; ● Determine the schedule for implementing the change; ● Incorporate the communication plan; ● Define the BU’s quality control/assurance process required; ● Final implementation of the regulatory change. NOTE: Whenever a regulatory change impacts system processes, the LOB impacted by a change and IT will be responsible for implementing

Activation A machine learning model and score associated with a one-time Customer Risk snapshot of initial customer risk taken at onboarding. Rating (“aCRR”) Continuous A machine learning model and score associated with a periodically Customer Risk updated (i.e. ongoing) assessment of customer risk. Activation Customer Risk Rating Standard | Block 4 Confidential and Not for Distribution Roles and Responsibilities Role Responsibility Compliance ● Review alerts and investigate associated activity Operations ● Decision alerts ● Escalate accounts for unusual or suspicious activity International and ● Reviewers of the standard and individuals / businesses escalated Regional Chief by Compliance Operations for the specific region Compliance Officers (“CCO”) Square & Cash ● Approve the Compliance Customer Risk Standard document CCOs Regulatory ● Establish and maintain CRR models, parameters and thresholds Compliance and queueing infrastructure Machine Learning Model Risk ● Complete periodic model validation on the CRR models and Management associated queueing logic Cash Compliance ● Provide reporting to senior leadership on queue completion, Analytics performance, and enhancements Activation Customer Risk Rating Standard | Block 5 Confidential and Not for Distribution 1. Introduction The cornerstone of a strong anti-money laundering (“AML”) compliance program is the adoption and implementation of risk-based due diligence policies, procedures, and processes for all customers, particularly those that present a higher risk for money laundering and terrorist financing. In order to achieve this, Block, Inc. (“Block”) has designed and implemented a customer risk rating standard that assesses a customer's profile, assigns a risk rating, and monitors the risk levels of customers. This Activation Customer Risk Rating (“CRR”) Standard (“Standard”) enables Block to apply the appropriate risk-based controls to mitigate exposure to money laundering, terrorism financing, and financial crime. Improper assessment of a customer’s risk can have a cascading effect, creating deficiencies in multiple areas of internal controls and resulting in an overall weakened AML compliance standard. This document covers the development, usage, validation and change management requirements of a machine learning (“ML”)-based activation CRR model (“aCRR”). 2. Purpose All user accounts, once successfully onboarded (activated), will be risk-rated using a ML-based aCRR model. The aCRR model defines the risk ratings for customers at onboarding, producing a one-time snapshot of initial customer risk that may be consumed by Compliance Operations teams. The relevant team leads, along with Governance & Compliance Engineering & Technology, will establish the criteria for the usage of the aCRR model output. 3. Scope This Standard is established by Block and is applicable globally to Square and Cash App where applicable. Activation Customer Risk Rating Standard | Block 6 Confidential and Not for Distribution 4. Standard Requirements 4.1 Process Presentation Figure 1. Square process flow diagram of aCRR model evaluation. Figure 2. Cash App process flow diagram of aCRR model evaluation and input to due diligence reviews Activation Customer Risk Rating Standard | Block 7 Confidential and Not for Distribution 4.2 Automated Risk Rating Assignment 4.2.1 Risk Scope The goal of the aCRR model is to assess the risk associated with an onboarded account with respect to money laundering, terrorism financing and illegal/suspicious activity/financial crime.1 While Politically Exposed Person (“PEP”) matches, sanctions, and Terms of Service violation risks are monitored by other Compliance programs, the aCRR model will not incorporate Politically Exposed Person (“PEP”) matches, sanctions, and Terms of Service violation risks into the generated risk score at onboarding. 4.2.2 Eligible Customer Population Scope All user accounts are generally in scope for risk assessment at onboarding for both Square and Cash App customers for all geographies where the products are in operation. In the event that Block expands a line of business to a new geography where it had previously not operated and no historical data or equivalent proxy from a similar geography is available at launch, a heuristics based approach may be used to collect sufficient outcomes based data2 required to build an ML based model. Alternatively, an ML model that is available in a similar geography may be deployed in the new geography with appropriate controls. 4.2.3 Model Output The aCRR model evaluation produces a score between 0.0 and 1.0 for every user account on assessment, with 1.0 indicating maximum risk and 0.0 indicating minimal risk. For model usage purposes, thresholds may be applied on the continuous risk score and combined with additional business rules to generate alerts for review by the operations teams. 1 Sanctions and Terms of Service violation related actions are excluded 2 The time required to collect sufficient examples is a function of both volume and time period required for the labels to mature. These are in turn dependent on the nature of the product and membership. The decisions are made by the relevant ML team managers. Activation Customer Risk Rating Standard | Block 8 Confidential and Not for Distribution 4.2.4 Model Features An analysis is performed to assess which features are available for consideration in the model. Regulatory requirements, guidance, and industry best practices are used to determine the features that need to be considered for the model. Details of the specific features used in the aCRR model can be found in the relevant aCRR System Design documents. The following types of signals (or features) may be included in the model (non-exhaustive): ● Location and time zone based (IP address or self declared); ● Onboarding self attested information based (e.g. usage intent, source of wealth/income, employment status); ● Identity Verification information and attempts from third party vendors (e.g. Onfido, TLO, LexisNexis, IDology etc.); ● Third party information based on email/IP/phone number including, but not limited to dimensions like known risk indicators etc (Emailage, Ekata, Neustar, Twilio); ● (Stated and/or Inferred) Business Type3 (self attested and/or inferred); ● Hard Connectivity to existing or past assets (e.g. bank accounts, payment cards, SSN, Employer Identification Number4, IP addresses) previously associated with Risk/Compliance actions; ● Connectivity to existing or past user accounts (e.g. via CashApp Contact Books) associated with previous risk/compliance actions5; ● Historical transactional activity based signals of connected Square accounts6; ● Historical transactional activity based signals from customers sharing the same IP address 7; and ● Behavioral signals about the activation process (e.g. app installs, app & device versions, cashtag length etc.). 3 Square: Stated or inferred business type (MCC) features are not available at activation time, so the ML model does not use them as input signals. However, MCC information is combined with the aCRR scores in the business logic for alerting decisions. 4 EIN is for Seller only 5 Cash only 6 Cash only (time period from onboarding event to risk assessment) 7 Square only Activation Customer Risk Rating Standard | Block 9 Confidential and Not for Distribution 4.2.5 Explainability Every aCRR model evaluation and risk rating also logs an ‘explanation’ that provides insight into the features and/or signals that contributed to the specific customer risk score and their specific weights or relative contribution importance.
