Document Details 18 Anti-Money Laundering, Combating the Financing of Terrorism & Sanctions Product Risk Assessment Standard | Block 3 Confidential and Not for Distribution Definitions Definitions Terminology Definition Inherent Risk Level of risk of business processes and activities without considering the perceived mitigating effect of related controls Control Activity An activity that mitigates (some of) the risks associated with conducting business processes that could otherwise cause negative outcomes for the company, employees, shareholders, and/or community it services Control Mechanism designed to enable business to be conducted in an orderly Environment and efficient manner and ensure adherence to prescribed practice; Control The measure of how well a control is designed and/or operating to Effectiveness achieve the control objectives and address the cause and/or consequence of the risk. Launch For the purposes of the Product Risk Assessment, launch refers to the moment a Product or Feature is available for use by end users (inclusive of customers and internal employees). Product Risk A standardized set of evaluation points that is commissioned by the Assessment (PRA) Global Policy and Governance (GPG) team, which are intended to evaluate the inherent risk(s) associated with products and features. The evaluation points and risk assessment is focused on money laundering, terrorist financing, and sanctions risk only.1 PRA Tool A user-friendly worksheet designed by the CGR-RA Team for business unit compliance to 1)determine if a triggering event has occurred, requiring a PRA; and, 2)complete a PRA Products and For the purposes of the PRA, Products and Features refers to the services Features (Products) that product teams build and release to end users, inclusive of internal customers. Product Initiative The process of bringing a new product to the market, renewing an existing product and introducing a product in a new market. Residual Risk Level of risk inclusive of considering the perceived mitigating effect of related controls. 1 Work is underway to expand the scope of PRAs to include all compliance risk types. Anti-Money Laundering, Combating the Financing of Terrorism & Sanctions Product Risk Assessment Standard | Block 4 Confidential and Not for Distribution Roles and Responsibilities Role Responsibilities Compliance ● Manages the Anti-Money Laundering / Countering the Financing Governance and of Terrorisim & Sanctions Product Risk Assessment Standard Reporting Risk ● Develops and maintains the methodology Assessment Team ● Designs and manages tools and reporting (CGR-RA) ● Provides oversight and guidance for the PRA process Business Unit ● Responsible for maintaining and owning the PRAs related to the Compliance (i.e. products and features managed by their respective business units Product ● Responsible for designating an Assigned Person (as needed) to Compliance execute PRAs Management ● Facilitates collecting any information that must be acquired from (PCM), Global the product or business teams (such as information about product Regulatory controls) Oversight, Assigned ● Reviews and confirms accuracy of PRA Tool responses Persons) ● Responsible for ensuring all PRAs and associated control documentation are stored in the PRA Tracker at go/RA Anti-Money Laundering, Combating the Financing of Terrorism & Sanctions Product Risk Assessment Standard | Block 5 Confidential and Not for Distribution 1. Introduction This Product Risk Assessment Standard (“Standard”) outlines Block’s approach to conduct Anti-Money Laundering (“AML”) and Countering the Financing of Terrorism (“CFT”) & Sanctions Product Risk Assessment(s) (“PRA”) and ensure a consistent and standardized approach across all business lines and markets in which we operate. The Compliance Governance and Reporting Risk Assessment Lead is responsible for management of this Standard. PRAs are used to: ● Self-identify products and features that may pose potential AML/CFT & Sanctions risks; ● Measure, document, and prioritize risk; ● Evaluate and develop sound controls; ● Assess current control environment relevant to the product or feature launch; and ● Identify opportunities to strengthen the control environment as appropriate. The PRA is an AML/CFT & Sanctions assessment that measures the product-specific inherent risks to inform and align control activities prior to the launch of new Products and Features. This process requires the use of a worksheet (“PRA Tool”) to standardize the approach and drive consistency in the evaluation of risks. 2. Purpose This Standard outlines Block’s requirements and process for managing risks associated with its products and services, ensuring compliance with AML/CFT sanctions regulations, and other related laws and standards. This Standard further aims to protect Block’s business integrity, maintain customer trust, and minimize financial and reputational risks. 3. Scope This PRA process is applicable to all products & services within Block’s businesses, including but not limited to Square, Cash App, Afterpay and TIDAL business units. PRAs are conducted by each Block Anti-Money Laundering, Combating the Financing of Terrorism & Sanctions Product Risk Assessment Standard | Block 6 Confidential and Not for Distribution business unit on all Products and Features whose characteristics and functionality may present the risk of money laundering, terrorist financing, or sanctions violations. The PRA is used to ensure that Compliance and Business stakeholders are aware of the AML/CFT and Sanctions inherent risks prior to launch of the product with adequate time to consider whether to enhance existing or implement new controls. Note: Following a determination that a PRA is required, it is a standing condition for go-live of a new product that controls are identified and documented for all identified areas of increased risk identified by the PRA Tool. See section 5.3 Align Risk-Proportionate Controls for more information. 4. Product Risk Assessment Process Overview The PRA is a Block enforced requirement that is completed prior to product launch. Business Unit Compliance is responsible for completing the PRA in addition to coordinating any requirements from relevant stakeholders. The PRA must be completed in accordance with the following steps: 1. Qualify need for PRA using the ‘Triggering Event Evaluation’ within the PRA Tool; 2. Determine Inherent Risk rating by completing the assessment in the PRA Tool; 3. Align risk proportionate controls to all areas of increased risk identified by the PRA Tool; and 4. Communicate results to relevant stakeholders. Further explanation of each step is found below in the Global Methodology section. 5. Global Methodology The PRA should be conducted in accordance with the requirements set forth above and the methodology described below. Anti-Money Laundering, Combating the Financing of Terrorism & Sanctions Product Risk Assessment Standard | Block 7 Confidential and Not for Distribution 5.1 Confirm Triggering Event When launching new products, materially enhancing existing products, registering new entities, or following a merger & acquisition, Block's exposure to money laundering, terrorist financing and sanctions risk may be impacted. Certain activities require a PRA. These activities are referred to as a Triggering Event. PRAs are required to be completed whenever there is an applicable Triggering Event, in alignment with the relevant program and tools managed by the CGR-RA Team. Examples of Triggering Events for which a PRA must be completed include but are not limited to: ● Introduction of a new product or feature that is: ○ Not currently offered by the business unit; ○ Being launched for the first time; or ○ Introduced to Block through mergers or acquisitions. ● Material changes to an existing product or feature including but not limited to: ○ The introduction of new payment methods, transaction types, or payment options such as credit facilities, investment options, e-wallets, or crypto/virtual currencies; ○ The addition of new delivery channels, such as mobile app integrations, online marketplaces, or blockchain integrations; ○ Changes to product jurisdiction, including expansion into new regions, new geographic markets, or changes from domestic-only to cross-border functionality; ○ Introduction of product to new customer types, including: corporate customer segment, non-profit organizations, government segments, or other changes to initial product targets; ○ An increase of transaction limits or thresholds; ○ Change of a product from unregulated to regulated; and 5.2 Determine Inherent Risk Rating The PRA Tool serves as a structured mechanism for identifying and assessing the inherent risks pose

Document Details 16 Risk and Controls Documentation Standard | Block 3 Confidential and Not for Distribution Definitions Terminology Definition Compliance Risks For the purpose of this document, Compliance Risks and Controls refer and Controls to Compliance related risks and controls. Enterprise Risk The strategic enterprise level of process, overseen by the Board and Management implemented by Management and other personnel to identify potential (‘’ERM’’) risks and manage them within Block’s Risk Appetite to enable Block to achieve its strategic, operational, reporting, and compliance objectives. Enterprise Risk An annual assessment of the top management identified Inherent Risks Assessment categories that if incurred can expose Block to financial Impacts, (‘ERA”) regulatory actions, or loss of shareholder confidence, competitive advantages, and reputation. Compliance Risk Any Risk Assessment undertaken of a specific Level I, II, III or IV risk, Assessment owned by Compliance, and in line with the ERM Risk Categorisation. (“CRA”) This includes the ARA, and may also be referenced as Regulatory RCSAs and include other RCSAs owned by Compliance. Annual Risk An annual process for identifying, measuring, and reporting Assessment AML/CFT/Sanctions risks and the effectiveness of established control (“ARA”) environments designed to mitigate these risks with an objective of informing risk management strategies and organizational objectives. Risk Control A continuous bottom up assessment used to identify, assess and Self-Assessment document risks & controls within an organization to evaluate their (“RCSA”) effectiveness in optimizing risk portfolios. It serves as the primary tool for implementing the Enterprise Risk Assessment (ERA) at the business level. RCSA Playbook Risks and Controls Self Assessment (“RCSA”) playbook that serves as comprehensive guidance, templates and rubrics to support the practical implementation of the RCSA. It includes observable characteristics or criteria used to consistently evaluate where we are or aim to be with respect to risks, controls, and appetites. Compliance Risk A worksheet template which defines the minimum data elements that and Controls need to be captured in order to generate a harmonized and consistent Template risk & control inventory that supports and conforms to this standard. Risk and Controls Documentation Standard | Block 4 Confidential and Not for Distribution Roles and Responsibilities Role Responsibility Brand Program The individual responsible within each Brand for the documentation of Manager - Risks & risks and controls. This person works with key stakeholders to ensure Controls that the risk and control inventory is current and complete and in conformance with these standards. Control Owner The individual responsible for the design, implementation and oversight of control effectiveness of specific controls to reduce inherent risk. They ensure controls are appropriately designed, regularly reviewed and updated to reflect changes to the risk environment. The Control Owner ensures controls operate as intended and provide oversight, reviews evidence and support during assessments or audits to maintain control effectiveness. Control Performer The individual responsible for executing the day-to-day activities of specific controls as designed. They ensure controls are performed accurately, consistently, and on schedule. The Control Performer provides evidence of control execution during assessments or audits and highlights any challenges, gaps, or inefficiencies to the Control Owner for resolution. Enterprise Risk Builds and oversees the global enterprise risk function for Block Management including its enterprise risk management framework and related policies (“ERM”) and processes. ERM owns the ERA process for the overall execution of enterprise risk strategies and risk mitigation. Product Product Compliance Management is the Compliance Team responsible Compliance for partnering cross-functionally with product, risk, compliance Management operations, legal, and other teams to design and advise on the building Team of compliance risk controls and conduct assessment and mitigation (PCM) functions regarding compliance risks. Risk Scenario Responsible for defining, maintaining, and updating risk scenarios that Owner (Level IV) are relevant to their assigned area. The Risk Scenario Owner supports the evaluation of the risk scenarios during the RCSA process and ensures risk scenarios are clearly documented, well understood, and actionable, enabling teams to assess and mitigate risks effectively. Risk and Controls Documentation Standard | Block 5 Confidential and Not for Distribution 1. Introduction This Compliance - Risk and Controls Documentation Standard (“Standard”) outlines Block, Inc. 's (“Block”) approach to establishing a unified and systematic approach to compliance related risk and control documentation across Block and its brands. It provides the fundamental guidelines for maintaining an accurate and comprehensive record of all compliance risk-related activities and control measures, with an objective of ensuring consistency in risk management practices to promote the effectiveness of Block’s control environment. Block’s RCSA Playbook must be consulted for guidance in conjunction with this Standard while documenting Compliance related risks and controls. The Head of Global Policy & Governance is responsible for management of this Standard with delegation to relevant Brand Program Manager - Risks & Controls. This delegation includes responsibility and ownership for enforcement of these standards. 2. Purpose The purpose of this standard is to define the specific requirements and minimum standards for documenting both compliance risks and their corresponding controls across Block and its affiliated brands. The standard aims to ensure that all risk and control information is systematically recorded and properly maintained, thereby supporting effective oversight and compliance with regulatory requirements. It serves as Compliance’s Standard for meeting the expectations of external partners, regulators, and auditors, while simultaneously providing clear guidelines for internal stakeholders. By implementing this Standard, Block strengthens its ability to identify, assess, and mitigate risks effectively, ultimately safeguarding the company's interests and ensuring compliance with applicable regulations across all jurisdictions in which it operates. 3. Scope This Standard is established by Block, Inc. (“Block”) and is applicable globally across all Block brands, subsidiaries, and jurisdictions to all Compliance personnel within Block who are responsible for risk and control documentation. It covers the documentation processes related to compliance risks, as well as the controls designed to mitigate these risks. The Standard mandates adherence to specified documentation practices during the creation, review, updating, and retention of risk and control Risk and Controls Documentation Standard | Block 6 Confidential and Not for Distribution records. It focuses exclusively on the documentation and identification aspects of Compliance Risks and Controls and does not encompass risk and control measurements. 4. Standard Requirements 4.1 Documentation Principles: The Standard’s requirements are embedded in the four fundamental principles to provide a structured approach to documenting Compliance Risks and Controls, with the objective of ensuring clarity, consistency, and comprehensiveness in the process. 1. Consistency: All documentation of Compliance Risks and Controls must adhere to a standardized format and level of granularity for consistent recording and reporting across all Block brands, facilitating comparability and coherence. The risk and controls repository must maintain scalability to meet the unique needs of different businesses while preserving core documentation standards across the enterprise. 2. Accountability and Ownership: Clear responsibility must be assigned for both the creation and maintenance of risk and control documentation. Each control instance must have an identified owner, who is accountable for the accuracy and completeness of the information. 3. Comprehensive Coverage: The documentation must encompass all key Compliance Risks and Controls, as well as the linkage between identified risks and their corresponding controls. 4. Regular Review and Updates: Risk and control documentation must be reviewed and updated per the scheduled frequency defined by the Brands or more frequently if trigger events occur, to account for changes in the business environment, operational processes, Issues and/or Incidents or regulatory requirements. This principle ensures that the documentation remains relevant and provides a reliable basis for decision-making. Compliance teams are empowered to further group like/ and or interconnected attributes to assist in risk management activities. Risk and Controls Documentation Standard | Block 7 Confidential and Not for Distribution 4
documents.GOV-031: RETIRED Compliance Audit, Exam & Independent Assessment Management Program Block Published September 2023 Table of Contents Table of Contents 2 Introduction 3 Definitions 3 Roles & Responsibilities 4 Audit, Exam, and Assessment Phases 5 Document Management 6 Document Owner 6 Scheduled Review & Ad-Hoc Maintenance 6 Exceptions 6 Related Documents 7 Revision & Approval History 7 Document Details 7 Compliance Audit, Exam & Independent Assessment Management Program | Block 2 Introduction Block, Inc. (the “Company” or “Block”) engages in several money transmission, payment activities, investment, and lending services which are regulated. Block maintains money transmission or electronic money institution licenses globally and as such is subject to examinations, audits and independent assessments. Block must also complete assessments for various aspects of its business to ensure we are meeting our legal, regulatory, and business partner requirements for compliance. This Audit, Exam, and Independent Assessment Management Program (the “Program”) articulates Block’s approach to managing and supporting various audits, regulatory examinations, and independent assessments. It outlines the roles and responsibilities of stakeholders to promote transparency, consistency, and collaboration. This program is established by Block, Inc. (“Block”) and is applicable globally across all Block business lines, subsidiaries, and jurisdictions. References to “staff” throughout this document shall refer to all full-time employees and fixed-term and contingent workers including interns, temps, consultants, vendors, third-party providers, and contractors. Definitions Regulatory examinations A regulatory examination is a review of a financial institution to determine whether the financial institution is compliant with the local laws and regulations. Block is subject to examinations of various aspects of its business to ensure it meets licensing and regulatory requirements. Independent assessments An Independent assessment is a review of Block’s Compliance program (either in whole or in part) as directed by contractual agreements or specific regulatory requirements. This may include bank partner audits, network and acquirer partner audits. Engagement A term used to describe a regulatory exam, audit, or independent assessment. Internal audit Internal Audit conducts an independent review of the compliance controls in an Compliance Audit, Exam & Independent Assessment Management Program | Block 3 objective manner to ensure that we comply with the company policies, global, federal and state regulatory compliance regulations. At Block, these audits are performed by a third party, such as Deloitte, Ernst & Young on behalf of the firm. Roles & Responsibilities Compliance Core ● Communicates the importance of compliance regulatory examinations, audits, and assessments to their teams. ● Fosters a collaborative and open environment where Leads are empowered to address questions directly. ● Provides support, direction, or guidance to leads for questions that are ambiguous or unclear. Compliance Leads ● Provide cohesive responses for audit, exam, and assessment questions. ● Ensure responses to questions posed by auditors and examiners are accurate, complete and submitted to the Exam and Audit team in a timely manner. ● Escalate responses that require review by Compliance core. ● Develop comprehensive management action plans (MAPs)/ Corrective Actions that address the root cause of issues and mitigate risks to acceptable levels. EXAM Team ● Provides support for and facilitates regulatory-driven audits, exams, and assessments (excluding contractual obligations). ● Facilitates interactions and communications with auditors and examiners. ● Engages Compliance Leads for audit, exam, and assessment requests and questions. ● Reviews and provides responses to draft observations by auditor/examiner. ● Works with auditors/examiners to finalize reports. Compliance Audit, Exam & Independent Assessment Management Program | Block 4 ● Provides guidance to the Compliance partner audit management team for managing the partner audits. Compliance Partner Risk Team ● Provides support for and facilitates partner audits. ● Facilitates interactions and communications with vendors or partners conducting the audit/assessment. ● Engages Compliance Leads for requests and questions. ● Reviews and provides responses to draft observations by vendors/ partners. ● Works with vendors/partners to finalize reports. ● Follows the guidelines provided by the EXAM team in order to manage the audit. Exam and Regulatory Response Managers ● Liaises with EXAM team on regulatory responses and requests from regulators and regulatory agencies as the point of contact for Compliance. GPG Lead ● Provides oversight of compliance audits, examinations, and assessments (and as needed for partner audits) Internal Audit ● Block’s third line of defense that conducts an independent review of Compliance controls in an objective manner to ensure that Block complies with company policies, global, federal and state compliance regulations. Audit, Exam, and Assessment Phases Compliance audits, regulatory exams, and assessments consist of the following phases: ● Initiation Phase - Block receives notification of an upcoming engagement. This notification may be a notice from state examiners, requests from business partners, or as part of a predetermined annual audit plan (“Reviewers”). Internal Audit and Compliance collaborate to plan required internal audits. Compliance Audit, Exam & Independent Assessment Management Program | Block 5 ● Planning Phase - This phase includes creating a timeline for each legal entity in scope for the engagement, setting up the working environment, and identifying appropriate stakeholders and directly responsible individuals (DRIs). This also includes preparation for kickoff meetings, walkthroughs, preparing DRIs for meetings, and creation of necessary presentations. ○ Interaction Model and Communication - The respective engagement management team is responsible for sending formal communications to relevant stakeholders for the scope, applicability, and roles and responsibilities for each engagement. This includes engaging an appropriate Counsel Legal team to ensure legal support for each of the jurisdictions and products in scope for the engagement. The respective audit management team ensures clear communication throughout the engagement. This ensures all stakeholders and DRIs are aware and informed of any requests or follow-up questions. ● On-site Phase - This phase includes managing, responding to, and escalating requests and follow-up questions. It also includes coordinating any meeting requests. The respective audit management team serves as the main point of contact for all requests during the engagement. ● Post-Engagement Phase - This phase includes working with the reviewers in completing the final report with management response and tracking and managing the issues or observations and CAPs/ MAPs to completion (link for MAP guidance). This also includes providing regular reporting to management, committees, or boards, as appropriate. Document Management Document Owner This document is owned by the EXAM team lead or their designee. Scheduled Review & Ad-Hoc Maintenance This document shall be reviewed and approved at least annually and on an ad-hoc basis in accordance with the Compliance Document Approval Matrix. Compliance Audit, Exam & Independent Assessment Management Program | Block 6 Exceptions Exceptions to the requirements, strategies, or procedures established by this document must be pre-approved by the EXAM team lead or their designee. Staff members may submit exception requests for review at go/policyhelp. documents.GOV-032: Compliance Committee & Forum Oversight Standard Block, Inc. Last Approved: Mar 31, 2026 Effective Date: Mar 31, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Compliance Committee & Forum Oversight Standard (the “Standardˮ) outlines Block, Inc.ʼs (“Blockˮ or “the Companyˮ) documented approach to the structure and operation of Compliance governance. The purpose of this Standard is to document the requirements and principles governing the Compliance Committee and Forum Oversight Framework (the “Frameworkˮ) that was established to ensure appropriate oversight and accountability of the Compliance program. The Framework outlines the structure for a Compliance governance model that maintains clear accountability, supports effective decision-making, and ensures appropriate oversight. Scope: This Standard applies to Block, Inc., its subsidiaries, and affiliates (collectively, “Blockˮ), including all products, business lines, and jurisdictions. Further, this Standard applies to all formally governed Compliance Committees and Forums and employees participating in the governance bodies. An exception to this scope is Square Financial Services (“SFSˮ), Blockʼs Utah state-chartered Industrial Bank federally regulated by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation, which has its own Compliance Program. Definitions: Terminology Definition Tracked Action Items Activities requiring execution or completion that arise and are agreed upon during Forum or Committee meetings. Tracked Action Items are documented, including a description of the action, the responsible party, and the expected completion timeframe. These items, which arise from Committee or Forum meetings require formal oversight, documentation, and closure verification through the governance process. Any task that does not meet this definition will not be centrally tracked, reported, or formally evidenced, and will instead be managed through standard BAU routines by the relevant program owners, stakeholders, and/or DRIs. 1 Terminology Definition Additional Observers Individuals who may attend a meeting where their participation is deemed relevant or necessary. Committee A formally constituted and authorized governance body that makes decisions, approves actions, addresses escalated risk issues, and/or provides management oversight (as documented in the Committee Charter). Committee Chair The chairperson (“Chairˮ) for a Committee meeting who, in accordance with the Committee Charter, is assigned ownership, responsibility, and accountability for the facilitation of the Committee meetings. Committee Charter A formal document that outlines and defines the Committeeʼs purpose, authority, composition, and responsibilities. The (“Charterˮ) Charter establishes the Committeeʼs role in supporting oversight of compliance with applicable laws, regulations, internal policies, and ethical standards. The Charter also defines meeting frequency, reporting requirements, and access to information to support effective communication, information, and alignment across the organization. Decision-Making The formal authority to approve actions, address escalated risk issues, and provide management oversight, which resides at Level 3 committees and above. Decisions, which require committee quorum, are documented through meeting minutes, and, as appropriate, reported upward to Level 2 Block Risk Council / ERM Issues Management) and ultimately Level 1 Board / Audit & Risk Committee). For example, the Compliance Strategy and Risk Committee CSRC and the Compliance Management System CMS Health Committee are formally constituted, Chief Compliance Officer-chaired governance bodies with voting members, chartered authority, and formal meeting minutes. They receive consolidated reporting from Level 4 forums and may make decisions on matters such as policy approvals, risk alignment, escalated issues, and programmatic changes. Delegate A temporary representative who attends a Forum or Committee meeting on behalf of a Required Attendee when the Required Attendee is unable to attend. Delegates may satisfy attendance requirements, but voting authority cannot be delegated. 2 Terminology Definition Directly Responsible A single person, independent of function, who is accountable for decision-making and ensuring a project meets its goals. Individual (“DRIˮ) Escalations Items requiring oversight or decision-making by the corresponding, higher-level body. Forum A structured, but non-decision-making body, designed to share information, align stakeholders, surface and discuss issues, and provide input or recommendations to decision-making authorities where appropriate. Forum DRI The individual assigned ownership, responsibility, and accountability for leading and managing the Forum meetings to ensure they run effectively and achieve their intended purpose. Forum Purpose Statement A formal document that outlines and defines the Forumʼs purpose, authority, composition, and responsibilities. (“Purpose Statementˮ) Framework Documents The collective set of documents that establish and govern the Compliance Committee and Forum Oversight Framework, including this Standard, Committee Charters, Forum Purpose Statements, and supporting artifacts. Immaterial Changes Non-substantive changes to a Framework Documentʼs content that do not impact requirements or guidance related to goals, principles, processes, or directives of the Framework. Immaterial Changes may include, but are not limited to: ● Updating grammar or formatting; ● Correcting or updating naming conventions, titles, or position names; ● New, modified, or removed Committee/Forum metrics that do not materially impact oversight or reporting outcomes; ● Minor refinements to reporting inputs or outputs that do not materially change the scope, decision-making, or oversight responsibilities; and ● Updates to attendees that do not alter quorum, decision-making authority, or required role representation, and are consistent with the applicable Charter or Purpose Statement. ● Alignment changes made to ensure Committee Charters and Forum Purpose Statements are consistent with Framework requirements, provided such changes: (i) are 3 Terminology Definition limited to conforming language, terminology, or processes to match updated Framework requirements; (ii) do not introduce new substantive requirements or materially change existing obligations; and (iii) are applied consistently across similar governance bodies within the same governance level. All Framework alignment changes must be documented with clear rationale linking to specific Framework updates. Information-sharing The structured cross-functional or upward flow of risk data, metrics, and operational insights through the governance hierarchy. Level 4 forums are the primary information-sharing bodies — they are non-decision-making forums designed to surface, discuss, and consolidate information from sub-Level 4 workstreams and DRIs within their defined focus areas. Forums evaluate risks comprehensively and report critical items, aggregated metrics, and recommendations upward to Level 3 committees. This includes operational performance data, emerging risk trends, issue status, and program updates. Information sharing ensures compliance leadership has visibility into key risk areas without over-relying on a single governance body. Level 1 Board Level) The Companyʼs ultimate governing body responsible for overseeing the Company's activity. Level 2 Senior Management A senior leadership level that functions as an aggregation and review layer for the Risk and Compliance organization before Level) Board-level oversight. Level 3 Management Compliance oversight level consisting of formally constituted Committees. Committee Level) Level 4 Forum Level) The operational layer consisting of specialized Forums. Material Changes Substantive changes to a Framework Documentʼs requirements or guidance that impact, or may impact, the goals, principles, processes, or directives of the Framework. Material changes may include include, but are not limited to: ● Creation or retirement of a Committee or Forum; ● Modification of the governance structure or upward reporting lines; 4 Terminology Definition ● Changes to a Framework Document's purpose, authority, scope, decision-making authority, quorum or attendance requirements, or reporting and escalation structure; and ● Introduction of new or more restrictive requirements, or changes that materially impact business operations, risk controls, or regulatory compliance. Meeting Materials The presentation documents, inclusive of information as outlined in the relevant Forum Purpose Statement or Committee Charter, that is shared with attendees of a Committee or Forum and serve as a record of content discussed. Meeting Minutes The formal written record of a Committee meeting that documents key elements such as the date, time, attendees (and quorum), agenda items discussed, decisions made, as applicable, meeting outcomes, and Action Items. Members Collectively, the participants of a Forum or Committee meeting, including (when and as applicable) the Forum DRI, Regular Attendees, Required Attendees, Committee Chair, Additional Observers, and Delegates. Periodic Review The recurring reassessment of the adequacy and accuracy of the Forum or Committee and actions taken to make the revisions as necessary. Periodic reviews must occur at least annually. Regular Attendees Individuals who are regularly invited to join a Committee or Forum, but are not required to attend per the Forum Purpose Statement or Committee Charter. In the case of Committees where voting may occur, these are non-voting members. Required Attendees Individuals who are required by the Forum Purpose Statement or Committee Charter to attend a meeting. In the case of Committees where voting may occur, these are the voting members. 5 2. Compliance Committee and Forum Oversight Framework Requirements The following requirements outline the

documents.GOV-034: Escalation Framework for Non-Completion of Compliance Training Employees) Block, Inc. Last Approved: Mar 30, 2026 Effective Date: Mar 30, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: Block, Inc (“Blockˮ) requires employees (“Staff Membersˮ) to complete mandatory Compliance training. This Escalation Framework for Non-Completion of Compliance Training (“Frameworkˮ) defines the approach taken by Block for Staff Members who do not complete mandatory compliance training, as required. The purpose of this document is to outline the Escalation Framework for Employees who have not completed mandatory Compliance training despite undergoing the current Compliance training escalation process as outlined in the Block Compliance Mandatory Training Program Standard (the “Standardˮ). For mandatory Compliance training, the Standard outlines the end-to-end Training Campaign Cycle, which gives Learners 30 days to complete the assigned mandatory training on time, followed by a 7 day escalating communication process post-due date aimed at driving completion of compliance training. Select mandatory Compliance training may follow an adjusted timeline as outlined in the Program. This Framework takes effect for Staff Members who have not completed mandatory Compliance training at the conclusion of the Compliance Training Campaign Cycle which can be thought of as day 1 of the Consequences phase. This Framework is owned and maintained by the Compliance Training Team who partners with HR, People Ops, External Workforce, and Employment Counsel on changes to the Framework. Scope: This Framework is established by Block and is applicable across all Block business lines and subsidiaries. Definitions: Terminology Definition 1 Call-to-Action Action required to be completed within a specified timeframe. Disciplinary Disciplinary Action may include verbal or documented coaching, written or Action final warning, unpaid administrative leave, or termination of employment, as a result of a policy violation. Docebo (“Block Blockʼs Learning Management System (“LMSˮ) utilized and managed by the Academyˮ) People Team Learner An employee or external worker (e.g., contingent labor, contractors), with access to Block systems and Block credentials who are assigned a Workday profile is required to take Compliance Training. Note: The scope of this document includes only employee learners. Training The end-to-end management of deploying a mandatory compliance training Campaign Cycle module that generally allows 30 days for completion and post-due date escalation and notification period. Select mandatory compliance training may follow an adjusted timeline as outlined in the Compliance Training Program Standard. Roles and Responsibilities: Terminology Definition Mandatory The Training Team owns and manages the Compliance Training Program Compliance Standard and the Disciplinary Action Framework for Non-Completion of Training Team Compliance Training outlining the requirements and strategy for (“Training implementing those requirements. Teamˮ) Employment Review the disciplinary matrix and partner with the Human Resources team Counsel to ensure consistent enforcement. Human HR supports the business in executing Disciplinary Actions. Resources (“HRˮ) Leads Leads are people managers who have employees or External Workforce assigned to them in Workday. Leads are responsible for executing Disciplinary Action for non-compliance, with support from HR. People Ops Responsible for placing said Staff Member on unpaid administrative leave and/or termination at the request of the Directly Responsible Individual (“DRIˮ). 2 2. Standard Requirements 2.1. Disciplinary Action for Non-Completion of Compliance Training This section outlines recommended guidelines for managing Employees who have been assigned but not completed their required mandatory Compliance training despite notifications at various points throughout the Compliance Training Campaign Cycle. Monitoring and Reporting On an ongoing basis, the Escalation Framework Owner and/or their designee monitors activities for compliance and objective achievement. As outlined in the Compliance Management System Policy, Compliance will provide regular updates, as applicable, to the Block, Inc. Board and executive management. documents.GOV-035: Global Compliance Monitoring and Testing GCMT Program Block, Inc. Last Approved: Mar 26, 2026 Effective Date: Apr 1, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Global Compliance Monitoring and Testing Program (“Programˮ) outlines Blockʼs approach to Regulatory Compliance Testing and Monitoring within the Compliance organization. The GCMT Program Lead is responsible for management of this Program. Block, Inc. (herein referred to as “Blockˮ or the “Companyˮ) is committed to maintaining a robust Global Compliance Monitoring & Testing GCMT function and maintaining compliance with the laws and regulations governing the conduct of its business. As part of this commitment, Block developed this Program to define the GCMT function, its purpose, and general responsibilities. The GCMT function tests and reports on the effectiveness of internal controls so that control owners can take prompt remedial action. GCMT is part of the second line of defense in Blockʼs three lines of defense risk management model. GCMT brings a systematic, disciplined testing approach to Blockʼs oversight activities. Compliance testing serves as an independent and objective assurance activity designed to enhance Blockʼs oversight activities. GCMT is granted authority access to records, reports, systems, and personnel necessary to execute risk-based compliance monitoring and testing. The purpose of the GCMT function is to support Blockʼs risk governance framework. GCMT provides periodic monitoring and testing of compliance activities and control environments to determine whether internal controls over compliance activities are adequately designed and functioning effectively. Other approaches we can take include: ● ‘Go liveʼ Readiness Assessments ● Post-Implementation Reviews ● Consultations to evaluate the alignment between frameworks, policies, and regulations First and second line of defense employees, including Managers, Officers, and Executives, must be familiar with this Program. Scope: This Program applies to Block, Inc., its subsidiaries, and affiliates (collectively, “Blockˮ), including all products. Definitions: Terminology Definition Business Units Any business or administrative group or team that develops or supports Blockʼs products and services. Compliance Risk The risk of legal or regulatory sanctions, material financial loss, or loss to reputation that the company may suffer as a result of its failure to comply with compliance laws, regulations, codes of conduct, regulatory guidance and expectations, as well as group wide policies, rules and standards. Corrective Internal Corrective internal controls (or compensating controls) are designed to Controls correct undesirable outcomes or control failures that have occurred. Examples of corrective internal controls include software patches, disciplinary actions, new policies or procedures, and validation testing. Committee of The Committee of Sponsoring Organizations of the Treadway Commission Sponsoring COSO developed a model for evaluating internal controls. This model is Organizations of widely accepted and is recognized as the standard against which the Treadway organizations measure the effectiveness of their systems of internal control. Commission Detective Internal Controls designed to uncover undesirable events, errors, and irregularities Controls that may have occurred, for example, assessments of existing controls, self-assessments, and quality control reviews. Issues Fails to meet a specific requirement of contract, law, regulation, compliance policy, program document, or internal procedure. Institute of The Institute of Internal Auditors IIA is a recognized international Internal Auditors professional association which develops standards, guidance, and certifications. Internal Control A process effected by an entityʼs board of directors, management, and other personnel, designed to provide reasonable assurance regarding the achievement of objectives relating to operations, reporting, and compliance. 2 Enhancement A minor control weakness that is being addressed through a project or Opportunity initiative causing the residual risk to be considered as negligible i.e it does not meet the threshold level to be reported as an issueControl owners have the option to accept the proposed enhancement opportunity or accept the potential risk. Accepting the enhancement opportunity will require a management action plan MAP or if it is not accepted, a rationale for this decision. Preventive Proactive internal controls (such as policies, procedures, separation of Internal Controls duties and Quality Assurance reviews) designed to prevent a negative event from occurring. Recommendation The proposed measure(s) to be taken to correct an observed gap or out-of-compliance situation. Control owners have the option to accept the proposed recommendation or provide their own management action plan. Testing An end-to-end review of a business unitʼs processes, which includes the underlying policies, frameworks, and controls. The type and approach of testing will be driven by the agreed scope objective and may vary in degrees. 2. Block, Inc. Requirements 2.1. Testing Methodology The Testing methodology was built around the Committee of Sponsoring Organizations COSO of the Treadway Commission framework and the Institute of Internal Auditors IIA guidance. Compliance tests are conducted in four phases: 2.1.1. Administration ● Develop an annual test plan ● Obtain feedback, input and approval from stakeholders ● Distribute annual test plan to stakeholders and senior leadership ● Communicate the plan to appropriate local risk and Block level committees 2.1.2. Planning ● Review applicable regulations, policies, procedures, open issues, internal audits and independent reviews, as available ● Complete scoping document and outline testing focus areas ● Inform key stakeholders via engagement email and obtain completed information Request form ● Schedule kick off/walkthrough meeting ● Request view only system access 2.1.3. Testing ● Develop work papers, document test scripts, select testing techniques, and sampling methodology. ● Test samples and retain supporting documentation 3 ● Schedule status meetings ● Schedule a vetting meeting, present the Results Matrix, and ask control owners to provide a Management Action Plan MAP for each recommendation (if they accept GCMTʼs recommendations) or their rationale for not accepting GCMTʼs recommendations. 2.1.4. Planning ● Review applicable regulations, policies, procedures, open issues, internal audits and independent reviews, as available 2.2. Monitoring Methodology 2.2.1. Administration ● Develop the annual monitoring plan and calendar in alignment with GCMTʼs Annual Plan. For Afterpay Data Monitoring: ● Identify and prioritize any new key monitoring areas and metrics in coordination with Compliance, Legal, and operational partners—both during annual planning and through insights gained from ongoing testing reviews. ● Obtain stakeholder input, feedback, and approval on existing and or newly proposed metrics and monitoring coverage. ● Confirm resource assignments, roles, and responsibilities to support ongoing monitoring execution, analysis, and reporting. For a monitoring review: ● Determine the specific scope, objectives, and schedule during the annual planning cycle. ● Develop the detailed scope and review criteria in conjunction with stakeholders. ● Conduct targeted reviews and issue reports within agreed timeframes 2.2.2. Requesting Data ● If the tester has access to the data, write/update code to extract the latest data or request data to be supplied by the appropriate business data team or business unit stakeholder. If the tester does not have access to the data, submit a JIRA ticket data request, set up time with the appropriate data analytics teams to share your data requirements and or work with the data team to define your requirements. 2.2.3. Reporting ● Issue monitoring reports, dashboards, and/or data to appropriate stakeholders in accordance with the agreed rhythms. ● Issues are entered into Blockʼs Risk, Controls, and Issue Management system with action owners and due dates. 2.3. Additional Information 4 2.3.1. Conduct The GCMT function is expected to: ● Act with integrity ● Perform its duties in compliance with Blockʼs policies ● Ensure objectivity and independence ● Develop an annual, risk-based test plan ● Provide proper supervision to testers performing compliance reviews ● Encourage management to adopt appropriate policies, procedures, and controls 2.3.2. Risk Assessments Risk assessments are performed by a third party or Block personnel not directly involved in testing activities (e.g., 1LOD, Risk and Compliance personnel). Where a risk assessment has not been completed, GCMT will undertake a process to identify and document the key risks and controls from stakeholders and business documentation. 2.3.3. Risk-Based Annual Monitoring & Testing Plan The Global Compliance Monitoring and Testing Program Lead prepares an annual risk-based monitoring and testing plan considering available information, such as: ● Regulatory inventory for key requirements/guidance applicable to a product, ● Compliance and Anti Money Laundering and Sanctions risk assessments ● Risk control inventory mapped to products and regulations, ● Regulatory data monitoring metrics of key controls ● Current and emerging regulatory landscape ● Historical results of compliance control effectiveness ● internal audit coverage areas, ● Independent reviews, ● New regional or product expansion. The Compliance Monitoring and Testing Lead also considers requests submitted by the Block Chief Compliance Officer, Global and regional Compliance Leads, management, Global and regional Legal and Privacy Leads and bank partners. The Head of Governance, Compliance approves the consolidated annual monitoring and testing plan. Bank partners may approve sub-sections of the annual monit
documents.GOV-036: Global Geographic Risk Ratings Methodology & Program Block Published January 2024 Table of Contents Table of Contents 2 Introduction 3 Definitions 3 Roles & Responsibilities 3 Methodology 4 Governance 6 Document Management 6 Document Owner 6 Scheduled Review & Ad-Hoc Maintenance 7 Exceptions 7 Related Documents 7 Revision & Approval History 7 Document Details 7 Global Geographic Risk Ratings Methodology & Program | Block 2 Introduction The Global Geographic Risk Ratings Methodology & Program (the “Program”) details the methodology and data sources used to calculate Geographic Risk Ratings (GRR) for money laundering, terrorist financing, economic sanctions, bribery, and corruption. The methodology includes a quantitative risk analysis based on data points and research, as well as a qualitative risk analysis conducted by Compliance Subject Matter Experts (Compliance SMEs). This Program is established by Block, Inc. (“Block”) and is applicable globally across all Block business lines, subsidiaries, and jurisdictions. References to “staff” throughout this document shall refer to all full-time employees and fixed-term and contingent workers including interns, temps, consultants, vendors, third-party providers, and contractors. Definitions FATF The Financial Action Task Force (FATF) leads global action to tackle money laundering, terrorist and proliferation financing. FINCEN The Financial Crimes Enforcement Network (FinCEN) is a bureau of the U.S. Department of the Treasury which safeguards the financial system from illicit use and money laundering. FINTRAC The Financial Transactions and Reports Analysis Centre of Canada (FINTRAC) is Canada's financial intelligence unit and anti-money laundering and anti-terrorist financing supervisor. Roles & Responsibilities Global Policy & Governance The Global Policy & Governance team’s responsibility is to ensure geographic risk methodology accurately reflects company risk with respect to the compliance subject matter, approve the final risk scores, and maintain geographic risk scoring and integrate into company risk assessments where applicable, including the annual AML & Sanctions Risk Assessments, new product risk assessments, or any other assessment of Global Geographic Risk Ratings Methodology & Program | Block 3 inherent risk that implicates country risk from a compliance perspective. Compliance Engineering & Technology The Compliance Engineering & Technology team’s responsibility is to implement and maintain final scoring in enhanced customer risk models and transaction monitoring systems, as applicable. Business Unit’s Compliance Teams The Business Unit’s Compliance teams responsibilities are to operationalize the final (Including International Compliance Leads, risk scoring into underlying procedures and Product Compliance and Operational decision processes (e.g. due diligence, product risk making) assessments, new business ventures) Strategic Operations The Strategic Operations team’s responsibility is to integrate risk scoring into Merger and (i.e. Deal Desk) Activity due diligence and discussions Procurement The Procurement team’s responsibility is to integrate risk scoring into vendor due diligence, including ongoing due diligence Methodology For the quantitative risk analysis, Block leverages Knowyourcountry.com’s Country Ratings, which is a composite financial crimes index with each country receiving a score between 1 (highest risk) and 100 (lowest risk). It assesses corruption, money laundering, tax haven, terrorist financing, and sanctions risks, among other data points. Knowyourcountry.com’s scores are converted to Geographic Risk Ratings of Low, Medium, and High, as illustrated in this chart. Score Country Risk Tier Country Risk Rating 70.01 - 100 1 Low 50.01 - 70 2 Medium 0 - 50 3 High Global Geographic Risk Ratings Methodology & Program | Block 4 In order to tailor these scores/ratings to the specific financial crimes risks facing Block and its subsidiaries, as well as to align with regulatory guidance on high risk countries for AML purposes, Block then considers the following risk factors into the final scores/ratings. Any proposed changes must be documented and approved by the Global Policy & Governance (GPG) lead or the Head of Sanctions. The new risk scores will be implemented after approval by the SMEs and the GPG lead or the Head of Sanctions. documents.GOV-037: Regulatory Change Management Standard Block, Inc. Last Approved: Jan 29, 2026 Effective Date: Jan 29, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: Block, Inc. (“Blockˮ) conducts business in compliance with all applicable laws, rules, and regulations (“LRRsˮ). This Regulatory Change Management (“RCMˮ) Standard (“Standardˮ) is designed with the objective of ensuring consistent and effective compliance with currently applicable LRRs. To meet this objective, this Standard establishes the framework for systematically managing and adapting to Regulatory Changes that impact Blockʼs customer facing activities, services and operations (“Customer Impacting Activitiesˮ) within the jurisdictions that it operates. RCM is an end-to-end process that supports Block identifying, managing, and maintaining effective and consistent compliance with the LRRs that are applicable to its Customer Impacting Activities. This Standard is designed to establish a framework for: ● Defining the overarching guiding principles for managing Regulatory Changes ● Establishing roles and responsibilities to drive accountability for the overall Regulatory Change management process ● Tracking and monitoring enacted LRRs within the scope of RCM ● Communicating changes to LRRs to the impacted stakeholders ● Ensuring that stakeholders implement risk mitigation measures and document compliance with applicable LRRs Scope: This standard is established by Block to set out a consistent and effective approach to managing compliance with enacted LRRs applicable to its U.S. based operations. Regulatory changes include changes to existing LRRs, along with newly enacted LRRs. The following are outside the scope of RCM (i) laws applicable to proposed or contemplated products until the product is made available to customers, (ii) LRRs applicable to Blockʼs non-Customer Impacting Activities, and (iii) LRRs applicable solely to Cash App Taxes. 1 The scope of LRRs covered by this Standard will constantly change. The Regulatory Change Management team will incorporate additional LRRs to the Obligations library as it becomes aware of new or changed LRRs and new or changed Customer Impacting Activities. This Standard governs the management of go-forward regulatory changes, and does not apply to regulatory changes enacted prior to the RCM program launch date Sept 30, 2025. Definitions: Terminology Definition Applicability Refers to determining which LRRs (also referred to collectively as Obligations) govern Blockʼs Customer Impacting Activities. Ascent An AI driven Regulatory Change Management tool that allows Block to track new and changed LRRs. Ascent breaks the LRRs into Obligations, and facilitates the systematic review of new or changed Obligations to determine Applicability. Customer Customer Impacting Activities includes all of Blockʼs products, services, and Impacting directly supporting operations. It does not include Blockʼs corporate Activities activities, such as corporate governance, tax compliance, and Blockʼs employee related activities. Directly DRI is a designated/directly responsible individual, accountable for decision Responsible making. Go/DRI Individual (“DRIˮ) LRR A law, rule, or regulation duly enacted by a governing body in a jurisdiction. Obligation Any individual requirement or expectation that Block must comply with to fulfil legal and compliance responsibilities. Protecht Protecht is a Governance Risk and Compliance (“GRCˮ) tool managed by Enterprise Risk Management (“ERMˮ) that Block utilizes to enable Compliance Risk Management programs, including RCM. Protecht serves as a system of record for RCM. Regulatory Change Any addition, removal, or modification of LRRs. Regulatory Regulatory Change Management is a process designed to monitor, track, Change review, and ensure measures are taken to comply with Regulatory Changes Management that are applicable to Customer Impacting Activities. Regulatory Regulatory Change Owners are Staff Members responsible for managing Change Owner the assessment and implementation of regulatory changes. Regulatory Change owners serve as the conduit to the business. Regulatory Change Owners typically consist of Product Compliance Managers, Compliance 2 Terminology Definition Program Leads or Managers, or other individuals responsible for ensuring a Regulatory Change has been assessed and relevant risk treatments have been fully executed and evidenced appropriately. The Regulatory Change Owner will be responsible for representing that the Regulatory Change is ready for closure by ensuring that all necessary actions have been taken to ensure compliance with the Regulatory Change for the brand or product they represent. Regulatory Library A centralized collection of LRRs applicable to Blockʼs Customer Impacting Activities. RCM Program The individuals designated by Counsel and Compliance, respectively, to Manager manage the RCM program. Staff Member(s) An employee or external worker (e.g., contingent labor, contractors) hired by Block who supports the operations covered by this RCM Program. 2. Standard Requirements This Standard establishes a cohesive framework that guides Blockʼs RCM process, promoting uniformity across all activities associated with Regulatory Changes. It is structured into key elements, each representing a crucial component of the end-to-end programmatic approach to effectively managing Regulatory Changes. The RCM framework utilizes two tools: 1 Ascent, which identifies Regulatory Changes and reduces them into Obligations, and 2 Protecht, which is Blockʼs GRC tool and RCM system of record. The efficacy of the RCM process is monitored by the RCM Program Managers. 2.1. Obligation Identification Through Ascent, Regulatory Counsel monitors LRRs to identify Regulatory Changes that impact Customer Impacting Activities. Regulatory Counsel monitors more broadly than existing LRRs to ensure it identifies new and changed Applicable LRRs not currently included in the Regulatory Library. 2.2. Block Applicability Assessment The relevant Counsel subject matter expert (“SMEˮ) is responsible for determining which Obligations shall enter the Regulatory Change Management process because Counsel has determined that they are applicable to Blockʼs Customer Impacting Activities. Once determined Applicable to Customer Impacting Activities, the SME makes a preliminary determination as to which Block brand(s) the Regulatory Change may apply. 3 Obligations deemed applicable to Block are then assessed for a product-level Applicability determination. In some cases, a determination may be made that a Regulatory Change is “immaterial,ˮ i.e., it has no or de minimis impact on Customer Impacting Activities. Examples include, but are not limited to, minor grammatical or non-substantive changes to an obligation. Where it has been determined that the change is immaterial, an RCM Program Manager or other stakeholder may determine that it is appropriate to bypass completion of the Risk Assessment and close the Regulatory Change with sufficient justification. 2.3. RCM Risk Assessment The RCM Risk Assessment measures the impact of Regulatory Changes and assesses the current control environment, which enables assessment of the calculated residual risk. The RCM Risk Assessment utilizes the Enterprise Risk Management ERM Methodology and scales with the intent of assessing Blockʼs readiness for complying with Regulatory Changes. An RCM Risk Assessment must be completed by the assigned Regulatory Change Owner for each applicable Block brand, unless the Regulatory Change is deemed immaterial as described above. The Regulatory Change Owner(s) will typically be a Product Compliance Manager (“PCMˮ), International Compliance Lead, Financial Crime Program Managers, or Consumer Protection Compliance Program Managers responsible for evaluating the regulatory risks associated with the Regulatory Change and assessing the adequacy of existing controls to mitigate these risks. In consultation with Compliance leadership and key stakeholders, an RCM Program Manager will assign a Regulatory Change Owner for each impacted brand. An RCM Risk Assessment must be performed for, or aligned to, each Regulatory Change. In the event that one RCM Risk Assessment can be performed for multiple associated new/changed obligations, that Risk Assessment must be manually aligned to each applicable obligation in Protecht. The RCM Risk Assessment follows the below steps: 1. Impact Assessment (absent controls) 2. Probability Assessment (absent controls), which comprises: a. Likelihood Assessment (absent controls) b. Frequency/Exposure Analysis 3. Control Assessment (degree of mitigation) 4. Residual Risk outcomes The RCM Risk Assessment process and corresponding results are intended to inform decisions made in the Risk Treatment phase of the Regulatory Change Management process. The residual risk outcomes should be indicative of the priority and/or urgency of the Regulatory Change, and should also correlate to the type and scope of action that must be taken to address the Regulatory Change. 4 2.3.1. Inherent Risk Assessment Inherent risk is the pure risk exposure that exists prior to consideration of controls or other mitigation and management activities. The risk of Regulatory Changes are assessed to establish the severity of consequences (impact) and their probability of occurrence (likelihood + frequency of exposure). Impact Assessment: The impact of a potential risk event to Block without controls in place to mitigate the risk. Refers to the potential consequences or severity of a risk occurring. See the Impact Rating guidance in Protecht). Likelihood: Quantifies the chance or frequency with which a specific risk is expected to occur based on historical data, expert judgment, or analysis.
