documents.GOV-038: Service Level Agreement and Backlog Management Standard Block Inc. Last Approved: Mar 30, 2026 Effective Date: Mar 30, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: The Block, Inc. (“Blockˮ) Service Level Agreement (“SLAˮ) and Backlog Management Standard (“Standardˮ) outlines the strategy for adherence related to SLAs. The key components of this strategy include planning and resourcing for sufficient staffing, and developing remediation plans in the event there is variance to the planning and alerts age outside the established SLA. This Policy is established by Block, Inc. (“Blockˮ) and is applicable globally across all Block business lines, entities, and jurisdictions. References to “staff members'' throughout this document shall refer to all full-time employees, fixed-term, and contingent workers (such as BPO who support the operations and/or subsidiaries covered by this Policy, and include interns, temporary workers, consultants, vendors, third-party providers, and contractors. Scope: This Standard applies to Block, Inc., its entities, and affiliates (collectively, “Blockˮ), including all products. Definitions: Terminology Definition Service Level A defined timeframe within which a unit of work must be completed. This Agreement SLA refers to the allowed number of hours, calendar days, or business days to complete a review of an alert, suspicious activity report SAR investigation, or other unit of work (such as an Office of Foreign Assets Control OFAC report or partner escalation). In some cases, such as remediations, the SLA may be expressed as a specific calendar date rather than a number of days. 1 Out of SLA An alert or other unit of work (i.e. a SAR case) that has remained open past (“OOSLAˮ) the standard SLA but has not yet reached Backlog status. The Out of SLA (“OOSLAˮ) period spans from 1 day past the SLA deadline up to 2x the standard SLA. For example, if the standard SLA is 60 days, an alert is considered Out of SLA from day 61 through day 120. At day 121, the alert moves to the Backlog. Backlog An alert, SAR investigation, or other unit of work that has remained open greater than 2x the standard SLA. For example, if the standard SLA is 60 days, an alert moves to the Backlog on day 121. Internal SLA An internal SLA is determined by the Operations Lead as appropriate to the risk and complexity and is typically reflected as the due date in case management systems and metrics reporting. The SLA can range from 24 hours/1 day, 7 days, 30 days or 60 days. SLA may be measured in calendar days or business days, depending on the workflow. For certain workflows, including SSP-enabled alerts, internal SLA may operate in multiple stages, including: ● Initial alert review SLA, ● Defined customer response waiting period, and ● Follow-up adjudication SLA once the case is re-queued for analyst action. Regulatory SLA A Regulatory SLA is an external deadline set by regulators, such as filing a SAR within 30 days of determining suspicion or filing an OFAC report within 10 days. Regulatory SLAs are binary. Alert An alert is generated notification in queues within case management systems triggered by predefined rules or risk indicators that require review by Compliance Operations to determine whether further action or escalation is necessary. Queues A queue is a designated workflow within a case management system where alerts or cases are routed for review by a specific team based on predefined risk criteria or business rules. Alert queues generate alerts based on heuristics or machine learning models, or a combination of both. Escalate to SAR An Escalate to SAR action is taken when there is reason to investigate unusual activity further by a dedicated investigations team. When an Escalate to SAR action is taken and a case is generated for investigation, the internal SLA starts. SARs may also be referenced as Suspicious Transaction Report STR and Suspicious Matter Report SMR and other regional variations. SAR An investigation by a dedicated team that will review activity and determine Investigation whether there is suspicious activity, triggering the need to make a regulatory report. The regulatory filing SLA begins upon the start of a SAR investigation. 2 SAR Filing The filing of suspicious activity report with the required regulatory body. The process is defined in separate program and procedure documents by region. OFAC Report The filing of a report on a sanctioned customer or transaction. The process is defined in the Global Sanctions Compliance Program document. Square Secure A Square Secure Profile SSP is a request for information that is sent to a Profile SSP merchant to obtain additional information or documentation needed to complete review and adjudication during an investigation. For workflows that use SSPs, the original alert review is considered complete when the SSP is issued, provided issuance occurs within the applicable queue SLA. Subsequent case handling, including seller response review or no-response adjudication, is governed by separate workflow timing and queue rules. 2. Block, Inc. Requirements 2.1. Capacity Management 2.1.1. Capacity Model Block leverages a capacity model for the planning and resourcing of its staffing in order to ensure there are sufficient resources, both internally and externally, to maintain adherence to SLAs. Greater detail about the capacity model and process used is outlined in the Capacity Model Manual Updates Runbook , which is owned by the Compliance Analytics CAX team. The capacity model is updated on a quarterly basis in partnership with Operations Leads. 2.1.2. Variance Monitoring Once forecasts for volumes are provided for the capacity model, CAX conducts the initial forecasting. Following this, the Compliance Operations Program Management (“COPMˮ) team socializes these forecasts with Operations Leads and Workforce Management (“WFMˮ) Lead. CAX reviews actual v. forecasted alert volumes on a monthly basis. After WFM reviews the forecasts, they confirm whether or not the forecasts are in line with their expectations for the program for budget and staffing. This review includes: ● Any known issues, enhancements, or changes to the program related to existing staffing capacity, alerts, or technical issues ● Variations from past volumes and their root causes ● Other factors that may affect volumes 3 Once the forecasts are finalized, COPM provides visibility to the Head of Compliance Operations or their delegates. For special projects, WFM partners with Operations Leads. Daily Monitoring and Remediation The Operations Leads are responsible for monitoring daily alert volumes and adherence to SLAs with self-serve dashboards. They report progress of resolving any alerts that are out of SLA or in backlog, and they track, report, and document progress against remediation plans to the Head of Compliance Operations. Operations Leads are responsible for ensuring appropriate progress, tracking resource allocation, and overseeing progress against remediation plans. 2.1.3. SLA Adherence Operations Leads review team adherence to SLAs and work allocation at least weekly through Case Management Systems and SLA reporting. In general, inventory is worked in accordance with SLA aging, with cases prioritized based on proximity to their deadline. However, in certain circumstances—such as specialized projects, credible threats of harm, life-threatening situations, or indicators of a missing child—inventory may be reprioritized based on elevated risk considerations to ensure timely handling of higher-risk matters. Where leveraged, the outsourcing vendor will also provide weekly reporting to Operations Leads on adherence to SLAs, progress on any alerts out of SLA or in backlog and confirm sufficient external staffing is allocated to the work to complete it within the expected timeframe. Managing SLA adherence is a collaborative effort between WFM and Operations Leads, with no single owner; however, ultimate responsibility for adherence to SLAs remains with Block. 2.1.4. Variations If variations present a risk of volume beyond the capacity of the existing staffing (internal or external) and the potential for alerts to fall out of SLA and/or into backlog, an out-of-cycle capacity request is triggered. In these instances, the Compliance Operations Program Manager COPM, in collaboration with Operations Leads, drafts documentation outlining the increased volume and corresponding capacity needs. This request is then escalated to the Workforce Management WFM team to secure additional staffing support (internal, external, or both), while also providing visibility to the Head of Compliance Operations. Out-of-cycle capacity requests are raised ad-hoc via the Compliance Capacity Upcoming Initiatives Tracker and/or high priority tickets filed via go/COPMintake for WFM. a. This recurring governance forum enables Compliance leaders to assess operational metrics, trends, SLA and backlog risks, and overall program health, and to determine necessary changes or remediation actions across Compliance Operations. 4 2.1.5. Out of SLA and Backlogs Remediation Plans for Out-of-SLA or Backlog Alerts When alerts are out of SLA or in backlog, Operations Leads work with WFM to develop and enact remediation plans. The Operations Leads, a Compliance Operations Program Manager, and/or their delegates partner on plan development. Remediation plans include: ● Timeline to resolve out-of-SLA or backlog alerts ● Strategy to manage incoming alerts and prevent backlog growth ● Staffing timeline and resource allocation ● Priority ranking of out-of-SLA areas based on program risk ● Downstream impacts (e.g., SAR escalations, investigation timelines, regulatory SLAs) Operations Leads Responsibilities: ● Share mitigation steps to maintain SLA adherence ● Report staffing limitations or concerns ● Assist in preparing out-of-cycle capacity reviews (as noted above) ● Ensure additional staff (internal or external) are pr
documents.GOV-039: Compliance Quality Program Standard Block, Inc. Last Approved: Mar 27, 2026 Effective Date: Mar 27, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose This Compliance Quality Standard establishes a consistent framework for assessing and improving the quality and effectiveness of compliance processes across Block, Inc. and its subsidiaries (collectively, “Blockˮ). Block has designed its Quality Program to consist of two parts: Quality Control (“QC”) and Quality Assurance (“QA”). The Program ensures that Compliance Operations, with responsibility for implementing processes and procedures to meet Blockʼs BSA/AML and Sanctions policy and program as it relates to areas such as Transaction Monitoring (“TM"), Suspicious Activity Reporting (“SARˮ), Enhanced Due Diligence (“EDDˮ), and Know Your Customer (“KYCˮ) reviews, meet regulatory obligations and internal quality benchmarks. The Quality Program may be leveraged for other areas as designated, such as Appeals, Unacceptable Use and Terms of Service Violation, within the scope of the Compliance Operations program. Block is committed to maintaining a robust Quality Program and compliance with the laws and regulations governing the conduct of its business. As part of this commitment, Block developed this Standard to govern the Quality Program and define its objectives, principles, methodology, and governance, ensuring independent, accurate, and transparent evaluation of compliance reviews to strengthen Blockʼs control environment and promote continuous improvement. This Standard will be supported by procedures and instruction; separated into specific control and assurance components, or by function performing quality, where relevant. A primary function of the Quality Program is evaluation of the effectiveness of the Compliance operational functions in meeting the objectives and obligations under the BSA/AML and Sanctions programs. The Quality Program primarily focuses on output and decisions related to alerts and investigations, and provides a feedback loop for continuous improvement after a decision has been made. The Quality Control QC function primarily focuses on real-time evaluation of decision correctness either prior to a decision being made or shortly thereafter. The Quality Assurance QA function) evaluates decisions made with a systematic review of existing workflows, processes, procedures, training and where relevant, systems, to detect any weaknesses or inefficiencies that 1 could compromise quality, introduce errors that expose the compliance operations program to gaps, or hinder effectiveness of the operations program meeting its obligations. Once identified, these deficiencies are then thoroughly analyzed to understand their root causes, paving the way for targeted remediation efforts. The goal of the Quality Program is to provide recommendations and reporting that drive correction of issues as well as implementation of lasting solutions that prevent recurrence and enhance overall operational procedures and program in fulfilling its objectives under the BSA/AML and Sanctions programs. Scope This Standard applies globally to Block, Inc. entities and products, specifically those defined within Covered Products, as applicable to Square, Square Financial Services (“SFSˮ), Cash App, Cash App Invest (“CAIˮ) Afterpay and Clearpay, and covers all compliance-related reviews, investigations, and quality control and assurance processes. In addition to application to Blockʼs BSA/AML and Sanctions programs, the Quality Program may be leveraged for other areas as designated, such as Appeals, Unacceptable Use and Terms of Service Violation, within the scope of the Compliance Operations program. In-scope Coverage Unless otherwise explicitly stated, directed, or approved through governance channels, the following areas are in scope for this Standard and the Quality Program: ● Compliance Operations: All Compliance Operations functions as performed within queues in Regulator or Notary are in scope for review, unless explicitly excluded. Any exceptions or exclusions must be formally documented or tracked through CAX queue metadata (e.g., queues designated as Not Reported). ● Specialized Compliance Areas: The Quality Program may be applied to select compliance functions performed by specialized teams where quality oversight is required for operational functions performed within those teams, including but not limited to: ○ High Risk Customer Escalations (“HRCEˮ) ○ Special Investigations Unit (“SIUˮ) ○ Global Sanctions Team (“GSˮ) ● Appeals: Appeals processes associated with compliance decisions are in scope for Quality review to ensure consistency, fairness, and adherence to documented standards. This Standard focuses on the quality of Compliance Operations output and decisions (alerts, auto-decisions, investigations, filings) rather than the underlying systems, tools, or processes that generate those outputs. Validation of screening systems, list maintenance, screening settings, population selection, off-grid or ad hoc workflows that are not formally tracked within approved 2 case management or operational systems are outside the scope of this Standard, unless they are expressly brought into scope through documented governance approval. Definitions Terminology Definition AML/CFT Laws Global AML/CFT Laws are the anti-money laundering (“AMLˮ) and counter-terrorist financing (“CFTˮ) laws, regulations, and supervisory expectations applicable in each jurisdiction where Block operates. These requirements generally establish obligations for customer due diligence (including identity verification), ongoing monitoring, recordkeeping, sanctions compliance considerations, and reporting of suspicious activity to relevant authorities. In the United States, these obligations are established under the Bank Secrecy Act (“BSAˮ); in other jurisdictions, they are established under equivalent local legal and regulatory frameworks. Average Score The mean quality score across all reviews conducted within a given timeframe, may be used to assess overall Quality performance and directional trends. BSA Bank Secrecy Act) The Bank Secrecy Act BSA is the primary anti-money laundering AML law (often called "BSA/AML") in the US. It authorizes the Department of the Treasury to impose reporting and record-keeping requirements on financial institutions and businesses to detect and prevent money laundering, tax evasion, and other criminal activities. The Bank Secrecy Act of 1970, also known as the Currency and Foreign Transactions Reporting Act, is a U.S. law requiring financial institutions in the United States to assist U.S. government agencies in detecting and preventing money laundering. Calibration The structured process used to align Quality reviewers and managers on scoring standards, interpretations, and expectations to ensure consistent application of Quality criteria across teams and entities. Calibration sessions may involve group reviews of sample cases to compare assessments, resolve discrepancies, and reinforce a shared understanding of quality standards and decision rationale. Continuous Improvement The practice of using Quality insights to drive process enhancements, training, and system improvements that strengthen overall compliance performance. Coverage Quality maintains comprehensive oversight across all active compliance operational processes, ensuring review 3 coverage across BSA/AML and Sanctions operations, balanced with risk-based flexibility. Covered Products Block defines its Covered Products as the products and services that Block offers pursuant to a license or registration and that are subject to BSA/AML related laws and regulations. Certain other products may also be considered Covered Products, such as where required under partner bank expectation and/or contractual obligation. Each Business Line is responsible for maintaining a documented and up-to-date list of products and services that meet this definition. Deep Dive Review An in-depth Quality analysis that may report on operational patterns, trends, and/or cross-program comparisons to identify systemic issues, recurring patterns, or opportunities for program alignment. Entity-Level Program A Quality Program and process tailored to a specific brand, product, or regional entity within Block that aligns with enterprise Quality principles. Exception / Exemption A formally approved deviation from a policy, standard, or procedure. Exceptions must be justified, documented, and include compensating controls to mitigate associated risks. This term encompasses both exemptions and exception requests. Issue Management The process of tracking, resolving, and verifying remediation of findings identified through Quality, audit, or other assurance functions. KYC Know Your Customer) The regulatory process of identifying and verifying customersʼ identities to prevent financial crime. Materiality The measure of how significant an error or issue is, typically based on its potential regulatory or operational impact. Compliance Operations Forum A governance forum that reviews Quality results, risks, and remediation progress. Operational Deficiency A gap or error in a system, control, or process, which has the potential to compromise compliance quality or adherence to regulations. Operational Queue A workflow or case category managed by compliance operations teams, typically organized by case type, product, or review process. Passing Rate The proportion of reviewed cases that meet or exceed the minimum acceptable quality threshold, expressed as a percentage of total reviews completed within a defined period. 4 Programmatic Risk A broader risk that could impact the success of the compliance program, such as technology limitations or resource constraints. Quality Assurance QA An independent function that reviews compliance activities to ensure accuracy, consistency, and adherence to internal and regulatory standards. Quality Report A formal doc

Empirical Risk A risk score determined using machine learning and data, reflecting the likelihood that a customer or account poses a financial crime risk. Intrinsic Risk Risk of a customer as determined by the presence of a specific attribute, or intrinsic risk factor. Intrinsically, high-risk customers are considered high risk regardless of empirical risk. Activation Customer A machine learning model and score associated with a one-time snapshot of Risk Rating (“aCRR”) initial customer risk taken at onboarding. Continuous A machine learning model and score associated with a periodically updated Customer Risk (i.e. ongoing) assessment of customer risk. Rating (“cCRR”) Know Your Customer KYC includes the process of establishing risk-based controls to reasonably (“KYC”) identify and verify the identity of Block’s customers. Continuous Customer Risk Rating Standard | Cash App | U.S. 4 Confidential and Not for Distribution Roles and Responsibilities Role Responsibility Regulatory The Regulatory Compliance Machine Learning team is responsible for Compliance Machine maintaining both the aCRR and cCRR models, ensuring accuracy and Learning Team compliance with regulatory requirements. This includes managing the aCRR model for onboarding risk assessments and the cCRR model for ongoing risk evaluation. Customer The COM Team is responsible for conducting customer risk reviews based on Onboarding and the risk ratings generated by the Customer Risk Rating models. They ensure Management that the standard is in compliance with best practices and applicable (“COM”) Team regulations (e.g., AML/BSA requirements). Additionally, the COM Team makes suggestions for improvements to the standard, including overrides where necessary, and assists with the maintenance of the CRR system. This team plays a critical role in operationalizing the CRR models, ensuring that high-risk customers receive appropriate Enhanced Due Diligence (“EDD”) and supporting ongoing monitoring efforts. Continuous Customer Risk Rating Standard | Cash App | U.S. 5 Confidential and Not for Distribution 1. Introduction The cornerstone of a strong anti-money laundering (“AML”) compliance program is the adoption and implementation of risk-based due diligence policies, procedures, and processes for all customers, particularly those that present a higher risk for money laundering and terrorist financing. In order to achieve this, Cash App has designed and implemented a customer risk rating program that assesses a customer's profile, assigns a risk rating, and monitors the risk levels of customers. This Customer Risk Rating Standard (“Standard”) outlines Cash App’s approach to monitoring and managing customer risk throughout the customer lifecycle. The Head of Customer Onboarding and Management (“COM”) is responsible for management of this Standard. This Continuous Customer Risk Rating (“cCRR”) Standard enables Cash App to apply appropriate ongoing risk-based controls to mitigate potential exposure to money laundering, terrorist financing, and financial crimes. This document covers the development and design of a machine learning (“ML”)-based cCRR Standard. While the activation Customer Risk Rating (“aCRR”) model handles the one-time risk rating of accounts post-activation (or onboarding), the cCRR model is responsible for the ongoing risk rating of accounts during the lifetime of the account on the platform. Improper assessment of a customer’s risk can have a cascading effect, creating deficiencies in multiple areas of internal controls and resulting in an overall weakened AML compliance program. 2. Purpose Cash App conducts business in compliance with all applicable laws and regulations including but not limited to the Bank Secrecy Act (“BSA”), AML laws, and countering the financing of terrorism (“CFT”) regulations. The purpose of this Standard is to further Cash App’s ability to provide a structured and ongoing approach for assessing customer risk. This Standard supports the company’s efforts to fulfil regulatory obligations by employing a risk-based approach to monitor and manage customer behavior throughout the customer lifecycle. The aCRR model assesses the risk level of an account at the time of onboarding, while the cCRR model reassesses account risk throughout the account's lifecycle. Together, these models enable Cash App to maintain ongoing vigilance in identifying and mitigating financial crime risks. The cCRR model is designed Continuous Customer Risk Rating Standard | Cash App | U.S. 6 Confidential and Not for Distribution to continuously evaluate customer activity, providing updated assessments of the potential risk of financial crimes. In accordance with industry best practices and regulatory guidance, this Standard outlines the requirements for the development, maintenance, and execution of the cCRR Standard. It ensures that customers posing a high risk are subject to Enhanced Due Diligence (“EDD”) and that the standard remains compliant with the evolving regulatory landscape. By adhering to this Standard, Cash App aims to safeguard its platform from misuse, protect its customers, and maintain compliance with the relevant laws governing financial services. 3. Scope This Standard is established by Cash App and is applicable to all Cash App entities in the United States. 4. Standard Requirements 4.1 Standard Design Accounts are periodically risk rated on ongoing account activity or change in their profile information. Cash App allows customers to operate more than one account on the platform. Customer risk is defined as the highest level risk across all accounts operated by the customer. These customer risk ratings may be used in part to determine which customers may require enhanced due diligence by Compliance teams. 4.1.1 Empirical Risk Cash App uses machine learning and a data-driven approach to assess the ongoing risk of each account. These assessments include a variety of sources of information about an account, and the model is trained using historical data. Model-based assessment of account risk is known as empirical risk. 4.1.2 Activation Customer Risk Rating (aCRR) The aCRR model assigns a risk score to new accounts immediately after onboarding (alias-link event). The aCRR model produces a score between 0.0 and 1.0, with 1.0 indicating maximum risk and 0.0 indicating minimal risk. The aCRR model is designed to facilitate the early detection of high-risk accounts that may require EDD within the first seven days of activation. Please refer to Block’s Activation Customer Risk Rating Standard for details. Continuous Customer Risk Rating Standard | Cash App | U.S. 7 Confidential and Not for Distribution 4.1.3 Continuous Customer Risk Rating (cCRR) The cCRR model produces a score between 0.0 and 1.0, with 1.0 indicating maximum risk and 0.0 indicating minimal risk. The cCRR model leverages a wide range of information about an account and its behavior. This score is considered a current assessment of the risk of suspicious/illicit activity posed by an account given the information available as of the evaluation date. Cash App refers to accounts that display high cCRR model scores as empirically high risk, meaning that their overall data (i.e. their actions and collection of features) led the model to consider them sufficiently risky to receive additional scrutiny in the form of an EDD review. The cCRR score thresholds that define an account's risk rating are selected using a framework that balances precision and recall, outlined in Cash App’s cCRR System Design document. 4.1.4 Model Features The features considered for use in the cCRR model were selected based on regulatory requirements, guidance, and industry best practices. Details of the specific features used in the aCRR and cCRR models can be found in Cash App’s cCRR System Design and related documents. 4.2 Intrinsic Risk Cash App believes that if a customer displays certain attributes (i.e. overrides), they ought to be considered high risk regardless of their empirical model scores. Cash App refers to this as intrinsic customer risk; the specific attributes that lead to this assessment are referred to as intrinsic risk attributes. An account is considered to pose an intrinsic risk if it displays one or more intrinsic risk factors. These accounts are subjected to regular monitoring a
documents.GOV-041: RETIRED Transaction Monitoring Modeling Program Cash App Published May 2023 Table of Contents Table of Contents 2 Introduction 3 Definitions 3 Roles & Responsibilities 4 Process Presentation 5 Automated Alert Generation 6 Initial Review: Suspicious Activity Thresholds 6 Second Review: Risk Tolerance 6 Retuning Triggers and Frequency 7 Alert Prioritization 7 Quality Control 8 Explainability 8 Document Management 9 Document Owner 9 Scheduled Review & Ad-Hoc Maintenance 9 Exceptions 9 Related Documents 9 Revision & Approval History 9 Document Details 10 Transaction Monitoring Modeling Program | Cash App 2 Introduction Block, Inc. (herein referred to as the “Company” or “Block”) is committed to complying with applicable laws, regulations, and guidelines to monitor, detect, prevent, and report possible money laundering, financial crimes, and terrorist financing. Transaction monitoring is a vital component of ensuring compliance, as it provides a mechanism for evaluating suspicious activity and transactions across Block’s platforms. Block utilizes various proprietary algorithms to create alerts for the Transaction Monitoring team to review. This includes automated alert queues and periodic or manual reports for review. Alert queues and reports are generated using machine-learning, heuristics, or manual methodologies. Block’s Transaction Monitoring team queues are established and maintained by the Compliance Engineering & Technology data team. Any new queues or recommended changes to the existing queue models are reviewed and approved in accordance with the Model Change Management Process. This Transaction Monitoring Modeling Program (the “Program”) articulates how Transaction Monitoring alert queues are created for analyst review, as a result of automated transaction monitoring. Note that this Program is only applicable to transaction monitoring models that use a combination of heuristics and machine learning for alert generation (also known as alert risk rating models). This Program is not applicable to transaction monitoring models that use only heuristics. This policy is established by Cash App and is applicable to Cash App. References to “staff” throughout this document shall refer to all full-time employees and fixed-term and contingent workers including interns, temps, consultants, vendors, third-party providers, and contractors. Definitions Above the Line (ATL) A phrase used to indicate that an account activity exceeds defined thresholds. Alert Risk Rating Model (ARR) A methodology/model used in risk determination for transactions and accounts. Below The Line (BTL) A phrase to indicate that an account activity falls below defined thresholds. Transaction Monitoring Modeling Program | Cash App 3 Model Retraining The process of updating the model based on new or updated datasets. Model Retuning The process of resetting the score thresholds, based on new or updated datasets. Negative Action The result of a completed review when an account has been found to be in violation of the Cash App Terms of Service or engaging in unwanted, high-risk activity. It includes denylist, suspicious activity reporting, and other adverse reporting. Roles & Responsibilities Transaction Monitoring Team ● Review alerts and investigate associated activity ● Decision alerts ● Escalate accounts for unusual or suspicious activity Compliance Engineering & Technology Data ● Establish and maintain transaction Team monitoring queue parameters and thresholds ● Complete periodic model validation on the transaction monitoring queues ● Provide reporting to senior leadership on queue completion, performance, and enhancements Transaction Monitoring Modeling Program | Cash App 4 Process Presentation The diagram below illustrates the transaction monitoring process: Transaction Monitoring Modeling Program | Cash App 5 Automated Alert Generation Initial Review: Suspicious Activity Thresholds The initial review of account activity occurs when any activity exceeds a set threshold. Any activity that exceeds this threshold is potentially suspicious and warrants further review. Note: In accordance with the FinCEN suspicious activity report (SAR) filing limit, aSAR must be filed by a covered money services business (MSB) when the MSB knows, suspects or has reason to suspect that the transaction or pattern of transactions is suspicious and involves $2,000 or more. The appropriate heuristics are dollar amount or count-based and chosen based on the specific typology under consideration. Heuristics not based on the above dimensions are used sparingly and, when used, are set based on typology definition and expert business judgment (e.g., recurring relationship and velocity). The heuristic threshold is typically set at the 84th percentile for the active population (aka P84). This corresponds roughly to mean plus one standard deviation for a normal distribution, but the percentile approach is followed because these heuristics are not usually normally distributed. For certain low risk typologies, the heuristic thresholds may be adjusted higher to the 97.5th percentile of the active population (aka P97.5 which corresponds to mean plus two standard deviations of a normal distribution). Second Review: Risk Tolerance The second-level review is completed by a machine learning model, called the Alert Risk Rating model (ARR), that leverages hundreds of signals, based on transactional and user activity, to ascertain whether an account is likely to be negatively actioned on human review. The ARR model threshold is tuned to adhere to a false negative rate or risk tolerance policy defined below. The risk tolerance policy is set based on the Company's appetite for risk. Higher tolerance makes operations more scalable by allowing the system to cut out more false positives, but increases the risk of bad activity that goes undetected/unreported. The industry standard of 5% risk tolerance is adopted, but may be adjusted in the future for specific typologies/segments within a typology based on Company policy or risk appetite. In the scenario where the productivity of the heuristics based typology is already below the Risk Tolerance Threshold (RTT) of 5%, based on expert business judgment, an 80% recall rate Transaction Monitoring Modeling Program | Cash App 6 is targeted for the second level review by the ARR model. (Note: 80% recall rate will be well below the 5% RTT.) Risk tolerance is per AML typology, unless otherwise specified. This makes individual typology performance monitoring feasible. If an account could have alerts generated by multiple typologies, it is considered covered if an alert is generated by at least one of those typologies for performance measurement purposes. Retuning Triggers and Frequency Historical data suggests that month-to-month concern event rates may be fairly volatile. Therefore, a small randomly selected fraction of BTL alerts are reviewed by transaction monitoring analysts on an ongoing basis. Analysts do not know that these alerts are BTL, but will appear in the alerting queue as is typical. This performance data will be used to measure adherence of the system to the set risk tolerance. Alert Prioritization A value at risk approach is proposed for alert review prioritization by analysts. This provides a framework for minimizing regulatory risk in the event that alerts are temporarily backlogged due to operational capacity constraints. Value at Risk = Size of Activity * Risk Score (ARR Score) The ARR model score can be viewed as a measure of the model’s confidence in the account being involved in suspicious activity prior to analyst review. Volume and amount of activity is a measure of the size of the activity in the customer account. A value at risk approach prioritizes alerts for analyst review by balancing the size of activity and the model’s confidence in the activity being involved in suspicious activity. The value at risk approach (P0 > P1 > P2) is illustrated below. The actual number of prioritization segments, as well as the heuristics chosen for each dimension, are specific to the typology being considered. Transaction Monitoring Modeling Program | Cash App 7 Analysts prioritize alert review starting with the highest priority. In the event that alerts are backlogged, the low value at risk alerts present the least amount of regulatory risk. Note that for some typologies that have low alert volume, all alerts are grouped into a single queue with a default priority and reviewed manually in queue order. Quality Control An independent quality control team is responsible for measuring and maintaining agent review quality in order to ensure label quality. The issues identified will trigger appropriate actions (including but not limited to retraining) in order to correct any problems. BTL and ATL productivity evaluation systems will be in place to maintain machine review quality, a continuous and automated BTL sampling, and model monitoring. Explainability Every account that is scored by the ARR model will log an ‘explanation’ that provides insight into the signals that contributed to the specific model score. Negative contributions suggest that the particular signal contributed to lower the score (i.e. risk of the account), while positive contributions suggest the opposite. The sum of contributions from all the model’s constituent signals result in the final risk score Transaction Monitoring Modeling Program | Cash App 8 attributed to the alert. Signals are grouped by a handful of specific themes to aid in human interpretability. Explainability is logged primarily for regulatory and audit purposes. Document Management Document Owner This document is owned by the Head of Compliance Engineering & Technology Data, Cash App, or their designee. Scheduled Review & Ad-Hoc Maintenance This document shall be reviewed and approved at least annually and on an ad-hoc basis in accordance with the Compliance Document Approval Matrix. documents.GOV-042: Fitness and Propriety Standard Clearpay Finance Limited Last Approved: Mar 25, 2026 Effective Date: Mar 25, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: This Fitness and Propriety Standard (“Standardˮ) outlines Clearpay Finance Limitedʼs (“CFLˮ) approach to Fitness and Propriety of all staff members acting in the role of either a Significant Management Function (“SMFˮ) or Certified Function (“Certifiedˮ) under the Senior Managers and Certification Regime (“SMCRˮ), a UK Regulation under the Financial Conduct Authority (“FCAˮ). The Chief Compliance Officer (“CCOˮ) is responsible for management of this Standard. CFL conducts business in compliance with all applicable laws, rules and regulations. This Standard outlines the process by which CFL seeks to ensure that this Standard is to individuals acting as a SMF, a Certified individual or a non-executive director role have been appropriately assessed as being suitable to perform the role in line with the FCAʼs Fit and Proper (“F&Pˮ) requirements. These requirements are outlined in the FCAʼs Fit and Proper test for Employees and Senior Personnel FIT 2. Scope: The Standard applies to any staff member carrying out a role that has been assessed as being capable of causing significant harm to CFL, the broader market or its customers. These individuals may or may not be in a Senior Management position, but the nature of their role is considered to have significant management responsibility and/or is within a significant business unit. This applies regardless of contract of employment or geographical location. Definitions: Terminology Definition CFL Clearpay Finance Limited 1 An employee or external worker (e.g., contingent labor, Staff Member(s) contractors) hired by the Company who supports the operations covered by this Standard FCA The Financial Conduct Authority Senior Managers and Certification Regime The FCAʼs UK regulatory framework for individual accountability (“SMCRˮ) Significant Management Key leadership roles requiring FCA pre-approval under SMCR Function (“SMFˮ) Certified Function/Certified Roles requiring annual certification by the firm under SMCR Individual Non-Executive Director Non-Executive Directors are subject to SMCR, even where not SMF (“NEDˮ) holders and subject to FCA pre-approval Fit and Proper (“F&Pˮ) The regulatory standard for assessing individual suitability Statement of Document outlining SMF duties and accountabilities Responsibilities (“SoRˮ) FCA rules governing individual behaviour requirements for all staff Conduct Rules members Mandatory employment references covering 6 years of history that financial services firms need to share with each other when an Regulatory References employee or director moves from one firm to another (for candidates of Senior Managers Functions, Non-Executive Directors and Certification Functions) Disclosure and Barring UK criminal record checking service Service (“DBSˮ) County Court Judgement Court orders for unpaid debts (“CCJˮ) Individual Voluntary Formal debt repayment agreements Arrangements (“IVAsˮ) 2 Continuing Professional Ongoing training and skill development requirements Development (“CPDˮ) Event-driven assessment F&P review triggered by specific circumstances or incidents Assessment of an individualʼs ability to manage personal finances Financial soundness responsibly Verification of an individualʼs credit history and financial standing Credit checks 2. Standard Requirements 2.1. Core Principles of Fitness and Propriety The FCA will assess the F&P of SMFs. The firm is expected to assess the F&P of Certified Individuals and non-SMF Non-Executive Directors. The following three pillars form the assessment: Honesty, Integrity, and Reputation This involves considering if the individual has been open and honest in their dealings with the firm and regulators. It includes: ● checks for any criminal record (especially relating to financial crime or dishonesty), ● adverse findings from previous regulatory bodies, significant civil actions (e.g., disqualification as a director); and ● any other information that could call their integrity into question. Regulatory references are a key part of this. Competence and Capability This assesses whether the individual has the necessary skills, knowledge, and experience to perform the specific SMF, Certified Individual or NED role effectively. It involves verifying qualifications, training, and experience relevant to the responsibilities they will undertake. It also considers their track record in previous roles and their understanding of the regulatory landscape and the specific business area they will oversee. 3 Financial Soundness This assesses whether the individual has managed their own financial affairs properly. Significant financial difficulties, such as County Court Judgements CCJs, bankruptcy, or Individual Voluntary Arrangements IVAs, could indicate that an individual is not financially sound, especially if the role involves managing client money or significant financial decision-making. However, the context of any financial issues is important and will be considered on a case-by-case basis. Evidence requirements Firms must collect extra evidence when assessing candidates for Senior Manager positions, Certification Functions or NED roles (even if not a Senior Manager). These are explained below. 2.2. SMF, Certified Individual and NED F&P Assessment Pre-Approval Initial Assessment) A thorough F&P assessment must be conducted before appointing the individual to the role in question. In addition: ● For a SMF this assessment must be completed before the individual is proposed to the FCA for approval as an SMF. The firm must attest to the FCA that they are satisfied the candidate is fit and proper. ● For a Certified Individual the firm must attest that they are satisfied the individual is fit and proper to perform the function for which they are certified on a formal record of certification. Ongoing Assessment CFL has an ongoing duty to ensure these individuals continue to be Fit and Proper. CFL must conduct a formal F&P assessment for these individuals at least annually with adequate records and evidence stored and retrievable. The FCA Handbook does not prescribe a fixed frequency or method for reassessment. In addition: ● CFL must re-certify its Certified Individuals as fit and proper to perform the role for which they have been certified at least annually. documents.GOV-043: Training and Competence Standard Clearpay Finance Ltd Last Approved: Mar 25, 2026 Effective Date: Mar 25, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: Clearpay Finance Limited (“Clearpayˮ) is committed to upholding the highest standards of conduct and governance. This Training and Competence Standard (“Standardˮ) outlines Clearpayʼs approach to complying with the Financial Conduct Authority (“FCAˮ)ʼs requirements to ensure all staff possess the required competence to undertake their responsibilities to Clearpay regulated activities. The Chief Compliance Officer is responsible for management of this Standard. Scope: This Standard applies to Clearpay, all its employees, outsourced service providers, consultants, contractors, suppliers and agents (“Clearpay employeesˮ). It is the duty of all Clearpay employees to be familiar with this Standard and any operational routines concerning Training and Competence which are relevant to their work. Mandatory compliance training administered in person and through Clearpayʼs Learning Management System (“LMSˮ) is managed under this Standard. Clearpay updates its Compliance Training Calendar on an annual basis , which details the schedule and audiences for global and regional mandatory compliance training for the calendar year. Clearpay is authorised and regulated by the FCA to enter into regulated consumer credit agreements with customers (“consumersˮ or “customersˮ). Under the FCAʼs Systems and Controls requirements (“SYSCˮ) Clearpay is required to ensure it employs personnel with the skills, knowledge and expertise necessary for the discharge of the responsibilities allocated to them. Clearpay also needs to ensure its systems and controls enable it to satisfy itself of the suitability of anyone who acts for it, including an assessment of their competence. This assessment should take account of the level of responsibility of that individual. Definitions: Terminology Definition 1 Assignment Generally, enterprise-wide mandatory compliance training is assigned to new hires within a week of onboarding and they are given 30 days to complete the training. For enterprise-wide mandatory compliance training assigned annually, staff are generally given 45 days to complete the training. Exceptions are outlined in the Compliance Training Calendar. Enrollment Staff Members who are assigned training based on eligibility criteria Audience determined and approved by the Content Owner for each training course. Training The end-to-end cycle to manage a specific training course. Campaign Staff Member(s) All employees and fixed-term and contingent workers who support the operations and/or subsidiaries covered by this Standards, and includes interns, temporary workers, consultants, vendors, third-party providers and contractors. FCA Financial Conduct Authority Clearpay is authorised and regulated by the FCA. 2. Standard Requirements 2.1. Board Training Board Training is delivered on an annual basis to the Clearpay Board by the Chief Compliance Officer and/or Money Laundering Reporting Officer (“MLROˮ) or their delegate. This training may include but is not limited to AML, Anti-Bribery & Corruption (“ABCˮ),and Consumer Protection topics along with any additional content identified by the CCO. If a Board Director is also an employee, they will be waived from employee-level training if they attended the Director-level training. 2.2. Intake Process New training requests for in-house development are considered and prioritised on a case-by-case basis by the Clearpay Training Team (“CTTˮ). The CTT then determines the best learning solution for required training, and the order in which the request will be prioritised against the Compliance Training Calendar (“CTCˮ). For training that will require vendor support, the CTT will manage the relationship, advise on system requirements, evaluate vendor options, customise vendor content, and support use of vendor content. 2 2.3 Training Records Training records are maintained and stored in accordance with Blockʼs Data Classification & Handling Policy. Training records include: ● Training Content, including versions of training materials; ● Records of Training Completion from LMS; and ● Where applicable, records of Training Assessments and corresponding results. ● Records are stored within the LMS and secure shared drives managed by the CTT. 2.4 Communication Plan 2.4.1 Annual Training Communication Plan In order to ensure completion of required foundational corporate Compliance training and maintain a culture of compliance, Clearpay has established the below communication and escalation process to ensure team members are encouraged to complete training by the required due date. Once training is assigned, employees must complete training within 30 days from annual training enrollment and enrollment for new hires (unless otherwise noted in the Compliance Training Calendar). 2.4.2 New Hire & Other Modified Escalation Process The CTT review new hire completion rates are reviewed on a monthly basis and notifications are sent to employees and their leads for two monthly cycles or until complete for learners who are 30 days overdue. Additionally, at times, select training may need to have a customised escalation process based on audience, requirements, campaign length, and/or subject matter. This determination is made in partnership between the CTT and the Content Owner. In these instances, a modified escalation process will be developed and applied to the campaign. 2.4.3 Training Non-Completion Clearpay requires all Staff Members to complete and understand mandatory Compliance Training within established timeframes. Staff Members who do not complete training by established due dates may be subject to further escalations and or disciplinary action. Upon the conclusion of a Training Campaign cycle, the CTT will provide a final list of Staff Members with incomplete training to the HRBPs at the completion of the Training Campaign. 2.5 Enrollment Waivers At times, it may be necessary, based on audience updates or changes to training requirements, to make changes to an approved Enrollment Audience after training has been assigned to employees 3 via LMS. The CTT manages a formal exception process for how these changes are managed and processed, following one of the two pathways described below. ● Waivers: Waivers are enrollment exceptions that deviate from the audience Assignment parameters for an individual or group of individuals. Waivers are reviewed on a case-by-case basis by the CTT and Content Owners. For waivers (both individual and bulk), requestors must submit a request in writing providing a business justification for the unenrollment request. The CTT will review requests and work with respective Content Owners to determine if additional approvals are required (i.e. such as from Employment Counsel). If the waiver request is approved, the CTT will fulfill the request in the LMS and maintain a record of the request and its approval. ● Changes to Training Audience: For any other changes to the training audience after training has been assigned (e.g. adding employees missed), Content Owners must make the request to the CTT in writing, detailing which course and how the audience needs to be updated. The CTT will maintain a record of the request and its approval. 3. Compliance & Enforcement 3.1. Roles and Responsibilities: Clearpay Roles: ● Chief Compliance Officer CCO ○ Advises on Clearpay training content review, provides input on relevant training requirements ○ Delivers training to Clearpay Finance Ltd Board. ○ Reviews and approves waivers for training based on eligibility; this responsibility may be delegated ● Money Laundering Reporting Officer MLRO ○ Advise on AML training content review, provides input on relevant training requirements ○ Reviews and approves waivers for AML training based on eligibility; this responsibility may be delegated ● Compliance Training Team CTT ○ Owns and manages this Compliance Training & Competence Standard which outlines the requirements and strategy for implementing the requirements. ○ Advises on training development and design, escalation process, communications plan, and other CTT-owned processes and enterprise-wide mandatory compliance training. ○ Responsible for monitoring adherence to Standards requirements. ○ Supports development of Compliance Training for the Clearpay Finance Ltd Board which are delivered and facilitated by the Chief Compliance Officer (“CCOˮ). ● Content Owners: ○ Own training content, and in collaboration with the Compliance Training Team 4 (“CTTˮ), are responsible for ensuring that their content and enrollment deliverables are completed by target dates. ○ Provide the Enrollment Audience requirements for their training to the CTT. ○ Review and approve final training content, audience, and waiver requests (based on eligibility criteria) prior to campaign launch ● Human Resources Business Partners (“HRBPsˮ): ○ Where Staff Members fail to complete their required Compliance Training HRBPs (in partnership with People Leads) determine and support disciplinary action, as appropriate. ● People Leads: ○ People Leads should ensure timely completion of training for all their team members. ○ Where Staff Members fail to complete their required Compliance Training requirements, People Leads determine and or support disciplinary action, as appropriate. 3.2 Monitoring and Reporting On an ongoing basis, the Standard Owner and/or their designee will monitor Standard related activities to evaluate adherence to the Standard and determine whether outputs adhere to established objectives. Specifically, training results are reported and submitted to the Clearpay Finance Ltd Board on a quarterly basis. Training reports include completion rates for each training and can be broken down by region, cost center, and team code. Training results are reported and submitted to the Clearpay Finance Ltd Board on a quarterly basis

Document Details 12 Compliance Incident Management Standard | Block 3 Confidential and Not for Distribution Definitions Terminology Definition Incident An unforeseen, unplanned or near-miss event that negatively impacts our operations, quality of services and/or function. An Incident may be caused by either (or a combination of) internal control failure(s) or an external event outside of the organization’s control. An incident is separate from but can be related to Issues a high level control improvement, actions, risks or regulatory changes, etc. Issue A deficiency, gap, or weakness in the design, performance, or operating effectiveness of a process or control or the absence of a control. Production An Engineering event impacting one or more of the following: Incidents (Non-Security 1. a significant service (e.g., payment rails, Cash App) Incident) 2. a significant number of services (e.g., refunds, reporting, & logins) 3. a significant number of customers (e.g., x% of certain customers) 4. a significant miss in service level availability (e.g., product/service is X% down) Security/Privacy A confirmed breach, policy violation, or event that threatens the Incident confidentiality, integrity, or availability of Block Information, requiring investigation and response. Any loss or unauthorized access to, or acquisition or use of, Block’s information maintained by, or on behalf of, Block, or malicious or non-malicious activities or vulnerabilities that result in unauthorized modification or damage to Block technology or systems, including the Block network or mobile applications. A non-exhaustive list of examples includes computer system intrusion, suspected or actual breaches, compromises, or other unauthorized access to Block systems, data, applications, or accounts, denial of service (DDoS) attack or an attack that prevents or impairs the authorized use of networks, systems, or applications. SEV Levels Severity levels are defined by impact (e.g., SEV 0 to SEV 4), guiding prioritization and response efforts. SEVs is shorthand for a Product Incident. Block SEV Levels ● SEV 0: Adversely affecting crucial, time-sensitive business activities for our Sellers, resulting in financial loss or damage to their reputation. For example: blocking Sales or Deposits. Triggers Compliance Incident Management Standard | Block 4 Confidential and Not for Distribution Terminology Definition update to issquareup.com ● SEV 1: Adversely affecting crucial business activities for our Sellers, with the potential to incur financial loss or harm their reputation. For example: blocking Payroll runs or administrative tasks Triggers update to issquareup.com ● SEV 2: Disrupting non-essential operations that can reasonably be postponed during the incidents’ occurrence. For example: delaying a non-critical report ● SEV 3: Minimal disruption to non-essential operations. Cash SEV Levels: ● SEV 0: Any incident that threatens Block or Cash in any market with serious reputational, regulatory, or business harm. ● SEV 1: Any incident that significantly impacts a large group of customers or causes widespread work halting for Cash engs. ● SEV 2: An incident that inconveniences a large group of customers or Cash engs (e.g. work halting for 30 or more engineers). ● SEV 3: An incident that affects a small enough group that there is no noticeable impact to customer success, or where there is an obvious workaround. Internal SEVs have minor impact or impact few employees. ● SEV 4: Indicates that external customers or developers are not impacted. Square SEV Levels: ● SEV 0: Any incident that stops a seller from running their business. Threatens Square in any market with serious reputational, regulatory, or business harm. (e.g., blocking Sales or Deposits) ● SEV 1: Any incident that significantly impacts a large group of customers or causes work halting (e.g. blocking Payroll runs or administrative tasks) ● SEV 2: An incident that inconveniences a large group of customers or engs, or where there is an obvious workaround. ● SEV 3: An incident that affects a small enough group that there is no noticeable impact to customer success or the seller ● SEV 4: Indicates that external customers or developers are not impacted. Preemptive SEVs. On-Call Bug A bug is an error, flaw, failure, or fault in a computer program or system that causes it to produce an incorrect, or unexpected result, or to behave in unintended ways. Compliance Incident Management Standard | Block 5 Confidential and Not for Distribution Terminology Definition Depending on the impact, bugs can be fixed immediately &/or live in our codebase indefinitely, or simply fall somewhere in between. incident.io An incident management platform in use at Block. There are two distinct, highly customized instances currently in use; one for the Cash App and one for Square. The two instances operate independently in both workflow and configuration. incident.io is integrated with Slack, PagerDuty, and Jira. Rootly An incident management platform for managing and resolving Information Security and Privacy related incidents. Rootly is integrated with tools like Slack and Jira to automate and streamline incident response processes. PagerDuty PagerDuty is a SaaS incident command system used by Block across all brands. Fire Hydrant An incident management platform used for Production Incidents for Afterpay. It is integrated with Slack, PagerDuty, and Jira. Datadog Datadog is a monitoring and analytics tool used by Engineering to track the performance and health of applications and infrastructure. Stabilization Stabilization is the process of restoring normal activity. All Incidents require stabilization. Remediation Distinct from Stabilization, Remediation is the process of undoing the harm caused. Remediation is not required for all Incidents. Roles and Responsibilities Role Responsibility Compliance Responsible for monitoring processes and operations to identify and escalate Compliance-related Incidents, including Anti-Money Laundering (“AML”)/Countering Financial Terrorism (“CFT”) & Sanctions-related Incidents. Detection and Responsible for detecting, assessing, and responding to Security Response Team Incidents. (“DART”) DART operational teams handle the initial response for all Information Security/Privacy Incidents using the DART Security Incident Response Compliance Incident Management Standard | Block 6 Confidential and Not for Distribution Runbook (“IRP”). For Information Security/Privacy Incidents that do not meet the threshold to trigger the IRP, the Runbook guides resolution of those other Incidents. DART is responsible for assessing and responding to any potential Security/Privacy Incidents. These incidents may be detected through various sources, including internal or external IT sources, employees, anonymous reports, third parties, the press, or law enforcement. Anything suspicious should be reported to DART, which actively monitors for Security/Privacy Incidents and evaluates their seriousness. Incident Response After an evaluation, if a Security/Privacy Incident is deemed Critical or Team (“IRT”) High Impact, Block will mobilize the IRT, which is responsible for managing and resolving Security Incidents, led by the CISO. Computer Security The DART team that investigates and resolves Security/Privacy Incidents. Incident Response Team (“CSIRT”) Compliance Group Advise the IRT on compliance-related decisions during an incident, Lead ensuring that actions taken do not expose the organization to legal risks. Counsel Group Responsible for overseeing legal aspects of the incident response, Lead ensuring compliance, providing guidance, coordinating with external legal entities, assessing legal risks, managing stakeholder communication, and maintaining documentation for potential legal proceedings. Privacy Group Lead Responsible for managing and protecting data privacy during an incident, ensuring compliance with data protection laws, advising on privacy issues (including determining whether notification to data subjects, regulators and / or partners is necessary), coordinating with regulators, overseeing privacy safeguards, managing breach notifications, and maintaining records of privacy-related action Service Providers / When Block is informed of an Incident at a third party, the IRT may rely Third Party on a DRI to facilitate communication with their partner or vendor. All communications with 3rd parties regarding a Block Security Incident or investigation must be approved by Block Legal. Technology In instances of high severity Production Incidents, Block will mobilize Operations Teams TechOps, which is responsible for coordinating and managing towards (“TechOps”) stabilization and recovery. Document Details 12 Regulatory Compliance Monitoring Program | Square | Squareup International Limited 3 Confidential and Not for Distribution Definitions Terminology Definition Regulatory The risk of legal or regulatory sanctions, material financial loss, or loss to reputation Squareup International Limited (“SILˮ) may suffer as a result of its Compliance Risk failure to comply with laws, regulations, regulatory guidance, codes of conduct, regulatory expectations and group wide policies, amongst other things. Employees All employees and fixed-term and contingent workers who support the operations and/or subsidiaries covered by this Policy, and include interns, temporary workers, consultants, vendors, third-party providers, and contractors. Roles and Responsibilities Role Responsibility Three Lines of The Company operates a Three Lines of Defence model. This model reflects Defence the segregation between business units (first line), the compliance and risk management functions (second line), and the independent internal audit function (third line). First Line of The first line of defence is responsible for maintaining the effectiveness of Defence operations processing and controls in their respective units. In terms of compliance monitoring the businessʼ role is to identify, manage and escalate emerging issues or risks to Compliance and senior management to include the Regulatory Governance Committee and the SIL Board if warranted. Also to facilitate EU Compliance in conducting its own monitoring, the first line is required to provide data, documentation, management information etc., on request, in a timely manner. If non-compliances or concerns are identified via monitoring, the relevant business leads are to work with EU Compliance to determine the root cause and associated action plans to address the same. Regulatory Compliance Monitoring Program | Square | Squareup International Limited 4 Confidential and Not for Distribution Second Line of EU Compliance as a second line function is responsible for providing Defence ongoing oversight, guidance on requirements and independently evaluating the first lineʼs adherence to defined standards. EU Compliance is responsible for conducting compliance monitoring in accordance with this Regulatory Compliance Monitoring Program and its underlying compliance monitoring procedure, reportable to the EU Chief Compliance Officer (“EU CCOˮ), the Regulatory Governance Committee and the SIL Board, when required. Enterprise Risk function, as another limb of the second line, is responsible for the assessment, monitoring and reporting of risk, to include financial crime, regulatory, financial, technology, strategic and operational risks. Third Line of The third line, internal audit function is responsible for providing independent Defence assurance on the effectiveness of risk management, including control activities, by performing audits of business level activities and second-line functionality. Regulatory The role of the RGC is to assist SILʼs executive director(s), Chief Executive Governance Officer and other senior managers in relation to their oversight of SILʼs risk Committee (“RGCˮ) and compliance management framework and current risk exposures. In order to facilitate this, subject-matter experts update the Committee on areas including, but not limited to, the results of the Compliance monitoring reviews. The RGC is to review, assess and challenge these results. SIL Board (“Boardˮ) The Board is responsible for managing and overseeing all aspects of the Companyʼs business including, but not limited to, ensuring compliance with all applicable laws and regulatory guidelines/obligations. To this end, the Board is to review, assess and challenge the results of the Compliance monitoring reviews, reported to it. Regulatory Compliance Monitoring Program | Square | Squareup International Limited 5 Confidential and Not for Distribution 1. Introduction This Regulatory Compliance Monitoring Program (“Programˮ) outlines SILʼs approach to be applied by the EU Compliance team when conducting second line compliance monitoring.The EU CCO is responsible for the management of this Program. This Program will be executed by SILʼs EU Compliance team, and is separate to and distinct from the Global Compliance Monitoring and Testing (“GCMTˮ) Program. Both teams that execute on these respective programs form part of the second line of defence in SIL. Both monitoring and testing are important tools used for the oversight of SILʼs compliance risk management program, but serve two different oversight roles to achieve this objective. This Program involves the ongoing surveillance, review, and analysis of key management information, metrics and risk indicators, established by EU Compliance and or the relevant business area under review, allowing SIL to identify potential non-compliances. The GCMT program is a dynamic, risk-based, independent compliance oversight process designed to periodically select and review a regulatory area and report on the operating effectiveness of internal controls and governance processes. The two programs combined bring a systematic, disciplined testing approach to SILʼs oversight activities and provides key stakeholders and Board with relevant risk and control information so that control owners can take prompt remedial action. 2. Purpose SIL conducts business in compliance with all applicable laws and regulations. In support of this, this Program has been designed to: ● Manage Regulatory Compliance Risks; ● Identify, correct, and report compliance-related gaps and deficiencies; ● Satisfy the monitoring expectations of SILʼs regulator; and ● Provide assurance to Board, senior management, and stakeholders on the status of compliance. Regulatory Compliance Monitoring Program | Square | Squareup International Limited 6 Confidential and Not for Distribution 3. Scope This Program is established by SIL and is applicable to all its Employees. 4. Program Requirements 4.1 Risk-Based Regulatory Compliance Monitoring Schedule Compliance management begins with the assessment and identification of risks. Using a risk based approach, the Compliance monitoring schedule (“the Scheduleˮ) focuses on key Regulatory Compliance Risk for EU Compliance to monitor, and sets out the types of monitoring to be conducted, the frequency of such monitoring and the reporting of issues arising from the monitoring. The EU CCO must approve the annual Schedule which is prepared by the EU Compliance Team and any changes thereto. Scheduled Compliance monitoring may be moved to accommodate staffing or resources constraints/needs, to align with changes to risk levels etc., provided the EU CCOʼs approval is obtained. 4.2 Regulatory Compliance Monitoring Methodology At a high level, the methodology used for conducting monitoring activity Figure 1 is: 1. Data/facts; 2. Monitoring; and, 3. Management Acts. This data is to be obtained from/reported by the first line of defence to EU Compliance as requested, in a timely manner. ● Monitor EU Compliance is to then monitor this data for exceptions compared to processes, policies, procedures or programs that have been designed to ensure compliance. In other words, EU Compliance is to monitor adherence to regulatory requirements and/or internal policies and procedures. The types of monitoring to be conducted may include analysis of data, key risk indicators, reports, trends as well as sample/dip testing of files, transactions, cases, account records, etc. It is important to note that this Program should evolve,as well as the types of data subject to monitoring, as the business evolves and its risk profile changes. ● Management Acts ○ Root Cause Analysis Root cause analysis is an approach used to analyse problems before trying to solve them.
