documents.GOV-047: Regulatory Change Management Standard Square | Square AU Pty Ltd Last Approved: Mar 29, 2026 Effective Date: Mar 29, 2026 1. Overview Introduction and Purpose: The landscape of the regulatory environment governing financial institutions in Australia is continually evolving. The ability to effectively navigate and promptly adapt to these regulatory changes is a critical requirement for Square AU Pty Ltd (“Square AUˮ) to mitigate associated legal, operational, and regulatory risks. The Regulatory Change Management Standard (the “Standardˮ) is designed to create a robust system for proactive monitoring, interpretation, and effective responses to changes in regulations. This Standard encompasses critical assessments, planning, implementation and monitoring processes to ensure Square AU is aligned with all relevant regulatory requirements. It also covers key elements such as identifying business stakeholders, assessing the impact of a regulatory change, developing plans to address changes, and actively monitoring these plans to ensure ongoing effectiveness. Scope: This Standard is established by Square AU and applicable to Covered Products offered by Square AU. This Standard is managed and maintained by the Square AU Compliance team and sets out the approach to design and execution Square AUʼs Regulatory Change Management Standard. The Standard also applies to material third-party vendors or fintech partners where regulatory change affects their activity on behalf of Square AU. Definitions: Terminology Definition Anti-Money A set of regulatory rules and investigative techniques aimed at preventing Laundering and combating various illegal activities related to the financial system (“AMLˮ) Counter-Terrorist A set of regulatory rules and investigative techniques designed to detect, Financing prevent and disrupt the flow of funds of terrorist organisations and activities. (“CTFˮ) 1 Counter- A set of regulatory rules and preventive measures designed to detect, Proliferation prevent and disrupt the flow of funds that could contribute to the Financing development, acquisition or transfer of nuclear, chemical or biological weapons and their means of delivery, in breach of international sanctions or United Nations Security Council resolutions. The Anti-Money As part of its broader regulatory compliance obligations, Square AU Pty Ltd Laundering and (“Square AUˮ) is required to comply with the AML/CTF Act 2006 Cth, as Counter-Terroris amended from time to time. The AML/CTF Act is designed to deter, detect m Financing Act and disrupt money laundering and terrorism financing. 2006 (“AML/CTF Actˮ) AUSTRAC The Australian Transaction Reports and Analysis Centre (‘AUSTRACʼ) performs a dual role as AML and CTF regulator and financial intelligence unit. ASIC Australian Securities & Investments Commission (‘ASICʼ) is Australiaʼs integrated corporate, markets, financial services and consumer credit regulator. It is an independent body set up under the Australian Securities and Investments Commission Act 2001 ASIC Act) and carries out most of the work under the Corporations Act. ASO The Australian Sanctions Office (‘ASOʼ) is the Australian Governmentʼs sanction regulator. DFAT The Department of Foreign Affairs and Trade (‘DFATʼ) promotes and protects Australiaʼs international interests to support Australiaʼs security and prosperity. Covered Includes all products and services offered by Square AU Pty Ltd that require Products a financial services licence or registration to offer and must comply with local laws. Certain other products may also be considered Covered Products, such as where required under partner bank expectation and/or contractual obligation. 2. Standard Requirements 2.1. Regulatory Landscape Regulatory requirements may change in the future given the dynamic nature of the financial services industry and potential to expand Square AUʼs product offerings. While Square AU operates under Block Inc., responsibility for managing regulatory changes within the Australian jurisdiction rests exclusively with the Square AU Team. Australiaʼs key regulators and bodies for the financial 2 services industry, some of whom we actively monitor for regulatory change, are shown in the below table: Regulator Description ASIC Australian Security and Australiaʼs integrated corporate, markets, financial services and Investments Commission) consumer credit regulator. ASIC administers and enforces a range of legislation. AUSTRAC Australian AML/CTF regulator and FIU; administers the AML/CTF Act 2006, Transaction Reports and enforces reporting obligations TTR, IFTI, SMR, and AML program Analysis Centre) compliance. ASO Australian Sanctions Australian Governmentʼs sanctions regulator1, which sits within DFATʼs Office) Department of Foreign Affairs and Trade) Regulatory Legal Division in the Security, Legal and Consular Group. ASO works to ensure the efficient administration of Australian sanctions laws, consistent with Australiaʼs international obligations and foreign policy objectives. AFCA Australian Financial External dispute resolution scheme for financial services; decisions Complaints Authority) and determinations can create precedent and operational change requirements. ACCC Australian Competition Enforces the Competition and Consumer Act 2010, including the and Consumer Commission) Australian Consumer Law; regulates fair trading, surcharging rules, and anti-competitive conduct. 2.2. The Regulatory Change Management Process Regulatory changes that may impact Square AU, including but not limited to those relating to AML/CTF or counter-proliferation financing, AFSL and licensing obligations, financial services or payments regulation or consumer protection, are actively monitored through trusted external sources such as the the federal register of legislation, ASIC newsletter and AUSTRAC InBrief Newsletter. The Square AU Team, including but not limited to Compliance, Legal and Risk areresponsible for monitoring the changes and documenting the changes in AU Regulatory Change Tracking and Management. A comprehensive register is maintained for: ● All considered regulatory changes (including those deemed not applicable) ● Dates of change, source, impact assessment, status, action owner, SLA for completion, and closure 1 Any update to Australian sanctions laws is managed by Blockʼs third party screening vendors LexisNexis, and not tracked through the local regulatory change management process. 3 ● Mapping of regulatory requirements to each Covered Product is maintained and reviewed semi-annually. Additionally, the Square AU Regulatory Program Manager, in conjunction with internal Legal Counsel, conducts a preliminary assessment to ascertain whether the regulatory change is relevant. Where applicable, an impact assessment will be undertaken, followed by a decision-making process to determine if modifications to existing programs and procedures are necessary. Where required, external Legal advice may be sought to ensure that the organisationʼs interpretation of regulatory requirements is accurate and that all proposed changes are both adequate and compliant with applicable laws and regulations. 2.2.1 Actioning AUSTRAC guidance In particular, under AML/CTF regulations, Square AUʼs AML/CTF compliance officer must conduct a review of its risk assessment if AUSTRAC communicates information to Square AU or to its sector more broadly - including through published AUSTRAC guidance, that identifies or assess risk associated with the provision of Square AUʼs designated services. The risk assessment must be reviewed as soon as practicable after AUSTRAC communicates the relevant information. Any updates resulting from the review must be made as soon as practicable after the review is completed. Where updates are required, they must be approved by Square AU CCO. Square AUʼs AML/CTF compliance officer must notify the board in writing as soon as practicable after making any update to the risk assessment. Following the review of the risk assessment, Square AUʼs AML/CTF compliance officer must also review and, if necessary, update its AML/CTF policies as soon as practicable. Any updates must be documented in Square AUʼs AML/CTF Program, including the date the updates were made, within 14 days of the update being implemented. The Square AU CCO is ultimately accountable for the management and oversight of the Regulatory Change Management Standard. 2.3 Communication and Training When any regulatory change is confirmed to be applicable to Square AU and required updates to any relevant program and/or procedure have been implemented, the Square AU CCO and delegates are responsible for communicating the change to the organisation and its stakeholders. Training is provided on a risk based approach and subject to the nature of the regulatory changes. 4 3. Compliance & Enforcement 3.1. Roles and Responsibilities: ● Square Australia (“AUˮ) Chief Compliance Officer (“CCOˮ): ○ Oversees the operational functions of the Regulatory Change Management Program (the “Programˮ) in compliance with the local regulatory requirements and is responsible for monitoring and reporting on compliance with this Program. ○ The Square AU CCO may delegate to others the authority to adopt, develop and implement procedures and reporting that support this Program. ○ The Square AU CCO is also responsible for approving and signing off on each regulatory change within the register. ○ Reports on regulatory change to global and local governance committees as appropriate and based on risk. ● Square AU Regulatory Counsel: ○ Provide specialist legal insight and support in the assessment and interpretation of new or amended regulatory requirements. ○ Works closely with the Regulatory Program Manager to conduct preliminary impact assessments of regulatory changes, determine their applicability to Square AUʼs operations, and ensure that the organisationʼs response is aligned with applicable laws. ● Squar

Introduction and Context This Incident Response Plan outlines the structure and processes to ensure that Square Canada can effectively respond to disruptions in a timely manner and in accordance with the requirements of the Retail Payments Activities Act (“RPAA”). This structure supports the escalation, management, and remediation of incidents to maintain operational resilience. Square Canada will both rely upon and will feed into Block Inc.’s existing Incident and Crisis Management processes in the event of a disruption impacting our retail payment activities. The purpose of developing and implementing a localised Square Canada Incident Management structure is to ensure that the impact of the disruption to Square Canada is fully understood, informing decision making regarding the actions required to mitigate any potential or further impacts to our retail payment activities. This playbook establishes the procedures for incident management at Square Canada in alignment with Block’s global incident response protocols. The objective is to ensure a structured and efficient response to incidents impacting Square Canada while maintaining coordination with Block’s central incident response teams. 1.1 Prerequisite Reading This document does not replace Block’s existing incident plans and runbooks, rather it is to be used as an extension of existing processes to ensure Square Canada’s alignment with RPAA incident response and reporting requirements. It is recommended that Directly Responsible Individuals (“DRI”s) directly or indirectly impacted by this runbook familiarize themselves with Block's Business Continuity Plan. 1.2 Roles & Responsibilities The listed roles and responsibilities outlined here are intended to strengthen collaboration and streamline information sharing for reporting incidents under RPAA. This is not an exhaustive list, but rather a high-level guide to understand the typical workflow for RPAA-compliant incident reporting. Detailed responsibilities are further elaborated in Block’s existing incident plans. This includes providing oversight, supporting incident assessment, escalation, and approving regulatory reporting and notification where required. 2. Definition of an Incident An incident, for the purposes of this Incident Management Playbook and in accordance with the RPAA, refers to an event or series of related events that is unplanned by a payment service provider and that results in, or could reasonably be expected to result in, the reduction, deterioration or breakdown of any retail payment activity that is performed by the payment service provider. Incidents may arise from a variety of sources including, but not limited to, cybersecurity breaches, system outages or failures, data corruption, third-party service disruptions, operational errors, fraud, or external threats. Activation Customer A machine learning model and score associated with a one-time snapshot of Risk Rating (“aCRRˮ) initial customer risk taken at onboarding. Continuous A machine learning model and score associated with a periodically updated Customer Risk (i.e. ongoing) assessment of customer risk. Intrinsic Customer Risk of a customer as determined by the presence of a specific attribute, or Risk intrinsic risk factor. Intrinsically high-risk customers are considered high risk regardless of empirical risk. Above the Line A phrase indicating that an account meets all predefined criteria. (“ATLˮ) Below the Line A phrase indicating that an account does not meet all the predefined criteria. (“BTLˮ) Identity Verification The process of confirming an individual or business identity. Roles and Responsibilities Role Responsibility Compliance ● Review alerts and investigate associated activity Operations ● Decision alerts ● Escalate accounts for unusual or suspicious activity International ● Approver of the standard Compliance Leads ● Reviewer of individuals/businesses escalated by Compliance for the specific region Continuous Customer Risk Rating Standard | Square 4 Confidential and Not for Distribution Policy & Governance ● Development and maintenance of Blockʼs Model Governance Policy Team Model Risk ● Complete periodic model validation of the CRR models Management Team Regulatory ● Establish and maintain CRR models, parameters, thresholds, and Compliance queueing infrastructure Machine Learning ● Provide reporting to senior leadership on queue completion, performance, and enhancements Continuous Customer Risk Rating Standard | Square 5 Confidential and Not for Distribution 1. Purpose The cornerstone of a strong anti-money laundering (“AMLˮ) compliance program is the adoption and implementation of risk-based due diligence policies, procedures, and processes for all customers, particularly those that present a higher risk for money laundering and terrorist financing. In order to achieve this, Square has designed and implemented a customer risk rating standard that assesses a customer's profile, assigns a risk rating, and monitors the risk levels of customers. This Continuous Customer Risk Rating Standard (“Standardˮ) outlines Squareʼs approach to assessing and monitoring risks posed by customers. The Regulatory Compliance Machine Learning Lead or their designee is responsible for management of this Standard. This Continuous Customer Risk Rating (“CRRˮ) Standard (the “Standardˮ) enables Square to apply appropriate risk-based controls to mitigate potential exposure to money laundering, terrorism financing, and financial crimes. Improper assessment of a customerʼs risk can have a cascading effect, creating deficiencies in multiple areas of internal controls and resulting in an overall weakened AML compliance program. All new and/or active customers are risk-rated daily using a ML-based Continuous CRR (“cCRRˮ) model. This model defines the risk ratings for customers, which may be used in part to determine the customers that may require enhanced due diligence by Compliance teams. The relevant team leads, along with Regulatory Compliance Machine Learning, establish the criteria for the usage of the cCRR model output. This document covers the development and design of a machine learning MLbased CRR standard for all markets in which Square operates, and is applicable to all Staff Members and subsidiaries used for the Square business. Exclusions, if any, will be case-by-case and documented explicitly. Model Usage Intended Usage1 The intended usage of the cCRR model is to: 1 Reflects key usage scenarios without necessarily being exhaustive. Specific details are captured in the system documentation. Continuous Customer Risk Rating Standard | Square 6 Confidential and Not for Distribution ● Generate regular risk scores for customer accounts; ● Combine the model score with heuristic criteria to identify empirically high-risk customers for manual review; and ● Potentially be used in part to determine other applications, such as KYC information refresh frequency, quality control sampling, etc. Non-Intended Usage The intended usage of the cCRR model does not include taking automatic negative action (i.e. without human intervention) on a large number of customers. 2. Customer Risk 2.1 Empirical Customer Risk Square uses machine learning models to assess the risk of each customer. These assessments of customer risk include a variety of sources of information about a customer, and models are trained using historical data. We refer to model-based assessments of customer risk as empirical risk. Square uses two machine learning models to assess empirical risk across the life of a customer: the Activation CRR and Continuous CRR models. Activation Customer Risk Rating (aCRR) Every customer who onboards through the automated payment onboarding flow and successfully passes identity verification (“IDVˮ) is evaluated by the aCRR machine learning model. The aCRR model evaluation produces a score between 0.0 and 1.0, with 1.0 indicating maximum risk and 0.0 indicating minimal risk. This score should be thought of as a one-time snapshot assessment of the risk associated with the attributes of a customer that are observable during onboarding. A customerʼs aCRR score is stored and used as an input into the cCRR model. Additional details on aCRR can be found in Activation Customer Risk Rating Standard | Block . Continuous Customer Risk Rating (“cCRRˮ) Customers globally are evaluated daily by the cCRR machine learning model. The cCRR model produces a score between 0.0 and 1.0, with 1.0 indicating maximum risk and 0.0 indicating minimal risk. The cCRR model leverages a wide range of information about a customer and their behavior. This score should be Continuous Customer Risk Rating Standard | Square 7 Confidential and Not for Distribution considered a current assessment of the risk of suspicious/illicit activity posed by a customer given the information available as of the evaluation date. Square refers to customers who display high cCRR model scores as empirically high risk, meaning that their overall data (i.e. their actions and collection of attributes) led the model to consider them sufficiently risky to receive additional scrutiny in the form of Enhanced Due Diligence (“EDDˮ) review. 2.2 Intrinsic Customer Risk Machine learning-based empirical assessments of customer risk can be powerful, and in some markets, these empirical assessments of risk may be sufficient to fully describe the high-risk population under Squareʼs CRR Standard. In some markets, however, there may be certain characteristics a customer displays that Square believes should lead them to be considered as high risk, regardless of their empirical model scores. Square refers to this as intrinsic customer risk; the specific attributes that lead to this assessment are referred to as intrinsic risk factors. Which attributes, if any, are considered to be intrinsic risk factors will vary across markets, based upon the specific risk profile of each market. A customer is considered to be intrinsically high risk if they display one or more intrinsic risk factors. Intrinsically high-risk customers are subjected to regular monitoring equal to or exceeding that of empirically high-risk customers. 3. It involves creating processes, procedures, and controls to monitor and enforce compliance with these rules, aiming to prevent legal issues, financial penalties, and reputational damage. Essentially, it's about making sure the organization plays by the rules and operates ethically and responsibly. Block, Inc. (“Block”) For the purposes of this Policy, Block refers to the parent company of Afterpay US, Inc. and its subsidiary, Afterpay US Services, LLC. Compliance Management System Policy | Afterpay US, Inc. 6 Confidential and Not for Distribution 1. Introduction This Compliance Management System (“CMS”) Policy (“Policy”) outlines the Company’s commitment to implementing and maintaining an effective CMS. Afterpay US, Inc.’s CMS is structured to ensure adherence to all relevant laws, regulations, and internal policies. It involves processes, procedures, and controls to monitor and enforce compliance with these rules, aiming to prevent legal issues, financial penalties, and reputational damage. Cash App Head of Compliance and/or designee is responsible for management of this Policy. 2. Purpose The purpose of this Policy is to set forth the expectations of the Afterpay US, Inc. Board of Directors (the “Afterpay US Board”) for managing and mitigating applicable regulatory compliance risk as it pertains to all U.S. business operations within the Company. This Policy sets forth the framework within which the Company implements its CMS. The Afterpay US Board recognizes that maintaining an effective CMS is critical for mitigating the risks arising from violations of laws and regulations and the potential for customer harm and is necessary to maintain a high level of public confidence in Block’s safe and sound operating practices. A critical aspect of managing and mitigating regulatory compliance risk is the development and maintenance of a CMS that is integrated into the overall framework for product design, delivery, and administration of all product and service life cycles. In general, the Company achieves this standard by ensuring a sound compliance structure is part of daily operations and that the Company identifies Issues and initiates appropriate and timely remediations. It is Afterpay US, Inc.’s policy to comply with all applicable federal and state laws and regulations. Pursuant to this Policy, Afterpay US, Inc. will take prudent and reasonable steps to ensure compliance, including careful consideration of applicable laws and regulations related to the Company. 3. Scope This Policy is applicable to Afterpay US, Inc., and its operations related to the Company. Compliance Management System Policy | Afterpay US, Inc. 7 Confidential and Not for Distribution 4. Policy Requirements 4.1 Requirements for the CMS The Afterpay US Board recognizes that sound compliance risk management, like other areas of operational management, depends on the Company establishing a comprehensive program of risk controls and adequate oversight. The Company requires the CMS to be tailored to the Company’s organizational needs, business strategy, operational complexity, product diversity, market circumstances, regulatory environment, and risk profile. Because these essential criteria are not static, the Afterpay US Board also requires the CMS to have a strong infrastructure while remaining dynamic in its ability to respond to changes.
